Modernizes the two oldest theme files. Must ship with @supports/var() fallback (contrast-color is binary black/white, weak on mid-tones) — correctly flagged. Native CSS, royalty-free. Strong, but a notch below the gates/interchange items because it's an upgrade to an existing system rather than a net-new capability.
topPicks.license: n/a (native CSS, CSS Color L5 — royalty-free)

topPicks.name: Native scroll-timeline Z-dolly depth scene (animation-timeline + translateZ)
topPicks.why: Confirmed net-new and zero-dep: scroll-animations.css uses native scroll-timeline but has ZERO z-axis/perspective; parallax only translates X/Y; css-3d coverflow is a horizontal carousel. This is scroll = camera dolly along +Z, the literal 'spatial depth on flat screens' headline effect, GPU-composited off main thread, degrades to a static layered hero (Firefox). Ships with @supports + reduced-motion. On-trend spatial-design surface with no WebGL cost.

topPicks.name: Progressive Blur (graduated backdrop-filter depth-of-field ramp)
topPicks.why: Confirmed gap: glassmorphism.css is single-layer and glass-refraction does chromatic aberration — NOTHING ramps blur by distance. The ~7-layer increasing-blur + offset-mask stack gives true feathered focus falloff (single backdrop-filter gives a hard edge), the canonical 2026 visionOS/spatial surface for hero fades, fixed nav over content, modal scrims, image bottom-fades. Zero-dep, reimplement from primitives (don't copy author stops). Needs a prefers-reduced-transparency flat-scrim fallback — correctly flagged.

topPicks.name: Broken-grid layout primitive (dense-grid overlap + z-stack)
topPicks.why: Confirmed structural gap: vault has tidy bento/masonry/12-col but NO overlapping/misaligned/z-stacked page layout. Encoding it as line-number placement + capped rotation tokens (--bg-rot, --bg-z, --bg-bleed) keeps it deterministic and verifier-safe (outer grid bounded). High differentiation as the counter to bento saturation. Repo has no license file → reimplement clean from plain CSS Grid spec (correctly flagged). Pairs with the deconstructed-hero archetype and the overflow-gate refinement.
topPicks.license: plain CSS Grid spec (repo unlicensed → reimplement)

topPicks.name: CSS initial-letter + text-box-trim baseline kit
topPicks.why: Confirmed ZERO hits for initial-letter/text-box-trim/half-leading; vault drop-cap is the old ::first-letter float in text-pack-2.css. These are the modern primitives that make drop caps and baseline alignment print-grade without magic numbers, and the technical core that elevates the editorial-spread from 'looks editorial' to 'measures like print'. Correctly flagged support caveat: text-box-trim is Chromium-ahead (FF behind flag) and initial-letter unsupported in FF — MUST ship with the float-drop-cap + margin-rhythm fallback. Verifier runs headless Chrome where both ARE supported, so the gate stays clean.

topPicks.name: Measure / CPL floor + ceiling enforcement (45-75ch, WCAG 80 ceiling)
topPicks.why: Upgrades an existing crude check (vault only flags approxCh>95 — loose ceiling, NO floor, no measure-vs-font-size relation) into a proper two-sided, font-size-aware gate measuring true ch via TextMetrics. Adds the missing floor (<45ch = cramped sidebar prose, the equally-common failure) and the WCAG-backed 80ch hard ceiling. Reports selector + measured ch so the fix is obvious. Pairs naturally with the widow gate as one typography pass. Vanilla, no dep.

topPicks.name: Live-region announcer (aria-live polite/assertive utility)
topPicks.why: Confirmed absent: no programmatic aria-live anywhere; toasts/notifications are visual-only, so 'Saved!'/'Error' is silent to AT. One ~25-line singleton announce() lets toasts, combobox result counts, form validation, drag-reorder and command-palette all become audible — highest a11y leverage per line. Could double as a verification hook (flag interactive components with no announce path). Vanilla, MIT-equivalent, WAI-ARIA spec.

topPicks.name: forced-colors / Windows High Contrast support layer
topPicks.why: Confirmed zero coverage (no forced-colors/Canvas/CanvasText anywhere). The vault composes gradient/glassy surfaces with hairline-alpha borders and shadow-only separation — exactly what vanishes in forced-colors, a real net-new a11y gap for low-vision users. Cheap, zero-dep, degrades to nothing off-mode. Pairs with the light-dark skill and is the support-layer the forced-colors VERIFICATION gate checks. Correct guidance flagged: patch only what disappears, never blanket forced-color-adjust:none.

topPicks.name: Tabbed feature-showcase section (tab-nav <-> sticky product preview)
topPicks.why: Confirmed structure.skill gap: vault has generic UI tabs (tabs/animated-tabs/tabs-pack-2) and device-frame mockups but NOT the composed section marrying a tab rail to a swapping sticky preview pane — no 'feature-tabs'/'product-tour' slot in any archetype. Standard on Figma/Linear/Vercel/Notion 2026 pages. Net-new as the wiring/archetype (reuses existing tabs + device-frame + progress-bar), high SaaS realism payoff. Composition of vault primitives.

topPicks.name: Deconstructed-hero archetype (compose-genre / structure archetype)
topPicks.why: Confirmed: hero-pack 'asymmetric' is just a 2-col big-left/small-right split — no overlapping/z-stacked deconstructed hero, and structure.skill maps every genre to tidy archetypes. Adds a counter-default the engine can pick ON PURPOSE to dodge the bento cliche for editorial/agency/portfolio briefs. Deterministic (fixed line-placed slots), reuses the broken-grid primitive. Lives as a structure.skill archetype + one hero variant — small surface, real differentiation.

topPicks.name: Style Dictionary 4 (DTCG build/transform target)
topPicks.why: The downstream proof that a DTCG export is real: round-trip vault tokens -> Style Dictionary -> CSS and diff against the original palette to prove fidelity. Correctly framed as the export skill's reference consumer + round-trip verifier, NOT a runtime snippet (it's a Node build tool, no CDN). Apache-2.0, canonical. Adds credibility ('export -> Style Dictionary -> CSS' is the path every design-system team runs). Pairs with and depends on the DTCG export skill.

topPicks.name: DTCG token-file validator (@terrazzo/parser or design-tokens-format-module)
topPicks.why: The 'is this a valid design-token file?' gate that makes DTCG export/import trustworthy: validates $type/$value shape, resolves/detects circular {alias} refs, checks color objects — for both export-out (so Figma/Style Dictionary never reject) and import-in. Runs Node-side in the verify harness like the existing render-smoke/lottie-check tools. Both options MIT. Prefer @terrazzo/parser for full alias-cycle resolution. Value tied to shipping the DTCG skill.

topPicks.name: Dismiss-stack manager (DismissableLayer pattern, vanilla)
topPicks.why: Confirmed via search: dialog/drawer/menubar/context-menu/dropdown/popover each attach their OWN document-level Escape/outside-click listener, so one Escape collapses a whole nested stack (wrong per Radix semantics — nested layers close one level at a time). A ~40-line registerLayer/popLayer LIFO with a shared keydown router fixes every overlay at once. Pure vanilla, pairs with a11y.js. Slightly below it because it's narrower in scope.

topPicks.name: Type-ahead (printable-character listbox/menu search) helper
topPicks.why: Confirmed absent across select/menubar/dropdown/tree-menu/combobox (Arrow/Enter/Esc only). typeAhead(items,getLabel) implements the WAI-ARIA APG buffered-string + 500ms-reset + same-char-cycle rule — the single most-missed listbox affordance (type 'g' to jump to Germany). Small, vanilla, instantly upgrades 5 components. Headless UI famously got this rule wrong, so doing it right is a differentiator. Pairs with a11y.js.
topPicks.license: public spec (W3C WAI-ARIA APG) — impl original/MIT

topPicks.name: Brutalist 'intentional clash' palette (.pal-acid-brutal / .pal-raw-paper)
topPicks.why: Fills the one structural hole the neo-brutalist preset needs to point at: saturated primaries on off-white with PURE-BLACK ink and OPAQUE hard borders. Confirmed the closest palettes (crayon, playful-bright) use soft translucent rgba borders + pastels — the literal opposite; vercel-mono/mono-noir are monochrome. Must keep WCAG/APCA on off-white (bright fills + black ink only, never colored text on colored bg) — correctly flagged. Ship light + optional dark. Value gated on shipping the brutalist preset.

topPicks.name: Departure Mono (brutalist pixel-mono typeface)
topPicks.why: Adds the missing TYPE dimension to the brutalist system: vault's mono-technical (ui-monospace/SF Mono) reads 'technical SaaS', not 'raw brutalist'. Departure Mono is the signature 2024-26 anti-design display face, OFL-1.1 (embed/self-host OK). Ship as a new 'brutalist-mono' font pair (Departure headings + Inter/Space Grotesk body) behind an @font-face that degrades to ui-monospace. Headers/labels ONLY — body stays a legible grotesk per NN/g (correctly flagged). Lettra Mono is paid → inspiration only.

topPicks.name: Deckle / torn-edge filter utility (feTurbulence + feDisplacementMap on element edges)
topPicks.why: Confirmed gap: image-distortion-hover.js uses feDisplacementMap only as a JS hover-warp on IMAGES, and buttons-extra torn edges are fixed gradient-mask cutouts (button-only). No general reusable deckle/rough-edge displacement utility that distorts the ACTUAL edges of any element. Vanilla SVG+CSS, dimension-agnostic, tuned presets (subtle paper -> heavy torn). Core building block for the tactile-brutalism genre. Freely reimplementable.

topPicks.name: scroll-state() container queries (stuck/snapped/scrollable/scrolled)
topPicks.why: Confirmed net-new (zero scroll-state/container-type:scroll-state files): replaces the rAF+scroll-listener logic behind headers-pro sticky shadows, scroll-shadow edges and snap highlighting with off-main-thread CSS — fewer JS handlers, better verify pass-rates. Real value, but ranked here because it's Chrome/Edge-only (133+/144+) and MUST sit behind @supports with static-style degradation (correctly flagged) — an enhancement, not a replacement, until FF/Safari ship.

topPicks.name: Native CSS pin & scrub via named view-timeline-name + timeline-scope
topPicks.why: Confirmed net-new tier: scroll-animations.css uses only ANONYMOUS view()/scroll() on single elements; ZERO files use view-timeline-name/scroll-timeline-name/timeline-scope. Named timelines + timeline-scope + position:sticky give CSS-only pin+scrub sequences (image-sequence/MacBook-lid/horizontal-pin/tracing-beam), the zero-dep replacement for several gsap/scroll JS handlers. Must NOT re-add the anonymous reveals/parallax (already present) and must document the animation-shorthand-resets-animation-timeline pitfall. Needs @supports + JS fallback for Safari pre-26.

topPicks.name: In-page motion toggle (WCAG 2.2.2 Pause/Stop/Hide)
topPicks.why: Confirmed gap (no in-page motion toggle/data-reduce anywhere): vault honors OS prefers-reduced-motion but has no UI control, and 2025 consensus is OS-reliance isn't bulletproof. A tiny toggle setting [data-reduce-motion] on <html> (persisted) + a one-line CSS contract makes the whole 920-snippet library WCAG 2.2.2-compliant with one control, composing OS-query OR user-override. Low surface, broad impact. Pairs with the reduced-motion gate.

topPicks.name: COLRv1 color fonts + CSS font-palette (@font-palette-values / override-colors)
topPicks.why: Confirmed gap: no color-font/font-palette/COLRv1 anywhere — vault's 'colorful headline' tools are gradient-text clips and SVG, not palette-driven chromatic glyphs. Free CDN COLRv1 families (Nabla/Foldit/Bungee Spice/Rocher) recolored entirely from CSS gives brand/dark-mode recoloring of one font file for free. Degrades cleanly (Safari renders base outline in solid color). OFL fonts + unencumbered CSS technique. Raises display-type craft; feeds the typographic gate.

topPicks.name: Velocity-driven variable-font axis (weight/width on scroll/pointer speed)
topPicks.why: Confirmed distinct from everything present: variable-font-cursor.js maps cursor DISTANCE (not speed), marquee.js uses velocity only for strip POSITION, webgl-drag-gallery warps IMAGES by velocity — none drive a font axis by velocity. ~30 lines vanilla beside variable-font-cursor.js as the 'velocity' sibling, reduced-motion clamps to fixed weight. The specific 'motion-as-identity / weight-on-velocity' 2026 signal and a bespoke-feeling tell. Original MIT-equivalent impl.

topPicks.name: Cutout-collage layout (CSS Grid + clip-path polygons)
topPicks.why: Confirmed gap: gallery-pack has polaroid-scatter (rotated rectangles) and mosaic-featured but NOTHING with clip-path torn/cutout edges or overlapping interlocked shapes — the editorial-collage/moodboard aesthetic the watercolor/print skills gesture at but can't lay out. Pre-bake ~6 named clip-path classes + grid-area presets so output is deterministic and verifier-safe. Fully vanilla (Rellax parallax optional, not required). Complements rather than duplicates polaroid-scatter.

categories.trends: 2025-2026 in web imagery is defined by three converging shifts, all of which this category's net-new finds target. (1) The compact-placeholder war matured: ThumbHash (MIT, Figma's Evan Wallace, ~25-byte, aspect+alpha, ~120 lines of zero-dep JS) is now the battle-tested canonical, with CSS-only LQIP (Lean Rada's single --lqip integer, pure-CSS decode, ~6 bytes inline, zero JS) as the lightest-touch floor and SplatHash (Feb 2026, 16-byte/22-char, ~0.067ms decode) as the bleeding-edge contender to watch. The vault has shimmer skeletons and a clip-path reveal-mask but ZERO runtime per-image placeholder — a confirmed gap. (2) Native scroll-driven CSS went mainstream: animation-timeline: scroll()/view() (Chrome/Edge 115+, Safari 26 full early 2026, Firefox still flagged) + steps() unlock JS-free Apple-style frame-scrub and parallax off the compositor, and the new CSS Carousel pseudo-elements (::scroll-marker/::scroll-button, Chrome 135+/Safari 18.2+, Firefox absent) replace whole classes of carousel JS — the vault's scroll layer is 100% JS/IntersectionObserver with continuous easing only, never steps() or scroll-timeline. For richer reveals, the AirPods pattern evolved from N JPEGs toward a single MP4 scrubbed via requestVideoFrameCallback + WebCodecs (with canvas frame-sequences as the frame-accurate/mobile-robust fallback per the Oct 2025 Codrops OPTIKKA case study). (3) Performance and provenance became enforceable: the Core Web Vitals discipline (explicit width/height for CLS, fetchpriority=high + no lazy on the LCP hero, AVIF/WebP ) is now codifiable as a headless save-gate dimension — and the vault's gate today only counts imagery PRESENCE (imageCount===0 / GRADIENT-HEAVY tells), with no per- audit, no LCP/CLS observer, making these the highest-leverage moat extensions. Separately, runtime icon delivery shifted from bundled sets to on-demand resolvers (Iconify API / iconify-icon web component over 200k+ ISC/MIT/Apache icons, currentColor-themed, no build), and C2PA Content Credentials (MIT tooling; EU AI Act labeling mandate Aug 2026) is becoming expected for AI-generated imagery. NOTE: this candidate list is heavily duplicated — ThumbHash appears 4×, CSS-LQIP 2×, native-scroll-CSS 3×, and several near-identical verification gates — so the ranking below dedupes to the canonical instance of each.

topPicks.name: ThumbHash (vanilla decode + build-time encode, runtime per-image placeholder)
topPicks.why: Highest design-impact, lowest-risk net-new. Verified gap: vault has skeleton-pack.css (shimmer) + effects/image-reveal-mask (clip-path) but NO runtime placeholder showing a blurred preview of the actual image. MIT, ~120-280 lines, fully vanilla-reimplementable (Uint8Array+btoa, zero deps), encode side rides the existing Sharp pipeline in images.skill.md. The ~25-char hash lives as data-thumbhash in HTML and carries aspect ratio, so it kills CLS by construction and raises the perceived-quality floor on every . This is the canonical instance of 4 duplicate ThumbHash entries in the list; it both SATISFIES and is VERIFIED by the placeholder gate below. Pair as the higher-fidelity hero option above CSS-LQIP.

topPicks.name: responsive-picture.js — vanilla <picture>/srcset emitter (art-directed crops + AVIF/WebP/JPEG + fetchpriority + width/height)
topPicks.why: Closes the exact seam images.skill.md leaves open: the skill does Sharp/ImageMagick encoding and literally says 'serve via <picture>/srcset + sizes' but ships NO markup emitter — and the vault confirmed has only blocks/image-crop.js (interactive cropper) + layout/aspect-ratios.css, nothing that writes responsive picture markup. Zero-dep, single-source API (one hero path in → correct media-then-type source ordering, srcset width descriptors, sizes, width+height for CLS, loading/decoding/fetchpriority out). Encodes the locked 2026 canonical rule (media=art-direction, srcset/sizes=resolution-switching). Directly feeds the responsive-imagery verification gate. The Sharp side already exists; this is the missing composed-markup half.
topPicks.license: Pattern (MDN/web.dev canonical) — reimplemented vanilla, no dep

topPicks.name: Per- imagery-quality verification gate (Lighthouse-grounded: width/height + LCP-not-lazy + fetchpriority + modern-format + alt + placeholder)
topPicks.why: The moat. CONFIRMED from source (designbook-mcp/server.js gateFindings + server.js coherence): the save-gate today checks ONLY coherence score, the GRADIENT-HEAVY-NO-IMAGERY tell, and imageCount===0 for 5 mandatory genres — there is NO per- width/height/lazy/fetchpriority/format/alt/placeholder audit anywhere. This turns the vague 'imagery floor' into a hard, copy-resistant 0-100 subscore mapping straight to CLS+LCP. Most checks are static HTML parse; LCP-identity and rendered-vs-intrinsic come free from the headless render the gate already drives. It is the enforcement layer that makes the ThumbHash/LQIP/picture-emitter finds an enforceable standard rather than optional. Consolidates the 3 near-duplicate verification-gate entries (placeholder gate, responsive gate, per-img gate) into one capability.
topPicks.license: Reference rules Apache-2.0 (web.dev/Lighthouse) — reimplemented vanilla, no dep

topPicks.name: Native CSS scroll-driven primitives (animation-timeline: scroll()/view() — parallax depth layers + steps() sprite/frame scrub)
topPicks.why: Genuinely net-new capability the engine encodes NOWHERE. Verified: every parallax/scroll snippet in the vault is JS (scroll-fx-pack.js IntersectionObserver, gsap/parallax.js, hero-parallax.css is CSS-transform; animations/scroll-animations.css is JS-class-driven with continuous easing only — never steps(), never scroll-timeline). Lets the engine emit compositor-driven, jank-free parallax and JS-free Apple-style frame-scrub where supported (Chrome/Edge 115+, Safari 26 full early-2026), degrading to static frame where unsupported (Firefox still flagged) and disabled under reduced-motion. Public web platform, MIT-reimplementable. Merges the 3 duplicate scroll-driven-CSS entries; ship gated behind @supports (animation-timeline: scroll()) with the scroll-timeline polyfill (Apache-2.0) behind a need()-guard.
topPicks.license: Native CSS (public web platform); polyfill Apache-2.0

topPicks.name: Iconify runtime icon pipeline (iconify-icon web component / on-demand API loader, need()-guarded)
topPicks.why: Fills the single biggest icon gap: the vault has NO icon-rendering mechanism at all — blocks/icon-picker.js is emoji chars (70+), social-icons expects pasted SVG. One ~3KB component (<iconify-icon icon='lucide:home'>) or a ~40-line loader resolves any of 200k+ icons (Lucide ISC, Phosphor/Tabler/Heroicons MIT, Material Symbols/Remix Apache — all attribution-free) at runtime with currentColor theming, 7-day immutable cache, CORS *, no key, no build, inherent tree-shaking. Behind a need()-guard it degrades to the 36 pure-CSS icons / emoji set. Pin to the license-clean prefixes per asset-libraries.md. This is the loader that makes the icon-family taste skill actionable. (Note shadow-DOM render: set color/font-size on the host.)

topPicks.name: Icon-family taste decision skill (Lucide / Phosphor / Tabler / Heroicons / Material Symbols / Remix)
topPicks.why: The missing taste layer, not a duplicate: asset-libraries.md only has a flat 'default to Lucide/Phosphor/Tabler' line with NO vibe mapping, no stroke/grid/weight specs, and omits Heroicons/Material/Remix. This .skill.md maps page aesthetic → one coherent set (verified 2026 specs: Lucide ISC 24px/2px clean-SaaS; Tabler MIT 5,800+ dense-dashboards; Phosphor MIT 6-weight playful/editorial; Heroicons MIT outline/solid pairs for Tailwind product; Material Symbols Apache variable-font Google-feel; Remix Apache neutral system UI) so the engine picks instead of mixing. Pairs directly with the Iconify loader above. All six families permissive, no attribution.
topPicks.license: Skill content; references ISC/MIT/Apache — all permissive

topPicks.name: Vanilla blur-up loader (img.decode() + CSS filter transition)
topPicks.why: The exact runtime load-transition the vault is missing, ~15 lines zero-dep. A tiny LQIP shown with filter:blur(20px) scale(1.06); a full-res loads off-DOM and on await img.decode() (paint-ready, so NO flash of half-decoded image — the key edge over onload) swaps and transitions filter:blur(0). Complements (does not duplicate) effects/image-reveal-mask (geometric clip-path) and skeleton-pack (generic shimmer). Reimplement as media/blur-up.css + blur-up.js, do NOT vendor a lib. Guard reduced-motion + decode() rejection. Sits one tier below ThumbHash (needs a real LQIP byte payload) but is the cleanest decode()-gated reveal mechanism and pairs with both ThumbHash and CSS-LQIP.

topPicks.name: CSS-only LQIP (Lean Rada single --lqip integer, zero-JS placeholder)
topPicks.why: The lightest-touch placeholder floor and natural default for deterministically-composed pages: ~6 bytes of inline style='--lqip:N', decoded ENTIRELY in CSS (OKLAB base + 3x2 brightness grid → radial+linear gradients via calc()/mod()/round()), painting on first CSS parse with no JS, no canvas, no decode step. Lower fidelity than ThumbHash but unbeatable cost; ideal as the no-JS fallback beneath blur-up/ThumbHash. LICENSE CAVEAT: the author declares no explicit license on the post — treat the published bit-layout as inspiration and ship a clean-room reimplementation of the encoder+CSS, do not copy the script verbatim. Gate behind @supports (color: oklab(0 0 0)); degrade to solid average-color on older engines. (Consolidates 2 duplicate entries.)
topPicks.license: Technique/algorithm (author license unspecified) — clean-room reimplement, do NOT copy code

topPicks.name: scroll-video (currentTime-bound hero scrubber) + scroll-frames (canvas image-sequence) + scroll-media.skill.md decision tree
topPicks.why: The premium product-reveal pattern the vault entirely lacks: it has video PLAYERS (video-player-pro) and element scroll-scrub (scroll-scrub.js tweens DOM only) but NOTHING that scrubs a video by scroll or draws an image-sequence to canvas. Ship BOTH ends of the tradeoff plus the decision skill: scroll-video binds one short MP4's currentTime to scroll via requestVideoFrameCallback (now cross-browser: rVFC coalesces seeks, kills jank) for light 3-6s clips; scroll-frames preloads a WebP/AVIF sequence + drawImage per rAF (DPR-scaled, decode()-gated) for frame-accurate/mobile-robust heroes (the OPTIKKA migration path). The skill encodes the keyframe-density jank playbook (ffmpeg -g 10, Firefox needs denser) and the reduced-motion=poster+#t= fallback. All vanilla, no dep; bundle the WebCodecs/tar single-file preload strategy (from @mediamonks/fast-image-sequence, MIT, as inspiration) into the snippet rather than CDN-loading it.
topPicks.license: Vanilla reimplementation (public web-platform techniques); inspiration refs MIT

topPicks.name: Ambient scene-motion layer for illustrations.js (per-group looping choreography)
topPicks.why: Turns the vault's already-shipped 12 static duotone scenes from decorative-but-dead into living hero/spot art with zero new raster assets and zero deps — exactly what the imagery floor wants. Verified net-new: svg/illustrations.js bodies are essentially flat (only ~2 <g> groups) and svg/illustrations.css animates only the WHOLE element (.ill--float, single reduced-motion block). This wraps each semantic layer in <g class='ill-anim ill-anim--drift/bob/twinkle/sway' style='--ill-delay:..'> with transform-box:fill-box for correct in-place pivots, slow 4-18s staggered sine loops, transform-only for cross-browser safety, aria-hidden on decorative motion, and one prefers-reduced-motion freeze. Requires refactoring scene bodies into named <g> layers (or a post-mount tagger). Theming untouched (currentColor/--ill-accent).
topPicks.license: MIT (reimplemented technique; Smashing article reference only)

topPicks.name: CSS Carousel API (::scroll-marker / ::scroll-marker-group / ::scroll-button())
topPicks.why: Native zero-JS carousel chrome (dot indicators + prev/next buttons + browser-managed focus/keyboard a11y) on top of a scroll-snap container — replaces a whole class of carousel JS, on-brand for the zero-dep ethos. Verified net-new: components/carousel-pack.css + layout/scroll-snap.css are snap-only with no markers/buttons. Support is narrower than scroll-driven animations (Chrome/Edge 135+ Mar 2025, Safari 18.2+ Dec 2025, Firefox absent mid-2026), so gate behind @supports (scroll-marker-group: after) and degrade to the bare scroll-snap strip the vault already ships (markers/buttons simply don't render). Native CSS, no license.

topPicks.name: LCP-element discovery + CLS gate via PerformanceObserver in the save-gate render
topPicks.why: The live-render mechanism that makes the imagery-quality gate's LCP/CLS legs trustworthy rather than guessed. Verified: render-smoke.mjs has ZERO PerformanceObserver/largest-contentful-paint/LayoutShift code — neither an LCP nor a CLS probe exists in the verify pipeline. A small headless helper registers PerformanceObserver({type:'largest-contentful-paint'}) (plus a largest-above-fold- fallback) to identify the LCP element and assert it is not loading=lazy, carries fetchpriority=high, and is in the static HTML; and a LayoutShift observer fails the gate above CLS>0.1 during any skeleton→content swap. ~10% of real pages lazy-load their LCP image and pay 200-800ms; this structurally guarantees the engine never ships that. Thin incremental wiring on the headless Chrome the gate already drives. Reusable for non-image LCP diagnostics too.

topPicks.name: dotLottie v2 enhancements — state machines + runtime theming (setThemeData) + GPU/worker backends
topPicks.why: Pure enhancement of an already-bundled dep (verified: media/lottie-player.js wraps @lottiefiles/dotlottie-web@0.74.0, MIT) — NOT a new dependency. The player today only wires load/scroll/hover playback. State machines (stateMachineLoad/Start, typed inputs, auto-attached pointer listeners) unlock Rive-like branching interactivity on the free MIT runtime with no paid Rive export seat — the most distinctive 2025-2026 dotLottie feature, currently unreachable. setThemeData rules map the page's --accent/--bg tokens onto color slots so vector motion inherits the 50+ pal-* themes (directly serving lottie-rive.skill.md's 'tokens not baked brand colors' rule). WebGL2/WebGPU + DotLottieWorker/OffscreenCanvas are opt-in perf wins for dense heroes. Ship state-machine + theming as primary; GPU/worker as feature-detected opt-in (navigator.gpu fallback).

topPicks.name: feSpecularLighting/feDiffuseLighting lit-emboss + feComponentTransfer grain-in-gradient (paper-texture.css + .grain-solo)
topPicks.why: Two net-new SVG-filter primitives that attack the gradients-look-AI tell with tactile depth. Verified: every vault grain snippet (effects/gradient-noise.css, noise-grain.css, layout/section-bg-pack.css) uses only flat fractalNoise/feTurbulence speckle; feSpecularLighting appears only in image duotone (effects/duotone.js) and feComponentTransfer appears NOWHERE. (1) feSpecularLighting+feDistantLight produces a LIT, embossed paper/fiber surface with real highlight/shadow (surfaceScale+elevation) — a printed-on-stock read. (2) feComponentTransfer (feFuncA tableValues) composites grain AND gradient inside ONE data-URI SVG background-image (no ::after overlay/z-index plumbing) with fine control over which noise values show. Both MIT-reimplementable from MDN/SVG-spec primitives — do NOT ship fffuel output (inspiration-only, non-redistributable). Pairs with print-typography.skill.md.
topPicks.license: Technique MIT-reimplementable from MDN/SVG spec; fffuel output NOT redistributable (inspiration only)

topPicks.name: Generated-image palette coherence gate (ΔE2000 vs page tokens)
topPicks.why: Operationalizes art-direction.skill.md's prose rule as a measurable gate. Verified the rule exists verbatim (art-direction.skill.md line 47: name the palette mood 'so the photo sits in the design instead of on top of it') and is currently UNMEASURED. After generating an image, REUSE the existing utils/gradient-extract.js (confirmed present: k-means dominant-palette extractor) and compute ΔE2000 distance to the page's design tokens; flag for regen if the image clashes (cool-blue hero on warm-cream editorial). CIEDE2000 is ~40 lines of vanilla math, zero-dep. Net-new: the vault has the extractor and the verify harness but no coherence check tying generated imagery to the token palette — a direct, cheap extension of the imagery-floor moat.

topPicks.name: Hero LCP preload pattern (<link rel=preload as=image imagesrcset/imagesizes fetchpriority=high>)
topPicks.why: The <head> companion to the picture emitter, closing the LCP loop images.skill.md has no guidance for. A rel=preload as=image link whose imagesrcset/imagesizes MIRROR the hero's srcset/sizes so the browser preloads the exact resolution it paints; Google's own test cut LCP 2.6s→1.9s from fetchpriority alone. Small but high-leverage. Ship as an optional 'hero mode' of the picture emitter. Caveat: emit ONLY in the single-format resolution-switching case (imagesrcset can't disambiguate art-directed media sources), never the multi-crop case — so it's bounded, not universal.

topPicks.name: @starting-style + View-Transition skeleton→content reveal (CSS-only + VT crossfade)
topPicks.why: The missing SWAP discipline beneath the vault's skeleton SHAPES. Verified: vault has skeleton-pack.css shimmer/pulse/wave shapes but the moment of swap is a hard cut; zero hits for @starting-style/transition-behavior/allow-discrete, and view-transitions.js/named-transitions.js are page/route+shared-element scoped, not in-place skeleton crossfade. Ship two layered helpers: @starting-style{.loaded{opacity:0}} + transition allow-discrete (Baseline 2026 across all engines, degrades to instant swap) as the default .reveal-on-load, and document.startViewTransition(swap) (same-doc VT Baseline Firefox 144 Oct 2025) as the richer crossfade where supported. Both web-platform, no license, reduced-motion guarded. Pairs with the CLS gate above to make 'zero-CLS reveal' provable.

topPicks.name: C2PA Content Credentials pipeline (c2patool/c2pa-python sign + c2pa-wc badge)
topPicks.why: The 2025-2026 provenance standard for AI imagery, and it fixes a real latent bug: verified images.skill.md runs `magick in.jpg -strip ...` which DESTROYS C2PA manifests. C2PA v2.1 added explicit AI-generation assertion types (trainedAlgorithmicMedia) and the EU AI Act mandates AI-content labeling by Aug 2026 — provenance is becoming expected. All tooling MIT (c2patool, c2pa-rs, c2pa-python, c2pa-wc v0.14.x on jsDelivr). A skill wires sign-after-optimize-never-strip + optional browser credential badge, turning the local mflux generator into a verifiable, standards-compliant feature. Flag: signing needs an X.509 cert (self-signed fine for local/dev; CA cert only for public trust chains). Lower rank only because it's adjacent to the core imagery-quality moat, not central to it.

topPicks.name: Scroll-driven 'when to go JS-free' decision note + scroll-media skill addendum
topPicks.why: The safety layer that makes the native-CSS scroll finds emit deterministically rather than ad hoc. Codifies WHEN to emit pure-CSS scroll scrubbing (@supports-guarded animation-timeline+steps() with a static-frame fallback for evergreen Chromium/Safari 26+) vs route to scroll-scrub.js/canvas (Firefox parity, precise pause/scrub, crossfade). Encodes the 2026 support matrix, the two @supports guards, the Firefox non-zero-animation-duration gotcha, and mandatory prefers-reduced-motion — and tells the verifier's imagery/coherence gates that a static fallback frame must exist. Additive per the vault's additive-only policy. Fold into images.skill.md or a small scroll skill rather than a standalone.

topPicks.name: Theme-aware + performance-gated grain decision-tree (fold into print-typography/color-grade skill)
topPicks.why: The moat-y guidance that turns the vault's three grain snippets (+ the two new SVG-filter ones) into a coherent, theme-safe, verify-passable choice. Verified gap: no vault grain file references prefers-color-scheme or a paint-cost budget; only gradient-noise has reduced-motion. Covers (1) theme-awareness — invert grain blend/opacity under prefers-color-scheme (soft-light↔overlay flip) so grain survives a theme switch; (2) performance ceiling — numOctaves<=3, bound filter paint with contain:paint/clip-path, steps()-quantize+reduced-motion-off animated grain, and a raster-escape-hatch (tiny WebP/AVIF) for large mobile areas. Value 3 (skill/glue, not a new capability); best folded into print-typography.skill.md or color-grade.skill.md as a 'tactile finish' section, not a standalone.

topPicks.name: Iconify search/autocomplete → upgrade icon-picker from emoji to real line-icons (INSPIRATION / smallest add)
topPicks.why: Smallest of the icon adds and an enhancement layer on an existing block, so ranked last among the keepers. blocks/icon-picker.js is emoji-based (verified) with a documented hook for custom SVG sets but no discovery; the Iconify /search?query=&limit= endpoint returns ranked fully-qualified names rendered as live previews via the SVG endpoint, turning it into Notion/Linear-grade search over 200k icons with zero local index. Behind a need()-guard it degrades to the current emoji set. Scope prefixes to lucide/ph/tabler for license cleanliness. Only worth doing AFTER the Iconify loader (find #5) lands, since it depends on the same resolver.
topPicks.license: API MIT; filter results to ISC/MIT sets

topPicks.name: SplatHash + variable-axes icon animation + transform-based pseudo-path-morph (WATCH / INSPIRATION cluster)
topPicks.why: Three forward-looking technique notes to track, not ship as primaries. (1) SplatHash (MIT, Feb 2026, 16-byte/22-char, ~0.067ms decode, bit-identical Go/TS/Python) is the newest/fastest/smallest placeholder hash but lacks ThumbHash's alpha+broad porting and is only ~200 stars — flag as inspiration, ThumbHash stays the recommended default until adoption matures. (2) Material Symbols variable-axes animation (font-variation-settings FILL/wght/GRAD transitions, zero JS, one glyph morphs outline→filled) is a distinct motion primitive from svg-animations.js's two-path morph — but Material-Symbols-only (needs the variable font), so a CSS recipe in the icon skill, reached for only on Google-feel pages. (3) Transform-based seamless liquid/cloud loop with an OPTIONAL Chromium-only CSS d: enhancement tier — net-new vs the vault's vertex-matched icon morph, but the d: tier MUST be enhancement-only (Firefox/Safari hard-toggle, no tween). All MIT/permissive or web-platform; grouped as the 'watch' tier so the primaries above stay the clear recommendations.

backlog.name: Ordered (Bayer) dithering post-FX pass for ShaderRunner — shaders/dither.glsl.js
backlog.what: A composable GPU ordered-dithering fragment-shader pass: recursive Bayer-matrix (2x2/4x4/8x8) threshold map in screen-space, optional UV pixelation pre-pass, two-tone ink/paper or N-step palette quantization. Reimplemented vanilla (no glslify/postprocessing dep) as {fragment, proceduralFragment, defaults} mirroring the existing HalftoneShader API so it drops straight onto ShaderRunner over an image (u_tex) or a procedural fbm/gradient source. This is the dominant 2024-2026 retro/print web look and is genuinely NOT in the vault: halftone.glsl.js is a rotated dot-grid (continuous dot radius per cell), CSS stepped-gradient.css .dither-* is SVG turbulence noise — neither does ordered per-pixel threshold quantization. Bayer is the canonical, texture-free, sub-0.2ms-at-4K technique. Source patterns: Codrops June/July 2025 tutorials (recursive Bayer macro) + hughsk/glsl-dither (MIT) for the matrix functions.
backlog.license: Reimplement vanilla. Basis: hughsk/glsl-dither MIT; niccolofanton/dithering-shader and Codrops tutorials are unlicensed walkthroughs (technique, not code-copy). The Bayer recurrence is public-domain math.
backlog.note: Mirror HalftoneShader's {fragment, proceduralFragment, defaults} contract and ShaderRunner imageUniforms so it composes identically. Add to shaders.skill.md's existing dithering line (currently only points at restricted/inspiration sources). Tunables: u_pixelSize, u_matrix (2/4/8), u_inkColor, u_paper, u_steps for multi-level.

backlog.name: Blue-noise dithering variant + CC0 tileable blue-noise texture
backlog.what: A second dither mode that samples a small tileable blue-noise texture (texture2D(uNoise, gl_FragCoord.xy/64.0)) as the threshold instead of the Bayer grid, optionally shifting the sample per-frame to make the pattern near-invisible (animated grain). Blue noise is the perceptual middle ground between structured Bayer (visible cross-hatch) and white noise (clumpy) — the look Maxime Heckel popularized for 2025-26 retro shading and for killing gradient banding. The enabling asset is Christoph Peters' free blue-noise texture set: CC0 public domain, tileable, 16x16 up to 1024x1024, single/multi-channel PNG — bundle the 64x64 8-bit as a base64 data-URI so the snippet stays zero-network. This complements (does not duplicate) the Bayer pass: same ShaderRunner mount, different threshold source.
backlog.license: CC0 / public domain (texture, no attribution required). Shader code reimplemented vanilla.
backlog.note: Ship as a u_mode/u_noise uniform branch within the same dither.glsl.js so Bayer and blue-noise share one snippet. Inline the 64x64 PNG as a data-URI to avoid a network dep (ShaderRunner imageUniforms can take it). This is the only piece needing an external asset — CC0 makes it clean.

backlog.name: Maxime Heckel — 'The Art of Dithering and Retro Shading for the Web'
backlog.what: The single best 2025 reference for the dither aesthetic: walks white-noise vs ordered-Bayer vs blue-noise tradeoffs, gives the 4x4 Bayer GLSL and the texture-based blue-noise approach, and explicitly notes error-diffusion / Floyd-Steinberg is NOT fragment-shader-friendly (sequential) — which justifies why the vault should ship ordered+blue-noise and skip true error diffusion. Use as the skill-doc decision reference for when to pick each mode (banding-kill vs print look vs animated grain) and for the visual-quality knobs (per-frame noise shift, luminance-vs-per-channel quantization). Inspiration/reference, not a dependency.
backlog.license: Blog/tutorial — code shown is illustrative, no explicit OSS license. Treat as technique reference, reimplement.
backlog.note: Fold the Bayer-vs-blue-noise-vs-skip-error-diffusion decision tree into shaders.skill.md alongside the new dither snippet. FLAG sibling sources: LYGIA dither funcs (lygia.xyz/color/dither) = Prosperity License (non-commercial default) and paper-design/shaders = PolyForm Shield — both inspiration-only, do NOT vendor as deps.

backlog.name: Liquid-glass card (SVG feDisplacementMap as backdrop-filter + feSpecularLighting rim)
backlog.what: The canonical iOS 26 Liquid Glass recreation: a JS-generated displacement map (Snell refraction -> RGB-encoded X/Y offsets, R=128+x*127 / G=128+y*127) referenced by `backdrop-filter: url(#filter)` via feDisplacementMap, with a feSpecularLighting/feImage rim highlight and 3-pass RGB chromatic aberration. This is TRUE background/lens refraction the vault completely lacks — its glass-refraction.css is only chromatic RGB-split text + an edge-bleed blob, and glassmorphism.css is plain blur. Worth adding as a self-contained `.liquid-glass` card snippet: a small JS map-builder + the SVG filter + the CSS. High-value, on-trend, net-new.
backlog.license: No code released (article walkthrough only) — reimplement from the described pipeline; treat as inspiration-grade source, ship original vault code
backlog.note: CRITICAL caveat to bake in: `backdrop-filter: url(#svg)` is Chromium-only (Chrome/Edge/Brave/Arc). Firefox/Safari ignore it and must fall back to plain `backdrop-filter: blur()` + inset rim shadow via @supports — degrades cleanly, no errors. Supports fixed-size rounded-rects/circles only; resizing forces a full map rebuild (animate `scale`, not geometry). Pairs with a need()-style guard.

backlog.name: liquidGL (naughtyduk)
backlog.what: The WebGL screen-space refraction upgrade path: turns any DOM element into a refracting glass pane by snapshotting the page background with html2canvas, uploading it as a texture, and UV-displacing it in a fragment shader (refraction/bevelDepth/bevelWidth/frost/magnify/tilt). Unlike the SVG approach it works cross-browser (Chrome/FF/Safari/Edge) because it never relies on backdrop-filter:url(). Best framing for Design Book: a cdn-snippet WebGL UPGRADE behind a need()-guard that the SVG vanilla-snippet degrades to/from. Most mature option in the space (628 stars, ~221 commits, vanilla JS, syncs with Lenis/Locomotive/GSAP).
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: Hard dependency on html2canvas (v1.4.1+) — two scripts, both must be guarded; throws if missing. Caveats: CORS-clean images only, fixed-position elements ignored, CSS animations not captured live, Safari unstable when glass >50% viewport, very long docs can exceed GPU texture limits. Fallback is CSS backdrop-filter frost. Note vs. the closed WebGL alt liquid-glass.ybouane.com (SaaS demo, inspiration-only).

backlog.name: prefers-reduced-transparency + @supports(backdrop-filter:url) fallback gate
backlog.what: A verification/gate capability that makes any glass component ship-safe: (1) detect @media (prefers-reduced-transparency: reduce) and swap to a solid high-contrast surface (opacity ~0.95, no blur); (2) require an @supports fallback chain so the Chromium-only `backdrop-filter: url()` lens degrades to plain blur in FF/Safari; (3) enforce a contrast floor on text over translucent glass. This is the moat — the vault has glassmorphism but no diagnostic that catches the two ways glass breaks (no backdrop support + reduced-transparency users). Naturally extends the existing verify save-gate.
backlog.license: Web standard (MDN/CSSWG) — no license constraint
backlog.note: No existing vault coverage of prefers-reduced-transparency or backdrop-filter:url() @supports fallback (grep clean). Headless-Chrome-checkable: emulate the media feature and assert the surface goes opaque + text contrast passes.

backlog.name: nikdelvin/liquid-glass (pure CSS+SVG components)
backlog.what: A pure-CSS+SVG-filter reference implementation (feDisplacementMap + feGaussianBlur + feColorMatrix, no WebGL) shipping LiquidGlass container / LiquidText / LiquidButton with documented Safari fallback to blurred glassmorphism. Useful as a concrete, framework-agnostic reference to validate the vault's own liquid-glass snippet against — confirms the technique generalizes beyond a single card to text and buttons. Lower priority than the kube.io technique (which is the better pedagogical source) but a clean working cross-check.
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: Reference-grade: pull the SVG filter + CSS structure, ignore the Astro/Tailwind/Anime.js site wrapper. Same Chromium backdrop-filter:url() caveat applies. Two adjacent MIT alts: rizroze/liquid-glass (zero-dep single file but only 3 stars/3 commits, low-traction) and dashersw/liquid-glass-js (WebGL2, 448 stars but ~1 commit / dormant, no fallback).

backlog.name: three.js WebGPU TSL compute particles (webgpu_tsl_compute_attractors_particles)
backlog.what: The canonical, MIT, self-contained pattern for a GPGPU compute particle field: declares position/velocity storage buffers via instancedArray(COUNT,'vec3'), advects them in a renderer.compute() pass written in TSL (Fn/Loop/If), renders with spriteNodeMaterial. ~262k particles in the stock example, scales to 1M+. The single biggest reason to add it: WebGPURenderer auto-falls-back to WebGL2 for RENDERING with zero extra code, so the render path is solved for free. Gives Design Book the 'compute tier' it completely lacks (vault is fragment-quad shaders + vertex-only point clouds only). Best shipped as a CDN-guarded snippet (three WebGPU build + three/tsl) that need()-loads three and feature-detects navigator.gpu, with swappable advection kernels (curl-noise, attractor, flow-field).
backlog.license: MIT (three.js)
backlog.note: CRITICAL fallback caveat the question asks about: compute shaders do NOT run under WebGPURenderer's WebGL2 fallback (compute is WebGPU-only). TSL/WebGPURenderer only auto-falls-back the RENDER path. So a compute snippet must pair with a non-WebGPU advection path (see transform-feedback entry) or degrade particle count + run advection on CPU. Feature-gate on navigator.gpu before instantiating WebGPURenderer in compute mode.

backlog.name: WebGL2 Transform Feedback GPGPU particles (the no-compute fallback path)
backlog.what: The vanilla-reimplementable technique that answers the 'how should it fall back' half of the question. WebGL2 has no compute shaders, so GPU particle state (tfPosition/tfVelocity VBOs) is advanced by a transform-feedback vertex shader with rasterization disabled, ping-ponging two buffer sets each frame — still fully on-GPU, still handles 1M particles, no compute API needed. This is the correct middle rung of the degradation ladder (WebGPU compute -> WebGL2 transform-feedback -> CPU/2D-canvas reduced count). Add as a zero-dep vanilla snippet so the compute snippet has a real GPU fallback for the ~20-30% of mid-2026 browsers (notably default-off Firefox + older devices) without WebGPU, rather than collapsing straight to slow CPU canvas. WebGL2 is ~96% supported.
backlog.license: Public-domain / freely usable tutorial technique (webgl2fundamentals is BSD-style; the technique itself is unencumbered — reimplement from spec)
backlog.note: Reimplement the curl-noise/flow advection in a GLSL ES 3.00 vertex shader; do NOT vendor cabbibo/glsl-curl-noise (no license — flagged in vault shaders.skill.md). Port curl from a clean base (stegu/webgl-noise, MIT). This is the load-bearing fallback rung the question demands.

backlog.name: Compute-tier 'GPU particle field' compose-genre + skill (WebGPU->WebGL2->CPU ladder)
backlog.what: The decision-tree that ties the above together into Design Book's first 'compute' capability. A short .skill.md plus an addable hero/background archetype that: (1) feature-detects navigator.gpu, (2) runs WebGPU compute advection (curl-noise / attractor / boids) on position+velocity storage buffers at 1M+ points when available, (3) drops to WebGL2 transform-feedback GPU advection, (4) drops to a capped CPU/2D-canvas count (reuse the vault's existing effects/particles.js or vortex.js as the floor), and honors prefers-reduced-motion at every tier. Maxime Heckel's Oct-2025 field guide is the authoritative open code/explainer for the TSL buffer+compute structure. This is the highest-leverage form: it gives the engine a deterministic 'compute hero' it can compose and a skill that encodes the exact fallback ladder + the compute-doesn't-survive-WebGL2-fallback gotcha.
backlog.license: MIT (relies on three.js MIT + reimplemented vanilla WebGL2; Heckel blog code is open-source / educational)
backlog.note: Net-new: vault's 3d-motion/shaders skills only LINK GPGPU/WebGPU as 'inspiration' and flag it a future direction; no skill encodes the compute tier or its fallback ladder. The skill must state the verification angle: headless Chrome may run WebGPU under SwiftShader/llvmpipe inconsistently, so the save-gate should screenshot-verify the chosen tier actually drew (echoes the vault's existing 'screenshot is truth for WebGL draw' memory) rather than trust navigator.gpu alone.

backlog.name: piellardj/particles-webgpu (clean vanilla WebGPU reference)
backlog.what: A framework-free, pure-WebGPU particle simulation running entirely on the GPU — useful as the no-three.js reference for hand-rolling the WGSL compute kernel + position/velocity buffer layout when the vault wants a truly zero-framework compute snippet (avoids shipping the full three WebGPU build just for a background). Confirms the bare-metal structure: storage buffers, @compute @workgroup_size kernel, bind groups, ping-pong. Pair with takumifukasawa/webgpu-particle-compute-shader-curl-noise-demo for the specific curl-noise advection kernel.
backlog.license: VERIFY before vendoring — license not declared on the repo card; treat as study-only until confirmed (likely MIT given author's other repos, but unconfirmed). takumifukasawa demo license also unconfirmed.
backlog.note: Flag: license unconfirmed on both piellardj and takumifukasawa repos — do NOT vendor; reimplement the WGSL kernel from the (clean) three.js TSL pattern instead. Listed as inspiration, not a dep. Lower value because the three.js TSL route already gives a confirmed-MIT, fallback-capable path.

backlog.name: TSL-vs-raw-GLSL decision skill (when to author shaders in Three Shader Language)
backlog.what: A decision-tree .skill.md that tells Design Book WHEN to reach for TSL/WebGPU vs the vault's existing raw-GLSL runner.js. The core insight researched: TSL is one JS node graph (material.colorNode/positionNode from 'three/tsl') that WebGPURenderer lowers to WGSL on WebGPU and AUTO-FALLS-BACK to GLSL/WebGL2 when WebGPU is absent — write-once, dual-backend, no second renderer. Rule of thumb worth encoding: raw GLSL via runner.js still wins for tiny zero-dep fullscreen-quad effects (the vault's whole shaders/ corpus); TSL wins for material-integrated effects (displacement, custom lighting, postFX nodes) and future-proofing toward WebGPU. Must flag the friction: await renderer.init() is mandatory, and (see other candidate) TSL is ES-modules-only. Net-new because the vault's current TSL mentions are just stubs that point at an external skill, with no decision logic on choosing TSL vs the vault's own raw-GLSL stack.
backlog.license: MIT (three.js docs/API)
backlog.note: Partial overlap: shaders.skill.md already has a short 'WebGPU / TSL note (2026 direction)' and 3d-web.skill.md points to dgreenheck/webgpu-claude-skill. Net-new is the actual DECISION TREE (TSL vs the vault's own raw-GLSL runner.js), not another pointer. Best as an expansion of shaders.skill.md plus a cross-link, rather than a brand-new file.

backlog.name: Minimal TSL dual-compile runner (ESM + import-map, WebGPURenderer with WebGL fallback)
backlog.what: A minimal `tsl-runner` inspiration snippet: an import-map ('three'/'three/webgpu' -> build/three.webgpu.js, 'three/tsl' -> build/three.tsl.js, 'three/addons/' -> examples/jsm/) plus a <script type=module> that does `new WebGPURenderer()`, `await renderer.init()`, and applies a TSL colorNode (uniform(time), uv(), sin, vec3, Fn, mix) to a NodeMaterial — one graph, WGSL on WebGPU, auto GLSL fallback on WebGL2. Value: it is the future-proof companion to shaders/runner.js (which is raw-GLSL/WebGL2-only). CRUCIAL CAVEAT that makes this INSPIRATION not a drop-in cdn-snippet: since r161 three.js removed UMD global builds, so WebGPU+TSL is ES-modules-only via import maps — it CANNOT use the vault's existing window.THREE need()-guard pattern that every 3d/*.js relies on. So it is a different snippet archetype (module + import-map, async init) the engine would have to learn, hence ship as inspiration/reference first.
backlog.license: MIT (three.js)
backlog.note: Not duplicative of shaders/runner.js (raw GLSL, WebGL2, window-global) nor 3d/scene-runner.js (WebGLRenderer, window.THREE). Integration friction is real: ES-modules-only + await init() + import map breaks the vault's UMD-global + need()-guard convention. Recommend inspiration-level so the maintainer decides whether to introduce an ESM-module snippet class; do NOT mislabel as a clean cdn-snippet.

backlog.name: PingPongRunner — vanilla two-texture FBO compute primitive (companion to ShaderRunner)
backlog.what: A self-contained ~120-line vanilla WebGL2 (WebGL1-fallback) runner that allocates TWO float/half-float textures + framebuffers and swaps them each step (front=read sampler, back=render target), running a user fragment shader N steps/frame then blitting front to screen with an optional display shader. This is the EXACT capability the vault is missing and structurally cannot fake: the existing shaders/runner.js draws only to the default framebuffer (gl.drawArrays to screen, no gl.createFramebuffer anywhere), and shaders/fluid.glsl.js's own header admits it approximates fluid 'without requiring multi-pass FBO ping-pong... for a true ping-pong fluid sim you'd need an extended runner.' Adding PingPongRunner unlocks an entire net-new effect class: Gray-Scott reaction-diffusion, Conway/cellular automata, slime-mold/Physarum trails, true Stam fluid, heat/flame, erosion. The technique is public-domain / freely reimplementable (nullprogram CC0-style; Jim Fisher and tdhooper/webgl-grayscott MIT as additional references), so it ships as a pure zero-dep vanilla snippet, no CDN. Highest-value because it is an enabling primitive (verification/moat-adjacent) rather than a single effect.
backlog.license: Public domain / MIT (reimplemented from nullprogram CC0 + Jim Fisher + tdhooper/webgl-grayscott MIT references)
backlog.note: Net-new primitive. Closest existing thing is shaders/runner.js (single-pass, screen-only) which it complements, not duplicates. Requires EXT_color_buffer_float (WebGL2) / OES_texture_float for state precision — guard and degrade to a static/CSS fallback when absent, matching the vault's need()-guard convention.

backlog.name: grayscott.glsl.js — Gray-Scott reaction-diffusion shader pair (state + display) for the new runner
backlog.what: The first concrete effect to ship on top of PingPongRunner: a two-shader pair — a state-update fragment (Laplacian 3x3 diffusion + Gray-Scott feed/kill reaction writing A,B into texture channels) plus a display fragment that maps B to a palette. Produces the iconic organic coral/leopard/fingerprint/mitosis patterns that are highly on-trend for 2025-26 hero backgrounds and impossible in the vault today (all 16 current shaders are single-pass procedural). Ships with named presets (coral, mitosis, fingerprint, spots-and-loops, worms) as feed/kill tuples — mirrors how gradient-flow.glsl.js / mesh-gradient-wgl.glsl.js already ship palette/preset tables. Reimplemented vanilla from MIT references (tdhooper/webgl-grayscott, piellardj/reaction-diffusion-webgl, jasonwebb/reaction-diffusion-playground all MIT); the GLSL kernel is ~30 lines and trivially inlinable.
backlog.license: MIT (reimplemented from tdhooper/webgl-grayscott + piellardj/reaction-diffusion-webgl, both MIT)
backlog.note: Depends on the PingPongRunner primitive landing first. Pointer can seed B to let users 'paint' growth — pairs naturally with the runner's u_mouse.

backlog.name: gpu-io
backlog.what: MIT GPGPU library (Amanda Ghassaei) that abstracts exactly the ping-pong FBO bookkeeping — GPULayer state textures, GPUProgram passes, automatic WebGL2/WebGL1 fallback, float16 precision handling, multi-pass step loops. Official examples cover reaction-diffusion, fluid (Stam), Conway/cellular automata, and Physarum/slime-mold — the entire effect class the vault lacks. Ships a UMD build (window.GPUIO) on jsDelivr (cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/gpu-io, v0.2.7), so it fits the vault's cdn-snippet + need()-guard pattern (like OGL for webgl-drag-gallery or Tweakpane for LiveControls) and degrades gracefully when WebGL/float-textures are absent. Best role: the HEAVY-DUTY option for complex multi-buffer sims (full fluid, agent systems) where hand-rolling the vanilla runner gets unwieldy. CAVEAT for value/maintenance: author announced Oct-2022 'switching gears,' GitHub shows no tagged releases and limited recent activity — feature-complete and stable but effectively in maintenance-only mode; flag it as a stable-but-dormant dep, prefer the vanilla PingPongRunner as the default and gpu-io as the escalation path.
backlog.license: MIT (deps @amandaghassaei/type-checks, @petamoriken/float16, changedpi, file-saver — all MIT)
backlog.note: Lower value than the vanilla primitives precisely because of the dormant-maintenance flag and added CDN weight; recommend as the 'when vanilla isn't enough' option, behind a need()-guard with a static fallback. Not GPL/AGPL — license is clean.

backlog.name: Timothy Lottes CRT shader (FixingPixelArt) — vanilla crt.glsl.js
backlog.what: The canonical single-pass CRT fragment shader: scanline beam profile (Gaussian Scan()), RGB shadow-mask / aperture-grille (Mask(), rotatable for less chroma error), and barrel/curvature warp (Warp()) over a sampled input texture. ~123 lines of code, explicitly headered 'PUBLIC DOMAIN CRT STYLED SCAN-LINE SHADER by Timothy Lottes'. It is the exact missing piece: the vault has plasma/voronoi/aurora procedural shaders + a fullscreen-quad ShaderRunner (which already supports imageUniforms sampler2D), CSS-only post-processing.css, and Three.js-only UnrealBloom — but NO GPU CRT/scanline/aperture-grille/barrel pass. Reimplement as shaders/crt.glsl.js exposing CrtShader.fragment + .defaults + .presets (trinitron/arcade/amber-terminal/pvm/green-phosphor), mirroring the PlasmaShader module shape so it drops onto the runner with one ShaderRunner.create() call. Pairs directly with the existing plasma/demoscene shader and the terminal/retro-grid/perspective-tunnel genre cluster.
backlog.license: Public Domain (header-declared by Timothy Lottes)
backlog.note: Public domain = cleanest possible license for inlining. Lottes provides scanlines + mask + warp but NOT bloom or chromatic edge bleed; add a small phosphor-glow (separable Gaussian taps) + RGB-offset chromatic step from CC0/public-domain technique to complete the 5-effect brief. Runs procedurally (CRT-grid demo) or post-processes any image via imageUniforms:{source:'shot.png'}.

backlog.name: crt-fx (stefanlegg) — MIT WebGL CRT effect set + presets
backlog.what: Zero-dependency WebGL1/GLSL-ES CRT library covering the full brief in one place: scanlines, 10 phosphor masks (aperture-grille, shadow-mask, slot-mask + 7 more), chromatic aberration, bloom, barrel curvature, noise/flicker, vignette, color bleed, plus 10 named presets (trinitron, arcade, pvm, vhs, terminal, amberTerminal, consumer90s, pcMonitor, retrogaming, cinematic). MIT-licensed. Best used as the FEATURE+PRESET reference to round out the Lottes-based vanilla snippet — its preset parameterizations (mask type, curvature amount, bleed) map cleanly to uniform presets, and its named looks are good genre vocabulary (amber-terminal, trinitron). It is multi-pass framebuffer + image-only input, so depend on it as inspiration/parameter-source rather than vendoring the pipeline class.
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: MIT and feature-complete, but multi-pass + image-only and lightly maintained; lift the preset PARAMETER tables and mask-type taxonomy into the vanilla crt.glsl.js rather than vendoring. Could alternatively be a need()-guarded cdn-snippet if a true multi-pass image filter is wanted later.

backlog.name: Blur Busters crt-beam-simulator (Rejhon + Lottes)
backlog.what: MIT-licensed (2024, Mark Rejhon + Timothy Lottes) single-pass GLSL+HLSL shader that simulates a CRT electron beam temporally — soft phosphor fade + rolling scan + per-pixel MPRT brightness redistribution — to reduce motion blur on 240Hz+ OLED/LCD. Genuinely novel and reputable, but it targets the TEMPORAL/motion domain (needs high refresh, looks wrong at 60Hz, no barrel/aperture-grille aesthetic), so it is not a drop-in retro-look fullscreen pass. Worth noting as inspiration for an advanced 'authentic phosphor decay' option, not as the primary CRT aesthetic snippet.
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: MIT and well-maintained but solves a different problem (high-refresh motion-blur reduction, not the scanline/mask/curvature retro aesthetic the brief asks for). Requires 240Hz+ to look right; degrades to flicker at 60Hz. Inspiration only.

backlog.name: Luminance cross-hatch + pencil-ink editorial shader (npr-hatch.glsl.js)
backlog.what: A vanilla GLSL fragment shader that turns any image texture into a hand-drawn editorial illustration: compute per-pixel luminance, then layer modulo-based diagonal/vertical/cross stripe patterns at 3-4 luma thresholds (darker = denser hatch) for ink shading, plus an optional Sobel edge pass for ink outlines. The technique is fully documented in Maxime Heckel's 'Moebius-style post-processing' (Mar 2024) and the classic clicktorelease cross-hatch, but is trivially reimplementable from first principles (luminance + mod stripes + sobel) with zero dependency. This is the single most on-brand NPR gap: it is the 'bespoke > generic gradient' authenticity-law aesthetic in shader form, and the vault has nothing like it (halftone.glsl.js is dot-grid, watercolor.css is CSS paint, dither is SVG-noise). Best as runner.js-compatible {fragment, proceduralFragment, defaults} with palette + line-density + edge-strength uniforms.
backlog.license: Technique/public-domain math (luminance + modulo stripes + Sobel); reference posts have no stated license so reimplement clean. clicktorelease cross-hatch is three.js/geometry-lit, used only as inspiration.
backlog.note: Net-new image post-FX. Adjacent but distinct from halftone.glsl.js (dots), stepped-gradient.css .dither-* (SVG-noise), watercolor.css (CSS). Reimplement vanilla; no license dependency. The 'editorial/illustrative' look the brief explicitly wants.

backlog.name: Anisotropic Kuwahara oil-painting / painterly filter (kuwahara.glsl.js)
backlog.what: A multi-pass edge-preserving smoothing filter that gives any image a brushed oil-painting / impressionist look. Pipeline: (1) Sobel-derived structure tensor to find local edge orientation, (2) elliptical sector sampling (Papari 8-sector circular kernel) selecting the sector with lowest color variance, with polynomial weighting for performance. Maxime Heckel's Oct 2024 'On Crafting Painterly Shaders' is the definitive modern walkthrough (R3F screen-space post-process, vanilla GLSL inside). Net-new: vault has zero edge-preserving / painterly image filters. Pairs as the 'painterly editorial' counterpart to the hatch shader; together they cover the brief's hand-drawn-vs-oil comparison. Ship a basic-Kuwahara proceduralFragment for cheap demos and the anisotropic path for stills (it is expensive, so gate heavy radius behind a quality flag).
backlog.license: Algorithm from Kyprianidis et al. 'Anisotropic Kuwahara Filtering on the GPU' (GPU Pro) + Papari — public research. LYGIA's ready impl is Prosperity-licensed (non-commercial) so do NOT CDN it; reimplement from the paper/blog.
backlog.note: FLAG: LYGIA (lygia.xyz) Kuwahara is Prosperity License = non-commercial restricted, treat as inspiration only. Computationally heavy (multi-pass) so best for hero/still images, not scroll-heavy pages; gate behind reduced-motion / quality flag.

backlog.name: Painterly / post-processing-as-a-medium NPR skill reference (Maxime Heckel series)
backlog.what: Maxime Heckel's 2024-2025 shader series (Painterly Shaders, Moebius post-processing, Shades of Halftone, Post-Processing as a Creative Medium) is the single best modern decision-tree for stylized screen-space NPR: when to use edge-preserving smoothing (Kuwahara) vs luminance-threshold hatching vs ordered dithering vs sobel ink outlines, plus the structure-tensor / multi-pass plumbing they share. Worth distilling into a short shaders-adjacent skill ('npr-stylize') so the engine can pick the right editorial filter for a brief (hand-drawn-ink vs oil-painterly vs newsprint-zine) and reuse one shared luminance/sobel base. Inspiration-grade: technique source, not a dependency.
backlog.license: Editorial blog content (no code license) — use as decision/technique reference, reimplement any GLSL clean.
backlog.note: Pairs with shaders.skill.md / art-direction.skill.md. Captures the brief's core question (hatch/stipple/pencil vs Kuwahara/oil) as a routing skill so the two new shaders are chosen correctly. Inspiration, not a dep.

backlog.name: Classic single-pass Kuwahara filter (4-quadrant) as a vault shader
backlog.what: The canonical single-pass painterly filter: a (2a+1)x(2a+1) window split into 4 overlapping quadrants; output = mean of the lowest-variance quadrant -> edge-preserving 'oil paint / abstraction' look on any photo. The underlying math is public-domain (ships in ImageMagick/Blender), so it is fully reimplementable as a zero-dep GLSL frag for the existing shaders/runner.js (which already exposes imageUniforms/sampler2D). This is the highest-impact 'turn any stock or locally-generated photo into bespoke art' transform and directly serves the imagery-floor + authenticity-law goals. Net-new: vault has duotone, halftone, color-grade, watercolor (CSS background) but NO photo-to-painterly GPU filter.
backlog.license: Algorithm public-domain (Kuwahara 1976); this reference repo is unlicensed so reimplement from the public math, do not copy the file verbatim
backlog.note: SINGLE-PASS BUDGET: cost is 4*(r+1)^2 texture fetches. Radius 2 = 36 fetches (cheap, retains detail), radius 4 = 100 fetches (good painterly default, ~window-size 9), radius 5-6 (window 11-13) = 144-196 fetches is the practical single-pass ceiling for 60fps on a hero-sized quad; cap MAX_SIZE at 6 and expose `radius` as the abstraction knob. Reimplement the public-domain algorithm rather than lifting the unlicensed .frag.

backlog.name: Generalized 8-sector Kuwahara (Acerola / GarrettGunnell Post-Processing)
backlog.what: MIT-licensed reference (806 stars, pushed 2024) containing three escalating Kuwahara variants: Kuwahara.shader (basic), GeneralizedKuwahara.shader (8 circular sectors + polynomial weighting -> kills the blocky artifacts of the classic 4-quadrant version), and AnisotropicKuwahara.shader. The GENERALIZED 8-sector variant is the sweet spot for the vault: it is still single-pass (no separate structure-tensor pass needed, unlike the anisotropic one) but produces a far smoother, more convincing brushstroke look. HLSL but the algorithm ports 1:1 to GLSL for shaders/runner.js. Use as the 'high-quality' tier above the classic filter.
backlog.license: MIT (confirmed at repo level via gh api)
backlog.note: Generalized 8-sector is single-pass; budget ~ N_sectors * samples-in-sector. Keep kernel radius <= 6 and N=8 for a single-pass 60fps frame. Expose hardness/sharpness/zeta knobs. The ANISOTROPIC variant in the same repo is MULTI-PASS (Sobel structure tensor -> tensor blur -> filter) so flag it as a future tier, not the first ship.

backlog.name: Anisotropic Kuwahara painterly pipeline (Kyprianidis structure-tensor method)
backlog.what: The top-tier painterly look: structure-tensor (Sobel) -> orientation/anisotropy -> filter with an ELLIPTICAL kernel rotated to local feature direction, giving directional 'brush flowing along edges' strokes that look hand-painted rather than smeared. Maxime Heckel's 2024-25 R3F deep-dive (already cited as the key author in shaders.skill.md) walks the exact GLSL pipeline with 8 sectors and a polynomial weight approximating the Gaussian for performance. This is INSPIRATION/a future quality tier, not the first ship: it is genuinely multi-pass (3 render targets) which exceeds the single-pass vanilla-snippet budget the question asks for.
backlog.license: Blog/code open-source (author publishes code); academic method (Kyprianidis et al., GPU Pro) is freely implementable
backlog.note: MULTI-PASS (structure tensor + tensor blur + filter) so it does NOT fit the single-pass snippet budget; ship as a documented upgrade path / skill note above the single-pass classic + generalized tiers. Pairs naturally with a new entry in shaders.skill.md and art-direction.skill.md (photo->painterly as an authenticity move).

backlog.name: Acerola-style ASCII edge-detect post-pass (font-atlas + DoG + Sobel directional glyphs)
backlog.what: A reusable fullscreen GLSL pass that converts ANY source texture (image/video/webcam/canvas/3D scene rendered to a target) into ASCII: a font-atlas texture is baked from a <canvas> (draw glyph ramp ' .:-=+*#%@' into a horizontal strip -> texImage2D), the source is downsampled into NxN cells, cell luminance (0.2126R+0.7152G+0.0722B) is quantized to bucket-select a glyph column, and an Acerola-style edge layer runs Difference-of-Gaussians then a Sobel operator whose gradient ANGLE picks a directional edge char (| _ / \) overlaid on the fill. This is the recognizable 2025 terminal/dev-tool aesthetic and is exactly what the vault lacks. therealozp/asciify is the clearest reference implementation of the full DoG->Sobel->directional-glyph pipeline (the algorithm is reimplementable as vanilla GLSL; the repo itself is Go/CLI so it is a spec/inspiration, not a dep). Reimplement as a vanilla fragment shader that drops straight onto the vault's existing ShaderRunner, which already accepts sampler2D imageUniforms and renders a fullscreen quad in WebGL2.
backlog.license: Reference repo: license unspecified (treat as inspiration). Algorithm is public-domain technique (Acerola, ShaderToy lineage) -> safe to reimplement as MIT vanilla GLSL.
backlog.note: Net-new. Vault's ASCII = hard-coded three.js torus (scenes-pack.js) + text-banner tool (ascii-banner.js) + halftone shader; none is a generic image-input ASCII pass and NONE do edge detection. Host on existing shaders/runner.js (ShaderRunner) which already supports imageUniforms. The font-atlas-from-canvas + DoG/Sobel directional-glyph layer is the differentiator vs the bare luminance ramp.

backlog.name: Codrops 'Creating an ASCII Shader Using OGL' (render-to-target -> fullscreen ASCII pass)
backlog.what: Codrops tutorial (Nov 2024) + MIT-friendly repo (github.com/andrico1234/codrops-ascii-ogl) showing the canonical two-pass pattern: render a scene to an OGL RenderTarget, then sample that texture in a second fullscreen ASCII fragment shader. ~40-line shader, ~200 lines total, depends only on OGL (a lib the vault already uses for its velocity-warped drag gallery) + Lygia for noise + Tweakpane (also already in the vault). Establishes the reusable render-to-FBO -> post-pass plumbing the vault has never documented for shaders, and pairs perfectly as the 'how to wire it on OGL' companion to the edge-detect technique above. Worth adding as a cdn-snippet/skill node: 'turn any OGL/three render target into ASCII'.
backlog.license: OGL is MIT; Codrops tutorial code is permissive/educational. Lygia is permissive (Patreon-attribution); Tweakpane MIT.
backlog.note: OGL is already a known vault dependency (drag gallery) and Tweakpane is already integrated for live-controls, so this slots in with near-zero new surface. Note: this tutorial uses PROCEDURAL bitwise glyphs (no font atlas) and NO edge detection -> it is the pipeline/wiring half; combine with candidate 1's font-atlas + Sobel/DoG layer for the full effect. Behind a need('ogl') guard.

backlog.name: isladjan/ascii — MIT font-atlas ASCII EffectPass for three.js/postprocessing
backlog.what: Small MIT three.js + pmndrs/postprocessing EffectPass that bakes an ASCII character texture from an HTML canvas and samples it in the fragment shader to replace pixels by luminance. Concrete, copyable reference for the 'generate font atlas from canvas, sample by luminance bucket' step (the exact mechanism the vault's three.js stack can host), and a clean integration target for anyone already on EffectComposer. Useful as the three.js-flavored counterpart to the OGL tutorial and the vanilla ShaderRunner reimplementation.
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: Net-new mechanism (font-atlas-from-canvas + EffectPass) but ties to pmndrs/postprocessing which the vault does not currently bundle, and it has no edge detection. Best used as inspiration/reference for the font-atlas baking step rather than a new dependency; the vanilla ShaderRunner reimplementation (candidate 1) is the preferred shippable form.

backlog.name: runner.js post-FX chain: HDR-threshold mip bloom + ACES/AgX tonemap (vanilla, layered over the existing WebGL host)
backlog.what: The vault's emissive shaders (godrays, plasma, raymarch, fluid, voronoi) all run single-pass through shaders/runner.js (pure WebGL2, renders straight to the canvas, NO FBO). There is no real bloom or tonemap on that path — the only 'real' bloom (3d/postprocessing-bloom.js, UnrealBloomPass) is hard-bound to three.js EffectComposer and CANNOT layer over runner.js. Net-new addition: extend runner.js (or a sibling 'post.js') with an offscreen-FBO ping-pong chain that does a bright-pass threshold, a progressive mip downsample/upsample bloom (CoD/Froyok 13-tap down + 3x3-tent up, reimplemented from the CC-BY-documented LearnOpenGL Phys-Based Bloom guide — algorithm only, no code copied), then a tonemap pass. This single chain lifts EVERY emissive shader already in the vault at once. Worth adding because it is high-leverage, fully reimplementable, and uses the host architecture the vault already ships.
backlog.license: Algorithm reference: LearnOpenGL guest article (CC-BY-4.0 prose, code attribution required) + Froyok/Bloom (github.com/Froyok/Bloom, MIT). Reimplement from the math; do not paste. Resulting snippet is original vault code.
backlog.note: Anti-dup checked: only shaders/fluid.glsl.js uses an FBO (for its own sim, not reusable). No threshold/mip bloom on the runner.js path; postprocessing-bloom.js is three.js-only. This is the load-bearing gap.

backlog.name: dmnsgn/glsl-tone-map (AgX + ACES + Neutral + Filmic + Uncharted2 + Reinhard, raw GLSL)
backlog.what: MIT collection of 10 tone-mapping operators as standalone raw .glsl files AND ES-module strings: AGX, Neutral (Khronos PBR), ACES, Filmic, Lottes, Reinhard, Reinhard2, Uchimura, Uncharted2, Unreal. The vault has ZERO HDR tone mapping — color-grade.css is CSS filter() only (LDR, post-clamp), and shaders/lut-grade.glsl.js is a backlog LUT sampler, not an HDR display transform. This is the clean drop-in for the tonemap pass of the post-FX chain above: take the HDR scene+bloom sum and map it through AgX or ACES instead of letting WebGL hard-clip at 1.0. AgX (Sobotka) is the current Blender/2024-25 default and desaturates blown highlights instead of clipping to white — exactly what fixes over-bright godrays/plasma. Each operator is ~5-15 lines, trivially vendored or behind a need()-guard.
backlog.license: MIT (confirmed via repo LICENSE.md)
backlog.note: Complements color-grade.skill.md (which is LDR CSS filter grading) — this is the HDR display-transform tier it lacks. Cleanest single source for ACES+AgX. Alt MIT AgX source if a no-LUT analytic form is wanted: github.com/bWFuanVzYWth/AgX (MIT) and Iolite minimal-AgX writeup.

backlog.name: Dual-Kawase blur (downsample/upsample) as the bloom blur kernel
backlog.what: Dual-Kawase (Marius Bjorge / ARM, popularized by Kawase's 2003 GDC talk) is the cheapest high-quality bloom blur: a chain of downsample passes with a 4-tap-ish kernel then upsample passes, near-Gaussian quality at a fraction of the texture taps — ideal for mobile/WebGL where the vault's shaders must hold 60fps. It is the blur half of the bloom pass in candidate #1; offering it as a selectable kernel (dual-Kawase vs the mip 13/9-tap) lets the chain trade softness vs cost. The frost.kiwi 2025 writeup (Summer of Math Exposition) is the best modern explainer with a live WebGL demo; reimplement the kernel from the math. Distinct from the vault's existing blurs — there is no separable/Kawase/multipass blur in shaders/ today (only fluid.glsl.js's sim FBO).
backlog.license: frost.kiwi article: no explicit code license stated (treat as read-and-reimplement reference, not a dep). Clean MIT reference implementations exist (e.g. JujuAdams/Kawase, GameMaker) to cross-check the math.
backlog.note: Inspiration, not a dep (article code unlicensed). Pairs with candidate #1 as the blur kernel; reimplement from the documented 4-tap down / interpolated up pattern.

backlog.name: Bokeh depth-of-field pass (hexagonal/circular CoC) for the post-FX chain
backlog.what: Depth-of-field is completely absent from the vault (grep: no bokeh/dof/depth-of-field anywhere). A screen-space bokeh DoF pass — circle-of-confusion from a depth/proxy buffer driving a disc/hexagonal-tap blur — adds the cinematic focus-falloff that reads as 'rendered, not flat' on raymarch-sdf and 3d scenes. For the pure-2D runner.js shaders that lack real depth, a cheaper tilt-shift-style radial/linear-gradient CoC still gives the look. This is the lower-priority third pass of the same FBO chain (after bloom, before tonemap). Mobile-DoF references (atomirex efficient WebGL DoF) and pmndrs DepthOfFieldEffect document the gather-bokeh approach to reimplement.
backlog.license: Reimplement from documented gather-bokeh / hexagonal-DoF technique (multiple permissive references). pmndrs/postprocessing DepthOfFieldEffect is Zlib if the three.js path is wanted instead.
backlog.note: Lowest of the four — biggest payoff needs a depth buffer (only the 3d/ three.js scenes have one); for flat runner.js shaders it degrades to tilt-shift. Ship after bloom+tonemap land.

backlog.name: 2D screen-space metaball field shader (sum-of-charges + isosurface threshold + screen-space smin)
backlog.what: A single-pass fullscreen fragment shader that sums Blinn-style inverse-distance charges from N moving 2D point blobs (positions/radii passed as a uniform array), applies an isosurface smoothstep threshold for crisp-but-anti-aliased gooey edges, merges with a screen-space polynomial smooth-min, and color-bands via a cosine palette. This is the canonical flat 'lava-lamp / organic-blobs / liquid-nav' GPU background. The vault's existing lava-lamp.css and canvas-fx.js metaballs both fake it with the CSS blur(20px)+contrast(20) trick (no real field, blurry, can't do per-blob color/threshold control or pointer-charge injection); raymarch-sdf.glsl.js is 3D. A real GPU 2D field is sharper, cheaper than blur+contrast on large canvases, and tunable (threshold/k-merge/blob count). The math is public-domain (Blinn 1982) so it is a vanilla reimplement, not a dependency.
backlog.license: Public-domain technique (Blinn 1982); reference articles are tutorials, no code license needed
backlog.note: Drop-in as shaders/metaballs.glsl.js behind ShaderRunner.create() exactly like the other 17 *.glsl.js (uniform vec3 u_blobs[N] + u_threshold + u_kmerge + cosine-palette presets, u_mouse injects an extra charge). Net-new GPU 2D variant; lava-lamp.css/canvas-fx.js stay as the lightweight CSS-trick fallback. Reuse raymarch-sdf's palette naming (cosmic/gold/mint/etc) for consistency.

backlog.name: Liquid / metaball nav indicator (merging active-pill that oozes between items)
