backlog.what: A nav/tab indicator where the active highlight behaves like a viscous blob — it stretches, splits, and re-merges (metaball goo) as it travels between menu items, optionally flinging particle droplets on click. React Bits 'GooeyNav' (MIT) popularized it in 2024-2025. The vault has SVG goo FILTERS (gooey-menu.css, gooey-input.css, blob-cursor.css) and a static tab-bar active-indicator LINE, but no nav indicator that gooey-merges between targets — a distinct, high-demand interaction pattern (Awwwards 'liquid nav'). Reimplementable vanilla as a small JS component driving a CSS/SVG goo container, or upgraded to use the metaball shader above for a true screen-space merge.
backlog.license: MIT (React Bits) for the reference; reimplement vanilla (no React)
backlog.note: Vanilla reimplement: a .liquid-nav JS that animates an absolutely-positioned blob between .liquid-nav-item rects inside an SVG goo filter (FlIP-style measure + spring), with a click particle burst. Overlaps the EXISTING SVG-goo primitives but the moving/merging-indicator interaction is net-new vs the static gooey-menu and the plain tab-bar line. Lower value if scoped only to SVG goo; pairs well with candidate #1 for a screen-space version.

backlog.name: Canvas-sampled particle-text/image (vanilla WebGL instanced-points, assemble/disperse)
backlog.what: The canonical, reimplementable technique for the signature 2024-2026 hero reveal: draw text (fillText) or an image to a hidden 2D canvas, getImageData() once, threshold by luminosity to keep only opaque/bright pixels, and map each kept pixel to a target XY + sampled color. Render the cloud as instanced point-quads (InstancedBufferGeometry) or gl.POINTS; in the vertex shader lerp each particle from a scattered random spawn toward its target (assemble) and push it away along a cursor/raycast displacement texture (disperse), springing back when the pointer leaves. This is the missing piece: the vault's particles (effects/particles.js, 3d/particles-galaxy.js, vortex, GPGPU flow-field, three.quarks) are all background/abstract/flow-field and NEVER form text or an image from sampled pixels. Pairs directly with the existing scroll/reveal genres and hero blocks. Reduced-motion degradation is explicit and clean: under prefers-reduced-motion, skip the scatter-and-assemble animation entirely and render particles at their target positions immediately (or just paint the source canvas/text statically), and disable the cursor-displacement RAF loop so it's a still pointillist render with no motion.
backlog.license: MIT-style demo (Bruno Imbrizi interactive-particles repo); technique itself is unencumbered and vanilla-reimplementable. Uses three.js (already a need()-guarded vault dep) or can be pure WebGL2 with no lib.
backlog.note: Net-new: distinct from effects/particles.js (network/snow/dust), 3d/particles-galaxy.js (spiral stars), vortex.js (curl orbit), and the GPGPU flow-field snippets — none sample a canvas to form text/image targets. Ship as a need('three')-guarded cdn-snippet (e.g. 3d/particle-text.js, global ParticleText.init(el,{text|image, assembleMs, disperseRadius})) with a 2D-canvas fallback for no-WebGL and a reduced-motion static-render path. The data-extraction + assemble/disperse logic is the reusable core; keep curl/noise drift optional (reuse shaders/noise-flow.glsl.js).

backlog.name: Noise-threshold dissolve-to-particles (disperse half) — Codrops 2025
backlog.what: Complements the assemble technique with a polished DISPERSE: a uProgress uniform sweeps a Perlin-noise threshold across the surface/text; fragments below the threshold are discarded while a uEdge band glows and emits particles at the dissolving boundary. Drives the 'text/logo dissolves into particles' exit that the assemble snippet's inverse needs, giving the vault a matched reveal+conceal pair for hero/section transitions. Technique generalizes from a generic mesh to text/logo geometry. Worth adding as the disperse mode of the particle-text snippet (or a sibling), tied to scroll progress via the existing scroll genre. Reduced-motion: drop to an instant opacity crossfade with no emitted particles.
backlog.license: Codrops demo, no explicit license stated in article — treat as inspiration/reimplement (the noise-threshold + edge-emit shader is short and original-reimplementable), not a copied dep.
backlog.note: Not duplicative: vault 'morph' is geometry/clip-path/cube only; vault text effects are DOM-character reveal/scramble/wave — no noise-dissolve-to-particles. Fold into the particle-text snippet as a disperse mode rather than a separate dep.

backlog.name: particlesGL (naughtyduk)
backlog.what: A turnkey WebGL library that converts DOM elements (images, SVG, TEXT, video, 3D models) into mouse-reactive particle clouds with velocity displacement and spring-back — exactly this angle's effect, packaged. Useful as a reference implementation/inspiration for parameter design (density, displacement radius, return easing) and as proof the effect is a live 2025 trend.
backlog.license: RESTRICTED — dual license: free for personal/non-commercial only; commercial use requires a PAID license. FLAG: do not ship as a vault dep.
backlog.note: Inspiration only because of the commercial-paid license. Mine its API surface and behavior, but implement the effect from the permissive canvas-sample technique above. Also note OssamaRafique/Interactive-Image-Particles-WebGL (MIT, standalone, ~28 stars) — genuinely permissive but old and a flat 2D dot-network rather than a true assemble/disperse GPU cloud, so weaker than reimplementing the Codrops pattern.

backlog.name: gkjohnson three.js LUTPass — trilinear 3D-LUT post pass (.cube/.3dl loaders + manual trilinear GLSL)
backlog.what: The canonical, permissive reference implementation of a single full-screen 3D-LUT color-grade pass: LUTCubeLoader/LUT3dlLoader parse Adobe .cube and Autodesk .3dl text into a DataTexture3D, and LUTPass samples it with manual trilinear interpolation (Garret Johnson's algorithm, MIT). Worth adding because the vault's color-grade.skill.md ALREADY lists this exact tier as backlog (`shaders/lut-grade.glsl.js` over the existing `shaders/runner.js` fullscreen-quad host) but has never shipped it — the host exists, the shader does not. Reimplement the loader+trilinear sampler as a vanilla snippet (no three.js dep) so any shader or image gets one cohesive cinematic grade in a single post pass. This is the GPU sibling of the CSS-filter `.grade-*` looks: it does true 3D color remaps CSS filters cannot express.
backlog.license: MIT (three.js); trilinear algo MIT (Garret Johnson)
backlog.note: Reimplement vanilla (drop three.js) per the skill's own 'hand-roll the sampler, no Prosperity/LYGIA includes' guidance. WebGL1 needs the Hald-strip fallback; WebGL2 can use a real sampler3D. Pure shader+loader code is MIT — only the LUT *data* has licensing constraints (see palette item).

backlog.name: mattdesl/glsl-lut — 2D-strip lookup-table color transform (glslify module)
backlog.what: A tiny MIT GLSL module: `transform(vec4 color, sampler2D lookup)` samples a 512x512 PNG strip LUT (the classic Instagram-filter approach). Worth noting as the lightweight fallback path for the WebGL LUT tier — it needs no 3D textures (WebGL1-safe everywhere) and the core is only a few lines, trivially inlined into a vanilla snippet. Inferior to the trilinear 3D approach (it's effectively bilinear within 2D tile slices, so banding in smooth gradients), so it's the 'cheap/compat' branch rather than the primary one.
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: Use as the WebGL1 compat fallback inside the same lut-grade snippet, or as inspiration. Bilinear-on-2D-strip bands on smooth ramps vs gkjohnson's trilinear — prefer the 3D-texture path as default, this as graceful degradation.

backlog.name: Named in-house CC0 HaldCLUT set (bake-your-own film-look palette)
backlog.what: A 'palette' analog for the GPU tier: a small set of named, self-baked CC0 HaldCLUT PNGs (e.g. teal-orange, bleach-bypass, kodak-portra, fuji-velvia, noir) that the LUT pass loads — extending the existing named `.grade-*` CSS looks into true 3D color remaps. Worth adding because there is STILL no cleanly bundleable cinematic .cube pack: FilterGrade/RocketStock/IWLTBAP forbid redistribution, Pat David / NatronGitHub HaldCLUTs are CC-BY-SA (attribution + ShareAlike), and even MIT-labeled GitHub collections like YahiaAngelo/Film-Luts carry an explicit copyright caveat on the LUT *data*. So the only attribution-free, vault-safe path (exactly as color-grade.skill.md prescribes) is to BAKE your own neutral-identity HaldCLUT, push CSS-filter/ffmpeg transforms through it, and ship those as CC0 — owing nobody attribution.
backlog.license: CC0 (your own bake). External packs FLAGGED: redistribution-forbidden (FilterGrade/RocketStock/IWLTBAP), CC-BY-SA (Pat David/Natron), MIT-with-data-caveat (YahiaAngelo).
backlog.note: Do NOT bundle any downloaded cinematic pack. Bake from an identity HaldCLUT (level-8, 512x512 PNG) run through the vault's own CSS-filter grade stacks or `ffmpeg -vf` — this also keeps the GPU looks byte-for-byte aligned with the CSS `.grade-*` looks for cross-tier coherence.

backlog.name: WEBGL_lose_context survival probe (context-loss recovery gate)
backlog.what: A save-gate diagnostic that, after a shader/3D snippet renders, grabs the live GL context via the WEBGL_lose_context extension, fires loseContext() then restoreContext(), waits for the webglcontextrestored event, and re-runs the existing pixel-variance 'did it draw' check. PASS = snippet redraws after a forced context loss; FAIL = stays black / throws / never re-acquires resources. This is exactly the failure that takes real shader pages down in production (driver hiccup, tab backgrounding, GPU reset) and the vault currently has no check for it — render-smoke proves first-frame draw only, never recovery. Naturally extends the existing PRESERVE_WEBGL init-script hook and the measure() reader already in render-smoke.mjs; degrades gracefully (skip if the extension is unavailable, which matches the harness's KNOWN_ISSUES pattern).
backlog.license: Web standard (Khronos WEBGL_lose_context, no dep)
backlog.note: Ties directly to the webgl-drawverify memory: reuses page.screenshot/pixel-variance as truth, just adds a lose/restore cycle around it. No competing design tool gates on context-loss survival — strongest moat add.

backlog.name: EXT_disjoint_timer_query_webgl2 GPU frame-time budget probe
backlog.what: A frametime-budget diagnostic: wrap one render frame of a shader snippet in beginQuery(TIME_ELAPSED_EXT)/endQuery, then read QUERY_RESULT (nanoseconds) on a later frame to get the actual GPU cost of the draw, checking the GPU_DISJOINT_EXT flag to discard throttled samples. Gate flags snippets whose GPU frame time blows a budget (e.g. >8-12ms on the harness GPU) — i.e. heavy raymarched/SDF/fluid shaders that will jank on real hardware. Pair with a CPU-side requestAnimationFrame delta sampler (performance.now() across N frames, count frames over the ~16ms budget) for a dropped-frame/jank count when the GPU timer isn't available. The vault ships raymarched/SDF/fluid/godray shaders with NO performance floor today; this is the 'frametime budget' the WHY asks for. Figma's MIT webgl-profiler is a compact reference implementation to mirror.
backlog.license: Web standard extension; reference impl github.com/figma/webgl-profiler is MIT
backlog.note: GPU timer is desktop-Chrome-only and disjoint-flag-noisy — must degrade to the rAF-delta CPU jank counter when QUERY_RESULT is unavailable, matching render-smoke's HARNESS_SOFTWARE fallback discipline.

backlog.name: Black-frame / NaN-output correctness probe (frame-content sanity gate)
backlog.what: A correctness diagnostic that upgrades render-smoke's binary 'drew = variance>5' check into a shader-aware verdict: after settle, readback the canvas and flag (a) all-black/all-transparent output (the #1 shader failure: a NaN uniform like time/resolution collapses every fragment to black, or a missing texture), and (b) degenerate/uniform output where >99% of pixels are one color despite the snippet claiming an animated effect. Because NaN can't be read back from a composited 8-bit canvas directly, detect it by its signature — a frame that is fully black/uniform when the snippet is a known-animated shader family, or pixels stuck at the checkerboard sentinels if a debug shader is used. This catches the exact 'all-black/NaN output' the WHY names, which the current variance check can miss for a shader that produces a flat non-black color. Reuses the existing offscreen-canvas measure() reader in render-smoke.mjs — pure addition, no new dep.
backlog.license: N/A (vanilla pixel readback, no dep)
backlog.note: Partial overlap with render-smoke's variance check (it already rejects pure-black/single-color), so this is an upgrade of that assertion (per-family expected-animation + uniform-color rejection), not a wholly separate gate. Scope as 'sharpen the existing canvas-drew verdict for shaders/' to avoid duplication.

backlog.name: @supports + Firefox-1ms graceful-degradation harness for scroll-driven CSS
backlog.what: The vault's animations/scroll-animations.css ALREADY uses native animation-timeline: scroll()/view() with animation-range entry/cover for reveal/parallax/through effects — BUT it has NO @supports guard. Its reveal keyframes start at opacity:0 (from), and its only documented fallback is a SEPARATE JS file ('Use scroll-animations.js as the fallback'). Shipped CSS-only (the CDN-safe case), every reveal is STUCK INVISIBLE in any engine without animation-timeline, and it omits the Firefox quirk where a zero/auto animation-duration silently no-ops the timeline. Net-new, addable fix is a pure-CSS pattern: (a) default un-wrapped state = final VISIBLE state (opacity:1, no transform); (b) wrap the animating rules in @supports (animation-timeline: view()) AND @media (prefers-reduced-motion: no-preference); (c) give every scroll/view animation a non-zero animation-duration: 1ms so Firefox applies it. This makes the existing snippet correct stand-alone (no JS) and degrade to static-visible. Best shipped as BOTH a one-line hardening of scroll-animations.css and a coherence/verification rule that flags any animation-timeline rule lacking an @supports guard or a non-zero duration.
backlog.license: n/a (CSS pattern / spec idiom)
backlog.note: The reveal PRIMITIVES are already in animations/scroll-animations.css; the GAP is the missing @supports/1ms-duration degradation harness + a gate rule. Verify=the moat: a check that fails build if a view()/scroll() rule can leave content opacity:0 in non-supporting browsers.

backlog.name: Named scroll/view timelines + timeline-scope (cross-element zero-JS scrub)
backlog.what: view-timeline-name / scroll-timeline-name plus timeline-scope let a DIFFERENT element scrub against another element's scroll progress — e.g. a reading-progress bar driven by an article's scroll, a sticky caption scrubbed by a sibling image, or a nav indicator tracking section visibility, all with ZERO JS on the compositor thread. The vault's existing scroll-animations.css is entirely SELF-driven (each element animates against its own view()); grep confirms 0 files use view-timeline-name or timeline-scope. This is the genuinely net-new primitive set: timeline-scope: --t on a common ancestor hoists a named timeline so siblings can reference animation-timeline: --t. It pure-CSS replaces JS scrub patterns the vault does by hand (scroll/scroll-scrub.js data-scrub-from/to, components/timeline-vertical-progress.js, animations/scroll-animations.js .scrub()/.progress()). Ships as a small vanilla .css snippet set (named-progress bar, sticky-scrub caption, sibling-driven reveal) carrying the same @supports/1ms/reduced-motion harness.
backlog.license: n/a (CSS spec feature, MDN/CSSWG)
backlog.note: Distinct from the self-driven reveals already in scroll-animations.css and from JS scrubbers (scroll-scrub.js / timeline-vertical-progress.js). timeline-scope is the load-bearing piece — without it named timelines are descendant-only.

backlog.name: animation-range exit/contain pin-and-release primitives (reveal-hold-exit, sticky pin via range)
backlog.what: The vault's scroll-animations.css uses only the entry and cover ranges. The net-new primitives are exit and contain ranges: 'reveal on entry, HOLD while in view, animate out on exit' (animation-range: entry 0% exit 100% with a 4-stop keyframe), and pure-CSS sticky-pin scenes driven by contain (element scrubs only while fully containing the scrollport). These pure-CSS replace JS pin/scrub logic in scroll/scroll-pin.js, scroll/horizontal-pin.js, and scroll/sticky-scroll-reveal.js for the simple cases, off-main-thread. Small vanilla .css addition to the existing reveal family, same @supports/reduced-motion/1ms harness.
backlog.license: n/a (CSS spec feature)
backlog.note: entry+cover ranges already used; exit/contain ranges and the reveal-hold-exit pattern are not. Lower value than named timelines because partly overlaps the JS pin snippets for complex cases (named timelines / JS still needed for true horizontal-pin).

backlog.name: timeline-scope + named view-timeline CSS-only scroll-spy (active-nav / TOC / per-section progress)
backlog.what: The vault already has the plain scroll() progress bar (animations/scroll-animations.css .scroll-progress-bar, scaleX 0->1 on scroll(root)) and the scroll() shrink/solidify header (.hdr-auto), so a basic reading-progress bar is NOT net-new. What IS net-new: hoisting a section's `view-timeline-name` to a common ancestor with `timeline-scope`, so a DISTANT element (a nav link's underline, a TOC dot, a per-section progress ring) animates from that section's view progress with zero IntersectionObserver. Today the vault only does this in JS (headers-pro.js scroll-spy). Pattern: section { view-timeline-name: --s1 } ; body { timeline-scope: --s1 } ; nav a.is-link-1 { animation: light-on linear both; animation-timeline: --s1; animation-range: cover; }. Worth adding as a vanilla-snippet wired behind @supports (timeline-scope: none) so it degrades to the existing headers-pro.js scroll-spy.
backlog.license: N/A (CSS feature / standard)
backlog.note: NOT Baseline: Firefox lacks view()/timeline-scope. Must ship behind @supports + keep headers-pro.js / reading-aids.js as fallback. The bare scroll() progress bar itself is duplicative (scroll-animations.css).

backlog.name: section-driven per-block progress rings/labels via view() + animation-range (vs scroll-pin.js --p)
backlog.what: scroll-pin.js exposes per-section progress by writing --p in a rAF onProgress callback (main-thread). The native equivalent gives each section its own view() timeline and an animation-range window (e.g. `entry 0% cover 50%`) so a ring/label/dot fills as that specific section scrolls through the viewport, fully off-main-thread and compositor-driven. The vault's scroll-animations.css uses view() only for one-shot reveals (fade/slide/scale-in) and contain-through effects; it does NOT use view() to drive a measurable per-section progress UI element. A small `.sec-progress` snippet (ring stroke-dashoffset or a label width keyed to a section's view-timeline) is a clean drop-in for article/landing genres that currently reach for scroll-pin.js just to read a number. Degrades to scroll-pin.js where view() is unsupported (Firefox).
backlog.license: N/A (CSS feature / standard)
backlog.note: Adjacent to scroll-animations.css (which uses view() only for reveals, not a progress readout) and scroll-pin.js (JS --p). Net-new as a progress-UI use of view(); flag Firefox gap, ship behind @supports.

backlog.name: CSS animation-trigger + timeline-trigger (scroll-triggered animations, Chrome 145/146)
backlog.what: A 2026-new native CSS primitive that fires a time-based animation when an element crosses a scroll offset, instead of scrubbing progress to scroll position the way animation-timeline/scroll-timeline (already in the vault's scroll-animations.css) does. Two layers: timeline-trigger: --t view() <activation-range> / <exit-range> defines WHEN the trigger arms/disarms; animation-trigger: --t <enter-action> <exit-action> binds an animation to it. The action keywords are exactly the behaviors the vault currently fakes with IntersectionObserver: play-once = play at natural speed on enter and never replay (the 'once' flag); play-forwards = replay every re-entry; play-forwards play-backwards = alternate (forward on enter, reverse on exit); plus pause/reset/replay/none for fine control. exit-range lets you decouple the disarm threshold from the arm threshold (e.g. arm at entry 100%, reverse only after fully exiting). Worth adding because the vault has ZERO coverage and its current 'play-once-on-enter-and-stay' path requires aos-lite.js (IntersectionObserver + once:true + io.unobserve) — this does it in pure declarative CSS with no JS, no scrub-jank, and no reverse-on-scroll-up artifact.
backlog.license: n/a (web platform standard, CSS Animations Level 2 / Animation Triggers Level 1 spec)
backlog.note: Package as a zero-dep .css (e.g. scroll/animation-trigger.css) with 3 ready classes — .trig-once / .trig-replay / .trig-alternate — each driven by timeline-trigger + animation-trigger + trigger-scope. Gate the whole block behind @supports (animation-trigger: --x play-forwards) {...}; in the @supports-not branch, fall back to the EXISTING aos-lite.js IntersectionObserver classes (data-aos + data-aos-once) so behavior is identical on Firefox/Safari and there is no double-fire. Minor source disagreement on ship version: Chrome blog/Bram.us say 145, utilitybend/CSS-Tricks say 146 — note 'Chrome 145+' and feature-detect rather than version-sniff. Anti-dup verified: grep for animation-trigger/timeline-trigger across the vault returns nothing; scroll-animations.css only uses animation-timeline (scrubbing), which is a different primitive.

backlog.name: CSS scroll-state() container queries (stuck / snapped / scrollable / scrolled)
backlog.what: A pure-CSS way to restyle descendants based on a container's live scroll state. You set `container-type: scroll-state` on the element to track, then gate styles with `@container scroll-state(...)`: `stuck: top/bottom` (is this position:sticky element actually pinned right now), `snapped: x/y/inline/block` (is this scroll-snap item the active one), `scrollable: top/right/bottom/left` (can it still scroll that way — perfect for fade/arrow hints), and `scrolled: top/bottom/block-end/none` (direction of the most recent scroll — enables hide-on-scroll-down headers). This directly replaces the JS the vault leans on today: the is-stuck class toggling behind sticky-patterns/navbars/header-pack and the IntersectionObserver in scroll-snap-scenes.js — with zero JS, zero observers, and no scroll listeners. The strongly net-new snippet is a pair: (1) a sticky header that gains shadow/compact/color-shift only when `scroll-state(stuck: top)`, (2) a snap card that reveals its hidden figcaption only when `scroll-state(snapped: x)`, plus a scroll-hint arrow driven by `scrollable:` and a hide-on-scroll-down nav driven by `scrolled: bottom`. Fallback is trivial and clean: wrap the whole block in `@supports (container-type: scroll-state){}` so unsupported browsers simply keep the static base style — the exact support-guard pattern the question asks about, and a natural fit for the vault's need()-guard / graceful-degradation house style.
backlog.license: N/A (native CSS feature, public-domain spec)
backlog.note: Net-new: vault has classic sticky-patterns.css, scroll-snap.css, container-queries.css (SIZE queries only), and JS-based scroll-snap-scenes.js/scroll-pin.js — none use scroll-state. Ship behind @supports (container-type: scroll-state); base styles are the fallback. Reduced-motion: wrap reveal/transform variants in prefers-reduced-motion: no-preference per the Chrome demo.

backlog.name: Native CSS Carousels API (::scroll-button() + ::scroll-marker / ::scroll-marker-group, :target-current)
backlog.what: A JS-free carousel built entirely from CSS pseudo-elements on a scroll-snap container: `::scroll-button(left/right)` generates real, focusable prev/next buttons that auto-disable at the ends; `::scroll-marker-group` + per-item `::scroll-marker` generate accessible dot navigation; `:target-current` styles the active dot. The browser wires up click-to-scroll, keyboard/focus, ARIA, and disabled-state for free — Chrome's a11y team notes it's 'very difficult to build a more accessible carousel than this.' This is adjacent to scroll-state and surfaced during the same investigation. The vault's carousel-pack.css and DragGallery are scroll-snap + JS; nothing uses these pseudo-elements. A net-new vanilla snippet: a scroll-snap strip with markup-free arrows + dots, behind an @supports guard, degrading to the existing CSS scroll-snap strip (which the vault already ships) when unsupported.
backlog.license: N/A (native CSS feature, public-domain spec)
backlog.note: Distinct from scroll-state, but same 'no-JS scroll UI' family. Vault carousel-pack.css/carousel work is scroll-snap + JS; no pseudo-element carousel exists. Fallback: @supports (selector(::scroll-marker)) — degrade to plain CSS scroll-snap (already in scroll-snap.css). Slightly lower value than scroll-state since it overlaps an existing carousel pack, whereas scroll-state replaces JS the vault has no CSS alternative for.

backlog.name: Cross-document (MPA) View Transitions plumbing — pageswap/pagereveal + @view-transition { navigation: auto }
backlog.what: A zero-dep JS module that does the real MPA View-Transition dance the vault is missing: it registers parser-blocking pageswap (outgoing) and pagereveal (incoming) listeners, guards on e.viewTransition, and coordinates a transition across two physically separate same-origin documents. Pairs with the @view-transition { navigation: auto } at-rule (which the skill already names but treats as 'No JS'). The vault's view-transitions.js / named-transitions.js / transitions-pro.js are ALL same-document — they wrap document.startViewTransition() with a callback or an SPA DOM-swap, and explicitly do real navigation only via transitions-pro's bindLinks overlay hack (no native snapshot across the load). grep for pageswap/pagereveal/navigation.activation/types returns nothing. Worth adding because cross-document VT hit cross-browser interop in Q1 2026 (Chrome 126+, Safari 18.2+, Firefox in progress / behind flag) and is THE native way to get app-like page-to-page motion on plain multi-page sites with no router. Degrades to a normal navigation when unsupported.
backlog.license: Public domain / W3C spec + Chrome-team example patterns (CC-BY / reimplementable, no packaged dep)
backlog.note: NET-NEW: vault VT is 100% same-document (callback/SPA-swap). Implement as transitions/mpa-view-transitions.js. Non-negotiables for the snippet: register listeners in a PARSER-BLOCKING <head> script (not async/defer/module) or pagereveal misses the first paint; always guard `if (!e.viewTransition) return`; same-origin only (cross-origin + programmatic location.href are excluded by design); the new page must reach renderable state within ~4s or the transition silently dies — wire viewTransition.finished.catch for diagnostics.

backlog.name: Types-based directional cross-document transitions (forward/back via navigation.activation + :active-view-transition-type())
backlog.what: The directional layer on top of the MPA plumbing: in pageswap/pagereveal, compute a direction by comparing navigation.activation.from.url vs navigation.activation.entry.url (and navigation type push/traverse/replace), then e.viewTransition.types.add('slide-forward' | 'slide-back'), and branch the animation in CSS with the :active-view-transition-type(slide-back) pseudo-class. This is what makes back/forward feel correct (content slides the opposite way) and lets list-to-detail differ from detail-to-list — impossible with the vault's current data-vt-type attribute hack, which only exists for same-document ViewTransitions.run({type}). ViewTransition.types is a Set-like object (add/delete/clear); can also be declared statically via the at-rule's `types:` descriptor. Distinct, high-value capability that turns a generic cross-fade into intent-aware motion.
backlog.license: Public domain / W3C spec (CSS View Transitions L2); MDN docs CC-BY-SA, code reimplementable
backlog.note: NET-NEW vs vault's data-vt-type (which is same-document-only and string-tag, not navigation-direction-aware). Best shipped as the CSS half (transitions/mpa-view-transitions.css with ::view-transition-old/new(root) keyframes keyed by :active-view-transition-type(...)) bundled with the JS snippet above. Remember to NOT manually clear types — the transition object is per-navigation, so types reset automatically; but dynamically-assigned view-transition-names DO need cleanup after viewTransition.finished or they leak into the next navigation.

backlog.name: Just-in-time per-navigation view-transition-name assignment (setTemporaryViewTransitionNames pattern)
backlog.what: A small helper for shared-element morphs across real page loads: instead of decorating every card/hero with a static view-transition-name (which throws if two share a name at snapshot time, and bloats CSS), you read the target URL from e.activation.entry.url in pageswap (and navigation.activation.entry.url in pagereveal), find ONLY the element that participates in this specific navigation (e.g. the clicked card -> the detail hero), assign a unique name just for the snapshot, and auto-remove it when viewTransition.finished resolves. This is the cross-document analog of what named-transitions.js does for same-document swaps — but named-transitions.js mutates one live DOM tree, whereas this coordinates two separate documents that never share JS state, so the only channel is the pageswap/pagereveal activation entries. Enables thumbnail-to-hero morphs on a plain MPA gallery/blog.
backlog.license: Public domain / Chrome-team example pattern (Apache-2.0 docs, reimplementable)
backlog.note: Complements rather than dups named-transitions.js. Ship the setTemporaryViewTransitionNames(pairs, finishedPromise) helper inside the same mpa-view-transitions.js module. Caveat to encode: pair with ::view-transition-old/new(name){object-fit:cover} to stop bitmap snapshot distortion on differently-sized images, and gate the whole morph under prefers-reduced-motion (fall back to a plain cross-fade or instant swap).

backlog.name: Directional View Transitions via native types + :active-view-transition-type()
backlog.what: A vanilla snippet that upgrades the vault's VT wrapper from its homegrown data-vt-type attribute to the NATIVE types mechanism: document.startViewTransition({ update, types: ['slide', direction] }) plus CSS keyed off html:active-view-transition-type(forwards|backwards). A determineDirection() helper reads the Navigation API (compare navigation.currentEntry.index against the destination entry, or e.activation in pageswap/pagereveal) so back-navigation automatically reverses the slide (old slides right / new from left) without writing a second transition. The vault has NO forward/back awareness today — its run() just sets a custom attribute and has no direction concept. This is the #1 missing transition primitive. Reduced-motion + unsupported-browser fallback fold into the existing wrapper.
backlog.license: Public-domain technique (native CSS/JS API)
backlog.note: Net-new vs transitions/view-transitions.js (which uses a non-native data-vt-type attribute and has zero directional logic). Ship as an additive upgrade: add opts.types (array) + opts.direction:'auto' to ViewTransitions.run, emit the real types and a paired named-transitions.css block with html:active-view-transition-type(...) selectors. Update view-transitions.skill.md to prefer native types over data-vt-type.

backlog.name: VT-name save-gate diagnostic (duplicate / unset / silent-skip detector)
backlog.what: A headless-Chrome verification capability that catches the silent morph-killer no competitor detects. Per spec, two elements sharing a view-transition-name at capture time makes the browser SKIP the transition with an InvalidStateError DOMException (rejecting transition.ready) WHILE the update callback still runs — so the DOM updates correctly and the morph just never plays, leaving no obvious devtools signal. The gate would: (1) static-scan composed HTML/CSS for >1 element resolving to the same view-transition-name (incl. names set in <style>/inline) and flag DUPLICATE-VT-NAME; (2) flag a data-vt-morph / NamedTransitions pairing where the destination name is missing → MISSING-VT-NAME (no-op morph); (3) at runtime, instrument startViewTransition to catch transition.ready rejection and transition.updateCallbackDone errors, surfacing VT-SKIPPED with the DOMException name. This is a pure save-gate moat: a 'your morph silently died' tell.
backlog.license: Public-domain technique (spec-defined behavior)
backlog.note: checkCoherence in mcp-server/server.js is a string-scan with no VT awareness; view-transitions.skill.md WARNS that duplicate names throw but ships no detector. Net-new gate. Exact signal to assert: transition.ready rejects with InvalidStateError + DOM still mutated = silent skip. Pairs naturally with candidate #1 (directional VT) as a save-gate it can verify.

backlog.name: @vtbag/utensil-drawer (mayStartViewTransition, escapeViewTransitionName, setViewTransitionNames)
backlog.what: Tiny framework-agnostic vanilla helpers for VT plumbing: mayStartViewTransition() (chains closely-timed transitions into one, scoped-transition support, graceful fallback), escapeViewTransitionName() (lets names use chars beyond A-Za-Z0-9-_ via CSS.escape-style encoding — relevant because the vault's NamedTransitions generates names from selectors), and declarative name assignment with :in-viewport support. ISC-licensed (permissive), npm @vtbag/utensil-drawer v1.2.16, repo active (pushed 2026-04).
backlog.license: ISC (permissive)
backlog.note: Recommend as INSPIRATION, not a dep — low adoption / bus-factor and the vault is vanilla-first. Worth lifting the escapeViewTransitionName idea and the chain-into-one-transition pattern into NamedTransitions rather than adding a CDN dependency. The vtbag.dev 'fails and fixes' page is also the best reference source for the verifier in candidate #2.

backlog.name: scroll-marker-group + ::scroll-marker + ::scroll-button() (CSS Carousel API)
backlog.what: Native CSS carousel pseudo-elements that generate pagination dots (scroll-marker-group: after; > li::scroll-marker { content:' ' }) and prev/next arrows (::scroll-button(left/right)) with ZERO markup and ZERO JS. The active dot is styled purely in CSS via ::scroll-marker:target-current, and markers behave like focusable anchor links (keyboard + a11y for free). Worth adding because the vault's only auto-highlighting active-dot path is JS IntersectionObserver in scroll-snap-scenes.js — this is the net-new declarative version that makes a snap carousel feel native with far less JS. Ship behind @supports (scroll-marker-group: after) so it progressively enhances the vault's existing carousel-pack.css / scroll-snap.css markup and falls back to the current JS dots.
backlog.license: n/a (web platform feature)
backlog.note: Net-new: vault has scroll-snap-stop:always (scroll/scroll-snap-scenes.css) and JS-driven dots (scroll/scroll-snap-scenes.js) but NO scroll-marker/scroll-button CSS-generated nav. Not Baseline — must wrap in @supports and degrade to the existing JS pagination.

backlog.name: scroll-state(snapped) container queries
backlog.what: container-type: scroll-state lets children react to whether their item is currently snapped, via @container scroll-state(snapped: x | y | inline | block | none). Enables auto-dimming/auto-scaling non-active slides (@container not scroll-state(snapped: x) { opacity: .25 }) and highlighting the active item with zero JS and zero scroll listeners — purely the compositor. Also exposes scroll-state(stuck: top/bottom) (detect a position:sticky element that has actually stuck) and scroll-state(scrollable: top/right/...) (show scroll-affordance shadows only when there is more to scroll). Net-new refinement layer over the vault's basic scroll-snap.css and a more performant alternative to IntersectionObserver threshold-guessing.
backlog.license: n/a (web platform feature)
backlog.note: Net-new: grep of INDEX.md confirms no scroll-state/snapped()/stuck/scrollable usage. Chromium-only — ship behind @supports (container-type: scroll-state) as a pure-enhancement skin so non-Chromium just sees the static snap carousel. The stuck/scrollable variants are independently useful (sticky-header state, scroll-shadow affordances).

backlog.name: Scroll Snap Events (scrollsnapchange / scrollsnapchanging, SnapEvent.snapTargetBlock/Inline)
backlog.what: Native JS events that fire on the scroll container when the browser commits a new snap target (scrollsnapchange) or previews a pending one mid-gesture (scrollsnapchanging). SnapEvent exposes snapTargetBlock / snapTargetInline — direct element references to the snapped child. This replaces the vault's IntersectionObserver + threshold:0.55 heuristic in scroll-snap-scenes.js with a precise, event-driven active-scene/active-dot signal (no ratio tuning, no scroll throttling, fires exactly once per snap, right before scrollend). A small zero-dep enhancement to the existing ScrollSnapScenes module: prefer the native event when present, keep IO as fallback.
backlog.license: n/a (web platform feature)
backlog.note: Net-new: vault uses IntersectionObserver for active-state (scroll/scroll-snap-scenes.js); these native snap events are not referenced anywhere. Best as a feature-detected upgrade inside the existing ScrollSnapScenes.init rather than a new file. Lower value because it overlaps the IO path it improves.

backlog.name: Pinned-graphic scrollytelling COMPOSE-GENRE (explainer / data-journalism archetype)
backlog.what: A net-new page archetype for the engine: a sticky/pinned graphic column + a stepper text column whose steps advance discrete scene state as they scroll past (Pudding/NYT/Reuters explainer pattern). The vault has the *ingredients* (scroll-pin.js, sticky-scroll-reveal.js, scroll-animations.css view() reveals, dataviz.skill, charts) but no GENRE_SEQUENCES entry that wires them into a pinned-graphic+stepper editorial page. Section sequence would be: intro/hero -> pinned-scene (sticky media + N steps) -> optional second scene -> method/credits -> footer. This is the archetype the engine literally cannot compose today (confirmed: GENRE_SEQUENCES = saas/agency/portfolio/ecommerce/restaurant/startup/blog/landing only). Built mostly on native position:sticky + a small step-state driver, degrading to static stacked figures.
backlog.license: N/A (own archetype; pattern is public-domain editorial convention)
backlog.note: Reuses existing scroll-pin.js/sticky-scroll-reveal.js/dataviz as slot fillers; the net-new part is the genre sequence + a 'scene' shell (sticky graphic + stepper) in structure.css. High value: opens an entire publishing/editorial category. Should ship with the reduced-motion + non-supporting-browser static fallback baked into the shell so it passes the verify gate.

backlog.name: Native sticky-pinned scene: position:sticky + animation-timeline:view() ranges + @property step-state (vanilla snippet)
backlog.what: A zero-JS reimplementation of the vault's JS sticky-scroll-reveal: a position:sticky graphic frame plus per-step view-timeline names, where each step's view() range drives keyframes (clip-path/opacity wipes) AND interpolates @property-registered numeric custom properties (e.g. --step-progress, --active 0..N) that the pinned graphic reads to morph state. This is distinct from animations/scroll-animations.css, which only does single-shot enter/exit reveals + parallax — it has NO pinned-while-stepping state machine and no @property interpolation. Pairs with a @supports(animation-timeline:view()) guard that degrades to a stacked static layout (and the vault's existing scroll-pin.js as the JS fallback for Firefox). Runs off-main-thread, GPU-composited.
backlog.license: Open web standard (CSS Scroll-Driven Animations L1, W3C) — freely implementable
backlog.note: The @property-typed custom-property interpolation (a CSS 'state machine' a pinned graphic reads) is the genuinely net-new primitive here and the engine of the genre above. Vanilla, zero-dep, CDN-free. Must ship reduced-motion + @supports fallback to clear the verify gate.

backlog.name: CSS @container scroll-state() queries (stuck / snapped / scrollable)
backlog.what: A 2025-2026 CSS primitive that lets a position:sticky element know, in pure CSS, WHEN it is pinned (@container scroll-state(stuck: top)) and restyle itself — no JS scroll listener, no IntersectionObserver. For scrollytelling this is the missing piece that makes the pinned scene self-aware: the graphic can switch to a 'pinned' treatment (full-bleed, chrome on, progress rail visible) only while stuck, and revert when the scene scrolls off. Also covers snapped: for scroll-snap scenes and scrollable: for edge shadows. Completely absent from the vault (grep of INDEX/asset-libraries/creative-arsenal returns nothing). Reimplementable as a tiny vanilla CSS snippet behind a @supports guard.
backlog.license: Open web standard (CSS Conditional Rules / container queries, W3C)
backlog.note: Net-new and Chromium-only today, so it must be additive/enhancement-only behind @supports(container-type: scroll-state) with the existing JS .is-pinned class path (headers-pro already uses .is-pinned via JS) as the cross-browser baseline. Strong fit as a polish layer on the pinned-scene shell and on sticky nav/headers.

backlog.name: Scrollama 3.x
backlog.what: The canonical vanilla-JS scrollytelling step engine (IntersectionObserver-based) behind Pudding/NYT-style explainers. v3.2.0, MIT, zero dependencies, CDN-ready (unpkg ESM + UMD). Provides exactly the three callbacks the genre needs — onStepEnter/onStepExit (discrete state per step), onStepProgress (0..1 within a step), and the opinionated sticky-graphic helper — and as of v2+ it explicitly DEFERS pinning to native position:sticky, so it layers cleanly on top of the native CSS scene as the robust cross-browser fallback driver (it works in Firefox/Safari where view() ranges don't). The vault's sticky-scroll-reveal.js is a bespoke viewport-center image-swapper; scrollama is the battle-tested, IO-based, progress-capable upgrade. No reference to it anywhere in the vault.
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: Best as a need()-guarded CDN snippet that the pinned-graphic genre falls back to when @supports(animation-timeline:view()) is false (Firefox) — gives the genre full cross-browser reach without abandoning the native-first path. MIT + vanilla + IO means it degrades gracefully (no IO polyfill shipped since v1.4, but IO is universally supported in 2026). Note: it does NOT do layout/charts — pair with the vault's existing dataviz/charts snippets for the graphic itself.

backlog.name: Pure-CSS horizontal pinned section (view-timeline + translateX, zero JS)
backlog.what: A zero-JS horizontal scroll / pinned gallery: a tall wrapper (e.g. height:500vh) declares a named view-timeline (view-timeline-name:--pin; view-timeline-axis:block); a position:sticky;top:0 child holds an inner track that animates translateX(calc(-100% + 100vw)) with animation-timeline:--pin and animation-range:contain 0% contain 100%. Vertical page scroll drives horizontal motion entirely on the compositor — no wheel listeners, no rAF, no lerp. Worth adding because it is the accessible, native-momentum replacement for the vault's wheel-hijacking horizontal-scroll.js: real trackpad/keyboard/scrollbar behavior, 60fps off the main thread, and it degrades gracefully (under @supports-not the track just renders inline/scroll-snap). Net-new: the vault's scroll-animations.css already uses scroll()/view() but ONLY for vertical reveals/parallax/progress-bar — there is no horizontal-pin variant anywhere; horizontal-pin.js is the closest but still JS-driven.
backlog.license: Public domain (W3C spec feature; demo code by Bramus/Chrome team, unlicensed sample)
backlog.note: Directly retires a known-janky pattern (layout/horizontal-scroll.js wheel hijack). Ship behind @supports(animation-timeline:view()) with the existing horizontal-pin.js / scroll-snap as the non-supporting fallback so coherence/overflow gates stay green in headless Chrome.

backlog.name: Native CSS carousel pseudo-elements (::scroll-marker / ::scroll-button / ::scroll-marker-group)
backlog.what: Chrome/Edge 135+ (Safari 19/26 following) added pure-CSS carousel controls: scroll-marker-group + ::scroll-marker generates accessible position dots (rendered as a tablist to screen readers, with :target-current state and arrow-key focusgroup navigation), and ::scroll-button(left/right) generates prev/next buttons that scroll ~85% of the scrollport — all bound to a scroll-snap container with zero JS. Worth adding as the accessible dots/arrows layer that pairs with the horizontal pinned section and the vault's existing scroll-snap utilities: today the vault's carousels (testimonials.js, css-3d coverflow) need JS for indicators/keyboard, and accessibility is bolt-on. This makes the markers free, keyboard-navigable, and screen-reader-correct by default.
backlog.license: Public domain (W3C CSS Overflow / carousel spec; Chrome demo samples unlicensed)
backlog.note: Net-new: vault has scroll-snap.css + JS-driven carousels but NO ::scroll-marker/::scroll-button. Newer/less-supported than view-timeline — gate behind @supports(selector(::scroll-marker)) and keep an existing JS pager as fallback; verify markers render in the headless-Chrome save-gate (Chrome version dependent).

backlog.name: Non-composited animation gate (Chrome trace compositeFailed reader)
backlog.what: A headless-Chrome verification capability that captures the DevTools trace during a scripted scroll and reads Chrome's `Animation` trace events for the `compositeFailed` bitmask + `unsupportedProperties[]` array. Chrome already emits, per animation, exactly why it could NOT run on the compositor: the six reason codes are Unsupported CSS Property (e.g. width/top/height/box-shadow), Custom CSS Properties (animating a `--var`), Transform depends on box size, Filter may move pixels, Composite mode != 'replace', and Incompatible animations on the same target. This is the cleanest possible save-gate signal: it is Chrome's own ground truth, not a heuristic. Worth adding because Design Book already renders every composed page in headless Chrome — wiring `page.tracing.start()` (Puppeteer) or a CDP `Tracing` session around an auto-scroll, then post-filtering for events with a non-zero composite-failed mask, turns 'this scroll animation will jank' into a deterministic gate finding with the offending node + property named. No snippet library can offer this; it is pure verifier moat. Implement standalone (read the trace directly) rather than shelling out to full Lighthouse to keep it fast and dependency-light.
backlog.license: Apache-2.0 (Lighthouse audit is reference only; the trace data is from Chrome itself, no dep needed)
backlog.note: gsap.skill.md (lines 132-133) only states the compositor-only RULE as generation guidance; nothing VERIFIES it. coherence_check covers hex/durations/radius/scale-hovers only. This is a net-new diagnostic. Reimplement as a trace reader (no GPL/runtime dep) — Lighthouse source is just the spec for the bitmask flags.

backlog.name: Static compositor-safety lint for scroll keyframes (CSS pre-render check)
backlog.what: A static-analysis sibling to the trace gate, run on the composed page's CSS/JS BEFORE (or alongside) headless render: flag any `@keyframes` / WAAPI keyframe / scroll-scrub binding whose animated property set is outside {transform, opacity, filter} — i.e. animating `width/height/top/left/right/bottom/margin/padding` (forced layout per frame) or a custom `--var` (never compositable). Also flags `transform` keyframes that interpolate a percentage-based translate or `scale` tied to box size (the 'transform depends on box size' reason). Because Design Book OWNS the composed HTML/CSS, this is detectable without even rendering — a fast first-pass that catches the pitfall the QUESTION names (animating width/top) deterministically and points at the exact rule. Worth adding as the cheap front-line of the same moat: the trace gate is ground-truth but needs a scripted scroll; the static lint is instant and explains the fix ('animate transform/opacity instead'). Pairs naturally with the existing coherence_check rule list as a new rule class.
backlog.license: N/A (technique — own implementation; Lighthouse property list is the reference)
backlog.note: Complements (does not duplicate) the trace gate above. coherence_check today has zero perf rules — this extends that same advisory check class with a layout-thrash rule. Avoids the GSAP-only scoping of the existing gsap.skill.md guidance by checking ALL keyframes/scroll bindings page-wide.

backlog.name: Native CSS scroll-driven timeline snippet pack (scroll()/view()/animation-range + reduced-motion fallback)
backlog.what: A zero-dep CSS snippet set using the now-baseline-ish `animation-timeline: scroll()` / `view()`, `view-timeline`/`scroll-timeline`, `animation-range: entry/cover/exit`, and `timeline-scope`. The vault's entire scroll-motion section (ScrollScrub, ScrollPin, TracingBeam, sticky-scroll-reveal, horizontal-pin) is JS rAF-driven — it has NO native CSS scroll timelines at all, despite these running ON THE COMPOSITOR for free (the exact perf win the QUESTION is about). Worth adding as the compositor-correct counterpart to the JS snippets: reveal-on-scroll, scroll progress bars, pinned-section parallax, and image cover-reveals that stay at 60fps because the browser drives them off-main-thread. Critically, ship each with the documented correctness gotchas baked in so they pass the new gate: `@media (prefers-reduced-motion: no-preference)` wrapper, `animation-fill-mode: backwards` (so the 0% keyframe applies before the range starts), `overflow: clip` not `hidden` (so the target doesn't accidentally become a scroll container), and a `@supports (animation-timeline: scroll())` fallback to the existing JS path for the ~15% unsupported (Firefox-behind-flag) tail.
backlog.license: N/A (web standard / own CSS)
backlog.note: Net-new: grep confirms zero `animation-timeline`/`scroll-timeline`/`view-timeline`/`animation-range` anywhere in scroll/ effects/ taste/. All existing scroll motion is JS-driven. These are the compositor-safe versions and double as the 'good' fixtures the verifier rewards.

backlog.name: will-change overuse / lifecycle audit
backlog.what: A small verification check that flags `will-change` anti-patterns the QUESTION calls out and that a headless render can detect: (1) `will-change` declared on a large number of elements simultaneously (each forces a persistent compositor layer → GPU memory blowup, can make the page SLOWER), (2) `will-change` set in a static stylesheet on elements that are never animated (it should be applied just-before-animation and removed after via JS), and (3) `will-change` on properties that aren't even being animated. Detectable from the composed page by counting matched selectors at render time and cross-referencing against which elements actually receive transitions/animations/scroll bindings. Worth adding because it's the third specific pitfall named in the angle and it's a quick, high-signal advisory rule that complements the compositor gate — together they cover the 'too few compositor layers (jank)' AND 'too many compositor layers (memory)' failure axes. Lives in the same advisory class as coherence_check.
backlog.license: N/A (technique — own implementation)
backlog.note: gsap.skill.md line 133 says 'let GSAP manage will-change; don't hand-set it on many elements' — again only generation GUIDANCE, no verifier enforces it, and it's GSAP-scoped. This is a net-new page-wide gate. Lower value than the trace gate because it's the most heuristic of the four (count thresholds need tuning to avoid false positives).

backlog.name: Spring-to-linear() easing token generator (vanilla, à la spring-easing / Linear Easing Generator)
backlog.what: A ~30-line vanilla function that samples a damped harmonic oscillator (stiffness/damping/mass/velocity) over time and emits a CSS `linear(0, 0.013 0.6%, ... 1)` string usable directly as a transition/animation easing token. okikio/spring-easing's CSSSpringEasing does exactly this (`easing: linear(${out})`), and Jake Archibald/Adam Argyle's Linear Easing Generator proves the algorithm. Worth adding because the vault's spring.js is a rAF JS animator and easing.js only emits JS f(t) functions — NEITHER produces a CSS-native `linear()` spring token. This gives the engine real, GPU-composited multi-bounce spring overshoot in pure CSS (and as a WAAPI easing arg), and is the missing primitive that lets both findings below drop GSAP. Reimplement vanilla rather than ship the dep (last release May 2023). Pairs directly with the blessed-motion taste layer as a named `--ease-spring` / `--ease-bounce` token.
backlog.license: MIT (CSS portions Apache-2.0, after Jake Archibald)

backlog.name: Zero-dep WAAPI FLIP for list reorder/filter (getBoundingClientRect + element.animate + linear() spring)
backlog.what: A vanilla FlipLite: snapshot child rects with getBoundingClientRect (First), mutate DOM (reorder/filter/sort/grid↔list = Last), Invert via transform, then Play with `el.animate([...], {easing: 'linear(...)', duration})` using the spring linear() token above. This is a true drop-in replacement for the vault's gsap/flip-layout.js (which hard-requires GSAP core + the Flip plugin from CDN) with the SAME `.animate(mutateFn)` ergonomics but ZERO dependency. It also subsumes page-transition-morph.js's single-element case. The vault's view-transitions.skill.md currently routes any 'spring physics / stagger' FLIP need to GSAP — this removes that dependency entirely. Adds stagger + reduced-motion + works where View Transitions can't (multi-element overshoot, fine WAAPI control, older browsers).
backlog.license: N/A (original vanilla pattern; WAAPI is a web standard)

backlog.name: View Transitions list reordering with `view-transition-name: match-element` (+ moveBefore)
backlog.what: Modern same-document list reorder/filter: one `document.startViewTransition()` around an insertBefore/moveBefore mutation, with every list item set to `view-transition-name: match-element` so the browser auto-FLIPs every row's old→new position with no per-item unique names and no manual rect math. match-element became Baseline Newly Available in Oct 2025 (Firefox 144, Safari 18.4, Chrome) — genuinely net-new since the vault was built. The vault's named-transitions.js still requires hand-assigned unique view-transition-name pairs (thumb→hero), which is exactly the tedium match-element eliminates for N-item lists; and moveBefore (preserves element state/focus during reorder) isn't referenced anywhere. Best as a decision-branch added to view-transitions.skill.md plus a small declarative helper, with the WAAPI-FLIP above as the pre-baseline/Firefox-types fallback. The 'kick flip' (a per-element overshoot keyframe on the moved item) is the inspiration flourish.
backlog.license: N/A (web standard / CC-licensed article technique)

backlog.name: Sticky stacking cards (position:sticky + view-timeline scale-down stack)
backlog.what: The Apple/Linear-style multi-card pattern where each card pins to the top of the scroll range via position:sticky on its inner content, and as the next card scrolls in it physically stacks on top while the card below scales down (transform: scale(calc(1.1 - 0.1*reverse-index))) and dims. The canonical Bramus/scroll-driven-animations.style demo does it in PURE CSS: a named view-timeline-name on the cards container, animation-timeline bound to it on each card-content, and animation-range: exit-crossing <start%> <end%> spread evenly across cards via CSS index custom properties (--index/--numcards). Worth adding because it is THE dominant 2025-2026 feature/'how it works'/process landing pattern and the vault has NO scroll-driven multi-card stack — only a single-element 3D pin (container-scroll/macbook-scroll) and a two-column text+sticky-visual swap (sticky-scroll-reveal). A vanilla .css/.js snippet (CSS scroll-timeline path + a tiny IntersectionObserver/scroll JS fallback for Safari/Firefox, since native scroll-driven animations are Chromium-only as of mid-2026) is directly composable into landing genres and degrades to a plain sticky stack with no animation.
backlog.license: Technique/CC-style demo (Apache-2.0 reference impl); vanilla-reimplementable, no dependency
backlog.note: Distinct from vault's container-scroll/macbook-scroll (single element) and sticky-scroll-reveal (Aceternity two-column visual swap). Native CSS path needs @supports(animation-timeline:view()) guard + JS fallback because scroll-driven animations are Chromium-only; degrades gracefully to a static sticky stack. Add reduced-motion guard to match vault a11y convention.

backlog.name: Scroll-stack reveal / 'scaling card deck' (Framer-Motion useScroll → vanilla scroll-progress port)
backlog.what: The Skiper-UI 'Card Stack Scroll' / ReactBits-style ScrollStack variant: a vertical deck of project/feature cards that all share a sticky pin point, and a continuous scroll-progress value drives each card to a decreasing targetScale (card N shrinks more than N+1) so the deck reads as a cascading parallax of nested cards rather than discrete pin-then-next. It is the JS-driven cousin of the pure-CSS stacking cards: where CSS animation-range gives discrete per-card scroll windows, this uses one scroll-progress→scale mapping for a smoother continuous 'breathing deck' look. Worth adding as the JS-controlled tier of the same family: the vault's existing scroll-scrub.js / scroll-pin.js (--sp-progress) already expose scroll progress, so a vanilla 'StickyStackDeck' can reuse that to set per-card --scale with no library. Reference is React+Framer-Motion+Lenis (not a dep we'd ship) — treat as inspiration for the continuous-scale math, ship vanilla.
backlog.license: Skiper UI free with attribution (attribution-required) — inspiration only, do NOT vendor; Framer Motion is MIT but is a React dep we avoid
backlog.note: Inspiration, not a dep (attribution-required + React/Framer/Lenis stack). Reimplement the continuous scroll-progress→targetScale mapping in vanilla on top of the vault's existing scroll-scrub.js progress, distinct from the discrete CSS animation-range approach. Pairs as the 'continuous' variant of the sticky-stacking-cards snippet above.

backlog.name: animation-range: exit-crossing / contain / entry-exit ranges for scroll-driven stacks
backlog.what: Not a new effect but a missing primitive: the named-range timeline phases (cover / contain / entry / exit / entry-crossing / exit-crossing) that let a single view-timeline drive different elements over precisely sliced sub-windows of the scroll. The stacking-cards demo depends entirely on animation-range: exit-crossing <start%> <end%> with per-card % computed from CSS index vars. The vault already uses animation-timeline: view()/scroll() (scroll-animations.css, headers-pro.css) but never the named-range phases — so it can't currently express 'animate element A only during the last 25% of element B's exit'. Adding a small documented set of range recipes (a skill/snippet note) unlocks not just stacking cards but pinned text-swap timing, staggered reveal-by-position, and progress-scrubbed headers, all in pure CSS. Low-cost, high-leverage addition to the scroll-driven toolbox the vault half-uses.
backlog.license: Web standard (CSS Scroll-Driven Animations) — no license concern
backlog.note: Fits as a short decision-tree addition to a scroll skill (when to use which range phase + @supports guard + JS fallback), not a standalone effect. It is the enabling primitive behind the stacking-cards snippet.

backlog.name: Scroll-driven Cover Flow (native CSS view-timeline + rotateY/translateZ)
backlog.what: Apple-style Cover Flow built with ZERO JS: a scroll-snap row where each item declares view-timeline-name + view-timeline-axis:inline, then binds a @keyframes rotate-cover (translateX/rotateY(-45deg) -> rotateY(0) translateZ(1em) scale(1.5) -> rotateY(45deg)) to animation-timeline:<that-view-timeline>, with a separate adjust-z-index keyframe and perspective:40em on the item. Worth adding because the vault's ONLY coverflow (components/css-3d.js) is JS-scroll-event driven (getBoundingClientRect + rAF on every scroll). A native-CSS view()-bound version runs entirely on the compositor thread (no main-thread JS, no rAF loop), degrades to a flat scroll-snap carousel where unsupported, and is the canonical 'depth coverflow without a WebGL/JS dep' snippet the brief asks for. Distinct from the OGL drag gallery (WebGL) and three.js instanced grid.
backlog.license: Article/CC; technique is unencumbered (reimplement). Reference demo at scroll-driven-animations.style is Apache-2.0.
backlog.note:
Anti-dup CONFIRMED: grep for any file combining animation-timeline with translateZ/rotateY/perspective returns ZERO. css-3d.js coverflow is JS-event driven, not native scroll-timeline. Needs @supports(animation-timeline:view()) guard + flat scroll-snap fallback; pair rotateY/translateZ on inner (not the
  • ) so it doesn't change scroll width. Add prefers-reduced-motion off-switch per vault policy.

backlog.name: Pure-CSS scroll-driven perspective depth-parallax (translateZ layers, no JS)
backlog.what: Parallax DEPTH built two ways and worth shipping as one snippet: (a) Keith-Clark-style real-3D depth — perspective on a container + preserve-3d, layers at different translateZ with scale-compensation (scale = 1 + (translateZ*-1)/perspective) so far layers move slower; and (b) the modern scroll() / view() timeline variant binding per-layer @keyframes at different speeds (animation-timeline:scroll(root) for bg, view(-100px) for content). The vault has scroll-fx-pack (perspective:8px translateZ bg layers) but it is a static perspective trick, and animations/scroll-parallax is plain 2D translateY tied to scroll(root). A snippet that explicitly couples NATIVE scroll-driven timelines to layered translateZ depth (compositor-thread, 60fps, no JS) is net-new and the zero-dep alternative to the three.js/OGL depth effects.
backlog.license: CSS-Tricks article (technique, unencumbered); Keith Clark method is public/MIT-spirit
backlog.note: Partial adjacency: scroll/scroll-fx-pack.css (static perspective layers) and animations/scroll-animations.css .scroll-parallax (2D translateY via scroll(root)). Neither binds translateZ DEPTH layers to a native view()/scroll() timeline. Differentiate clearly in INDEX tags (depth-parallax vs flat-parallax) to avoid being seen as a dup. @supports guard + reduced-motion.

backlog.name: Scroll-driven stacking / sticky-stack cards (native view-timeline)
backlog.what: Cards that stack and scale/recede as they overlap during scroll, driven purely by view-timeline + position:sticky (scale + slight translateZ/rotate as each card pins, so the outgoing card sinks behind the incoming one). Common 2025 long-form/landing pattern. The vault has dialog-stack (static Apple notification fan, .dstack) and sticky-scroll-reveal/sticky-stagger (JS IntersectionObserver), but NO native scroll-timeline stacking-cards snippet. Adds a zero-JS, compositor-thread 'cards stack on scroll' that composes well into the existing page genres.
backlog.license: Apache-2.0 (reference demos, Bramus/Google)
backlog.note: Anti-dup: INDEX 'stacking' only matches dstack (static fan) and sticky-scroll-reveal (JS observer). Net-new as a NATIVE scroll-timeline version. Lower value than coverflow/depth-parallax because the JS-observer sticky-stagger covers ~80% of the use case already; main win is dropping the JS + main-thread cost.

backlog.name: 3D scroll-driven text on a cylinder/tube (CSS perspective + scroll-driven progress)
backlog.what: Type wrapping a 3D cylinder/tube (rotateX with perspective:70vw, transform-style:preserve-3d, trig-positioned rows) animated by scroll progress. NOTE this Codrops build is GSAP ScrollTrigger driven because it needs per-frame Math.sin/cos positioning — NOT reimplementable as pure native CSS (timelines can't do real-time trig). Flag as INSPIRATION/skill note, not a vanilla snippet. The vault already has GSAP + scroll-scrub + scroll-text-reveal + scroll-text-zoom, so a usable subset (static CSS perspective tube + bind opacity/rotateZ to scroll-scrub) is a small skill addition, not a new dep.
backlog.license: Codrops/Tympanus (tutorial; MIT-style demo code typical)
backlog.note: Lowest value / most duplicative-adjacent: GSAP scroll-scrub + 3D-text reveal already exist. Only the cylinder/tube framing is novel. Treat as a one-paragraph technique note in 3d-motion.skill.md or gsap.skill.md, NOT a new snippet — it requires JS trig and overlaps existing scroll-scrub tooling.

backlog.name: scroll-motion-router.skill.md (cross-doc VT + scroll-primitive decision tree)
backlog.what: A net-new orchestration SKILL that sits ABOVE the existing view-transitions.skill.md and routes the compose engine to the right primitive: (1) MPA cross-document VT via @view-transition{navigation:auto} for real same-origin page loads, (2) SPA-swap via ViewTransitions.run()/NamedTransitions.morph() for in-document DOM mutation, (3) native scroll-driven (animation-timeline: scroll()/view(), already in scroll-animations.css) for scrubbed/progress motion, (4) scroll-triggered/IntersectionObserver reveals (AosLite/ScrollFx) for fire-once-on-threshold. The 2024-2026 gap the current skill MISSES and this skill would codify: VT `types` descriptor + pagereveal/pageswap events to detect direction, `:active-view-transition-type(forwards|backwards)` for directional slides, `view-transition-name: match-element` for naming-collision avoidance (no more hand-assigning unique names), `view-transition-class` for grouped styling, and nested groups (Chrome 140) vs root transitions. Each branch ends in a prefers-reduced-motion -> instant-swap fallback and a no-startViewTransition fallback. Pure orchestration over primitives the vault already ships plus the two net-new snippets below — complements, does not duplicate, view-transitions.skill.md.
backlog.license: N/A (authored skill / web-platform spec)

backlog.name: Directional cross-document MPA View Transitions (types + :active-view-transition-type)
backlog.what: A vanilla snippet pair completing the MPA half of the VT story the current skill only names in one table row. CSS: @view-transition{navigation:auto; types:slide} on BOTH same-origin pages plus html:active-view-transition-type(forwards)/(backwards){ ::view-transition-old/new animation-name swaps }. JS: a pagereveal listener that compares navigation.activation.from.url vs entry.url and calls e.viewTransition.types.add('forwards'|'backwards') so back/forward slides go the correct way. Gated behind feature-detect (falls back to plain navigation) and prefers-reduced-motion (drops to cross-fade/instant). This is genuinely net-new: the vault's view-transitions.js is SPA-only (document.startViewTransition wrapper) and has zero @view-transition/types/pagereveal code. Gives the compose engine app-like MPA navigation without a SPA router.
backlog.license: Web platform (no dep)

backlog.name: @container scroll-state() queries (stuck / snapped / scrollable)
backlog.what: CSS-only replacement for a whole class of JS scroll listeners the vault currently does with IntersectionObserver/scroll handlers (HeaderKit's .is-stuck/.is-scrolled toggling, scroll-spy, sticky-state styling). `@container scroll-state(stuck: top){...}` styles a position:sticky element ONCE it actually sticks; scroll-state(snapped: x|y) styles the currently snapped scroll-snap child; scroll-state(scrollable: top|bottom) reveals affordances when a container can still scroll. No JS, no rAF, no layout-thrash. Net-new: vault has layout/container-queries.css (size/style queries only) and JS-driven sticky headers, but zero scroll-state coverage. The decision-tree skill above would hand off here for 'style by scroll condition' (vs animate by scroll progress).
backlog.license: Web platform (no dep)

backlog.name: animation-trigger (CSS scroll-triggered animations)
backlog.what: Declarative CSS that fires a full animation when an element crosses a view threshold and (optionally) reverses/resets on exit — the fire-and-forget cousin of scroll-driven (which scrubs). `animation-trigger: --t view() once|repeat|alternate` replaces the AosLite/ScrollFx IntersectionObserver reveal pattern with zero JS for the common case. Distinct from the vault's existing native scroll-driven CSS (animation-timeline: scroll/view, which ties progress to scroll position) and from the JS reveal snippets. Worth flagging but LOWER value now: Chrome 145 / 2026, Chrome+Edge only, very early adoption (~1% usage) — so it lands in the decision tree as the 'CSS-native path when supported, else fall back to AosLite' branch, more inspiration/progressive-enhancement than a load-bearing dep today.
backlog.license: Web platform (no dep)

backlog.name: COLRv1 color-font auto-recolor (@font-palette-values + override-colors)
backlog.what: A zero-dep CSS pattern: one small WOFF2 COLRv1 display face (Nabla/Bungee Spice/Rocher, all OFL via Google Fonts css2 CDN) recolored to ANY vault palette by emitting a @font-palette-values rule with override-colors per color index, then font-palette:--name on the heading. One ~30KB font renders full-color gradient display type that re-themes to every pal-*. Crucial gotcha to bake in: CSS custom properties do NOT work inside @font-palette-values, so the rule text must be string-generated from the palette (var() silently no-ops) - exactly the kind of trap a snippet should pre-solve. Net-new: vault has mesh/aurora gradients and tons of type effects but zero color-font / CPAL-palette coverage.
backlog.license: Fonts: SIL OFL 1.1 (Nabla, Bungee Spice) / free-commercial (Rocher Color, Harbor Type) ; technique: native CSS, no lib
backlog.note: CDN-loadable via fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Nabla / Bungee+Spice / Rocher. Guard with @supports font-tech(color-COLRv1). Generate the override-colors rule from the active pal-* (NOT var()).

backlog.name: color-font palette-binding + animation skill (palette interpolation vs variable-axis)
backlog.what: A .skill.md decision tree that picks the right animatable dimension for color display type. Two distinct, often-confused mechanisms: (1) font-palette interpolation - MDN confirms animation-type is 'by computed value', so modern Chromium/Firefox SMOOTHLY @keyframes between two @font-palette-values (e.g. base-palette:2 -> 3), while older engines flip discretely; (2) variable-axis animation via font-variation-settings on the font's COLR-aware axes (Nabla EDPT 0..200 / EHLT 0..24 = extrusion depth + highlight; Rocher bevel/shadow), which animates the gradient/3D look itself and is fully real animation. Skill encodes: bind palette indices to pal-* tokens, choose palette-cross-fade vs axis-morph, and set the discrete-flip fallback expectation. Net-new decision content; nearest skill print-typography.skill.md covers variable text but not COLR color/CPAL.
backlog.license: documentation/native CSS
backlog.note: Encode the gotcha that palette transitions interpolate only in modern Chromium/FF and flip-discrete elsewhere; axis animation (EDPT/EHLT) is the reliable cross-engine motion path.

backlog.name: font-palette-paints verification (COLRv1 painted vs silent Safari mono-fallback)
backlog.what: A headless-Chrome diagnostic that fits the vault's 'screenshot is truth' moat. Safari (macOS/iOS, still true per FontLab May-2026) does NOT render COLRv1 glyphs - it falls back to the base outline in a SINGLE solid CPAL color while still parsing the font-palette CSS, so a page can look 'fine' (no error, no overflow) yet have lost all color and gradient intent. The gate: render the heading, sample pixels, and assert >1 distinct hue / measurable color variance inside the glyph bounds; if it's a single flat color, font-palette silently fell back - fail or warn. Mirrors the documented WebGL draw-verify lesson (pixel-sample to prove it actually painted). Net-new verification capability.
backlog.license: n/a (diagnostic)
backlog.note: Detect via @supports font-tech(color-COLRv1) for the CSS path AND a pixel-hue-count assertion in the render gate; pair with a monochrome/serif fallback font so Safari degrades to readable solid type instead of relying on COLR.

backlog.name: @property-registered axis customs (--wght/--wdth/--opsz) composed into font-variation-settings via calc()
backlog.what: Register each variable-font axis as its own @property of syntax '<number>', then compose them into one font-variation-settings: 'wght' var(--wght), 'wdth' var(--wdth), 'opsz' var(--opsz) declaration. Because registered customs interpolate as typed numbers, you transition/keyframe each axis INDEPENDENTLY (e.g. hover bumps only --wght while a breathe keyframe drives --opsz) with zero JS. This sidesteps the vault's two known weaknesses in variable-fonts.css: (1) the font-variation-settings shorthand reset gotcha where setting one axis wipes the others, and (2) the inability to drive axes from different triggers at once. It is a strictly cleaner, more controllable default for headlines than the current raw-shorthand keyframes. NET-NEW: vault has NO @property axis registration anywhere (only @property for conic angles/progress rings). CAVEAT TO SHIP WITH IT: this does NOT run on the compositor — registered customs are explicitly non-composited (Chromium #1411864, still open 2024-2026) and font-variation-settings itself repaints/relayouts per frame. The win over VariableFontCursor is 'no JS, browser-throttled, auto-pauses offscreen, one-line reduced-motion', NOT GPU offload. Pair with will-change:font-variation-settings + contain:layout on the text block to bound the repaint.
backlog.license: MIT (vanilla reimplementation; technique is public CSS)
backlog.note: Add as a new block in typography/variable-fonts.css (or vf-axes.css). MUST correct the compose engine's assumption: there is no compositor-thread variable-font animation; sell it as zero-JS + reduced-motion-clean, not GPU-cheap.

backlog.name: Fluid optical sizing — opsz axis bound to viewport via @property + clamp()/calc()
backlog.what: A self-adjusting headline pattern: register --opsz as @property '<number>' and drive it from the element's rendered font-size (or a clamp() fluid-type curve) so the optical-size axis tracks size automatically — small text gets sturdier opsz, large display text gets refined high-contrast opsz. Combined with the vault's existing fluid-type.css this gives genuinely 'designed-for-this-size' headlines with no JS and no media-query soup. NET-NEW: vault's variable-fonts.css only lists opsz in a comment and never animates/binds it; fluid-type.css scales size but not the optical axis. This is the one variable-font axis the vault completely ignores and the one that most separates 'pro typography' from 'AI-default sans'. Low risk, high taste payoff, degrades to a static value on non-opsz fonts.
backlog.license: MIT (vanilla; Roboto Flex / Fraunces / Source Serif 4 expose opsz, all OFL fonts)
backlog.note: Best with OFL variable fonts that expose opsz (Roboto Flex, Fraunces, Source Serif 4, Bricolage Grotesque). Note that opsz is normally auto via font-optical-sizing:auto; this pattern is for *intentional* art-directed override beyond the auto curve.

backlog.name: Non-composited animation gate (DevTools 'not composited' detection in the verify step)
backlog.what: A verification capability for the headless-Chrome save-gate: flag animations that fall off the compositor (animating font-variation-settings, registered custom properties, width/height/top/left, box-shadow, filter on large areas) and warn when count/area exceeds a budget. Chrome exposes this — the Animations panel marks non-composited animations, and the reasons are queryable via the CDP Animation domain / trace events. This directly serves the brief's own confusion: the engine currently can't tell composited (transform/opacity) from main-thread-repaint animations, so it can't warn when a 'cheap' headline effect actually janks on a long page. Turning that into a gate is exactly the kind of diagnostic moat Design Book values, and it makes the variable-font snippets above safe to ship with honest guidance instead of the compositor myth.
backlog.license: N/A (Chrome DevTools / CDP, already the gate's runtime)
backlog.note: Implement against the CDP Animation domain or trace events in the existing headless-Chrome verifier; surface 'N non-composited animations (font-variation-settings/custom-prop)' as a coherence/perf warning, not a hard fail.

backlog.name: SVG textPath running-type engine (curved headline / circular badge / wavy sine ticker)
backlog.what: A zero-dep vanilla snippet that lays live text on an arbitrary inline <path> via <textPath> and drives startOffset from scroll OR time via requestAnimationFrame. One primitive covers three high-impact presets the vault lacks entirely: (1) curved/arched editorial headline, (2) infinitely rotating circular badge/seal text around a logo, (3) wavy 'sine path' ticker (path d='M0 50 Q ...' repeating S-curves) as a more expressive cousin of the flat marquee. Worth adding because the vault has SVG path draw/morph and getTotalLength scroll-draw (google-gemini-effect, TracingBeam) and flat marquee/ticker, but NOTHING places type ON a path — an explicit scope gap. Degrades to static curved text with JS off. Pacing: read pathEl.getTotalLength(), wrap startOffset modulo length for seamless loop; couple to scroll via scroll position or ScrollTimeline. Pair with vault's variable-fonts.css for weight-on-curve. KEY GOTCHAS to bake in: startOffset is a PRESENTATION ATTRIBUTE, not a CSS property, so CSS scroll-driven animations cannot target it — must use rAF/JS (or deprecated SMIL <animate>); set BOTH href and xlink:href for max compat; Safari/WebKit has a long-standing startOffset marquee-animation bug (jitter/clipping near wrap) so animate via JS not SMIL and avoid relying on negative offsets; getComputedTextLength() (text length) differs from path getTotalLength() — use the path length for loop modulo; the textPath 'side' attribute (left/right, flips text to inside/outside of a circle) is unsupported in older Safari, so flip via path direction reversal instead as a fallback.
backlog.license: N/A (native SVG + vanilla JS, MIT-style snippet)

backlog.name: CSS Motion Path per-character text-on-path (selectable, offset-path/offset-distance)
backlog.what: A distinct, CSS-first alternative to <textPath>: split a heading into per-character spans, give each offset-path: path('...') and an offset-distance computed from its char index (custom property), optionally staggering offset-distance to animate the string sliding along the curve.
