Net-new value vs the textPath engine because the text stays REAL SELECTABLE/COPYABLE DOM (textPath text is selectable but not reflowable HTML, and char-level coloring/hover is awkward) and each glyph can be individually animated/hovered — good for playful nav, hover-scatter headlines, and accessible curved labels. integrationForm vanilla-snippet: a tiny JS that wraps chars in spans + sets --i, plus a CSS file. Gotchas to document: only the path() value is broadly supported for offset-path (no shape()/ray() reliably yet in 2025 Safari), so the curve is fixed in px and not fluid-responsive — provide a JS re-layout on resize; offset-distance IS animatable by CSS scroll-driven animations (unlike SVG startOffset), making this the scroll-scrub-friendly variant. Complements rather than duplicates the textPath snippet.
backlog.license: N/A (native CSS Motion Path + vanilla JS)

backlog.name: text-on-path.skill.md (decision tree: textPath vs offset-path vs canvas; pacing + cross-browser gates)
backlog.what: A short skill that routes the composer between the two snippets and encodes the gotchas so generated output is robust: choose <textPath> for badges/arched display headlines and ticker-on-sine (best glyph spacing along true arc length); choose CSS offset-path per-char when text must be selectable/individually animated or scroll-scrubbed via CSS; mention canvas measureText only for thousands of glyphs. Encodes the verification-relevant rules: animate startOffset via rAF not SMIL (deprecation + WebKit bug), use getTotalLength() modulo for seamless loop, set both href+xlink:href, avoid 'side' attr (reverse path instead), respect prefers-reduced-motion (freeze offset), and ensure the SVG <text> has an accessible label / the curved string is also present as readable text. Worth adding because the vault's strength is its decision-tree skills + verification moat; without this skill the engine has no guidance to pick the right text-on-path primitive or to dodge the Safari pitfalls, which would otherwise fail headless-Chrome coherence/overflow gates.
backlog.license: N/A (authored skill doc)

backlog.name: troika-three-text (vanilla three.js CDN snippet)
backlog.what: Drop-in three.js SDF text mesh that parses .ttf/.otf/.woff directly (Typr) and bakes a glyph SDF atlas on-the-fly in a web worker — kerning, ligatures, RTL/Arabic, unicode fallback all handled. Renders crisp at any zoom and is far cheaper than the vault's extruded TextGeometry (one quad per glyph vs. tessellated 3D mesh). Ships a UMD build (dist/troika-three-text.umd.js) so it loads straight from jsdelivr/unpkg behind a need()-guard with no bundler; only peer dep is three.js, which the vault already loads. Net-new: the vault's `3d/floating-text.js` is extruded geometry and `shaders/sdf-text.glsl.js` is a fullscreen shader sampling a pre-baked atlas whose builder is explicitly 'not included' — neither is a real, typeable, layout-correct SDF text object. Fills the zero-build gap that drei's React-only Text/Text3D (mentioned in 3d-web.skill.md) doesn't cover for vanilla pages.
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: MIT, fully CDN-safe via UMD. Pair with the SDF-warp inspiration below: troika exposes glyphGeometryDetail (subdivide each glyph quad) + createDerivedMaterial / onBeforeCompile so a custom vertex/fragment shader can warp the SDF glyphs. need()-guard should fall back to the existing floating-text.js extruded text when troika isn't loaded.

backlog.name: SDF glyph-warp shader (melt / ripple / bulge) — Codrops 'Gommage' technique
backlog.what: Technique for distorting MSDF/SDF glyph text in-shader: sample the multi-channel distance value per fragment, then (a) threshold it against animated Perlin noise for a progressive melt/dissolve, and/or (b) displace the subdivided glyph quad in the vertex shader (sin-wave ripple, radial bulge from a pointer uv) before the SDF AA pass. The Codrops Gommage demo (Jan 2026, three.js + TSL) dissolves MSDF text into dust/petals using per-letter glyphUv + center attributes; the same math is trivially expressible in WebGL/GLSL on troika's derived material or on the vault's existing sdf-text.glsl.js. Net-new: the vault has pointer-displace, distortion, image-distortion, and gl-transitions warps, plus text-wave/text-ripple (DOM-level) — but nothing warps glyph SDF text, and sdf-text.glsl.js only has a trivial decorative sine on the whole texture. This adds smoothstep-correct edge AA preserved THROUGH the warp (the hard part), which the existing warp shaders don't do because they distort raster images, not distance fields.
backlog.license: Codrops tutorial code (MIT-style, reimplement); underlying msdfgen MIT, msdf-bmfont-xml MIT
backlog.note: Inspiration, not a dep: WebGPU/TSL in the demo but the SDF-threshold-vs-noise melt and vertex-displace ripple/bulge are plain GLSL — reimplement as a glsl.js companion to troika (best) or extend sdf-text.glsl.js to warp uv before the texture2D sample. Atlas via donmccurdy's web MSDF tool or msdf-bmfont-xml (both MIT) when a pre-baked path is wanted.

backlog.name: opentype.js
backlog.what: Pure-JS (no WASM) parser/writer for OTF/TTF/WOFF fonts that turns any live font + text string into drawable SVG path geometry: opentype.parse(arrayBuffer) -> font.getPath(text,x,y,size) -> path.toPathData() gives the SVG 'd' string (or path.toSVG() a full <path> element), plus per-letter glyph.getPath()/glyph.toSVG() and raw path.commands (M/L/C/Q/Z). This is the missing bridge the vault lacks: its svg-animations.js draw/morph and getTotalLength+stroke-dasharray machinery only work on HAND-AUTHORED paths, and text-pack-2's 'outline-fill' is merely a CSS -webkit-text-stroke hover toggle that CANNOT path-length-animate. Wrapping opentype.js behind a need()-guard unlocks a whole net-new class of headline effects keyed off the SAME stroke-dasharray pipeline already in the vault: per-letter stroke draw-on (write-on headlines), outline-to-fill morph, fill-sweep, and SVG-clip liquid-fill — for ARBITRARY uploaded/Google fonts, not pre-made icons. Degrades gracefully: if the lib or font fails to load, fall back to plain rendered text. Worth adding because it is the single dependency that converts the vault's existing path-draw moat from icons-only to any-typography.
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: print-typography.skill.md ALREADY NAMES opentype.js, but only as a print/font-FILE tool (subset/instance/inspect) — there is zero runtime kinetic-typography usage anywhere in the snippet dirs (confirmed by grep). The net-new addition is the runtime cdn-snippet (glyph->SVG path effect engine), NOT the lib mention. CDN guard should degrade to plain text on failure.

backlog.name: GlyphDraw text-to-path effect engine (opentype.js-powered vanilla snippet)
backlog.what: A concrete vanilla cdn-snippet that sits ON TOP of opentype.js and exposes the four requested effects as a one-call API mirroring the vault's existing SVGAnim/SplitReveal ergonomics, e.g. GlyphDraw.init('[data-glyphdraw]', {font, effect:'draw'|'outline-fill'|'fill-sweep'|'liquid-fill', stagger, duration, trigger:'auto'|'inview'}). Implementation: load font via opentype.parse, build one <path> per letter with toPathData(), measure each with getTotalLength(), then drive (a) draw-on with stroke-dasharray/dashoffset transition, (b) outline-to-fill by animating fill-opacity after the stroke completes, (c) fill-sweep via an animated linearGradient or clipPath wiping across the filled glyphs, (d) liquid-fill via an SVG <clipPath> of the glyph outlines with a rising wave rect behind it. Per-letter stagger reuses the vault's split-text mental model but on REAL outlines. This is the addable artifact (the snippet), distinct from the raw library — it is what compose_page would actually drop into a hero. Net-new vs text-pack-2 (CSS-only, no path geometry) and svg-animations.js (hand-authored paths only).
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: Reuses the vault's existing getTotalLength/stroke-dashoffset draw pipeline (svg-animations.js, google-gemini-effect.js) — low integration risk. Must respect prefers-reduced-motion and fall back to rendered text if opentype.js or the font fails. Ships as a paired cdn-snippet+vanilla wrapper; the opentype.js dep is the only network need().

backlog.name: text-box-trim / text-box-edge cap-height trim utility
backlog.what: A tiny zero-dep CSS utility that trims the invisible half-leading above cap-height and below the baseline of large type, so headings sit flush to card/image edges, button labels are truly optically centered (equal padding finally looks equal), and a big number + small label ($49 /mo) align on the same baseline. Syntax: `text-box: trim-both cap alphabetic` (shorthand) or longhand `text-box-trim` + `text-box-edge`. This is the single biggest 'amateur vs pro' tell in big-type layout and the vault has nothing for it — only `.leading-*` line-height utilities, which control line spacing, NOT the line-box slack above caps. Ship classes like `.trim-cap` (trim-both cap alphabetic), `.trim-top`/`.trim-bottom`, plus a `--leading-trim` toggle for heading-pack and hero composition.
backlog.license: n/a (native CSS property, no dependency)
backlog.note: Perfect progressive enhancement: unsupported browsers ignore it and you get the old slightly-loose leading (a pixel or two), the most graceful degradation possible. Pair with the canonical fallback so it never regresses on Firefox.

backlog.name: Leading-trim @supports progressive-enhancement fallback pattern
backlog.what: The correct cross-browser recipe so the trim utility is safe to compose everywhere: set a font-relative `margin-block` baseline (approximates the trim via negative margin / known half-leading offset), then cancel it inside `@supports (text-box-trim: trim-both) { ... margin-block: 0; text-box: trim-both cap alphabetic; }`. Browsers with native support get pixel-perfect trimming; Firefox/old Safari get the margin approximation instead of a broken layout. This is the load-bearing glue that lets Design Book turn on cap-trimming in hero/heading genres without risking a save-gate regression on a non-supporting engine.
backlog.license: n/a (native CSS)
backlog.note: Bundle with the trim utility as one snippet. Without this guard, turning on text-box-trim is a Firefox foot-gun; with it, it's pure upside.

backlog.name: Untrimmed-leading-gap detector (composed-hero verification gate)
backlog.what: A headless-Chrome diagnostic that flags amateur half-leading slack in composed heroes: for large heading text intended to sit flush to a top edge / sibling image / baseline, measure the rendered gap between the element's content-box top and the actual cap-height glyph top (via getBoundingClientRect on the text vs a canvas/Range cap-height probe, or by diffing computed line-box height against cap height). If the gap exceeds a threshold and `text-box-trim` is NOT applied, warn that the hero looks unpolished. Since the vault's verifier runs Chrome 133+, native trim is observable — the gate can also confirm the trim actually took effect. This is exactly the moat capability the brief flagged: catching the optical-misalignment tell that coherence scoring currently can't see.
backlog.license: n/a (built on existing headless-Chrome harness)
backlog.note: Highest-leverage item: it's a net-new diagnostic, not just a snippet. Cap-height probe can reuse Capsize font metrics (below) for the expected-cap-height baseline rather than measuring per-glyph.

backlog.name: Capsize (@capsizecss/core + @capsizecss/metrics)
backlog.what: Font-metrics library that computes the exact trim offsets (cap height, ascent/descent, line-gap) for system + Google fonts, the JS predecessor to native text-box-trim. Not worth adding as a runtime dep (needs a build step, no clean CDN/ESM single-file path, and native CSS now covers the common case), but its @capsizecss/metrics dataset is the authoritative source of per-font cap-height ratios. Use it as inspiration / a precomputed lookup to power the leading-gap verification gate's 'expected cap-height' math and to generate accurate `margin-block` fallback magic-numbers per font instead of guessing.
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: MIT and permissive, but build-step-only (not CDN-safe / not vanilla single-file) so treat as inspiration + a metrics data source for the verification gate, not a shipped dependency.

backlog.name: Typography coherence gate — headless line-level type-setting diagnostic (Range.getClientRects line census)
backlog.what: A new verification check, run inside the existing headless-Chrome lab, that measures ACTUAL rendered lines (not source markup) per text block via the canonical Range.getClientRects() incremental-expansion algorithm (expand a Range one char at a time; rect count = current line index). From that one census it scores: (1) widows/orphans — a last visual line that is a single word OR < ~1/3 the block width; (2) measure — body/prose blocks whose mean rendered line falls outside 45-90ch (computed as lineWidthPx / measured glyph-advance, WCAG 1.4.8 baseline ≤80ch); (3) runaway lines — any text block exceeding the max measure. It is the missing line-level layer of the gate: the current taste mode only checks type-scale RATIO, rhythm, and hierarchy — never how text actually wraps. Worth adding because gradients-without-imagery is the #1 AI tell and bad line-setting (orphans, 120ch body measure) is the #2 tell, and it is invisible to every check the gate runs today.
backlog.license: Technique/algorithm (Range.getClientRects is a standard Web API, MIT-equivalent to reimplement; Bennadel post is reference only)
backlog.note: Net-new diagnostic, not a snippet. Reimplement the algorithm in the lab's headless page.evaluate() and fold the result into the coherence/taste score (e.g. a new 'typeset' sub-score). Vault has authoring helpers (typography/fluid-type.css .text-balance/.text-pretty, vertical-rhythm.css, heading-pack.css) but NOTHING that verifies they took effect at render time.

backlog.name: text-wrap:balance silent-cap detector (Chromium 6-line / Firefox 10-line ignore)
backlog.what: A targeted lab check that flags every element with computed `text-wrap:balance` whose rendered line count (from the same getClientRects census) exceeds the silent fallback cap — 6 lines in Chromium, 10 in Firefox — where the browser silently reverts to normal wrapping with NO warning. The vault's heading-pack.css and fluid-type.css both ship `.text-balance`, so composed pages routinely apply balance to multi-line leads/intros that quietly do nothing, giving a false sense of polish. Pairs with a sibling rule: headings/leads that have NO text-wrap (balance or pretty) at all get flagged as 'missing balance on a heading' (the easy, free typographic win). Because the lab already renders in headless Chrome at named viewports, the line count is free per viewport — and balance can pass at desktop but break the 6-line cap at mobile width, which only a per-viewport check catches.
backlog.license: CSS standard behavior (per CSS Text Module spec; no dependency)
backlog.note: Slot into the same typeset diagnostic. Detection = computed-style text-wrap === 'balance' AND getClientRects line count > 6 (Chromium lab default). Also emit the inverse: heading/lead with text-wrap:normal → 'add text-wrap:balance'. Cite developer.chrome.com/docs/css-ui/css-text-wrap-balance + MDN text-wrap.

backlog.name: text-wrap:pretty orphan-window audit (Knuth-Plass last-4-lines, <1/3-width short-line)
backlog.what: A refinement of the widow/orphan rule that encodes how Chromium's `text-wrap:pretty` actually works so the gate's scoring matches browser reality and gives the correct fix. Chromium's pretty is a limited Knuth-Plass pass that only adjusts the LAST 4 LINES and only acts when the last line is ≤ 1/3 the available width with no break opportunity (WebKit similar). So the gate's orphan check should (a) use that same '<1/3 width OR single word' definition for the violation, and (b) when it finds a long-form prose block with an orphan, recommend `text-wrap:pretty` (not balance — balance is for short headings), and flag prose blocks > the 6/10-line cap that mistakenly use balance. This makes the diagnostic's fixes browser-accurate instead of naive, and distinguishes the two fixes (balance for ≤6-line headings, pretty for long prose) — the exact decision the vault's heading-pack/fluid-type authoring story leaves to chance.
backlog.license: CSS standard behavior (no dependency)
backlog.note: Not a standalone find — it is the correctness spec for the orphan branch of the typeset diagnostic, so the gate's auto-suggested fix (pretty vs balance) is right. Sources: developer.chrome.com/blog/css-text-wrap-pretty, webkit.org/blog/16547.

backlog.name: Pure-CSS odometer / rolling-digit reel (@property <integer> + tabular-nums + per-digit translateY)
backlog.what: A zero-JS rolling-odometer where each digit is a vertical 0-9 reel and only the changed digits roll (carry effect), driven by a registered @property --n {syntax:'<integer>'} that interpolates between values plus per-digit mod()/calc() offsets, transform: translateY() with steps()-feel timing, and font-variant-numeric: tabular-nums so columns never jitter. Worth adding because the vault's count-up.js / gsap counter.js / Stats only tween textContent (numbers blur/jump), and text-flipping-board is a split-flap (whole-glyph snap), not a continuous mechanical roll. This is the distinct 'mechanical mileage' kinetic feel for hero stats, dashboards, price tickers. Reimplementable as a vanilla CSS+tiny-JS snippet (JS just writes the data-value attr; CSS does the motion). Pair a JS reel-builder (column of stacked digits, translateY = -digit*1em) as the broad-support fallback for browsers lacking sibling-index()/mod().
backlog.license: Technique (article, no license needed) — reimplement clean
backlog.note: Net-new vs count-up.js, gsap/counter.js, Stats (all textContent tween) and text-flipping-board.css (split-flap snap). Ship two coordinated reel variants: pure-CSS @property and JS-built stacked-digit column (transform translateY) for broad support.

backlog.name: NumberFlow (vanilla <number-flow> custom element)
backlog.what: Apple-Wallet-grade animated number component with a true framework-free custom element: import 'https://esm.sh/number-flow' then el.update(value). Built on Web Animations API + Intl.NumberFormat (zero deps), so it does spin/fade/transform digit transitions with real currency/percent/compact formatting and localization, a `trend` prop (digits always roll up/down), respectMotionPreference (reduced-motion honored by default), and a `continuous` plugin that tweens through in-between numbers (true fluid metric ticker, not just snap). 6.3k stars, used by X and Dub.co. Worth adding as the premium 'value flux' option behind a need()-guard: it nails formatting + localization + a11y that a hand-rolled reel won't, and degrades to the plain number if the script fails to load. Distinct from the vault's count-up (no per-digit motion) and split-flap.
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: CDN via esm.sh; guard with need() and render the literal number as the no-JS fallback. Pairs with the vanilla odometer snippet (vanilla = always-on baseline, NumberFlow = premium formatted/localized upgrade).

backlog.name: GRAD-axis weight-surge on value change (reflow-free numeric flux)
backlog.what: Make a number visibly 'pulse' bolder/denser the instant it changes by animating the variable-font GRAD (grade) axis instead of wght: font-variation-settings:'GRAD' 0 -> 'GRAD' 100 with transition, then back. GRAD increases stroke density WITHOUT changing glyph advance width, so a tabular/aligned metric surges in weight on update and settles, with zero layout reflow and zero column jitter (wght would break tabular alignment and shift neighbors). Trigger on the value-change event (toggle a .is-flux class, or drive a @property --grad). This is the 'weight-reactive number flux' the angle asks for and is net-new: the vault's variable-font work (variable-fonts.css transitions, variable-font-cursor) is cursor-distance / hover driven, never value-change driven, and never uses GRAD for reflow-safe tabular numbers. Pairs perfectly with the odometer reel and NumberFlow to add a tactile 'data just updated' accent. Supported by Roboto Flex, Recursive, and other GRAD-axis variable fonts already referenceable.
backlog.license: Technique (article) — reimplement; uses freely-licensed variable fonts (Roboto Flex/Recursive, OFL)
backlog.note: Distinct from variable-fonts.css (hover wght/slnt transitions) and variable-font-cursor (cursor-distance driven). Net-new axis (GRAD) + net-new trigger (value-change, not pointer). Requires a GRAD-axis font; gate on font load, fall back to wght or a color flash.

backlog.name: OpenType feature animation snippet (font-feature-settings / font-variant reveals: swash-on-hover, ligature-reveal, ss01-ss20 toggle, fractions, contextual alternates)
backlog.what: A zero-dep CSS+tiny-JS snippet that animates OpenType FEATURES (not axes): swash-on-hover (font-variant-alternates / 'swsh'), discretionary-ligature reveal ('dlig'/'liga'), stylistic-set cycling ('ss01'-'ss20'), contextual alternates ('calt'), and auto-fractions ('frac'). The crucial design insight the vault is missing: font-feature-settings is NOT tweenable — toggling a feature is an INSTANT glyph swap, the exact opposite of the vault's existing font-variation-settings axis tweens. So the kinetic value comes from the discrete reveal, which you SOFTEN with a companion transition (clip-path wipe, fade, or a wght/wdth axis tween underneath) so the swash/ligature 'blooms' in on hover/scroll/active. Worth adding because it's an entirely distinct expressive dimension from the vault's cursor-driven axis morphing — swashes flicking out on hover, code ligatures resolving, ss-sets cycling on a heading — at zero dependency. Should use the standardized font-variant-* properties first (better intent/support) and fall back to font-feature-settings for ssXX/swsh.
backlog.license: Technique (CSS spec) — public; demo fonts OFL
backlog.note: NON-DUPLICATIVE: vault's typography/variable-fonts.css + effects/variable-font-cursor.css only animate AXES via font-variation-settings (wght/wdth/slnt/CASL). No file references font-feature-settings, font-variant-ligatures/alternates/numeric, swsh, ssXX, calt, dlig, or frac. Pair the snippet with a note that the swap is discrete (mask/fade it). Ships with a reduced-motion guard like the sibling vf snippets.

backlog.name: Feature-rich variable-font picker note: Recursive, Fraunces, Mona Sans / Hubot Sans (axes + stylistic sets together)
backlog.what: A 'feature-rich font picker' note for the OpenType snippet + print-typography skill, curating the OFL variable fonts that expose RICH axes AND stylistic sets/swashes simultaneously, so a single @font-face powers both axis-tween AND feature-reveal effects. Recursive (arrowtype, OFL): 5 axes (MONO, CASL, wght, slnt, CRSV) + ss01-ss20 incl. single-story a, simplified 6/9, and code ligatures (dlig) — already a font the vault names, so this just unlocks its feature layer. Fraunces (Undercase Type, OFL, on Google Fonts): wght/opsz/SOFT/WONK axes + display swash alternates and a 'Wonk' stylistic toggle — ideal for editorial swash-on-hover. Mona Sans + Hubot Sans (GitHub, OFL): wght/wdth/slnt(+opsz) + 8 stylistic sets. Worth adding because it tells the composer WHICH fonts make the feature-animation snippet actually fire (most Google fonts ship no ssXX/swsh), turning the technique from theoretical to usable.
backlog.license: All three OFL (SIL Open Font License 1.1) — permissive, embeddable
backlog.note: Recursive is already referenced for axis work but its ss01-ss20 + dlig feature set is unused. Fraunces and Mona/Hubot are net-new picks. Slot as a small table in print-typography.skill.md (font -> axes -> stylistic sets/swsh) so compose knows which fonts enable the feature-reveal snippet. CDN-safe: Google Fonts (Fraunces, Recursive) + jsDelivr/unpkg for Mona/Hubot.

backlog.name: GraphemeSplit — Intl.Segmenter grapheme-cluster text splitter (a11y-safe primitive)
backlog.what: A zero-dep splitter that wraps each visible grapheme (not code unit) in an inline-block span using `new Intl.Segmenter(undefined,{granularity:'grapheme'})` so emoji surrogate pairs, ZWJ emoji (👨‍👩‍👧‍👦), regional-indicator flags, combining diacritics (é), and Indic/Thai/CJK clusters stay intact as one animatable unit. A11y contract: set `aria-label`=full text on the host element + `aria-hidden='true'` on every glyph span (the GSAP/Splinter pattern) so screen readers announce the word, not 'T-I-T-L-E'. Copy/paste stays intact because the spans hold the real text nodes contiguously (no injected NBSP/markup between them). Falls back to `[...str]`/`split('')` when Segmenter is absent. Worth adding because EVERY vanilla text splitter in the vault (text-wave.js, text-reveal.js, text-ripple.js, encrypted-text.js, and the GSAP-bound SplitReveal) currently uses `text.split('')`/`text[i]`, which mojibake-breaks emoji+CJK and has no copy/paste guarantee — this is the correctness-focused base primitive they should all delegate to.
backlog.license: Intl.Segmenter is a web platform API (no license); the snippet is original vault code (MIT-style)
backlog.note: Net-new: grep for 'Intl.Segmenter|grapheme|granularity' across the vault returns zero hits. Ship as effects/grapheme-split.js exposing a global (e.g. GraphemeSplit.split(el,{type:'grapheme'|'word'})) returning {spans, revert}; then have it back the existing kinetic snippets. Pair the spans with `unicode-bidi:isolate`-safe inline-block + a `data-grapheme` attr for downstream wave/stagger drivers.

backlog.name: autoSplit reflow pattern — ResizeObserver + document.fonts re-split (line-aware)
backlog.what: The pattern GSAP SplitText 3.13 adopted for survivability under line wrapping: when you split into lines (or want wave/stagger to follow the new line boundaries), the split is stale the moment the container resizes narrower or a webfont finishes loading. Fix = revert to the original innerHTML and re-run the split, debounced ~200ms, driven by a ResizeObserver on the host plus a `document.fonts.ready` / 'loadingdone' listener. This is the missing 'survives line wrapping and reflows on resize' half of the question. Net-new because no vault snippet re-splits on resize/font-load — SplitReveal and the effects/text-*.js splitters all split once at init and never reflow, so any line-grouped or width-dependent stagger drifts after a resize or late font swap.
backlog.license: Pattern (not GSAP code) — reimplemented vanilla; original vault code
backlog.note: Fold into the GraphemeSplit primitive as an opt-in {reflow:true} that debounces, calls revert(), re-segments, and re-binds the stagger/wave indices. Honor prefers-reduced-motion (skip the wave, keep static text). Inspired by GSAP's autoSplit; GSAP itself is already in the vault under gsap.skill.md but is a paid-historically/now-free plugin we should NOT depend on for the vanilla path.

backlog.name: Kinetic-text a11y decision tree (split-or-not, aria-label vs duplication)
backlog.what: A short skill-style decision tree codifying when per-glyph splitting is safe and how to keep it screen-reader/copy-paste clean: (1) only split on semantic hosts (h1–h6, p, button) where aria-label is reliably announced — generic divs often aren't; (2) default aria-label-on-host + aria-hidden-on-spans; (3) for cases where aria-label is unreliable, use the duplication fallback (visually-hidden real text node for AT + aria-hidden animated spans); (4) hard rule from current best-practice writeups: 'kinetic text must remain semantically unbroken to AT — never split a div's text into letter nodes without an aria-label'; (5) gate the whole effect behind prefers-reduced-motion. Worth adding as the governing rubric so the vault's many text-animation snippets stop shipping the 'T-I-T-L-E' screen-reader bug.
backlog.license: Editorial/best-practice synthesis (article is reference, not code)
backlog.note: No existing skill covers kinetic-typography a11y (print-typography.skill.md is print, not motion). Could be a small section appended to a typography/kinetic skill rather than a whole new file. The vault's SplitReveal already does aria-label+aria-hidden, so the skill should bless that and extend the duplication-fallback + reduced-motion rules to the vanilla snippets that currently skip them.

backlog.name: @formatjs/intl-segmenter (Intl.Segmenter polyfill)
backlog.what: A spec-compliant polyfill for Intl.Segmenter (grapheme/word/sentence) for the rare legacy engines below Baseline 2024. Relevant only as an optional escape hatch for the grapheme splitter; given Baseline-2024 coverage and a cheap `split('')` fallback, the splitter does not actually need it — flagging it as inspiration/optional rather than a recommended dependency, since it's ~heavy and unnecessary for modern targets.
backlog.license: MIT (FormatJS / Format.JS)
backlog.note: MIT and CDN-loadable, but adding it would contradict the vault's zero-dep ethos for a tiny legacy slice. Recommend NOT bundling; mention only as the fallback option if a target audience genuinely needs pre-2024 browsers. Lower value because the native API + split('') fallback already covers ~all real traffic.

backlog.name: Container-relative fluid type scale (cqi + modular-scale clamp)
backlog.what: A fluid type scale whose clamp() preferred value is driven by container inline size (cqi) instead of viewport (vw), combined with a modular-scale ratio that compounds each step from a base (--type-ratio, e.g. 1.25 major-third / 1.33 perfect-fourth). Pattern: `font-size: clamp(max(1rem, var(--fs) - var(--fs)*var(--fs-diff)), var(--fs-fluid, 5cqi), var(--fs))` wrapped in `@supports (font-size: 1cqi)` with a `vw` fallback so it degrades gracefully where containers aren't set. Worth adding because the vault's fluid-type.css is viewport-vw-only: a card heading in a 360px sidebar and the same component in a 900px main column get identical vw-driven sizes today, which is component-incorrect. cqi makes each instance size to its OWN container — exactly the right behavior for a snippet library whose blocks get dropped into arbitrary layouts. Net-new: cq-text-responsive only does stepped @container breakpoints (jumps), and deck.css cqw is slide-only; neither is a smooth, ratio-based, drop-in scale.
backlog.license: Inspiration/technique (ModernCSS article by Stephanie Eckles); reimplement as zero-dep CSS
backlog.note: Reimplement as fluid-type-cq.css: a parallel set of .ct-* classes (or upgrade fluid-type.css with a cqi @supports block). Ship the @supports vw->cqi fallback so it never breaks the verify gate when a block has no container ancestor. Pairs with the existing container-queries.css container-type setup.

backlog.name: Optical (size-aware) letter-spacing — tracking that auto-tightens as type grows
backlog.what: A one-line, zero-dep CSS rule that makes letter-spacing tighten automatically as font-size increases, mimicking how real display cuts (e.g. SF Pro Display vs Text) ship tighter tracking: `letter-spacing: clamp(-0.05em, calc((1em - 1rem) / -10), 0em)`. Because it's em-relative against the constant rem root, the value grows negative only as a heading gets larger, so big display type tightens while body text stays at 0 — no JS, no per-class tracking tokens. Pair it with `font-optical-sizing: auto` (default-on for fonts with an opsz axis like Roboto Flex / Source Serif 4 / Fraunces) so the glyph shapes ALSO refine at display sizes, giving a true display-vs-text optical treatment from a single fluid size. This is the missing coupling: the vault's variable-fonts.css animates wght/wdth/slnt on hover but never controls opsz or couples tracking to size, and its tracking-* classes are fixed manual steps. Strongly net-new and on-scope ('size-aware tracking', 'opsz coupling').
backlog.license: Inspiration/technique (Cloud Four, Tyler Sticka); reimplement as zero-dep CSS
backlog.note: Ship as utility classes: .track-optical (auto-tightening clamp) + .opsz-auto/.opsz-display (font-optical-sizing + explicit opsz via font-variation-settings for fonts that need manual coupling). Document that it shines on display/hero headings; guard with @supports. Natural companion to candidate #1 in a single fluid-display snippet.

backlog.name: Utopia Core (utopia-core / utopia-core-scss)
backlog.what: Framework-agnostic JS/TS (and SCSS port) that programmatically generates the clamp() strings behind utopia.fyi: calculateClamp(minWidth,maxWidth,minSize,maxSize), calculateTypeScale() and calculateSpaceScale() return objects with ready clamp values, given min/max screen widths + min/max base sizes + a ratio. Useful as the build-time generator that produces the fluid scale Design Book ships — so the vault snippet emits exact, verified clamp() literals (zero runtime dep) instead of hand-tuned 'magic numbers'. Relevant because it can output cqi-based clamps too (swap the unit), making it the canonical engine behind candidate #1's scale. Best treated as inspiration/build-tool: Design Book composes deterministically and ships vanilla CSS, so the generated values get baked in rather than calling Utopia at runtime.
backlog.license: MIT (trys/utopia-core, James Gilyead & Trys Mudford)
backlog.note: npm view was network-blocked in sandbox; license/maintenance confirmed from repo + known project (MIT, active). NOT a runtime CDN dep for the vault — use the calculation logic to generate the clamp() literals that get hard-coded into the fluid-type-cq snippet. Cite as 'the math behind' the scale.

backlog.name: Fluid type & optical-tracking skill (decision tree: vw vs cqi, ratio choice, opsz/tracking coupling)
backlog.what: A .skill.md that codifies WHEN to reach for each fluid-type tool so composition stays coherent: choose vw clamp for page-level rhythm vs cqi clamp for reusable components (drop-in blocks); pick a modular ratio by genre (1.2 minor-third editorial, 1.25 major-third marketing, 1.333+ perfect-fourth/expressive display); turn on font-optical-sizing:auto + the auto-tightening letter-spacing only on display/hero tiers, never body; and always wrap cqi in @supports with a vw fallback so the headless-Chrome save-gate never sees collapsed text. OddBird's Feb-2025 'Reimagining Fluid Typography' adds the rationale (avoid pixel math, anchor to user prefs/root). This ties the two new snippets + Utopia math into one decision tree the engine can consult, like the existing print-typography/design-system skills but specifically for fluid+kinetic sizing.
backlog.license: N/A (vault-authored skill; sources MIT/article-CC)
backlog.note: Sits alongside print-typography.skill.md and design-system.skill.md, which mention type scale/tracking but give no vw-vs-cqi guidance and no opsz/size-aware-tracking rule. Keeps the additive-only policy: it directs use of the new cqi + optical-tracking snippets without altering fluid-type.css.

backlog.name: Seedable multi-variant text-decode (mulberry32-driven decode.js)
backlog.what: A zero-dep TextDecode snippet that wraps a tiny seeded PRNG (mulberry32, ~5 lines) so the scramble/decode is fully deterministic from a seed, plus a `variant` switch the vault lacks: 'sweep' (directional left-to-right / right-to-left / center-out reveal), 'cascade' (per-line staggered decode using line-split), and configurable glyph banks. Net-new because all four existing vault decode effects (text-scramble.js, text-reveal.scramble, encrypted-text.css, buttons-text-fx.js) call Math.random() directly — none can reproduce the same frame-by-frame char sequence twice, which conflicts with the engine's reproducible-compose seed semantics noted in memory. Same seed -> identical decode every render; the verify/screenshot gate then becomes stable for any decode-bearing hero.
backlog.license: Public domain (mulberry32 by Tommy Ettinger / bryc) + vault-authored

backlog.name: Glyph-set decode banks (katakana / binary / hex / symbols / braille)
backlog.what: A small named-glyph-bank module exposing katakana (アイウエオカキ…), binary (01), hex (0-9A-F), box-drawing/symbols (!<>-_\/[]{}=+*), and braille blocks as swappable `chars` presets for the decode effect. Motion's ScrambleText ships exactly these presets as the modern reference, but it is Motion+ paywalled and React-only (inspiration, not a dep). The vault's encrypted-text.css has aesthetic skins (cyber/acid/matrix) but no actual alternate glyph alphabets — every JS decoder uses one Latin/symbol pool. Banks give cyberpunk/terminal/data-stream flavor variety cheaply and pair with candidate #1's seed for reproducibility.
backlog.license: N/A (glyph data) — reference impl is Motion+ proprietary, reimplement

backlog.name: Glitch-block reveal (clip-path / mask mosaic decode)
backlog.what: A reveal where text resolves through animated rectangular blocks/mosaic tiles (clip-path inset or mask grid sweeping across) rather than per-character glyph cycling — a structurally different decode flavor from the vault's char-cycle scramble. Distinct from effects/glitch.css and glitch-burst.css (those are RGB-split/chromatic jitter, not a block-by-block reveal) and from transitions-pro's page-level blocks/mosaic (that's a full-screen overlay, not in-place text reveal). Seedable via #1 for which blocks fire when. Adds a 'data-decrypt over a grid' aesthetic common in 2025 cyberpunk/hacker UIs.
backlog.license: Reimplement (CodePen demos are reference only)

backlog.name: GlitchedWriter
backlog.what: MIT, zero-dependency, ~4.5KB, CDN-on-jsDelivr decode/scramble engine with named presets (Nier, terminal, typewriter, encrypted, bitbybit, cosmic, zalgo), ghost chars, queued multi-text, and step/finish callbacks. Best used as INSPIRATION / a reimplementation reference for the preset taxonomy and ghost-char behavior rather than a shipped dep: it is lightly maintained (133 stars, last activity modest) and still uses non-seeded randomness, so it does not satisfy the deterministic-compose requirement on its own. Mine its preset list and 'ghost letter' technique into the seedable snippet above.
backlog.license: MIT

backlog.name: Kinetic type poster / motion-wordmark page genre
backlog.what: A NET-NEW compose-genre archetype: a type-as-the-product page where the wordmark/statement IS the layout. Deterministic section sequence: (1) oversized variable-font hero wordmark (clamp() to ~14-22vw, fills viewport), (2) scroll-scrubbed axis-morph statement (wght/wdth/opsz interpolate as you scroll), (3) big-statement editorial manifesto blocks with generous negative space + numbered pacing, (4) marquee band (looping big type), (5) giant footer wordmark. Sane defaults: pick an expressive variable head font (Fraunces opsz/SOFT or Bricolage/Roboto Flex wght+wdth — all already importable), aesthetic=editorial or energetic, sparse palette, blessed durations. Crucially it ships a REDUCED-MOTION STATIC VARIANT: morph collapses to a fixed heavy/condensed instance, marquee freezes, all glyphs render at final axis values — so it passes verification (no overflow on the oversized hero, coherence score, imagery floor met by type-as-imagery). The vault has every kinetic-type PRIMITIVE (variable-fonts.css, variable-font-cursor, marquee, scroll-text-zoom, split-text, big-stretch, giant-wordmark footer, oversized-hero heading) but NO genre that bundles them into one composable type-forward archetype with verification-passing defaults. This is the high-leverage assembly: it turns ~8 scattered snippets + a font-pair + a taste preset into one `structure.skill.md` archetype (#6) with a section-sequence + section-frames + a structure.html demo, exactly like the existing SaaS/agency/restaurant references.
backlog.license: n/a (assembled from existing MIT/permissive vault parts + OFL/Apache fonts)
backlog.note: Genre/archetype gap, not a single effect. Agency archetype mentions 'huge type statement' but is portfolio-goal-driven, not type-as-the-whole-page. Add as structure.skill.md archetype #6 (e.g. 'type-poster / wordmark / brand-statement') + a structure/ demo page. Reduced-motion static variant is the verification-pass requirement (oversized hero must not overflow; type counts toward the imagery floor).

backlog.name: CSS scroll-driven font-variation-settings axis morph (animation-timeline: scroll()/view())
backlog.what: Pure-CSS, zero-JS scroll-scrubbed variable-font morphing: an @keyframes block animates `font-variation-settings` ('wght'/'wdth'/'opsz') and the element binds it to scroll via `animation-timeline: view()` + `animation-range: contain` (or a named scroll-timeline). The browser interpolates axis values on the compositor as you scroll — no scroll listener, no rAF, no jank. This is the load-bearing 'scroll-scrubbed axis morph' primitive for the poster genre. NET-NEW because the vault's scroll-scrub.js (and every other scroll/* file) is a JS scroll-listener implementation; there is currently NO `animation-timeline`/`scroll()`/`view()` anywhere in the vault. Ships as a vanilla-snippet (e.g. typography/scroll-axis-morph.css) with: a `@supports (animation-timeline: view())` guard so non-supporting browsers (it landed Chrome 115+/FF 110+/Safari 18 Sep-2024) fall back to the `from` keyframe (a 'reasonable static fallback' per the author); a `@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)` block that disables the timeline and pins the final axis instance. Performant, accessible, degrades gracefully — exactly the vault's house style.
backlog.license: CSS web standard (no dep / public technique)
backlog.note: Confirmed absent: grep for animation-timeline/scroll-timeline/view()/scroll() across INDEX.md + scroll/*.css/*.js returns nothing — all existing scroll effects are JS listeners. Add the @supports + prefers-reduced-motion guards so it satisfies the save-gate (static fallback = the genre's reduced-motion variant). Doubles as a general-purpose zero-JS scroll-driven CSS capability beyond the poster genre.

backlog.name: Editorial type-poster palette + taste preset ('big-statement / brutalist-editorial')
backlog.what: A named TastePresets bundle + matching sparse palette tuned for the type-poster genre, so the genre composes coherently at seed 0. The 2025-2026 type-forward look is high-contrast and near-monochrome: paper/off-white or ink-black ground, ONE saturated accent (the only color on the page), an expressive variable head font (Fraunces opsz, Bricolage Grotesque wght+wdth, or Roboto Flex), tight tracking, oversized scale. Binds {aesthetic: editorial-or-energetic, palette: the new 2-3 color 'ink+paper+1-accent' set, fontPair: a variable-axis head pairing, motion: restrained/blessed-durations, density: airy, house: oversized-hero + marquee + giant-wordmark footer, avoid: gradients-as-only-color / stock photos}. This is the 'cohesion glue' that makes the poster genre look intentional rather than like a pile of type effects — same role TastePresets already plays for the other genres. Net-new as a preset+palette PAIR scoped to type-forward layouts; the existing presets (clean-saas, luxury-noir, editorial, playful-pop, dev-tool) don't target a near-monochrome type-poster.
backlog.license: n/a (color set + preset config)
backlog.note: Lower value than the genre/technique because the building blocks (Fraunces import, editorial aesthetic, font-pairing system) already exist — this is the named bundle + sparse palette that makes the poster genre's seed-0 defaults coherent and verification-passing. Reuse blessed durations; keep accent count to one to clear the coherence anti-slop checks.

backlog.name: Exotic-axis OFL variable-font palette (curated kinetic-type catalog: Fraunces SOFT/WONK, Recursive CASL/MONO/CRSV, Roboto Flex GRAD/YOPQ/YTAS, Sixtyfour BLED/SCAN)
backlog.what: A named, palette-style reference asset cataloging permissive (OFL) CDN-hosted variable fonts by their UNUSUAL animatable axes, each with the exact font-variation-settings tag + safe range + a suggested kinetic morph. e.g. Fraunces 'SOFT' 0->100 (sharp->inky) + 'WONK' 0->1; Recursive 'CASL' 0->1 (linear->casual), 'MONO' 0->1 (sans<->mono), 'CRSV' cursive; Roboto Flex parametric 'GRAD'/'YOPQ' thin-stroke/'YTAS' ascender; plus full Google-Fonts CDN <link> + jsDelivr/Fontsource woff2 paths. Worth adding because the vault's variable-font snippets only ever touch wght/wdth/slnt and lean on Inter/Recursive/Roboto Flex generically -- this turns the engine's existing axis-animation machinery (vf-custom, variable-font-cursor) loose on genuinely expressive axes with zero license risk, exactly the 'palette-like curated list' the angle calls for.
backlog.license: SIL OFL 1.1 (all listed fonts)
backlog.note: Partial overlap: Recursive/Roboto Flex are NAMED in effects/variable-font-cursor.css and typography/variable-fonts.css, and Fraunces is a static head font in taste/aesthetic.css -- but their exotic axes (CASL/MONO/CRSV/GRAD/SOFT/WONK/YOPQ/YTAS) are never catalogued or animated. Net-new = the curated axis-tag+range+morph table, not the font names. CASL appears only as a placeholder CSS example with no font behind it.

backlog.name: Sixtyfour (CRT variable font with BLED + SCAN axes)
backlog.what: Monospaced pixel/CRT display variable font by Jens Kutilek (Commodore 64 / Amiga lineage) with two genuinely novel animatable axes: 'SCAN' (size of horizontal scanlines) and 'BLED' (horizontal pixel bleed from CRT phosphor latency). Animating BLED/SCAN via font-variation-settings produces a real CRT-warmup / signal-degradation kinetic effect that is impossible to fake with the vault's existing scanline SHADER overlays (those composite lines OVER text; here the glyphs themselves bleed and gain raster lines). A distinctive, net-new retro-display headline option behind a CDN <link>.
backlog.license: SIL OFL 1.1
backlog.note: Vault has CRT/scanline EFFECTS (effects/text-flicker.css crt, blocks/loaders-text.css scanline, 3d hologram scanline overlay) but NO CRT font and nothing that animates per-glyph raster bleed. Distinct integration: load font + a vf-morph keyframe on BLED/SCAN behind a need()-guard, degrades to monospace fallback if the axis is unsupported.

backlog.name: Nabla (COLRv1 color variable font: depth + highlight axes)
backlog.what: Chromatic isometric color display font (Arthur Reinders Folmer / Just van Rossum) built in the COLRv1 gradient color-font format, with animatable 'depth' (3D extrusion) and 'highlight' (gradient light angle) axes -- so a single <h1> can morph from flat to extruded 3D lettering, gradient-shaded, with no images and no WebGL. Net-new because the vault has zero color-font (COLRv1) coverage; its '3D text' today is CSS stacked-shadows (text-pack-2 retro-3d) or three.js. A COLRv1 font gives crisp, resolution-independent, animatable 3D/gradient type as plain text -- great for the kinetic-type genre and cheap relative to a shader.
backlog.license: SIL OFL 1.1
backlog.note: No COLRv1 / color-font asset anywhere in vault (grep for COLRv1/color font = empty). Caveat: the gradient/color (CPAL/COLR) layers are NOT all exposed as named OpenType variation axes in every browser -- depth/highlight animate via font-variation-settings, but full color control may need @font-palette-values; document the supported-axis subset and provide a flat fallback. Worth pairing with Rocher Color (bevel/shadow axes) and Foldit as alternates in the same color-font note.

backlog.name: Spark (@sparkjsdev/spark) — splat-viewer.js cdn-snippet
backlog.what: Advanced 3D Gaussian-splatting renderer for THREE.js (World Labs, MIT). Add as a NEW 3d/splat-viewer.js cdn-snippet behind a need()-guard, exactly mirroring the existing 3d/model-viewer.js pattern (lazy module load, the exact CDN
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: YES — right choice. Reuses scene-runner + model-viewer's exact need()-guard/poster pattern; SplatMesh is a THREE.Object3D subclass so it composes with all existing 3d/ helpers. Only friction: ESM/importmap loading differs from the vault's window.THREE global-script convention, so the guard and the 3d-web.skill.md note must call that out.

backlog.name: mkkellogg/GaussianSplats3D
backlog.what: The older, foundational MIT THREE.js splat renderer that introduced the streaming .ksplat format (WASM-sorted, classic global-friendly). Worth a one-line mention in the same 3d-web.skill.md splat section as the conservative alternative when a project is on the vault's classic window.THREE global-script path rather than ESM/importmap. Not a second snippet — Spark is the better default (newer, .spz mobile-first, GPU shader-graph, skeletal/dynamic, World Labs backing); GaussianSplats3D is the fallback to name so the skill's decision tree is honest about the ESM-vs-global tradeoff.
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: Add as a one-line alternative in the splat section of 3d-web.skill.md, not as a separate snippet. Its value over Spark is mainly compatibility with the classic global-script loading style.

backlog.name: shi3z/sparkjs-skill (Spark decision-tree skill)
backlog.what: An existing community SKILL.md documenting Spark's API surface (loading splats, SplatMesh, SparkRenderer, formats). Useful feedstock when authoring the vault's own splat section inside 3d-web.skill.md — confirms the public API shape (url/onLoad/onProgress/initialized, format list) so the decision tree and the splat-viewer.js snippet stay accurate. Inspiration/reference only; vendor the prose understanding, not the file (verify its license before copying any text).
backlog.license: Unknown — verify before copying any text
backlog.note: Reference feedstock for writing the vault's own splat decision-tree entry in 3d-web.skill.md. Do not vendor the file without a license check; treat as inspiration.

backlog.name: scene-runner-webgpu.js (WebGPURenderer + TSL bootstrap with auto WebGL2 fallback)
backlog.what: A second, API-compatible variant of the vault's existing 3d/scene-runner.js that boots THREE.WebGPURenderer (from the 'three/webgpu' ESM entry via importmap) instead of WebGLRenderer. It exposes the same SceneRunner.create(target, {onTick, onResize, destroy}) contract so all existing scene callbacks port over unchanged, but: (1) it is async (await renderer.init()) and drives the loop with renderer.setAnimationLoop instead of raw rAF; (2) it carries a need()-guard for window.THREE.WebGPURenderer + an explicit `if(!navigator.gpu) renderer = new THREE.WebGPURenderer({forceWebGL:true})` branch so machines without WebGPU degrade cleanly to the WebGL2 backend rather than throwing. WebGPURenderer ALREADY falls back to WebGL2 internally, so the snippet is genuinely small — the value is the vault-shaped wrapper (importmap doc, async init, fallback flag, dispose). Worth adding because every current 3d/ snippet hardcodes the legacy WebGLRenderer; this future-proofs the category and is the prerequisite for TSL node-material and GPU-compute snippets. Net-new: the vendored claude-skills/webgpu-threejs-tsl/ is agent KNOWLEDGE (docs + example fragments), not a runnable, fallback-guarded snippet in 3d/.
backlog.license: MIT (three.js, mrdoob)
backlog.note: Requires the ESM importmap pattern (three -> three.webgpu.js, three/tsl -> three.tsl.js on jsdelivr/unpkg), which differs from the vault's UMD window.THREE convention — the snippet should document both and the need()-guard must check for THREE.WebGPURenderer specifically. WebGPU is production across Chrome 113+, Firefox 141+, Safari 26 as of late 2025.

backlog.name: TSL node-material snippet (e.g. tsl-animated-material.js)
backlog.what: A small zero-GLSL material snippet built on three/tsl: drive material.colorNode / positionNode with TSL nodes (color, time, oscSine, uv, mix, positionLocal) so a mesh animates its shading in plain JavaScript with no GLSL strings. Sits on top of the scene-runner-webgpu variant above and degrades automatically because TSL compiles to BOTH WGSL (WebGPU) and GLSL (WebGL2 fallback). Net-new vs the vault's shaders/ snippets, which are all hand-written GLSL string pairs — TSL is the node-graph alternative that is far more composable and is the 2025 idiom Codrops/award studios now use. Adds a JS-authored shader path the vault currently lacks entirely as a runnable snippet (only present as agent docs).
backlog.license: MIT (three.js)
backlog.note: Caveat to encode in the snippet: not every TSL feature maps identically to the GLSL/WebGL2 fallback — keep the snippet to widely-supported nodes (color/time/uv/mix/osc) so it renders the same on both backends. Verify via forceWebGL:true in the vault's headless-Chrome gate.

backlog.name: TSL GPU-compute particle snippet (compute() storage-buffer particles)
backlog.what: A compute-shader particle snippet using TSL storage buffers + compute_init/compute_update functions that run per-vertex physics (spring/friction/noise) entirely on the GPU — the thing WebGLRenderer fundamentally cannot do without CPU round-trips. Codrops (Jul 2025) ships exactly this as a current creative-web technique. This is the payoff that the scene-runner-webgpu variant unlocks: a class of high-particle-count, GPU-driven effects the vault has no equivalent of (3d/particles-galaxy.js is a static CPU-built point cloud). It must guard: compute has NO WebGL2 fallback, so the snippet should detect navigator.gpu and either run compute or swap to a reduced CPU/instanced fallback effect, honoring the vault's degrade-gracefully law.
backlog.license: MIT (three.js); Codrops tutorial is reference/inspiration (reimplement, do not copy)
backlog.note: Compute shaders are WebGPU-only — unlike TSL render materials they do NOT degrade to WebGL2. The snippet must feature-detect navigator.gpu and provide a non-compute fallback (e.g. fall back to particles-galaxy.js) so it still renders on WebGL-only browsers and passes the verification gate. Flag in the snippet header.

backlog.name: GPUComputationRenderer curl-noise flow-field particles (WebGL FBO ping-pong)
backlog.what: A vanilla zero-config snippet that simulates 100k-1M points entirely on the GPU using three.js's bundled GPUComputationRenderer (jsm/misc, MIT). Two DataTexture variables (position + velocity) ping-pong through GLSL fragment shaders; the velocity sim is driven by a clean-room curl-noise flow field (gradient-of-simplex curl, NOT cabbibo/glsl-curl-noise which the vault correctly flags as unlicensed). Each texel = one particle's state; a THREE.Points render pass samples the position texture in its vertex shader. This is the dramatic net-new hero the question asks for: the vault's particles-galaxy.js is a static CPU point cloud rotated as a rigid body, with no simulation. GPUComputationRenderer is the maximally-compatible path (works on the WebGL build the vault already pins, ~98% device support) and is the natural companion to the existing scene-runner.js bootstrap.
backlog.license: MIT (GPUComputationRenderer is part of three.js examples/jsm)
backlog.note: Curl noise must be reimplemented clean-room: vault's shaders.skill.md explicitly flags cabbibo/glsl-curl-noise as 'no license' and LYGIA as Prosperity/non-commercial. Use stegu/webgl-noise (MIT, already approved in vault) as the simplex base and compute curl from finite-difference gradients. CDN snippet behind a need()-guard for window.THREE; degrades by capping count if FloatType render targets are unsupported.

backlog.name: TSL compute particles (WebGPU) — instancedArray + compute() + Fn
backlog.what: The modern WebGPU path the vault's skills name as 'the 2026 direction' but never operationalize. Uses Three Shading Language: instancedArray(count, 'vec3') creates a persistent GPU storage buffer (no per-frame upload), a compute Fn() updates each particle by instanceIndex with a curl/flow-field force, and the same buffer feeds a points/instanced render node — no FBO texture-packing gymnastics. Hits 1M+ points comfortably. WebGPURenderer auto-falls-back to WebGL (forceWebGL), so the snippet stays robust. Net-new because no vault skill mentions a single concrete TSL compute API (no instancedArray/compute/Fn/WebGPURenderer/three.webgpu anywhere) — they only point at the external webgpu-claude-skill and Wawa Sensei course. Packagable as one .html with an importmap to three.webgpu.js + three/tsl.
backlog.license: MIT (official three.js webgpu examples + TSL core)
backlog.note: Requires three.webgpu.js build + importmap (three/tsl, three/webgpu) — heavier than the vault's classic three.min.js global. Ship as a sibling 'webgpu' variant with WebGL fallback via WebGPURenderer({forceWebGL:true}); the GPUComputationRenderer snippet above is the safe default. Use TSL mx_noise / a clean-room curl Fn for the flow field, not LYGIA.

backlog.name: gpgpu-particles.skill.md — GPU-simulated particle decision tree
backlog.what: A short decision-tree skill that routes 'GPU-simulated / flow-field / 1M particle' requests: choose WebGL GPUComputationRenderer (max compatibility, the vault's pinned build) vs WebGPU TSL compute (cleaner, future-default, needs fallback); the FBO ping-pong mental model (state-in-texture, even/odd swap, FloatType caveats); curl-noise-from-gradient math; and crucially the license guardrails the vault already half-states scattered across shaders.skill.md/3d-motion.skill.md (avoid glsl-curl-noise/LYGIA, use webgl-noise/mx_noise). This consolidates knowledge currently split across three skills and turns 'GPGPU flow fields: see 3d-motion.skill.md' (a dangling pointer with no runnable target) into an actionable path tied to the two snippets above.
backlog.license: N/A (vault-authored skill; cites MIT three.js + permissive sources)
backlog.note: Partial overlap: 3d-motion.skill.md and shaders.skill.md already mention GPGPU flow fields as inspiration and list external repos (r3f-flow-field-particles, Particle-Curl-Noise, Three.js Journey lesson). Net-new value is the WebGL-vs-WebGPU routing + concrete TSL APIs + a vault-internal runnable snippet to point at, rather than only external/paid references.

backlog.name: rapier3d-compat (dimforge) — vanilla three-physics.js rigid-body helper
backlog.what: Rust/WASM 3D physics engine with a base64-inlined WASM ESM bundle (rapier.mjs, single self-contained file — no separate .wasm fetch, no MIME/CORS headaches). Net-new because the vault's ONLY physics is 2D DOM gravity.js (AABB, 'matter-js-lite') + spring.js + force-directed chart-network.js, and every 3d/ snippet is purely visual (particles, waves, morphs, raycast-hover) — there is zero real 3D rigid-body simulation. A tiny ThreePhysics helper would create a RAPIER.World(gravity), wrap meshes with RigidBodyDesc.dynamic()/fixed() + ColliderDesc.cuboid/.ball/.trimesh auto-derived from the mesh geometry, world.step() each tick, then copy body.translation()/body.rotation() onto mesh.position/mesh.quaternion. Unlocks falling/stacking/colliding meshes, drag-to-throw, and a 'dump a pile of boxes' hero that the vault literally cannot do today. Pairs with the existing scene-runner.js + raycast-hover.js for drag interactions.
backlog.license: Apache-2.0
backlog.note: BASE64-EMBEDDED WASM BUNDLE SIZE (the need()-guard question, measured live from jsDelivr 0.19.3): rapier.mjs = 2.24 MB uncompressed, ~814 KB true gzip-9, 836 KB over the wire — one self-contained ESM file, no separate .wasm. need()-guard MUST be async: RAPIER.init() returns a promise (it unpacks the inlined WASM at runtime), so the helper inits lazily and degrades gracefully if RAPIER is absent, exactly like scene-runner.js's window.THREE guard. CDN: load via importmap from esm.sh/skypack/jsdelivr — AVOID unpkg (serves the CJS build, breaks). This is BANDWIDTH-HEAVY (~0.8 MB gzip) so it belongs behind a need()-guard / opt-in genre, not a default include. NON-DUPLICATIVE vs the skill docs: Rapier is currently named in the vault ONLY as a React @react-three/rapier recommendation (3d-web.skill.md / 3d-motion.skill.md) — there is no vanilla three.js binding snippet and no rigid-body .js anywhere.

backlog.name: Rapier transform-sync tick pattern (translation()/rotation() -> mesh.position/.quaternion)
backlog.what: The canonical render-loop bridge between a physics solver and three.js: after world.step(), iterate the body<->mesh pairs and copy the rigid body's translation() (Vector3) onto mesh.position and rotation() (Quaternion {x,y,z,w}) onto mesh.quaternion. This is the small but load-bearing technique that makes a vanilla helper work; it generalizes to any future solver. Worth capturing as a documented pattern (in 3d-web.skill.md or a new physics note) so the engine knows the 'fixed-timestep step + per-frame copy + interpolation' shape and the gotcha that Rapier quaternions are plain {x,y,z,w} objects (use mesh.quaternion.set(...), not .copy of a THREE.Quaternion).
backlog.license: Apache-2.0
backlog.note: Documents HOW to wire the cdn-snippet above; not a separate dependency. Belongs as a short section in 3d-web.skill.md alongside the existing @react-three/rapier note, but covering the vanilla/no-React path the vault is missing. Mention fixed-timestep accumulator to avoid framerate-coupled simulation.

backlog.name: camera-controls.js (vault helper) — vanilla orbit + critically-damped lerp + fitToBounds
backlog.what: A zero-dep helper that reimplements the ESSENTIALS of drei's OrbitControls+Bounds for the vault's own window.THREE scenes: pointer-drag orbit via spherical coords (theta/phi clamp), wheel dolly, two-finger truck, smooth motion via a per-frame critically-damped lerp toward target spherical state, and fitToBounds(object) using new THREE.Box3().setFromObject(obj).getBoundingSphere() then placing the camera at dist = radius / sin(fov/2) along its current direction (rotation-invariant, ideal for orbit) — with an aspect-corrected box variant (fovh = 2*atan(tan(fov/2)*aspect), take max of X/Y) for tight non-square fits. Worth adding because this is LITERALLY the question's 'cleanest vanilla equivalent': it slots straight onto scene-runner's ctx.camera/ctx.renderer.domElement, needs no examples/jsm import and no CDN, and is the missing reusable orbit/frame helper for postprocessing-bloom, scenes-pack, wave-plane, raycast-hover et al. ~120 lines, pure pointer + vector math.
backlog.license: MIT (own implementation; math is public-domain trig)
backlog.note: Net-new: grep of 3d/ found NO orbit/damping/fit logic; scene-runner.js is static-camera only; model-viewer.js hides orbit inside Google's web component (bundles its own three). Should expose a ctx-compatible API e.g. CameraControls.attach(ctx,{damping,minPolar,maxPolar}) + ctx.controls.fitToBounds(obj,{padding}); call .update(dt) in scene-runner's onTick. Frame the bounding SPHERE (not box) for orbit so the fit stays valid at every azimuth.

backlog.name: camera-controls (yomotsu/camera-controls)
backlog.what: The canonical OrbitControls superset and the exact lib drei's <CameraControls> wraps. MIT, v3.1.2 (Nov 2025), actively maintained. Superset of OrbitControls: SmoothDamp inertia (smoothTime/maxSpeed instead of a raw dampingFactor), fitToBox/fitToSphere (the true auto-frame-object the question asks for), dollyToCursor, infinity dolly, programmatic rotateTo/dollyTo/setLookAt with promise-returning transitions, collision detection, and a rest/sleep event model. For the vault it belongs as a need()-guarded cdn-snippet wrapper that detects window.CameraControls (or the ESM install), calls CameraControls.install({THREE}), attaches to ctx.camera, and degrades to scene-runner's static camera if absent — giving Design Book a 'pro' tier above the hand-rolled helper for product/turntable/architecture-walkthrough scenes.
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: Gotcha to encode in the wrapper: it is ESM/peer-three (no UMD global on the main CDN path) and REQUIRES CameraControls.install({THREE}) before construction or it throws at runtime — so it must ride the same importmap pattern postprocessing-bloom.js already documents (three + three/addons/), then be exposed on window. Also a known drei issue (#1801) where fit + orbit fight causing model 'jump' — fit once on load, then hand control to orbit.

backlog.name: Three.js OrbitControls via importmap (three/addons/controls/OrbitControls.js)
backlog.what: The official, lowest-friction answer to 'where did OrbitControls go': it ships only as an examples/jsm addon, imported via the same importmap (three + three/addons/) the vault ALREADY uses in postprocessing-bloom.js. enableDamping=true + dampingFactor + controls.update() in the RAF loop gives the inertial orbit; pair it with the vanilla fitToBounds math (candidate 1) since OrbitControls itself has NO auto-frame. Worth capturing as a short skill note / snippet header in 3d-web.skill.md so the engine stops emitting the (broken) <script src=.../OrbitControls.js> global pattern that the three.js forum is full of failed attempts at — and instead reaches for the addon import + update() + manual target set.
backlog.license: MIT (three.js)
backlog.note: Inspiration/skill, not a new dep — the addon is already reachable through the existing importmap. Captures the 'enableDamping + dampingFactor + controls.update(dt) every frame' contract and the fact that the old examples/js script-tag global was removed (same removal note postprocessing-bloom.js already carries). Naturally folds into the camera-controls.js snippet's header as the 'or use the official addon' alternative.

backlog.name: scroll-camera-rig.js (vanilla Three.js scroll-choreographed camera + reveal timeline)
backlog.what: A zero-dep companion to the vault's SceneRunner that turns a single scrollProgress (0..1) into a cinematic camera fly-through plus keyed object reveals. Build a THREE.CatmullRomCurve3 from an array of waypoint Vector3s, sample camera.position = curve.getPointAt(t) where t is damped (lerp ~5%/frame) document scroll progress, and aim the camera with a look-ahead lookAt(curve.getPointAt(min(t+0.004,1))) so it faces the direction of travel without jitter. A small keyframe table (waypoints at t=0/0.33/0.66/1) cross-fades object opacity/scale/position so meshes 'reveal' as you scroll past their threshold. This is the canonical 'scrollytelling product reveal' / Apple-style scroll page, but as a vanilla rig instead of React. Worth adding because the technique is well-established and CDN-safe, yet the vault's 3d-motion.skill.md currently routes every scroll-camera request to React-only tools (drei ScrollControls/useScroll, theatre-js) — there is no vanilla path. CatmullRomCurve3, getPointAt, getTangentAt and Vector3.lerp are all core Three.js (MIT), so it reimplements cleanly behind the existing `window.THREE` need()-guard.
backlog.license: MIT (technique uses core three.js; reference repos are inspiration only / unlicensed)
backlog.note: Net-new. Pair with SceneRunner.create() and reuse the existing `--scroll-progress` body-var convention that instanced-grid scrollDriven already reads, plus utils/smooth-scroll.js (Lenis-like) for damped input. Distinct from: instanced-grid scrollDriven (maps scroll to a single wave-phase scalar, no camera move), webgl-drag-gallery (OGL image strip on Z, no spline/reveals), macbook-scroll & sticky-scroll-reveal (2D CSS). Reference GitHub repos (ClassOutside/ThreeJS_Camera_Follow_Path, MauryaAnurag/camera-move-path-threejs) carry no clear license — treat as inspiration, not deps.

backlog.name: prefers-reduced-motion 'jump-to-end' fallback for scroll-driven 3D (verification + reduced-motion contract)
backlog.what: A reduced-motion strategy specific to scroll-camera rigs: under (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce), do NOT scrub the camera along the spline. Instead snap the camera directly to each waypoint as its section scrolls into view (discrete IntersectionObserver step, no lerp), reveal objects at full opacity with no fly-in, and stop the look-ahead tangent aim. This preserves the narrative (you still see every product/state) while removing vestibular-triggering continuous camera motion. Every public scroll-camera tutorial surveyed (Builder.io Apple-style, digitalstrategyforce camera-spline guide, the Codrops Theatre.js fly-through) explicitly omits this — it is the vault's signature accessibility/anti-slop differentiator. As a verification capability it extends the headless-Chrome save-gate: assert that with reduced-motion emulated the rig renders the final/each-section composed frame (not a blank mid-spline frame) and that no rAF camera tween runs — catching the common bug where a reduced-motion user lands on an empty stage because the reveal never fired.
backlog.license: n/a (technique + diagnostic)
backlog.note: Net-new for the 3D-scroll case. The vault enforces reduced-motion broadly (motion-profiles zeros durations, model-viewer disables auto-rotate, webgl-drag-gallery goes flat) but has no scroll-camera-specific 'snap-to-waypoints, render every reveal' contract or a gate that screenshots the reduced-motion end-state. Bundle this with the scroll-camera-rig snippet so the rig ships accessible by default.

backlog.name: scrollytelling-3d-reveal compose-genre (pinned-canvas product-reveal page archetype)
backlog.what: A page archetype the engine can compose: a position:sticky full-viewport WebGL canvas (the scroll-camera-rig scene) behind a tall scroll spacer whose sections carry the copy. As you scroll, the pinned 3D camera flies its spline and successive product states/meshes reveal in lockstep with the text panels passing over them — the 'signature scrollytelling product reveal' layout. It composes from existing parts: structure.css for the spacer/section rhythm, the new scroll-camera-rig.js + SceneRunner for the pinned scene, typography reveals (scroll-text-reveal) for the overlaid copy, and an imagery floor satisfied by the live 3D render. High demand (it's the default 'expensive agency landing page' shape) and currently uncomposable end-to-end in vanilla — the vault can pin sticky text/image panels (sticky-scroll-reveal) but cannot choreograph a real 3D camera behind them.
backlog.license: n/a (archetype assembled from MIT/vault parts)
backlog.note: Net-new genre. Depends on candidate 1 shipping. Reuses sticky-pin + structure + scroll-text-reveal; the Codrops 2026 gallery is cited as inspiration for the mood/palette-shift-on-scroll background touch, not as a dep (it's a Z-translated image strip, a different pattern from a spline camera reveal).

backlog.name: Scene3D — vanilla declarative scene-graph mini-DSL (object-tree → THREE.Scene)
backlog.what: A tiny (~150-line) zero-dep builder that walks a plain JS/JSON tree — {type:'mesh', geometry:['box',{w,h,d}], material:{std:{color,metalness,roughness}}, position:[x,y,z], rotation, scale, children:[...]} plus sibling nodes for lights/groups/camera — and recursively emits a real THREE.Scene via a small type registry (geometry-name→THREE.*Geometry, material-name→THREE.*Material). It is the literal vanilla R3F/drei analogue the category is missing: the vault's entire 3d/ dir (scene-runner, scenes-pack, particles-galaxy, wave-plane, etc.) is hand-coded imperative `new THREE.X(); scene.add()` — there is NO data→scene path. This turns 3D into composable data so Design Book can emit/seed/diff/serialize scenes the same deterministic way it composes pages, decks, and diagrams. Pairs with the existing scene-runner.js (which already owns the renderer/camera/RAF/resize loop) — the builder just produces the `scene` object scene-runner mounts. Crucially NOT a dup of three's native ObjectLoader/toJSON (verified: that format uses UUID cross-refs + flattened 4x4 matrices and is explicitly 'not for hand-authoring') — this is the ergonomic, AI-emittable authoring layer ObjectLoader is not, while staying round-trippable to it as a serialization escape hatch.
backlog.license: N/A (vault-authored; targets three.js MIT)
backlog.note: Author into 3d/scene-graph.js (global Scene3D), keep the existing need(window.THREE) CDN guard from scene-runner.js, and add an INDEX.md row 'Declarative 3D scene from a JS/JSON tree → 3d/scene-graph.js + 3d/scene-runner.js'. Must degrade gracefully if THREE is absent (return null), and verify under the headless-Chrome save-gate via a screenshot draw-check (per the vault's WebGL draw-verify memo: drawImage pixel-sampling lies without preserveDrawingBuffer).

backlog.name: drei-vanilla (pmndrs)
backlog.what: Official pmndrs port of drei's helpers to framework-free vanilla three.js: MeshTransmissionMaterial (glass), MeshDistortMaterial/MeshReflectorMaterial, AccumulativeShadows, Caustics, Clouds, Outlines, Billboard, Grid, CameraShake, PCSS soft shadows. These are the exact 'premium look' abstractions that make R3F+drei scenes look expensive — and they're standalone functions, so the Scene3D mini-DSL above can expose them as material/staging node types (e.g. material:{transmission:{...}}) behind a need()-guard. Net-new vs the vault: vault has hand-rolled postprocessing-bloom.js and scenes-pack's shaderBall, but none of drei's transmission/accumulative-shadow/caustics quality. Active (v1.25.0, Sept 2025), MIT, 611 stars.
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: CDN-guarded behind need(); it depends on three (peer) — load three first, then drei-vanilla. Add only the high-value helpers (transmission material, accumulative shadows, outlines) as optional node-type resolvers in the Scene3D registry so a missing CDN degrades to a plain MeshStandardMaterial rather than breaking the scene. Cite in 3d-web.skill.md (already names drei-vanilla in passing — promote it to a real registry hook).

backlog.name: three-elements + Lume (HTML-as-scene-graph convention)
backlog.what: Two prior-art declarative-three projects worth mining as INSPIRATION for the mini-DSL's naming/registry conventions, not as deps. three-elements maps custom HTML tags 1:1 to three classes (<three-mesh> → THREE.Mesh, <three-box-buffer-geometry> → THREE.BoxBufferGeometry, attributes → constructor args/props) — a clean, predictable type-registry scheme to mirror in the JS-object-tree keys. Lume (lume/lume) is the live, more ambitious version (GPU-powered 3D HTML elements). Both prove the declarative-vanilla-three demand and give a battle-tested key/attribute convention so Design Book's DSL feels idiomatic. NEITHER is a good dep: three-elements is dormant (author stepped away, 400 stars), and Lume is a Solid.js-flavored web-component framework that bundles its own three and is far heavier than 'minimal'.
backlog.license: MIT (both)
backlog.note: Use only the element→class mapping convention and attribute-coercion rules as a design reference for the Scene3D registry; do not vendor or CDN either. Lume's alpha versioning + Solid dependency disqualify it as the 'minimal vanilla R3F analogue'. Worth a one-line mention in 3d-web.skill.md's declarative-options table.

backlog.name: data-driven-3d-scene (compose-genre archetype)
backlog.what: A new compose archetype that closes the loop the question is really aiming at: once Scene3D exists, Design Book gains the ability to COMPOSE a 3D scene from a data spec the same way it composes web pages / mobile flows / slide decks / architecture diagrams. The genre = a deterministic generator that emits the {geometry,material,position,children} tree (e.g. a product-hero turntable, a 3D-card stack, a parametric instanced grid, a hero centerpiece + 3-point lighting rig) from palette + taste-preset inputs, hands the tree to Scene3D.build(), mounts via scene-runner, and passes the headless-Chrome coherence/draw-verify gate. This is the structural net-new capability — 3D becomes a first-class composed output, not a hand-coded effect. Currently the vault can only drop pre-baked scenes-pack scenes; it cannot generate a novel scene from data.
backlog.license: N/A (vault-authored)
backlog.note: Depends on the Scene3D snippet (candidate 1) landing first. Wire palette → material colors and taste-preset → lighting/material defaults so 3D inherits the same deterministic seed semantics as compose_page. Verify with the WebGL screenshot draw-check (preserveDrawingBuffer or readPixels, per the vault memo) plus an imagery-floor exemption note since a live canvas isn't a static image.

backlog.name: WebGL context-lost + getError probe (preflight before the pixel-variance gate)
backlog.what: An in-page probe injected via addInitScript that (a) attaches a 'webglcontextlost' listener to every canvas and (b) wraps getContext so that, at assert time, it can call gl.isContextLost() and gl.getError() on each live context. WHY NET-NEW: render-smoke.mjs forces preserveDrawingBuffer and reads R/G/B variance, but a context that was lost AFTER drawing one frame (GPU-process crash, VRAM exhaustion, the ~16-context-per-tab limit) leaves a STALE non-blank buffer that passes the variance check as a false green. The variance gate cannot see a lost context; isContextLost()/getError() is the only signal. Closes the exact 'canvas present but context-lost' hole the angle calls out. Pure WebGL API, no dependency.
backlog.license: n/a (native browser API)
backlog.note: grep of tools/ confirms NO isContextLost/getError usage today. Distinct from the existing preserveDrawingBuffer+variance check (that's already in render-smoke.mjs ASSESS — do not re-add). Add as a preflight gate: fail the family if any context reports lost or a non-NO_ERROR gl.getError() at read time.

backlog.name: WebGL shader compile/link instrumentation (silent-failure catcher)
backlog.what: Wrap HTMLCanvasElement.getContext so the returned WebGL context's compileShader/linkProgram are instrumented: after each, check getShaderParameter(s, COMPILE_STATUS) and getProgramParameter(p, LINK_STATUS); collect getShaderInfoLog/getProgramInfoLog on failure. WHY NET-NEW: a shader that fails to compile draws nothing but the clear color — and if that clear is a gradient/colored clear (common in shader snippets), the per-channel variance check can still pass, vacuously. Capturing compile/link failure gives a deterministic, specific failure reason ('FRAGMENT_SHADER: ...') instead of an ambiguous low-variance number. Spector.js (MIT) proves the wrap-getContext pattern and even ships an AI/MCP capture mode, but its last release is 0.9.x from Sept 2021 (stale) — so vendor the ~30-line probe vanilla rather than the whole lib.
backlog.license: MIT (Spector.js, reference only; the probe itself is native API)
backlog.note: Spector.js itself = INSPIRATION (stale dep), not a CDN add. The vanilla COMPILE_STATUS/LINK_STATUS probe is the addable verification capability. Pairs naturally with #1 in the same addInitScript-wrapped getContext.

backlog.name: WebGPU render verification (adapter / device.lost / error-scope probe)
backlog.what: A WebGPU equivalent of the WebGL gate, because the harness today only instruments 'webgl'/'webgl2'/'experimental-webgl' contexts and is BLIND to 'webgpu' contexts. Probe steps: assert navigator.gpu exists; that the page's requestAdapter() did not return null; that device.lost did not resolve already-on-creation with reason!=='destroyed'; and wrap the device with pushErrorScope('validation')/popErrorScope() to surface validation errors. For 'did it draw', a webgpu canvas can be sampled via the same drawImage->getImageData path (preserveDrawingBuffer is N/A; webgpu canvases are readable by default unless configured otherwise). WHY NET-NEW + HIGH VALUE: the vault has full WebGPU AUTHORING (three.js WebGPURenderer, TSL, claude-skills/webgpu-threejs-tsl) but ZERO WebGPU verification — a WebGPURenderer scene whose adapter request fails or device is lost would slip the gate or only be caught incidentally. This is the single biggest correctness gap as the vault follows the documented '2026 WebGPU/TSL direction'.
backlog.license: n/a (native WebGPU API; toji.dev guide is reference)
backlog.note: Authoring is covered (3d-web.skill.md, shaders.skill.md WebGPU/TSL note, claude-skills/webgpu-threejs-tsl) but VERIFICATION is not.
