Headless note: software WebGPU needs --enable-unsafe-webgpu (and Vulkan/SwiftShader on Linux CI); when WebGPU is genuinely unavailable in the harness env, gate should treat a graceful WebGL fallback as pass, not force-fail.

backlog.name: rAF liveness / FPS-floor sanity (frozen-animation detector)
backlog.what: Count requestAnimationFrame ticks over a fixed ~1s window during the assess phase and require a minimum delta (e.g. >5 ticks) for families that declare themselves animated (shaders/, 3d/). WHY NET-NEW: render-smoke samples 4 frames purely to find the BEST pixel signal — it never checks that the scene is still ANIMATING. A snippet that compiles, draws one good frame, then throws inside its rAF loop (or never re-schedules) reads as a perfect pass on the static frame but is actually frozen for the user. A monotonic rAF-tick count is the cheap liveness signal. Launch Chrome with --disable-background-timer-throttling / --disable-renderer-backgrounding so headless tick counts are stable.
backlog.license: n/a (native API)
backlog.note: Lowest of the four: needs an 'animated vs intentionally-static' classifier (the harness already distinguishes INTERACTIVE_CANVAS/MIC families, so the same exemption list applies). Best added after #1-#3. Not the same as the existing multi-frame best-signal sampling, which measures pixels, not motion.

backlog.name: gltf-load.js — vanilla GLTFLoader + Draco/Meshopt/KTX2 CDN-decoder pipeline (with onProgress + poster)
backlog.what: A zero-config helper that dynamic-import()s three's ESM addons from CDN — GLTFLoader, DRACOLoader (setDecoderPath -> jsm/libs/draco/), KTX2Loader (setTranscoderPath -> jsm/libs/basis/ + detectSupport(renderer)), and MeshoptDecoder (setMeshoptDecoder, await .ready) — wires all three into one loader, then exposes load(url, {onProgress, onLoad, onError}) with a poster placeholder until ready. Net-new because the vault's model-viewer bundles its OWN uncontrollable loader, and ALL existing Draco/Meshopt guidance is R3F-only (drei useGLTF / gltfjsx) — there is no way today to load a real compressed .glb into the vault's own SceneRunner/scenes-pack three.js scenes. Production glTF is almost always Draco/Meshopt/KTX2-compressed, so without decoders those assets fail to load entirely. Draco + KTX2 run their WASM decode in a Web Worker pool (DRACOLoader workerLimit default 4), so a heavy model decodes off the main thread = no freeze, which is the whole point.
backlog.license: MIT (three.js)
backlog.note: ARCHITECTURE CAVEAT the snippet must encode: GLTFLoader + decoders exist ONLY as ESM addons under three/addons/ (examples/jsm/), NOT on the UMD window.THREE global the vault's 3d/*.js snippets assume. So the snippet must dynamic-import() the addons from CDN (esm.sh/jsdelivr) or ship with an importmap — it cannot read them off window.THREE. SECOND CAVEAT: the decoder CDN path MUST be pinned to the exact same three@x.y.z as the loaded three (version-mismatch is the #1 reported failure; KTX2 .transcodeImage / Draco DecodeArrayToPointCloud errors come from drift). Pair with the existing need()-guard convention so it degrades to the poster if CDN/WebGL is absent. Pin e.g. https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/three@0.180.0/examples/jsm/libs/{draco,basis}/.

backlog.name: Progressive-LOD swap technique (mesh-screen-density LOD, inspired by @needle-tools/gltf-progressive)
backlog.what: The state-of-the-art pattern for loading large models without a long blank wait: ship a tiny initial .glb with embedded low-quality proxies (scene appears immediately), then lazily fetch higher-quality geometry/texture LOD variants on demand based on each mesh's on-screen pixel density (not raw camera distance), and swap them in imperceptibly. Reported 70-95% reduction in perceived load time (e.g. 56 MB -> 300 KB initial + up to 8 MB streamed, most never fetched). needle's runtime is MIT, CDN-available (jsdelivr), and wires into vanilla three with one call useNeedleProgressive(gltfLoader, renderer). This complements gltf-load.js: decoders fix the 'compressed asset won't load / decode freezes the thread' problem; progressive-LOD fixes the 'big asset is a long blank wait' problem.
backlog.license: MIT (runtime library)
backlog.note: FLAG: the runtime is MIT and CDN-safe, BUT GENERATING the LOD variant files requires Needle Cloud (paid SaaS) or Unity/Blender integrations — the vault cannot produce LOD assets deterministically. So treat as INSPIRATION (reimplement the screen-density LOD-swap + low-poly-proxy-first pattern on top of the new gltf-load.js using THREE.LOD), not as a turnkey dep. The reusable, vendor-free version: load a small proxy GLB first, render it, then load the full GLB and swap into the same node (poster -> proxy -> full), all decoding off-thread via the worker pool.

backlog.name: pmndrs/postprocessing (vanilla effects-composer.js)
backlog.what: A single curated vanilla snippet (effects-composer.js) that wraps the pmndrs postprocessing library's EffectComposer + one merged EffectPass with toggleable 'cinematic' effects: DepthOfFieldEffect (bokeh), ChromaticAberrationEffect, VignetteEffect, NoiseEffect (film grain), SSAOEffect, plus SMAAEffect for AA. This is the post-FX stack BEYOND the vault's bloom-only snippet — and it's the look that separates pro 3D from default-flat Three. Crucially, pmndrs merges multiple effects into ONE fullscreen pass (vs three's addons which run a separate framebuffer per pass), so a 5-effect cinematic stack stays cheap. Net-new: the vault only has postprocessing-bloom.js (bloom) and a CSS-filter fake (post-processing.css); it has no WebGL multi-pass composer. Ships behind a need()-guard reading effects off window, with graceful fallback to plain renderer.render() if the library or WebGL2 is unavailable, matching the vault's existing ES-module importmap pattern.
backlog.license: Zlib (permissive, OK)
backlog.note: ES-modules only (no UMD/IIFE) — load via importmap exactly like the existing postprocessing-bloom.js header. Pin three peer version. CDN: unpkg/jsdelivr postprocessing@6.39.x. Degrade: if effects fail to load, fall back to direct render (no composer).

backlog.name: N8AO (N8python) — SSAO pass for the composer
backlog.what: A drop-in, temporally-stable, artist-controllable SSAO pass that is the single biggest cheap realism win for any Three scene — it grounds objects with contact shadows the default flat renderer never produces. It exposes N8AOPass for vanilla three EffectComposer AND N8AOPostPass for the pmndrs postprocessing composer, so it slots cleanly into the same curated effects-composer.js as the 'AO' toggle. Worth adding because SSAO is the one effect pmndrs' built-in SSAOEffect does noticeably worse than N8AO (N8AO has screenSpaceRadius mode, half-res + denoise, and HBAO quality). Net-new: vault only name-drops n8ao in 3d-web.skill.md prose; there is no usable snippet.
backlog.license: CC0 (public domain — zero restrictions)
backlog.note: Ships a real UMD/CDN build: https://unpkg.com/n8ao@latest/dist/N8AO.js (script-tag friendly, unlike pmndrs core). Requires three + (for the Post variant) postprocessing present. Graceful fallback: skip the AO toggle if N8AO global is absent. Recommend aoRadius 16-64px with screenSpaceRadius=true.

backlog.name: realism-effects (0beqz) — SSGI / Motion Blur / TRAA
backlog.what: Higher-end cinematic effects beyond the SSAO/DOF/CA/vignette/grain core: screen-space global illumination (SSGI), screen-space reflections, temporal AA (TRAA), and camera motion blur — the techniques behind 'this can't be real-time WebGL' demos. Best treated as an inspiration/advanced-tier reference for the effects-composer skill rather than a default dependency: it's ESM-only, heavier, and a v2 rewrite is in progress, so it's not ideal for the always-on imagery floor. Useful as the documented 'when you need more than the cheap stack' escalation in the skill's decision tree.
backlog.license: MIT (permissive, OK)
backlog.note: ESM-only, no script-tag UMD; CDN via jsDelivr/npm. Costly (SSGI/TRAA need a VelocityDepthNormalPass) — document as advanced-tier, not the default cinematic preset. Flag v2 instability before pinning as a hard dep.

backlog.name: Procedural IBL via RoomEnvironment + PMREMGenerator (scene.environment)
backlog.what: Three.js ships a built-in RoomEnvironment (a procedural studio-room Scene, no asset) that PMREMGenerator.fromScene() bakes into a prefiltered radiance map. Assigning it to scene.environment instantly gives every MeshStandardMaterial/MeshPhysicalMaterial real roughness-aware reflections and soft fill light — the exact thing the vault's 3d/ scenes lack. A ~12-line procedural-environment.js helper [`const pmrem=new THREE.PMREMGenerator(renderer); scene.environment=pmrem.fromScene(new RoomEnvironment()).texture; pmrem.dispose()`] could be threaded through scene-runner.js so particles-galaxy/cube-morph/instanced-grid/floating-text/raycast-hover all gain credible PBR shading with ZERO external file and one extra addon import. This is the single highest-leverage fix for the documented '#1 AI tell' (flat default metal). Energy-preservation for IBL on metallics landed in recent three releases, so reflections look brighter/correct out of the box.
backlog.license: MIT (three.js core + addons)
backlog.note: RoomEnvironment imports from 'three/addons/environments/RoomEnvironment.js' — same CDN need()-guard pattern the vault already uses for OrbitControls/postprocessing addons. Vault has React drei <Environment> and the <model-viewer> web component's baked IBL, but NEITHER applies to the raw vanilla three.js scenes in 3d/ (all verified to use ad-hoc DirectionalLight/AmbientLight + MeshStandardMaterial with no scene.environment). Net-new for vanilla.

backlog.name: RGBELoader + PMREMGenerator.fromEquirectangular (.hdr IBL path) with CC0 PolyHaven fallback
backlog.what: The 'real photo' upgrade tier: load an equirectangular .hdr via RGBELoader, run it through pmrem.fromEquirectangular(tex).texture, set scene.environment (and optionally scene.background). Critical, non-obvious gotcha worth encoding as a snippet: roughness is BROKEN on a raw equirect .hdr envMap — it ONLY works after PMREM conversion, a bug people hit constantly. A second snippet beside procedural-environment.js would let a scene opt into a CC0 PolyHaven HDRI (vault already references PolyHaven CC0 + ambientCG CC0 in blender.skill.md) for true outdoor/sunset/studio lighting when RoomEnvironment's neutral grey isn't enough. Degrades gracefully: if the .hdr fails to load, fall back to RoomEnvironment.
backlog.license: MIT (RGBELoader/PMREM); HDRI assets CC0 from PolyHaven/ambientCG
backlog.note: blender.skill.md already lists PolyHaven/ambientCG as CC0 HDRI sources but ONLY for the drei <Environment> React path / Blender pipeline — there is no vanilla three.js .hdr→PMREM loader snippet. Pairs as the 'asset-backed' tier above the assetless RoomEnvironment default. Note the must-PMREM-or-roughness-breaks caveat in the snippet header.

backlog.name: Photoreal render config: ACESFilmic/AgX tone mapping + environmentIntensity/environmentRotation
backlog.what: IBL alone isn't enough — without tone mapping and correct color management, an HDR environment clips to washed-out white and still reads as 'AI flat'. The completing config: renderer.toneMapping = THREE.ACESFilmicToneMapping (or AgX, Blender 4's new default, also in three now) + toneMappingExposure tuning, and the newer scene.environmentIntensity (dial IBL strength) / scene.environmentRotation (turn the studio lights to sculpt highlights). outputColorSpace defaults to sRGB in modern three, so the missing pieces are specifically tone mapping + exposure + env intensity/rotation. These ~4 renderer lines belong in scene-runner.js defaults so EVERY vault 3D scene gets cinematic contrast, not just IBL ones.
backlog.license: MIT (three.js core)
backlog.note: Verified absent: no toneMapping/ACESFilmic/AgX/outputColorSpace/environmentIntensity in scene-runner.js or any 3d/ snippet or 3d-web.skill.md. Pure renderer-config, zero new dependency — cleanest as a vanilla default baked into the shared scene-runner so it lifts all scenes at once.

backlog.name: 3d-web.skill.md 'don't-ship-flat-PBR' decision branch (IBL-first staging rule)
backlog.what: A short skill addition codifying the authenticity law for 3D: 'Never light a PBR scene with bare DirectionalLight+AmbientLight — always set scene.environment first (RoomEnvironment procedural by default; CC0 .hdr+PMREM when art direction needs real sky), then add 1-2 lights only for accents.' Plus the decision tree: RoomEnvironment (assetless, instant, neutral studio) vs .hdr/PMREM (asset, slower, scene-specific mood) vs the <model-viewer>'s built-in 'neutral'/'legacy' presets (for the model-viewer primitive only). This turns the two snippets above into a verifiable rule the composer/coherence gate can enforce — the moat is checking that 3D scenes actually set an environment.
backlog.license: n/a (vault content)
backlog.note: Extends existing 3d-web.skill.md rather than a new file. Optionally a 'verification' form: a coherence/diagnostic check that flags any three.js scene rendering MeshStandardMaterial with metalness>0 but no scene.environment set (the literal flat-metal AI tell) — that detection capability is the defensible part.

backlog.name: webxr-scene (vanilla WebXR immersive-ar/vr compose-genre + helper)
backlog.what: A net-new compose-genre + helper that opens a real WebXR session (immersive-ar with hit-test reticle placement, or immersive-vr room-scale) using three.js's own ESM webxr addons (ARButton/VRButton from three/addons/webxr/), with strict feature-detection (navigator.xr + isSessionSupported('immersive-ar')) and a graceful 2D fallback to the vault's existing model-viewer/orbit scene when XR is unsupported. This is the canonical, framework-free pattern: requestSession → requestReferenceSpace('viewer') → requestHitTestSource → per-frame frame.getHitTestResults → reticle.matrix.fromArray(hit.getPose().transform.matrix) → place mesh on controller 'select'. WORTH ADDING because the vault's model-viewer.js only delegates one-tap AR to the OS (ar-modes='webxr scene-viewer quick-look') — it cannot author a custom in-session experience (reticle placement, room-scale, in-AR interaction). This rounds out the immersive lane without duplicating model-viewer, which stays the product-showcase primitive while webxr-scene becomes the custom-experience primitive.
backlog.license: MIT (three.js + its webxr addons)
backlog.note: CDN-SAFE but a different loading model than the vault's 3d/*.js: the webxr addons are ESM-only (three/addons/webxr/ARButton.js, VRButton.js) and require an import map (three.module.js), NOT the UMD window.THREE global the existing 3d/ snippets assume. The helper must self-inject an importmap behind its need()-guard. VERIFICATION caveat for the headless-Chrome save-gate: navigator.xr is absent in headless Chrome, so the gate can only verify the 2D-fallback render path, not a live session — the genre's value is that the fallback is the verified default and XR is progressive enhancement. Browser reality 2026: immersive-ar = Android Chrome/Edge/Samsung Internet + Quest Browser; immersive-vr adds Quest/Galaxy XR/visionOS Safari; iOS/macOS Safari + Firefox have NO WebXR (hence the mandatory 2D fallback).

backlog.name: WebXR DOM Overlay (dom-overlay feature for in-AR 2D HTML UI)
backlog.what: The modern best-practice for rendering interactive 2D HTML/CSS UI INSIDE a handheld immersive-ar session: request the session with requiredFeatures including 'dom-overlay' and domOverlay:{root: document.body}, then a single DOM element composites as a transparent-background topmost layer over the camera passthrough + WebGL. Touch regions use a beforexrselect handler so taps on buttons don't leak through as world hit-tests. WORTH ADDING because it is the bridge that lets the vault's huge existing CSS/UI inventory (buttons, panels, command-palette, sheets) live inside an AR experience — a capability the vault has zero coverage for and that distinguishes a real WebXR genre from model-viewer's OS-handoff. It is the AR-UI counterpart to the in-scene WebGL uikit the 3d-web skill already names for React.
backlog.license: W3C spec / browser-native API (no dependency; pairs with MIT three.js)
backlog.note: Best implemented as a sub-pattern of the webxr-scene helper (a domOverlay option), not a standalone dep — it's a session config flag, not a library. Handheld-AR only (Android Chrome); headsets ignore it, so any UI must also exist as in-scene WebGL/world-locked content for the VR path. Degrades cleanly: outside a session it's just normal DOM.

backlog.name: three.js XR controller + XRHandModelFactory input layer
backlog.what: The hand/controller input half of the immersive lane: renderer.xr.getController(0/1) for ray/grip input + 'select'/'squeeze' events, XRControllerModelFactory for controller meshes, and XRHandModelFactory (spheres/boxes/mesh) for the 25-joint hand skeleton exposed by Quest/Galaxy XR/Vision Pro. WORTH ADDING because the WHY-IT-MATTERS explicitly calls out hand/controller input and room-scale, and the vault has nothing for it — its only spatial-input snippets are device-tilt (gyroscope) and MediaPipe webcam hand-tracking, both 2D-screen effects, not in-headset 6DoF input. This is what makes the immersive-vr branch of webxr-scene actually interactive (grab/point/place) rather than a passive 360 view.
backlog.license: MIT (three.js addons)
backlog.note: ESM addons (three/addons/webxr/XRControllerModelFactory.js, XRHandModelFactory.js) — same import-map requirement as ARButton. Ships as the controller/hand option of the webxr-scene helper rather than a separate snippet. Not verifiable in headless Chrome (no XR device); ships as progressive enhancement on top of the verified 2D fallback. Distinct from the vault's MediaPipe VisionReact (screen-space webcam hands) and device-tilt — those don't give 6DoF in-headset poses.

backlog.name: point-cloud-viewer.js (vanilla three.js PLY/PCD scan viewer with EDL)
backlog.what: A net-new vanilla three.js snippet that loads a real scan (PLY via PLYLoader, PCD via PCDLoader — both OFFICIAL three.js addons available on CDN, zero extra dep) into a THREE.Points cloud with OrbitControls, then renders it with a color-by-height ramp (map point.y to a viridis/turbo gradient via a vertex/fragment shader on PointsMaterial) and an Eye-Dome-Lighting post-process pass. EDL = render scene + depth to a render target, then a fullscreen shader darkens each pixel by how much closer its neighbors are — the screen-space depth-edge shading that makes an unlit, structureless gray scan suddenly read as crisp 3D geometry (walls, curbs, scan lines). This is the single feature that distinguishes a *data* point cloud from particles-galaxy's decorative confetti. Verified: the vault has particles-galaxy.js (decorative spiral), scene-runner.js (camera/raf boilerplate to build on), postprocessing-bloom.js (the EffectComposer pattern EDL reuses), and <model-viewer> for meshes — but ZERO point-cloud DATA viewer, ZERO PLY/PCD load, ZERO EDL, ZERO color-by-height. Directly serves Design Book's architecture-diagram / archviz / survey composing.
backlog.license: MIT (three.js + its PLYLoader/PCDLoader addons; EDL technique is public, reimplementable)

backlog.name: Eye-Dome Lighting (EDL) post-process pass
backlog.what: The EDL shading algorithm itself, packaged as a reusable EffectComposer pass (an EDLPass alongside the vault's existing UnrealBloom usage in postprocessing-bloom.js). EDL is image-based, GPU-only, ~one extra fullscreen pass: for each pixel it compares log-depth to its 4-8 neighbors and attenuates luminance by the summed depth difference, optionally multi-scale (half/quarter res) for softness. It is THE standard for unlit point clouds (CloudCompare, Potree, ParaView, ArcGIS Pro, ESRI all use it) precisely because point clouds have no normals to light. Worth adding as a named, documented pass because it generalizes beyond point clouds — it sharpens depth on ANY structureless geometry (wireframes, voxel fields, dense particle scenes, even the vault's instanced-grid). The canonical three.js PR (mrdoob/three.js#14016, MIT) is an exact reference implementation; it was never merged, so there is no official three.js EDL — making a clean vanilla version genuinely net-new and non-duplicative.
backlog.license: MIT (reference PR) / technique is patent-free, published by Kitware/CEA

backlog.name: @loaders.gl/las (LAS/LAZ + COPC LiDAR loader)
backlog.what: Framework-independent LAS/LAZ parser (the actual LiDAR survey format, not just PLY/PCD) that returns flat typed arrays (position, intensity, classification, color) ready to drop straight into a THREE.BufferGeometry — no framework, works with vanilla three. Lets the viewer ingest genuine LiDAR/survey deliverables and color-by-intensity or color-by-classification (ground/building/vegetation), the standard archviz/GIS modes. Behind a need()-guard it degrades to PLY/PCD when absent. Caveat to document: @loaders.gl/las only handles up to LAS 1.3; pair with copc.js for LAS 1.4 / Cloud-Optimized Point Cloud streaming. This is the bridge from 'demo point cloud' to 'real survey data' and complements (does not duplicate) the PLY/PCD snippet.
backlog.license: MIT

backlog.name: Potree (large-LiDAR octree web renderer)
backlog.what: The reference WebGL renderer for MASSIVE point clouds (billions of points) — built on three.js, ships its own polished EDL, hierarchical octree LOD streaming, measurement/annotation tools, and is BSD-2-Clause (FreeBSD), so license-safe. Note as INSPIRATION, not a CDN dep: it requires preprocessing every cloud through the native PotreeConverter into a proprietary octree (.bin/EPT) format and bundles a heavyweight viewer — wrong fit for a zero-build vanilla-snippet vault, and the lighter @pnext/three-loader fork is stale (last release 2021). Value to Design Book is as the design/UX reference (EDL look, color ramps, navigation, measurement HUD) the lightweight in-vault viewer should imitate, plus the documented escape hatch: 'for >5M points, hand off to Potree'. Distinct from Gaussian splats — splat viewers render learned radiance, not measured survey geometry.
backlog.license: BSD-2-Clause / FreeBSD (permissive)

backlog.name: glass-material.js — vanilla MeshPhysicalMaterial transmission glass (native, zero-dep)
backlog.what: A drei-equivalent glass material snippet built ONLY on stock three.js MeshPhysicalMaterial. As of r164 (well before the vault's pinned three), MeshPhysicalMaterial natively supports the full glass parameter set the question asks about: transmission (0-1), thickness, ior, attenuationColor/attenuationDistance (tinted absorption), roughness (frosted/transmission-roughness blur), AND a native dispersion property (per-channel chromatic split, the diamond/prism look) — no custom shader required. Three.js auto-allocates the internal transmission render target when any material has transmission>0, so refraction of the scene behind the object works out of the box. A GlassMaterial.preset(name) factory (clear-glass ior1.5, frosted, diamond ior2.42+dispersion, tinted-bottle, acrylic) plugs straight into the existing 3d/scenes-pack + scene-runner material slot, which today only uses MeshStandardMaterial/MeshBasicMaterial. This is the single highest-leverage 3D add: the signature 'expensive' product-hero look (perfume bottles, glass logos, liquid orbs) with zero new dependencies and full save-gate compatibility.
backlog.license: MIT (three.js itself; snippet is original vault code)
backlog.note: NOT covered: vault 'glass' (effects/glassmorphism.css, effects/glass-refraction.css) is all 2D CSS backdrop-filter/chromatic-aberration; 3d/ scenes use only MeshStandardMaterial/MeshBasicMaterial; 3d-web.skill.md only name-drops drei's MeshTransmissionMaterial as a React concept with no vanilla snippet. CAVEAT: transmission/dispersion look flat without an env-map to refract — pair with the RoomEnvironment helper below so it is self-contained and verifiable (no HDR asset needed).

backlog.name: RoomEnvironment + PMREMGenerator — no-asset procedural studio env-map helper
backlog.what: A tiny helper (3d/studio-env.js) that builds a procedural PMREM environment texture from three's bundled RoomEnvironment example module — a softbox-lit room with no external HDR/image asset. Assigning it to scene.environment instantly gives MeshPhysicalMaterial transmission/metalness/clearcoat something real to refract and reflect, which is exactly what makes glass read as glass instead of grey. This is the missing prerequisite for the glass-material snippet (and an immediate upgrade for the existing metallic scenes-pack 'shader ball' / rim-light scenes). The vault has NO env-map/PMREM/HDR helper for raw THREE.Scene materials today (only the <model-viewer> web component has a baked environment attribute, which does not apply to hand-built scenes). RoomEnvironment ships inside the three distribution, loads from the same CDN already used, and produces zero-asset, deterministic, screenshot-stable lighting.
backlog.license: MIT (three.js examples)
backlog.note: Loads three's examples/jsm RoomEnvironment + PMREMGenerator behind a need()-guard; degrades to scene.environment=null (materials still render, just less glossy). No external file fetch, so it does not threaten the verification imagery floor or determinism.

backlog.name: drei-vanilla MeshTransmissionMaterial (N8 backside-buffer glass: chromatic aberration + roughness blur + distortion)
backlog.what: The pmndrs vanilla (no-React) port of drei's MeshTransmissionMaterial — ~324 self-contained lines that subclass MeshPhysicalMaterial via onBeforeCompile, replace the transmission_pars/transmission_fragment chunks, and sample an FBO buffer to add what native MeshPhysicalMaterial CANNOT do: it can 'see' OTHER transmissive/transparent objects (stacked glass, liquid-in-bottle), plus author-controlled chromaticAberration, anisotropicBlur, noise distortion/temporalDistortion, and a backside render pass for thick solids. This is the genuine ceiling beyond native — the 'two layers of glass overlapping correctly' and animated caustic-distortion looks. Best added as an OPTIONAL upgrade tier behind a need()-guard in glass-material.js: when drei-vanilla is present use MeshTransmissionMaterial, else fall back to the native MeshPhysicalMaterial path. Costs an extra scene render pass per material (the snippet should default to a tiny 256/512 buffer + low samples for verification budget).
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: drei-vanilla is referenced by name in 3d-web.skill.md but no snippet exists. ESM module (examples/jsm-style import) — needs an importmap or ESM CDN (esm.sh/jsdelivr +esm); confirm it composes under the vault's global window.THREE script-tag model before shipping, and keep it as the optional drei-parity tier on top of the native vanilla baseline (candidate #1), not the default.

backlog.name: AmbientLightSensor lux auto-theme wrapper (illuminance -> --room-lux/--theme/--bloom CSS vars)
backlog.what: A ~40-line vanilla wrapper that constructs `new AmbientLightSensor()` behind feature-detection + a `navigator.permissions.query({name:'ambient-light-sensor'})` guard, then maps live `illuminance` (lux) to discrete bands (light/dim/dark) and a continuous `--bloom` intensity, writing `--room-lux`, `data-room-theme`, and a normalized `--bloom` to the root so CSS owns all styling — exactly the vault's 'write CSS vars, the input layer is swappable' contract from reactive-input.skill.md. Worth adding because the vault has ZERO ambient-light coverage and this is a non-duplicative reactive source (orthogonal to device-tilt/gyro/mic/webcam). CRITICAL CAVEAT that must ship in the snippet: as of 2026 this API is ~0.39% global support — removed from Chrome/Edge/Safari/Firefox DESKTOP over privacy concerns, 'disabled by default' on Chrome-for-Android, and only fully on in Firefox-for-Android; the old `enable-generic-sensor-extra-classes` chrome://flag was REMOVED so there is no dependable user flag. So the sensor is a bonus enhancement, NOT the mechanism. Value comes from pairing it with a robust fallback.
backlog.license: Web standard (W3C Ambient Light Sensor / Generic Sensor) - no dependency, reimplementable
backlog.note: FLAG: not shippable as a primary signal in 2026 (~0.4% support, no reliable flag, removed from all desktop engines for privacy). Only add if the snippet's headline behavior is the fallback and the lux path is explicitly labeled progressive-enhancement-only. Requires HTTPS + 'ambient-light-sensor' Permissions-API grant + permissions-policy; handle sensor 'error' (NotAllowedError/NotReadableError) by silently degrading.

backlog.name: prefers-color-scheme + light-dark() graceful-fallback auto-theme (the actual shippable core)
backlog.what: The degrade target that makes the ambient-light snippet honest and useful for the other ~99.6% of users: a tiny CSS-first auto-theme that keys off the `prefers-color-scheme` media feature and CSS `light-dark()` (Baseline since 2024, ~95% support in 2026, 'widely available' ~Nov 2026), exposing the same `data-room-theme` / `--bloom` tokens the lux wrapper writes — so the page auto-switches light/dim/dark and scales bloom from the system/OS color scheme with zero JS and zero permission, then the AmbientLightSensor layer (when present) simply overrides the tokens for finer room-brightness response. Net-new as a packaged 'one token set, two drivers (system + sensor)' pattern; the vault has palettes/aesthetic master-switch but no documented system-color-scheme auto-theme contract tied to a reactive sensor.
backlog.license: Web standard (CSS Color 5 / Media Queries) - no dependency
backlog.note: This is the load-bearing half of the find: ship it as the default, with AmbientLightSensor as opt-in enhancement layered on the SAME --room-theme/--bloom tokens. Pairs with the existing .pal-* palettes and taste/aesthetic.css master switch. Honors prefers-reduced-motion by freezing bloom (matches reactive-input.skill.md degrade rule).

backlog.name: ShakeDetect — DeviceMotion acceleration peak/jerk detector (vanilla snippet)
backlog.what: A zero-dep companion to effects/device-tilt.js that listens on DeviceMotionEvent.accelerationIncludingGravity, computes acceleration magnitude m = sqrt(x^2+y^2+z^2), and fires when m crosses a tunable threshold (rest ~9.8; a shake spikes >15-20) with a debounce timeout (~600-1000ms) so one physical shake fires once. Reuses device-tilt's exact iOS 13+ DeviceMotionEvent.requestPermission() gesture flow. Exposes ShakeDetect.init({threshold, timeout, onShake}) and also writes a transient --shake CSS var (1→0 decay) so styling stays in CSS. Worth adding because it fills the one empty cell in the reactive-input decision table — phone tilt is covered (orientation), but acceleration/shake/jerk gestures (shake-to-shuffle / clear / undo / re-roll) are not, and they are a staple mobile interaction that the vault currently cannot compose.
backlog.license: N/A (web standard — DeviceMotionEvent, reimplemented in-house)

backlog.name: shake.js (alexgibson) — canonical pattern reference
backlog.what: The de-facto reference implementation of accelerometer shake detection for the mobile web: ~4KB, MIT, custom 'shake' DOM event from devicemotion with threshold (default 15) and timeout (default 1000ms) options. Not worth shipping as a dependency — it predates iOS 13 permission gating (no requestPermission flow), its last real update was ~2020, and it is an event-emitter with no CSS-var output. Add it as the algorithm reference the vault's own ShakeDetect snippet is modeled on (threshold/timeout/debounce semantics), plus the @zouloux/shake fork which swaps the global 'shake' event for per-instance handlers (useful if multiple shake targets are needed).
backlog.license: MIT

backlog.name: reactive-input.skill.md decision-table row for acceleration/shake
backlog.what: Add a row to the existing reactive-input.skill.md TL;DR table: 'Phone shake/jerk gesture → fire event / pulse' | source: accelerometer (DeviceMotion) | snippet: effects/shake-detect.js | gate: iOS 13+ asks on first gesture, same flow as device-tilt; no fallback (desktop has no accelerometer) → degrade to a manual button. This codifies the source-selection decision (orientation gamma/beta for continuous tilt vs acceleration magnitude for discrete gestures) and the critical platform caveat: LinearAccelerationSensor / Generic Sensor API is NOT supported on iOS Safari, so DeviceMotionEvent.accelerationIncludingGravity is the only cross-platform source — the skill should steer composition to it, not the newer Generic Sensor API.
backlog.license: N/A (vault-authored skill content)

backlog.name: compass-heading.js (vanilla --heading sensor source)
backlog.what: A zero-dep input module that resolves a true-north compass heading and writes a normalized CSS var (e.g. --heading in turns 0..1 plus --heading-deg) on a target, mirroring device-tilt.js's API. iOS path: DeviceOrientationEvent.requestPermission() on a user gesture, then read event.webkitCompassHeading (already clockwise-from-north). Android/Chromium path: deviceorientationabsolute, heading = (360 - alpha) % 360. Exposes webkitCompassAccuracy as --heading-accuracy and a lerp smoother to kill jitter. This is genuinely absent: device-tilt.js gives tilt-x/y (relative gyro/pointer) but NO absolute facing direction, and reactive-input.skill.md's decision table has no 'which way am I facing' row. It reuses the exact permission+pointer/static fallback discipline already blessed in the vault.
backlog.license: web standard (DeviceOrientation Event Spec) — no dependency
backlog.note: Primary base MUST be DeviceOrientation, not the Generic Sensor Magnetometer/AbsoluteOrientationSensor (Chromium-only, no Safari/Firefox). Reliability is good-not-perfect: iOS accuracy ~10-20deg, Android needs high-accuracy location + is prone to magnetic interference/calibration jumps/drift. Snippet must surface accuracy and a 'figure-8 to calibrate' hint, and degrade to a draggable/manual dial when heading is unavailable or denied.

backlog.name: wayfinder / compass-rose directional UI archetype
backlog.what: A compose archetype + companion CSS that consumes --heading: a rotating compass rose / AR-ish wayfinder where N-E-S-W ring and a target-bearing needle counter-rotate to keep the user's facing direction up, plus a 'rotate radial-nav to face a POI' mode. The visual primitive (radial-nav.css, trig-positioned items via --i/--rnav-count) ALREADY exists in the vault but is static/click-driven; the net-new piece is binding ring rotation to live heading (transform: rotate(calc(var(--heading) * -1turn))) and computing relative bearing = targetBearing - heading. Pairs with geolocation for 'point me toward X' POI wayfinding. Spatial/wayfinding is an unserved compose genre.
backlog.license: vanilla-reimplementable (own CSS/JS; technique inspired by public CSS radial-menu galleries)
backlog.note: Builds directly on existing radial-nav.css + the proposed compass-heading.js, so it's mostly composition glue. Must honor prefers-reduced-motion (freeze ring, show numeric bearing) and provide a desktop/manual fallback since heading is mobile-only.

backlog.name: reactive-input.skill.md 'facing/heading' decision row + sundial preset
backlog.what: A skill update: add a 5th row to reactive-input's TL;DR table — 'Which way am I facing -> compass heading -> compass-heading.js -> iOS asks on gesture, Android needs high-accuracy + calibration, falls back to manual dial' — codifying the heading source as a first-class sibling of tilt/sound/webcam and documenting the cross-platform normalization + accuracy/calibration caveats so the engine wires it correctly. Includes a small 'sundial' demo preset (a gnomon/shadow whose angle tracks heading, or maps heading+time to a sun azimuth) as a tasteful, non-AR way to exercise the var. The skill family is the documented decision layer and currently has a gap exactly here.
backlog.license: n/a (documentation)
backlog.note: Low effort, high leverage: makes the new --heading source discoverable and enforces the consent+degrade rules already governing the reactive-input family.

backlog.name: Stylus-reactive ink source (PointerEvent pressure + tilt + twist, vanilla)
backlog.what: A zero-dep canvas ink/brush source that reads the full PointerEvent stylus signal set — pressure (0..1), tiltX/tiltY (-90..90deg), altitudeAngle (0..pi/2) + azimuthAngle (0..2pi), and twist (0..359deg) — and maps them to a variable-width stroke whose nib LEANS with tilt (bristle/calligraphy lean from azimuth, contrast from altitude). Crucially it normalizes the two MUTUALLY-EXCLUSIVE orientation sets the spec exposes: read altitude/azimuth if present, else derive them from tiltX/tiltY (altitude = atan-of-tilt, azimuth = atan2(tiltY,tiltX)). On e.pointerType==='mouse'/'touch' (no pressure/tilt) it degrades to constant width using the existing velocity-smoothing path. This is the exact graceful-degrade-source shape reactive-input.skill.md is built around (pointer/gyro/mic/webcam) and the vault has NO stylus signal source today. altitudeAngle/azimuthAngle went Baseline in Dec 2024, so tilt is finally reliable to ship in 2026.
backlog.license: Web standard (W3C Pointer Events L3, May 2026) — no dependency
backlog.note: Anti-dup: cursor/cursor-paint.js exists but is pointer-position-only (color/lineWidth/smoothing/rainbow/glow/eraser) with NO pressure/tilt/twist; reactive-input.skill.md decision table has no stylus source. New source should write --ink-pressure/--ink-tilt-x/--ink-azimuth CSS vars like the other reactive sources so styling stays in CSS and the input layer stays swappable.

backlog.name: getCoalescedEvents() high-fidelity stroke sampling
backlog.what: Browsers coalesce rapid pointermove events for performance, throwing away intermediate stylus samples — which produces faceted, low-fidelity ink at fast speeds. getCoalescedEvents() returns every un-coalesced sample (and each carries its own pressure/tilt), letting the ink source reconstruct the path at the pen's native sample rate (120-240Hz). This is a small but high-leverage moat: it is the single technique that separates 'toy canvas paint' from 'feels like real ink', and it pairs directly with the stylus source above. Pure web API, trivially vanilla, degrades to the single event (length-1 array) on hardware that does not coalesce.
backlog.license: Web standard (Pointer Events L3) — no dependency
backlog.note: Ship as part of/alongside the stylus ink source. No vault snippet uses getCoalescedEvents today (grep of INDEX.md shows pointer effects use plain pointermove). Optional escalation: pointerrawupdate for ultra-low-latency, but note perf cost in the skill.

backlog.name: perfect-freehand (getStroke)
backlog.what: The canonical pressure-sensitive freehand stroke generator (by tldraw's Steve Ruiz). Pure data: feed it [x,y,pressure] points and it returns the outline polygon of a tapered, variable-width stroke; options thinning (incl. negative to invert), smoothing, streamline, taper-start/end. No DOM dependency, MIT, ~5KB, ~5.6k stars, multi-language ports — exactly the kind of tiny pure-function lib that wraps cleanly behind a need()-guard and degrades to a constant-width path2d when pressure is absent. Reimplementable as a vanilla snippet, but it is small/permissive enough to cdn-snippet directly. Powers the variable-width half of the ink source so the vault does not have to hand-roll outline tessellation.
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: MIT + zero-dep + pure-data = safe CDN behind need(); fallback path = plain canvas quadratic-bezier (already in cursor-paint.js) when lib/pressure unavailable. Could also be inspiration-only (reimplement getStroke) to stay fully vanilla per vault's zero-dep preference.

backlog.name: Sketch / signature / annotation compose genre
backlog.what: A new compose-genre archetype that the engine can produce: a stylus-aware drawing surface — signature pad (sign-here legal/checkout), freehand annotation overlay (markup on an image/doc), or sketch/note canvas. It composes the stylus ink source + getCoalescedEvents + a tool HUD (the vault already has cursor/ctool-* pen/brush/eraser cursors and an infinite-canvas + minimap), exports PNG/SVG, and degrades to finger/mouse drawing on non-stylus devices. This is the user-facing payoff that justifies the source: it slots beside the existing web-page/mobile-flow/deck/diagram genres as a distinct interactive archetype the vault cannot currently compose, and it has obvious real product uses (e-sign, PDF/image markup, whiteboard notes).
backlog.license: n/a (composition of vault snippets + MIT perfect-freehand)
backlog.note: Reuses existing pieces: cursor/cursor-tools.css (ctool-pen/brush/eraser HUD), components/infinite-canvas.js + canvas-minimap.js for pan/zoom, effects/watercolor.css for paper grain. Net-new glue = the stylus source + genre recipe; no signature/annotation/sketch genre exists in AGENTS.md/structure.skill.md.

backlog.name: Low-latency ink loop (getCoalescedEvents + getPredictedEvents + rAF)
backlog.what: A zero-dep drawing-loop pattern that fixes the latency/quality gap in the vault's existing pen surfaces. The technique: buffer pointer input, and ONCE per rAF frame (a) drain e.getCoalescedEvents() to recover the full sub-frame motion history the browser merged away (smoother fast strokes), (b) draw the committed path from real points, then (c) extend it with e.getPredictedEvents() extrapolated points so the wet stroke tip visually leads the actual pointer (cuts perceived lag), and (d) erase/redraw the predicted segment from real history on the next frame so prediction error never persists. This is exactly the missing layer: signature-pad.js, cursor-paint.js, and whiteboard-pack.js all draw one quadratic segment inline in pointermove with no coalesced history, no prediction, and no rAF decoupling. Worth adding as a drop-in upgrade that materially improves any signature/whiteboard/sketch surface for free.
backlog.license: Web standard (no dependency) — MIT-equivalent to reimplement

backlog.name: desynchronized low-latency canvas hint
backlog.what: One-line opt-in low-latency mode: canvas.getContext('2d', { desynchronized: true }) (also works for webgl/webgl2). It tells the browser to bypass the normal compositor queue and push the canvas buffer closer to the display, shaving a frame or two of latency — the cheapest possible ink-latency win and a natural pairing with the coalesced/predicted loop above. Feature-detect with ctx.getContextAttributes().desynchronized and fall back silently. Caveats worth encoding: possible tearing on front-buffer devices, attributes immutable after first getContext, and translucent desynchronized canvases can't have DOM stacked above them. The vault's three canvas pens all call getContext('2d') with no options, so this is net-new.
backlog.license: Web standard (no dependency)

backlog.name: pointerrawupdate high-frequency input event
backlog.what: A higher-frequency sibling of pointermove that fires as fast as JS can handle (uncoalesced cadence) for high-precision input like stylus drawing. Used to FEED the rAF input buffer (collect raw points on pointerrawupdate, render once per frame) rather than drawing inline. Carries its own getCoalescedEvents() too. Best documented as part of the same low-latency inking skill/snippet — it's the input-collection half of the pattern and is absent from every drawing snippet in the vault (all listen only to pointermove). Feature-detect ('onpointerrawupdate' in window) and fall back to pointermove.
backlog.license: Web standard (no dependency)

backlog.name: DelegatedInkTrailPresenter (ink.requestPresenter / delegated ink trail)
backlog.what: The OS-delegated ink API: navigator.ink.requestPresenter(canvas) returns a presenter whose updateInkTrailStartPoint() lets the browser/compositor draw the wet trailing ink ahead of your JS rendering, in your chosen color/diameter, until the next pointermove. This is the lowest-latency option that exists (the trail is drawn by the OS, not your rAF loop) but is Chromium-only and behind a relatively niche API surface, so it belongs as inspiration / a progressive-enhancement note layered on top of the coalesced+predicted snippet rather than a portable dependency. Flagging it so the skill can mention 'if navigator.ink exists, hand the trail to the compositor; else use predicted-events.'
backlog.license: Web standard (no dependency) — WICG ink-enhancement, MIT spec

backlog.name: $1 Unistroke Recognizer (vanilla reimplementation)
backlog.what: The canonical UW $1 recognizer: an instance-based nearest-neighbor template matcher that classifies a single pointer-path stroke against named templates with rotation/scale/position invariance (~97% accuracy from one template each). Ships 16 built-in symbols out of the box including check, circle, X (via the 'delete' zigzag), arrow, caret, triangle, rectangle, star, brackets, pigtail. The whole algorithm is ~100 core lines (full JS ~470 lines / 13 KB with templates) and trivially reimplementable as a zero-dep snippet: feed it a pointer-path point array, get back {name, score}. This is the missing shape/symbol gesture vocabulary the vault lacks — it upgrades the 4-direction Swipe detector into a draw-to-command layer (draw a check to confirm, an X to dismiss, a circle to select). Pairs directly with the existing Whiteboard/SignaturePad/following-pointer snippets which already capture pointer paths. Define your own templates by recording one example stroke.
backlog.license: New BSD (3-Clause) — free use/modify/redistribute; keep copyright header. Effectively permissive/public-domain for snippet use.
backlog.note: Reimplement as vanilla (do NOT add stale npm dep). Vault has Swipe/SwipeActions/SignaturePad/Whiteboard/long-press/pinch-zoom but no symbol/shape classifier — confirmed net-new via grep of INDEX.md and reactive-input.skill.md. Belongs under interactions/ + reactive-input.skill.md.

backlog.name: $Q Super-Quick / $P Point-Cloud Recognizer (multistroke + scribble)
backlog.what: The point-cloud cousins of $1. $P treats a gesture as an UNORDERED point cloud, ignoring stroke number, order, and direction — so it recognizes multistroke symbols (an X drawn as two separate strokes, a hash, a plus) and scribble-to-delete (a back-and-forth scrawl) that $1's single-ordered-stroke model can't. $Q is the super-quick refinement: ~142x faster than $P and slightly more accurate, built for low-power/mobile pointer input. About half the code is shared with $1, so it slots into the same vanilla snippet as an optional 'multistroke/cloud' mode. This is what unlocks the marquee scribble-to-delete UX cited in the angle, plus erase-gesture and free-form multistroke commands.
backlog.license: New BSD (3-Clause) — permissive; keep copyright header.
backlog.note: Ship as the multistroke/scribble option alongside $1, or as a single recognizer snippet with a 'cloud' mode flag. $Q preferred over $P for the mobile/pointer footprint. Net-new — no point-cloud GESTURE matcher in vault (3d/particles-galaxy 'point-cloud' is unrelated rendering).

backlog.name: Penny Pincher recognizer
backlog.what: A streamlined stroke-gesture recognizer (Taranta/LaViola) that matches on a vector of point-to-point direction angles, prized for very fast, accurate time-limited recognition. Conceptually it's a leaner template-distance variant in the $-family lineage. Worth noting as INSPIRATION for the fast-path scoring inside the vanilla recognizer, but it is 'not intrinsically a multistroke recognizer' and less flexible than $P — so it should not be the integration base; $1 + $Q already cover the needed symbol set with better-known, BSD-clean source.
backlog.license: Academic paper; no canonical permissive JS reference distribution found — treat as technique to borrow, not a dep.
backlog.note: Inspiration only — flagged because no clean redistributable JS source; less capable than $P/$Q for the vault's needs. Don't add as a snippet; mention as a perf option in the skill.

backlog.name: voice-command.js — keyword-grammar voice source (transcript → intent → CustomEvent)
backlog.what: A vanilla zero-dep reactive-input SOURCE that wraps SpeechRecognition (continuous + interimResults) and routes the recognized transcript through a small JS keyword grammar — `{ 'next slide': fn, 'go *page': fn, 'dark mode': fn }` with literal/wildcard/optional tokens and Levenshtein-tolerant matching — instead of just printing a transcript. On a match it fires a namespaced CustomEvent (`voicecmd:next-slide`) and/or sets a CSS var (`--voice-intent`), exactly mirroring how device-tilt/sound-react expose state, so styling and behavior stay declarative and the input layer is swappable. This is the missing 'speech-to-INTENT' counterpart to the vault's voice-input/voice-transcribe (which only do speech-to-TEXT) and sound-react (which only does amplitude). Mandatory no-support fallback: feature-detect `window.SpeechRecognition||webkitSpeechRecognition`; when absent (Edge, Firefox, locked-down Safari) it degrades to a visible text-command input or hotkey map and dispatches the same events, so downstream code is identical. Opt-in/gesture-gated (mic only starts on a click, never on load) and honors prefers-reduced-motion for any visual reaction. Pairs directly with the existing ai/voice-input.css mic UI.
backlog.license: Web standard (no dependency)
backlog.note: Net-new: vault's ai/voice-input.js + ai/voice-transcribe.js do speech-to-TEXT (dictation); sound-react.js does amplitude only. No existing snippet maps keywords→intent/events. Add a 'Voice keyword → intent/event' row to reactive-input.skill.md decision table. Consent+gesture and degrade rules already match that skill's family contract.

backlog.name: On-device SpeechRecognition (Chrome 139+): processLocally + available()/install() + SpeechRecognitionPhrase biasing
backlog.what: The 2026-current way to do voice commands well. Chrome 139 (Aug 2025) shipped on-device recognition: set `recognition.processLocally = true` so audio/transcripts never leave the device; gate it with the static `SpeechRecognition.available({langs:['en-US'], processLocally:true})` (returns 'available' | 'downloadable' | 'downloading' | 'unavailable') and `SpeechRecognition.install({langs})` to fetch a language pack behind a user gesture. Crucially, the old grammar system (`SpeechGrammarList`/`recognition.grammars`) is DEPRECATED and a no-op — the modern replacement is `recognition.phrases = [new SpeechRecognitionPhrase('next slide', boost)]` for CONTEXTUAL BIASING, which boosts your keyword set's recognition accuracy; you then still do the literal keyword→intent match in JS. Worth capturing as the canonical capability the voice-command snippet feature-detects and layers on (privacy + offline + accuracy), with graceful fall-through to cloud recognition where on-device isn't installed.
backlog.license: Web standard (no dependency)
backlog.note: Net-new capability not used by existing voice snippets (they predate processLocally/phrases and reference no biasing). Detect via `'available' in SpeechRecognition` and `'phrases' in SpeechRecognition.prototype`; always fall back to cloud/no-bias. Document in reactive-input.skill.md Privacy section: 'voice commands processed on-device when supported.'

backlog.name: annyang (v3.0.0, 2026)
backlog.what: The reference 2KB zero-dep MIT voice-command library — and it just shipped v3.0.0 (Mar 2026, ESM+CJS+script-tag), so it is freshly maintained, not abandoned. Its command grammar is the gold-standard mental model to reimplement: named variables `'show :section'`, splats `'search for *query'`, optional words `'(please) open settings'`, plus `annyang.isSpeechRecognitionSupported()` for the fallback gate and `resultMatch`/`resultNoMatch` callbacks. Best added to Design Book as INSPIRATION for the vault's own vanilla voice-command.js grammar parser (the vault prefers zero-dep reimplementation over a CDN dep for something this small), but it is also CDN-safe (cdnjs) if a need()-guarded cdn-snippet is ever wanted. Citing it documents the canonical token syntax (`:var`, `*splat`, `(optional)`) the vault snippet should match.
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: Permissive/safe, but its whole value (2KB grammar matcher over SpeechRecognition) is trivially reimplementable vanilla — prefer reimplementing the `:var`/`*splat`/`(optional)` grammar in voice-command.js rather than shipping a dep. Listed as inspiration, optionally a cdn-snippet behind need().

backlog.name: Spectral-flux onset detection -> --beat pulse (extend sound-react.js)
backlog.what: A ~30-40 line vanilla addition to the existing sound-react.js loop: keep the previous AnalyserNode magnitude spectrum, sum the positive frame-to-frame differences (max(0, cur[i]-prev[i])) into a 'flux' value, run an adaptive threshold (rolling mean + k*stddev over the last ~1s) and a local-max peak-pick to fire a beat. On a beat, write a decaying --beat CSS var (set to 1, ease back to 0 over ~120ms) so styling pulses on the downbeat purely in CSS. This is the headline net-new signal: sound-react today only exposes amplitude (--snd-level/bass/treble), which lags and smears; spectral-flux fires on transients/onsets so visuals actually HIT on the beat. Reuses the exact AnalyserNode plumbing and the established 'drive CSS vars from a sensor' pattern in reactive-input.skill.md. Reimplement from the algorithm (audiojs/beat-detection is MIT reference) rather than shipping the lib, since getByteFrequencyData gives magnitude-only and the static-threshold dev.to example needs the adaptive/peak-pick upgrade to be musical.
backlog.license: MIT (reference algorithm; reimplemented vanilla)
backlog.note: Net-new vs sound-react.js (amplitude only). Add --beat (decaying pulse) + optional --beat-count; keep the existing AnalyserNode. Freeze under prefers-reduced-motion like sound-react does. Energy-flux fallback (RMS first-difference, no FFT) for CPU-constrained / percussive cases.

backlog.name: pitchy (McLeod Pitch Method) -> --pitch / --note
backlog.what: Pure-JS, zero-dependency pitch detector using the McLeod Pitch Method (normalized square difference / autocorrelation). PitchDetector.findPitch(getFloatTimeDomainData buffer, sampleRate) returns [hzFrequency, clarity 0-1]. Wire it into the sound-react AnalyserNode loop (just needs analyser.fftSize and getFloatTimeDomainData, which the snippet can add) and emit net-new musical signals: --pitch (normalized log-frequency 0-1 gated by clarity > ~0.9 so noise doesn't jitter it) and optionally --note (0-11 semantic pitch class). Lets visuals map COLOR/hue or position to the actual melody/note, not just loudness. ESM-only, loads straight from esm.sh in the browser with no build step, so it fits the cdn-snippet need()-guard pattern and degrades to amplitude-only if it fails to load.
backlog.license: 0BSD (Zero-Clause BSD, maximally permissive)
backlog.note: ESM-only (no CommonJS). CDN-guard behind need(); fall back to --snd-* if absent. Gate --pitch on the returned clarity to avoid jitter on noisy/polyphonic input (monophonic works best).

backlog.name: realtime-bpm-analyzer -> stable --bpm / --tempo-phase
backlog.what: Zero-dependency TypeScript/JS library that computes a STABLE beats-per-minute from an AnalyserNode/audio-node/mic stream in realtime (<5KB gzipped), emitting 'bpm detected' events (data.bpm[0].tempo). Complements the per-frame --beat pulse with a smoothed musical tempo: the engine can derive a steady --bpm and a predicted --tempo-phase (0..1 sawtooth synced to the grid) so animations can anticipate the next downbeat instead of only reacting to detected onsets - the difference between jittery reactive flicker and a confident, on-grid groove. CDN-guarded like other lib-backed reactive snippets; degrades to the vanilla spectral-flux --beat if not loaded.
backlog.license: Apache-2.0 (commercial license also offered)
backlog.note: No official unpkg/jsdelivr listed in docs but it's plain npm/ESM so esm.sh/jsdelivr should resolve; verify the CDN path at integration time. Apache-2.0 is permissive (note the NOTICE-file requirement). Best for stable tempo; pair with the vanilla --beat for transient hits.

backlog.name: face-react.js — MediaPipe FaceLandmarker expression/gaze/head-pose CSS vars
backlog.what: A sibling to interactions/vision-react.js that swaps HandLandmarker for FaceLandmarker (same @mediapipe/tasks-vision@0.10.x Apache-2.0 package, same lazy dynamic-import + .enableCamera() consent gate + pointer fallback). Create it with outputFaceBlendshapes:true and outputFacialTransformationMatrixes:true, then per frame write a family of normalized CSS custom properties: gaze from iris landmarks (468-477) and the eyeLookIn/Out/Up/Down blendshapes -> --face-gaze-x/y (-1..1); head-pose decomposed from the 4x4 facialTransformationMatrix -> --face-yaw/pitch/roll; and expression from named blendshapes -> --face-smile (mouthSmileLeft+Right), --face-brow (browInnerUp/browDownLeft), --face-mouth-open (jawOpen), --face-blink (eyeBlinkLeft+Right). Styling stays in CSS, the input layer is swappable -- exactly the reactive-input family contract. Net-new because vision-react only emits hand --vr-x/y and never instantiates FaceLandmarker, blendshapes, or transform matrices. Worth adding: it unlocks an entire expression/gaze axis of reactive input the vault cannot currently express, at zero new dependency cost.
backlog.license: Apache-2.0 (MediaPipe Tasks Vision; CDN ESM + Google .task model) — same dep already vetted in reactive-input.skill.md

backlog.name: Attention-aware presence behavior: look-away-to-dim / lean-in-to-focus
backlog.what: A small behavior layer (a .js + the CSS recipe) built on the head-pose/gaze source above: derive a presence state from yaw/pitch magnitude + face-detected flag + a debounce, then drive a single --attention scalar (0=looked-away, 1=engaged). CSS maps it to dimming an unfocused page (filter:brightness/opacity), sharpening/scaling the focused region on lean-in (head-pose z / face bounding-box growth), and pausing autoplay/animation when the user looks away. This mirrors the real, now-shipping OS pattern -- Windows 11 Adaptive Dimming, Dell Optimizer presence detection, ST/ASUS Adaptive Screen Dimming + Wake-on-Attention (2025-2026) -- but that pattern is NOT exposed to web pages, so a MediaPipe browser version is genuinely net-new and on-trend. Distinct from vision-react's positional --vr-x/y: this is a stateful attention/engagement signal, not a cursor. Must ship reduced-motion-off, consent-gated, and pointer/idle fallback (e.g. tab visibility + mousemove) per the family rules. Worth adding: turns the raw face source into a finished, demoable attention-aware UI archetype.
backlog.license: Pattern/technique (no dep beyond the Apache-2.0 MediaPipe source above); OS implementations are inspiration/precedent only, not code

backlog.name: WebGazer.js (webcam gaze/eye-tracking library)
backlog.what: The best-known browser eye-tracking library (self-calibrating gaze prediction from clicks/mousemoves, on-device, heatmap). Relevant as the obvious 'just use a gaze lib' alternative -- but FLAG: it is dual-licensed LGPLv3 (and a GPLv3 fork), with a commercial-use carve-out only for companies under $10M valuation. That is a restricted/copyleft license that does not meet the vault's MIT/BSD/Apache bar, so it should NOT be a dependency. It is useful only as INSPIRATION confirming the gaze-on-the-web demand; the recommended path is the Apache-2.0 MediaPipe iris-landmark gaze in face-react.js, which is permissive, already-vetted, and avoids WebGazer's calibration UX entirely.
backlog.license: LGPLv3 / GPLv3 (restricted copyleft; commercial carve-out <$10M valuation) — FLAGGED, do not vendor

backlog.name: Global rAF scroll-velocity signal source (--scroll-vel / --scroll-dir on :root)
backlog.what: A tiny zero-dep util that runs ONE shared requestAnimationFrame loop, reads scrollY/scrollX from a passive scroll listener, computes a lerp-smoothed velocity, and writes four inheriting CSS custom properties to :root: --scroll-vel (signed, normalized ~-1..1), --scroll-speed (abs), --scroll-dir (-1/0/1), and --scrolling (0/1). This is EXACTLY the velocity math currently trapped inside marquee.js (dy/dt*16, *0.95 decay) and the OGL drag gallery, hoisted into reusable infrastructure so ANY element/effect can subscribe via plain CSS (e.g. skew, blur, RGB-split, gradient-shift, marquee speed) without re-implementing a scroll loop. Pairs perfectly with the vault's existing 'write a CSS var, style in CSS' reactive-input family (--tilt-x/y, --snd-*). Worth adding because it's a one-time-cost singleton signal source that unlocks a whole class of speed-reactive effects and de-duplicates the velocity code in two places. Reference impl: magic-spells/scroll-progress (MIT, zero-dep web component, 2.2kb gzip, shared rAF + IntersectionObserver gating, exposes --scroll-progress + --scroll-progress-velocity) confirms the pattern is sound and 2025-current; reimplement as a vault util rather than depend on an early-stage (0-star, Jul 2025) lib.
backlog.license: reimplemented in-house (reference: magic-spells/scroll-progress MIT)
backlog.note: Must gate on prefers-reduced-motion (freeze --scroll-vel at 0) per the vault's reactive-input rules. Single global rAF + passive listener; do NOT add per-element scroll handlers. Lerp/decay constant should be tunable (marquee uses 0.95).

backlog.name: Pure-CSS scroll direction + speed detection (@property + scroll() timeline + delayed transition)
backlog.what: A JS-free technique (Bram Van Damme / Bram.us) that derives scroll velocity entirely in CSS: declare two @property <number> customs (--scroll-position, --scroll-position-delayed), animate both 0->1 with animation-timeline: scroll(root), then put a transition: --scroll-position-delayed 0.15s linear on body so the delayed copy LAGS. The differential calc(--scroll-position - --scroll-position-delayed) is the velocity; max(v, -1*v) gives speed and dividing gives direction (-1/0/1) — all without sign()/abs() (which Chrome reverted behind a flag, so the calc() form is the safe one). This is the CSS-only scroll-direction trick the brief explicitly asks for: a zero-JS fallback that lets reduced-JS / static pages still react to scroll direction & speed. Net-new: the vault's only CSS-only scroll-direction is the header headroom fallback (.hdr-auto) which is a binary scrolled/at-top toggle, NOT a continuous signed velocity. Best as the CSS-only twin of the rAF source above: ship both so the same --scroll-vel/--scroll-dir contract is available with or without JS.
backlog.license: public technique / blog (Bram.us, 2023, still current 2026)
backlog.note: Reimplement, not a dep. Limitation: customs animate on main thread (paint during scroll) and Firefox support is partial — so this is the graceful CSS-only fallback, with the rAF JS source as the universally-supported primary. Use the calc() max() form, not sign()/abs(), for support.

backlog.name: Scroll-state container queries (@container scroll-state(scrollable / stuck / snapped))
backlog.what: A 2025-2026 CSS primitive (Chrome 133+) that lets descendants style themselves based on their scroll container's STATE — whether it can still scroll in a direction (scrollable: top/bottom/left/right), whether a position:sticky element is currently stuck, and whether a scroll-snap child is snapped. This is adjacent-but-distinct from velocity: it answers 'where am I in the scroll' declaratively (e.g. fade a 'scroll for more' hint only while scrollable: bottom, restyle a sticky header only when stuck) with zero JS and zero scroll listeners. Net-new: the vault does sticky/snap layout and JS headroom pinning, but has nothing using scroll-state() queries. Complements the velocity source — together they give a full declarative scroll-reactivity story (speed+direction via --scroll-vel, position-state via scroll-state()).
backlog.license: web standard (CSS Overflow / Conditional)
backlog.note: Progressive-enhancement only (Chromium-first, Safari/FF lagging) so must degrade to a static style. Distinct from --scroll-vel: state vs speed. Lower value than the velocity source but cheap, declarative, and reinforces the same 'react to scroll' category.

backlog.name: Vibration API named-pattern haptics helper (Haptics.fire('success'|'tap'|'warning'|'longpress'))
backlog.what: A ~40-line zero-dep helper that wraps navigator.vibrate behind a tiny SEMANTIC vocabulary of named patterns instead of raw ms arrays: tap=[10], success=[15,40,25], warning=[40,30,40], error=[60,40,60,40,60], longpress=[12,0,20], select=[8]. Haptics.fire(name) feature-detects ('vibrate' in navigator), respects a global Haptics.mute(true) and prefers-reduced-motion (default off), and is a pure no-op where unsupported (iOS Safari, Firefox 129+) — return false, never throw. Worth adding because the vault has rich gesture INPUTS (swipe.js onSwipe, long-press.js onLongPress, pull-to-refresh onRefresh, sortable, pinch-zoom) but NO tactile OUTPUT channel; this closes the input->feedback loop. It is the exact structural twin of the existing interactions/ui-sounds.js (fire-and-forget, named effects, global mute, reduced-motion-aware) — proving the vault's own pattern, just tactile instead of audio. Pairs one-liner into existing callbacks: onLongPress(el){ Haptics.fire('longpress'); }.
backlog.license: Web standard (W3C Vibration API, public domain spec) — helper is original MIT-style vanilla
backlog.note: NOT a duplicate: grep shows zero navigator.vibrate usage in the tree; the only 'haptic' hits are an inspirational comment/tag on long-press.js and a soft-skill doc. Sibling/governing skill: reactive-input.skill.md (which today only covers input sources) or audio-ui.skill.md alongside ui-sounds.js. CAVEAT to bake in: iOS has NO Vibration API and the old <input type=checkbox switch> .click() haptic trick was PATCHED in iOS 26.5 — so the snippet must NOT attempt that hack; ship as Android-fires / iOS-noops. Devices in Silent/DND may also stay silent — feedback must be decorative, never load-bearing.

backlog.name: WICG Web Haptics API (semantic named-effect successor)
backlog.what: A WICG proposal (opened Jan 30 2026, MS Edge Explainers + Device & Sensors WG, cross-shared with the Haptic Industry Forum) for a cross-platform SEMANTIC haptics API: developers request a predefined named effect with optional intensity (not raw waveforms/durations), and the user agent maps it to the closest native OS haptic. Explicitly motivated by navigator.vibrate being 'mobile-centric, low-level, and not how designers think about haptic intent.' Worth adding as INSPIRATION + design vocabulary: it validates and future-proofs the named-pattern approach above (design 'success/warning/tap' intents, not ms arrays), so the vanilla helper's public API should mirror these effect names and gain a transparent upgrade path when the standard ships. It is also the eventual route to iOS/desktop haptics that navigator.vibrate can never reach.
backlog.license: WICG proposal / W3C process (open, royalty-free track)
backlog.note: Inspiration only — unshipped. Use it to shape the helper's named-effect API surface (and a note in the governing skill) so the vault is aligned with where the platform is heading. Do NOT add as a dependency or claim support.

backlog.name: presence-source.js (vanilla attention/idle/presence source)
backlog.what: A zero-dep reactive-input source that fuses a throttled inactivity timer (pointer/key/scroll/touch activity) with the Page Visibility API and writes coarse CSS custom properties the rest of the page styles off of: --presence (1 active -> 0 away), --idle (0/1 after N seconds quiet), --away (1 when tab hidden/blurred). Pattern: Presence.init({idleMs, onIdle, onActive, onAway, onReturn}) sets data-presence='active|idle|away' on <html> plus the vars, so CSS alone can do ambient dimming (filter:brightness(calc(...))), auto-pause motion (animation-play-state:paused under [data-presence='away']), and a 'welcome back' reveal on return. WHY ADD: this is the conspicuously-missing LIGHTEST rung of the existing reactive-input ladder (pointer -> gyro -> mic -> webcam) — it needs NO permission and works everywhere, making it the safest live source in the vault. It also fixes a real correctness gap: nothing in the vault pauses motion/loops when the tab is backgrounded (battery/CPU waste) or restores cleanly on return. Mirrors the family's exact conventions (writes CSS vars, degrades, freezes under prefers-reduced-motion).
backlog.license: Web standard (Page Visibility L2 + setTimeout); no dependency
backlog.note: Slots into reactive-input.skill.md as a new row 'User attention/presence -> visibility+timer -> presence-source.js -> free, no permission'. All 'idle' hits in INDEX.md are unrelated UI component states (button idle, dynamic-island idle); no presence/visibility-driven behavior or --presence/--idle/--away vars exist.

backlog.name: Idle Detection API (IdleDetector) — permission-gated escalation rung
backlog.what: The native IdleDetector reports userState 'active|idle' AND screenState 'locked|unlocked', delivering one signal a JS timer fundamentally cannot: the user physically walked away (OS screensaver/lock), even while the tab is foregrounded. Add it as the OPTIONAL TOP rung of the presence source: Presence.enableSystemIdle() calls IdleDetector.requestPermission() from a user gesture, then upgrades --away/data-presence to a true 'stepped-away' state for deeper ambient screensaver behavior; if unsupported or denied, it silently stays on the timer+visibility fallback. WHY ADD: this is the exact escalation pattern the family already uses (vision-react: pointer by default, .enableCamera() opts into the heavy/permissioned source). It makes the presence source future-proof without ever blocking the no-permission baseline.
backlog.license: Web standard (WICG, secure-context). FLAG: requires the 'idle-detection' permission + a user gesture; Chromium-only (Chrome/Edge 94+), NOT in Firefox/Safari. Must be feature-detected and strictly optional.
backlog.note: Permission-gated and Chromium-only, so it MUST be the optional enhancement, never the default — the timer+visibility fallback carries the effect everywhere. No IdleDetector usage exists in the vault.

backlog.name: Page Lifecycle freeze/resume + bfcache pageshow (correct resume/restore)
backlog.what: Companion technique so the presence/auto-pause source resumes correctly across the FULL page lifecycle, not just tab-switch. Listen for document 'freeze'/'resume' (tab frozen/discarded by the browser) and window 'pageshow' with event.persisted (restored from back/forward cache) in addition to visibilitychange: pause RAF loops / video / shader uniforms on freeze+hidden, and on resume/pageshow re-arm timers and play the 'welcome back' state. Also check document.wasDiscarded to do a clean re-init. WHY ADD: visibilitychange alone misses bfcache restores and OS-initiated freezes, which leaves paused animations stuck or double-fires loops — a subtle bug the vault's many RAF/scroll/audio effects would inherit. This is the robustness layer that makes auto-pause actually correct.
backlog.license: Web standard (WICG Page Lifecycle); pageshow/persisted is Baseline, freeze/resume Chromium
backlog.note: Best folded as guidance INTO the presence-source snippet + a reactive-input.skill.md rule ('resume on resume/pageshow, not just visibilitychange'), rather than a standalone file. No freeze/resume/pageshow/bfcache handling exists anywhere in the vault.

backlog.name: Elastic rubber-band overscroll container (iOS UIScrollView resistance curve + spring release)
backlog.what: A both-edges elastic overscroll archetype: a scroll container that, when you pull past top OR bottom, translates content with the real iOS UIScrollView rubber-band resistance curve f(x,d,c) = (x*d*c)/(d+c*x) with c=0.55 (NOT a flat linear multiplier), then releases back to zero via a damped spring. Wires overscroll-behavior:contain to kill scroll-chaining/native pull-to-refresh hijack, detects edge via scrollTop===0 / scrollHeight, and drives transform from pointer/wheel delta. WHY ADD: the vault's interactions/pull-to-refresh.js uses a flat resistance:0.5 and a hard 'transition: transform 0.18s ease-out' snap (top edge only) — that is NOT a rubber band; it has no nonlinear stiffness and no spring. This reuses the existing animations/spring.js solver for release, so it is a small, cohesive net-new: the correct iOS feel + bottom-edge support + the 0.55 curve are the load-bearing additions. Distinct from bottom-sheet velocity-dismiss (which moves a panel toward a snap point on flick velocity) — this stretches in-flow content and always returns to rest.
backlog.license: Public-domain math (UIScrollView curve); reimplement from scratch
backlog.note: Net-new vs the existing linear PTR. Reuse animations/spring.js for the release so it stays zero-dep and on-brand. Honor prefers-reduced-motion (freeze, no bounce). The 0.55 constant + bottom-edge are what make it feel native.

backlog.name: Spring-release pull-to-refresh upgrade (iOS-feel) for interactions/pull-to-refresh.js
backlog.what: An upgraded PTR mode that replaces the current linear resistance + CSS ease-out snap with (a) the nonlinear iOS rubber-band resistance curve while pulling and (b) a spring (spring.js) settle to the trigger position and back, plus overscroll-behavior:contain on the scroller so the browser's native PTR doesn't fight it. Adds a 'progress past trigger' that keeps resisting harder rather than running away linearly. WHY ADD: makes the vault's existing PTR feel native instead of springy-but-linear; small surface-area change that meaningfully lifts perceived quality on the mobile-design compose genre. Keeps the same PullToRefresh.init() API + onRefresh Promise contract.
backlog.license: N/A (in-house upgrade)
backlog.note: Could ship as an opt-in option (e.g. {physics:'spring'}) on the existing snippet rather than a new file, to avoid a near-duplicate. Borderline overlap with the existing PTR — propose as enhancement, not a new component.

backlog.name: Native CSS elastic overscroll arrival (Chrome 145+) — graceful-degradation note
backlog.what: Reference/inspiration, not a dep: atomiks/elastic-scroll-polyfill was ARCHIVED March 2026 with the note that Chrome 145+ now does elastic overscroll natively and Firefox added it years ago. Signal for Design Book: the rubber-band archetype above should treat native overscroll as the baseline and only enable the JS elastic layer where it is absent or where the bounce must be inside an in-page container (not the document edge). It is also the canonical worked example of the wheel/trackpad delta -> rubber-band -> spring-back loop to reimplement from.
backlog.license: MIT (archived, read-only since 2026-03-12; last real release v2.1.0 in 2019)
backlog.note: Do not add as a CDN dep (archived, native support landed). Value is purely as the reimplementation reference and as the 'detect native, only polyfill when missing' guidance for the vanilla snippet above.

backlog.name: Two-finger affine transform gesture (rotate + scale + translate from two moving pointers, DOMMatrix-based)
backlog.what: A vanilla zero-dep PointerEvent recognizer that tracks two simultaneous pointers and computes a full 2D affine transform every frame: scale = dist(now)/dist(start), rotation = angle(now)-angle(start) via atan2, translate = midpoint(now)-midpoint(start), composed with DOMMatrix around the initial midpoint origin (translate(o) * translate(dT) * rotate(dR) * scale(dS) * translate(-o)). This is the exact interaction the vault is MISSING: pinch-zoom.js reads the two-pointer distance and midpoint but throws away the angle, so it can pinch+pan but never rotate. Adding the angle term + matrix composition turns a single-axis zoom into a true manipulation surface (object stays stuck to both fingers) for image/sticker/map/canvas editors. Worth adding because it is the canonical mobile editor gesture, it is small, and it cleanly reuses the existing pointers Map pattern already in the codebase.
backlog.license: Article/educational (MIT-equivalent reimplementation) — technique, reimplement in-house; not a dependency
backlog.note: Net-new vs interactions/pinch-zoom.js (scale+pan only, no rotation) and components/infinite-canvas.js (viewport pan/zoom, not per-object affine). Ship as e.g. interactions/transform-gesture.js exposing onTransform({matrix, scale, rotation, tx, ty}); writes target.style.transform = matrix.toString(). touch-action:none + setPointerCapture already idiomatic in the vault. Single-pointer falls back to drag; pointerup re-bases the gesture. The transform-gesture npm lib (MajorBreakfast, MIT) is the same idea but stale since 2014 — use as inspiration, reimplement vanilla.

backlog.name: On-object free-transform gizmo (8 resize handles + rotation handle, Figma/Photoshop-style, DOM + canvas)
backlog.what: The mouse/desktop counterpart to the two-finger gesture and the actual 'manipulation surface' UI: a bounding-box overlay drawn around a selected element with 8 scale handles (corners + edges), a detached rotation handle above the box, and a move body. Drag math decomposes/recomposes a 2D matrix so a rotated box still resizes along its own local axes (the hard part naive crop UIs get wrong). The vault's image-crop.js has 8 axis-aligned resize handles but NO rotation and stays axis-locked; image-tools.css rotates only via a slider/90deg buttons. A real gizmo unifies move+scale+rotate on one widget and is the core primitive for any spatial editor compose-genre (design tool, map annotator, slide-object editor, sticker placer). Pairs with candidate #1 so the same object responds to both mouse handles and two-finger touch.
backlog.license: MIT (senocular/TransformTool) — but experimental/2013-era; treat as reference, reimplement clean
backlog.note: Net-new: no free-transform/gizmo/rotate-handle anywhere in the vault (grep confirmed). Modernize with PointerEvents + DOMMatrix decompose; expose TransformGizmo.init(el,{onChange, snapAngle:15, lockAspect}) emitting {x,y,scaleX,scaleY,rotation}. Anti-slop angle: snap rotation to 15deg and show a live HUD readout (reuses cursor-hud.js coords). Konva.js Transformer / Fabric.js are the production CDN equivalents but are heavyweight canvas frameworks (MIT) — note as cdn-snippet fallback only, not the default.

backlog.name: Tiny 2D affine matrix utility (compose / invert / decompose / DOMMatrix bridge)
backlog.what: A ~3KB zero-dep helper that both candidates above silently need: multiply/translate/rotate/scale builders, applyToPoint, inverse(), and — critically — decomposeTSR() to pull rotation/scale/translation back OUT of an accumulated matrix (needed so a gizmo can resize a rotated object along local axes and so screen-space pointer deltas map to object-space). The vault currently has ZERO affine-matrix tooling (grep: no DOMMatrix, no affine in any .js); every spatial editor reinvents ad-hoc trig. A small shared primitive de-duplicates that and underpins both the gesture and the gizmo, plus screenToWorld/worldToScreen for infinite-canvas. chrvadala/transformation-matrix is MIT, actively maintained (2024-2025), tree-shakeable, and CDN-importable; epistemex/transformation-matrix-js (MIT) is the larger classic alternative.
backlog.license: MIT (chrvadala/transformation-matrix; epistemex alt also MIT)
backlog.note: Smallest, most reusable piece — or reimplement the ~40 lines vanilla to stay truly zero-dep (DOMMatrix covers compose/invert natively; only decomposeTSR is custom).
