Best shipped as utils/affine.js feeding the gesture + gizmo + infinite-canvas screen<->world. Value 3 alone (plumbing) but it is the load-bearing dependency that makes #1 and #2 robust rather than trig-hacks.

backlog.name: Gray-Scott reaction-diffusion ping-pong shader (rd.glsl.js + FBO swap runner)
backlog.what: A net-new vanilla WebGL2 snippet: a tiny 2-texture ping-pong loop (run N RD sub-steps per frame into framebuffer A/B, then a colorize pass) plus a Gray-Scott fragment shader (5-point Laplacian + standard du/dv update). Worth adding because the vault's 18 shaders ALL run on the single-pass `shaders/runner.js` fullscreen quad — `fluid.glsl.js` explicitly notes 'for a true ping-pong fluid sim, you'd need an extended runner; this version' is only an approximation. RD is the canonical stateful/iterative shader and produces organic coral/Turing-skin/labyrinth textures that mesh-gradient, aurora, plasma, voronoi and noise-flow CANNOT (those are stateless per-frame). Ships as `shaders/reaction-diffusion.glsl.js` + a small `PingPong` helper, degrading to a static fallback if WebGL2/float-textures are absent.
backlog.license: Reimplemented (Gray-Scott equation is public-domain math; reference impls robinhouston gist=no-license, timseverien CodePen). Original code, no copied source.
backlog.note: Adds reusable ping-pong/FBO infra the vault lacks (fluid.glsl.js wants exactly this). Guard behind WebGL2 + EXT_color_buffer_float / OES_texture_float feature-detect; static-image fallback for reduced-motion and the headless-Chrome verify gate.

backlog.name: Named Gray-Scott parameter presets (f/k table → designed-texture map)
backlog.what: A data map of named (f, k, dA, dB) presets so the RD snippet reads as 'designed texture' not a science demo: e.g. coral, mitosis/self-replicating-spots, worms, fingerprints, labyrinth/maze, Turing-spots, marble-veins, plus paired color ramps (bone/ivory marble, coral-pink, copper-patina, ink-on-paper). The f/k numbers are public mathematical constants (jasonwebb playground's parameterPresets.js documents ~24: 'Worms' 0.058/0.065, 'Self-replicating spots' 0.03/0.063, 'Fingerprints' 0.037/0.06, 'Mazes' 0.029/0.057, 'Turing patterns' 0.042/0.059, etc; freakingfantastic exposes 'Coral Branching', 'Mitosis', 'Worms'). Pairing each pattern with a curated colorize ramp is what makes output look art-directed — directly serves the vault's authenticity law (bespoke organic background, not a stock gradient).
backlog.license: f/k values are public-domain constants; color ramps are original. (Source playground itself is CC-BY-NC-SA — values only, not its code.)
backlog.note: Ship as `presets`+`palettes` on the RD shader object, mirroring how gradient-flow.glsl.js / mesh-gradient-wgl.glsl.js already expose named palettes. This is the piece that converts a sim into a usable design generator.

backlog.name: gpu-io (amandaghassaei) — GPGPU ping-pong abstraction
backlog.what: MIT WebGL2/WebGL1 GPGPU library that abstracts the framebuffer ping-pong + GLSL3→GLSL1 fallback and ships a reaction-diffusion example; it is the maintained successor to amandaghassaei's now-deprecated ReactionDiffusionShader. Relevant as the cdn-snippet escape hatch IF the vault ever wants a heavier, multi-layer GPGPU path (n-body, fluid, RD all on one engine) rather than a bespoke 2-FBO loop. Flagging primarily so it's evaluated against the vanilla path — for a single RD background the hand-rolled snippet is leaner and avoids a dep.
backlog.license: MIT (4 transitive MIT deps: type-checks, float16, changedpi, file-saver)
backlog.note: Likely LOSES to the vanilla snippet for this use-case (npm deps + staleness + bundle weight vs ~200 lines of self-contained WebGL2). Recommend as fallback/inspiration, not the primary integration.

backlog.name: Raw-WGSL curl-noise compute flow field with navigator.gpu -> canvas2D fallback (need()-guard cdn-snippet)
backlog.what: A single zero-Three.js background snippet that runs the flow field on a raw WebGPU compute pipeline (WGSL: snoise4 + curlNoise advecting a GPUBuffer of particle positions, rendered via a thin render pass) and, when navigator.gpu / requestAdapter() is absent, degrades to the vault's existing canvas2D curl-noise path. This is the modern GPU-compute demonstrator the vault lacks: every current WebGPU-compute asset routes through Three.js+TSL (claude-skills/webgpu-threejs-tsl), which is a heavy CDN dep; vortex.js and interactive-canvas.js cover only the CPU canvas half. The okaydev 'Dive Into WebGPU Part 4' tutorial is the canonical, reproducible recipe (curl-noise.wgsl.js, 100k particles, init+update compute passes) to reimplement from. Worth adding because it makes the GPU->CPU ladder a literal need()-guard: same contract as scene-runner.js but checking navigator.gpu instead of window.THREE, falling through to canvas2D so the save-gate still renders clean on non-WebGPU headless Chrome.
backlog.license: Tutorial code, no explicit license — reimplement clean (curl/simplex from stegu/psrdnoise MIT base, not cabbibo/glsl-curl-noise which the vault already flags as no-license)

backlog.name: webgpu/webgpu-samples — official particles (HDR) WGSL compute sample
backlog.what: The W3C WebGPU group's official samples repo (BSD-3-Clause, ~2.1k stars, ~474 commits, actively maintained). Its 'particles' sample is a no-framework, raw-WGSL compute particle simulation storing state in a GPUBuffer and rendering with an HDR pass — the reference implementation for the GPU half of the snippet above, and the cleanest permissively-licensed source to borrow buffer-layout, workgroup-dispatch, and bind-group patterns from. Worth adding as the trustworthy, license-clean grounding for any raw-WebGPU-compute vault asset; pairs with the okaydev curl-noise recipe (sample gives structure, okaydev gives the flow-field math). Not duplicative: the vault's only raw-WGSL reference today is inside the Three.js/TSL skill, never standalone.
backlog.license: BSD-3-Clause (permissive, safe to adapt)

backlog.name: WebGPU GPU->canvas2D degradation ladder as a need()-guard verification rule
backlog.what: A diagnostic/gate capability: extend the need()-guard contract so any WebGPU-compute snippet must (1) feature-detect via `if (!('gpu' in navigator)) return fallback()` AND `await navigator.gpu.requestAdapter()` returning null, (2) provide a canvas2D fallback that the headless save-gate can actually render (headless Chrome frequently has no WebGPU adapter, so a GPU-only snippet would silently produce a blank canvas and slip past a naive screenshot check). This is the moat angle: a coherence/imagery-floor rule that fails a WebGPU asset which renders blank when no adapter is present, forcing the GPU->CPU ladder. Directly relevant given MEMORY note 'WebGL draw-verify: screenshot is truth for did-it-draw' — same failure mode extends to WebGPU adapters. Net-new: the vault's need() guards only check globals (window.THREE), never an async adapter request, and no verification rule asserts a non-blank CPU fallback.
backlog.license: N/A (spec + internal capability)

backlog.name: takumifukasawa/webgpu-particle-compute-shader-curl-noise-demo
backlog.what: A standalone raw-WGSL compute-shader curl-noise particle demo (no Three.js, index.html + main.js). Topically the most on-the-nose match to the angle, useful as a second cross-reference for the WGSL curl-noise compute approach. Flagged low because it is a tiny demo (1 star, ~13 commits, not maintained as a library) with no stated license and no fallback — strictly inspiration to confirm the technique, not a dependency. Mentioned for completeness so a future researcher does not re-surface it as if addable.
backlog.license: No license stated — do NOT vendor; reference only

backlog.name: FastNoiseLite (JS port) — canonical all-in-one seedable noise module
backlog.what: MIT, zero-dependency, single self-contained JS file (npm fastnoise-lite v1.1.1, published 2024-03-05) that bundles every noise type the vault would ever need behind one seedable API: OpenSimplex2, OpenSimplex2S (smoother, fewer grid artifacts), Perlin, Value, Value-Cubic, and Cellular/Worley — plus FBM/ridged/ping-pong fractals and domain warp, in 2D and 3D. Seedable via setSeed() and reproducible. This is the strongest answer to 'single canonical seedable noise primitive': one file, one constructor, switchable noiseType, so a vault snippet can expose flow-fields, terrain, blue-ish cellular, and fBm clouds without a second dep. Worth adding because the vault currently has ZERO real noise in JS — only sin-wave 'simplex-ish' fakes in vortex.js/wavy-background.js and curl noise locked inside GLSL shaders. A canonical noise() underpins flow fields, particle drift, organic blob morphs, terrain/heightmaps, stippling, and procedural textures the generative-art skill already gestures at but cannot actually produce on CPU.
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: Ship behind a need()-guard with a tiny vanilla fallback (the simplex-noise.js / splitmix32 combo below) so it degrades gracefully. JS file is self-contained; vendor it as one file rather than via npm require (ES module / global).

backlog.name: simplex-noise.js (jwagner) — smallest/fastest pure-simplex vanilla primitive
backlog.what: MIT, ~2KB minified+gzipped, tree-shakeable, dependency-free createNoise2D/3D/4D returning -1..1; ~73M ops/sec (2D) on a 5950X — the smallest and fastest seedable simplex option. As of v4.0.3 it no longer bundles a PRNG: you pass your own seeded function (the docs use alea), which makes it the ideal VANILLA-reimplementable core — a ~40-line noise.js the vault fully owns, no CDN, no need()-guard. This is the lightweight fallback / default when FastNoiseLite's breadth is overkill (e.g. a single flow-field or wavy hero). Net-new: the vault has no standalone CPU noise module at all; this becomes the canonical zero-dep one and lets the existing fake-noise effects (vortex.js, wavy-background.js, interactive-canvas field) be re-pointed at a real, seedable, reproducible source.
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: v4 requires an external seeded PRNG — pair with the splitmix32/sfc32 seed snippet below. 4D support is a real edge over FastNoiseLite (good for looping/animated noise via a time 4th axis).

backlog.name: Tiny seedable PRNG (splitmix32 / sfc32 / mulberry32) vanilla util
backlog.what: Public-domain 4-6 line seedable PRNGs that the vault is missing entirely: splitmix32 (~4 lines, fixes mulberry32's documented flaw of skipping ~1/3 of 32-bit values), sfc32 (~6 lines, passes PractRand/BigCrush — best quality), mulberry32 (fastest). This is the seed half of a canonical generative primitive: a deterministic random() that turns one number/hash into a reproducible piece, which the generative-art skill explicitly calls for ('always seed the PRNG so a piece is reproducible from one number') but the vault provides no implementation for — and which simplex-noise.js v4 now REQUIRES as a separate dependency. Bundling a 6-line seedRandom(seed) alongside the noise module makes the whole generative stack reproducible and CDN-free.
backlog.license: Public domain ('Software licenses are annoying' — bryc/code)
backlog.note: Recommend splitmix32 as the default (fast + no value-skip bug), sfc32 when statistical quality matters. Pairs directly with simplex-noise.js v4's create*(prng) signature. Also add a string->uint32 hash (e.g. cyrb53/xmur3) so seeds can be human strings.

backlog.name: OpenSimplex2 / OpenSimplex2S artifact-quality decision (skill knowledge)
backlog.what: Not a usable JS dep (reference is C/Java; JS ports like open-simplex-noise are ~5 years stale), but the decision knowledge is net-new and load-bearing for 'choosing a single canonical module.' The tradeoff: classic Perlin shows axis-aligned/directional streaking; original simplex (jwagner) is fastest but has subtle directional bias in 3D/4D; OpenSimplex2S ('SuperSimplex', reachable in JS only via FastNoiseLite) gives the most isotropic, artifact-free gradients at a slight speed cost — best when noise is the visible surface (mesh-gradient displacement, terrain, large organic fields) rather than a hidden flow-field. Worth capturing as a short decision tree in the generative-art skill: 'flow-field/cheap drift -> simplex-noise.js 2D; visible terrain/displacement/large fields -> FastNoiseLite OpenSimplex2S; need 4D loop -> simplex-noise.js 4D; cellular/cracked/Voronoi look -> FastNoiseLite Cellular.' Note the simplex patent expired 2022-01-08, so all of these are now freely usable.
backlog.license: MIT (OpenSimplex2 ref); FastNoiseLite MIT is the practical JS carrier
backlog.note: Inspiration/decision-tree only — do NOT add a stale standalone open-simplex JS port as a dep. The actual code path is FastNoiseLite (OpenSimplex2/2S) above.

backlog.name: Generative brand-identity compose-genre (seed in → mark + pattern + palette out)
backlog.what: A new compose-genre archetype: input one brand string/hex, output a coherent identity sheet — a parametric mark (shapes placed on an N×N grid under an aesthetic ruleset, à la the MIT Media Lab 7×7 system that yields ~40k SVG permutations), a matching seamless tiling pattern, and a palette, all reproducible from the seed. This is the headline net-new: it directly composes the vault's existing determinism + palette infra (palette-generator.js, gradient-avatar's hashing) into a real artifact the engine emits and VERIFIES. The seed→params→render contract is the spec: brandString/hex → 32-bit seed → PRNG stream → params {grid positions, motif choice, rotation, palette index} → SVG. Distinct from gradient-avatar (single identicon) and from CSS pattern packs (static, not seed-parametric).
backlog.license: Concept/technique (reference systems: MIT Media Lab by Pentagram/Richard The; Whitney responsive-W by Experimental Jetset) — reimplement, no dep
backlog.note: Reference systems are case studies/inspiration, not deps. Builds on components/gradient-avatar.js + utils/palette-generator.js + structure.css/svg.skill.md. Verifier already enforces coherence/imagery floor — a generated mark+pattern satisfies the imagery floor without a photo.

backlog.name: seedable-rng (xmur3 + sfc32 / mulberry32 / splitmix32)
backlog.what: A ~15-line zero-dep primitive: xmur3(string) hashes a brand string/hex into a 32-bit seed, then sfc32 (or splitmix32, which has a full period unlike mulberry32) turns that seed into a REPRODUCIBLE STREAM of float draws in [0,1). This is the missing determinism substrate. The vault's gradient-avatar uses FNV-1a but only derives ONE number / one color set — there is no reusable seeded PRNG that yields many independent reproducible draws (needed for placing N shapes, choosing motifs, rotations, pattern offsets). This snippet is the foundation the brand-identity genre and any seeded-procedural snippet sits on; also useful standalone for reproducible particle/flow-field seeds. Public-domain reference implementations.
backlog.license: Public domain (bryc/code) / MIT-equivalent reference implementations
backlog.note: generative-art.skill.md mentions seeded randomness only via p5 randomSeed()/fxhash $fx.rand() (workflow notes), not a standalone vanilla PRNG snippet. grep for mulberry/sfc32/xmur3/prng in vault returns only that skill — no actual primitive ships. Prefer splitmix32/sfc32 over mulberry32 (full period).

backlog.name: prng.js — vanilla sfc32 + mulberry32 + xmur3 (bryc/code pattern, public domain)
backlog.what: A ~30-line zero-dep module vendoring the canonical seedable PRNGs: sfc32 (128-bit state, 2^128 period — the algorithm fxhash standardized on for $fx.rand()), mulberry32 (32-bit, single-int seed, tiny — ideal default), and xmur3 to expand a string/hash seed into the four 32-bit words sfc32 needs. All operate purely in integer/Uint32 domain via Math.imul + |0, so the sequence is byte-identical across V8/SpireMonkey/JavaScriptCore and Node, unlike Math.random() (unseedable, engine-specific per the V8 blog). API: const rand = sfc32(...xmur3(seed)); rand()->[0,1), plus rand.int(n)/rand.pick(arr)/rand.range(a,b) helpers. This is THE missing primitive: the vault mentions seeded randomness conceptually (p5 randomSeed, $fx.rand) but ships no actual cross-platform RNG an engine can call. Public-domain functions, so directly vendorable (no license risk, unlike the unlicensed hl-gen.js).
backlog.license: Public domain / Unlicense (bryc/code reference functions)
backlog.note: Vendor the ~30 LOC directly — do NOT add a runtime dep. Standardize the engine on sfc32 as default (matches fxhash) with mulberry32 as the tiny single-seed option. Avoid xoshiro128**: bryc and others document biased low bits (fails linearity/binary-rank). gradient-avatar.js's FNV hash is avatar-only and not a general RNG, so no overlap.

backlog.name: reproducible-render verification gate ('same seed = same pixels')
backlog.what: A headless-Chrome diagnostic that extends the existing verify save-gate: render a seeded composition twice (or on two viewport-independent passes), hash the canvas/PNG bytes (e.g. SHA-256 of toDataURL or getImageData), and FAIL if the two hashes differ — proving the piece is actually deterministic. It also statically flags determinism-breakers the engine must never emit in seeded mode: Math.random(), Date.now()/performance.now() used for generation (vs frameCount), and reads of screen size/devicePixelRatio/geolocation/battery — exactly the pitfall list Highlight's hl-gen.js FAQ documents. This is the moat: the vault already verifies 'renders clean / no overflow / coherence / imagery floor' but has no reproducibility gate, and 'same seed = same pixels' is the core promise of a reproducible-art engine. Pairs with the prng.js snippet (the gate enforces that the snippet is actually used).
backlog.license: MIT-compatible (pattern; Highlight FAQ is reference/prior-art, repo itself unlicensed)
backlog.note: Build on the existing headless-Chrome verify harness. Two gotchas to encode: (1) WebGL needs preserveDrawingBuffer or a forced readback before hashing (matches the vault's existing WebGL draw-verify memory note); (2) GPU-rasterized canvas/text can differ across machines, so for the strict gate run both passes in the same Chrome and compare, and treat cross-machine identity as a CPU-render/2D-only guarantee. Highlight repo has no LICENSE file — treat its FAQ as inspiration for the pitfall checklist, not copyable code.

backlog.name: seeded-generative / reproducible-art skill (.skill.md decision tree)
backlog.what: A decision-tree skill that codifies HOW to write reproducible generative pieces in the vault: choose mulberry32 (one int seed) vs sfc32 (string/hash seed via xmur3, fxhash-grade) ; route ALL randomness through the single seeded rand() (never Math.random); drive animation by frameCount not wall-clock; keep RNG call-order stable (the #1 determinism bug per hl-gen FAQ); make layout seed-derived not viewport-derived; and how to seed GLSL noise deterministically (pass seed as a uniform, since the vault's noise is GLSL/CSS). Cross-references generative-art.skill.md and shaders.skill.md rather than duplicating them — it fills the 'reproducibility discipline' gap those skills explicitly punt on ('always seed the PRNG' is stated but never operationalized).
backlog.license: Documentation/pattern (fxhash docs + hl-gen FAQ as references)
backlog.note: Keep tight and additive per the vault's additive-only policy. Should explicitly defer to generative-art.skill.md for flow-fields/plotter output and shaders.skill.md for noise — its unique job is the PRNG choice + determinism checklist that feeds the verification gate above.

backlog.name: Seed-reproducibility gate (render-twice self-consistency hash/bounded-diff)
backlog.what: A NET-NEW verification capability for seeded generative-art snippets: render the same seed twice in one harness run (two fresh contexts, identical viewport), capture both via page.screenshot(), and assert the two outputs are byte-identical (sha256 of the PNG/raw RGBA) OR within a bounded pixelmatch diff (e.g. mismatch ratio < 0.001). This proves the piece is actually deterministic from its seed — the exact contract fxhash/Art Blocks long-form art lives or dies on (same hash -> identical pixels). The vault's existing render-smoke only proves a canvas 'drew' (variance + color count) and the §5 layer only does BASELINE regression diffing (stored image vs current, OS/GPU-keyed). Neither verifies SELF-CONSISTENCY of a seed within a single run, so it requires no committed baselines and is GPU/OS-portable (both renders share the same machine). This extends the save-gate moat: overflow -> coherence -> imagery-floor -> seed-determinism. It is reusable code (sha256 + the already-pinned pixelmatch@7), not a dependency. Surfaces as a new --seed-check mode on render-smoke.mjs / a coherence_check sibling that only runs for snippets tagged seed/deterministic/generative.
backlog.license: n/a (own code; uses already-vendored pixelmatch MIT + node:crypto)
backlog.note: Distinct from tools/RENDER-HARNESS-SPEC.md §5 (baseline regression diff vs a committed image, OS/GPU-keyed). This is intra-run self-consistency (render N times, assert equal) — no baselines, GPU-portable. generative-art.skill.md covers the CREATOR side (canvas-sketch/fxhash seeding) but has no gate that VERIFIES determinism.

backlog.name: Chrome deterministic-rendering flag profile (--deterministic-mode + --disable-skia-runtime-opts + software raster)
backlog.what: The exact Chrome launch-flag profile that makes the seed-determinism gate above trustworthy by killing the known nondeterminism sources. --deterministic-mode is an experimental meta-flag that sets --run-all-compositor-stages-before-draw, --disable-new-content-rendering-timeout, --disable-features=PaintHolding, --in-process-gpu and software GL; --disable-skia-runtime-opts disables runtime-detected CPU SIMD paths in Skia so raster matches across hardware; software rasterization (the vault's existing SwANGLE --use-gl=angle --use-angle=swiftshader-webgl --enable-unsafe-swiftshader, plus --disable-gpu for 2D) replaces GPU raster, which research measures at 12-17 inconsistent px per 10k-px region from driver texture-cache jitter vs ~99.7% pixel-perfect on CPU raster across 100 runs. Combined with the vault's existing --force-color-profile=srgb. The skill text accompanying it should also pin the nondeterminism taxonomy to neutralize before asserting: unseeded Math.random (seed the PRNG / mock it), wall-clock + rAF + Date.now drift (freeze time / seek by integer frame = floor(frame)/fps), antialiasing + subpixel hinting (snap to integer device pixels, includeAA:false or perceptual tolerance), OffscreenCanvas/worker rendering (bypasses main-thread capture), and float-order GPU shortcuts (software raster). IMPLEMENTATION CAVEAT (2026): HeadlessExperimental.beginFrame was removed in Chromium 147+, so the atomic-frame capture must use Page.captureScreenshot / Playwright page.screenshot() with rAF timing controls — which the vault's harness already does — NOT beginFrame.
backlog.license: n/a (Chromium runtime flags, public)
backlog.note: Partial overlap: vault already uses srgb + SwANGLE for software WebGL. NET-NEW pieces are --deterministic-mode, --disable-skia-runtime-opts, the CPU-vs-GPU pixel-perfection rationale, and the documented nondeterminism taxonomy (RNG/timers/AA/float/OffscreenCanvas) as a gate-design skill. beginFrame-removed-in-147 nuance prevents shipping a broken capture path.

backlog.name: Viewport-invariance / responsive-seed check
backlog.what: A second axis of the determinism gate, unique to RESPONSIVE generative art: render the same seed at two viewport sizes (e.g. 1440x900 and 768x1024, or 1x vs 2x DPR) and assert the composition is structurally equivalent after normalization — same dominant-color signature / same perceptual hash / same number of drawn primitives — rather than byte-equal (sizes differ, so exact hash cannot match). Catches the common generative bug where layout/particle count is keyed off raw pixel width instead of a seeded normalized space, so a piece 'looks different' on mobile vs desktop despite the same seed. canvas-sketch's settings.dimensions + pixelsPerInch model (already the vault's blessed export harness) is the reference for size-independent seeding. Complements the render-twice gate: render-twice proves temporal determinism; viewport-invariance proves spatial/seed-normalization determinism. Pairs naturally with a perceptual-hash comparator (dHash/pHash, ~30 lines, no dep) so 'equivalent at any size' is checkable with a bounded Hamming distance.
backlog.license: MIT (canvas-sketch reference; own comparator code)
backlog.note: Net-new. The vault verifies a canvas drew at ONE viewport (1100x800 fixed in render-smoke); nothing checks that a seed produces equivalent output across viewports/DPR. Needs perceptual hash (not byte hash) because dimensions differ — see candidate 4.

backlog.name: imagehash-style perceptual-hash tolerance tier (dHash/pHash bounded-equality)
backlog.what: A small vanilla-reimplementable perceptual-hash comparator (dHash = 64-bit gradient hash, or pHash = 32x32 DCT low-freq hash; ~30-40 lines of JS over getImageData) that gives the determinism gate a graceful tolerance tier between 'byte-identical' (too strict — fails on benign AA jitter) and 'looks roughly similar' (too loose). Used as: strict tier = sha256 byte-equal (CPU raster, same viewport, must match exactly); tolerant tier = Hamming distance of dHash <= N (for viewport-invariance and for hardware-GL runs where 1-2px AA jitter is expected). dHash specifically generates fewer false positives than average-hash and is computing-speed-cheap. This makes the gate robust to the unavoidable antialiasing/subpixel nondeterminism the research flags, so it gates real determinism bugs without false-failing on sub-pixel noise. Pure-JS, zero native dep, runs in-page after getImageData — same readback path the harness already uses. Distinct from pixelmatch (per-pixel count) which the vault has only for baseline diffing: a perceptual hash is a compact, order-independent fingerprint suited to 'same seed -> same fingerprint' assertions and cross-size comparison.
backlog.license: imagehash is BSD-2-Clause (reference only); reimplement in vanilla JS (no dep)
backlog.note: Reimplement, don't add a Python/native dep (imagehash is Python). The value is the dHash/pHash ALGORITHM as a tolerance tier on top of the render-twice gate; pixelmatch already covers exact per-pixel diffing for the strict tier.

backlog.name: d3-delaunay
backlog.what: Standalone, no-dependency Voronoi/Delaunay geometry module (wraps Delaunator internally). The high-level fit: delaunay.from(points).voronoi([0,0,w,h]) yields voronoi.cellPolygon(i) -> closed [[x,y],...] vertex arrays directly usable as SVG <polygon> points, canvas fill paths, OR CSS clip-path:polygon(). This single primitive powers all three in-scope layouts: (1) STIPPLING via Lloyd relaxation — iterate points toward each cell's weighted centroid (sum pixel-darkness-weighted positions / weights) to converge a centroidal Voronoi tessellation that resolves an image into dots; (2) MOSAIC — seed points (random/grid/poisson), fill each cellPolygon with the source image's average color; (3) CELL-SHATTER — render one DOM fragment / clip-path shard per cell and animate them outward. Worth adding because the vault has ZERO geometric Voronoi capability — only a procedural GLSL cell-noise shader (shaders/voronoi.glsl.js) that paints a pattern but produces no relaxable points or polygons. CDN-safe behind a need()-guard, degrades to a plain grid/image if the lib fails to load.
backlog.license: ISC (permissive, MIT-equivalent — note: question said MIT, it is ISC which is functionally identical and CDN-safe)
backlog.note: ISC not MIT (equivalent permissive). CDN: https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/d3-delaunay@6 (UMD exposes global `d3`, use d3.Delaunay). No-dependency build bundles delaunator; ~12KB min+gzip region. Vault's shaders/voronoi.glsl.js is a procedural GPU pattern and does NOT cover this geometric use.

backlog.name: Delaunator
backlog.what: The tiny (~3KB) Mapbox sweep-hull Delaunay triangulation engine that d3-delaunay sits on top of. Operates on a flat Float64Array of coords and returns a flat .triangles index array — extremely fast and allocation-light. Worth noting as the leaner alternative when you only need TRIANGLE meshes rather than Voronoi cells: low-poly/'crystalline' image triangulation, triangulated mesh-gradient backdrops, or triangle-shard shatter effects. For most Design Book layouts d3-delaunay is the better pick (gives both triangles and cells + centroid rendering); Delaunator is the right call only if you want the absolute-smallest dependency and will compute centroids/cells by hand. Recommend shipping d3-delaunay as the primary and mentioning Delaunator as the lighter triangle-only option in the same snippet's comment.
backlog.license: ISC (permissive)
backlog.note: ISC. CDN: https://unpkg.com/delaunator@5/delaunator.min.js (UMD global `Delaunator`). Largely redundant if d3-delaunay is added (which bundles it) — propose as a secondary/alternative, not a separate snippet.

backlog.name: Lloyd-relaxed CVT stippling + Voronoi-mosaic technique (vanilla, on d3-delaunay)
backlog.what: The reusable algorithm to package as the actual snippet around d3-delaunay, so it ships as a turnkey generative effect rather than a raw lib. Pattern: (a) sample N candidate points by rejection sampling weighted to source-image darkness; (b) each frame, build the Voronoi, accumulate per-cell weighted centroids by scanning pixels (or cell area for unweighted), move each point to its centroid (Lloyd's iteration); (c) after ~10-80 iterations the points form a centroidal Voronoi tessellation — render as dots (stipple), filled cells (mosaic), or detached shards (shatter). All three are one codebase with a `mode` flag, seeded for determinism (fits the vault's seeded-compose model), and animatable (the relaxation steps themselves are a satisfying convergence animation). This is the 'how', distinct from the library 'what' — it is the thing that makes d3-delaunay land as a generative-art effect and a verifiable composed output (renders clean, no overflow). Document in generative-art.skill.md alongside p5/canvas-sketch.
backlog.license: MIT/ISC (Bostock notebook + d3-delaunay)
backlog.note: Reimplement as vanilla JS wrapping the cdn d3-delaunay; weighted variant needs a hidden canvas getImageData pass for pixel darkness. Cross-reference generative-art.skill.md (currently lists flow fields/plotter but no CVT/stippling).

backlog.name: Deterministic L-system turtle (parametric, 2D + isometric) — vanilla snippet
backlog.what: A tiny zero-dep L-system + turtle-graphics engine: axiom + production rules (e.g. 'F => F[+F][-F]'), deterministic string-rewrite generations, branching via a [ ] context stack, render to a CanvasRenderingContext2D. Worth adding because the vault has NO grammar-based/rewrite-system generative tool — its generative set is all noise/flow-field/particle/shader (continuous fields), whereas an L-system produces crisp recursive BOTANICAL/branching vector marks (ferns, vines, dragon curves, lightning, coral, decorative dividers/corners) that look like nothing in the current effect library. Reimplement as a ~60-line vanilla snippet (lsystem.js) so it is dependency-free and license-clean, seeded by a number for reproducibility. KEY UPGRADE for the question's 'dimension-agnostic' ask: the turtle's step is just a 2D vector, so swap the forward-projection through a 2D-to-isometric transform (x'=(x-y)*cos30, y'=(x+y)*sin30) and the SAME axiom renders flat OR isometric — pairs directly with the vault's existing effects/isometric-grid.css. Add an SVG-path output mode (turtle 'F' emits 'L x y') so it also feeds the svg.skill plotter/AxiDraw path and stays crisp at any scale.
backlog.license: MIT (rgmiranda/turtle, the seed reference)
backlog.note: Reference lib is canvas-only and deterministic-only (no stochastic, no SVG). Reimplement in ~60 lines vanilla and ADD: (1) seeded PRNG for optional stochastic rules, (2) SVG-path output mode, (3) isometric projection toggle. MIT + zero-dep means clean to vendor. Cross-ref generative-art.skill.md + svg.skill.md + isometric-grid.css.

backlog.name: lindenmayer (nylki) — parametric/stochastic/context-sensitive L-system reference
backlog.what: The most feature-complete classic JS L-system: classic + parametric (objects-as-symbols with numeric params) + stochastic (weighted/function productions) + context-sensitive, MIT, zero runtime deps, renderer-agnostic (you supply 'final' functions, so it drives canvas, SVG, or three.js). Value here is as the SPEC/inspiration source for the snippet above — it documents the full Algorithmic-Beauty-of-Plants command set and the parametric+stochastic production model that makes plants look organic rather than mechanically self-similar (probabilistic branch angles, age parameters). Lift its API shape; don't ship it as a dep because of the maintenance gap.
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: Stale maintenance is the reason to treat as inspiration, not a CDN dep. Borrow its parametric+stochastic production model (the part rgmiranda/turtle lacks) when building the vanilla snippet.

backlog.name: Space-colonization branching (Runions/Algorithmic Botany) — vanilla generative snippet
backlog.what: Space colonization grows organic branch/vein networks toward scattered 'attractor' points (leaf venation, tree canopies, coral, river deltas, root systems) — a fundamentally different look from L-systems (irregular, space-filling, non-self-similar) and absent from the vault. The algorithm is small and reimplementable in vanilla JS: place attractors, each node grows toward the average of nearby attractors within attraction distance, prune attractors inside kill distance, then thicken branches from tips to root. Deterministic when the attractor set is seeded. Outputs canvas or SVG paths (crisp vector botanical marks). Excellent as a hero background, generative divider, or 'grown' logo treatment. Worth adding as a companion to the L-system snippet so Design Book covers BOTH grammar-based (L-system) and attractor-based (space colonization) botanical generation.
backlog.license: Jason Webb reference repo license unclear (verify before lifting code); the algorithm itself (Runions et al., U. Calgary) is public/academic — reimplement from the paper, not the repo
backlog.note: Reference repo depends on KDBush + vec2 and has no documented seed/license clarity — reimplement vanilla from the published algorithm with a seeded attractor distribution for determinism. SVG export of dense networks is heavy; cap node count or rasterize. Inspiration, not a dependency.

backlog.name: SwissGL (google/swissgl) — Neural CA + GPGPU sim family
backlog.what: Minimalist <1000-LOC single-file WebGL2 wrapper (Apache-2.0) by Alexander Mordvintsev (the NCA inventor) that ships self-contained demos: NeuralCA.js (Growing/Self-Organising Textures), ReactionDiffusion.js, ParticleLenia.js, FancyLenia.js, Physarum.js, GameOfLife.js. Each demo is one drop-in JS file with the tiny 8-bit-quantized model weights baked into the GLSL shaders — the whole NCA runs fully on the GPU, no model download, no ONNX/WASM. Worth adding because it gives the vault genuinely 'alive' self-organising / self-healing regenerating textures (grow-from-seed, damage-and-heal) that nothing in the vault currently produces — the generative-art skill is p5/canvas-sketch/three/paper and the shaders skill is GLSL primitives; neither does emergent CA. Best as a guarded cdn-snippet: load swissgl.min.js behind a need()-guard with a WebGL2 capability check, degrade to a static gradient-mesh/aurora background (both already in vault) when WebGL2 is absent.
backlog.license: Apache-2.0 (CDN: @pluvial/swissgl on jsDelivr, dist/swissgl.min.js). Demo site cells2pixels is CC-BY-SA-4.0 but the SwissGL lib + repo demos are Apache-2.0.
backlog.note: License clean (Apache-2.0). Caveat: author calls it unstable — pin a version. CDN is the community @pluvial mirror (v0.0.0), so vendoring the single swissgl.min.js into the vault is the safest guard; it's <1000 LOC zero-dep.

backlog.name: Gray-Scott Reaction-Diffusion (ping-pong fragment shader)
backlog.what: The classic Gray-Scott reaction-diffusion system rendered as a two-texture ping-pong GLSL pass — coral/fingerprint/Turing patterns that grow, merge and self-heal organically. This is the NON-neural cousin of NCA: no model weights at all, just two PDE update equations in a shader, so it is fully vanilla-reimplementable as a zero-dep .js/.glsl snippet (no CDN, no license drag). Worth adding as the lightweight, always-available 'alive texture' that the vault lacks — it is the degradable fallback tier under the heavier SwissGL NCA, and a strong standalone generative background in its own right. Reference implementations are everywhere (pmneila/jsexp, SwissGL demo) and the math is public-domain.
backlog.license: Algorithm public-domain (Gray-Scott / Pearson 1993); reference code Apache-2.0 (SwissGL) / MIT (community ports) — reimplement clean.
backlog.note: Confirmed not in vault — effects/ has caustics, fire, flickering-grid, gradient-mesh but no reaction-diffusion/CA. Pure-shader, no NN, no dependency — cleanest possible add and the graceful-degradation tier for the NCA cdn-snippet.

backlog.name: Generative-life skill: NCA / Reaction-Diffusion / Lenia decision tree
backlog.what: A small .skill.md that captures WHEN and HOW to deploy 'emergent / self-organising' visuals — the decision tree the vault is missing: reaction-diffusion (vanilla, always-on) vs. SwissGL NeuralCA (heavy, guarded, self-healing PBR/3D textures) vs. Particle-Lenia/Physarum (artificial-life motion). Crucially it records the corrective engineering fact this research surfaced: the in-browser NCA path is WebGL/GLSL with tiny (<10k-param) 8-bit-quantized weights baked into shaders — NOT onnxruntime-web. ONNX/WASM (multi-MB) is strictly heavier and the wrong tool here, so the skill should steer composition to the GLSL path and reserve ONNX only for models that can't be hand-written as shaders. Sits alongside generative-art.skill.md and shaders.skill.md and cross-references them rather than duplicating.
backlog.license: N/A (vault-authored decision-tree doc; cites CC-BY Distill articles + Apache-2.0 SwissGL).
backlog.note: Anti-dup checked: generative-art.skill.md (p5/canvas-sketch/three/paper) and shaders.skill.md cover none of NCA/reaction-diffusion/Lenia. This skill is the glue that prevents the cdn-snippet from being misused and records the ONNX-vs-GLSL perf verdict.

backlog.name: Particle Lenia (continuous artificial-life motion field)
backlog.what: A continuous-space artificial-life system (SwissGL ParticleLenia.js / FancyLenia.js) where a fixed set of particles self-organise into pulsing, swimming, cell-like clusters from simple interaction rules — perpetual, non-repeating organic motion with no training, no model weights, fully on-GPU. Distinct from NCA (no neural net) and from the vault's existing particle/flow-field work (which is noise-driven, not emergent-dynamical). Worth adding as inspiration / optional cdn-snippet for 'living' hero backgrounds and loaders that feel biological rather than procedural. Same Apache-2.0 SwissGL host as the NCA path, so it rides the same guard.
backlog.license: Apache-2.0 (SwissGL demo); algorithm from Mordvintsev et al., open.
backlog.note: Flag as inspiration first (reimplementable as a vanilla compute-on-GPU snippet) to avoid over-committing to the 'unstable' SwissGL API; promotes to cdn-snippet if the SwissGL guard is adopted for the NCA item. Not duplicative of vault flow-fields/particles — those are noise-based, this is emergent-dynamical.

backlog.name: Vanilla seedable boids field (spatial-hash neighbor lookup + named behavior presets)
backlog.what: A net-new zero-dep canvas snippet: classic Reynolds separation/alignment/cohesion with a uniform spatial-hash grid for O(n) neighbor queries (so it scales to a few thousand 2D agents at 60fps in pure canvas), driven by an injected seedable PRNG (mulberry32) so the same seed always lays out the same flock — matching the vault's deterministic compose engine. Ships named design presets (e.g. 'murmuration', 'school', 'swarm', 'embers', 'calm-drift') that are just {perceptionRadius, alignW, cohesionW, separationW, maxSpeed, turnLimit, jitter} bundles. Worth adding because the vault has particles (O(n^2) connect lines), curl-noise vortex, and a flow-field mode, but NO actual flocking/emergent-agent behavior with tunable design presets — and none of it is seed-deterministic. The behavior model is proven by @thi.ng/boids (7 composable, weighted behaviors + HashGrid2/3 accel) and the preset taxonomy by the Antlii BOIDS playground; reimplement, don't depend (thi.ng is ALPHA + headless + 7 internal deps; Antlii has no public source/license).
backlog.license: Apache-2.0 (reference model: @thi.ng/boids) — clean-room vanilla reimplementation, no code copied
backlog.note: Anti-dup verified: effects/particles.js is O(n^2) connect-lines + mouse-repel with Math.random() (no flocking, no seed, no spatial hash); effects/vortex.js is curl-noise orbit (not flocking); backgrounds/interactive-canvas.js has 'field'(flow-field) + 'network' modes but no boids; only a WebGPU/TSL compute-shader boids EXAMPLE exists in claude-skills docs (inspiration, not a usable vanilla snippet). Add a mulberry32 seed param to match the deterministic compose engine; respect prefers-reduced-motion (cut count + freeze).

backlog.name: @thi.ng/boids
backlog.what: Headless, modular flocking engine (~2KB brotli ESM): 7 composable weighted behaviors (alignment, cohesion, separation, pursuit/seek, field-based steering, polyline follow, Braitenberg steering), 2D+3D via defBoid2()/defBoid3(), and pluggable spatial acceleration through the IBoidAccel interface (HashGrid2/3 from @thi.ng/geom-accel). The cleanest reference for HOW to structure a tunable, preset-driven flocking system with proper neighbor-query acceleration. It is the model to mirror for the vanilla snippet above — not a direct dep because it's ALPHA, ships no renderer, and drags in 7 internal @thi.ng packages, which fights the vault's zero-dep VANILLA bias.
backlog.license: Apache-2.0
backlog.note: Permissive (Apache-2.0) but ALPHA + headless + 7 internal deps = use as the architecture/behavior reference for the vanilla-snippet, not as a CDN dep. Determinism is achievable by feeding it a seeded vector source; seeding is not built-in.

backlog.name: @individual11/boidsjs
backlog.what: TypeScript, zero-dep-core canvas flocking lib (on jsDelivr, ESM+CJS) with three selectable algorithms — classic Reynolds (exposes alignmentWeight/cohesionWeight/separationWeight/perceptionRadius), optimized 7-nearest-neighbor selection, and dynamic flow fields (scale/strength/timeShift) — plus a first-class React wrapper and a clean Engine({canvas, boidCount, algorithm, reynoldsOptions, flowFieldOptions}) API. Confirms the exact design-tunable parameter surface (named cohesion/alignment/separation weights + flow-field blend) the vault snippet should expose, and shows flow-field steering can be a boids MODE rather than a separate angle.
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: MIT + zero-dep + CDN-loadable, so technically dep-safe, BUT v0.x with ~0 downloads and no documented deterministic seeding — too immature to ship as a cdn-snippet. Use as the API/preset-surface reference for the vanilla-snippet; borrow its Engine option shape and 7-NN optimization idea.

backlog.name: Antlii BOIDS preset taxonomy (parametric flocking playground)
backlog.what: A p5.js parametric playground (by Anatolii Babii) whose tunable-parameter set is an excellent ready-made vocabulary for design presets: alignment force, cohesion strength, separation intensity, steering/turn limit, velocity drag, noise angle, neighbor-consideration count, vision radius, turning sharpness, plus speed/turn-rate-driven coloring. It also validates the 'saveable named presets' UX. Pure reference for naming + ranges of the snippet's behavior presets — no usable code, no public license.
backlog.license: Unknown / none published (closed showcase)
backlog.note: SaaS-style showcase, no source/license -> inspiration only. Mine its parameter taxonomy and 'save/reload named presets' pattern to name and bound the vault snippet's presets (murmuration / school / swarm / embers / calm-drift).

backlog.name: Differential growth (organic-line) — vanilla SVG snippet
backlog.what: Node-and-edge growth loop that buckles a closed/open polyline into coral, brain-coral, meander and lichen forms. Five rules: attract toward chain neighbors, align to minimize local curvature, repel within a min-separation radius (spatial-hash/grid accelerated), inject a new node when an edge exceeds max-length, and bias growth for asymmetry. With a seeded PRNG the output is fully deterministic and exports as an SVG polyline/path — exactly the print-quality stroke output the compose engine wants. NOT currently in the vault: the closest neighbors (lava-lamp metaballs, flow-field canvas, mesh gradients) are pixel/blur effects, not growing vector geometry. Core algorithm is ~150 lines and trivially reimplementable zero-dep, which is required because the canonical reference impls are license-restricted (see note).
backlog.license: Algorithm is public/unencumbered; canonical reference impl (Jason Webb, jasonwebb/2d-differential-growth-experiments) is CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 — NON-COMMERCIAL, do NOT vendor it. Reimplement from the documented rules instead (the algorithm itself is not copyrightable).
backlog.note: Ship as a clean-room vanilla .js emitting <path>/<polyline> + seeded PRNG; degrades to a static seed render with reduced-motion. Treat the Webb repo as INSPIRATION only (CC BY-NC-SA). This is the marquee net-new generative family for editorial/poster compose genres.

backlog.name: d3-contour
backlog.what: Marching-squares contour/isoband generator: feed it a scalar field (e.g. seeded simplex/fBm noise the vault already has, or a reaction-diffusion buffer) and it returns GeoJSON MultiPolygons that map 1:1 to SVG <path> d-strings — instant topographic / contour-map / 'reaction-front' organic line art. Deterministic (no internal RNG; same field in → same paths out), so it satisfies the compose engine's reproducibility requirement and renders clean to print SVG. Fills the contour/isoline leg of the angle. Not in the vault — data-viz is charts only (bar/line/pie/sankey/treemap), no contour/marching-squares, and the AGPL-licensed MarchingSquares.js alternative is unusable.
backlog.license: ISC (permissive, MIT-equivalent)
backlog.note: CDN: https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/d3-contour/+esm . Pairs perfectly with the vault's existing simplex/fBm noise as the input field. Wrap behind a need()-guard; no DOM dep — pure path-string output. Strongly prefer this over GPL/AGPL contour libs (MarchingSquares.js is AGPL-3.0 — flag as inspiration only).

backlog.name: d3-delaunay (Voronoi + Lloyd relaxation → packing / stippling)
backlog.what: Delaunator-backed Voronoi/Delaunay engine that emits SVG path strings directly (when no canvas context is passed) and supports in-place Lloyd relaxation. Covers the circle-packing / Voronoi-stippling leg: seed points (deterministic via a seeded PRNG) → relax → render cells or centroid dots for organic poster fills, weighted stippling, and cellular 'cracked-surface' textures. Deterministic given fixed input points. Net-new: the vault has metaballs and dot-grids but no Voronoi/Delaunay/cellular tessellation or relaxation primitive anywhere.
backlog.license: ISC (permissive)
backlog.note: CDN: https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/d3-delaunay/+esm . voronoi.render()/renderCell() return SVG d-strings — no canvas needed for print output. Provides true circle-packing-adjacent cellular layouts that the lighter Kusama-style recursive packer (Mads Stoumann, DEV) can complement as a pure-vanilla fallback.

backlog.name: Recursive circle packing (Kusama-style) — vanilla SVG snippet
backlog.what: The simplest deterministic circle-packing approach: place candidate circles, grow each until it touches a neighbor or the bounds, reject overlaps, repeat with a seeded PRNG. ~60-100 lines of zero-dep JS emitting <circle> elements — gives Kusama-dot posters, packed-disk fills, and bubble compositions with no library at all. A lightweight vanilla companion to the d3-delaunay cell approach for when a CDN dep isn't warranted. Not in the vault (dot-grid backgrounds are CSS uniform grids, not size-varied collision packing).
backlog.license: Tutorial code is MIT-spirit / freely usable; reimplement clean-room (algorithm is trivial and public).
backlog.note: Pure vanilla, seeded, SVG-native — ideal zero-dep poster fill. Lower value than the differential-growth/d3 pair because it's a single static pattern, but it rounds out the circle-packing leg without a dependency.

backlog.name: Canvas2D Bayer / ordered-dithering + Floyd-Steinberg/Atkinson error-diffusion pack
backlog.what: A vanilla JS, canvas2D-only dithering snippet: a recursive Bayer threshold-matrix generator (2x2->4x4->8x8) for ordered/ordered-grain dithering, plus Floyd-Steinberg and Atkinson error-diffusion kernels operating directly on getImageData/putImageData. Takes any source (image, CSS gradient drawn to canvas, or procedural noise) and quantizes it to a 1-4 bit palette for true retro/riso/lo-fi/e-ink looks. NET-NEW because the vault's only dithering is CSS/SVG-turbulence (stepped-gradient.css .dither-*) which fakes the look with SVG feTurbulence and cannot do real per-pixel error diffusion or palette quantization. Pure 2D context, zero deps, no WebGL.
backlog.license: Public-domain algorithm (Bayer 1973, Floyd-Steinberg 1976, Atkinson); reference impls MIT (DitherSpace, vanilla JS)
backlog.note: Distinct from effects/stepped-gradient.css (.dither-* = SVG feTurbulence fake) and shaders/halftone.glsl.js (GLSL). This is real CPU error-diffusion + ordered Bayer in a 2D canvas. Reimplement directly — no dependency.

backlog.name: Domain-warped fBM value-noise field on canvas2D (Inigo Quilez recipe)
backlog.what: A canvas2D port of IQ's fbm(p + fbm(p + fbm(p))) layered domain-warp recipe: a tiny CPU value-noise function (hash + bilinear smoothstep interpolation), fBM octave summation, and two-level warp using the canonical offsets (5.2,1.3 / 1.7,9.2 / 8.3,2.8), rendered to an offscreen 2D canvas and mapped through a palette LUT for marble/cloud/topographic/ferrofluid backgrounds. NET-NEW as a vanilla deliverable: the vault only has this as a GLSL/WebGL shader (noise-flow.glsl.js, gradient-flow.glsl.js) that needs the shader runner + WebGL context — there is no CPU/canvas2D version that works with no GPU, degrades on low-end devices, and is plottable/static-exportable. IQ explicitly notes the math is 2D and CPU-portable.
backlog.license: Article/technique freely usable; IQ Shadertoy source is reference. Value-noise reimplementation is original code (MIT-able)
backlog.note: Anti-dup nuance: vault's domain-warp is WebGL-bound. The value is a no-GPU, reduced-motion-friendly, static-exportable CPU variant for the same look. Render at low res + CSS upscale for perf.

backlog.name: Blue-noise dithering via void-and-cluster threshold texture (canvas2D)
backlog.what: A vanilla JS generator for a small tileable blue-noise threshold texture (void-and-cluster: Gaussian-energy ranking + iterative swap), then per-pixel threshold dithering on a 2D canvas. Blue noise gives the designer-grade grain that Bayer can't — even point distribution, no repeating cross-hatch, no clumpy white-noise — for film-grain overlays, two-tone duotone posters, and animation-stable dithered backgrounds. NET-NEW: the vault's grain is SVG feTurbulence (white-noise-ish) and its dithering is ordered/SVG; nothing produces a true blue-noise spectrum. demofox's 2025 follow-up confirms it's the current best-practice. Precompute the 64x64 texture once (or ship it as a tiny data array) so runtime is just a threshold compare — cheap and vanilla.
backlog.license: Algorithm public-domain (Ulichney void-and-cluster); reference impls MIT/CC0 (demofox, MomentsInGraphics)
backlog.note: Pair with the dithering pack as the 'designerly grain' option. Can ship a precomputed CC0 blue-noise PNG (momentsingraphics.de) to skip the generator entirely and stay trivially vanilla.

backlog.name: simplex-noise.js (jwagner)
backlog.what: 2 kB min+gz, dependency-free MIT simplex noise (createNoise2D/3D/4D, ~70M calls/s) importable as an ES module from a CDN. Would be the optional fast-noise engine behind the canvas2D domain-warp/fBM and blue-noise-art snippets when you want crisp gradient-noise over hand-rolled value noise (e.g. smoother flow fields, 4D for seamless looping animation). NET-NEW as a named, need()-guarded engine: the vault references Perlin/simplex only conceptually in the algorithmic-art/generative-art skills and inside GLSL — there's no actual JS noise lib registered for canvas2D use. Degrades gracefully: snippet falls back to the inline value-noise if the CDN module is absent.
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: Optional accelerator, not required — keep the canvas2D noise snippets working with inline value-noise so they stay zero-dep; simplex-noise.js is the behind-a-guard upgrade path.

backlog.name: Typed param-manifest convention (fxhash $fx.params-style schema) for vault generative snippets
backlog.what: A tiny, MIT, framework-agnostic CONVENTION for a generative snippet to declare a small set of named, typed, headlessly-drivable knobs: an array of `{id, name, type, default, options}` where type is number {min,max,step} | boolean | color | select {options} | string, plus a single `seed` the engine rerolls. fxhash proves this exact shape in production (deterministic `$fx.rand()` seeded off the hash). Worth adding because the vault's `utils/live-controls.js` (Tweakpane) exposes knobs only to a HUMAN at runtime — there is no machine-readable manifest the compose engine can enumerate and set headlessly. A `data-params` manifest convention (reimplemented vanilla, ~30 lines: parse manifest, apply values, expose `setParam`/`reroll(seed)`) turns any generative snippet into something the engine can drive in a loop. This is the structural half of 'seed reroll + score' that's missing.
backlog.license: MIT (fxhash params-boilerplate)
backlog.note: Reimplement vanilla as a `utils/param-manifest.js` companion to live-controls.js — do NOT add fxhash SDK as a dep (it's platform-coupled to the blockchain bytestring). Same typed schema, but `ParamManifest.read(el)` / `.set(el, id, val)` / `.reroll(el, seed)` over a `<script type="application/json" data-params>` block. The vault's generative-art.skill.md already endorses seeded PRNG (canvas-sketch/$fx.rand), so this slots in as the missing 'expose the knobs' layer.

backlog.name: Headless aesthetic-fitness scorer (hue-entropy + saturation/hue contrast + balance over canvas pixels)
backlog.what: A pixel-level scoring function the headless-Chrome harness can run on a rendered generative frame to RANK variants, not just prove one drew. The aesthetics-research toolbox (Redies et al., open methods) gives the concrete, cheap, vanilla-computable metrics: Shannon entropy of the HSV hue channel (colorfulness/variety), color contrast = sqrt(2*sigma_hue*sigma_sat) over the hue/saturation channels, lightness/luminance balance, and quadrant-variance for compositional balance. Worth adding because this is the actual VERIFICATION moat the angle asks for: the vault's render-smoke harness already samples canvas pixels for variance + distinct-color COUNT (a binary 'did it draw'), but stops there — it has no aesthetic-quality score and cannot say variant A beats variant B. A ~60-line `scoreFrame(imageData) -> {entropy, contrast, balance, score}` closes the loop: reroll seed -> render -> score -> keep argmax.
backlog.license: Methods are public-domain research (CC-BY paper); reimplement vanilla — no GPL code copied
backlog.note: This is net-new vs the harness's existing pixel check (tools/render-smoke.mjs lines 175-194 compute only variance + color-count for a drew/blank gate) and vs coherence_check (which scores HTML slop-tells, not generative aesthetics). Ship as a `tools/` diagnostic + an mcp `aesthetic_score(canvasPixels)` gate so it ties into the existing variants/coherence tooling exactly as the angle wants.

backlog.name: ParamExplorer — exploration-agent strategies (random / Gaussian-around-liked / CMA-ES / open-ended)
backlog.what: The 2025 framework (arxiv 2512.16529) that names the exact problem in the search seed: generative param spaces are high-dimensional and good outputs occupy small fragmented regions, so blind seed-reroll is wasteful. It contributes a taxonomy of exploration AGENTS worth borrowing as the search strategy inside the vault's reroll loop: Random (uniform sample), Gaussian (perturb params around an already-good config), CMA-ES (learns param interdependencies), and Open-ended (spawns new clusters). The param schema it expects from a sketch (float / integer / choice with ranges) matches the typed-manifest above. Worth adding as INSPIRATION: instead of the harness rerolling N flat random seeds, it can hill-climb — render M, score them, then Gaussian-perturb the winner's knobs and re-render — finding strong variants in far fewer renders.
backlog.license: LGPL-2.1 + Python backend + p5/custom-UI client — DO NOT vendor
backlog.note: FLAG: LGPL-2.1 and a Python/p5 server — not CDN-safe or vanilla as-is, and its selection is human-rated not automatic. Take ONLY the agent taxonomy (Gaussian-perturb-the-winner beats flat random reroll) and pair it with the vanilla aesthetic scorer above so selection is automatic/headless. The vault's book_variants already does flat-seed 'compose N, keep best by coherence' at PAGE level; this upgrades the search from random to guided and extends it to snippet-level knobs.

backlog.name: Hat / Spectre aperiodic monotile generator (SMKGS substitution)
backlog.what: The 2023 'hat' and 2024 'spectre' (Tile(1,1)) aperiodic monotiles by Smith/Myers/Kaplan/Goodman-Strauss — a single shape that tiles the plane with NO periodic repeat, ever. The reference generator is a compact substitution system (~250 lines: buildSpectreBase + recursive metatile/supertile substitution over ~4-5 generations) that emits a patch of polygon outlines you stroke/fill on canvas or SVG. This is the headline net-new family: the vault has flow fields, particles, shaders, mesh gradients but ZERO tiling systems, and a provably non-repeating monotile background is a genuinely fresh, recognizable 'we know our math' decorative motif. Seedable in the way that matters for decoration: the geometry is deterministic, but a seeded PRNG drives per-tile fill color (from a vault palette), accent/'mystic' tile placement, hue rotation, and stroke weight — so one seed -> one reproducible, distinct, non-tiling wallpaper. Vanilla-reimplementable from the public reference; christianp/aperiodic-monotile (CC0) and hatviz (BSD-3) give clean, permissive geometry to port.
backlog.license: BSD-3-Clause (hatviz) / CC0-1.0 (christianp/aperiodic-monotile) — both permissive; reimplement from reference
backlog.note: Geometry is permissive/public-domain; reimplement as a zero-dep .js that draws to canvas (background) with a seeded coloring layer. Pair with generative-art.skill.md cross-ref. Verifies cleanly (static fill, no overflow). The 'seeded' angle = deterministic geometry + seeded decorative coloring, which is exactly the non-repeating-background use case.

backlog.name: Truchet tile background (seeded, vanilla)
backlog.what: Truchet tiles = a square tile with an asymmetric motif (two quarter-circle arcs, or a diagonal, or the multi-scale 'smith/curve' variant) placed on a grid in one of 2-4 random rotations; the random orientations weave into flowing maze/labyrinth/curve patterns with no global repeat. It is the single most ROI-efficient generative background to add: ~40 lines of vanilla canvas, a seeded PRNG (mulberry32/xmur3) picks each cell's orientation, so one seed -> one reproducible decorative field. Multi-scale Truchet (recursively subdividing cells) and the arc/diagonal/triangle variants give a whole family from one snippet. Net-new: vault has patterns.css/patterns-mega.css (static CSS repeating patterns) but nothing generative or non-repeating. Truchet.js exists on npm but is stale (2019, ~2KB, no seeding) — better to ship vanilla with seeding built in than depend on it.
backlog.license: Public-domain technique; existing Truchet.js is MIT but stale/unseeded — reimplement vanilla
backlog.note: Reimplement vanilla with a seeded RNG — do NOT add Truchet.js as a dep (2019, 19 stars, no seeding). Variants: arcs (classic), diagonals, multi-scale, weighted-density. Slots next to backgrounds/patterns.css as the generative counterpart.

backlog.name: Wang tile texture generator (seeded edge-matching)
backlog.what: Wang tiles = square tiles with colored edges; you fill a grid so adjacent edges match, which forces a non-periodic, seamless, non-repeating texture from a small tile set (the classic technique behind tileable-but-not-obviously-repeating game/decor textures, and the conceptual ancestor of the hat/spectre result). For Design Book this is a seeded background: define a tiny set of edge-colored motif tiles (lines/curves/dots that connect across edges), then a seeded greedy/stochastic placement walks the grid honoring edge constraints -> reproducible flowing pattern with no visible tiling seam. Vanilla-reimplementable (~60-80 lines canvas). Net-new and complementary to Truchet (Wang = edge-constraint matching; Truchet = free random orientation) — together they form a small 'aperiodic backgrounds' set with the monotile.
backlog.license: Public-domain technique; reference implementations are MIT/BSD across languages
backlog.note: Reimplement vanilla; the value is the seeded edge-constraint placement producing seamless non-repeating texture. Slightly lower value than Truchet/monotile because it overlaps conceptually — best shipped together as one 'aperiodic-tiling backgrounds' snippet family rather than three separate items.

backlog.name: Penrose P3 (kite/dart) deflation tiling background
backlog.what: Penrose P3 tiling via deflation/subdivision: start from a few kite/dart (or thin/fat rhombus) prototiles and recursively subdivide N generations -> the iconic 5-fold aperiodic non-repeating pattern. The classic, instantly-recognizable aperiodic decorative motif (predates hat/spectre by ~50 years; pairs with it as the 'rhombus' counterpart to the monotile). Multiple permissive pure-JS implementations exist (arnfred/Penrose MIT, guinetik/penrose-js renders to canvas/bitmap, rictorlome p5). Deflation is deterministic; seedable for decoration via per-tile fill color, golden-ratio inflation count, and rotation — one seed -> one reproducible Penrose wallpaper. Vanilla-reimplementable (~100 lines, just half-tile recursion + complex-number rotation).
backlog.license: MIT (arnfred/Penrose, guinetik/penrose-js) — permissive; reimplement vanilla
backlog.note: Reimplement the deflation recursion vanilla (no dep). Good as the 'rhombus 5-fold' member of the aperiodic backgrounds family; slightly lower priority than hat/spectre (which is the genuinely fresh 2023-2024 hook) but higher recognizability. Seed drives coloring + inflation depth + rotation.

backlog.name: CSS Anchor Positioning (anchor-name / position-anchor / position-area / position-try-fallbacks / @position-try)
backlog.what: Native CSS for tethering a floating element to an anchor with browser-computed auto-flip/shift, replacing JS getBoundingClientRect math. As of June 2026 this is Baseline 2026 (Chrome/Edge 125+, Firefox 147+, Safari 26+, ~83% of users). Worth adding as a vanilla CSS primitive: `button{anchor-name:--a}` + `[popover]{position-anchor:--a; position-area:block-end; position-try-fallbacks:flip-block, flip-inline}` gives a self-flipping tooltip/popover/menu in ~6 lines, with `@supports(anchor-name:--x)` as the exact capability guard to fall back to the vault's existing JS Popover/Tooltip/DropdownMenu (all currently JS-positioned via getBoundingClientRect manual-flip in popover.js/tooltips.js/dropdown-menu.js/context-menu.js). Net-new: the vault has NO anchor-name/position-try usage anywhere and NO @supports/CSS.supports guard pattern in any JS component.
backlog.license: Web platform (no license / CSS standard)
backlog.note: Caveat to encode in the snippet: Chrome 125 shipped old names inset-area/position-try-options, renamed to position-area/position-try-fallbacks in Chrome 129 — ship only the new names. Safari 18.2-18.3 had partial @position-try. Provide @supports(anchor-name:--x){} progressive-enhancement block that, when absent, leaves the existing JS Popover to position. This is a verification-friendly platform baseline the engine can prefer over JS positioning.

backlog.name: HTML Popover API + Invoker Commands (popover attr, command/commandfor, popover=hint)
backlog.what: Declarative, zero-JS open/close + top-layer + focus/light-dismiss/a11y for popovers and dialogs. `popover` attribute reached Baseline widely-available April 2025 (Chrome 114+, Safari 17.4+, Firefox 125+). Invoker Commands (`<button command="toggle-popover" commandfor="menu">`) reached Baseline late-2025/early-2026 (Chrome 135, Firefox 144, Safari 26.2) — so a trigger+panel needs ZERO JavaScript and the browser handles top-layer (kills the vault's z-index/stacking-context bugs noted in WHY) and light-dismiss. Pairs with anchor positioning above to form one native popover/tooltip/menu primitive. `popover=hint` gives auto-dismissing tooltip semantics (a hint popover closes when another opens, unlike auto). Net-new: no popover=/commandfor/showPopover usage in vault; all current floating UI is JS-driven.
backlog.license: Web platform (HTML/WHATWG standard)
backlog.note: Best shipped as the structural+behavior half of the same primitive as candidate 1 (CSS does positioning, popover+command does open/close/top-layer). Degradation: where command/commandfor is unsupported, a tiny addEventListener('click', el.togglePopover()) shim covers it; where popover itself is unsupported (rare in 2026), fall through to the vault's JS Popover. Verification-friendly: native top-layer means screenshots render the panel above everything without z-index hacks.

backlog.name: need()-style capability-guarded native-first floating primitive + skill
backlog.what: A decision-tree skill (.skill.md) plus a thin JS guard that wraps candidates 1+2 into the vault's existing need()/guard idiom: prefer native (anchor-positioning + popover + invoker commands) when `CSS.supports('anchor-name','--x') && HTMLElement.prototype.hasOwnProperty('showPopover')`, else degrade to the vault's JS Popover/Tooltip/DropdownMenu. This is the actual NET-NEW deliverable the QUESTION asks for: a single primitive that eliminates JS positioning math on modern browsers but never regresses on old ones, and a skill that tells the engine WHEN to emit native vs JS (tooltip/popover/menu/hover-card decision tree, including the interest-invoker caveat below). The vault currently has no capability-guard pattern in any component JS, so this also introduces a reusable guard convention.
backlog.license: N/A (vault-authored skill over web-platform features)
backlog.note: Include the interop caveat: Interest Invokers (interestfor / popover=hint hover-triggering, Chrome 139 experimental, chromestatus 4530756656562176) are NOT yet Baseline — WebKit opposes the hover-tooltip use case on touchscreen grounds. So treat interestfor as inspiration/progressive-enhancement only and keep JS hover for the hover-card path; the skill should gate it behind its own narrower feature check.

backlog.name: Native CSS carousel (::scroll-button + ::scroll-marker + scroll-marker-group)
backlog.what: A zero-JS carousel built entirely from CSS overflow-module pseudo-elements: ::scroll-button(left|right) generates real, focusable, auto-disabling prev/next <button>s that scroll ~85% (or one snapped item) of the track; ::scroll-marker on each item auto-generates dot navigation collected into ::scroll-marker-group (positioned before/after the track); :target-current styles the active dot; combined with scroll-snap-type:x mandatory + scroll-snap-stop:always for one-item paging. Browser supplies ARIA automatically (buttons = focusgroup, markers = tablist), so keyboard + screen-reader nav is free. Worth adding because it replaces the entire JS layer of a dots+arrows carousel with declarative CSS — directly shrinking the JS surface the verifier must audit, and it is net-new (vault has CSS scroll-snap and JS carousels but no native scroll-button/scroll-marker primitive).
backlog.license: Web platform / public spec (CSS Overflow Module Level 5, W3C) — no license restriction
backlog.note: NOT Baseline as of mid-2026 — Chrome/Edge only; Safari & Firefox still in progress. MUST ship behind @supports (scroll-marker-group: after) so the pseudo-element rules only apply where supported. The base layer (outside @supports) should be a plain scroll-snap strip — reuse the vault's existing layout/scroll-snap.css + carousel-pack 'minimal' patterns — so non-supporting browsers degrade to a usable swipeable carousel with no broken controls. Net-new pseudo-elements: ::scroll-button(), ::scroll-marker, scroll-marker-group property, :target-current. Snippet should be CSS-only (one .css file, zero .js).

backlog.name: scroll-marker-group progressive-enhancement guard pattern
backlog.what: The specific @supports query — @supports (scroll-marker-group: after) — that gates native carousel controls so Chrome/Edge get the JS-free buttons+dots while Safari/Firefox fall back to a bare scroll-snap strip. This is the load-bearing reliability detail that makes the native carousel shippable today rather than a Chrome-only toy. It is a reusable verification/authoring convention: any vault snippet using bleeding-edge scroll pseudo-elements should standardize on this guard so the verifier's clean-render + no-overflow gate passes in headless Chrome AND the artifact still works in non-Chromium preview. Pairs with scroll-snap-stop:always to make button presses advance exactly one slide.
backlog.license: Web platform / public spec — no restriction
backlog.note: Bundle this guard INTO the native-carousel snippet rather than as a standalone file — it is the fallback contract, not a separate component. Outside @supports: scroll-snap fallback (reuse layout/scroll-snap.css). Inside @supports: ::scroll-button()/::scroll-marker/:target-current styling. This keeps the snippet honest in headless-Chrome verification (controls render) while degrading gracefully everywhere else, with zero JS either way.

backlog.name: interestfor / Interest Invokers polyfill (mfreed7/interestfor)
backlog.what: A declarative hover-intent primitive built on the new `interestfor` HTML attribute (native in Chromium 142, Oct 2025). One element points `interestfor="#card"` at a popover; the browser/polyfill fires 'show interest' on mouse-dwell, keyboard (Alt/Opt+Up), and touch long-press, with CSS-controlled `interest-show-delay`/`interest-hide-delay` (0.5s default) and `:has-interest`/`:target-of-interest` pseudo-classes for styling. Worth adding because it solves the exact failure mode the vault's `popover.js` hover trigger has (raw mouseenter/leave, flicker, no touch path, no keyboard parity) DECLARATIVELY — pair it with the existing native popover work as a need()-guarded enhancement that degrades to the current behavior when the attribute is unsupported. The BSD polyfill makes it safe in Safari/Firefox today.
backlog.license: BSD-3-Clause (permissive, OK)
backlog.note: Single-file polyfill, no npm/CDN-pinned host shown — vendor the min.js as a vault asset behind a `if (!('interestForElement' in HTMLButtonElement.prototype)) load()` guard so it no-ops in Chromium 142+. Falls back gracefully.

backlog.name: Safe-triangle (hover-intent) vanilla primitive
backlog.what: A zero-dep JS hover-intent engine: open-on-dwell (configurable show delay), close-on-leave-WITH-delay, and the 'safe triangle' — track the cursor and build an invisible polygon from the pointer to the popover's near edge so diagonal travel toward the card doesn't close it (point-in-polygon test on pointermove). This is the actual INTENT engine the vault is missing (`tooltips.js` only has a show-delay, no close-delay, no triangle, no touch suppression). Same primitive Floating UI's `safePolygon` and Radix HoverCard use, but reimplemented vanilla so it can wrap ANY trigger+panel (the static `popover-pack` hover-card, citation popovers, megamenus). Add touch suppression (skip on coarse pointers / suppress the synthetic hover) and focus parity for a slop-proof default.
backlog.license: MIT-style technique (reimplement; Floating UI safePolygon is MIT for reference)
backlog.note: Reimplement vanilla (don't ship Floating UI). Ship as e.g. `interactions/hover-intent.js` exposing `HoverIntent.wrap(trigger, panel, {showDelay, hideDelay, safeTriangle:true})`. Inspiration source: Floating UI safePolygon (MIT), Radix/Ariakit.

backlog.name: WCAG 1.4.13 hover-popover conformance gate
backlog.what: A verification capability for the headless-Chrome save-gate that checks any hover-triggered popover/tooltip against WCAG 1.4.13's three testable rules: Dismissible (Escape closes it without moving the pointer), Hoverable (moving the cursor onto the panel keeps it open — i.e. the safe-triangle/close-delay actually works), and Persistent (no auto-hide timer; stays until hover/focus leaves or user dismisses). Also assert focus parity (focusable trigger shows on focus, hides on blur) and touch sanity (doesn't trap open on tap). This is moat-grade: it's a net-new diagnostic that catches the single most common hover-popover slop failure (flicker/trap/timeout) automatically, and it's the natural gate that makes the two snippets above provably correct rather than just present.
backlog.license: N/A (verifier capability)
backlog.note: Drive via CDP: dispatch mouseenter, wait show-delay, assert visible; move pointer onto panel, assert still visible; press Escape, assert hidden; tab to trigger, assert focus-shows. Feed pass/fail into the coherence score for any page containing hover popovers.

backlog.name: NumberFlow (barvian) per-digit rolling-number transition
backlog.what: MIT, zero-runtime-dep animated number component (ships a vanilla <number-flow> custom element alongside React/Vue/Svelte). The reference implementation of the 2024-2026 'number flow' micro-interaction: parses a value via Intl.NumberFormat, diffs it digit-by-digit, and animates the SHAPE of each digit place changing — every digit place is its own overflow-masked column of stacked 0-9 glyphs that translateY independently, with gradient fade masks at the column edges (--number-flow-mask-height/-width) and a WAAPI spring (spinTiming/transformTiming/opacityTiming as EffectTiming, e.g. easing.dev / Grajeda spring generators). Places are inserted/removed as magnitude changes, with tabular-nums alignment and a 'trend' (up/down) direction. Worth adding because it is the canonical, trendy primitive for prices/stats/timers and is genuinely distinct from the vault's count-up (which interpolates a single VALUE and rewrites textContent) and from flip-clock/split-flap (mechanical half-fold flap, not a smooth masked roll). v0.6.0, released Feb 2026 (112 releases, 7.4k stars) — actively maintained. Reimplement as a zero-dep snippet: a column-per-digit DOM, overflow:hidden + CSS mask-image linear-gradient on each column, transform:translateY(calc(-1em * digit)) driven by element.animate() with a spring-sampled easing array, plus Intl.NumberFormat to split integer/group/decimal/sign parts and reconcile place count on update. Ships with reduced-motion handling (animated=false) to satisfy the vault's verify gate.
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: Distinct from data-viz/count-up.js (value interp, no per-digit roll), components/flip-clock.css + effects/text-flipping-board.* (mechanical split-flap half-fold), and typography/gradient-numbers.css (static). MIT, CDN-safe (esm.sh) but better reimplemented as vanilla to stay zero-dep. WAAPI custom-element approach is widely supported in 2026; gate behind reduced-motion fallback.

backlog.name: Pure-CSS odometer / rolling-digit (@property + counters + translateY)
backlog.what: A no-JS companion to the NumberFlow primitive: a rolling-digit odometer built entirely in CSS using typed @property integer custom properties, counter-set + mod()/round() to autofill each digit place from a single value attr(), and stacked translateY keyframes to slide each column 9->0 in staggered ~0.11s steps (per-place delays cascade like a real odometer). Distinct value vs the NumberFlow snippet: this variant needs zero JavaScript, so it composes into static pages, slide decks, and scroll-driven contexts (pair the @keyframes with animation-timeline:scroll() / view() for a scroll-rolled counter) where shipping a script is undesirable. Worth adding as the CSS-only tier of the same 'animate the digit shape changing' family — the vault currently has NO pure-CSS rolling-digit primitive (count-up is JS-only and value-based; gradient-numbers is static). Reimplement as a small .css snippet: a .odo wrapper with --amt, N .digit spans each masked by overflow:hidden, mod()/round(down) math to pick the glyph, and a count @keyframes animating the typed @property. Provide a JS-optional shim only to set --amt; everything visual is CSS.
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: Inspiration/technique to reimplement (no installable dep). Newer CSS math (mod/round/sibling-index) is bleeding-edge — ship with a static-numeral fallback so the verify gate passes on engines lacking it. Complements, does not duplicate, the NumberFlow JS snippet: this is the zero-JS / scroll-driven tier.

backlog.name: Spring-FLIP island morph engine (measure → spring-tween width/height/radius, vanilla)
backlog.what: A vanilla controller that morphs a SINGLE pill element between N arbitrary content states by FLIP-measuring the natural size of the next state (getBoundingClientRect on a hidden/cloned content layer) and driving width/height/border-radius with the vault's own spring.js (closed-form damped oscillator) instead of a fixed cubic-bezier. This is exactly how cult-ui/Framer-Motion islands feel 'liquid': the shell springs to the MEASURED size of whatever content you give it, so one island can hold a now-playing row, a timer, a call card, or a verification toast without hardcoded per-state pixels. The vault has the island shells (mobile/ios-dynamic-island, components/dynamic-island) AND spring.js — but they are not wired together and both islands snap to hardcoded fixed widths/heights with a fixed bezier. Wiring spring.js + content-measurement into the island is the net-new, signature 2024-26 micro-interaction the angle asks for. Worth adding because it turns a fixed 5-state demo into a general status/now-playing/live-activity widget that morphs to ANY content with real bounce.
backlog.license: MIT (pattern from cult-ui, github.com/nolly-studio/cult-ui, confirmed MIT; reimplemented vanilla, no Framer-Motion dep)
backlog.note: ANTI-DUP: island SHELLS exist (mobile/ios-dynamic-island.css/.js, components/dynamic-island.css/.js) but both use hardcoded fixed-px per-state sizes + fixed cubic-bezier(0.32,0.72,0,1) and never call the vault's spring.js or measure content.
