The MORPH ENGINE (measure+spring+arbitrary content) is net-new. Ship as an upgrade/companion to ios-dynamic-island.js, not a new shell.

backlog.name: interpolate-size / calc-size() auto-size morph (animate pill to content-natural width/height, no JS)
backlog.what: Modern CSS lets a pill transition between a fixed compact size and auto (content-sized) with ZERO JS: set `interpolate-size: allow-keywords` on :root, then `transition: width/height .4s ...` works even when the target is `width:auto`/`height:auto`/`max-content`. This is the no-JS path to the same 'morph to whatever content fits' behavior the FLIP engine does manually — perfect as the progressive-enhancement layer of the island (CSS handles auto-size morph in Chromium 129+, falls back to instant snap elsewhere, and the spring-FLIP engine can layer on for cross-browser + bounce). The vault's view-transitions.skill.md and ios-dynamic-island.css predate this and use only fixed-px states; calc-size()/interpolate-size is not referenced anywhere in INDEX.md. Net-new CSS capability that removes the biggest pain of morphing pills (animating to auto height).
backlog.license: Web platform / CSS standard (no license)
backlog.note: Not in INDEX.md (grep for calc-size/interpolate-size returns nothing). Chromium-only today — pair with spring.js FLIP fallback for Safari/Firefox. Add as a small CSS recipe + a global :root opt-in note in view-transitions.skill.md.

backlog.name: Content cross-fade + view-transition-name swap on island state change (no content snap)
backlog.what: The visible flaw in the vault's current islands: when data-state changes, the SHELL tweens but the CONTENT (icon/title/buttons) hard-swaps/snaps. Production islands fade-out old content and fade-in new while the shell springs, and the polished way to do this in 2024-26 is to wrap the per-state content swap in document.startViewTransition() (the vault already has ViewTransitions.run) with a per-state `view-transition-name` so the browser cross-fades the content layer for free, synced to the shell morph. Chrome's 2025 VT update added `view-transition-name: match-element` (auto-naming) and nested groups (Chrome 140+) which make this trivial for the content blocks. Net-new because no island in the vault cross-fades content on state change, and view-transitions.skill.md doesn't cover using VT to sync a content swap with an element-geometry morph.
backlog.license: Web platform (View Transitions API)
backlog.note: Reuses existing ViewTransitions.run — additive. The new bit is the recipe: name the content layer, run the data-state swap inside ViewTransitions.run so content cross-fades while the shell springs. Document in view-transitions.skill.md + ios-dynamic-island.js.

backlog.name: cult-ui Dynamic Island state-machine vocabulary (named size presets + queued live-activity sequences)
backlog.what: The cult-ui island's real value beyond animation is its STATE VOCABULARY: a fixed set of named blueprints (compact, compactLong, compactMedium, large, long, medium, tall, ultra, massive, minimalLeading/minimalTrailing, empty) plus a scheduler (scheduleAnimation) that CHAINS states over time to script a 'live activity' (e.g. ring → timer → done). The vault's ios-dynamic-island has only 5 flat states (idle/dot/compact/expanded/call) and NO sequencing API. Adopting the named-preset map + a tiny queue (setSize, then a timeline of [state, delayMs] steps) makes the island a scriptable live-activity widget — the actual iOS use case. Inspiration-grade: it's React/Framer-Motion (MIT) so not a drop-in dep, but the preset taxonomy + queue API port cleanly to the vanilla island.
backlog.license: MIT (github.com/nolly-studio/cult-ui — confirmed via gh api)
backlog.note: Inspiration not dep (React/Framer-Motion). Port the named-preset map + scheduleAnimation([{state,delay}]) queue onto the vanilla island so it can script live-activity sequences.

backlog.name: Open Props easing tokens (--ease-spring-1..5, --ease-bounce-1..5)
backlog.what: Open Props ships a battle-tested, named set of spring/bounce easings authored as STATIC CSS linear() strings (e.g. --ease-spring-1: linear(0, 0.006, 0.025 2.8%, 0.101 6.1%, 0.539 18.9%, 0.721 25.3% ...) ramping 1=subtle to 5=dramatic). This is exactly the 'small set of named, blessed spring tokens, drop-in, zero JS runtime' the angle asks for. Reimplement as a vanilla CSS snippet (e.g. utils/ease-springs.css) exposing ~6-8 tokens: --ease-spring-{soft,smooth,snappy,wobbly,bouncy} + --ease-bounce. CRITICAL recipe detail confirmed in research: a spring linear() curve is incomplete without its PAIRED duration (springs overshoot/settle, so the curve assumes a fixed total time) — fits the blessed-durations policy in memory: spring tokens land ~400-700ms, Open Props pairs ~2s for full settle. Gate behind @supports (animation-timing-function: linear(0,1)) with a cubic-bezier(0.34,1.56,0.64,1) fallback, and a prefers-reduced-motion:reduce block that swaps all tokens to a flat ease/instant. Note: Open Props' --ease-elastic-* are cubic-bezier (not linear()), and the vault's utils/easing.js already covers cubic-bezier elastic — so propose ONLY the linear() spring+bounce subset to stay non-duplicative.
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: Net-new: vault has JS-runtime springs (animations/spring.js, physics.js) and JS f(t) easing (utils/easing.js for rAF), plus a View-Transition-scoped vt-spring in transitions/named-transitions.css — but ZERO CSS linear() easing and no --ease-* custom-property token file. MIT lets you copy the strings verbatim or regenerate. Reduced-motion-friendly by design.

backlog.name: Linear() Easing Generator (Jake Archibald & Adam Argyle)
backlog.what: Open-source tool that converts any JS easing function or SVG curve into an optimized CSS linear() string, with built-in Spring / Bounce / Simple-elastic / Material-emphasized presets and a stop-precision slider (fewer points = smaller token). This is the AUTHORING mechanism behind blessed spring tokens: it is NOT a runtime dependency — you run it once at design time and paste the static linear() output into the vault snippet. Use it (or its algorithm) to mint vault-specific named tokens beyond the Open Props set (e.g. a 'material-emphasized' or 'overshoot-subtle' token tuned to the vault's taste). Also the right reference for a future verification/diagnostic: validate that a hand-pasted linear() token's stop count is reasonable (perf) and monotonic-where-intended.
backlog.license: Apache-2.0 (code) / source-available on GitHub (jakearchibald/linear-easing-generator)
backlog.note: Inspiration/build-time tool, not a shippable dep (it's a web app). Cite it in the snippet header + a gradients/motion skill so future tokens are generated correctly rather than hand-tuned. Browser support for linear() is Chrome/Edge 113+, FF 112+, Safari 17.2+ — hence the @supports gate is mandatory.

backlog.name: spring-easing — CSSSpringEasing()
backlog.what: Tiny zero-dependency lib whose CSSSpringEasing() function takes physics params (stiffness/damping/mass) and returns a [linear()-string, duration] tuple. The duration is computed from the physics so the curve and its blessed duration are authored together — solving the 'springs need a paired duration' problem the vault's blessed-durations policy cares about. Best used as a BUILD-TIME generator (run once in Node, paste the static linear() + duration into utils/ease-springs.css) so the vault gains accurately-tuned named tokens without taking a runtime JS spring dependency. Lets the vault expose intuitive named presets (snappy 300/30, light-bounce 200/15, playful 180/8, heavy 120/14×2 from the research) as pure CSS.
backlog.license: MIT (CSSSpringEasing/getLinearSyntax portions Apache-2.0, derived from Jake Archibald)
backlog.note: Propose as inspiration/build-time generator, NOT a CDN runtime dep — the whole point of the angle is no physics-runtime JS. Its value is pairing each curve with a correct duration token. Overlaps conceptually with animations/spring.js (JS runtime) but produces static CSS output, which spring.js does not.

backlog.name: optimistic-action.js (Optimistic-action primitive — wrap any async action with pending/confirmed/failed)
backlog.what: A zero-dep vanilla orchestrator that codifies React 19's useOptimistic / TanStack-Query onMutate-rollback contract as a framework-agnostic DOM primitive. API sketch: Optimistic.run(el, { apply(el){...return undoFn}, task(){return promise}, onError, undoToast:true }). It (1) snapshots the element / captures an inverse, (2) applies the local change INSTANTLY and sets el.dataset.state='pending' so a CSS shimmer/dimming shows the change isn't yet durable, (3) on resolve sets data-state='confirmed' (brief settle), (4) on reject runs the captured inverse to ROLL BACK the DOM and sets data-state='failed' + fires an error toast with Retry, (5) on confirm optionally raises an undo toast wired to the inverse so a committed mutation is reversible. Worth adding because the vault has every ingredient SEPARATELY (toasts.js, inline-toast itoast-loading, success-checkmark, error-shake, feedback-pack timed-undo) but NO orchestrator that sequences capture->apply->pending->commit/rollback->undo on an arbitrary element. This is the single dominant 2025-26 interaction philosophy ('users now interpret delayed feedback as broken, not loading') and currently it is React-only across the ecosystem — a vanilla primitive is genuinely net-new and materially improves perceived speed (likes, follows, todo-toggles, reorders, archive feel instant).
backlog.license: MIT (pattern; reimplement from useOptimistic / TanStack Query semantics — no code copied)
backlog.note: Closest existing: blocks/buttons-states.js (button-scoped loading->success->error, no DOM rollback, no undo) and feedback/feedback-pack.js FeedbackPack.undo() (fire-and-forget undo snackbar, no optimistic apply / no pending shimmer / no rollback). Neither orchestrates the full optimistic lifecycle on an arbitrary element. Build as the missing glue that REUSES Toast/itoast/success-checkmark/error-shake for the visuals.

backlog.name: data-state pending/confirmed/failed CSS layer (.opt-* attribute-driven visual states)
backlog.what: A small CSS companion keyed entirely off [data-state] so the JS primitive stays logic-only and the look is themeable: [data-state=pending] -> dimmed + a moving shimmer sweep (the 'active processing, not stuck' gradient the trend research calls out, distinct from a full-element skeleton) + aria-busy; [data-state=confirmed] -> brief success pulse/checkmark settle; [data-state=failed] -> red ring + reuses the err-shake keyframe. Worth adding because the vault's shimmer lives only inside specific components (shimmer-button, product-card skeleton, sources-panel loading) — there is no GENERIC 'this row/card/toggle is mid-flight' state styling you can drop on any element. Pairs with the orchestrator but is independently useful for hand-rolled optimistic flows and honors prefers-reduced-motion (shimmer -> static dim).
backlog.license: MIT (original CSS)
backlog.note: Anti-dup: shimmer exists only component-locally (blocks/shimmer-button.css, components/product-cards-pro.css skeleton, ai/sources-panel.css loading). No generic data-state pending/confirmed/failed element styling. Reuse feedback/error-shake.css + feedback/success-checkmark.css for the confirmed/failed ends rather than re-implementing.

backlog.name: Undo-commit window (confirmed -> reversible undo toast that actually defers/reverses the mutation)
backlog.what: The Gmail/Linear 'soft-commit' pattern: a destructive or committed action (archive, delete, move, mark-done) shows the result instantly, then holds a 5s undo toast with a shrinking progress bar; clicking Undo reverses the mutation (re-inserts the row, restores state) BEFORE the real network commit fires, or issues a compensating action if it already fired. Unlike feedback-pack's FeedbackPack.undo() — which is a fire-and-forget notification whose onUndo you must wire by hand — this binds the undo directly to the inverse captured by the optimistic primitive and DEFERS the irreversible side-effect until the window elapses, so Undo is real, not cosmetic. Worth adding because deferred-commit + reversible-undo is the canonical safe-destructive-action UX of 2025-26 and the vault has only the toast shell, not the commit-deferral logic.
backlog.license: MIT (original; pattern is public UX, reimplemented)
backlog.note: Half-covered: feedback/feedback-pack.js FeedbackPack.undo() + .fbk-undo CSS give the SHRINKING-BAR TOAST VISUAL (set alreadyInVault considerations on the visual). NET-NEW is the commit-deferral + inverse-reversal logic. Ship as a method on the same optimistic primitive (Optimistic.commit(el,{undoMs:5000, doCommit, undo})) that REUSES .fbk-undo so it is a logic addition, not a duplicate component.

backlog.name: Pure-CSS scroll shadows / overflow fade via animation-timeline: scroll(self)
backlog.what: The self-axis scroll-driven technique: bind a keyframe animation to the scroll container's OWN scroll position with `animation-timeline: scroll(self)` (or `scroll(x self)`), animating `@property`-typed length custom props that drive a `mask: linear-gradient(...)` (or inset box-shadow). Result: the top/bottom (or left/right) fade appears ONLY where there's more content to scroll, follows scroll position in real time, and self-detects overflow — because a scroll-timeline only advances when the element actually overflows. This is net-new: the vault's existing `scroll-area.js` reproduces this exact effect with a JS scroll listener + ResizeObserver + MutationObserver toggling `.is-top/.is-bottom`. The CSS-only version removes the JS dependency entirely (the kit's CSS already ships the mask gradients — only the class-toggling JS is being replaced) and degrades gracefully to no-fade in non-supporting browsers. The vault DOES use `scroll(root)`/`view()` (scroll-animations.css, headers-pro) but NEVER `scroll(self)` for container overflow affordances — that is the specific gap.
backlog.license: n/a (CSS technique / public-domain pattern)
backlog.note: Add as a `.sa-fade-css` variant inside the existing components/scroll-area.css that uses scroll(self) so the fade works with zero JS; keep scroll-area.js as the @supports-not fallback. Needs @property + @supports(animation-timeline:scroll()) guard. Reduced-motion safe (it's positional, not motion).

backlog.name: CSS overflow detection as a verification signal (scroll() boolean + style query)
backlog.what: Uses a scroll-driven animation purely as a DETECTOR: an animation bound to `animation-timeline: scroll(y self)` only progresses if the element overflows, so a `@property`-typed `--scroll: 1` toggle (read via a `@container style(--scroll: 1)` style query, or a registered custom prop) becomes a pure-CSS boolean for 'this region is actually scrollable'. Lets you reveal a 'scroll for more' hint, gradient cue, or shadow ONLY when content overflows — with no JS measurement. For Design Book this is most valuable as a VERIFICATION/diagnostic capability: the headless-Chrome save-gate (which already checks for overflow) can use this same primitive to assert that any scroll affordance is wired to real overflow, catching 'fake' or always-on scroll cues that read as slop. Net-new: nothing in the vault detects scrollability in CSS or gates on affordance-honesty.
backlog.license: n/a (CSS technique)
backlog.note: Best slotted into the coherence/anti-slop gate: a check that 'scroll affordance present ⇒ element genuinely overflows'. Also usable as an authoring helper. Style-query branch is Chromium-only today, but the verifier runs headless Chrome so that's fine.

backlog.name: Native CSS Carousel pseudo-elements (::scroll-button, ::scroll-marker, ::scroll-marker-group, ::column)
backlog.what: CSS Overflow Level 5 primitives that turn a scroll-snap container into a fully accessible carousel with ZERO JS: `::scroll-button(left/right)` generate browser-managed, stateful, focusable prev/next buttons (scroll ~85% per press); `::scroll-marker` + `::scroll-marker-group` generate keyboard-navigable pagination dots with `:target-current` for the active dot; the browser inserts them as real siblings with correct ARIA roles, tab order, and disabled-at-edges state. Pinterest reported ~2000 lines of JS carousel cut to ~200 lines of CSS. Net-new: the vault's carousel-pack is JS-driven (click listeners) and has no scroll-button/scroll-marker support; this is a distinct, lower-slop, a11y-by-default primitive for scrollable galleries/strips — adjacent to the scroll-affordance theme (the buttons/markers ARE scroll affordances).
backlog.license: n/a (web standard, CSS Overflow L5)
backlog.note: Ship as a `.carousel-css` variant guarded by @supports(selector(::scroll-marker)); falls back to the existing scroll-snap strip. Pairs naturally with the scroll(self) edge-fade above for a complete CSS-only scroll affordance kit. Reduced-motion: set scroll-behavior accordingly.

backlog.name: scrollbar-gutter: stable (+ both-edges) — reflow-free scrollbar reservation
backlog.what: `scrollbar-gutter: stable` reserves space for the scrollbar even when content doesn't (yet) overflow, eliminating the classic horizontal content-shift/reflow jump when a scrollable region toggles between non-overflowing and overflowing (e.g. dynamic lists, modals, tab panels). `both-edges` keeps layout symmetric. This is the unglamorous-but-high-signal completeness piece of a polished scrollbar kit: it kills a specific layout-jank tell. Net-new — `scrollbar-gutter` appears nowhere in the vault, and the existing scroll-area.css custom-scrollbar primitive doesn't address gutter stability. Baseline-supported (all evergreen browsers since 2024), so no fallback needed.
backlog.license: n/a (CSS standard, baseline)
backlog.note: Trivial, zero-risk addition to components/scroll-area.css (e.g. `.sa { scrollbar-gutter: stable; }` and a `.sa-gutter-both` modifier). Smallest-effort, highest-certainty item of the four; raises the coherence/no-reflow signal on any container whose content length changes.

backlog.name: Inline AI action bar (caret/selection-anchored 'Ask AI' command bar)
backlog.what: The actual 2026 trend the question targets: a single bar that fuses cmd-K + slash + selection-toolbar and adds an AI lifecycle. It anchors a virtual element at the caret or text selection (not centered like a palette), opens to an idle suggestion list of contextual verbs (Improve/Shorten/Translate/Continue/Fix grammar/Ask...), accepts a free-text prompt, then transitions through streaming -> result-preview with Accept/Discard/Retry/Insert-below. Tiptap's AI Menu is the canonical reference for the state machine and verb taxonomy. Net-new vs the vault: command-palette is global+static+synchronous; slash-menu inserts static blocks; ai-watermelon ctxbar is just a styled input with a hotkey and an onSubmit (no suggestions, no ranking, no async result, no anchoring). This is the compose shell that orchestrates all of those plus the vault's existing streaming-text renderer into one coherent inline interaction. Tiptap's component itself is React + Content-AI (paid/gated) so it is inspiration/reference for the UX state machine, not a usable dep; the bar shell is a clean vanilla reimplementation (a positioned div + the vault's streaming-text + slash-menu trigger logic).
backlog.license: MIT (Tiptap core); AI Menu UI relies on Tiptap Content AI which is commercial/gated
backlog.note: Partial overlap with components/slash-menu, ai/ai-watermelon-pack (ctxbar), ai/streaming-text, ai/inline-controls, components/command-pill -- but NONE combine caret/selection anchoring + AI suggestion list + async result lifecycle (idle->input->streaming->accept/discard). Reimplement vanilla, reusing streaming-text.js for the result render; tag clearly as 'not a global palette'.

backlog.name: Frecency ranking (frequency + recency usage scoring)
backlog.what: A small vanilla scoring function that ranks command/action results by usage history using double-exponential decay (Mozilla's 'frecency': score = sum over visits of weight * e^(-lambda*(t_now - t_visit))), optionally blended with fuzzy-match quality. This is precisely the 'result ranking' axis where an inline AI bar should differ from a global palette: a palette can stay fuzzy-only and stable, but an inline bar surfaces *contextual* AI verbs and benefits from boosting the user's most-recent/most-used actions for the current block type. The vault's command-palette.js uses a naive static fuzzyScore (score/haystack.length) with no recency, frequency, or context weighting, and slash-menu uses plain substring filtering -- frecency is entirely absent (grep for frecency/recency/usage-rank returns nothing relevant). A ~30-line localStorage-backed frecency util (record on select, decay on read, blend with fuzzy) is a clean, verifiable, zero-dep upgrade that meaningfully differentiates inline-bar ranking from palette ranking.
backlog.license: MIT (fre reference impl); algorithm is public (Mozilla Places)
backlog.note: Net-new: command-palette.js does static fuzzy-only ranking, slash-menu.js does substring filter; no recency/frequency/decay anywhere. Ship as a tiny shared ranking util (recordUse/score) that both the new inline bar and the existing palette can opt into.

backlog.name: CSS Anchor Positioning (caret/selection anchoring, Baseline 2026) + Floating UI fallback
backlog.what: The positioning primitive that makes a caret/selection-anchored bar feasible mostly in CSS. anchor-name/position-anchor/anchor() plus @position-try flip/shift fallbacks reached Baseline 2026 (Chrome/Edge 125+, Firefox 147+, Safari 26 -- ~83% of users). For the inline AI bar you anchor to a zero-size virtual marker placed at the caret (or the selection's bounding rect) and let the engine flip/shift to stay in viewport -- exactly the collision-avoidance a centered palette never needs. Floating UI (MIT, CDN ESM/UMD, ~tiny core) is the JS fallback for older browsers and supports virtual elements anchored to cursor coordinates, which is what caret anchoring requires. The vault only references 'floating-ui' as a tag on popover.css and has no anchoring util at all; CSS Anchor Positioning is entirely absent. This is the missing geometry layer beneath the inline-bar pattern and is independently useful for tooltips/popovers/menus.
backlog.license: CSS spec (royalty-free); Floating UI MIT
backlog.note: Not in vault as a capability (only a stray 'floating-ui' tag on components/popover.css). Pair: CSS anchor positioning as the primary path + a small Floating UI computePosition virtual-element fallback behind a need()-guard. Verification angle: gate can assert the bar stays within viewport bounds (no overflow) when anchored near edges.

backlog.name: Inline-bar vs command-palette keyboard-model & ranking skill
backlog.what: A decision-tree skill that encodes the two questions the angle asks: (a) keyboard model -- global palette is a modal launcher (mod+K toggles a centered overlay, Esc closes, Up/Down/Enter, focus-trapped, anchored to viewport center) whereas the inline bar is caret-local and dual-triggered (slash '/' inline OR mod+K on a selection), stays in document flow, Enter submits a prompt rather than firing a static action, and Tab/Up-Down cycle AI suggestions vs. inserting; (b) result ranking -- palette favors stable fuzzy-only ordering (keystrokes only filter), while the inline bar should reorder by context (block type / selection presence) + frecency, and degrade to a free-text 'Ask AI' affordance when no command matches (the Cursor 'cmd-K names the change in 3-4 words' model). Mozilla's urlbar ranking doc is the canonical reference for combining recency/frequency with match quality without jarring reorders. This captures the 'how does it differ' answer as a reusable, composable rule set rather than a one-off snippet.
backlog.license: N/A (documentation/decision rules)
backlog.note: No skill covers command surfaces/keyboard models today (skills are domain-specific: gsap, shaders, color, etc.). Small .skill.md tying together the new inline-bar genre + frecency util + anchoring snippet, with explicit 'inline bar != global palette' contrasts and when-to-use-which guidance.

backlog.name: ViewTransitions.list() — per-item VT list reorder/filter/sort (match-element) with WAAPI-FLIP fallback
backlog.what: A new vanilla method on the existing transitions/view-transitions.js that animates IN-PLACE list mutations: assign view-transition-name to each item (using the 2025 `match-element` keyword, Baseline Newly Available — Chrome 137+/Safari 18.2+/Firefox 144+), run the add/remove/reorder DOM mutation inside startViewTransition, and the browser FLIPs every item to its new slot for free (movers slide, removed items exit, added items enter), with `::view-transition-group(*)` controlling duration/stagger. Why net-new: the vault's ViewTransitions.run() only cross-fades the whole list (no per-item naming), and the only multi-item FLIP reorder in the vault (gsap/flip-layout.js) NEEDS the GSAP Flip plugin (a CDN dep). This is the missing ZERO-DEP path the question targets — high-impact for tables, kanban, galleries. Ships as `ViewTransitions.list(container, mutateFn, {item, stagger})`.
backlog.license: n/a (native browser API; reimplement in-vault)
backlog.note: Distinct from ViewTransitions.run (cross-fade only, no per-item names) and gsap/flip-layout.js (needs GSAP Flip plugin / CDN dep). Pitfalls to bake in: clear names after transition.finished; assign unique names only DURING the swap (duplicate names at capture throw); cap snapshot count on huge lists; honor prefers-reduced-motion (skip morph).

backlog.name: Batched WAAPI-FLIP fallback for multi-item layout animation (no startViewTransition)
backlog.what: The zero-dep fallback half of the question: when document.startViewTransition is absent (or under reduced-motion you want manual control), animate many items to new positions with First-Last-Invert-Play — but BATCH all getBoundingClientRect reads before the mutation and all reads-after in one pass to avoid layout thrash, then drive each item with element.animate() (WAAPI) translating from old→new rect. The vault's existing ViewTransitions.flip() does this for ONE element only; this generalizes it to a whole list/grid and is what makes the VT enhancement degrade gracefully instead of snapping. Ships as the internal fallback path of ViewTransitions.list() (and usable standalone as ViewTransitions.flipAll(items, mutateFn)).
backlog.license: n/a (public technique; reimplement in-vault)
backlog.note: Key correctness point: separate the read phase from the write phase (batch all rects), or N items cause N forced reflows. Pairs as the fallback branch of finding #1 so the same call site works on every browser.

backlog.name: view-transition-class + ::view-transition-group(*) wildcard for shared per-item motion
backlog.what: CSS authoring layer that makes the list pattern practical: instead of writing keyframes for every item, tag items with `view-transition-class: card` and style them once via the wildcard `::view-transition-group(*.card)` (and `::view-transition-old/new`) to set duration, easing (e.g. a bounce/spring curve), and per-item stagger via animation-delay tied to index. Also covers element-scoped `el.startViewTransition()` (Chrome 147+ stable) so a single list/table can transition WITHOUT freezing the whole page's pointer input — important for kanban/tables where the rest of the UI must stay live. This is the taste/ergonomics knob that turns the raw API into a composable Design Book micro-interaction.
backlog.license: n/a (native CSS)
backlog.note: Best delivered as a small .css companion to finding #1 (e.g. transitions/vt-list.css) plus 4-5 sentences in view-transitions.skill.md adding a 'list mutation' row to the pick-table. Element-scoped VT should be feature-detected (typeof el.startViewTransition) and fall back to document-level.

backlog.name: CSS Anchor-Positioned Sliding Indicator (anchor-name / position-anchor + :has fallback)
backlog.what: A segmented-control / tab-group whose shared sliding indicator is positioned with pure CSS anchor positioning: the active (or :hover) segment gets `anchor-name: --seg`, and a single `::after` on the track sets `position-anchor: --seg; left: anchor(left); right: anchor(right)` with a `transition`. The browser's layout engine interpolates the indicator's position and WIDTH natively as the active anchor switches — so it tracks equal-width AND content-width segments, survives container resize, and even multi-row wrap, with ZERO per-resize JS. The vault's three sliding controls (atabs/animated-tabs, ios-seg, segctl in nav-pack) ALL position their pill via JS getBoundingClientRect + ResizeObserver; none use anchor positioning, and none survive a resize without a JS recalc. As of 2026 anchor positioning is Baseline (Chrome 125+, Firefox 147+ Jan 2026, Safari 26, ~83-91% coverage), so a vanilla snippet that uses anchor() when supported and falls back to the existing transform/JS path via `@supports (anchor-name: --x)` is genuinely net-new and broadly useful for filters/view-switchers in web + deck genres. Worth adding because it eliminates the resize-JS class of bug and is the modern, declarative way to do the single most-requested 'sliding indicator' interaction.
backlog.license: Public technique (MDN/CSS-Tricks article; W3C CSS Anchor Positioning spec)
backlog.note: Net-new vs vault. Existing sliding controls (atabs, ios-seg, nav-pack segctl) are all JS-rect/ResizeObserver-positioned — confirmed via components/animated-tabs.js. No anchor-name/anchor()/position-anchor appears anywhere in components or blocks. Ship with an @supports guard + transform fallback so it degrades to the current JS path on the ~10-17% of traffic without support; Safari needs 26 for @position-try but core anchor()/position-anchor works in 18.2+.

backlog.name: Generic accessible Toggle-Group (role=radiogroup + roving tabindex, W3C APG)
backlog.what: A platform-neutral (non-iOS) segmented control implemented as the W3C APG Radio Group pattern: `role=radiogroup` wrapper with `aria-label`, each segment `role=radio` + `aria-checked`, and a roving-tabindex keyboard model — exactly ONE segment is `tabindex=0`, the rest `tabindex=-1`; Arrow Left/Up and Right/Down move focus, wrap at the ends, and selection follows focus (the arrowed-to segment becomes checked); Space selects; Tab enters/leaves the whole group as one stop. This is the correct a11y contract for a 'choose one of N' control and pairs directly with the anchor-positioned indicator above. The vault has roving tabindex ONLY in components/tabs.js (role=tablist underline tabs); its actual segmented/toggle controls — bgrp-segmented, ios-seg, atabs, nav-pack segctl — have NO arrow-key model, no radiogroup role, and no roving tabindex (ios-seg.js and nav-pack.js have zero keydown/aria handling). A small JS module (~40 lines) that upgrades any `.tgroup > [role=radio]` markup with the APG keyboard + ARIA wiring, plus optional wrap support (flex-wrap on the track, indicator still follows via anchor), is net-new, vanilla, and reusable across web/deck/app genres.
backlog.license: W3C APG (W3C Software and Document License, permissive)
backlog.note: Net-new. Roving tabindex exists ONLY for role=tablist tabs (components/tabs.js), NOT for the segmented/toggle-group family. bgrp-segmented (blocks/button-groups.css) has no sliding indicator and no keyboard model; ios-seg/atabs/nav-pack segctl have sliding indicators but JS-positioned and no roving tabindex/radiogroup ARIA. Could also surface as a short skill (decision tree: tabs/tablist vs toggle/radiogroup vs single switch) but the vanilla snippet is the addable artifact.

backlog.name: CSS-only (input:checked ~ :has) segmented control — no-JS baseline variant
backlog.what: A fully JS-free segmented control: N `<input type=radio name=group>` + `<label>` pairs, with the sliding thumb driven by `:checked` sibling/`:has()` rules and the thumb's translateX derived from `:nth-of-type` of the checked input (or, on modern engines, anchored to the checked label). Because it's native radio inputs, it is keyboard- and form-accessible for free (real radio semantics, arrow keys, name-grouped value submission) with no script at all — ideal as the deterministic, verify-friendly default the compose engine can emit when no JS bus is wired. The vault's CSS-only `:checked` usage is confined to single radios/toggles (blocks/radios.css, toggles.css, checkboxes.css); there is no CSS-only MULTI-segment group with a shared sliding indicator. Value is mainly as the zero-dep fallback tier beneath the anchor-positioned and APG-JS variants (progressive enhancement: works everywhere, animates the thumb via transform, no resize JS).
backlog.license: MIT (basilebong/segmented-control-css) — technique is reimplementable, not a dependency
backlog.note: Net-new as a multi-segment group; vault's :checked patterns are single-control only (radios.css/toggles.css). Reimplement from scratch (don't vendor) to fit token system. Caveat: pure-:nth-child translateX assumes equal-width segments; content-width sliding still wants the anchor() variant. Best framed as the bottom progressive-enhancement tier of the same component, so arguably merge with candidate 1 rather than ship standalone.

backlog.name: Toast/live-region accessibility verification gate (toast-a11y rule block in checkCoherence)
backlog.what: A new diagnostic rule block inside the existing checkCoherence() HTML-string scorer (mcp-server/server.js) that flags inaccessible toasts/snackbars/notification banners. Detection (all pure regex on the composed HTML, same technique the scorer already uses for gradient-heavy-no-imagery and unblessed-duration): (1) an element whose class/role marks it a toast/snackbar/notification-banner exists but has NO role="status|alert" and NO aria-live — warning type 'toast-no-live-region', hint 'role=status/aria-live=polite for info, role=alert/aria-live=assertive for urgent; the live region must exist in the DOM before the toast is injected'; (2) a toast container that holds a <button>/<a> AND is a live region — warning 'interactive-content-in-live-region', hint 'move focus to an alertdialog instead; live regions announce raw text and drop button semantics'; (3) a JS auto-dismiss timer (setTimeout/duration option) under 5000ms — warning 'toast-autodismiss-too-fast', hint 'WCAG 2.2.1: min readable ~ 1s/3 words + 3s base (>=6s typical); action toasts >=8000ms; or make it dismissible/persistent'; (4) no focusable dismiss control / no Esc handler on a persistent toast — warning 'toast-no-keyboard-dismiss'. Worth adding because it is the single highest-leverage NET-NEW capability: the vault has ~8 toast STYLES but ZERO accessibility verification, and its OWN blocks/toasts.js ships duration:4000 with no aria-live/role — i.e. the gate immediately earns its keep by catching the vault's own shipped snippet. This is the moat: a diagnostic, not another style.
backlog.license: N/A (original rule logic; grounded in WCAG 2.2.1 / 4.1.3 + Sara Soueidan, MIT-spirit references)

backlog.name: WCAG 2.2.1 / 4.1.3 readable-duration formula for auto-dismiss timing
backlog.what: The concrete, codifiable timing thresholds the gate (and any toast snippet) should enforce: WCAG 2.2.1 Timing Adjustable says auto-vanishing messages effectively must be extendable/dismissible; the field consensus minimum-readable formula is '1 second per 3 words + 3 seconds base' (a 10-word toast => ~6s), with action-bearing toasts at >=8000ms so keyboard users can tab to the Undo. WCAG 4.1.3 Status Messages adds that the toast must be programmatically announced WITHOUT moving focus (hence the live region). This is worth adding as the numeric backbone of both the verification gate AND a short skill: it converts a fuzzy 'don't dismiss too fast' into a testable number (sub-5s = fail, action+sub-8s = warn) and a default-duration recommendation that fixes the vault's current 4000ms default. Net-new because nothing in the vault encodes accessible timing rules; the existing 'blessed durations' token set (90-480ms) is about animation easing, a completely different axis from notification dwell time.
backlog.license: W3C Document License (permissive, attribution)

backlog.name: Accessible-toast skill: aria-live politeness + focus + keyboard-parity decision tree
backlog.what: A compact decision-tree .skill.md ('accessible-toast' or folded into reactive-input.skill.md) that the compose engine consults when it emits any toast/snackbar/banner: choose role=status+aria-live=polite (info) vs role=alert+aria-live=assertive (urgent); ensure ONE persistent live-region container is mounted at page load (not created per-toast); NEVER put interactive content in a live-region toast (escalate to alertdialog + move focus + restore focus on close — the vault's dialog.js already does the focus-trap half); guarantee keyboard parity for swipe-to-dismiss (every swipe-dismiss toast needs a focusable X button + Esc); cap simultaneously-visible toasts (queue the rest) to avoid covering content; pause auto-dismiss on BOTH hover and focus. Worth adding because the vault's toast snippets (toast-stack-2 advertises swipe-dismiss, toasts.js pauses on hover only) have NO keyboard parity or aria-live, and there is no guidance file telling the engine how to wire them accessibly. It pairs naturally with the verification gate (skill = how to build it right; gate = catch it when built wrong).
backlog.license: N/A (original decision tree; cites Scott O'Hara / Adrian Roselli / Sonner patterns)

backlog.name: Sonner-style accessible-by-default toast upgrade for blocks/toasts.js
backlog.what: A vanilla, zero-dep upgrade of the existing blocks/toasts.js mirroring Sonner's accessibility model (Emil Kowalski, MIT): mount a single persistent aria-live region on init (polite default, assertive for type:error), bump default duration from 4000 to a WCAG-safe value (>=6000, >=8000 when an action button is present), pause-on-hover AND pause-on-focus (currently hover-only), add Esc-to-dismiss + a focus-reachable X with focus restoration to the trigger, and queue/cap stacked toasts. This is the remediation companion to the gate: rather than ADD a competing toast lib (vault already has plenty), retrofit the one the vault ships so it passes its own new verification. Flagged as integration note: keep it vanilla — Sonner itself is React, so it is INSPIRATION for the API shape, not a CDN dep. Slightly lower value than the gate because it is a fix to an existing snippet rather than a new capability, but it closes the loop so the gate doesn't flag the vault's flagship toast forever.
backlog.license: Sonner is MIT (used as inspiration only; reimplementation is original vanilla)

backlog.name: CDP DOM.getTopLayerElements — open-state overlay gate (verification primitive)
backlog.what: Chrome DevTools Protocol exposes DOM.getTopLayerElements() (returns NodeIds of every element currently promoted to the browser top layer, in LIFO order) plus a DOM.topLayerElementsUpdated event. This is the authoritative, zero-dependency oracle for the single hardest part of the proposed gate: 'did this overlay actually reach the top layer, and is the stacking order correct.' The vault's existing render-smoke (tools/render-smoke.mjs) already drives Playwright Chrome but only asserts default-CLOSED state (preview-non-empty, canvas-drew, zero console errors) — checkCoherence in mcp-server/server.js is purely static regex on HTML and never opens anything. A new gate would: (1) find each overlay trigger via a data-verify-open convention or by calling the component's documented open API (Popover.init/show, Drawer open, Dialog.confirm, command-palette/combobox toggles already in the vault), (2) open it, (3) call getTopLayerElements via a Playwright CDPSession to confirm the panel is genuinely top-layer (vs. just high z-index inside an overflow:hidden ancestor — the #1 clipped-dropdown bug), and (4) compare getBoundingClientRect against viewport + each overflow!=visible ancestor's clip rect to catch off-screen popovers and clipping. Worth adding because it is the exact moat: open-state bugs that silently pass today.
backlog.license: N/A (Chromium/CDP, BSD-style; accessed through existing playwright-core@1.60.0 devDependency)
backlog.note: getTopLayerElements is marked experimental in CDP but stable in shipping Chrome; pin behind the same channel:'chrome' launch the harness already uses. Pairs with a per-component 'open recipe' map so the gate knows how to expand each overlay.

backlog.name: tabbable (focus-trap/tabbable) — focus-trap verification oracle
backlog.what: MIT, zero-dependency, browser-ready library (v6.5.0, released 2026-06-20) that returns the ordered list of tabbable nodes within a container, using real visibility checks (Element.checkVisibility / getClientRects) so display:none, visibility:hidden, zero-size and content-visibility nodes are correctly excluded. This is the missing oracle for the focus-trap half of the gate. The vault SHIPS focus traps (components/drawer.js, components/dialog.js, feedback/celebration-screen.js all tagged focus-trap) but has NO capability that VERIFIES the trap actually holds. With tabbable injected into the page after opening an overlay, the gate can assert: the trap container has >=1 tabbable node, the first tabbable receives focus on open, and simulated Tab from the last tabbable + Shift+Tab from the first wrap inside the container (focus never escapes to background) — the precise broken-focus-trap failure mode the brief calls out. Also catches the inverse bug: a modal whose background is NOT inert, where tabbable(document.body) still surfaces background controls. Reimplementable as a vanilla snippet but the library is tiny, battle-tested, and CDN/ESM-loadable, so use it as the harness-side dev oracle.
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: Use as a harness-side oracle injected via page.addScriptTag (ESM build), NOT shipped into snippets — the vault's components stay zero-dep. The vault references 'focus-trap' only as a component TAG/implementation, never as a verification dependency, so this is non-duplicative.

backlog.name: Clip-rect / overflow-clipping detector for expanded overlays (vanilla technique)
backlog.what: A small vanilla-JS technique: walk an opened overlay's ancestor chain, and for every ancestor whose computed overflow is not 'visible', intersect the overlay's getBoundingClientRect with that ancestor's client/padding rect; if the overlay's visible area shrinks below ~100% (or it lands fully outside the viewport), it is being clipped or pushed off-screen. getBoundingClientRect reports the FULL unclipped box, so a naive in-viewport check misses overflow-hidden clipping entirely — this ancestor-clip walk is the correct detector and the exact thing the current harness lacks. Complements the CDP top-layer check: top-layer says 'is it escaping clipping ancestors at all'; this technique quantifies 'by how much is it clipped' and also catches the layout-overflow-when-expanded case (a combobox dropdown or command palette that, once populated, overflows its container or the viewport, producing the document-overflow the gate should reject). Net-new because render-smoke only checks the closed preview node; nothing measures an expanded overlay against its clip ancestors.
backlog.license: MIT-equivalent (public gist / standard DOM APIs, vanilla-reimplementable)
backlog.note: Implement directly in the harness as part of the open-state loop; no third-party dep. IntersectionObserver(root: ancestor) is the reflow-free variant for batch checks.

backlog.name: @axe-core/playwright — open-state a11y sweep (secondary / mostly inspiration)
backlog.what: Runs the axe-core a11y engine against the live page from the same Playwright context the harness already uses. Relevant here mainly because it VALIDATES the brief's thesis: axe only evaluates what is currently in the DOM and visible, so overlays that default to CLOSED are skipped — meaning a11y bugs inside an open command palette/combobox (missing role=listbox, aria-expanded, aria-activedescendant, broken contrast on the open panel) are false-negatives today. Re-running axe AFTER the gate opens each overlay would surface those. Listed as a lower-priority/secondary candidate because (a) it overlaps the vault's existing static a11y checks in tools/audit.js (focus-removed etc.), and (b) its license is restricted.
backlog.license: MPL-2.0 (axe-core engine) — FLAG: weak copyleft / file-level reciprocity; acceptable as an isolated dev-time tool under tools/ but do NOT bundle into shipped snippets
backlog.note: Treat as inspiration / optional dev-only add-on, not a core dep, due to MPL-2.0. The reusable insight is 'reopen the a11y sweep per-overlay-open-state'; the top-layer + tabbable + clip-rect trio (above) is the permissive, zero/MIT-dep core of the moat.

backlog.name: Customizable native <select> (appearance: base-select + ::picker(select) + <selectedcontent>)
backlog.what: The 2026 platform primitive that lets a real <select> be fully CSS-styled while keeping native a11y, keyboard nav, form submission, and value semantics. Opt in with `select, ::picker(select){appearance:base-select}`, then add a first-child `<button><selectedcontent></selectedcontent></button>` (browser clones the chosen <option> into selectedcontent), style the open list via `::picker(select)`, the arrow via `::picker-icon`, the tick via `option::checkmark`, and state via `:open`/`option:checked`. Options can now hold rich markup (icon+label spans, SVG, img). The picker renders in the top-layer and animates with `transition...allow-discrete` + `@starting-style` like a popover. Worth adding because the entire dropdown is CSS-only with native semantics intact — and it slots cleanly behind a capability guard with the vault's JS Select/Combobox as the fallback.
backlog.license: Web platform feature (no license / royalty-free)
backlog.note: Net-new: grep found zero `appearance: base-select`, `::picker`, or `selectedcontent` in the vault. Ship as a `@supports(appearance:base-select)` progressive-enhancement layer that upgrades a plain native <select>; in unsupported browsers the <button>/<selectedcontent> markup is ignored and rich-option content collapses to trimmed textContent, so it degrades to a working native select — OR wire the existing JS `blocks/select.js` / `blocks/combobox.js` as the non-supporting fallback. Caveats to encode in the snippet: the inner <button> is inert (no interactive children in options); React/Next dev-mode logs DOM-nesting + hydration warnings for <button> in <select> and <span> in <option>, and `<selectedcontent>` needs a TS intrinsic-element shim — so author it as vanilla HTML, not framework JSX. Decorative ::checkmark/::picker-icon are excluded from the a11y tree (use aria-hidden on icon spans). Pairs naturally with candidate #2 (native anchor positioning).

backlog.name: CSS Anchor Positioning (anchor-name / position-anchor / position-area / @position-try)
backlog.what: Tether any element to an anchor purely in CSS — `anchor-name` on the reference, `position-anchor`/`anchor()`/`position-area` on the floating element, and `@position-try` + `position-try-fallbacks` for automatic auto-flip/shift when it would overflow the viewport. Replaces the JS measure-loop that Floating-UI-style code runs on every scroll/resize. Worth adding because the vault's popover, tooltip, dropdown-menu, context-menu, command-palette, citation-popover and the select menu are ALL JS-positioned today; a native anchor-positioning utility/snippet removes that JS for the common cases and stays glued correctly during scroll without listeners.
backlog.license: Web platform feature (no license / royalty-free)
backlog.note: Net-new: grep for `anchor-name:`, `position-anchor:`, `position-area:`, `@position-try` returned zero hits — every floating panel in the vault (`components/popover.js`, `blocks/tooltips.js`, dropdown/context-menu/command-palette) computes positions in JS. This is Baseline-safe to ship today, with a `@supports(anchor-name: --x)` guard falling back to the existing JS positioners. Highest leverage as a small anchored-popover/tooltip/dropdown snippet plus a `@position-try` auto-flip recipe; also the idiomatic way to position the customizable-select picker in #1.

backlog.name: field-sizing: content (CSS auto-grow inputs & textareas)
backlog.what: One CSS declaration that makes an <input>/<textarea> shrink-wrap and grow to its content with no JavaScript. `textarea{field-sizing:content; max-height:...}` replaces the classic keystroke handler that resets height to auto then to scrollHeight. Worth adding as a tiny vanilla utility because the vault currently auto-grows via JS.
backlog.license: Web platform feature (no license / royalty-free)
backlog.note: Net-new as a CSS primitive: the only auto-grow in the vault is JS — `components/chat-input.js` recomputes `text.style.height = Math.min(text.scrollHeight,160)` on every keystroke; no `field-sizing` anywhere. Ship guarded by `@supports(field-sizing:content)` (the existing chat-input JS is the fallback) and always pair with `max-height`/`max-width` so it falls back to a scrollbar at the cap. Smaller scope than #1/#2 but a clean JS-removal that fits the composer/ai-inputs blocks.

backlog.name: case-study compose-genre (single-project story page)
backlog.what: A net-new structure.skill.md archetype + reference page (structure/case-study.html) with a FIXED section spine for a long-form single-project story: Nav → Hero (project title as type statement + one hero shot/video, NO logo cloud) → Client meta band (client, year, role/scope, deliverables, stack, optional live link — a .s-cluster row or sticky sidebar) → Overview/intro (2-3 line .s-container-narrow) → Challenge/Problem (.s-section, business pain) → Approach/Process (numbered steps via feature-blocks .fbk numbered, 3-5 phases) → Metric band ('big pull-metrics', .s-stats + count-up, percentage AND absolute, lead with the biggest) → Before/After (media/image-compare) → Visual gallery (image-gallery-pro / masonry) → Testimonial (quote-blocks testimonial-frame, attributed) → Scoped CTA ('Start a project like this', .s-section--accent) → Next-project link (footers reveal/giant, prev/next case nav). Why add it: the vault has all the PRIMITIVES (stats, quote cards, image-compare, count-up, numbered steps, sticky-scroll-reveal, footers) and an Agency archetype that is a portfolio INDEX ('selected work' grid) — but NOTHING that sequences them into the canonical three-act (challenge→approach→result) single-project deep-dive that every award agency ships per project. It is the highest-leverage missing genre because it is 100% reuse of existing structure primitives — only the spine/ordering and a thin .cs-* meta/credits + prev-next-nav helper are net-new.
backlog.license: N/A (own authored vault content)
backlog.note: Net-new GENRE only; do NOT re-add primitives. Reuses: structure.css (.s-hero/.s-section/.s-stats/.s-cluster), feature-blocks (.fbk numbered steps), media/image-compare (before/after), data-viz/count-up + components/stats (metric counters), typography/quote-blocks (testimonial-frame), components/footers (reveal/giant + prev-next), components/cta-sections (scoped CTA), structure/section-transitions (pacing). Only authored additions: the archetype block in structure.skill.md (section sequence + pacing + 'classify by goal: deep-dive into ONE project → case-study'), a structure/case-study.html reference page, and a small .cs-meta credits row + .cs-nextproj prev/next footer-nav snippet if not expressible with existing s-* primitives.

backlog.name: case-study meta/credits + prev-next 'next project' nav helper
backlog.what: The one genuinely net-new structural micro-primitive the genre needs that the vault does not already express cleanly: (1) a client-meta / credits row — a definition-list-style band of label+value pairs (Client, Year, Role, Deliverables, Stack, Live link) rendered as a horizontal .s-cluster on desktop and stacking on mobile, optionally STICKY in a left rail while the story scrolls (reuse scroll-pin); and (2) a 'next project' / prev-next case navigation footer block — a full-bleed pair (or single) of large hover-reveal links to the adjacent case studies with thumbnail + project name + arrow, the signature closing move of award case-study pages. Why add it: every other genre slot maps to an existing snippet, but 'project credits band' and 'prev/next project pager' are case-study-specific furniture not covered by team-grid, stats, or footers. Small, CSS-first, fits the .s-* token system.
backlog.license: N/A (own authored vault content)
backlog.note: Confirmed gap: grep shows footers cover giant/reveal/marquee but NOT a project-credits dl-band or a prev/next CASE pager; stats covers metric band but not labelled meta pairs. Implement as ~1 small .css (+ optional sticky via existing scroll/scroll-pin.js) on the structure.css token system so it themes with every archetype. Keep zero-dep.

backlog.name: api-reference compose-genre (Stripe/Mintlify three-pane reference archetype)
backlog.what: A net-new page archetype the engine composes: a developer API REFERENCE page, structurally distinct from the vault's prose docs-layout. Standard structure (confirmed across Stripe, Mintlify, Scalar, Redoc): LEFT = endpoint nav grouped by resource/tag with method-colored badges (GET/POST/PUT/DELETE/PATCH) and a search filter; CENTER = per-endpoint method+path header, then parameter tables split into Path / Query / Body sections where each row shows name (mono) + type + required/optional badge + default + description, with nested objects expandable; then a response schema/attributes table and status-code rows (200/4xx/5xx); RIGHT = a STICKY request-sample rail that updates per endpoint with a language tab switcher (curl/Node/Python/Ruby/PHP/Go/Java/.NET) plus an example JSON response card, and a 'Try it' console stub (method+path+param inputs+Send). The vault has every ingredient as scattered dashboard/dev-portal widgets but no assembled reference page. This is the missing dev-audience archetype; high reuse for SDK/platform/AI-API docs. Build as a token-themeable backbone in structure/ (e.g. api-reference.css/.js) mirroring how saas.html/dashboard.html compose, reusing code-tabs.js for the language rail and admin-pack method-badge styling.
backlog.license: N/A (original archetype; pattern is industry-standard, not a dependency)

backlog.name: Endpoint parameter & response schema tables (vanilla snippet: method-badge nav + required/optional param rows + nested expandable + status-code response table)
backlog.what: The center-pane primitives the api-reference genre needs and the vault lacks. A zero-dep CSS+JS snippet providing: (1) an endpoint-nav list with HTTP method badges color-coded by verb and an active/scroll-spy state, grouped under collapsible resource headers; (2) a parameter table component (.api-params) where each row renders name + type + a required/optional pill + default value + description, and OBJECT/ARRAY rows expand to reveal nested child params (the Stripe/Redoc 'expandable attributes' pattern the vault has nowhere); (3) a response panel with status-code tabs (200/201/4xx/5xx) each showing a schema table + an example-JSON card. Distinct from docs-layout.css (which only does prose h1/h2/code/callouts) and from admin-pack's single api-endpoint widget (a flat badge, no params/responses). This is the load-bearing content layer that turns prose docs into a true reference.
backlog.license: N/A (original; pattern reference is permissive industry convention)

backlog.name: Scalar API Reference (@scalar/api-reference)
backlog.what: MIT, actively-maintained (106 releases, latest Jun 17 2026; 15.3k stars) standalone-script API reference renderer that takes an OpenAPI/Swagger spec and produces exactly the three-pane reference (left endpoint nav, center request/response schema, right multi-language code samples + built-in 'try-it' interactive console) — the one mainstream open-source renderer that ships BOTH beautiful docs AND a live request console in a single CDN-loadable library (Redoc has the layout but no console). Best added as a CDN-snippet behind a need()-guard: when a real OpenAPI spec is provided, mount Scalar for an instant interactive reference; degrade to the vanilla api-reference genre when offline/spec-less. Caveat: it's a heavy Vue-based bundle and themes to its own design language (won't inherit vault tokens cleanly), so it's the 'real-spec' escape hatch, NOT the default — keep the vanilla genre as the primary, token-themeable path. Also strong as INSPIRATION for the try-it console UX (method selector + param inputs + Send + response viewer).
backlog.license: MIT

backlog.name: Sticky per-endpoint request-sample rail (right-pane code card that locks while center scrolls + language tabs + example response)
backlog.what: The signature Stripe interaction the vault has no archetype for: the right-hand code column is a CARD that stays position:sticky and locked in the viewport while the developer scrolls the center descriptions, and its contents SWAP to the request sample for whichever endpoint is currently in view (scroll-spy driven). Combines a language tab switcher (reuse the existing code-tabs.js global), a curl/SDK request block with click-to-copy, and an example-JSON response card beneath. Vault's code-tabs.css is a flat tabbed block with no sticky behavior, no scroll-spy endpoint binding, and no paired request+response framing — so the 'sample rail' as a sticky, endpoint-synced unit is net-new. Small vanilla CSS+JS layer (IntersectionObserver scroll-spy + position:sticky) that wires the existing code-tabs into the reference genre's right column.
backlog.license: N/A (original; technique reference)

backlog.name: Link-in-bio page genre (creator hub archetype)
backlog.what: A net-new compose archetype: the mobile-first, single narrow centered column (~560-680px max-width) creator hub. Canonical section sequence: (1) profile header = circular avatar (optional Instagram-story ring / verified badge) + display name + @handle + one-line bio; (2) the body in one of three modes the engine picks — LINK-STACK (full-width rounded tappable link buttons, Linktree mode), BENTO MIX (asymmetric resizable tiles: link / image / video-embed / music / map / text, that restack to one column on mobile, Bento.me-descended mode), or a hybrid of a couple bento tiles atop a link stack; (3) optional embedded-media tiles (YouTube/Spotify/podcast thumbnails as tappable cards); (4) a social-icon row (brand-tinted); (5) a slim footer (powered-by / share). What makes it a DISTINCT archetype and not the vault's existing SaaS/Agency/E-com landing archetypes: it's a CONVERSION ROUTING HUB (many equal-weight destinations, zero single-CTA funnel, no nav/hero/feature/pricing sections), it's locked to one tap-target-friendly column, and it lives at phone width. The vault already owns every primitive — bento-grid.css, avatars.css, social-icons.css, buttons-social, content-cards-pack profile-hero, stories.css ring, gradient-avatar — so this is pure assembly: a structure/linkbio.html skeleton + a decision line in structure.skill.md ('single share-everywhere link / creator / many destinations -> link-in-bio'). High demand, mobile-first, trivially verifiable (single column, no overflow, imagery floor met by avatar+tiles).
backlog.license: N/A (original assembly of existing vanilla snippets)
backlog.note: Primitives exist (bento-grid.css, avatars.css, social-icons.css, content-cards-pack profile-hero) but NO genre assembles them; structure.skill.md archetypes 1-5 are all desktop multi-section landings, none is this. Confirmed zero hits for link-in-bio/linktree/biolink across INDEX.md + all *.skill.md.

backlog.name: Theme-picker / preset-swatch control (defining link-in-bio feature)
backlog.what: A vanilla JS+CSS theme-picker: a horizontal row of tappable theme swatches that live-swap a small set of CSS custom properties (--lb-bg, --lb-surface, --lb-fg, --lb-accent, --lb-btn-radius, --lb-font) on the page root, with the chosen theme persisted to localStorage. This is the ONE feature that most defines the link-in-bio genre as its own thing — every platform (Linktree presets, Beacons templates, Dotme animated themes) ships a theme/preset chooser as a first-class surface, where a creator picks a 'vibe' (Noir / Pastel / Y2K-chrome / Sunset / Mono) rather than tuning individual properties. The vault has color-picker.js and the taste token system but no genre-scoped PRESET SWATCH ROW that reskins a whole page from one tap. It doubles as a live demo control for the link-in-bio genre and is reusable for the atelier 'Store Studio'. Ships as ~30 lines: data-theme attributes -> token blocks. Degrades to a static themed page if JS is off.
backlog.license: N/A (original vanilla)
backlog.note: Distinct from blocks/color-picker.js (single-value HSV picker) and the taste presets (build-time, not an on-page swatch row). This is a page-reskin-from-one-tap control — the genre's signature interaction.

backlog.name: Creator link-in-bio palette set (Noir / Sunset / Pastel-Y2K / Mono-mint)
backlog.what: A handful of named palettes tuned for the link-in-bio genre's bold, high-contrast, brand-forward look — the aesthetic that reads as a 2026 creator page rather than a corporate landing. Candidates: a deep 'Noir' (near-black bg, vivid single accent), a 'Sunset' warm gradient duo, a 'Pastel-Y2K' chrome/lilac, and a 'Mono-mint' clean light theme. These feed the theme-picker swatch row directly and give the compose engine ready vibes to seed a link-in-bio page so it doesn't fall back on the AI-tell gradient look. Small, additive, no license concerns. Lower standalone value because the vault already has 61 palettes and aurora/mesh backgrounds, but a genre-matched named set tightens coherence scoring for this archetype.
backlog.license: N/A (original color sets)
backlog.note: Vault has 61 palettes but none named/grouped for the creator link-in-bio vibe; pairs with the theme-picker. Verify against list_palettes before adding to avoid near-duplicates of existing dark/pastel sets.

backlog.name: Podcast archetype (show page + episode page) compose-genre
backlog.what: A new media/show compose-genre with two variants. SHOW page grammar: hero (large square cover art + show title/tagline + host) → 'Listen on' platform chip row → latest/featured episode with inline player → episode list (number, title, date, duration pill, mini-play) with filter/search → about-host + newsletter/follow CTA. EPISODE page grammar: episode hero (cover + title + date + duration + guest) → inline player with chapter markers → show notes (summary + timestamped key-moments list + resource links) → full interactive transcript → guest bio → related/next episodes. The vault has every audio component (audio-player-pro, audio-waveform, waveform-regions, the 'podcast' content card) but structure.skill.md only ships saas/agency/restaurant/ecommerce/local-service genres — there is no media/podcast page-level grammar wiring these parts into a coherent show+episode flow. This is the highest-value net-new item: it's an archetype the engine composes, not just a snippet.
backlog.license: N/A (vault-authored archetype; built on existing structure.css backbone + media/* snippets)
backlog.note: Pairs with existing audio-player-pro/waveform components and the 'podcast' content card; add as a new archetype in structure.skill.md with show.html + episode.html references on the .struct backbone.

backlog.name: Hyperaudio Lite (interactive transcript player)
backlog.what: Super-lightweight (~10KB, zero deps, vanilla JS, no build) interactive transcript: words wrapped in <span data-m=ms> sync to an <audio>/<video>; click any word to seek, current word auto-highlights as it plays, heading elements with data-m act as chapter jumps, text selection produces a shareable timecoded URL. This is the load-bearing net-new technique for the episode page — the vault has NO transcript-sync/karaoke-highlight layout (voice-transcribe.css is live STT chrome; video-player-pro has captions/chapters but no scrollable clickable transcript). MIT, actively maintained (v2.4.2, May 2026, 46 releases), CDN-safe via a single script tag — ideal to ship as a cdn-snippet behind a need()-guard, or reimplement the ~120-line click-to-seek + highlight core as a vanilla-snippet (transcript-sync.js writing an .is-current class).
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: Core (click-to-seek + auto-highlight current word + chapter headings) is small enough to vanilla-reimplement as media/transcript-sync.js + .css; keep Hyperaudio as the reference. Degrades to a plain readable transcript if JS off.

backlog.name: 'Listen on' podcast platform chip row
backlog.what: The recognizable podcast UI block: a horizontal row of platform chips — 'Listen on Apple Podcasts / Spotify / Overcast / Pocket Casts / YouTube Music / RSS' — each with a small wordmark/glyph + the 'Listen on' label, as links to each platform. It appears on both the show hero and every episode page. The vault has social-LOGIN buttons (buttons-social.css), two-state subscribe TOGGLE buttons (buttons-toggle.css), and brand gradient buttons — but none is the 'Listen on <platform>' chip pattern (label + platform glyph, outbound link, not an auth/toggle). Small, pure-CSS, vanilla-reimplementable as blocks/listen-on-chips.css with inline-SVG platform glyphs.
backlog.license: N/A (vault-authored CSS; use only platform glyphs/wordmarks per each platform's brand-asset terms)
backlog.note: Distinct from buttons-social.css (OAuth) and buttons-toggle.css (subscribe toggle). Ship glyphs as inline SVG; flag that Apple/Spotify badge artwork has brand-usage rules.

backlog.name: MediaSession-backed persistent now-playing bar
backlog.what: A sticky bottom now-playing bar that survives show→episode navigation, paired with the MediaSession API so title/artist/cover-art and chapter metadata appear in OS/lock-screen media controls and seek-back/forward + next/prev chapter work from hardware keys. The 'sticky player + infinite-scroll episode list' is cited as the signature podcast-site interaction. The vault's closest pieces are static or generic: music-now-playing (a static widget card), bar-pulse (an EQ), scroll-island (a generic dynamic-island pill), and audio-player-pro (an inline, non-persistent player) — none is a persistent site-wide audio bar wired to navigator.mediaSession with setActionHandler + setPositionState + chapterInfo. Vanilla browser API, zero deps, degrades cleanly where unsupported.
backlog.license: N/A (native Web API)
backlog.note: Ship as media/now-playing-bar.css + .js: persistent bar + navigator.mediaSession metadata/artwork/chapters/action-handlers. Reuses audio-waveform for the scrubber; complements (does not replace) audio-player-pro's inline player.

backlog.name: Resume / CV compose-genre (print-first archetype: ATS single-column + designer two-column)
backlog.what: A net-new page archetype the engine composes from one data model into TWO blessed layouts: (1) ATS-SAFE = single-column, reverse-chronological, system font, semantic <section>s, zero icons/tables/textboxes (parsers read L-to-R and scramble multi-col), and (2) DESIGNER = two-column flex grid (left rail: contact/skills bars/education; right: experience timeline/projects) for human-read PDF. Section vocabulary: header(name+contact), summary, experience (timeline of role/company/dates/bullets), skills (bars or chips), education, projects, certs. It is print-first: ships a screen view AND a print stylesheet so 'export to PDF' is one Playwright call (vault already has that pipeline in print-typography.skill). Worth adding because Vault has print TOOLING but no print GENRE — this is the showcase that turns the PDF pipeline into a one-shot user deliverable, and the ATS-vs-designer fork is a real, opinionated design decision the engine can make.
backlog.license: JSON Resume schema MIT (data model only; layout is vanilla)
backlog.note: Reuse existing components/timeline.css + data-viz/progress-ring + gradient-progress for the designer variant's experience timeline and skills bars — only the print backbone + two layout shells are net-new. Default to ATS-safe; offer designer as the opt-in variant with a printed warning that it may not parse.

backlog.name: Print-CSS backbone snippet (@page A4/Letter, break-inside:avoid, print-color-adjust, screen→print bridge)
backlog.what: A zero-dep .css that encodes the page-break vocabulary the genre needs and that the vault is entirely missing: `@page { size: A4 (toggle Letter 8.5in 11in); margin: 15mm }`, `@page :first` for a tighter first page, `break-inside: avoid` (modern; alias legacy `page-break-inside`) on experience/education/project items so a role never splits across a page, `break-before/after: page` for hard section breaks, `orphans/widows: 2-3` for paragraph runts, and `-webkit-print-color-adjust: exact / print-color-adjust: exact` so accent colors and skill bars survive printing (Chrome strips backgrounds by default). Plus the screen↔print bridge: a centered A4/Letter 'paper' card with shadow on screen that flattens to a full-bleed page under `@media print`. This is the load-bearing, non-obvious CSS — generic enough to also harden the existing deck/diagram/docs genres' print paths.
backlog.license: N/A (vanilla CSS, standard properties)
backlog.note: Anti-dup: grep found NO @page/break-inside/page-break/print-color-adjust anywhere in INDEX.md; print-typography.skill.md is PDF-export tooling (Playwright/Paged.js/WeasyPrint), not page-break CSS. This snippet is the missing primitive that makes any print genre verifiable.

backlog.name: Print verification gate (paginate at A4/Letter, assert no mid-item splits + color-adjust intact)
backlog.what: Extend the headless-Chrome save-gate with a print-mode diagnostic: call emulateMedia({media:'print'}), set the viewport to exact A4 (794x1123px @96dpi) or Letter (816x1056px), then assert print-specific failures the current screen-only verifier cannot see — (a) page count is sane (ATS resume should be 1-2 pages; flag bloat), (b) no .resume-item straddles a page boundary (compute item bounding boxes vs page height; this catches break-inside regressions), (c) printBackground renders skill-bar fills (sample the PDF/screenshot to confirm color-adjust:exact worked, not a cleared white box). This is moat-shaped: it makes 'is this resume actually printable correctly?' an automatic gate, the same way the existing overflow/coherence gates work for screen.
backlog.license: Playwright Apache-2.0 (already referenced by the vault's print-typography skill)
backlog.note: Builds directly on the existing headless-Chrome verifier + the Playwright page.pdf() pattern already in print-typography.skill.md. Aligns with the user's memory note 'screenshot is truth for did-it-draw' — same principle applied to the print buffer.

backlog.name: JSON Resume data model + theme-fork as the genre's content contract
backlog.what: Adopt the JSON Resume schema (basics, work, education, skills, projects, certificates, languages) as the single structured input the resume genre composes from — author once, render into either the ATS or designer shell. Inspiration value: its 30+ MIT themes are a free reference library for the layout vocabulary (jsonresume-theme-even = clean two-column; kendall = single-column serif; elegant = minimal serif), letting the genre ship 2-3 named taste variants without reinventing field semantics. Using a real open standard (not an ad-hoc shape) means a user's resume.json round-trips with the broader ecosystem and is trivially re-themeable — matching the vault's token-override philosophy.
backlog.license: MIT (schema + most themes); a few themes carry other licenses — pin per-theme
backlog.note: Use as the data contract + a reference for field naming and the two-column/single-column split; do NOT vendor theme CSS wholesale (it's React/handlebars-coupled and visually dated) — reimplement layouts vanilla on the vault's structure tokens. Reactive Resume (the dev.to author's project) is AGPL — note as inspiration only, do not copy its code.

backlog.name: Event / Conference landing compose-genre (structure.skill.md archetype + conference.html reference)
backlog.what: A net-new page archetype with the convergent 2025-2026 conf-site section sequence: sticky nav w/ Register CTA -> hero (event name + date + venue/timezone + countdown + 2 CTAs) -> event-snapshot/fit-filter strip -> why-attend/outcomes -> multi-track agenda grid (day tabs + track filter) -> speaker grid -> sponsor TIERS (Platinum/Gold/Silver) -> tickets (early-bird urgency, 2-3 tiers) -> venue map + travel -> FAQ -> final register CTA + footer. Worth adding because it reuses pricing/team/countdown/maps primitives but the SKELETON (order, pacing, what fills each slot) is the missing piece -- the engine currently has no event genre to compose, so a conference brief degrades to a generic SaaS page. Add a structure/conference.html reference page on the existing .struct backbone.
backlog.license: N/A (own authored archetype on existing structure.css backbone)
backlog.note: Reuses countdown.css/.js, flip-clock.css, team-grid.css, pricing.css/pricing-toggle, maps-pack, structure.css. Net-new = the section sequence + the agenda/sponsor-tier slots below. Grounded in unicornplatform 9-module structure + Colorlib/muffingroup 2025-26 examples (Demuxed, Phocuswright).

backlog.name: Multi-track conference timetable grid (subgrid + round()/mod() time-to-row mapping)
backlog.what: The signature missing component: a vanilla-CSS multi-STAGE timetable where columns = parallel tracks/stages and rows = time (12 rows/hour, 5-min increments), session cards carry inline --start/--end (e.g. 1045) and CSS round()+mod() maps them to grid lines so a 90-min talk auto-spans the right rows; subgrid keeps all stages aligned. Day tabs switch programs; track-filter pills dim non-matching sessions; on mobile it scroll-snaps stage columns with sticky hour rail. This is categorically different from the vault's schedule-view.css (a Google-Calendar week view: days across top, personal-calendar idiom) and timeline-scrubber.css (video editor). 9elements documents the exact zero-dep technique with a JS fallback for the ~14% lacking round()/mod().
backlog.license: MIT-spirit (technique/article; reimplement, not a dep)
backlog.note: NOT covered by schedule-view.css (week calendar) or timeline-scrubber.css (multi-track video editor). Reimplement as components/agenda-grid.css (+ tiny .js for day-tab/track-filter). Source: 9elements multi-stage timetable + CSS-Tricks 'Building a Conference Schedule with CSS Grid'.

backlog.name: Sponsor-tier layout (Platinum / Gold / Silver / Community partner grid)
backlog.what: A tiered sponsor wall: a labeled band per tier with the higher tiers rendered larger and fewer-per-row (1-2 Platinum big, 3 Gold medium, 6 Silver small, then a dense Community grid), each tier visually weighted by logo size + cell prominence, plus a 'become a sponsor' CTA card. This is a distinct primitive from logo-cloud.css / .s-logos, which are flat single-weight 'trusted by' marquees with NO hierarchy -- conference sponsorship is fundamentally a HIERARCHY display and is a slot the event genre needs. Small vanilla CSS, reuses grayscale-on-default + color-on-hover treatment common to 2025-26 conf sites.
backlog.license: N/A (own authored CSS)
backlog.note: Distinct from logo-cloud.css / s-logos (flat, no tiers). Add as components/sponsor-tiers.css with .sptr-tier[data-tier]. Confirmed as a standard conf section across Colorlib/unicornplatform sources.

backlog.name: Speaker card with linked session (extend team-grid for conference speakers)
backlog.what: A speaker-card variant tying each person to their TALK: headshot + name + role/company + the session title + time/track chip, optionally flipping/expanding to a short bio + social links, and click-to-jump to that session in the agenda grid. team-grid.css gives the photo-wall/hover-bio base but has no session linkage, time/track chip, or 'keynote' emphasis tier -- which is exactly what 2025-26 conf speaker sections do ('speaker profiles with talk descriptions', bios 'tied to the session outcome'). A thin extension, not a new component family, so lower lift but completes the genre.
backlog.license: N/A (own authored CSS extending team-grid)
backlog.note: Partial overlap with team-grid.css (base) -- net-new is the session/time/track linkage + keynote tier + jump-to-agenda. Best shipped as a team-grid modifier (.tgrid-speaker) rather than a separate file.

backlog.name: email-newsletter (marketing/broadcast email compose-genre)
backlog.what: A NET-NEW compose-genre for marketing/broadcast newsletter emails — distinct from the vault's existing email-receipt.css (a fixed transactional receipt/invoice/order template). The genre composes from REORDERABLE modular blocks: preheader (hidden preview text) + brand/nav header + hero (image-or-image+text-overlay with headline/sub/CTA) + article-block (image + heading + dek + 'Read more') + 2-up feature grid (fluid-hybrid columns that stack on mobile) + bulletproof CTA band + social-icon row + legal/unsubscribe/address footer. Output is one 600px table-safe canvas with inline-style guidance. Worth adding because newsletters are a huge, recurring deliverable the engine currently cannot compose, and the modular block vocabulary maps cleanly to the existing compose pipeline (sections in genuine order, end on CTA+footer). MJML's component set (mj-section/mj-column/mj-hero/mj-button/mj-divider/mj-spacer/mj-social) is the canonical semantic map — reimplement, don't depend.
backlog.license: MIT (MJML, mjmlio/mjml — reimplementable inspiration for block semantics)
backlog.note: Net-new vs email-receipt.css. That file is a single fixed transactional layout (line-items table, totals, payment chip); this genre is a reorderable marketing-block system. MJML is MIT — use only as the semantic reference for what blocks exist and how they stack; do not ship MJML as a dep.

backlog.name: email-newsletter.css + bulletproof-button / dark-mode-meta snippet
backlog.what: Vanilla email-safe CSS+markup pack backing the genre: (1) the .news-* block styles (600px canvas, hero, article-block, fluid-hybrid 2-up grid using inline-block + max-width so it stacks with NO media query — works in Gmail), and (2) a true bulletproof CTA button = modern <a> for everyone wrapped against an [if mso]<v:roundrect> VML fallback for Outlook desktop, plus the dark-mode <head> meta pair (<meta name="color-scheme" content="light dark"> + <meta name="supported-color-schemes" content="light dark">) and prefers-color-scheme overrides with off-white/off-black (#FEFEFE/#0E0E0E) to dodge Gmail/Outlook color inversion. This is the concrete, droppable artifact a user inlines for a real send. The vault's email-receipt.css has a single bulletproof <a> but no VML/Outlook fallback and no color-scheme meta — so this is additive even to the transactional template.
backlog.license: MIT-equivalent / public technique (patterns are vendor-neutral; documented by Litmus, Email on Acid)
backlog.note: Make it inline-style-ready: real sends require inlined CSS; ship the class-based version for browser preview/coherence and document the inline export, exactly as email-receipt.css already advises.

backlog.name: Cerberus — responsive HTML email patterns
