backlog.what: The canonical hand-written, framework-free reference for the email-safe HTML the genre must emit: fluid (single-col), responsive (media-query stack), and hybrid (ghost-table + fluid-hybrid, media-query-free) column patterns, plus the <head> boilerplate (DOCTYPE, MSO conditionals, color-scheme meta) and the Outlook ghost-table column wrapper. Use as the spec for the engine's HTML output so the genre's blocks survive Outlook/Gmail without depending on MJML's transpiler. Pairs with MJML: MJML gives the block SEMANTICS, Cerberus gives the exact bytes. Worth adding as the verification target — the email verifier can check 'does it contain a 600px ghost-table wrapper / color-scheme meta / no unsupported CSS.'
backlog.license: MIT (TedGoas/Cerberus)
backlog.note: Inspiration/spec, not a dep — copy the ghost-table + fluid-hybrid markup patterns into the genre's emitter. Could seed a lightweight 'email-safe' verification check (600px width, color-scheme meta present, no flex/grid/position in output).

backlog.name: Comparison / 'X vs Y' competitor page genre (compose archetype #6)
backlog.what: A net-new page archetype for structure.skill.md: the high-intent SEO competitor/alternatives page. Canonical 2026 section sequence: Nav -> 'You vs Them' two-column verdict hero (your-logo vs their-logo + one-line positioning claim + dual CTA) -> at-a-glance verdict callout cards (3 win-statements, condensed) -> full feature matrix (check / partial / cross, sticky header, your column highlighted) -> pricing side-by-side (+ optional TCO/savings line) -> 'Why teams switch' / migration CTA band (data-import + setup help) -> social proof (G2 badges, switcher testimonials) -> FAQ accordion -> related-comparison links (programmatic SEO interlink). Every slot maps to EXISTING vault snippets (feature-comparison.css, pricing-tables-pack, marketing-pack, accordion-pack, cta-sections, .s-split) plus 2-3 small new blocks below. This is the missing 6th archetype — the vault has SaaS/agency/restaurant/ecommerce/local-service but no competitor page, which Powered By Search/Teamwork cite as a top-converting, must-have programmatic SEO page type (54% more comparison-page conversions). High value, low duplication, composes deterministically from parts already present.
backlog.license: N/A (vault-authored archetype)

backlog.name: Tri-state feature-support indicators (full / partial-limited / none) + 'honest/transparency' row variant
backlog.what: feature-comparison.css today has Yes(check)/No(dash)/Premium(star) but no explicit PARTIAL/limited tri-state — the exact thing NN/g (2026) and Semrush-style 'feature availability indicators' call essential for credible comparisons (full check, half/partial badge, cross). Add a `.fcmp-partial` cell (half-fill icon + optional 'limited'/'add-on'/'beta' label), tooltip-on-row support (`.fcmp-info` hover def), and a `.fcmp-honest` row modifier that deliberately marks where the COMPETITOR wins (green on their column) — the 'transparency row' pattern SaaSFrame found recurring in 2026 pages because one-sided tables convert like informational content. Small additive CSS+optional JS extension to an existing file; raises the comparison genre's anti-slop credibility floor.
backlog.license: N/A (vault-authored, extends MIT-spirit vault file)

backlog.name: Multi-competitor tab switcher + verdict callout cards block
backlog.what: Two recurring 2026 comparison blocks the vault lacks as named patterns. (1) Multi-competitor switcher: tabs/pills (You vs Trello | vs Asana | vs Monday) that swap the matrix dataset client-side without reload — Monday.com/Unbounce pattern for one page ranking on many 'alternative to' queries (programmatic SEO). The vault has tabs and infinite-canvas but no comparison-dataset-swap binding. (2) Verdict / 'at-a-glance' callout cards: a 2-3 card 'Bottom line' strip ('Pick us if… / Pick them if…' + one-line winner statement) that distills the matrix for skimmers. Both are small vanilla JS+CSS additions that plug directly into the comparison genre above; high reuse for any SaaS/dev-tool deck or page.
backlog.license: N/A (vault-authored)

backlog.name: TCO / savings 'cost-of-switching' calculator widget
backlog.what: A zero-dep interactive block cited as a 2026 differentiator on pricing-intent comparison pages: seats/usage slider -> live 'You pay $X, they pay $Y, you save $Z/yr' readout with an animated savings bar. Distinct from the vault's existing slider-pricing (single-product price calc) — this is a two-product DELTA/savings comparator that pairs with the pricing-side-by-side slot of the comparison genre. Pure vanilla (range input + tabular-nums + count-up). Modest scope, but it's the conversion hook the comparison archetype is missing and it reinforces the 'verdict' narrative with numbers.
backlog.license: N/A (vault-authored)

backlog.name: Incident detail / postmortem page (status-page history sub-archetype)
backlog.what: A single-incident page: severity badge (critical/major/minor/maintenance) + title + affected-components list + impact duration, then a vertical status-update timeline reading newest-first with timestamped stages (Investigating -> Identified -> Monitoring -> Resolved, each 'posted at HH:MM' with body text), and an optional blameless postmortem body using the canonical headings Summary / Timeline of events / Customer impact / Root cause / Remediation / Preventative actions. The vault's existing components/status-page.css (.sts) only renders a compressed inline incident TIMELINE inside the live snapshot; there is no dedicated per-incident detail/postmortem layout. This is the most-requested net-new piece and reuses the same --ok/--warn/--danger/--info tokens, so it drops in zero-dep next to .sts.
backlog.license: N/A (own CSS reimplementation; pattern from Atlassian Statuspage / Status.io / Instatus / BetterStack conventions)
backlog.note: Near-neighbor: status-page.css has an inline .sts-incident timeline, but not a standalone detail/postmortem page with stage-stamped updates + blameless headings. Add as e.g. .sts-detail / .sts-pm classes in the same file. Permissive: this is a reimplemented layout pattern, no third-party code.

backlog.name: Past-incidents archive (month-grouped history list + impact-level key)
backlog.what: The 'Incident History' index view: incidents grouped by month with a color-coded impact-level key at the top (Critical / Major / Minor / Maintenance / Retroactive), each row showing title, current state (resolved/monitoring), one-line summary, and total duration; clicking a row navigates to the incident-detail/postmortem page above. DigitalOcean's and GitHub's history pages are the reference layout. The vault has no month-grouped archive view — only the live snapshot's recent-incident sliver. Cheap, high-coverage, CSS-only, themeable on the existing status tokens.

backlog.name: Subscribe / notification-channels widget (email · SMS · Slack · webhook · RSS+Atom)
backlog.what: The 'Subscribe to Updates' popover panel that every status page exposes: tabbed channels — Email, SMS, Slack/Teams, Webhook, and an Atom/RSS feed tab that surfaces the canonical /history.atom and /history.rss feed URLs (copy-to-clipboard). The vault has a generic components/popover.css/.js host and buttons-toggle.css has a 'subscribe' two-state button, but no notification-channels PANEL CONTENT with the email/SMS/Slack/webhook/feed-link tab set. Small, self-contained, completes the status-page history archetype (a status page is incomplete without it). Can mount inside the existing popover.

backlog.name: status-page (full) compose-genre — snapshot + history + detail/postmortem + subscribe
backlog.what: A new multi-view page archetype the engine composes: section sequence = header with overall-status banner + Subscribe button -> live component groups (existing .sts snapshot) -> monthly uptime-history calendar (status-semantic day grid, see note) -> past-incidents archive (month-grouped) -> footer with RSS/Atom links; plus a linked incident-detail/postmortem route. The vault already documents a 'status page' page-type in structure.skill.md but only fills the LIVE snapshot; this promotes it to a full Statuspage/Instatus-class archetype with its history/postmortem/subscribe views wired in section order. Extends an existing strength (the saas-pages phase) rather than adding a new domain.

For the monthly uptime-history calendar, do NOT add a new generic component: data-viz/heatmap-calendar.css/.js already exists but is an INTENSITY contribution grid (levels l0-l4). The status-history calendar is semantically different (each day colored by the worst operational status that day + per-day incident hover-summary), so add it as a status-semantic variant/class on the .sts snippet, not as a duplicate heatmap. Genre recipe lives in structure.skill.md's decision tree.

backlog.name: Changelog Page genre (standalone release-notes archetype)
backlog.what: A full page-level compose archetype for a public product changelog / release-notes page, distinct from the existing changelog-popover widget and the static .rm-log feed. Section sequence: hero/header (title + 'Subscribe' + RSS button) -> sticky LEFT date/version rail (scrollspy TOC, year jump) -> reverse-chron entry stream where each entry = sticky date/version header + category chip(s) + linkable H2 anchor (copy-permalink on hover) + body + selective media -> footer 'Subscribe by email / RSS'. The canonical pattern across Linear, Vercel, Stripe, Clerk, Resend, Raycast (2024-2026). Worth adding because the vault can compose docs/roadmap/status SaaS pages but has NO changelog PAGE -- only a popover and a CSS-only feed block; this is the missing standalone page genre that ships as part of nearly every modern SaaS site.
backlog.license: N/A (original archetype; reimplemented from public-page conventions)
backlog.note: Reuse structure.css tokens + .s-section rhythm for the skeleton; the entry feed can extend .rm-log styling. The NEW page-level pieces are: sticky version rail + scrollspy, version anchors w/ copy-permalink, category filter bar, year-jump nav, RSS/subscribe affordances. Fits as archetype #6 in structure.skill.md ('Changelog / release-notes page') with a 'What changed / see what's new' intent-mapping cue.

backlog.name: Changelog interaction layer: filter chips + scrollspy version rail + copy-permalink (ChangelogPage.init)
backlog.what: The vanilla JS the genre needs and the vault never shipped for .rm-log: (1) category filter chips (All / New / Improved / Fixed / Security / Deprecated / product-area) that show-hide entries and sync to a ?filter= query param; (2) IntersectionObserver scrollspy that highlights the active version/date in the sticky left rail and supports #v2.4.0 deep-links + smooth scroll; (3) copy-permalink-on-heading-hover (clipboard write of the anchor URL, like docs '#' affordances). Net-new wiring -- filter-chips exist generically (search-pack/fbar) and copy-link exists only for SOCIAL share, neither wired to a changelog timeline; roadmap.css is CSS-only with no JS at all.
backlog.license: MIT (original; standard IntersectionObserver + Clipboard API)
backlog.note: Reuse the IntersectionObserver scrollspy approach already proven in headers-pro.js / sidebar-pack docs scroll-spy; reuse the clipboard helper from share-buttons.js. Degrades gracefully: with JS off, all entries show and anchors still work via native :target.

backlog.name: Changelog RSS/Atom feed autodiscovery + client-side feed builder + schema.org DataFeed
backlog.what: A small vanilla helper + head pattern that makes a changelog page 'developer-respecting': (a) inject <link rel=alternate type=application/atom+xml> / application/rss+xml / application/feed+json autodiscovery tags so browsers and readers find the feed; (b) a tiny function that serializes the in-page entry list to a valid Atom 1.0 XML string (RFC-3339 timestamps, <entry> per release, permalink <link>) as a Blob download / data URL; (c) schema.org DataFeed + structured-data JSON-LD in <head> for SEO. Net-new -- the vault has ZERO feed-syndication, RSS/Atom, or DataFeed coverage; RSS is the single most-cited 'must-have' that separates a real changelog page from a popover.
backlog.license: permissive (Atom RFC-4287 / schema.org are open specs; helper original MIT)
backlog.note: True server-generated feeds need a backend, but the autodiscovery <link> tags + schema.org JSON-LD + a client-side Atom string builder are fully vanilla and verifiable. Mark the actual XML endpoint as a build-step note. Pure signal for the developer-audience changelog use-case.

backlog.name: Version-header treatment: date-version (Stripe '2026-05-18') / semver badge + breaking-change warning flag
backlog.what: A typographic header component for changelog entries distinct from the small inline tag chips in .rm-log: a large sticky entry header that renders either a date-based API version (Stripe-style, e.g. 2026-05-18), a semver badge (v2.4.0, Framer-style), or a plain bold date (Linear-style), PLUS a high-visibility 'Breaking change' / 'Deprecated' warning badge treatment. These big version/date headers are the load-bearing scannable element of every top changelog page and the scrollspy-rail target. The vault's .rm-log only has tiny new/fix/improved chips -- no version-number or breaking-change header system.
backlog.license: MIT (original CSS)
backlog.note: CSS-only; tabular-nums for date/version alignment (pairs with typography/gradient-numbers tabular). Three header modes (date / semver / date-version) + warning/deprecated states. Becomes the anchor id source for the scrollspy rail in candidate #2.

backlog.name: Glossary / Encyclopedia compose genre (A-Z jump-nav + term-definition cards + see-also cross-links + infobox)
backlog.what: A net-new page archetype distinct from docs-layout (prose+TOC). Skeleton: sticky/floating A-Z alphabet jump-bar (letters dim when a section is empty), a search/filter combobox at top, then alphabetized term groups where each TERM is a self-contained definition card (term + short 'snippet' answer + longer body + example + a 'See also' chip row of related terms), plus an optional right-rail infobox (key facts / one-line TL;DR). Conventions confirmed across 2025 glossary best-practice writeups: clickable A|B|C jump links, smooth-scroll to letter sections, back-to-top, per-term deep-link anchors. This differs from prose docs structurally (browse-by-letter not read-top-to-bottom; thousands of short cards not one long article; cross-linked not linear). The vault has toc.css, docs-layout.css, combobox, sidebar-nav, popover — all the parts — but no genre that assembles them into a glossary skeleton, and the engine has no archetype for it in structure.skill.md.
backlog.license: N/A (genre/skeleton built from existing vanilla snippets)
backlog.note: Closest existing: docs-layout.css is explicitly prose+TOC (different read model). Add as a new archetype in structure.skill.md + a structure/glossary.html reference page reusing structure.css tokens. The only genuinely new CSS/JS atoms it needs are the A-Z jump-bar and the term-card grid; everything else reuses existing snippets.

backlog.name: Term-link hovercard / page-preview popover ('stretchtext') via Popover API + CSS Anchor Positioning
backlog.what: The signature wiki/digital-garden interaction the vault lacks: hovering an internal term link pops a small card previewing that term's definition (Wikipedia 'Page Previews' / Obsidian-Publish hovercards). 2025-2026 this is now doable largely declaratively — the Popover API gives implicit invoker-anchor pairing and top-layer/light-dismiss for free, and CSS Anchor Positioning (anchor()/position-area, shipped Chromium + Firefox late-2025, OddBird polyfill for Safari) auto-flips it near viewport edges. A tiny JS shim fetches/transcludes the target term's snippet on hover-intent with a delay. This is the cross-link mechanism that makes a glossary feel like a wiki rather than a list. The vault's popover.js is a click/hover floating PANEL and citation-popover.js is numbered SOURCE chips — neither previews an internal term/definition on link hover, and neither is built on the new Popover API + anchor-positioning primitives.
backlog.license: Web platform / MDN (public domain docs); OddBird polyfills are BSD/MIT
backlog.note: Distinct from popover.css/.js (generic panel) and citation-popover (source chips). Reimplement as a vanilla term-hovercard snippet: data-term link -> hover-intent -> anchored preview card pulled from the matching definition. Degrade gracefully to a plain anchor jump when JS/anchor-positioning is absent.

backlog.name: Related-terms / backlinks panel + mini force-directed term graph
backlog.what: Two complementary cross-linking surfaces that separate a knowledge-encyclopedia from prose: (1) a 'Linked from / Mentioned in' BACKLINKS panel auto-derived by scanning which other term cards reference the current term (the inverse of 'See also'); and (2) a small inline RELATED-TERMS GRAPH — a force-directed node-link mini-map of a term and its neighbors, the recognizable Obsidian/Roam local-graph affordance. Backlinks are pure DOM/data computation (parse data-term refs, invert the adjacency, render a chip list) — zero deps. The graph can be a compact SVG/canvas vanilla force layout (a few hundred lines, Verlet/spring) or, behind a need()-guard, d3-force via CDN with graceful fallback to the backlinks list. The vault has dataviz/charts but no node-link relationship graph and no backlink computation at all.
backlog.license: Pattern is original; CDN fallback option d3-force is BSD-3-Clause (permissive)
backlog.note: No backlink/inverse-link or node-link graph exists in the vault (dataviz.skill.md is charts: bars/lines/donut/heatmap, not relationship graphs). Ship backlinks as zero-dep vanilla; offer the local-graph as vanilla SVG first, with optional d3-force CDN behind need()-guard. Keep the graph cosmetic/optional so the imagery-floor and coherence gate stay happy.

backlog.name: DefinedTerm / DefinedTermSet JSON-LD auto-emit (AI-citation / AEO verification capability)
backlog.what: The reason this archetype is surging: glossary/'topic' pages are the highest-yield format for AI answer-engine citation (AEO/GEO). Schema.org DefinedTerm marks one term+definition; DefinedTermSet wraps the whole glossary; each term gets a stable @id fragment so other pages/sites deep-link individual definitions, and LLMs recognize the page as an authoritative reference for 'what is X?' queries. Make this a generator + a VERIFICATION gate (the vault's moat): when composing a glossary, auto-emit valid DefinedTermSet/DefinedTerm JSON-LD from the term cards, and have the headless-Chrome verifier assert it parses, that every term card has a matching @id and termCode/name, and that anchors resolve — an 'AEO floor' analogous to the existing imagery floor. Purely structural/SEO; nothing in the vault emits or validates schema.org/JSON-LD today.
backlog.license: Schema.org vocabulary (open, CC-BY-SA docs); JSON-LD is a W3C standard
backlog.note: No structured-data/JSON-LD emission or validation anywhere in the vault. Best as a verification capability (auto-generate + gate) tied to the glossary genre; also useful generically (FAQ/Article schema) later. Keep it invisible <script type=application/ld+json> so it never affects the coherence/imagery render gates.

backlog.name: MiniSearch full-text search overlay (engine behind need(), ⌘K results UI)
backlog.what: MiniSearch is a ~7kB-gzip, zero-dependency, MIT full-text engine (UMD build on jsDelivr: cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/minisearch@7/dist/umd/index.min.js) with prefix search, fuzzy (edit-distance) matching, BM25-style ranking, field boosting, and — crucially for snippet UX — a result shape (match, terms, queryTerms, storeFields) that tells you exactly which terms matched in which fields. That is the missing engine that turns the vault's content genres into searchable docs. The net-new piece is the OVERLAY UX wrapped around it: a 'cdn-snippet' that (1) auto-indexes content from the page (e.g. docs-layout .docs-prose headings/paragraphs, glossary terms, changelog entries) into a MiniSearch index, (2) renders a command-palette-style results overlay with extractMatchSnippet (earliest match ±~80 chars) + highlightMatch wrapping matched terms in <mark>, (3) keyboard nav (↑/↓/enter/esc), and (4) recent + suggested rows persisted in localStorage. Wire it behind a need('minisearch') guard so it degrades to the existing static-items command-palette if the CDN is unavailable. This is the recommended primary: MIT, smallest API surface, match-position data ready-made for the highlight layer, and synchronous (no worker plumbing) for the doc sizes Design Book composes.
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: Closest existing pieces: command-palette.js (fuzzy over a STATIC items array — no content indexing) and search-pack.css (CSS-only result-card, no engine). Neither indexes page content or generates ranked highlighted snippets. MiniSearch supplies the index+ranking+match positions; the overlay/snippet/recent UX is net-new. MIT, fully CDN-safe.

backlog.name: FlexSearch (alternate full-text engine — fastest, built-in highlight, Apache-2.0)
backlog.what: FlexSearch v0.8.2 (May 2025) is the fastest browser full-text engine, with a light build at ~4.5kB gzip and full bundle ~16kB, served from jsDelivr. Unlike MiniSearch it ships BUILT-IN result highlighting and document/multi-field search, plus configurable tokenization (forward/reverse/full/tolerant) for prefix and partial matching. Worth adding as the documented SECOND engine option for the same search-overlay 'cdn-snippet' — pick FlexSearch when the indexed corpus is large (it scales to 100k+ docs far better than MiniSearch's ~50k client-side ceiling) or when you want highlighting handled by the engine rather than a hand-rolled snippet extractor. NOTE the license correction: FlexSearch is Apache-2.0, NOT MIT as commonly assumed — still permissive/CDN-safe, but flag it as Apache so attribution/NOTICE expectations are tracked.
backlog.license: Apache-2.0 (not MIT — permissive, CDN-safe)
backlog.note: Apache-2.0, not MIT — flag for attribution. Same anti-dup story as MiniSearch: no full-text engine exists in the vault today. Use as the large-corpus / built-in-highlight alternative behind the same need()-guarded overlay.

backlog.name: Content-search compose genre (docs/changelog/glossary archetype with palette + ⌘K full-text)
backlog.what: A new compose-genre that assembles the existing CSS-only content shells the vault already has (docs-layout.css 3-column docs shell, changelog/roadmap feed, a glossary/definition list, toc.js scroll-spy, reading-aids progress) INTO a coherent, navigable 'documentation/knowledge-base site' archetype — and crucially makes it usable by wiring the MiniSearch/FlexSearch search overlay above as the genre's signature interaction (⌘K → highlighted-snippet results → jump-to-section via toc slugs). Today these content snippets exist but there is no genre that connects them or a search affordance tied to them, so composed docs pages are dead-end readers. Pagefind (MIT, WASM static-index search) is referenced as the design north-star and as INSPIRATION only — its build-time CLI crawl of pre-rendered HTML is the wrong fit for Design Book's runtime/no-build composition, so the engine should be MiniSearch/FlexSearch at runtime rather than Pagefind. The genre also gives the verifier a target: a coherence/imagery-floor check specialized for long-form content pages (TOC present, search wired, sections slugged, no orphan headings).
backlog.license: Pagefind MIT (inspiration only — build-time CLI, not a runtime CDN dep)
backlog.note: Pagefind = inspiration, NOT a dep (requires a build step to crawl HTML; incompatible with runtime composition). The addable thing is the genre + the runtime full-text overlay it standardizes on.

backlog.name: Email-safe CSS / HTML lint gate (caniemail data + bulletproof/600px/inline structural checks)
backlog.what: A genre-specific save-gate for the email genre. Two layers: (1) a caniemail-data layer — the shellscape/caniemail npm package is MIT, auto-released, and is the fresh, importable JSON of HTML/CSS feature support across ~40 email clients (Gmail, Outlook/Word engine, Apple Mail, etc.). For any CSS property/selector used in the email HTML it yields an objective per-client support verdict (supported / partial / none). (2) A structural-rule layer the gate enforces directly from the DOM in the existing headless Chrome: NO flex/grid/position (Outlook's Word engine drops them), table-based layout only, max-width 600px canvas, ALL styling inlined as style= attributes (no <style>/<link> that Gmail strips), bulletproof-button check (CTA is an <a> with padding+bg, not a styled <button>), images have width/height + alt, and a dark-mode safety note. The vault already SHIPS the artifact this would verify (components/email-receipt.css promises 'table-safe, 600px, bulletproof-button, inline the styles for real sends') but nothing checks the promise was kept — so this is pure net-new verification, not duplication. doiuse-email (MIT, shellscape) is the reference algorithm for walking parsed CSS against the data but is stale (2022) — reimplement its ~150-line lint loop vanilla over the caniemail JSON rather than depend on it.
backlog.license: MIT (caniemail data package; doiuse-email reference also MIT)
backlog.note: Net-new gate. Vault has the email-receipt.css PRODUCER and email-pack UI but zero email-safety VERIFICATION. Recommend bundling the caniemail JSON locally (it is small) so the gate is offline/deterministic and pin a snapshot for reproducible client output.

backlog.name: Print page-fit gate — A4/Letter page-count + horizontal-overflow + page-break-integrity via page.pdf()
backlog.what: A genre-specific save-gate for the print/resume/invoice genres, runnable with the vault's EXISTING headless Chrome (Playwright/Puppeteer print is already in print-typography.skill.md) and ZERO new runtime deps. The gate emulateMedia('print'), then runs four objective checks: (1) PAGE-COUNT FIT — call page.pdf({format:'A4'/'Letter', printBackground:true}), read the produced PDF's page count, and assert it against a genre target (resume = exactly 1; one-pager = 1; otherwise flag unexpected blank/overflow pages). Page count is read from the PDF bytes (count '/Type /Page' objects — no PDF lib needed) so it is fully deterministic. (2) HORIZONTAL OVERFLOW — in-page, compare document scrollWidth against the printable width (A4 210mm minus @page margins → px at 96dpi) to catch content/tables/images clipped off the right edge. (3) PAGE-BREAK INTEGRITY — flag elements taller than the printable column, and rows/cards that lack break-inside:avoid and would split across the page boundary. (4) IMAGE/BLEED — assert images have max-width:100% and that no absolutely-positioned element exceeds page bounds. All four map to a pass/fail + an offending-element list, exactly like the existing overflow/coherence gate but in print-media space, which the screen-space gate cannot see (because @media print restyles the page).
backlog.license: N/A (technique over already-bundled Apache-2.0 Playwright/Puppeteer)
backlog.note: Net-new: the existing save-gate measures SCREEN overflow/coherence; this measures PRINT-media page-fit, which is a different render pass and currently unchecked. deck.css and print-typography.skill.md PRODUCE print output but nothing VERIFIES it fits A4. Caveat: headless Chrome silently drops url() images referenced in @page rules — gate should warn if @page uses url() (require data: URIs).

backlog.name: Mailpit html-check (caniemail-mapped 175+ check scoring) — score model reference
backlog.what: Inspiration for the SHAPE of the email gate's output, not a dependency. Mailpit (Go, MIT) runs 175+ HTML/CSS checks that each map to a caniemail test and produces a single weighted 'support score' (0-100%) plus a per-feature breakdown of which clients fail. Adopting this scoring model — weight each used feature by its caniemail support % and the client-mix the user targets, then emit one email-safety score alongside the warning list — makes the email gate produce a comparable number to the vault's existing anti-slop 'coherence' score, so email pages get a trustworthy 0-100 'will-this-render-in-Outlook' figure. Mailpit itself is a server (SMTP testing UI), so it is not embeddable into the save-gate; take the score weighting + check taxonomy as the design reference and compute it vanilla over the caniemail JSON from candidate 1.
backlog.license: MIT (Mailpit) — but it is a standalone Go server, use as design reference only
backlog.note: Not a dep — server-shaped. Borrow the weighted-score model so the email gate's output mirrors the existing coherence-score UX. Lower value because it is a refinement of candidate 1's output format, not a separate capability.

backlog.name: product-onboarding / first-run page genre (compose-genre: "onboarding")
backlog.what: A new desktop app-first-run PAGE archetype the engine composes — distinct from the landing-page genres and from the existing onboarding WIDGETS. Anatomy (verified across Appcues/Userpilot/ProCreator 2026): app-shell sidebar/topbar -> welcome hero with a NAME/greeting + setup progress ring/bar -> getting-started checklist (3-5 activation tasks, first one pre-ticked) -> connect-integrations grid (logo + toggle/Connect cards) -> sample-data / 'load demo workspace' CTA band (defeats the blank-canvas problem) -> 'what's next' resource cards (docs/video/community) -> footer/help row. Add as a GENRE_SEQUENCES entry ('onboarding') in mcp-server/server.js with matching .s-* shells (welcomeHero, setupProgress, checklist, integrationGrid, sampleData, nextSteps), a structure.skill.md archetype #6, and a structure/onboarding.html reference page. Net-new: the engine has zero app/first-run page genres (only marketing-site + diagram/plan genres). High value because PLG first-run is one of the most-requested SaaS surfaces and the vault can currently only emit a marketing 'saas' landing or an analytics 'dashboard', neither of which is a setup-success page.
backlog.license: n/a (original genre spec)

backlog.name: connect-integrations grid shell (integration directory / app-marketplace card grid)
backlog.what: The one genuinely under-served SLOT in the proposed page: an auto-fit grid of integration cards (provider logo monogram + name + one-line + Connect button or status toggle, with 'Connected' state). The vault has integration ROWS/cards scattered (settings-pack integration-list = list rows, widget-pack integration-card = single toggle row, cards-pack .cdp-integration = one card, marketing-pack integration-row = logo strip) but NO grid shell that reads as a first-run 'connect your stack' wall with connected/empty states. Best added as a small vanilla CSS pattern (e.g. components/integration-grid.css with .intg-grid > .intg-card, .is-connected modifier, logo-monogram fallback so it satisfies the imagery floor without external assets) that the new genre's integrationGrid slot fills. Reuses the existing token system; ~1 file. Lower-than-genre value since pieces exist, but it's the missing connective tissue that makes the genre render convincingly.
backlog.license: n/a (original)

backlog.name: first-run.skill.md decision-tree (activation-first sequencing rules)
backlog.what: A short skill encoding the 2026 PLG sequencing logic so the genre composes the RIGHT order, not just markup: the Orient -> Activate -> Reinforce three-phase model; 'defer integrations/team-invite/advanced config until AFTER first value'; 'pre-tick the first checklist item and any task the user already did'; 'empty state IS the primary onboarding surface — one obvious action, human voice, never "No data yet"'; 'sample/demo data beats a blank canvas'; and the 3-5 task ceiling. This is the structure-skill analog for app first-run (the existing structure.skill.md only covers marketing pages and mobile-design.skill.md covers native flows — neither covers a desktop app first-run home). Makes the genre defensible/coherent rather than a random stack of widgets; pairs with the verification gate's coherence score.
backlog.license: n/a (original synthesis of public UX guidance)

backlog.name: CVD hue-collapse save-gate (Viénot-1999 + Brettel-1997 simulate → ΔE2000 collapse score)
backlog.what: A net-new verification capability: take the composed page's distinct semantic colors (or its screenshot, already produced by the render harness), simulate each under protanopia/deuteranopia/tritanopia, then compute pairwise CIEDE2000 distance. Flag any color pair that is distinguishable in normal vision but collapses below a just-noticeable-difference threshold under simulation — i.e. 'meaning encoded by hue alone'. This is exactly the pitfall color.skill.md warns about in prose (line 132: 'Never encode meaning by hue alone') with NO tooling behind it. The render harness gates render/overflow/a11y-focus/coherence/imagery but has zero CVD simulation, and the vault has no perceptual color-distance (ΔE) utility at all. The science is public-domain: Viénot 1999 is a single 3×3 matrix in linear RGB (accurate for red-green), Brettel 1997 is the only accurate model for tritanopia (~50-75 LOC). Critical correctness note: must linearize sRGB (gamma 2.4) before the matrix — skipping it is the classic Coblis/ColorJack bug. Reimplement vanilla from the papers/DaltonLens, not from the inaccurate Lokno/ColorJack gist.
backlog.license: Public-domain algorithms (Brettel 1997 / Viénot 1999 papers); reference impls MIT (DaltonLens, Colour)
backlog.note: Pairs with a missing ΔE2000/CIEDE2000 vanilla util. The biggest moat gap: turns a prose warning into an enforceable save-gate.

backlog.name: cvd-preview.js — live protan/deutan/tritan page preview (feColorMatrix / canvas)
backlog.what: A zero-dep vanilla snippet that applies the CVD matrices to a canvas or via SVG feColorMatrix (color-interpolation-filters="linearRGB") to render the composed page as protanopes/deuteranopes/tritanopes see it. Companion to the gate: the gate scores/flags, this snippet lets the workbench SHOW the simulated view. DaltonLens publishes drop-in feColorMatrix coefficients (e.g. Viénot protanopia 0.10889/0.89111/… and Brettel tritanopia two-plane matrices). Net-new: the vault has color-grade and shader effects but nothing that simulates color-vision deficiency. Must set linearRGB interpolation or the simulation is invalid.
backlog.license: MIT (matrices from DaltonLens / Colour reference impls; underlying papers public-domain)
backlog.note: Drop-in feColorMatrix values published openly. Avoid the disavowed ColorJack/Lokno matrices used by npm `color-blind`.

backlog.name: Machado-2009 severity-graded CVD matrices (from MIT-licensed Colour)
backlog.what: Precomputed RGB transform matrices for protan/deutan/tritan at severity 0..1 (1.0 = full dichromacy, intermediate = anomalous trichromacy), published openly in the MIT-licensed Colour Python library (colour.blindness.machado2009) — directly copyable as JS constants. Lets the gate model partial color-blindness (anomalous trichromats, the large majority of CVD population) not just full dichromacy, raising diagnostic realism. Net-new; complements Viénot/Brettel which are the dichromacy endpoints.
backlog.license: MIT (Colour library) / underlying Machado 2009 paper
backlog.note: Copy the severity-1.0 (and a mid-severity) matrices as JS constants; don't take a runtime dep.

backlog.name: @bjornlu/colorblind (MIT, zero-dep simulation library)
backlog.what: MIT, zero-dependency, browser-safe simulation library covering protanopia/deuteranopia/tritanopia/achromatopsia via research-backed matrices (simulation only, no daltonization). Useful as a cross-check reference to validate a vanilla reimplementation's numbers. NOT recommended as a live dep: last published 2022 (stale) and the whole thing is ~tens of LOC, so vanilla-reimplementing from the papers is cleaner and avoids the simulate-only limitation. Note for contrast: npm `color-blind` (skratchdot) pulls in `onecolor`, has no clear license, and uses the inaccurate ColorJack matrices — do not use.
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: Use as a validation reference for the vanilla impl, not a dependency. Avoid skratchdot/color-blind (deps + inaccurate matrices).

backlog.name: apcach reverse-contrast solver (WCAG-model path, vanilla reimplementation)
backlog.what: apcach composes a color to hit a TARGET contrast instead of checking after the fact: it fixes OKLCH chroma+hue and runs a bounded binary search over lightness (window {0,1}, max 20 iters), bisecting until measured contrast converges on the target. Reimplement the WCAG-model path (crToBg/crToFg + searchDirection lighter/darker/auto) as a ~40-line vanilla helper, e.g. fgForContrast(bgOklch, targetRatio, hue, chroma, dir) -> oklch(...). This is the missing inverse of the vault's existing forward WCAG checker: it makes --fg/--muted/--accent provably accessible BY CONSTRUCTION. WCAG math is plain relative-luminance (no encumbered dep), and the OKLCH lightness bisection is the whole trick. Polarity-aware so dark mode works. NET-NEW: vault has zero contrast-targeted composition anywhere.
backlog.license: MIT (apcach itself; WCAG path deps wcag-contrast + culori are MIT) — reimplement clean-room, no encumbrance
backlog.note: Ship the WCAG-model bisection as vanilla. The APCA-model path is the same algorithm but its core math (apca-w3) is 'Limited W3 License' (restricted, non-commercial, time-barred) — do NOT vendor apca-w3; treat APCA-flavored output as inspiration/optional-CDN only. The vanilla WCAG inverter is the clean, shippable win that the palette tokens can consume at build/compose time.

backlog.name: APCA Lc reverse solver (apcach APCA model) — perceptual + polarity-aware target
backlog.what: The genuinely perceptual version of the inversion: 'give me the OKLCH foreground that lands exactly Lc 75 on this bg', using APCA's Lc scale (Lc 0..106 dark-on-light, 0..-108 light-on-dark) which tracks human perception and dark-mode polarity far better than WCAG2 ratios. apcach exposes this via the same crToBg/crToFg with contrastModel:'apca'. This is the 'right' answer for a perceptual contrast target and is exactly what the vault's one-line APCA mention in color.skill.md gestures at but never delivers. Worth documenting as the aspirational target / for non-commercial demos via CDN behind a need()-guard.
backlog.license: RESTRICTED — apca-w3 core math is 'Limited W3 License' (commercial use prohibited w/o signed agreement; web-content-only; beta/time-barred)
backlog.note: FLAG: cannot be a vanilla dep due to the W3 license. Use as inspiration + optional non-commercial CDN demo only. The shippable, encumbrance-free version is the WCAG-model vanilla solver above; this entry documents WHY (perceptual + polarity) and the license trap so the vault doesn't accidentally vendor apca-w3.

backlog.name: CSS contrast-color() (Baseline 2026) as the graceful-degradation floor
backlog.what: Native CSS that auto-picks an accessible color — shipped in Chrome 147, Firefox 146, Safari 26, reached Baseline Newly Available April 2026. It only returns black/white via WCAG2 luminance (no APCA, no arbitrary OKLCH foreground), so it does NOT replace the reverse solver — but it's the perfect zero-JS FALLBACK floor: emit contrast-color(var(--bg)) for the simple b/w case and reserve the JS bisection helper for tinted --muted/--accent that need a specific Lc/ratio. Pairs with the solver as a progressive-enhancement story and is itself net-new to the vault (zero matches).
backlog.license: n/a (native CSS feature)
backlog.note: Not a substitute for the perceptual reverse solver (UA-defined, WCAG2-only, b/w-only) — position as the degradation floor the JS helper enhances. Behind @supports(color: contrast-color(white)).

backlog.name: apcach (constant-APCA / consistent-chroma ramp engine)
backlog.what: MIT JS library that composes a color from a *target* instead of a fixed lightness: apcach(contrast, chroma, hue) returns the OKLCH color whose APCA contrast vs a reference equals the requested Lc, holding chroma constant. crToBg()/crToFg() pin contrast against any bg/fg; maxChroma() finds the most-saturated in-gamut color for a given hue+contrast (no clamping/hue-shift). This is exactly the missing 'constant-contrast ramp' primitive: a step-600 generated at Lc 60 reads at the same contrast for red, blue, and yellow, and chroma stays even across hues — fixing the 'L step looks even but APCA contrast drifts across hues' problem that scales.css (fixed OKLCH lightness grid) and palette-generator.js (HSL harmony, WCAG-only) both have. It is the production engine behind Evil Martians' Harmonizer (@harmonizer/core depends on apcach ^0.6.4 + culori). Best as a small vanilla utility (palette-ramp-apca.js: loop Lc targets per hue -> emit --hue-50..950 oklch() tokens) reimplementing the apcach formula over culori, OR a CDN snippet (esm.sh/apcach) behind a need()-guard. Net-new: the vault has WCAG contrast and OKLCH ramps but no contrast-driven generator.
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: MIT, clean. Pair with culori (MIT) for OKLCH<->gamut math. APCA's apca-w3 source has a 'free for non-commercial / contact for commercial' clause historically — reimplementing the published APCA Lc formula (not vendoring apca-w3) sidesteps that; flag if vendoring apca-w3 directly.

backlog.name: Harmonizer (Evil Martians) — constant-contrast tonal-ramp methodology
backlog.what: The reference tool/spec for the whole approach: an OKLCH+APCA palette generator where every 'level' holds a fixed APCA contrast target and chroma is consistent across hues, so red-600 and blue-600 read identically and light/dark themes mirror exactly. Worth adding as a SKILL (.skill.md decision tree): 'when you need cross-hue-consistent token ramps, generate by contrast target not by lightness step; pick APCA Lc per level (Lc 15/30/45/60/75/90), hold chroma, solve lightness per hue.' This codifies the design rationale that scales.css and palette-generator.js lack, and gives the engine a rule for emitting consistent semantic token ramps (--c-danger-600 == --c-info-600 in contrast). It also documents the APCACH calculator (blend of APCA+OKLCH) and the level/contrast-target model used to build dark-mode mirrors.
backlog.license: No LICENSE file declared (license: None via GitHub API) — source-available, all-rights-reserved by default. Do NOT vendor its code; use as documented inspiration/method only. Implement via the MIT apcach engine instead.
backlog.note: Inspiration/method, not a dep (unlicensed repo). The implementable engine is apcach (MIT). color.skill.md already name-drops APCA + Adobe Leonardo conceptually but has NO constant-contrast ramp-generation procedure — this skill fills that gap.

backlog.name: spectral.js (Kubelka-Munk paint-like mix)
backlog.what: MIT, zero-dependency JS library (v3.0.0, Apr 2025, ~1.2k stars, actively maintained). Generates a spectral reflectance curve from any RGB by weighting 7 primary curves (White/Cyan/Magenta/Yellow/Red/Green/Blue), converts to Kubelka-Munk K/S, mixes linearly in K/S, then resolves back through CIE XYZ → OKLab/OKLCh with deltaE gamut mapping. The result is PIGMENT behavior: spectral.mix(['#002185',0.5],['#FCD200',0.5]) === '#3D933E' (blue+yellow=green), where every existing vault path (color-mix, gradient-extract k-means, GradientBuilder linear/mesh) would give additive mud/gray. Worth adding because it is genuinely novel for paint/art/illustration genres and lets gradient stops route AROUND the muddy mid-tone. Color class exposes .OKLCh directly so it drops straight into the vault's OKLCH-based color/gradient skills. API is small: mix(...[color,factor]), palette(a,b,size), gradient(pos,...stops). No CDN/unpkg listed (npm/release download only) and bundle size unpublished, so the clean integration is to vendor/reimplement the ~vanilla core as a zero-dep snippet behind the existing color util, not a need()-guarded CDN.
backlog.license: MIT (© 2025 Ronald van Wijnen)
backlog.note: Vault has NO pigment/Kubelka-Munk path — confirmed: only color-mix, gradient-extract (k-means), GradientBuilder (additive/perceptual RGB). MIT permits vendoring. No official CDN, so reimplement the core as a vanilla .js util (e.g. utils/pigment-mix.js) exposing mix()/palette() and returning OKLCH; pairs with color.skill.md + gradients.skill.md. ~38 spectral samples + 7 primaries are static tables, fully vanilla-reimplementable.

backlog.name: spectral.glsl (GPU Kubelka-Munk mix)
backlog.what: The same project ships spectral.glsl: a spectral_mix() GLSL function doing real-time Kubelka-Munk pigment mixing on the GPU for 2-4 colors with per-color tinting strength (live ShaderToy demo). Distinct from the JS snippet because it targets the vault's shader/mesh-gradient/aurora genres: a mesh or aurora gradient whose color interpolation is PIGMENT-correct (blue→green→yellow with no gray seam) instead of the usual smoothstep/mix() additive blend. Drop-in for the shaders.skill.md fragment pipeline. Worth adding as a separate item because integration form and target genre differ from the JS util — one is a color-math util, this is a fragment-shader primitive.
backlog.license: MIT (same repo)
backlog.note: Vault has many GLSL shaders + mesh/aurora gradients but NONE do pigment-correct interpolation — all use additive mix()/smoothstep. Ship as a paste-in GLSL helper for shaders/*.glsl. MIT, vanilla (no runtime dep).

backlog.name: Mixbox (Scrtwpns)
backlog.what: The other well-known pigment-mixing library, also Kubelka-Munk based, frequently cited as higher-fidelity than spectral.js (real-pigment latent representation). Listed here ONLY to FLAG the license trap: Mixbox is CC BY-NC 4.0 (non-commercial) and requires a paid commercial license, so it is NOT a usable dependency for Design Book. Value is as inspiration/benchmark — use it to sanity-check spectral.js output quality, not to ship. Including it prevents a future researcher from re-proposing it as MIT by mistake.
backlog.license: CC BY-NC 4.0 (non-commercial; paid commercial license) — RESTRICTED, do not ship
backlog.note: FLAGGED restricted-license. Inspiration only — benchmark spectral.js (MIT) against it; never vendor or CDN-load Mixbox.

backlog.name: pal-cloud-dancer (Pantone 2026 COTY — encode as warm-white token set, renamed)
backlog.what: Pantone's 2026 Color of the Year, Cloud Dancer (PANTONE 11-4201, hex #F0EEE9), is a soft warm/airy white — not the cool clinical whites the vault already has (pal-mono-snow #fff-ish, pal-clean-light). Encode as a full .pal-* warm-white token set (bg #F0EEE9 / surface #FBFAF6 / accent a muted greige) so Design Book has the single most-referenced 2026 trend color. The hex VALUE is not copyrightable; only the name 'Cloud Dancer'/'Pantone' is trademarked, so ship the class as pal-airy-white or pal-cloud-white and cite the trend in a CSS comment, not the class name.
backlog.license: Color values uncopyrightable; rename class to avoid Pantone(R) trademark
backlog.note: TRADEMARK: do NOT name the class pal-pantone-* or pal-cloud-dancer in shipped code; use a descriptive slug (pal-airy-white) and reference 'Pantone 2026 COTY' only in a comment. Distinct from existing whites — #F0EEE9 is warm/greige, vault whites are cool.

backlog.name: Pinterest Palette 2026 — five-color trend set (pal-cool-blue, pal-jade-trend, pal-plum-noir, pal-wasabi, pal-persimmon)
backlog.what: Pinterest's 2026 Palette publishes exact hex codes itself: Cool Blue #D7EFFF, Jade #AEB8A0, Plum Noir #351E28, Wasabi #E9F056 (electric chartreuse), Persimmon #FF5C34. This is the most safely-referenceable trend set because Pinterest releases the hexes openly and the descriptive color names (persimmon, jade, wasabi, plum) are generic/non-trademarkable as individual hues. Encode as 2-3 cohesive .pal-* themes — e.g. a Plum-Noir dark luxe theme (bg #351E28, accent Persimmon #FF5C34), a Cool-Blue light theme (bg #D7EFFF, accent Jade), giving Design Book a current consumer-trend look the 62 evergreen palettes lack. Wasabi/Persimmon are loud accents the vault has nothing equivalent to.

backlog.name: pal-transformative-teal (WGSN/Coloro 2026 COTY — encode as deep blue-green token set, renamed)
backlog.what: WGSN + Coloro's 2026 Colour of the Year, Transformative Teal (Coloro 092-37-14, hex approx #306263/#316064), is a deep ocean-blue-meets-forest-green. The vault's pal-wellness-teal and pal-ink-jade are lighter/brighter teals; #306263 is a darker, more sophisticated blue-green with no direct equivalent. Encode as a dark .pal-* theme (bg near #1a2e30, surface #243d3f, accent #316064) plus optionally the WGSN AW26/27 supporting hues (Luminous Yellow, Future Dusk purple, Nutrient Green, Subtle Peach) as accent-2 options. Coloro codes are proprietary but the resulting hex is a plain RGB value and uncopyrightable; 'WGSN'/'Coloro'/'Transformative Teal' are trademarks, so ship as pal-deep-teal and cite in a comment.

backlog.note: TRADEMARK: avoid pal-wgsn-* / pal-coloro-* / pal-transformative-teal in shipped class names. Hex #306263 is an approximation of a Coloro code (no official hex exists), so document it as 'approx'. Distinct from existing teals — darker and bluer.

backlog.name: pal-quiet-joy (Dunn-Edwards 2026 'A Quiet Joy' — 9+8 neutral-forward palette, COTY Viridian Odyssey)
backlog.what: Dunn-Edwards' 2026 trend collection 'A Quiet Joy' is 17 colors: 9 trend hues led by Color-of-the-Century Viridian Odyssey (DE1925, hex approx #2E484F, deep blue-green) + 8 warm neutrals (Milk Glass DEW358 #FAF7F0, Fleur de Sel, Antique White DEW351, Leather Bound, French Press Coffee, etc.). The 8-neutral run is the genuinely net-new part: the vault has industry neutrals (pal-paper, pal-cool-gray) but not a cohesive warm paint-derived neutral ramp. Encode as 1-2 .pal-* sets — a Viridian-Odyssey dark-green theme and a warm-neutral 'quiet' light theme — to give Design Book the interiors/architecture-current neutral story it's missing. Dunn-Edwards DE codes are proprietary; the derived hexes are plain values; rename classes to avoid the 'Dunn-Edwards' trademark.

backlog.name: Perceptually-uniform sequential colormaps (viridis / magma / inferno / plasma / cividis) — vanilla t->color sampler
backlog.what: A zero-dep JS utility that samples a value t in [0,1] to an RGB color along a scientific perceptually-uniform colormap, plus a matching CSS `linear-gradient(...)` legend string. Ship the 5 CC0/public-domain matplotlib+cividis ramps as small anchor-point tables (e.g. viridis #440154->#21918c->#fde725, cividis #00204d->#7c7b78->#ffea46, magma/inferno #000004->...->#fcfdbf/#fcffa4) and lerp in sRGB (or OKLab for smoother) between anchors. Net-new because the vault's `colors/scales.css` is Tailwind CATEGORICAL hue ramps (encode identity, not magnitude) and `data-viz/heatmap-calendar` only tints ONE hue by opacity — there is no continuous value->color mapping anywhere (grep for interpolateColor/viridis/lerpColor returns nothing). This is the missing primitive that powers real heatmaps, choropleths, density/2D-histogram plots, and contour fills. cividis is CVD-optimal and grayscale-safe, so it doubles as the print/accessibility default. Monotonic luminance also means the same ramp survives black-and-white printing — fits the vault's print-safe ethos.
backlog.license: CC0 / public-domain (viridis,magma,inferno,plasma are CC0; cividis is PNNL/DOE MIT-like, effectively public domain)
backlog.note: Values are public-domain; ship as a tiny anchor table reimplemented in vanilla JS (no dep). Add a one-line note in dataviz.skill.md: categorical palette = identity, sequential colormap = magnitude. cividis is the CVD/print-safe default.

backlog.name: Diverging colormaps for zero-centered data — Crameri vik/broc + ColorBrewer RdBu/BrBG
backlog.what: A diverging variant of the sampler: maps t in [0,1] with a fixed neutral midpoint (t=0.5) for data centered on a meaningful zero (correlation matrices, anomaly/delta maps, profit-vs-loss, sentiment). Ship Crameri `vik` (blue<->white<->red, MIT) and `broc` (blue<->white<->brown) as the perceptually-uniform, CVD-safe choices, plus ColorBrewer `RdBu`/`BrBG` (Apache-2.0) as familiar fallbacks. Crucially these AVOID the green-red trap that fails red-green CVD. Net-new: no diverging color scale exists in the vault — `scales.css` has no `diverging`/`sequential` concept and charts-pro has no two-sided ramp. Directly enables correlation heatmaps, choropleth swing maps, and delta dashboards the dataviz genre can't currently color correctly.
backlog.license: MIT (Crameri Scientific Colour Maps v8, 2023); ColorBrewer is Apache-2.0
backlog.note: Pair with the sequential sampler (same lerp engine, different anchor table + symmetric domain). Diverging REQUIRES a known zero — note in skill: use sequential when data has no natural midpoint.

backlog.name: Colorcet / CET perceptually-uniform colormaps (fire, bmy, kbc, isoluminant, cyclic)
backlog.what: Peter Kovesi's CET colormap family: linear (fire, bmy, kbc), isoluminant, and CYCLIC maps (for phase/angle/hue data that wraps, e.g. wind direction, time-of-day, orientation fields) — a category matplotlib's core set lacks. Cyclic maps are genuinely net-new and net-useful for the dataviz/generative-art genres (flow fields, periodic data). However license is CC-BY-4.0 (attribution REQUIRED), unlike the CC0 matplotlib set — so it's a lower-priority add and must carry credit if shipped.
backlog.license: CC-BY-4.0 (attribution required) — FLAG: not as frictionless as CC0/MIT options above
backlog.note: CC-BY attribution requirement makes the matplotlib CC0 set the better default; add Colorcet mainly for its CYCLIC maps (no equivalent in viridis family). Treat as inspiration/optional unless credit line is acceptable.

backlog.name: Gradient Hygiene linter/auto-fixer (chroma-dip + sRGB-banding detector)
backlog.what: A vault VERIFICATION capability that scans composed CSS for two-stop (and multi-stop) gradients lacking an `in oklch`/`in oklab` interpolation hint, computes each segment's midpoint in OKLCH, and flags two failure modes: (1) the 'muddy midtone / dead-zone' chroma dip — midpoint chroma falls measurably below both endpoints (the classic orange->blue-through-gray artifact), and (2) sRGB banding risk — a long, low-contrast gradient with no dither layer. The auto-fixer rewrites the gradient as `linear-gradient(in oklch <hue-arc> hue, ...)` and, for long subtle ramps, injects the vault's existing grain overlay. This is the moat the gradients skill admits is missing: it has a huge toolkit but 'no systematic guard against the muddy-midtone chroma dip or sRGB banding.' Net-new: nothing in INDEX.md lints/detects gradients; `gradient-builder.js`/`gradient-extract.js` only BUILD/EXTRACT, never diagnose. Detection rule is cheap and deterministic (parse gradient -> sample t=0.5 in oklch -> compare C), so it fits the headless-Chrome save-gate cleanly.
backlog.license: N/A (own implementation; concept is public-domain CSS Color 4 + color.js math)
backlog.note: Builds directly on the gradients skill's own stated gap (line 607 only mentions `in oklch` as a tip). Pairs with the existing coherence/overflow gate as a new gradient-quality diagnostic. Vanilla math; color conversions reimplementable in ~40 lines, no dependency.

backlog.name: OKLCH hue-arc selection rule (shorter / longer / increasing / decreasing)
backlog.what: A small decision-tree skill (or skill section) encoding WHEN to use each `<hue-interpolation-method>` keyword. Rule: default `shorter` is right for hue gaps under ~180deg with no dead-zone crossing; switch to `longer` when the short arc passes through desaturated/muddy hues (e.g. blue oklch(63% .3 317) -> purple oklch(63% .2 255) takes a richer 298deg path through reds/magentas); use `increasing`/`decreasing` to FORCE a clockwise/counterclockwise traversal that won't flip when the hue delta crosses 180deg during animation (critical for hue-cycling gradients and conic spins, which the vault has many of via `.ganm-rainbow`/`.ganm-hue`). The vault currently gives ZERO guidance here — the gradients skill mentions `in oklch` once and never the hue path. This makes the auto-fixer above 'know' which arc to emit instead of blindly defaulting to shorter (which can itself re-introduce a gray dead-zone).
backlog.license: N/A (CSS Color 4 spec feature, public)
backlog.note: Net-new decision logic. Directly powers the hygiene fixer's arc choice and de-risks the vault's existing animated/conic gradients (.ganm-rainbow, .ganm-hue, .ganm-conic-spin) which currently rely on implicit shorter-arc behavior.

backlog.name: Centered ±0.5/255 ordered-noise anti-banding dither (interleaved gradient noise)
backlog.what: A precise anti-banding recipe distinct from the vault's decorative grain: add per-pixel noise of amplitude exactly 1 quantization step centered on zero — `+= (1.0/255.0)*noise(x,y) - 0.5/255.0` — so bands dissolve into stipple WITHOUT shifting average brightness or adding a visible 'grainy' texture. This is the ~1-3% dither the question asks for, quantified. Vanilla-reimplementable two ways: (a) a tuned `feTurbulence` + `feComponentTransfer` SVG overlay calibrated to ~1/255 alpha (much subtler than the current `.dither-*` presets, which use baseFrequency 0.9 + heavy color-matrix and read as TEXTURE, not anti-banding); (b) a 1px-tiled blue/IGN noise PNG at ~0.4% opacity via `mix-blend-mode`. The vault's `effects/stepped-gradient.css` `.dither-*` is the OPPOSITE intent (it POSTERIZES into hard bands then grains them for a riso look) — there is no 'invisible' band-killer for smooth premium gradients. This is the dither half of the hygiene fixer's output.
backlog.license: N/A (technique; Jimenez interleaved-gradient-noise is freely published)
backlog.note: Quantified ±0.5/255 amplitude is the key add. Must be explicitly differentiated from existing `.dither-*` and `gradient-noise.css .gnoise` (both are visible-texture overlays); this one is calibrated to be invisible and brightness-neutral. Auto-injected by the hygiene fixer only on long low-contrast ramps where 8-bit banding actually shows.

backlog.name: CVD (color-vision-deficiency) decision branch + verify gate — adapted from meodai skill.color-expert
backlog.what: color.skill.md has exactly ONE throwaway CVD line ('never encode meaning by hue alone') and the verify/coherence gate checks NO color accessibility at all (confirmed: zero 'apca|contrast|colorblind' hits in mcp-server/server.js). Meodai's skill treats CVD as a first-class concern. Add a 'Color-blind safe?' branch to color.skill.md: ~8% of men have deuteranopia/protanopia; test chart/series colors and accent-vs-semantic pairs through a simulator; ensure ok/warn/danger stay distinguishable under sim; pair hue with shape/icon/position. Then wire a CVD pass into the verify gate as a NEW diagnostic — exactly the 'moat' verification form the vault prizes. Reimplementable in vanilla via the Brettel-Vienot-Mollon matrices (used by Culori filterDeficiency*/@bjornlu/colorblind, both MIT, zero-dep), so no runtime dep needed in shipped pages.
backlog.license: Skill: CC BY 4.0 (attribution only — adapt freely). Algorithm/refs: Brettel-Vienot-Mollon (public method); Culori MIT; @bjornlu/colorblind MIT.
backlog.note: Highest-value gap: turns a one-liner into a real branch AND adds a net-new gate diagnostic. The vault has no CVD coverage anywhere (grep confirmed).

backlog.name: APCA-by-construction branch (not just a WCAG rough-map footnote)
backlog.what: color.skill.md only mentions APCA as a rough conversion to WCAG ('Lc 60 ≈ 4.5:1'). Meodai frames APCA as the polarity-aware primary you BUILD palettes around, with the striking scarcity stat: of all hex pairs only 11.98% pass WCAG AA but just 0.08% (~1 in 1,250) pass APCA 90. Add a branch: pick text shade by target Lc (60 fluent body / 75 reading body / 90 preferred), account for text weight+size in the Lc target, and prefer APCA over WCAG-2 specifically for dark-mode where WCAG-2 is over/under-strict (the skill already hints this but gives no construction recipe). Pairs naturally with the OKLCH ramp section already in the skill — fix hue/chroma, step L until Lc target is hit.
backlog.license: CC BY 4.0 (skill text); APCA is open (SAPC-APCA, W3C-track)
backlog.note: Net-new as a decision branch; the current skill mentions APCA but gives no 'design to Lc target' workflow. Cheap, additive.

backlog.name: spectral.js — Kubelka-Munk pigment/paint color mixing
backlog.what: Vault has NO pigment-mixing capability (grep: zero hits for pigment|kubelka|munk|spectral mix across all skills). spectral.js does physically-plausible subtractive paint mixing via Kubelka-Munk: blue+yellow actually makes green (not the muddy gray RGB lerp gives), white shifts hue while lightening, CMY paths stay vivid. Tiny, zero-dep, MIT, works on CDN. Integration: a gradients/generative-art snippet ('paint blend' / spectral interpolation) behind a need()-guard that degrades to a normal OKLAB color-mix() when absent — gives a genuinely different, painterly look the vault's additive/mesh gradients can't produce. Also a 1-line addition to color.skill.md's harmony section ('mixing pigments ≠ mixing light').
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: Net-new visual primitive. Mixbox (the commercial alternative meodai also lists) is NON-FREE/source-available — flag it as inspiration only; ship spectral.js (MIT) instead.

backlog.name: Color-naming systems mini-table (ISCC-NBS / Munsell / XKCD / Japanese traditional / RAL) + color-name-lists
backlog.what: color.skill.md names palettes but has NO color-naming guidance (grep: zero ISCC|munsell|xkcd hits). Meodai covers six naming registers by purpose: ISCC-NBS for scientific precision, Munsell notation, XKCD for common perception, Japanese traditional for poetic/cultural, RAL for industrial reproducibility, CSS-147 web standard. Worth a small reference table in color.skill.md so the agent can NAME a generated swatch in the right register for the context (e.g. poetic Japanese names for a wellness/luxury brand, RAL for product). meodai's own color-name-lists npm (MIT, 18 naming systems, ~30k names) is the lookup source — usable as a build-time palette-naming util, not a runtime page dep.
backlog.license: color-name-lists: MIT; skill text: CC BY 4.0
backlog.note: Lowest of the four but genuinely net-new and tiny; a reference table plus an optional naming util. Skip the historical-pigment-chemistry rabbit hole — meodai's own skill notes it covers theory (Harris/Bezold/Sawyer/Lewis) not named-pigment chemistry, so there's no rich pigment-name set to fold in.

backlog.name: Runtime one-hue → token theme via relative-color syntax (oklch(from var(--accent) …))
backlog.what: A vanilla CSS snippet that derives an ENTIRE token system from a single --accent at runtime using CSS relative color syntax + color-mix(): --accent-hover/active by stepping L (oklch(from var(--accent) calc(l + .06) c h)), --surface/--bg-alt/--border by mixing the accent into the neutral (color-mix(in oklab, var(--accent) 6%, var(--bg))), --accent-2 by hue rotation (calc(h + 30)), and --on-accent via contrast-color(var(--accent)) (or a color-mix(in oklab, white, black) luminance fallback). This is exactly the 'build a palette from one brand hue' workflow color.skill.md describes — but the vault only does it by hand: all 61 palettes in colors/palettes.css are fully hand-authored token blocks (--bg/--surface/--border/--accent/--on-accent literally typed per theme), and color.skill.md's section 103 prescribes a MANUAL OKLCH ramp-stepping process producing static CSS. There is ZERO oklch(from …) usage in the vault (grep-confirmed). Net-new value: paste any brand hex into one --accent var and a coherent, themeable surface/border/hover/text system materializes live — drives runtime theme pickers, white-label/multi-tenant theming, and 'theme from logo color' without re-authoring a palette block.
backlog.license: N/A (native CSS, no dep)
backlog.note: Pair with an @supports (color: oklch(from white l c h)) guard that falls back to the existing static .pal-* tokens for the long tail of old browsers — fits the vault's need()-guard / graceful-degrade convention. Could ALSO ship as a one-paragraph addendum to color.skill.md section 103 ('to do this at runtime instead of by hand, use oklch(from var(--accent) …)').

backlog.name: OKLCH gamut-clamp guard pattern (clamp(0, calc(l + .08), 1) + in oklab) — verification/skill note
backlog.what: The set of gotchas that make runtime derivation actually robust, worth encoding as a skill rule + an optional coherence/verification check: (1) Inside oklch(from …), origin channels resolve to UNITLESS numbers — l is 0–1 (not a percentage; calc(l + 20%) is INVALID, must be calc(l + .08)), c is ~0–0.4, h is 0–360 degrees. (2) calc() output is NOT auto-guarded: lightness can exceed 1 or chroma go negative, producing clipped/garbage colors — wrap every derived channel in clamp() e.g. oklch(from var(--accent) clamp(0,calc(l + .08),1) c h) and clamp chroma to >=0. (3) Pushing chroma up on already-saturated hues silently clips at the sRGB/P3 gamut boundary, so a 'lighten' step can read as a hue/saturation shift on yellows/cyans — prefer color-mix(in oklab, …) for surfaces (perceptually even, no surprise hue rotation) and reserve big chroma bumps for accents only. (4) on-accent: contrast-color() (Baseline Newly Available Apr 2026 — Chrome 147/FF 146/Safari 26) auto-picks black/white text per WCAG luminance, but it's too new for a hard dependency, so gate it behind @supports and fall back to a luminance-threshold color-mix.
backlog.license: N/A (native CSS)
backlog.note: color.skill.md already teaches OKLCH ramp theory but says nothing about the runtime relative-color l-is-a-number-not-percent trap, the clamp() guard, or contrast-color() for --on-accent. A 6-line 'runtime derivation gotchas' subsection (or a coherence-check that flags un-clamped calc() in derived color tokens) is the moat-y, non-duplicative add. contrast-color() itself is too new to depend on — note as guarded-enhancement, not a core dep.

backlog.name: Nippon Traditional Colors (nippon-colors JSON)
backlog.what: MIT-licensed colors.json of ~480 named traditional Japanese colors with kanji + romaji + hex (e.g. 撫子 NADESHIKO #DC9FB4, 蘇芳 SUOH #8E354A, 一斥染 IKKONZOME #F4A7B9). The vault's only cultural color set today is the 6 Chinese-traditional pal-* themes; a Japanese counterpart is the obvious peer extension. Worth adding because it's the direct cultural sibling to the existing Chinese canon and gives editorial palettes for art/museum/luxury/wa-aesthetic genres. Curate ~6 token-mapped pal-* classes (e.g. nadeshiko pink, suoh deep-red, ai indigo, matcha/uguisu green, sumi ink, kincha gold) the same way pal-cinnabar etc. are derived — not a raw 480-swatch dump.
backlog.license: MIT (data); underlying color names are historical/JIS-derived, public domain
backlog.note: Vault has Chinese-traditional (pal-cinnabar/porcelain-blue/ink-jade/silk-ochre/rouge/sky-celadon) but ZERO Japanese set. Distill to ~6 pal-* classes mapped to --bg/--accent token model; don't import all 480.

backlog.name: Werner's Nomenclature of Colours (1814/1821) hex set
backlog.what: The canonical pre-photographic naturalist color reference (used by Darwin on the Beagle). The original 1814/1821 book is fully public domain (Smithsonian/Internet Archive scans are CC0); Nicholas Rougeux's modern reproduction publishes hex for all 110 hues openly (Skimmed Milk #e6e1c9 → Veinous Blood Red #3f3033, plus poetic names like Gallstone Yellow, Arterial Blood Red, Verdigris Green). Gives a uniquely literary/scientific-romantic, muted-historical palette family distinct from anything in the vault — perfect for museum, editorial, naturalist, and heritage-luxury genres. Curate a handful of token-mapped pal-* groups (e.g. a muted blues set, an ochre/earth set, a botanical-green set) rather than 110 swatches.
backlog.license: Original work public domain / CC0 (Smithsonian, Internet Archive). Rougeux's hex list published openly; safe to reference values
backlog.note: Highest-distinctiveness pick: a 200-year-old documented system with named hues, total public-domain provenance. Reimplement as ~4-6 muted pal-* themes, not a literal 110-entry table.

backlog.name: A Dictionary of Colour Combinations (Sanzo Wada)
backlog.what: matt deslauriers's MIT-cleaned port of Sanzo Wada's 1930s Japanese color theory: 159 named colors (hex/RGB/CMYK/LAB) organized into 348 designer-vetted 2-4 color COMBINATIONS (e.g. Burnt Sienna #ae5224). Unlike a flat swatch list, the combinations are ready-made harmony pairs — a natural fit for the vault's token model where each pal-* already needs --accent + --accent-2 harmony. Adds a vintage-modernist, sophisticated-muted palette family beloved in editorial/fashion design and absent from the vault. Curate ~6 pal-* themes seeded from Wada combinations.
backlog.license: MIT (mattdesl data port). NOTE: the CRAN 'sanzo' R package is GPL-3 — use the mattdesl JSON, not the R package
backlog.note: Use mattdesl/MIT version, AVOID GPL-3 jmaasch/cran 'sanzo' R pkg. Combinations map cleanly to --accent/--accent-2 harmony pairs.

backlog.name: Dutch Masters painting palettes (Vermeer / Rembrandt)
backlog.what: MIT R package with 6 palettes sampled directly from 17th-c. Dutch master paintings, each entry named by where it was taken on the canvas with hex (anatomy: red muscle #803B31, pink cheek #B47562, brown cloak #332826; plus milkmaid, pearl_earring, view_of_Delft, little_street, staalmeester). These are deep-chiaroscuro, ultramarine-and-ochre painterly grounds that map beautifully to the vault's dark-surface token sets — distinct from the brighter Chinese/Japanese canons. Strong for gallery, fine-art, and heritage-luxury composes. Reuse the hex values (the R wrapper is incidental); express as ~3-4 dark pal-* themes.
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: Only the hex values are needed (R packaging is irrelevant for the vault). Painterly dark grounds complement, don't overlap, pal-luxe-black-gold/midnight.

backlog.name: P3-with-sRGB-fallback token layer (@media dynamic-range/color-gamut + @supports override pattern)
backlog.what: A vanilla CSS token pattern: ship the existing sRGB hex as the base value, then conditionally override the SAME CSS var inside `@media (dynamic-range: high){ @supports (color: color(display-p3 0 0 0)){ :root{ --accent: color(display-p3 .95 .1 .25) } } }`. On P3 phones/laptops accents gain ~30% chroma; everywhere else the sRGB base is untouched — pure progressive enhancement, no JS, no CDN. This is exactly the missing layer: scales.css and all 61 palettes are confirmed 100% sRGB hex with zero color(display-p3) and zero color-gamut queries. Best applied as a sibling `colors/scales-p3.css` (and per-palette accent overrides) that only re-defines the vivid roles (--accent, --accent-2, neon/brand hues) where the extra gamut actually shows. The Chrome guide also recommends the dynamic-range guard (not just color-gamut) so SDR-but-P3 panels don't over-saturate.
backlog.license: Vanilla CSS technique (docs CC-BY, pattern unencumbered)
backlog.note: Net-new. color.skill.md only *mentions* OKLCH conceptually; no file emits color(display-p3) or @media color-gamut. Pair the override layer with a one-paragraph addendum in color.skill.md so the engine knows to load scales-p3.css alongside scales.css.

backlog.name: culori (toGamut / display-p3 / oklch) as the offline hex→P3 token generator
backlog.what: MIT, ESM-native color library (used by Tailwind v4 and Radix) with full CSS Color 4 support: oklch, color(display-p3 …), and a `toGamut()` that finds the max in-gamut chroma. Use it as a BUILD-TIME / one-shot script (Node, no runtime dep, no CDN) to mechanically transform the existing 61 palettes + 17 hue ramps into a matching `*-p3.css` override layer: parse each hex, push it toward the P3 gamut boundary in OKLCH (preserve hue+lightness, expand chroma), then emit `color(display-p3 r g b)`. This makes the wide-gamut layer reproducible and consistent instead of hand-tuned, and `toGamut` also lets the generator clamp anything that would clip. Currently zero color-conversion libs exist in the vault (no culori/colorjs/chroma), and palette-generator.js outputs hex only — so this fills both the generation gap and keeps the vault's zero-runtime-dep promise (it runs offline at author time, ships plain CSS).
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: Used as an author-time generator, not a shipped dependency — output is plain CSS, so it stays inside the vanilla/zero-dep ethos. Could also upgrade utils/palette-generator.js to optionally emit an oklch/P3 variant block.

backlog.name: Wide-gamut accent palette set (P3 neon / brand-pop named palettes)
backlog.what: A handful of NEW named palettes whose whole identity is colors that are impossible in sRGB — saturated P3 greens, electric cyans, hot magentas, and laser-orange CTAs — each shipped as `.pal-*` with an sRGB-clamped base and a P3 override. Genres like cyberpunk/neon landing pages, music/streaming, gaming, and crypto/brand sites currently flatten to the sRGB ceiling; a 'pal-neon-p3', 'pal-acid-p3', 'pal-laser-p3' trio gives the compose engine accents that visibly pop on modern panels while the verification gate still sees a valid sRGB fallback. Distinct from the token-layer candidate: that one upgrades EXISTING palettes in place; this adds a small set of palettes designed *for* the wide gamut where the difference is the point.
backlog.license: Vanilla (palette values; technique from WebKit blog, CC-BY)
backlog.note: Keep small (3-5). Each must define a real sRGB base so coherence/contrast checks pass on non-P3 capture; the P3 values live behind the @supports guard.

backlog.name: Out-of-gamut / P3-fallback verification check (headless-Chrome gate addition)
backlog.what: A new diagnostic for the save-gate: when a snippet/palette uses color(display-p3 …) or oklch() with high chroma, assert (a) an sRGB fallback declaration exists before it (so non-P3 devices aren't left with an inherited/garbage color), and (b) the P3 value, gamut-mapped back to sRGB, stays within a tolerance of the stated fallback (no jarring hue shift between gamuts). This is the vault's moat play — it's the only candidate that's a *capability* rather than content, and nothing in mcp-server currently inspects color/gamut at all. Can be implemented vanilla (regex for the dual-declaration pattern) plus an optional culori round-trip (sRGB→OKLCH→P3→sRGB ΔE) for the hue-drift check. Guards against the documented failure mode where graceful degradation 'gamut clamps to a color you don't love.'
backlog.license: Concept (Color.js docs MIT); implementable vanilla or with MIT culori
backlog.note: Extends the existing coherence/imagery save-gate rather than adding runtime weight. Lowest-effort version is the dual-declaration lint; the ΔE drift check is the premium tier.

backlog.name: Radix Colors 12-step semantic scale (role model + scale corpus)
backlog.what: Radix Colors is a 30+ scale system where every scale has 12 perceptually-tuned steps mapped to EXPLICIT UI roles: 1-2 app/subtle bg, 3 component bg (normal), 4 (hover), 5 (active/pressed), 6 subtle border, 7 component border, 8 focus-ring/strong border, 9-10 solid bg, 11-12 APCA-guaranteed text (Lc60/Lc90). Each scale ships light, dark, alpha, and dark-alpha variants. This is exactly the granularity the vault LACKS: today every .pal-* has one --border and one --accent with no encoded hover/active/pressed/focus-ring tiers (verified in palettes.css). Worth adding as a SKILL + token-extension that upgrades the 10-token role model to a ~12-role model (add --accent-hover, --accent-active, --border-subtle, --border-strong/--focus-ring), so composed components get correct interactive states for free. The skill already name-drops Radix but doesn't encode its step-to-role map.
backlog.license: MIT (@radix-ui/colors v3.0.0, npm i @radix-ui/colors; hex CSS vars + JS objects, also dark/alpha). Confirmed MIT.
backlog.note: color.skill.md mentions Radix only as a reason to move to OKLCH; the 12-step role-to-state map (hover/active/border/focus) is NOT encoded in the token model. This is the highest-value net-new item: granularity, not just more palettes. License clean (MIT) for verbatim hex use.

backlog.name: USWDS system color tokens (CC0 public-domain corpus)
backlog.what: US Web Design System ships 24 color families (red/orange/gold/yellow/green/mint/cyan/blue/indigo/violet/magenta/gray with cool/standard/warm and 'vivid' variants), each on a 5-90 grade ramp (~240+ tokens), all engineered around a magic-number contrast model. Because it is CC0 / US-government public domain, the hex values can be copied verbatim with zero attribution and zero ToS risk. Net-new value is two-fold: (a) it provides government/civic/healthcare/accessibility-first palette seeds the vault doesn't have (a 'pal-gov-*' / 'pal-civic-*' family), and (b) its cool/warm hue triples (e.g. red-cool vs red-warm, gray-cool vs gray-warm) are a clean public-domain source for the 'tint neutrals toward the brand hue' rule the color skill already teaches but doesn't ship vetted values for. Best added as a handful of vetted .pal-* sets seeded from USWDS grades.
backlog.license: CC0 1.0 Universal / US public domain (core tokens). Note: bundled fonts/icons are OFL/Apache, but the color token VALUES are CC0 — safe to copy.
backlog.note: Vault has Tailwind-vetted ramps (scales.css) but no civic/government family and no warm/cool neutral TRIPLES with vetted hex. CC0 is the cleanest license of all sources — copy verbatim, no attribution needed.

backlog.name: Color Hunt palette data (community corpus) — LICENSE BLOCKER
backlog.what: Color Hunt is a large curated gallery of 4-color community-submitted palettes, attractive as a seed for dozens of mood/aesthetic .pal-* sets. BUT it is a license trap and should NOT be ingested as a data corpus. There is no public CC0/MIT data dump and no official open API — the only 'APIs' are third-party scrapers (github.com/mwtrager/color-api, github.com/SrgGroot/colorhuntapi) that violate the site ToS. The ToS makes use of the site contingent on agreement and reserves termination rights; palettes are user-submitted (so Color Hunt cannot grant you redistribution rights to them anyway). While bare 4-hex tuples likely aren't independently copyrightable, scraping + redistributing the curated DATASET is a ToS/database-rights risk with no upside over CC0/MIT sources.
backlog.license: RESTRICTED / unclear — site ToS, no open data license, user-submitted content, scraper-only access. Treat as inspiration only; do NOT ingest as a corpus.
backlog.note: FLAG: source-available/ToS-restricted with community-uploaded content. Use Radix (MIT) + USWDS (CC0) + existing Tailwind ramps for ANY palette expansion; Color Hunt buys nothing legally clean that those three don't already provide. Tailwind v4 OKLCH defaults (MIT) are already vetted into scales.css, so no separate item needed there.

backlog.name: Palette pairing-matrix verification gate (role-aware N×N WCAG audit)
backlog.what: A build-time verification that, for each of the 61 palettes, enumerates the legitimate fg×bg role pairings the composer can actually emit (--fg/--muted/--faint on --bg/--bg-alt/--surface; --on-accent on --accent/--accent-2; --ok/--warn/--danger/--info as text on surfaces; --border vs --bg minimum-delta) and computes the full WCAG ratio for each, flagging any pairing below its role-specific threshold (body text 4.5:1, large/UI 3:1, non-text/border ~1.5 delta).
