Crucially it runs the audit twice — once for the light variant and once for the dark variant of each palette — so dark-mode polarity flips are caught (the vault's palettes.css already ships paired light/dark blocks with alpha-based --muted, which is exactly where opacity-derived muted text silently fails). This is net-new: the vault has a single runtime contrast-checker UI (components/color-tools-pack.css) and PaletteGenerator.contrast()/bestText() helpers, plus color.skill.md docs, but NO automated palette-level gate — the save-gate (audit.js static + render-smoke.mjs dynamic) verifies render-truth/overflow/coherence/imagery and never audits the token matrix. Turns every palette into a provably-safe role system and extends the verification moat to color. The math reuses the WCAG luminance/contrast formula already present in utils/palette-generator.js (lines 294-311), so it ships as a zero-new-dep node script alongside tools/audit.js. EightShapes Contrast Grid (MIT) is the canonical N×N reference algorithm/UX to mirror.
backlog.license: MIT (EightShapes Contrast Grid, as the reference pattern; the gate itself is vault-original)
backlog.note: Net-new gate. Reuses existing WCAG formula in utils/palette-generator.js (lines 294-311) and the token roles defined in colors/palettes.css (--bg/--surface/--fg/--muted/--on-accent/--accent-2…). Mirror EightShapes' enumeration but make it ROLE-AWARE (only audit pairings the composer emits, per-role threshold) and run it per light/dark variant. Slots next to tools/audit.js with an identical exit-code contract (process.exit(fails?1:0)) so `npm run verify` chains it.

backlog.name: culori (wcagContrast + wcagLuminance, MIT)
backlog.what: A mature, MIT, tree-shakeable color library whose wcagContrast(a,b) and wcagLuminance(c) functions can power the pairing-matrix gate's WCAG half without hand-rolling parsing of the vault's mixed color formats. The vault's palettes use hex, rgba() with alpha (e.g. --muted:rgba(242,243,247,.62)), and the gate must composite alpha-over-bg before measuring — culori handles parse + alpha compositing + relative luminance robustly, which the current bestText/contrast helper in palette-generator.js does NOT (it assumes opaque hex, so it can't correctly score the alpha-based --muted/--faint/--border tokens that are exactly where dark-mode pairings fail). v4.0.2 shipped June 2025; ESM, CDN-loadable via esm.sh/jsdelivr. Worth adding as the gate's optional engine (behind the same need()-guard pattern the vault uses), with a vanilla fallback to the existing formula for opaque pairs.
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: Fills a real correctness gap: alpha compositing for rgba()-based --muted/--faint/--border tokens, which the existing palette-generator.js contrast() (opaque-hex only) scores wrong. Use as the gate's engine; vanilla-reimplementable for the opaque case. Note culori has NO APCA function — pair with the APCA inspiration item below for the polarity-aware half.

backlog.name: APCA (Accessible Perceptual Contrast Algorithm) — polarity-aware contrast for the dark-mode half
backlog.what: APCA is the WCAG 3 draft perceptual contrast model that is polarity-aware (light-text-on-dark vs dark-text-on-light score differently), which is the single most important property for the gate's dark-mode requirement — WCAG 2 ratios are over/under-strict on dark surfaces, a fact color.skill.md already documents (Lc 60≈4.5:1, Lc 75≈7:1, Lc 45≈3:1). Adding an APCA Lc column next to the WCAG ratio in the pairing matrix lets the gate flag dark-mode pairings WCAG 2 would wrongly pass/fail. IMPORTANT LICENSE FLAG: the canonical npm package apca-w3 is NOT permissive — it's a limited-grant license (web/WCAG content only; explicit non-aerospace/medical/etc. carve-outs; trademark restrictions on the names APCA/SAPC; audit rights for paywalled apps). So do NOT vendor apca-w3 as a dependency. Instead reimplement the published APCA-W3 0.1.9 math (~40 lines: sRGB-to-Y, soft-clamp, the power-curve Lc formula) as a vanilla function inside the gate, and label the column 'perceptual Lc' rather than using the trademarked name in user-facing output to stay clear of the naming clause. Treat the package as inspiration, the algorithm as reimplementable.
backlog.license: RESTRICTED — apca-w3 limited-grant (W3/AGWG, web-content-only, trademark + audit clauses); NOT MIT/BSD/Apache
backlog.note: LICENSE FLAG: do not add apca-w3 as a dep. Reimplement the public APCA-W3 formula as a ~40-line vanilla function and avoid the trademarked name in output (label it 'perceptual Lc' / 'polarity-aware'). This is the piece culori lacks and the piece that makes the dark-mode polarity check correct. color.skill.md already cites the Lc↔ratio map, so the docs ground already exists.

backlog.name: color.skill.md decision rule: 'every palette must pass its pairing matrix before it ships'
backlog.what: A small but high-leverage addition to color.skill.md (and design-system.skill.md) codifying the role→threshold contract the gate enforces, so the AI composer treats it as a hard rule rather than guidance: a table mapping each token-role pairing to its required level (--fg/--muted on --bg/--surface = AA body 4.5:1; --faint = AA large 3:1 only, never body; --on-accent on --accent AND --accent-2 = 4.5:1; semantic --ok/--warn/--danger/--info as text = 4.5:1 on both --bg and --surface; --border vs --bg = perceptual delta not WCAG; and 'audit light and dark variants separately'). Today color.skill.md lists thresholds prose-style and says --muted should hit 4.5:1 'don't just lower opacity' — but there's no enumerated role-pairing contract and nothing ties it to the gate. This makes the skill the human-readable spec the verification enforces, closing the loop (skill states the rule → gate proves it → save-gate blocks on failure).
backlog.license: N/A (vault-authored content)
backlog.note: Extends existing color.skill.md (which has WCAG/APCA thresholds at lines 126-131 and the role table at lines 33-39) with the enumerated pairing-matrix contract + the explicit 'audit dark variant separately' rule. Pairs with the verification gate — skill is the spec, gate is the enforcement.

backlog.name: @material/material-color-utilities (quantize → Score → themeFromSourceColor)
backlog.what: Google's Material You color engine, the exact Apache-2.0 pipeline the brief names. QuantizerCelebi (Wu median-cut + WSMeans k-means in CAM16/HCT) reduces an image to N colors; Score.score then RANKS and FILTERS them (points for colorfulness + image coverage, drops near-monochrome) to pick ONE curated seed — the missing step the vault's k-means skips. themeFromSourceColor()/CorePalette then expand that seed into full tonal palettes (tone 0–100) for primary/secondary/tertiary/neutral, which map 1:1 onto the vault's role model (--bg=neutral-tone, --surface, --accent=primary, --on-accent picked at a contrast-safe tone). v0.4.0, published 2026-01-21, ZERO dependencies, ESM (type:module) so it loads from esm.sh/jsdelivr behind a need()-guard. This is the genuine end-to-end bridge from gradient-extract's `{dominant,accent,...}` to a contrast-checked, role-assigned theme that nothing in the vault does.
backlog.license: Apache-2.0
backlog.note: Partial overlap only on the FIRST step (quantization) with utils/gradient-extract.js, which stops at raw RGB k-means with no scoring/tonal/role/contrast output. MCU adds Score-curation + tonal-palette + harmonize — all net-new. Apache-2.0 NOTICE retention required if bundled; cleanest as a need()-guarded CDN dep that degrades to the existing gradient-extract fallback. Ships as one module — can pull just quantize+score+hct without the full Scheme machinery.

backlog.name: Material Blend.harmonize() — shift brand/UI colors toward the extracted seed
backlog.what: A small, separable function from the same Apache-2.0 package (and trivially vanilla-reimplementable from the published algorithm) that rotates one color's HUE a bounded amount toward a source/seed color in HCT, preserving its tone and chroma. This is the 'make these N swatches feel like they belong to ONE palette' operation — exactly what turns extracted-but-clashing image colors (or a fixed semantic set: ok/warn/danger/info) into a HARMONIOUS set anchored to the brand seed. The vault's color.skill.md teaches harmony SCHEMES as prose tables but ships no function that mechanically harmonizes arbitrary colors toward a hue. Pairs with seed extraction to keep semantic colors on-brand without going fully monochrome.
backlog.license: Apache-2.0
backlog.note: Algorithm is ~40 lines (HCT hue-rotation with a degree cap); can be reimplemented as a zero-dep vanilla-snippet citing the Apache-2.0 source, avoiding any bundle/NOTICE burden. color.skill.md only describes harmony schemes conceptually — no harmonize() exists in utils/. Best shipped as the 'role-assignment + harmonize' companion to the MCU seed-extraction snippet.

backlog.name: Okolors (OKLab k-means palette extractor)
backlog.what: A high-quality reference implementation of palette extraction done RIGHT: convert image pixels to OKLab, run k-means with proper perceptual color difference, and emit swatches at chosen Okhsl lightness levels for theming. It demonstrates the chroma-aware, perceptually-clustered approach the brief asks about and is the gold standard to benchmark a vanilla OKLab extractor against. Dual MIT/Apache-2.0, actively maintained (v0.9.0, 2025-11). However it's a Rust CLI/crate with no JS or WASM build — so it's an INSPIRATION reference for reimplementing gradient-extract's clustering in OKLab rather than a runtime dep.
backlog.license: MIT OR Apache-2.0
backlog.note: Rust-only (no JS/WASM) — not loadable in-browser, so flagged as inspiration not dep. Its value is the algorithm blueprint: swap gradient-extract.js's RGB-Euclidean k-means for OKLab-space clustering to stop muddy/averaged centroids. If a JS math base is wanted, do this on top of Culori 4.0.2 (MIT, zero-dep, OKLab/OKLCH + differenceEuclidean + WCAG contrast) or @texel/color 1.1.11 (MIT, zero-dep, minimal/modern) — both confirmed permissive and CDN-safe.

backlog.name: lttb.js (vanilla Largest-Triangle-Three-Buckets downsampler)
backlog.what: A ~40-60 line, zero-dep, O(n) JS pre-pass that reduces a 100k+ point series to ~1 point per chart pixel while preserving the visual contour (peaks, troughs, inflections). Algorithm: keep first+last points fixed, split the remaining N-2 points into M-2 buckets, set point C = average of the NEXT bucket, and pick the bucket candidate that maximizes triangle area |x1(y2-y3)+x2(y3-y1)+x3(y1-y2)|/2 with the previously chosen point A and C. Worth adding because every vault chart (chart-line/chart-area/sparkline/charts-pro) is pure SVG via createElementNS and silently chokes past a few thousand points; LTTB drops in as a pre-pass that feeds the SAME existing SVG path-builder, so it is a non-invasive perf primitive (100k->1k in ~15ms, 1M->1k in ~150ms). The vault has nothing for big-data charts.
backlog.license: MIT-compatible (original is the public Steinarsson thesis reference impl; multiple permissive ports: seanvelasco/lttb, pingec/downsample-lttb)
backlog.note: Reimplement vanilla (do not vendor). data-viz/dataviz.skill.md already STATES the <1k SVG / 1k-100k canvas rule but ships no tool to honor it; this is the missing primitive. Tie sample count to chart pixel width (1 sample/px) like Chart.js/ECharts.

backlog.name: min-max decimation fallback (peak-preserving downsample)
backlog.what: A second, even simpler downsampling mode (~15 lines): per output bucket emit the min AND the max sample (up to ~4 points per pixel). This is the companion to LTTB for NOISY signals (audio, sensor, finance ticks) where a single 1-sample spike must survive — LTTB's triangle-area heuristic can occasionally clip an isolated extreme, whereas min-max guarantees every vertical extreme is drawn. Notably this is Chart.js's DEFAULT decimation algorithm (not lttb), and ECharts added a dedicated 'minmax' sampling mode in 5.5 for exactly this reason. Worth adding as the 'preserve exact peaks/troughs' switch alongside LTTB's 'preserve overall trend' switch, so the vault can pick per data shape.
backlog.license: MIT (Chart.js core) — concept is unencumbered
backlog.note: Ship in the same lttb.js file as a sibling export (e.g. {algorithm:'lttb'|'minmax'}). Matches Chart.js/ECharts API surface so the skill can teach one mental model.

backlog.name: chart-line-canvas.js (canvas render path for the 1k-100k tier)
backlog.what: A single-<canvas> 2D line/area renderer that mirrors the existing ChartLine/ChartArea API but draws into one element instead of thousands of SVG nodes. This is the 'canvas' rung the dataviz skill's own rule already prescribes (1k-100k marks) but the vault never built. Combined with the LTTB pre-pass it lets a 100k+ point series draw in milliseconds: downsample to ~pixel-width points, then stroke one Path2D. Worth adding because it closes the documented SVG->canvas->WebGL ladder (the WebGL rung exists via shaders.skill.md; the canvas rung is the hole). Trade-off to document: loses per-element DOM/a11y, so keep SVG default and switch to canvas above a point threshold.
backlog.license: N/A (original vanilla code; canvas 2D API is platform)
backlog.note: Vault already uses canvas for FX (backgrounds/canvas-fx.js, confetti) but never for a chart DATA path. Renderer should accept the same {data,color,smooth,fill} opts as chart-line.js for drop-in parity, and auto-engage LTTB above ~2k points.

backlog.name: verification gate: 'large series rendered to SVG' perf/slop diagnostic
backlog.what: A new coherence/save-gate diagnostic that flags any composed chart whose data series exceeds N points (e.g. >2000) while it renders to SVG (or renders all points without a downsample pre-pass). This is net-new to the verification moat: today the headless-Chrome gate checks overflow/imagery/coherence but has no concept of a data-scale perf cliff. Production libs encode this exact threshold (Chart.js triggers decimation at 4x canvas width; ECharts samples when 'data is far greater than pixels'), so the gate has a principled cutoff. It turns the dataviz skill's prose rule ('<1k SVG, 1k-100k canvas, >100k WebGL') into an enforced, machine-checked save-gate — diagnostics are the vault's differentiator and nothing currently guards big-data charts.
backlog.license: N/A (internal gate logic)
backlog.note: Detect via DOM: count <path>/<circle>/<polyline> children of a chart container, or inspect series length passed to ChartLine/Area. Suggested fix in the message: route through lttb.js + canvas renderer. Pairs naturally with the two snippet candidates above as one shippable bundle.

backlog.name: chart-interaction.js — vanilla crosshair + shared tooltip + focus-dot decorator (bisector / pointer-inversion pattern)
backlog.what: A standalone, zero-dep overlay decorator that attaches a hover-interaction layer to ANY existing line/area chart in the vault (ChartLine, ChartArea, sparkline). The minimal vanilla pattern: (1) a transparent capture <rect> over the plot area; (2) on pointermove, convert clientX→local px via getBoundingClientRect (the vanilla equivalent of d3's xScale.invert, since the vault's x-scale is linear: i = round((px - padLeft) / plotW * (n-1))); (3) a bisector/nearest-index lookup snaps to the closest data index; (4) render a vertical crosshair line, a focus dot per series at that index, and a single SHARED tooltip listing every series' value at that x. The vault's charts already emit deterministic [x,y,v] points from a linear x-scale + v/maxVal y-scale (confirmed in chart-line.js), so the decorator can re-derive positions from {data,width,height,pad} or read rendered points — no per-chart rewrite. This is exactly the gap the question targets: every vault chart is currently static. One ~120-line file upgrades the whole data-viz folder at once, and it's non-duplicative (cursor-hud.js only snaps to a pixel grid and is data-blind; tooltips.js/.css are static element tooltips with no nearest-point logic).
backlog.license: MIT (reimplemented from public d3-graph-gallery / Tom Hazledine / d3noob patterns; the technique itself is unencumbered)
backlog.note: Net-new. Closest existing: cursor-hud.js (pixel-grid snap, data-blind), tooltips.js (static element tooltips), heatmap-calendar (per-cell tooltip only). None do bisector nearest-point snapping, pointer→data inversion, a SHARED multi-series tooltip, or a focus dot. Design it as ChartInteraction.attach(chartEl, {data, series, accessors}) so it decorates outputs rather than owning rendering. Add a synced-across-charts mode (one pointermove drives crosshairs on stacked charts) for dashboard composes. Respect prefers-reduced-motion for the dot transition.

backlog.name: d3-delaunay (Voronoi hitbox technique for nearest-point hover on scatter / dense multi-series)
backlog.what: For the cases where a linear bisector fails — scatter plots, 2D point clouds, and dense multi-series where the nearest point isn't simply 'nearest x-index' — the robust pattern is Delaunay/Voronoi hitboxes: Delaunay.from(points.map(p=>[x,y])).voronoi([l,t,r,b]) partitions the plot so each invisible cell is the region closest to one datapoint than any other. Render cells as transparent pointer-events:all SVG paths (voronoi.renderCell(i)) over the chart; mouseover a cell = exact nearest-point hit, no overlap, no fixed-radius guessing. delaunay.find(px,py) also gives a pure-JS nearest-point query with no DOM at all, making it a clean companion to candidate A for scatter/bubble charts. ISC-licensed, CDN-safe UMD (cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/d3-delaunay@6), ~tiny (Delaunator-based). Pairs perfectly with the vault's missing scatter interaction and would let chart-interaction.js fall back to Voronoi mode when points aren't monotonic in x.
backlog.license: ISC (permissive, same family as D3 core)
backlog.note: Net-new for hover hitboxes. The vault has chart-network.js (force-directed physics) but no Delaunay/Voronoi hitbox or nearest-point query. Ship behind a need()-guard that degrades to the candidate-A linear bisector when d3-delaunay isn't loaded, so it never hard-fails verification. Best as the 'scatter/dense' branch of the chart-interaction decorator, not a standalone chart.

backlog.name: dataviz.skill.md addendum — 'Make a static chart interactive' decision branch
backlog.what: A short decision-tree section appended to the existing dataviz.skill.md routing 'I want my chart to respond to hover': linear/time x-axis → bisector + pointer-inversion (candidate A); scatter/2D/dense → Voronoi hitboxes via d3-delaunay (candidate B); multiple stacked charts in a dashboard → shared/synced crosshair driven by one pointermove. It codifies the 'decorate, don't rewrite' rule (attach an overlay layer to existing ChartLine/ChartArea SVG rather than swapping in a heavyweight lib) and names the failure mode: fixed-radius circle hitboxes overlap and feel laggy; bisector/Voronoi snap exactly. The skill currently only routes library SELECTION (Plot/Recharts/visx/D3) and has no guidance on adding interaction to the vault's own static charts.
backlog.license: N/A (vault-authored skill content)
backlog.note: Lower standalone value but cheap and it's what makes candidates A/B discoverable by the compose engine. Should cross-link to the new chart-interaction.js snippet and the d3-delaunay guard.

backlog.name: Box-and-whisker chart (chart-box.js)
backlog.what: Canonical 5-number-summary chart: linear-interpolated quantiles (Q1/median/Q3), 1.5xIQR whiskers, and outlier dots. The single most-requested distribution chart in analytics reports and the vault has none. Drops straight into the existing data-viz/chart-*.js SVG pattern (sort -> quantile() -> draw box+whiskers+outliers). Supports grouped/categorical x like chart-bar. ~60 lines of pure vanilla; no library, no animation loop.
backlog.license: Public-domain algorithm (own implementation)
backlog.note: Net-new: grep of data-viz/ + dataviz.skill.md returns zero hits for box/whisker/quantile. Reuses the namespaced-global + SVGNS pattern already in chart-bar.js.

backlog.name: Violin plot via small Gaussian-KDE helper (chart-violin.js + kde.js)
backlog.what: Distribution shape as a mirrored density curve. The reusable part is a ~30-line vanilla Gaussian KDE (sum-of-normal-kernels on a sampled grid, Silverman/Scott bandwidth rule) that returns density points; the chart mirrors them into one SVG path and overlays the box-plot quartile lines. The KDE helper is the keystone dependency that also powers ridgeline, so it earns its place as a shared util. Degrades to a simple density-area if data is tiny.
backlog.license: Public-domain algorithm (own implementation)
backlog.note: Net-new: no KDE/kernel-density/violin anywhere in vault. Bandwidth choice (Silverman h=1.06*sigma*n^-1/5) is the one parameter to expose; document it so users don't over/under-smooth.

backlog.name: Beeswarm chart via one-pass dodge placement (chart-beeswarm.js)
backlog.what: Plots every raw observation at its true x while a greedy one-pass dodge picks the smallest non-overlapping perpendicular offset given a point radius (sort by x, scan placed neighbors, stack). Explicitly preferred over d3-force collision because it preserves ordering and needs no iterative simulation or rAF loop, so it renders deterministically in headless Chrome (matters for the verify save-gate). ~40 lines vanilla. Reimplement the algorithm, do not ship the dep.
backlog.license: Reference dep is BSD-3-Clause but UNMAINTAINED (last release Nov 2017) -> reimplement, treat as inspiration
backlog.note: Net-new: no swarm/beeswarm/dodge in vault. chart-network.js is force-directed graph, NOT a beeswarm. Static one-pass dodge avoids force sim => deterministic render, no preserveDrawingBuffer/animation-settle issues.

backlog.name: Ridgeline / joyplot (chart-ridgeline.js, reuses kde.js)
backlog.what: Vertically stacked KDE density curves with controlled overlap (mountain-range silhouette) for comparing a distribution across many groups in compact vertical space. Pure layout on top of the violin's KDE helper: one density path per group, fixed upward offset per row, shared x-scale. High visual payoff for time-series-by-category or model-comparison report sections; ideal for dashboard/report compose genres.
backlog.license: Public-domain algorithm (own implementation)
backlog.note: Net-new: no ridgeline/joyplot in vault. Adds almost no new code if the violin KDE helper lands first (overlap offset + per-row loop). Bundle all four under one 'distribution charts' addition sharing quantile()+kde()+dodge().

backlog.name: GeoChart — vanilla GeoJSON choropleth + projection engine (geoMercator/geoEqualEarth/geoAlbersUsa, auto-fit) generalizing the Greece bake
backlog.what: A zero-dep renderer that takes any GeoJSON FeatureCollection + a {id: value} map and emits an interactive SVG choropleth, exactly like the existing GreeceMap.js but with a pluggable projection instead of a hardcoded equirectangular bake. The projection math is tiny and fully vanilla: geoMercator is x=λ, y=ln(tan(π/4+φ/2)); geoEqualEarth is a ~10-line polynomial (coeffs A0=1.340264, A2=-0.081106, A4=0.000893, A6=0.003796); geoAlbersUsa is a two-cone composite. Add a 3-line auto-fit (project all rings, take bbox, scale+translate into the viewBox) — which is precisely the generalization of GREECE_MAP_PROJECTION. A geometry->SVG-path stringifier walks Polygon/MultiPolygon rings. Reuses GreeceMap's existing color-scale interpolation, legend, tooltip, hover/click, and pin (lat/lng auto-project) code wholesale. This turns the one-country Greece map into a world/US/any-region engine while keeping the exact same API surface and verification-friendly pure-SVG output. Net-new geographic capability the vault completely lacks (data-viz/ has bar/line/area/pie/radar/sankey/treemap/funnel/gauge/network — but no map).
backlog.license: original code; math is public-domain (Snyder, Šavrič et al.)

backlog.name: world-atlas (Natural Earth countries-110m TopoJSON)
backlog.what: The canonical small, CDN-hosted boundary dataset for a world choropleth: cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/world-atlas@2/countries-110m.json — Natural Earth Admin-0 at 1:110m, quantized, ~100KB, spherical decimal-degree coords (so it pairs with a real projection like geoEqualEarth/geoMercator). Public domain (Natural Earth). This is the data half of a world choropleth; combined with the vanilla projection engine above it lets a dashboard drop in a world map with zero mapping library. Ships as TopoJSON (delta-encoded arcs), so it needs a ~40-line topojson.feature() decoder — small enough to inline as vanilla, or guard behind a CDN need(). Fetched at runtime, degrades gracefully (show a stat table if offline).
backlog.license: Public Domain (Natural Earth data); world-atlas package ISC

backlog.name: us-atlas (Census states/counties, pre-projected Albers -albers-10m)
backlog.what: The US half: cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/us-atlas@3/states-albers-10m.json (and counties-albers, nation-albers). Critically, the -albers files are ALREADY projected with geoAlbersUsa into a 975x610 viewport — meaning the GeoJSON ring coordinates ARE the SVG path coordinates, needing ZERO projection math. This is the exact same trick as the vault's Greece bake (precomputed SVG paths + viewBox), so a US-states choropleth can ship as pure path strings + a viewBox, identical in shape to greece-map-data.js. Pairs perfectly with a 'bake-your-own-region' skill: pick a region, run it through a projection once, freeze the paths. ISC license, Census data is public domain.
backlog.license: ISC (package); US Census data public domain

backlog.name: bake-a-map.skill.md — decision tree mirroring the Greece pipeline for any region
backlog.what: A .skill.md that codifies the existing (undocumented) Greece bake into a repeatable recipe: (1) get GeoJSON/TopoJSON (world-atlas / us-atlas / geoBoundaries by ISO code), (2) pick projection by region (geoMercator for web-familiar, geoEqualEarth for whole-world equal-area, geoAlbersUsa for US, equirectangular bake for a small country like the Greece case), (3) project + compute auto-fit bbox -> viewBox, (4) freeze to path strings + an id->name table, mirroring greece-map-data.js (globals like REGION_PATHS, MAP_VIEWBOX, MAP_PROJECTION), (5) feed to the generic GeoChart renderer. Encodes the projection-choice tradeoffs, the quantize/simplify step for file size, and the 'when to bake vs project-at-runtime' rule. Turns a one-off artifact into a capability: any country/US-states/world map drops into a dashboard. Directly addresses the WHY (no generic engine; only a hardcoded Greece map).
backlog.license: original

backlog.name: MapLibre GL JS (v5) — guarded interactive slippy-map snippet with static degrade
backlog.what: BSD-3 GPU vector-tile map engine (OSS fork of mapbox-gl-js pre-license-change). The only credible way to give Design Book a REAL zoomable/pannable basemap with heat/point overlays — a vanilla reimplementation is infeasible. Ship as a need()-guard cdn-snippet (unpkg maplibre-gl.js + .css, lazy-injected like media/rive-player.js) pointed at a free no-key tile source (OpenFreeMap 'liberty' full-planet OSM vector, or MapLibre demotiles which are serverless GitHub Pages). Degrade path is clean and verifiable: on no-WebGL / blocked-CDN / blocked-tiles, the onerror handler swaps in a static fallback (the vault's existing backgrounds/world-map SVG or a baked still image) so the save-gate still renders without overflow. Net-new: vault has only static SVG maps (greece-map, world-map, maps-pack chrome) — nothing tiled/zoomable.
backlog.license: BSD-3-Clause (permissive). Tile data: OpenFreeMap/OSM = ODbL, needs visible attribution.
backlog.note: MapLibre engine is fully permissive and CDN-safe. The CAVEAT is the tiles, not the lib: bundle a free no-key source (OpenFreeMap liberty / MapLibre demotiles / raster OSM) and render the ODbL attribution control. Static degrade should reuse backgrounds/world-map.* so the imagery floor + no-overflow gate pass when WebGL/tiles are unavailable.

backlog.name: deck.gl (v9) standalone scripting bundle — GPU geo point-density overlays
backlog.what: MIT WebGL data-viz layer engine with a no-build standalone bundle (unpkg deck.gl@latest/dist.min.js, global `deck.*`). Adds the >100k-points / GPU lane the vault lacks: HeatmapLayer (GPU density) and HexagonLayer (binned aggregation) over a geographic basemap — exactly the 'point-density overlay' the brief asks for. Designed to compose ON TOP of MapLibre (shares the camera) so it pairs with the MapLibre snippet, or runs standalone with its own OrbitView. The vault's dataviz.skill.md name-drops deck.gl in a comparison table but ships no integration — this turns the mention into an actual guarded snippet. Same need()-guard + static-degrade contract: no-WebGL → fall back to the vault's SVG choropleth / charts-pro heatmap grid.
backlog.license: MIT (fully permissive, CDN-safe).
backlog.note: Permissive + CDN standalone confirmed. Pair with the MapLibre snippet for basemap+overlay; standalone-only mode avoids the tile/attribution dependency entirely. Degrade to charts-pro heatmap or greece-map choropleth when WebGL is absent.

backlog.name: globe.gl (vasturiano) — 3D point-density globe with KDE heatmap
backlog.what: MIT three.js wrapper for a spherical-projection data globe; UMD CDN (cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/globe.gl), `new Globe(el)`, no build. Distinct value vs flat MapLibre/deck.gl: a hero-grade interactive 3D globe with a built-in Gaussian-KDE heatmap layer (color/altitude by great-arc point density) plus points/arcs — the 'wow' geographic archetype for landing pages and decks, which the vault's flat dotted world-map only fakes. Fits the cdn-snippet lane behind a need()-guard; reduced-motion / no-WebGL degrades to a static globe image or the existing world-map SVG.
backlog.license: MIT (permissive).
backlog.note: CAVEAT for the guard: the globe.gl UMD CDN bundle SHIPS ITS OWN three.js, and the vault already loads three.js (3d-web.skill.md). Guard must detect/avoid a double three.js load (use globe.gl's three-globe + the vault's existing three.js, or accept the bundled copy and ensure only one runs) to prevent two THREE instances.

backlog.name: Chord diagram (vanilla SVG, circular relationship matrix)
backlog.what: A circular flow diagram for SYMMETRIC n×n relationship matrices (migration between regions, trade flows, co-occurrence, cross-tabs) — the canonical viz the vault is missing. Layout is fully vanilla-doable with arc + bezier math: (1) sum each row of the matrix; (2) map each node to a proportional arc segment around a circle (cumulative-angle sweep, like a donut chart — angle = 2π·rowSum/total); (3) sub-allocate each segment into sub-arcs, one per ribbon endpoint; (4) draw each ribbon as an SVG path = arc along source sub-arc, then a quadratic/cubic bezier whose control point is the circle CENTER (0,0), to the target sub-arc, arc back, bezier back to start, Z. Center control point is what gives the signature 'pinched through the middle' ribbon. ~120-160 lines, zero deps, same `Chord.create('#el', {matrix, labels})` shape as chart-sankey.js. The D3 d3-chord page is the reference for the math/terminology, NOT a dependency. Fills a distinct slot: Sankey = directed multi-stage flow, network = organic node-link, chord = symmetric matrix where every node relates to every node.
backlog.license: ISC (d3-chord reference only; reimplementation is original/unencumbered)
backlog.note: Distinct from data-viz/chart-sankey.js (directed stage flow) and chart-network.js (force-directed). No chord anywhere in INDEX.md, dataviz.skill.md, diagrams.skill.md, or charts-pro.js. Drop as data-viz/chart-chord.js + one row in dataviz.skill.md.

backlog.name: Arc diagram + hierarchical edge bundling (vanilla SVG, 1D node axis)
backlog.what: The linear-axis cousin of the chord diagram: nodes laid out along a straight line (or around a circle), edges drawn as arcs above the axis. Arc form: each edge between node i and j is a single quadratic/semicircular bezier whose height ∝ |i−j| (control point at midpoint x, raised y). Edge-bundling form: route edges toward a shared center/parent so related links visually merge into 'cables' — for the circular variant, bend each edge's control points toward the circle center (a center-pull bezier), which is the same center-bezier trick as the chord ribbon, so the two snippets share a math core. Great for showing relationships along an ORDERED axis (genome positions, timeline co-mentions, dependency graphs, character co-occurrence in a book) without Sankey's stage constraint or the network sim's nondeterminism (it's deterministic — good for the verify save-gate). ~90-130 lines vanilla SVG.
backlog.license: permissive (technique; reference pages are CC/educational, reimplementation original)
backlog.note: Net-new; no arc-diagram or edge-bundling in vault. Pairs naturally with the chord snippet (shares the center-pull bezier helper). Deterministic layout = passes headless verify without sim settling, unlike chart-network.js.

backlog.name: Circos / Krzywinski circular-genomics aesthetic (inspiration for chord styling)
backlog.what: The visual language that makes chord diagrams read as 'designed' rather than 'default': graduated outer tick-ring, segment labels rotated tangent to the circle, ribbon opacity/gradient encoding direction, and a thin gap (pad-angle) between segments so arcs don't kiss. Worth distilling into the chord snippet's defaults (palette-driven segment colors from the vault's existing palettes, ~0.02rad pad, hover-to-highlight one node's ribbons by dimming the rest) so the composed output clears the anti-slop coherence score instead of looking like a raw d3 example. Circos itself is Perl/SaaS-adjacent and NOT a dependency — purely a styling/decision reference for the vanilla snippet's defaults and an optional .skill.md decision note (chord vs sankey vs network vs arc).
backlog.license: GPL (Circos tool itself — inspiration only, do NOT vendor)
backlog.note: Inspiration, not a dep — Circos is GPL/Perl. Use to set the vanilla chord snippet's defaults (pad-angle, tangent labels, hover-dim) and to add a 'which relationship viz' row to dataviz.skill.md: chord=symmetric matrix, arc=ordered axis, sankey=directed stages, network=organic.

backlog.name: brush-zoom-overview (focus+context range selector decorator)
backlog.what: The canonical 'overview+detail' / 'focus+context' interaction reimplemented as a zero-dep vanilla decorator that wraps the vault's existing SVG chart-line.js / chart-area.js. Renders a short context strip beneath the main chart, lets the user drag a selection band on that strip to set the main chart's visible x-domain, with a draggable band (pan) and two edge handles (resize/zoom), double-click-to-reset, and a 350ms idle-timeout debounce. Algorithm is small and fully vanilla: pixel selection -> invert to data domain (linear map) -> reslice the series -> redraw the focus chart's path/axis. Worth adding because the vault's charts are pure static renderers with NO interaction layer at all, and zoom-to-a-time-range is the single most-requested dashboard gesture; this is the Stripe/Datadog-navigator look built on what the vault already ships.
backlog.license: Technique (reference impl ISC via d3-brush / Observable); vault impl is original vanilla, no dep
backlog.note: Reimplement, do not ship d3-brush. Decorator API e.g. ChartBrush.attach(chartEl, {data, onRange}) so it composes over existing chart-line/chart-area without replacing them. Pairs with the LTTB downsample angle (reslice on zoom) and the crosshair angle below.

backlog.name: chart-crosshair + nearest-point tooltip (companion hover layer)
backlog.what: A small vanilla hover layer for line/area time-series: on mousemove over the plot, do a nearest-index lookup (bisect on the x array) and draw a vertical guideline + a dot snapped to the series point + a follower tooltip showing the x label and per-series values. The 'snap to datapoint' / 'nearest' interaction mode is the de-facto standard (Chart.js, Highcharts, ZingChart all ship it). It is the natural partner to the brush-zoom decorator and is also completely absent from the vault's static charts. Tiny (~40-60 lines), zero-dep, works over the existing SVG charts.
backlog.license: Technique (Chart.js crosshair plugin reference is MIT); vault impl original vanilla
backlog.note: Keep separate from the brush snippet so each can be attached independently. Shared 'nearest index' helper can back both. No crosshair/tooltip exists on any vault chart today (chart-line.js/chart-area.js/charts-pro.js are render-only).

backlog.name: uPlot
backlog.what: MIT, ~50KB, Canvas-2D time-series chart that is the fastest non-WebGL plotter and ships exactly this overview+detail story: built-in drag-select zoom, wheel-zoom and brush via its plugin/hooks API, and a documented focus/select model that scales to millions of points. Relevant as an OPTIONAL escape hatch (CDN behind a need()-guard) for datasets far larger than the vault's hand-rolled SVG charts can handle, where redrawing an SVG path on every brush would jank. Not a primary add because it is a full chart engine that REPLACES the vault's charts rather than decorating them — so it duplicates chart-line.js conceptually.
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: Treat as inspiration / optional CDN fallback for huge series only. Borrow its brush+wheel-zoom hook model for the vanilla decorator; do NOT make it the default chart since it overlaps the vault's existing SVG chart suite and shifts rendering to Canvas.

backlog.name: Highcharts Stock Navigator (range-selector strip pattern)
backlog.what: The reference UX for the 'navigator strip': a miniature full-range series below the main chart with a translucent selection mask, two grab handles, a draggable window, optional scrollbar, and preset range buttons (1D/1W/1M/YTD/ALL). This is the exact Stripe/Datadog/financial-dashboard look the vault is missing and is the visual spec to copy for the vanilla decorator's appearance (mask opacity, handle affordance, preset-range pill row). Closed/commercial, so it is INSPIRATION only — never a dependency.
backlog.license: Commercial / proprietary (Highcharts Stock requires a paid license)
backlog.note: FLAG: proprietary, do not vendor or CDN-load. Use purely as the visual/interaction spec (navigator mask + handles + preset-range buttons) for the original vanilla brush-zoom snippet. The preset-range pill row is a cheap, high-value addition to bundle with the brush decorator.

backlog.name: ChartAnnotate — declarative annotation overlay (vanilla SVG)
backlog.what: A single zero-dep helper that draws a declarative annotation layer ON TOP of the vault's existing SVG charts (chart-line/bar/area), reusing their exact pad/plotW/plotH coordinate math and maxVal scale. Four annotation primitives, each placed in DATA coordinates (not pixels) — the convergent model across Recharts (ReferenceLine/Area/Dot), ECharts (markLine/markArea/markPoint) and Chart.js annotation plugin. Shape: annotations:[ {type:'line', y:0.99, label:'SLA', dash:true}, {type:'line', x:'2026-01', label:'launch'}, {type:'band', y1:0.95, y2:1.0, label:'goal'}, {type:'marker', x:'2026-03', label:'deploy v2'} ]. type 'line' = horizontal threshold (y=) or vertical reference (x=); 'band' = target/goal area between two y or two x values; 'marker' = named event dot+label at an x (deploy/release). It resolves data-x labels to plot pixels the same way the charts already do (i / (len-1) * plotW + pad.left) and y via (v/maxVal). This is the single highest-leverage dataviz add: every real report annotates charts (SLA line, goal band, 'launch' marker) and the vault currently cannot. Composable across bar/line/area because all three share one coordinate convention. Net-new, vanilla, ~120 lines.
backlog.license: MIT (pattern derived from Recharts MIT / Chart.js-annotation MIT / ECharts Apache-2.0 — all permissive; reimplemented, not vendored)
backlog.note: Reimplement, do not vendor. The vault's chart-line.js/chart-bar.js/chart-area.js already expose svg + el and a known coordinate system (pad/plotW/plotH, maxVal=niceMax) — pass {annotations:[...]} into create() or a sibling ChartAnnotate.apply(chart, annotations) that reads chart.svg. Honor the same label-anchoring and dark-stroke conventions already used (rgba(255,255,255,...) strokes). Add a one-line entry to dataviz.skill.md ('annotate any cartesian chart with threshold/reference lines, target bands, event markers').

backlog.name: Trend / forecast confidence band (high-low fill series)
backlog.what: A 'band' rendering mode for chart-line/chart-area where each datum can carry a [low, high] pair (or a separate forecast series with lo/hi) drawn as a translucent filled area behind the main line — the standard way analytics tools show forecast uncertainty / confidence intervals (uPlot 'High/Low bands', seaborn forecast-vs-actual bands, Grafana). Data model extension: {label, value, band:[lo,hi]} or a sibling series {label, forecast, lo, hi}. The renderer builds one SVG path along the hi points then back along the lo points (the area-fill technique chart-line.js ALREADY does for its baseline fill, just bounded by two value arrays instead of value→baseline). Pairs naturally with a dashed continuation of the line past 'now' to signal forecast vs actual. Turns a flat line into a report-grade 'projection with uncertainty' visual — currently impossible in the vault.
backlog.license: MIT (uPlot is MIT; technique only — reimplemented in vanilla SVG)
backlog.note: Small extension to chart-line.js/chart-area.js, not a new file necessarily. Reuse the existing area-path builder (it already closes a fill region) but compute the lower boundary from lo[] instead of the plot baseline. Add a 'forecast cutoff' x so the band + dashed line only render after the actual data ends. Keep colors derived from the series color at low opacity to match the existing fill convention (rgba(...,0.18)).

backlog.name: Annotated-report chart preset / 'dashboard with callouts' compose recipe
backlog.what: A thin taste/compose recipe (not new rendering code) that wires the annotation overlay + band into the existing dashboard.html structure reference and stat-with-sparkline tiles, so the compose engine can emit a 'report-ready' analytics page: a line chart carrying an SLA threshold line, a goal band, and 1-2 event markers ('launch', 'deploy'), plus a legend row listing the annotations (the Grafana/Amplitude convention where annotations appear in the legend). This is the coherence signal the brief calls out — it makes composed dashboards read as real reports rather than empty chart shells. Encodes sensible defaults: threshold lines dashed + labeled at right edge, event markers as vertical hairline + pill label at top, bands behind the data at low opacity.
backlog.license: N/A (internal recipe; patterns from Grafana AGPL docs used only as inspiration, no code)
backlog.note: Grafana itself is AGPL-3.0 (restricted) — use ONLY as inspiration for the legend/annotation UX convention, vendor nothing. Builds on existing structure/dashboard.html + data-viz/stat-with-sparkline.css. Depends on candidates 1 and 2 landing first. Add as a short decision branch in dataviz.skill.md: 'analytics report → annotate the hero chart (threshold + 1 band + named markers), list annotations in a legend row.'

backlog.name: chart-a11y verification gate (accessible-name / data-table-fallback / non-color-encoding linter)
backlog.what: A headless-Chrome diagnostic that runs over any composed page containing a chart and asserts the WCAG 2.x data-viz invariants as pass/fail, mirroring the existing overflow/imagery/coherence gate: (1) every <svg>/<canvas> chart root has role="img" + a non-empty accessible name (aria-label or aria-labelledby->\<title\>); (2) a structured <table> data fallback exists in the same <figure> (visible or in a <details> disclosure); (3) series are not distinguished by color alone (require a 2nd channel: <pattern>/dasharray/marker-shape/direct text label); (4) interactive data points are keyboard-reachable (tabindex=0 + key handler) OR an equivalent table exists; (5) any chart entrance animation is wrapped by @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce). I verified the vault's chart generators (chart-bar.js, chart-line.js, chart-pie.js, +12 more) emit bare SVG with NONE of role/aria-label/table/pattern/tabindex, and the verify.yml + coherence() gate has zero a11y dimension. This is the moat play: a diagnostic capability, not another chart.
backlog.license: N/A (own implementation; criteria are public WCAG/W3C guidance)
backlog.note: Strongest fit for Design Book's save-gate philosophy. coherence() in designbook/server.js and .github/workflows/verify.yml currently check overflow/imagery/authenticity/coherence only — no a11y. Reuse the same headless-Chrome harness; emit a chartA11y subscore + per-chart failures. Pairs 1:1 with the helper below (gate flags, helper fixes).

backlog.name: chart-a11y.js — vanilla zero-dep accessibility helper for SVG/Canvas charts
backlog.what: A tiny (~2-3kb) zero-dependency helper, ChartA11y.enhance(chartEl, {title, summary, rows, columns}), that retrofits the vault's existing chart-*.js output into compliance in one call: sets role="img" + aria-labelledby to an injected <title>/<desc>, generates a visually-hidden-or-disclosure <table> from the same data array the chart already consumes (caption + scoped <th>), adds tabindex=0 + arrow-key roving focus to data-point <g> nodes with per-point aria-labels, and a non-color encoding pass (applies SVG <pattern>/stroke-dasharray per series). Because every vault chart (chart-bar/line/pie/area/radar/...) is fed a {label,value} array, the helper can derive the table for free — no new authoring burden. Directly satisfies the gate above so composed pages pass.
backlog.license: N/A (own implementation; aligns with Chart.js a11y docs, MIT)
backlog.note: Net-new: data-viz/ has 15 chart generators + table.js but NONE emit role/aria-label/table-fallback/focusable points. charts-pro.css already has a reduced-motion block (added by an 'a11y-codemod'), so the helper should focus on name+table+focus+pattern and leave motion to CSS. Lives as data-viz/chart-a11y.js, callable after any ChartBar.render().

backlog.name: Highcharts Sonification module / Sonification Studio (data-to-audio audio charts)
backlog.what: The reference implementation for 'audio charts' — playing a data series as pitch-mapped tones (Tracks/instruments) so non-sighted users can hear trends, peaks, and outliers; the open Sonification Studio (Highsoft x Georgia Tech Sonification Lab) is the canonical UX. Worth capturing as INSPIRATION for a minimal Web-Audio 'play this chart' button: map the chart's value array to OscillatorNode frequency over time, x-axis -> time, y -> pitch, ~30-40 lines, zero-dep. This is the one a11y channel the vault completely lacks and that the WCAG search flagged as the emerging 2025 differentiator.
backlog.license: Highcharts is proprietary/non-commercial-free (NOT permissive) — DO NOT bundle; reimplement the technique in vanilla Web Audio
backlog.note: FLAG license: Highcharts is commercial/source-available, not MIT — use as inspiration only. Vault has audio-ui.skill.md + local-audio.skill.md (audio-REACTIVE visuals) but NOT data sonification (audio-as-output-of-data) — confirmed non-duplicative. A vanilla 'sonify(values)' could fold into chart-a11y.js as an optional play button. The BSD-3 'sonifier' lib (github.com/athersharif/sonifier) is permissive but stale (last release 2023), so prefer a fresh ~40-line vanilla impl over the dep.

backlog.name: StreamChart — vanilla rolling-window streaming line/area (ring buffer + RAF, FLIP-style time scroll)
backlog.what: A zero-dep canvas snippet that ingests points over time: fixed-size typed-array RING BUFFER (Float32Array head/tail, no array.shift() GC churn), a ROLLING TIME WINDOW that maps wall-clock now()-windowMs onto x, and a single requestAnimationFrame loop that scrolls the series left by translating sample x-positions against a moving time origin (Smoothie's strip-chart technique) rather than re-laying-out DOM. API shape: StreamChart.create(canvas,{windowMs,maxY|auto,series}) returns .push(seriesId,value,ts?) / .pushFrom(EventSource|WebSocket) / .pause()/.resume(). Decouples ingest rate from frame rate (buffer fills on data events, draw runs at 60fps), DPR-aware, and supports the 'monitoring dashboard' look (grid, min/max/last readout, threshold band, glow on newest point). This is the exact missing piece: the vault's dw-live tile is just a pulse dot + count-up; chart-line.js/sparkline.js are static one-shot SVG renders. Smoothie (MIT) is the canonical reference implementation to reimplement vanilla — timeless ~30KB technique, dormant but no reason to ship the dep when the pattern is small.
backlog.license: MIT (reference: Smoothie Charts) — reimplement vanilla, no dep
backlog.note: Net-new. Closest vault items are static: data-viz/chart-line.js + sparkline.js (one-shot SVG, no append-over-time), components/dashboard-widgets.js dw-live (CSS pulse + count-up only). dataviz.skill.md has zero streaming/rolling-window/ring-buffer content. Belongs in data-viz/ as e.g. stream-chart.js + a short 'Streaming / realtime' section appended to dataviz.skill.md.

backlog.name: webgl-plot (danchitnis)
backlog.what: Native-WebGL high-performance 2D line plotter purpose-built for realtime waveforms/oscilloscope/ECG/telemetry — tens of thousands of points at 60fps where Canvas2D chokes. Streaming model is line.setY(i,value) into a fixed-length WebglLine (arrangeX() spaces x in [-1,1]) then wglp.update() per RAF frame, i.e. a GPU-resident ring buffer — ideal for high-frequency signal scopes. MIT, zero deps, UMD + ESM CDN builds (jsDelivr/Skypack), v0.7.2 Dec 2024. Best as the need()-guarded escape hatch BEHIND the vanilla StreamChart: vanilla Canvas2D handles dashboard-rate streams (a few series, ~1-10Hz); webgl-plot kicks in for many-channel / kHz waveform demos. Complements the vault's existing WebGL-at-scale story (shaders.skill.md / regl) without duplicating it — none of those do streaming line plots.
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: Not duplicative: dataviz.skill.md lists chart libs (Chart.js/ECharts/D3/Plot) but none for realtime waveform/scope streaming, and shaders.skill.md covers generic WebGL not line-plotting. CDN-guard + graceful degrade to the vanilla StreamChart when WebGL unavailable.

backlog.name: Monitoring / observability dashboard — live-data compose genre
backlog.what: A new composable page archetype: the 'ops / monitoring dashboard' (Grafana/Datadog/status-page flavor) that wires the streaming chart into a real layout — header KPI row (count-up + delta), a primary rolling-window line/area panel ingesting on an interval, a multi-sparkline rail, a threshold/alert band, and a live event log. The engine seeds it with a deterministic synthetic data generator (sine+noise / random-walk / Poisson bursts) so the VERIFY pass screenshots a chart mid-stream that actually renders moving data — turning the static structure/dashboard.html into a living one. High demo value for slide-deck 'live data' moments and ops UIs. Distinct from the existing static dashboard structure page because it owns the time axis and the ingest loop.
backlog.license: n/a (composition of vault snippets)
backlog.note: structure/dashboard.html exists but is fully static (dw-live = pulse dot + countUp). This genre depends on the StreamChart vanilla-snippet above; ship that first, then this as the archetype that composes it with dashboard-widgets.css. Needs a seeded deterministic data source so coherence/imagery-floor verify can screenshot a populated chart.

backlog.name: Smoothie Charts strip-chart scroll technique (FLIP-on-time / blit-shift)
backlog.what: The specific anti-jank mechanic worth capturing as documented technique inside the skill: instead of re-binning/re-laying-out every frame, treat 'now' as a moving origin and translate the whole series left by (now - lastRender)*pxPerMs each frame — a FLIP/transform-on-the-time-axis so points slide smoothly between data ticks (sub-pixel interpolation) at 60fps even when data arrives at 1-4Hz. Pairs with the ring buffer (drop samples older than windowMs) and an optional Canvas2D micro-optimization: redraw only the newest trailing segment over a blitted shift of the prior frame (drawImage(canvas, -dx, 0)) for very long windows. This is the 'why it doesn't jank' explanation that makes the vanilla snippet correct rather than a naive shift()+full-clear redraw.
backlog.license: MIT (Smoothie) — technique, not a shipped dep
backlog.note: Folds into the dataviz.skill.md 'Streaming' section as the decision note (interval/RAF decoupling, sub-pixel time scroll, ring buffer to bound memory, blit-shift for long windows). Not a separate file — guidance that backs candidate #1.

backlog.name: viridis / magma / inferno / plasma / cividis (matplotlib perceptually-uniform colormaps)
backlog.what: The five CAM02-UCS perceptually-uniform sequential colormaps (van der Walt & Smith; cividis by Nuñez et al). Each is a 256-stop ramp engineered for constant lightness gradient and colorblind-safety (cividis is optimized specifically for deuteranopia). Ship them as a named 'data-scale' palette set: each ramp stored as ~9-16 anchor hex stops, exposed as both CSS custom-property gradient stops and a JS hex array. This is the canonical sequential-ramp answer and is the single biggest missing piece — the vault's scales.css ramps are per-hue Tailwind UI ramps (even within a hue but not a colorblind-safe cross-hue data encoding), and the heatmap-calendar/treemap snippets currently encode magnitude with a single hue or random categorical fills. Adding viridis-family ramps directly upgrades choropleth/heatmap/treemap magnitude encoding.
backlog.license: CC0 (public domain) — RGB/hex values are freely reusable, no attribution required
backlog.note: Vault gap confirmed: no viridis/magma anywhere except unrelated GLSL plasma/voronoi shaders. CC0 is maximally permissive. Store as static hex anchor arrays — no dependency.

backlog.name: ColorBrewer sequential + diverging schemes (subset: Blues/Greens/YlOrRd/Viridis-style sequential, RdBu/RdYlBu/BrBG/PiYG diverging)
backlog.what: Cynthia Brewer's cartographer-vetted discrete schemes — the standard for choropleths. Crucially provides DIVERGING ramps (two hues meeting at a light neutral midpoint, e.g. RdBu, BrBG, PiYG) which viridis does NOT cover and which the vault has zero of — diverging is the correct encoding for signed/centered data (above/below average, +/- change, sentiment). ColorBrewer also publishes an explicit colorblind-safe flag per scheme, so ship only the CB-safe ones. Store the 9-class anchor arrays as named palettes; an interpolator (below) fills between them. Pairs with viridis: viridis = sequential default, ColorBrewer diverging = signed-data default.
backlog.license: Apache-2.0 (color values explicitly free to use)
backlog.note: The diverging ramps are the unique value here — nothing in colors/scales.css or palettes.css does center-out two-hue encoding. Filter to only colorblind-safe schemes.

backlog.name: Vanilla 'data-scale' interpolator (sequential/diverging/quantized binning) — reimplemented from d3-scale-chromatic
backlog.what: A ~40-line zero-dep JS module that turns the palettes above into a usable encoder: dataScale(value, [min,max], ramp) -> hex, with sequential (linear t in [0,1]), diverging (pivot at a center value, t mapped center-out), and quantize (n discrete classes for legends/choropleths) modes. Interpolation is piecewise-linear between anchor stops — because viridis/ColorBrewer anchors are dense and pre-corrected, simple linear RGB/Lab interpolation reproduces d3's B-spline result to the eye, no dependency needed. This is the missing 'engine' that the heatmap, treemap, and a future choropleth snippet call — without it the palettes are inert hex lists. Include a tiny CVD-simulation check (deuteranope/protanope matrix transform) as an optional verification helper so the save-gate can confirm a chosen ramp stays distinguishable.
backlog.license: ISC (d3-scale-chromatic) — reference only; the reimplementation is original vanilla code, no dep shipped
backlog.note: Reimplement as vanilla — do not ship d3. The optional CVD-matrix simulator doubles as a 'verification' capability (the vault's moat) for colorblind-safety of any chart's colors.

backlog.name: Okabe-Ito colorblind-safe categorical palette (+ Tableau-10 style as a second qualitative set)
backlog.what: An 8-color qualitative set (#E69F00 #56B4E9 #009E73 #F0E442 #0072B2 #D55E00 #CC79A7 + black/grey) verified distinguishable under protanopia/deuteranopia/tritanopia via CVD simulators — the de-facto scientific standard for categorical/series colors, R 4.0+ default. This fixes the OTHER half of the dataviz color problem: the treemap, pie, bar, network, and radar snippets currently pull from a 7-color decorative array chosen for looks, not CVD-distinctness or against-background contrast. Ship as a named categorical palette (a 'series' analog to the sequential/diverging ramps) so multi-series charts default to a colorblind-safe rotation.
backlog.license: Public-domain / freely reusable (Color Universal Design, Okabe & Ito 2002; Wong, Nature Methods 2011)
backlog.note: Complements the sequential/diverging ramps to make a complete data-encoding triad (categorical/sequential/diverging). The vault's existing categorical chart palettes are decorative, not CVD-tested.

backlog.name: chart-chooser.skill.md (FT Visual Vocabulary intent→snippet router)
backlog.what: A new .skill.md that encodes the Financial Times Visual Vocabulary decision tree: 9 intent buckets (magnitude/comparison, change-over-time, part-to-whole, distribution, correlation, flow, spatial/geo, ranking, deviation) → from a plain-English data description it picks the RIGHT chart type, then routes to the EXACT existing vault snippet (e.g. part-to-whole+few-categories → chart-pie.js; flow → chart-sankey.js / chart-network.js; correlation → charts-pro.js scatter; change-over-time+single-series → chart-line.js or sparkline.js; magnitude → chart-bar.js; ranking → chart-bar.js ordered + table.js; calendar density → heatmap-calendar.js; geo/choropleth → greece-map.css/maps-pack.css). Worth adding because the vault's 22-file data-viz folder is currently addressable only by chart NAME (INDEX) or by LIBRARY (existing dataviz.skill.md) — there is NO 'I have data shape X + intent Y, which viz + which snippet?' router. This makes the whole folder addressable by intent for deterministic composition and, critically, FLAGS GAPS (intent→chart with no snippet, e.g. boxplot/violin/diverging-bar) instead of silently composing the wrong chart. The FT taxonomy itself (category→chart-type mapping) is factual and reimplementable; only the FT poster artwork/text is 'all rights reserved', so we reproduce the LOGIC, not the asset.
backlog.license: FT poster: Copyright © The Financial Times (all rights reserved) — taxonomy logic is factual/reimplementable; skill itself authored fresh
backlog.note: Does NOT duplicate dataviz.skill.md — that skill chooses a LIBRARY (D3 vs Chart.js vs ECharts); this chooses a CHART TYPE by data shape+intent and routes to a concrete vault snippet. Reproduce FT logic only (categories→chart types), never the copyrighted poster artwork/text. Cross-link both skills: chooser picks the chart, dataviz.skill.md picks the renderer.

backlog.name: AntV chart-visualization-skills + Chart Knowledge Base (CKB)
backlog.what: MIT-licensed, machine-readable intent→chart knowledge base (26+ chart types, each with a structured spec of when-to-use, required data fields, and parameters) plus AntV's CKB/Advisor recommendation rules. Best used as the authoritative REIMPLEMENTATION SOURCE for the chart-chooser skill above: it gives a clean, permissively-licensed encoding of 'data characteristics → recommended chart' that I can mine to write the skill's decision rules (e.g. nominal x + single quantitative y → bar; two quantitative → scatter; temporal x → line; part-to-whole + <=6 slices → pie) without touching FT's copyrighted poster. It also doubles as a sanity check on which chart types the vault is missing.
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: Reference/inspiration, not a runtime dep — Design Book composes vanilla snippets, not AntV/G2. Use its CKB rules as the permissive (MIT) backbone of the chart-chooser skill's logic so the skill cites an open source, not the all-rights-reserved FT poster.

backlog.name: Deviation, ranking & distribution gap-fill snippets (diverging bar · slope · lollipop/dumbbell · waffle · histogram/boxplot)
backlog.what: A small set of vanilla SVG snippets that close the FT taxonomy gaps the chart-chooser would otherwise dead-end on. Coverage audit of data-viz/ vs the FT 9 categories shows the vault has magnitude/comparison (chart-bar), change-over-time (line/area/sparkline/candlestick), part-to-whole (pie/donut/treemap), correlation (charts-pro scatter), flow (sankey/network), and partial geo — but is MISSING: deviation (diverging/diverging-stacked bar), several ranking forms (slope, lollipop, dumbbell), waffle/waterfall for part-to-whole, and true distribution shapes (histogram, boxplot/violin). Adding ~4-6 tiny zero-dep SVG snippets makes every FT intent route to a real snippet, so the chooser never has to flag a gap. Each is a <120-line vanilla JS/SVG builder in the same ChartBar.create() house style.
backlog.license: Original implementation (MIT-equivalent); patterns are standard chart forms, not copyrightable
backlog.note: Ship in the same vanilla house style as chart-bar.js/chart-line.js. Prioritize diverging-bar (deviation, the single biggest missing category) and slope+dumbbell (ranking/comparison-of-two-points) — these are the highest-frequency FT charts with no current snippet.

backlog.name: Bullet graph (Stephen Few spec)
backlog.what: Stephen Few's bullet graph: a compact linear KPI gauge replacement = featured measure (actual bar) + comparative measure (thin target tick) + 2-5 qualitative range bands (poor/satisfactory/good, encoded as single-hue intensities light-to-dark) on a shared quantitative scale. ~30-40px tall, stacks into report tables. The vault has goal-bar / usage-meter / proportion bars (single-value progress) and gauges, but NOTHING that overlays actual-vs-target-vs-bands — this is THE canonical exec-dashboard micro-chart and it's missing. Vanilla SVG (or even pure CSS): bands as rects, measure as a centered bar, target as a 2px vertical rule. Trivial to reimplement, high density payoff for report/deck genres.
backlog.license: Technique/spec (public, vendor-neutral); D3 reference impl gist by Mike Bostock is BSD-3

backlog.name: Horizon chart (banded/layered area)
backlog.what: Max-info-in-min-height time-series chart: split the value range into N equal bands, layer them on top of each other with increasing color intensity, mirror negatives in a contrasting hue. Gives ~Nx the resolution of an area chart in the same vertical space, so you can stack dozens of metrics as faceted small-multiples and still read trends. Vault has area/line/sparkline but no horizon banding — this is the analyst's tool for dense executive 'every-metric-on-one-screen' dashboards. Vanilla SVG: one <path> per band with a clip/translate per layer, or canvas for many rows. Confirmed net-new (zero hits in INDEX, data-viz folder, or any skill).
backlog.license: Technique (public); d3-horizon-chart by kmandov is MIT, D3 example is ISC

backlog.name: Win/loss (tristate) sparkline
backlog.what: Tufte-style binary/tristate micro-chart: a row of equal-width marks where each is an up-block (win/positive), down-block (loss/negative), or short neutral tick (tie/zero) — discards magnitude to show pure outcome streaks. Distinct from the vault's existing magnitude sparkline AND spark-bars (kpi-pack `.kpi-spark-bars`, which encode height/value, not win/loss/tie outcome). Ideal for status tables, OKR streaks, uptime/SLA pass-fail rows. Dead-simple vanilla: `data-winloss="1,-1,1,0,1"` -> equal-width rects above/below a baseline. Net-new, very cheap, pairs with the existing Sparkline global.
backlog.license: Technique (public domain concept, Tufte); reimplement vanilla

backlog.name: Small-multiples / trellis grid helper (shared-scale faceting)
backlog.what: A layout+scale helper that renders the SAME micro-chart (sparkline / bullet / win-loss / horizon) once per group in an aligned grid, with a SINGLE shared y-domain computed across all series so panels are visually comparable, plus aligned baselines, per-cell labels, and one shared axis. This is the missing 'glue' that turns the vault's individual micro-charts into a dense analyst panel. Distinct from charts-pro `sparkGrid`, which is a card grid with per-card mini bars and NO shared/synced scale (so cells aren't comparable). Net-new. Vanilla: compute global min/max, CSS grid of cells, reuse existing chart fns. Highest leverage for report/deck genres because it multiplies the value of every other micro-chart.
backlog.license: Technique (Tufte small multiples / Cleveland trellis); reimplement vanilla

backlog.name: vanilla scale primitives (scaleLinear/scaleLog/scaleTime/scaleBand) — d3-scale core, reimplemented
backlog.what: A tiny zero-dep `scale.js` exposing the four scale constructors that underlie Observable Plot/D3: scaleLinear (domain[]->range[] with .invert()), scaleLog (log transform), scaleTime (Date domain, calendar-aware), and scaleBand (ordinal->banded x positions with padding/bandwidth). Each is a closure returning a map fn plus .domain/.range/.invert/.bandwidth/.ticks. Worth adding because every chart in data-viz/ currently hand-rolls crude `v/maxVal*plotH` math with no inversion, no log/time support, and no banded layout — a shared scale is THE foundational primitive that upgrades line/bar/area/scatter at once AND is the literal prerequisite for crosshair/brush/annotation (they all need pixel->domain .invert()). d3-scale is ISC-licensed and ~30-60 lines per scale to reimplement vanilla; the math is public and stable (v4.0.2).
backlog.license: ISC (d3-scale, permissive/MIT-equivalent) — reimplemented vanilla
backlog.note: Anti-dup checked: vault's only 'scales' are colors/scales.css (color ramps) and structure spacing-scale — NOT data scales. data-viz charts have zero shared scaling; 125 occurrences of bespoke niceMax()/v/maxVal math confirm the gap. Reimplement rather than CDN d3-scale to stay zero-dep.

backlog.name: nice-ticks algorithm (d3-array ticks / tickStep / tickIncrement / nice)
backlog.what: The ~30-line load-bearing algorithm that makes axes look professional: tickStep picks a step that is a power of ten times {1,2,5} for a target count; tickIncrement handles sub-1 steps via integer math (avoids float drift); ticks() emits the uniformly-spaced human-readable values; nice() rounds the domain to those boundaries. This replaces the vault's `niceMax()` (which only ceilings to the next power of 10 — so a max of 47 becomes 100 and the axis reads 0/25/50/75/100 with one wasted half, instead of 0/10/20/30/40/50). Net-new because no chart in the vault generates real tick *arrays* — they hardcode gridSteps=4 and divide. Pairs with the scale primitive: scale.ticks(n) -> nice-ticks. Tiny, ISC, exact public algorithm.
backlog.license: ISC (d3-array) — reimplemented vanilla
backlog.note: Confirmed gap: data-viz/chart-line.js, chart-bar.js, chart-area.js all define a local niceMax() that ceilings to a power of 10 and use fixed gridSteps=4. No tickStep/tickIncrement/nice anywhere. This is the single highest-leverage primitive — it visibly upgrades every existing chart's y-axis.

backlog.name: vanilla axis renderer with built-in tick-label rotation + collision thinning
backlog.what: An `axis.js` that takes a scale + container and renders the SVG axis convention Plot/D3 use: a domain path, transformed tick groups (line + text), optional gridlines, and tick formatting via Intl.NumberFormat / a small time formatter. Crucially it bakes in the two things d3-axis itself LACKS and that d3fc had to add (axisLabelRotate/axisLabelOffset): automatic tick-label rotation (e.g. 45deg) and collision-aware label thinning so dense band/time axes stay readable instead of overlapping into mush. Worth adding because the vault's charts only drop raw text labels at fixed positions with no rotation and no overflow handling — long category or date labels currently collide, which would also trip the verifier's overflow/coherence gate. This is the render half of the scale+ticks foundation.
backlog.license: ISC (d3-axis concept) — vanilla impl; rotation/thinning is original
backlog.note: d3-axis has no native label rotation/overlap handling (confirmed via Scott Logic / d3fc) — so the vault's util can differentiate by shipping rotation + thinning out of the box. Ties directly into the save-gate: rotated/thinned labels prevent the overflow that the headless-Chrome verifier flags.

backlog.name: dataviz.skill.md axes/scale decision branch + chart refactor to shared scale
backlog.what: A short decision-tree addition to the existing dataviz.skill.md: when to use linear vs log (>2 orders of magnitude / ratios) vs time (Date domain) vs band (categorical) scales, when to enable gridlines/rotation, and a pointer to the new scale/ticks/axis utils — plus rewiring chart-line/bar/area to consume the shared scale instead of their private niceMax(). Worth adding because the foundation is only valuable if the engine knows to reach for it; the skill currently only lists external libs (Plot/Chart.js/ECharts) and gives no guidance on scale *choice* or axis quality for the vault's own charts.
backlog.license: N/A (vault-authored)
backlog.note: dataviz.skill.md exists and governs data-viz/, but it only recommends 3rd-party libs and says nothing about scale type selection, nice ticks, or axis rotation for the vault's hand-rolled charts. The branch + refactor is what makes the new primitives discoverable and actually applied.

backlog.name: Rendered-DOM contrast gate (text-node fg/bg pixel sampling in headless Chrome)
backlog.what: A new verification dimension: inside the existing headless-Chrome save-gate, walk every rendered text node, get its computed foreground color, and resolve the EFFECTIVE background by sampling the page screenshot's pixels under each glyph's bounding box (page.screenshot -> read RGBA at the text rects). This is the only way to measure legibility of text sitting on gradients, images, mesh/aurora backgrounds, and backdrop-filters — exactly the cases the current coherence_check (a static HTML-string heuristic that only flags GRADIENT-HEAVY-NO-IMAGERY / NO-IMAGERY in server.js checkCoherence) is blind to. Enforce a per-font-size contrast floor (large display vs body) and breach the gate on fails. Worth adding because the vault's whole moat is 'it renders clean or it doesn't ship', yet it never checks if the text is actually readable on the rendered pixels — the #1 AI tell after no-imagery. The sampling harness is the net-new asset; the scoring math is swappable.
backlog.license: Technique/harness is original; uses Puppeteer/Playwright screenshot+getComputedStyle (already in the gate). No third-party license needed for the sampling layer.
backlog.note: Net-new gate dimension. The existing gate samples render-cleanliness/overflow/imagery but never reads rendered text-vs-background pixels. Pair with the scoring candidate below.

backlog.name: WCAG21 contrast scoring via Color.js contrast() (unencumbered Lc-style floor)
backlog.what: The scoring half of the gate, kept license-clean. Color.js exposes contrast(a,b,{algorithm}) supporting WCAG21, APCA, Lstar, Weber, Michelson, DeltaPhi — all MIT. For Design Book's gate, use the WCAG21 ratio (fully unencumbered: 4.5:1 body, 3:1 large/bold) as the enforced floor, computed from the sampled fg/effective-bg pairs from the harness above. It is ~30 lines vanilla-reimplementable (relative luminance + ratio) so it can ship as a zero-dep snippet inside the gate rather than a CDN dep. Worth adding because it gives a defensible, citable legibility number with zero licensing exposure, and the per-size thresholds map cleanly onto the vault's existing 'large text ≥24px or ≥18.66px bold' rule already in color.skill.md.
backlog.license: MIT (Color.js); the WCAG2 luminance-ratio math itself is a published W3C formula, freely reimplementable
backlog.note: Prefer reimplementing the WCAG2 ratio as a vanilla snippet to avoid even a CDN dep in the gate hot path.

backlog.name: APCA (apca-w3) perceptual, polarity-aware Lc floor — INSPIRATION ONLY (restricted license)
backlog.what: APCA is the better contrast model for self-illuminated displays and the only one that's polarity-aware (correctly treats white-on-dark vs dark-on-white differently) and font-size/weight aware — ideal for a per-size legibility floor and for dark-mode pages where WCAG2 is over/under-strict. BUT the apca-w3 reference implementation's license is the blocker: it is a W3-restricted, source-available license, NOT BSD/MIT. It prohibits commercial use without a signed agreement, restricts use to WCAG web-content accessibility only (explicitly excludes other domains), and forbids modifying the approved constants — a non-compliant or out-of-scope copy is 'in breech of license'. So apca-w3 cannot be vendored into a commercial-grade design tool's gate. Recommendation: ship the WCAG21 floor as the enforced gate, and document APCA's Lc bands (Lc 75 body, Lc 60 ≈ 4.5:1, Lc 45 large, Lc 15 = invisible) as inspiration/advisory output, citing the public W3-LaTeX spec rather than copying apca-w3's code.
backlog.license: RESTRICTED — W3 'Compliant Code Only' / source-available; commercial use prohibited w/o signed license; web-a11y scope only; constants must not be modified. NOT permissive. Do NOT vendor.
backlog.note: FLAG: license is the reason to NOT add apca-w3 as a dep. Use as inspiration/advisory only; enforce with WCAG2 which is unencumbered.

backlog.name: Canvas-rasterize-behind-glyph worst-case contrast gate (vanilla verification primitive)
backlog.what: A new headless-Chrome save-gate verb that measures REAL rendered text contrast over gradient/image/backdrop-filter surfaces. Technique: (1) get glyph region with Range.getClientRects()/getBoundingClientRect on the text node; (2) sample the ACTUAL painted pixels of that clip rect via CDP Page.captureScreenshot or the harness's existing getImageData/gl.readPixels path (NOT a JS re-rasterizer); (3) worst-case pairing — for every sampled bg pixel along the glyph rect, compute contrast vs the text color and take the MIN; if the worst point passes, the region passes; report worst Lc + its coords. Worth adding because it is the single missing primitive: the vault already has WCAG/APCA on resolved hex (palette-generator.js, color.skill.md, color-tools-pack.css) and canvas 'did-it-draw' variance sampling (render-smoke.mjs), but NOTHING resolves the background luminance behind text on its signature mesh/aurora/holographic gradients. This makes contrast checks actually true on exactly the surfaces this vault generates, as a 'readability floor' gate alongside the imagery floor + coherence score.
backlog.license: N/A (own vanilla code; technique is public-domain)
backlog.note: Use the real browser compositor for sampling — do NOT use html2canvas/html2canvas-pro/html-to-image (all MIT but they DROP backdrop-filter blur and re-rasterize CSS gradients imperfectly, so they would lie about the very surfaces this gate targets). The harness already runs headless Chrome and already calls getImageData/gl.readPixels, so the sampling plumbing exists; this adds the glyph-rect clip + worst-case loop.

backlog.name: APCA Lc scoring math (clean-room vanilla reimplementation; apca-w3 as inspiration)
backlog.what: The APCA (Accessible Perceptual Contrast Algorithm, WCAG-3 candidate) scoring math — sRGBtoY luminance power-curve + the APCAcontrast soft-clamp/polarity formula — reimplemented as a tiny vanilla function. APCA is polarity-aware (light-on-dark vs dark-on-light score differently) and perceptually tuned, which is materially better than WCAG2's flat ratio for the gradient/glow surfaces this vault favors. It is the natural scorer for the canvas-sampling gate above (feed it the worst-case sampled bg luminance + text color, get an Lc; thresholds ~Lc60 large/bold, ~Lc75 body, ~Lc90 small). Pairs with keeping WCAG2 ratio as a secondary number.
backlog.license: RESTRICTED — apca-w3 ships under a non-standard 'W3/AGWG collaborative agreement with specific limitations and exclusions' + a trademarked tagline; NOT a clean MIT/BSD dep. FLAG: do not vendor the package. The published ALGORITHM/constants are documented and clean-room reimplementable (~40 lines).
backlog.note: Integration = inspiration, not a CDN dep, because of the restrictive license. Reimplement the sRGBtoY + APCAcontrast math from the public spec; cite the source. This is the scorer that the verification primitive consumes.

backlog.name: Deterministic lab-CLS gate: settle-then-read layout-shift PerformanceObserver
backlog.what: A vanilla, zero-dep diagnostic the headless gate injects via page.evaluate to measure Cumulative Layout Shift reliably in lab context. Core insight: web-vitals.js is known-flaky here (issue #180 — CLS 'comes and goes', because the lib only finalizes on visibilitychange/pagehide which Playwright/Puppeteer skip; LCP needs forced interaction). The fix is a raw PerformanceObserver({type:'layout-shift', buffered:true}) that accumulates entries (skipping hadRecentInput), gated behind an explicit DETERMINISTIC SETTLE protocol before reading: await document.fonts.ready (web-font swap shifts), then wait for N consecutive quiet RAF frames with no new shift entries, then resolve. This is net-new: the current gate checks static overflow at one instant, never shift-OVER-TIME — and the vault ships the exact top-3 CLS sources (font swaps, lazy imagery, GSAP/scroll animation). Worth adding as a hard gate metric (e.g. fail compose if lab-CLS > 0.1) plus a per-shift breakdown (sources/value) for the verify report.
backlog.license: Vanilla reimplementation (concept; web-vitals itself is Apache-2.0)
backlog.note: Pure vanilla — do NOT bundle web-vitals.js (it is the flaky thing being replaced). Snippet must run inside the existing Headless Chrome gate (server.js render-truth gate, ~line 774). Apache-2.0 only applies if you copy web-vitals source; the reimplementation is license-free.

backlog.name: Spec-correct session-window CLS scoring (max-window, not naive sum)
backlog.what: The accuracy upgrade that separates a real CLS gate from a wrong one. The naive snippet everyone copies (CLS += entry.value for all entries) over-reports vs CrUX, because true CLS is the MAXIMUM session window: entries are grouped into a window that resets when an entry is >1s after the previous shift OR >5s after the first shift in the window; CLS is the largest window's summed value. Implementing the LayoutShiftManager session-window algorithm (sessionValue/sessionEntries/firstEntry/lastEntry, track max) means the gate's number matches what Google/field tools report, so a 0.1 threshold is meaningful rather than arbitrary. Pairs directly with the settle-then-read protocol above — it is the scoring half of the same gate. Should also emit the worst window's contributing entries (largestShiftSources) so the verify report can point the composer at the offending element.
backlog.license: Algorithm is from the public CLS spec / web.dev (CC-BY); reimplementable
