backlog.note: Without this, a lab-CLS gate fires false positives on benign multi-shift loads. Compute BOTH naive-sum (debug) and max-session (gate) so authors see why a page fails. Reference impl: web-vitals src/lib/LayoutShiftManager.js.

backlog.name: Trigger-then-settle harness: scroll-to-bottom + font-ready + animation-quiesce before measuring
backlog.what: The lab-environment 'provocation' layer that makes the CLS number representative rather than artificially zero. In headless, lazy /IntersectionObserver content below the fold never loads (so its shift never fires) and font swaps may not have completed, so a measure-on-load reading is falsely clean. A deterministic harness step: (1) await document.fonts.ready, (2) programmatically scroll the full document height and back to top to force lazy imagery + reveal-on-scroll/GSAP ScrollTrigger animations to run, (3) wait until layout-shift entries go quiet for a fixed RAF/timeout budget, THEN snapshot. This is reproducible (same script, same result) unlike web-vitals' interaction-dependent finalization, and it specifically exercises the vault's lazy-image floor and scroll-animation snippets. Net-new gate capability layered on the existing headless harness.
backlog.license: Vanilla / public best-practice (Checkly docs, MIT-spirit examples)
backlog.note: Without scroll-provocation the gate green-lights pages whose worst shift is a below-fold lazy image — exactly the imagery the vault auto-fills. Use a bounded settle budget (cap total wait, e.g. 3-4s) so the gate stays fast and deterministic.

backlog.name: LCP-element lab gate (LargestContentfulPaint API tag check in the render harness)
backlog.what: Inside the existing Playwright/new-headless render-smoke harness, register a buffered PerformanceObserver for 'largest-contentful-paint' and inspect the final entry's `element`. The LCP entry exposes `.element` (the actual DOM node that became the largest paint), `.url` (for image LCPs), `.size`, and `.renderTime`. The save-gate flags a page when the LCP element is decorative chrome rather than real content: tagName CANVAS / a node whose only background is a gradient / a fixed full-bleed shader layer / an element with no text and no real img-src. This is the NET-NEW axis the brief asks for: today `checkCoherence` is pure regex on the HTML string (it counts gradients vs / tags) and cannot know which element actually wins LCP at runtime — so a three.js/OGL hero where the real headline paints late but a full-screen gradient/canvas is the largest paint passes the static check yet fails the user. The runtime LCP element tells you the truth. New headless Chrome runs the full paint pipeline (the harness already depends on rAF ticks for its canvas-drew check), and the LCP API is buffered so it is readable post-load without a real-user session. Reimplementable in ~30 lines of in-page JS, zero new dependency.
backlog.license: W3C spec / native browser API (no dep, MIT-equivalent platform API)
backlog.note: Complements (does not duplicate) checkCoherence's static gradient-heavy-no-imagery tell: that proves imagery EXISTS in markup; this proves the imagery is what actually PAINTS LARGEST. Add as a soft warning ('decorative-lcp') first, optionally hard-gate later. Caveat: viewport-size dependent, so pin the harness viewport (the spec already does).

backlog.name: Long Animation Frames (LoAF) main-thread-blocking gate with script attribution
backlog.what: Observe entryType 'long-animation-frame' (shipped Chrome 123+) during the harness's post-load settle window to catch the 'heavy shader blocks interactivity' failure directly. Each LoAF entry exposes `blockingDuration` (ms the main thread blocked high-priority/input tasks), `duration`, `renderStart`, and a `scripts[]` array with `sourceURL`, `sourceFunctionName`, `invoker`/`invokerType` (e.g. 'Window.requestAnimationFrame'), and `forcedStyleAndLayoutDuration`. This pinpoints exactly which script is janking the frame — so a GLSL/three.js/OGL render loop that monopolizes the main thread (long uniform updates, sync texture decode, layout thrash) is flagged WITH the offending sourceURL, not just 'feels slow'. It is the lab proxy for INP/responsiveness on a vault that leans hard on WebGL. The new-headless harness runs real animation frames (proven by the existing canvas-drew rAF dependency), so LoAFs are emitted; sum blockingDuration over the window as a TBT-style budget (e.g. warn >200ms). Reimplementable in‑page, zero dependency.
backlog.license: W3C/WICG spec / native Chromium API (no dep)
backlog.note: Net-new: the harness today only checks console-clean + canvas-DREW; nothing measures whether drawing BLOCKS the main thread. Pair with a synthetic interaction (Playwright page.click on the primary CTA) and read EventTiming/the intersecting LoAF for a lab-INP number. Chrome-only attribution; cross-origin iframe/worker scripts won't attribute (fine here).

backlog.name: web-vitals (Google) — attribution build as the in-page measurement script
backlog.what: Inject Google's web-vitals/attribution bundle (~2KB, Apache-2.0) into harness pages instead of hand-rolling the observers, then drive a synthetic interaction with Playwright and read onLCP/onINP/onCLS. As of v4 the INP attribution interface includes `longAnimationFrameEntries`, so one library yields both the LCP candidate and the LoAF(s) responsible for the worst interaction — exactly the two signals above, battle-tested against edge cases (bfcache, SPA soft-nav, multiple paints) that a naive PerformanceObserver misses. In a lab run you script the interaction (click the composed hero's primary button) so INP is actually produced without a real user. Best added as a DEV-only harness dependency (like playwright-core already is), not shipped to composed pages.
backlog.license: Apache-2.0 (permissive)
backlog.note: Convenience/robustness layer over the two native APIs above — use it to avoid reimplementing attribution edge cases. CDN-safe (unpkg/jsdelivr) but for a save-gate prefer pinning it as a tools/ devDependency next to playwright-core/pixelmatch. If a zero-dep harness is preferred, the native LCP+LoAF observers cover ~90% of the value alone.

backlog.name: blazediff (@blazediff/core)
backlog.what: MIT, pixelmatch-algorithm-compatible (same YIQ + anti-aliasing detection) but block-level-optimized: the pure-JS @blazediff/core is ~50% faster than pixelmatch with zero native dependencies, and an optional @blazediff/core-wasm tier is ~58% faster (up to 5x on 4K). Worth adding as a drop-in faster default OR a need()-guarded accelerator for the baseline gate: if/when diffing the full ~920-snippet vault per run becomes a measurable bottleneck, swap pixelmatch → @blazediff/core with no native-binary cost and no behavior change (same AA handling), and only reach for the WASM tier under heavy load. It directly answers the spec's open question ('odiff is the escape hatch only if diffing the full vault becomes a measurable bottleneck') with a pure-JS escape hatch that avoids odiff's native binary entirely. Also ships @blazediff/ssim and @blazediff/gmsd for optional perceptual scoring if pixel-ratio proves too brittle.
backlog.license: MIT (permissive)
backlog.note: Not in the spec (postdates it). Strongest fit because the pure-JS core preserves pixelmatch's API/AA semantics while being faster, so it's a no-binary upgrade path for the same OS/GPU-keyed baseline gate. Verify the core API matches pixelmatch's signature before a literal drop-in; otherwise it's a thin shim.

backlog.name: honeydiff (@vizzly-testing/honeydiff)
backlog.what: MIT, Rust/N-API native engine (~127-227M px/s, multi-core Rayon; ~9x faster than odiff and ~5.6x faster than pixelmatch on an 18M-px full-page shot). Beyond raw speed it returns RICH diff metrics the vault's gate doesn't have: SSIM + GMSD structural scores, CIEDE2000 perceptual color distance (default ΔE 2.0), spatial clustering of differences into connected regions (text-like-anomaly detection), conservative anti-aliasing detection, and WCAG contrast / color-vision-deficiency screening. For the moat, the interesting part is NOT speed but the metrics: a perceptual SSIM/CIEDE2000 score + clustered diff regions is a more robust 'silent rendering drift' signal than a raw changed-pixel ratio, and the WCAG/contrast screen overlaps the gate's anti-slop coherence ethos. Caveat: it ships prebuilt native binaries and requires Node 22+, Node-only (no browser/CDN). That's a heavier dependency than pixelmatch/blazediff for the deterministic composer's gate.
backlog.license: MIT (permissive)
backlog.note: Permissive but native-binary + Node-22 floor makes it a second-tier pick vs pure-JS pixelmatch/blazediff for a vault gate that already juggles macOS-dev vs Linux-CI baselines. Best framed as an OPTIONAL richer-metrics escape hatch (SSIM/CIEDE2000/spatial-cluster verdict) when the pixel-ratio gate proves too noisy — not the default engine.

backlog.name: Layout-tree fingerprint gate (bounding-box tree hash, color-blind structural diff)
backlog.what: A new verification gate: in the same Playwright page the render-smoke harness already drives, walk visible elements and serialize a structural fingerprint = ordered list of [tag, depth, role, quantized x/y/w/h] from getBoundingClientRect, then hash it. Diff is the symmetric set-diff of fingerprints (boxes added/removed/moved >Npx), NOT pixels — so it is totally indifferent to the composer's intended palette/seed variance but screams when the flexbox collapses, a section overlaps, a column reflows, or content overflows. Pairs with a same-pass overflow assertion (scrollWidth>clientWidth | any rect right>viewport) which the current gate astonishingly lacks. This is the exact answer to the question: a baseline that survives seed>0 color randomization but catches 'structure broke'. Quantizing coords (round to e.g. 4px) absorbs AA/font-hinting jitter that makes pixelmatch baselines OS/GPU-fragile (the spec admits macOS baselines won't match Linux CI) — a tree-hash baseline is machine-portable because geometry rounds the same everywhere. Vanilla, zero new deps (reuses installed Playwright + Chrome). Store baselines per-genre, not per-OS.
backlog.license: N/A (own code, Web API)
backlog.note: Net-new: render-smoke.mjs only asserts zero-error + non-empty preview + canvas-drew (R/G/B variance); it has NO geometry/overflow/layout check. The only diff layer is the opt-in pixelmatch screenshot diff (§5 of RENDER-HARNESS-SPEC.md) which is OS/GPU-specific and conceptually useless across the composer's seed-driven palette variance (server.js: 'seed 0 = byte-identical, each increment varies palette/snippet picks'). This is the moat the angle names.

backlog.name: Playwright ARIA snapshots (toMatchAriaSnapshot / page.ariaSnapshot)
backlog.what: Built into the Playwright the harness already uses (no new dep): page.ariaSnapshot() emits a compact YAML of the accessibility tree — roles, accessible names, levels, states, hierarchy — and is structure/semantics-only by construction, ignoring color/styling entirely. Diffing the YAML against a per-genre baseline catches 'a heading disappeared / list lost items / nav structure changed / button became a div' regressions that survive palette randomization. It is the cheap, human-readable complement to the geometric tree-hash: ARIA snapshot guards the SEMANTIC skeleton (did the content tree change), the bounding-box hash guards the GEOMETRIC skeleton (did the layout shift/overlap). Together they form a color-blind two-axis structural baseline tailored to a seeded generator. Bonus: a stable ARIA snapshot is itself an accessibility-coherence signal for the anti-slop moat.
backlog.license: Apache-2.0 (Playwright)
backlog.note: Confirmed absent: grep for aria.snapshot/ariaSnapshot/accessibility-tree across RENDER-HARNESS-SPEC.md, render-smoke.mjs, structure.skill.md, server.js returned nothing. Harness already depends on playwright-core@1.60 so this is zero new dependency — pure capability add.

backlog.name: IoU / box-overlap layout-distance scoring (LambdaTest SmartUI 'Layout Comparison', Applitools 'Layout' mode)
backlog.what: Inspiration for the scoring layer on top of the bounding-box fingerprint: commercial SaaS (SmartUI Layout Comparison, BETA May 2025; Applitools Eyes 'Layout' match level) verify that elements stay arranged correctly while explicitly excluding content/color/style differences — precisely the seed-variance problem. The reimplementable technique is per-element Intersection-over-Union (IoU) between matched boxes in baseline vs current, plus center-Euclidean-distance and an alignment-consistency check; aggregate into a 0-100 'layout-stability' score with a save-gate threshold, mirroring the existing coherence_check score. This upgrades the binary tree-hash diff into a graded metric that tolerates tiny intended reflow but fails on real breakage, and slots cleanly beside checkCoherence() as a second moat number. Do NOT add the SaaS as a dep — closed/cloud-only; take only the IoU+alignment math (textbook, ~40 lines vanilla).
backlog.license: Proprietary / SaaS (LambdaTest, Applitools) — technique only, not a dep
backlog.note: FLAG: both are closed cloud platforms — capture as inspiration for the scoring formula only. Validates that 'layout-mode, color-excluded' regression is now a named industry category (2025), confirming the moat direction. The math (IoU, center-distance, alignment) is freely reimplementable in the existing Node/Playwright harness.

backlog.name: Vertical-clip / line-clamp truncation gate (scrollHeight > clientHeight)
backlog.what: A scored save-gate dimension that sweeps text-bearing leaf elements and flags el.scrollHeight > el.clientHeight + 1 on elements whose computed overflow is hidden/clip (or that carry -webkit-line-clamp / a fixed height). This is the canonical, browser-universal way to detect content cut off VERTICALLY — exactly the AI-compose bug where a paragraph overflows a fixed-height card or a line-clamp eats the back half of a heading. Worth adding because the gate's R.layout today only checks horizontal escape (r.right > W); width-based checks are blind to line-clamp since the element's width stays pinned to its container. Pure DOM geometry, no pixel-diff, no baseline screenshot needed — notable because Playwright itself has no native solution for this (open issue microsoft/playwright#10686) and the community falls back to expensive visual regression. Slots into run() right beside hOverflow with a per-element penalty (~3-4 each) so it counts toward the gate score, plus a guard against legitimately scrollable containers (overflow:auto/scroll with a real scrollbar).
backlog.license: public-technique (MIT-compatible reimplementation)

backlog.name: Horizontal text-overflow / ellipsis truncation detector (scrollWidth > offsetWidth)
backlog.what: Companion gate check for SINGLE-LINE clipping: flag elements where getComputedStyle gives text-overflow:ellipsis (or overflow:hidden + white-space:nowrap) AND el.scrollWidth > el.offsetWidth + 1 — i.e. a label/title/breadcrumb/nav item whose text is silently cut with an ellipsis (or hard-clipped) because it didn't fit. The vault has many components prone to this (breadcrumb-pack truncate, tab-overflow strips, dropdown-menu, avatar group overflow, chips). The gate's existing r.right > W overflow check never fires here because the clipped text stays inside the element's box and inside the viewport — it's clipped LOCALLY by the element's own overflow:hidden, not by the page. Distinct from the line-clamp check (that's vertical/scrollHeight; this is horizontal/scrollWidth). Cheap to compute in the same per-element sweep; emit a 'truncated' list with the sel + a sample of the visible text so the agent knows what got eaten.
backlog.license: public-technique (MIT-compatible reimplementation)

backlog.name: Promote clipped-by-ancestor detection from inspect into the scored gate
backlog.what: inspect.js already has the right algorithm (lines 554-574 'clippedByAncestor'): walk an element's ancestors, and if any has overflow hidden/clip/auto and the child's getBoundingClientRect spills past that ancestor's rect (right/left/bottom/top), the child is clipped. But that logic only runs in the per-element inspect deep-dive (an agent-driven, opt-in single-element diagnostic) and is NOT scored — so the save-gate never penalizes it. The net-new addition: run a bounded version of this traversal in the gate's run() per-element sweep and add a penalty so genuinely clipped content (a badge, button, or absolutely-positioned element escaping and being chopped by an overflow:hidden parent — or escaping below the fold of a fixed-height hero) drops the gate score. This catches the 'absolutely-positioned element escaping its container' and 'fixed-height card chopping its child' cases that neither hOverflow nor the vertical-clip check fully cover. Reuses code that already exists and is proven; the work is wiring it into the scored path with a sane element cap and a same-page-as-content guard.
backlog.license: in-repo (already written in lib/inspect.js)

backlog.name: Vanilla focus-order / keyboard-reachability audit (gate verification)
backlog.what: A new diagnostic in the inspect engine that (1) collects the tabbable set, (2) flags positive tabindex (the 'tabindex trap' — overrides natural order, WCAG 2.4.3/2.4.7 failure), (3) compares DOM/tab order against visual order via getBoundingClientRect (top-to-bottom, left-to-right) and reports 'focus teleports' caused by flex `order`/grid-placement/absolute positioning, and (4) flags interactive elements that are visually present but unreachable (tabindex=-1, aria-hidden/inert ancestor, or 0-size). It plugs straight into lib/inspect.js: the per-element sweep already builds `interactive[]` with rects (line ~404) and the a11y block + penalty scoring (lines 329, 490-502) have an obvious slot. Net-new because the gate's a11y today is contrast/alt/labels/headings/dupe-ids only — no focus or tab-order signal anywhere. High-signal: this catches the single most common keyboard failure in generated UIs (visual reorder ≠ DOM order) that a pixel-blind model cannot otherwise see, and that axe-core itself flags for manual review rather than automating.
backlog.license: Public-domain technique (W3C/WCAG spec); reimplementable in zero-dep JS
backlog.note: Reimplement in vanilla, do NOT pull axe-core: axe-core is MPL-2.0 (file-level weak copyleft, a real caveat for the vendor/CDN-snippet path) AND per Deque/Pa11y docs only ~57% of WCAG is automatable with focus-order explicitly left to manual review — so vanilla both dodges the license and covers a gap axe leaves open. The interactive[] collector and penalty block already exist in /Users/dath/Documents/Claude Usable Stuff/designbook/lib/inspect.js, so this is an additive ~40-line change, not new infra.

backlog.name: Visible-focus-indicator audit (focus + getComputedStyle in headless)
backlog.what: A companion verification: for each tabbable element, programmatically el.focus() in the existing Puppeteer harness, read getComputedStyle, and flag elements whose focused state has no distinguishable indicator (outline:none AND no box-shadow/border/bg delta vs the unfocused state) — i.e. someone wrote `:focus{outline:none}` or `*{outline:0}` with no replacement, the classic WCAG 2.4.7 violation. WebAIM's 2024 million-page scan found 78% of home pages have detectable focus-indicator issues, so this is the highest-frequency keyboard failure after order. The technique is headless-safe (focus()+getComputedStyle needs no user gesture in Chrome/Puppeteer), deterministic, and slots into the same a11y block and penalty scoring as the order audit. Net-new: the gate currently has no :focus-state inspection at all.
backlog.license: Public-domain technique (WCAG 2.4.7); zero-dep
backlog.note: Pairs with the focus-order audit as one 'keyboard a11y' gate dimension. Care needed: compare focused vs unfocused computed style (not just outline==='none', since box-shadow/border/bg substitutes are valid), and skip elements relying solely on browser default which UA stylesheet still provides. Reimplement vanilla; no dependency.

backlog.name: tabbable (focusable/tabbable element resolver)
backlog.what: A tiny MIT, dependency-free utility from the focus-trap org that returns the correct tabbable vs focusable set for a container — it already handles the gnarly edge cases a naive querySelectorAll misses: positive-tabindex ordering, display/visibility/getClientRects visibility checks (configurable displayCheck), inert, closed <details>, disabled, and optional shadow DOM. Worth adding NOT as a runtime CDN dep but as the reference/spec for the vault's vanilla reachability detector — it is the canonical, battle-tested answer to 'is this element actually in the tab order,' so the gate's vanilla audit should mirror its selector list and visibility logic rather than reinvent (and get) the corner cases wrong.
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: Use as the correctness reference for the vanilla audit (selector list + displayCheck visibility logic), not as a shipped dep — the gate runs server-side in Node/Puppeteer where the audit is injected page-script, so vendoring its ~logic inline keeps the zero-dep promise. License (MIT) is clean if you ever do choose to vendor it. Contrast with axe-core (MPL-2.0) for the dependency path.

backlog.name: axe-core (curated a11y scan as a need()-guarded verification signal)
backlog.what: The industry-standard accessibility engine (the same one in Lighthouse, Playwright @axe-core, Cypress, WAVE-adjacent tools). Net-new because the vault's gate has NO real a11y check: checkCoherence (server.js:1287) is pure regex on the HTML string and only scores imagery/motion/token-coherence; nothing inspects alt-text, form labels, ARIA validity, landmark/region structure, or *rendered* color contrast. The existing components/color-tools-pack contrast-checker and utils/palette-generator WCAG helpers are END-USER widgets / authoring math, not a page-level scan. axe ships a single standalone axe.min.js (~550KB) from jsDelivr that exposes axe.run(context, {runOnly:{type:'rule', values:[...]}}) returning a promise with a violations[] array ({id, impact: minor|moderate|serious|critical, nodes, help}). The fit: run it INSIDE the existing headless-Chrome render-truth pass against the already-rendered DOM (where computed contrast is real), feed violations as an ADVISORY gate signal (like the soft authenticity penalties), never auto-fail. This is exactly the 'verification = the moat' play — turns a hand-rolled regex heuristic into the actual W3C ACT-validated engine.
backlog.license: MPL-2.0
backlog.note: LICENSING (the key nuance for need()): MPL-2.0 is FILE-LEVEL copyleft. Loading the UNMODIFIED axe.min.js from a CDN triggers ZERO copyleft obligation on the vault or any generated page — the consuming code is untouched, only axe's own files carry the MPL. (Note: package.json mislabels the SPDX as 'MPL-2.0' when the source headers are MPL-2.0 with Exhibit B / 'no-copyleft-exception' — see issue #4695 — but this is metadata only and does not change the unmodified-use story.) So it ships cleanly behind a need()-guard that degrades to the existing regex heuristic when the CDN is absent. CRITICAL: do NOT fork/patch axe's files into the vault — that would invoke the per-file copyleft. Keep it CDN-only and unmodified.

backlog.name: Curated axe rule subset for imagery-heavy marketing/portfolio genres
backlog.what: The high-value design decision, not the lib itself: axe ships ~90 rules but most are noise on a single-screen, JS-light generated marketing/portfolio/deck page. A curated runOnly:{type:'rule'} allowlist makes axe a precise signal instead of a wall of warnings. RECOMMENDED HARD-ISH SET (all WCAG-A/AA, near-zero false positives — these are genuine 'looks broken to a screen reader' defects on imagery-heavy pages): image-alt (critical), svg-img-alt + role-img-alt (serious — fires constantly because this vault leans on inline-SVG art and book_generate_image heroes), input-image-alt, button-name, link-name (serious — icon-only nav/social links are the #1 real miss), label, document-title, html-has-lang, html-lang-valid, color-contrast (serious — only trustworthy when computed on the RENDERED page, which this gate uniquely can do), aria-valid-attr / aria-required-attr / aria-allowed-attr / aria-roles, frame-title, meta-viewport. ADVISORY-ONLY (real but lower stakes): list, definition-list, duplicate-id-aria, heading-order. EXCLUDE AS NOISE for these genres: region & landmark-one-main & landmark-unique (best-practice — fire on every decorative full-bleed section), page-has-heading-one (hero wordmarks legitimately aren't an <h1>), scrollable-region-focusable (Safari-quirk false positives on overflow art containers), bypass/skip-link nags (the vault already ships responsive/skip-link.css), plus all best-practice and wcag2aaa tags. Mapping to genres: tighten link-name/button-name/svg-img-alt weighting for portfolio (icon-heavy), and color-contrast for deck/slide genres (big type on gradient).
backlog.license: MPL-2.0 (rule taxonomy from axe-core docs)
backlog.note: Curation is what keeps the gate from crying wolf. Run axe with runOnly by explicit rule-id list (not by tag) so best-practice/region noise never enters scoring. Surface violations the same way authPenalty does: small soft deductions + an actionable hint per finding (e.g. 'icon link with no accessible name → add aria-label'), never breaching the save-gate except possibly image-alt on a real with no alt (the one defect that's both certain and trivially fixable). Keep the existing regex heuristic as the graceful-degradation fallback when the CDN is unreachable.

backlog.name: Font-load gate via FontFaceSet (document.fonts.ready / FontFace.status)
backlog.what: A headless diagnostic that, after navigating the composed page, awaits document.fonts.ready then iterates document.fonts (FontFaceSet) reading each FontFace.status. status==='error' = a webfont that never loaded (404/CORS/bad URL); status==='unloaded' for a family the page actually uses = an undeclared/never-requested font. It also listens for the loadingerror event during settle. This is the exact failure the vault has zero coverage for today: render-smoke.mjs (the gate) checks render/console/canvas + an opt-in screenshot diff, but a 404'd webfont silently falls back to a system font and the page still 'renders clean' and passes — a textbook slop tell on a vault whose authenticity law is custom typography. The API is fully exposed in headless Chrome (Widely Available since 2020, also in workers as self.fonts), needs no new dependency, and slots into the existing page.evaluate ASSESS pass. Pairs naturally with the request/response 404 listeners already wired in testFamily (a .woff2 4xx is already observable on the wire; this turns it from a benign-ignored example asset into a typed 'declared-but-failed font' verdict).
backlog.license: Web standard (MDN / W3C CSS Font Loading) — no license
backlog.note: Net-new gate capability. The gate (render-smoke.mjs) has no font-load assertion; only checks render/console/canvas. typography/greek-fonts.css uses font-display:swap but nothing verifies the @font-face actually resolves. Build it inside the existing page.evaluate, zero deps.

backlog.name: Font-swap reflow / CLS gate via Layout Instability API (PerformanceObserver layout-shift)
backlog.what: A headless diagnostic that registers new PerformanceObserver({type:'layout-shift', buffered:true}) before the webfont swap, then awaits document.fonts.ready and sums layout-shift entry.value (filtering hasRecentInput) to quantify the reflow caused when the real font replaces the fallback. A non-trivial post-load shift means the @font-face has no size-adjust/ascent-override/descent-override and the fallback metrics mismatch — the jump-on-load 'flash' that reads as unpolished/AI-generated. This directly extends the moat: the gate currently has NO layout-stability signal at all (no CLS, no reflow measurement). It can emit a per-page cls/font-reflow number and fail (or warn) above a threshold, defending typographic polish the same way the overflow and coherence checks defend layout. Fully measurable in headless Chrome via the Layout Instability API (the same primitive Puppeteer/Lighthouse use). Complements, doesn't duplicate, the FontFaceSet gate above: that one catches fonts that never load, this one catches fonts that load but reflow.
backlog.license: Web standard (Layout Instability API) — no license
backlog.note: Net-new. No CLS/layout-shift measurement exists anywhere in tools/ (render-smoke.mjs, audit.js). buffered:true + hasRecentInput filter; measure across the document.fonts.ready boundary. Zero new deps — pure page.evaluate.

backlog.name: fontaine — automatic fallback metric overrides (size-adjust / ascent-override / descent-override)
backlog.what: MIT build-time tool (unjs) that reads @font-face rules and emits a companion fallback @font-face with computed size-adjust/ascent-override/descent-override/line-gap-override so the system fallback occupies the SAME box as the webfont — near-zero reflow on swap (their playground: CLS 0.24 -> 0.054). This is the FIX paired with the reflow gate above: when the gate flags a font-swap shift, fontaine's technique is the remedy. For Design Book it's best added as inspiration/vanilla-reimplementable: the metric math (parse font metrics, compute overrides) can be precomputed for the vault's actual font pairs and baked into the typography snippets + taste presets' fontPair, since the vault composes deterministically from a known font set rather than arbitrary user fonts. Today typography/*.css ships font-display:swap with NO metric overrides anywhere (grep confirms zero size-adjust/ascent-override in the vault), so every webfont page reflows on load. Adding precomputed-override fallbacks to the font pairs closes that gap and makes the reflow gate green by construction.
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: MIT, permissive. Build-time plugin (Vite/Webpack) so not a drop-in CDN dep — reimplement the override math for the vault's fixed font pairs and bake into typography snippets + taste fontPair. Vault currently has ZERO metric-override descriptors (grep: no size-adjust/ascent-override/descent-override).

backlog.name: FOIT/font-display window detection (invisible-text / swap-period audit)
backlog.what: A static + dynamic check that flags @font-face blocks whose font-display is missing or 'block'/'auto' — the values that produce a FOIT (Flash Of Invisible Text) window where text is invisible up to ~3s while the webfont fetches, a real perceived-slop / accessibility hit. Static side: scan composed CSS for @font-face lacking font-display:swap|optional (audit.js is already the zero-dep static gate and the natural home). Dynamic side: in headless, sample whether text-bearing elements have measurable bounding boxes immediately after first paint but before document.fonts.ready (invisible-text => zero/near-zero rendered glyph coverage during the block period). 'optional' is the strongest anti-reflow choice (no swap at all on slow loads) and the gate could nudge toward it. Net-new: nothing in audit.js or render-smoke.mjs asserts a font-display policy, and the vault inconsistently applies swap (greek-fonts.css has it; nothing enforces it vault-wide). Cheap, high-signal, and reinforces the same typography-polish moat.
backlog.license: Web standard (CSS Fonts Module) — no license
backlog.note: Net-new. audit.js (static gate) has no font-display lint; render-smoke.mjs has no FOIT-window check. Lowest-effort of the four — a regex lint on @font-face in the static gate plus an optional dynamic invisible-text sample. font-display:optional is the zero-reflow recommendation to encode.

backlog.name: Rendered line-measure linter (Range.getClientRects per-line width → true CPL vs 45–75ch band)
backlog.what: A headless-Chrome verification pass that, for every text block (p, li, blockquote, .prose, .s-lead), creates a Range over the text node and reads Range.getClientRects() — each rect is one rendered line and exposes its real pixel width. Divide each line width by the measured width of a '0' glyph (or font-size) to get TRUE characters-per-line, then flag blocks whose median CPL falls outside the Bringhurst 45–75ch comfort band (66 ideal). This is net-new because the coherence gate never measures rendered text at all, and because the ch CSS unit famously lies (it's the '0'-glyph width, not actual character count — per Rutter/swyx), so a max-width:65ch in source does NOT prove the rendered measure is correct. Measuring the actual laid-out geometry catches both too-wide hero copy (the classic AI-slop full-bleed paragraph) and over-narrow columns. Vanilla, native APIs only, runs inside the existing Playwright/Chrome harness via page.evaluate.
backlog.license: N/A (public-domain technique; native Range/getClientRects browser APIs)
backlog.note: Authoring side exists (vertical-rhythm.css uses max-width:65ch, prose) but there is NO rendered-measure check. audit.js coherence is source-regex only. Safari getClientRects quirk is irrelevant since the harness is headless Chrome. Reimplement vanilla; no dep needed.

backlog.name: Spacing-scale conformance gate (cluster rendered gaps → % that are token-scale multiples)
backlog.what: A geometry pass that walks flex/grid containers and sibling stacks, reads each computed gap / margin-block / padding via getComputedStyle (resolved to px) plus getBoundingClientRect deltas between siblings, then scores what fraction of those spacings are integer multiples of the project's spacing base (the structure backbone already implies a scale via --gap/--section-y/--gutter; default to an 8px/4px grid otherwise). Outputs a 'spacing-scale adherence %' and lists the off-grid offenders (e.g. a stray 25px gap in an 8pt system — the exact failure mode design-token literature cites). This is the rendered-geometry analogue of the existing px-radius string check, but for ALL spacing, measured after layout (so clamp()/% /vw values that resolve off-grid are caught, which source-regex cannot do). Cleanly extends the anti-slop coherence dimension into layout craft.
backlog.license: N/A (technique; USWDS spacing-units doc is reference, public-domain US-gov work)
backlog.note: density.css/structure.css PRODUCE a scale; nothing MEASURES conformance. audit.js only catches border-radius:Npx literals via regex — this measures resolved gaps/margins post-layout, a strict superset. Net-new.

backlog.name: Arrhythmia.js (vertical-rhythm validator: element y-positions must be line-height multiples)
backlog.what: A validator that iterates an element's children and verifies each one's start/end y-position is a multiple of the baseline line-height; on a miss it does a depth-first search to the deepest offending node and diagnoses whether height, padding, border, or margin broke the rhythm. This is the precise algorithm for the third heuristic in the question — consistent vertical rhythm / baseline-grid snapping — and the diagnostic 'which CSS property broke the baseline' output is exactly what a save-gate wants to report. It is INSPIRATION, not a dep: it requires jQuery, has no license file, ~13 commits, and is effectively unmaintained — so reimplement the y-position-modulo-baseline check vanilla inside the harness (pairs naturally with the line-measure and spacing-scale passes to form one 'layout-rhythm' coherence sub-score). PostCSS baseline-grid-overlay is a related but build-time-only / GPL-adjacent alternative, also inspiration-only.
backlog.license: FLAG: no license file in repo (rights unclear) + requires jQuery — do NOT vendor; reimplement the algorithm vanilla
backlog.note: vertical-rhythm.css authors rhythm; it does not VERIFY elements land on the baseline. Use Arrhythmia only as the algorithm blueprint (y-pos % baseline === 0, DFS to offender). License-unclear + jQuery dep = inspiration, not dependency.

backlog.name: Chrome CLI media-feature force flags re-verify pass (--force-prefers-color-scheme=dark / --force-prefers-reduced-motion)
backlog.what: A second gate render pass that re-runs the EXISTING in-page probe with Chrome launched under --force-prefers-color-scheme=dark and --force-prefers-reduced-motion, then re-evaluates the WCAG contrast logic (color-audit) and a new reduced-motion compliance check. Catches pages that go unreadable in dark mode (light-only palettes) or keep animating after the page declares reduce. Worth adding because the gate currently proves contrast in ONE media state only and never tests reduced-motion at all — this is exactly the multi-state coverage the moat lacks. Critically it slots into the gate's CURRENT architecture: inspect.js already shells out to the Chrome binary via execFile (--headless=new --dump-dom / --screenshot), so two extra force-flags add a dark/reduced re-verify with ZERO new dependencies. Note the load-bearing distinction: --force-prefers-color-scheme=dark sets the media feature the page's own @media queries answer (correct for testing a site's dark palette), whereas --force-dark-mode is renderer-level luminance inversion and is the WRONG flag here.
backlog.license: N/A (Chromium command-line flags, BSD-licensed engine)
backlog.note: On-architecture / zero-dep path. Confirmed gap: /Users/dath/Documents/Claude Usable Stuff/designbook/lib/inspect.js runs contrast (R.a11y.contrastFails, penalty 5/fail) once in light state; no media emulation anywhere in the engine. USE --force-prefers-color-scheme=dark, NOT --force-dark-mode (inversion). Re-uses the existing lum()/parseRgb()/bgOf() WCAG probe verbatim.

backlog.name: CDP Emulation.setEmulatedMedia multi-feature emulation (single-render, no relaunch)
backlog.what: The precise CDP method behind the DevTools Rendering panel: one call flips prefers-color-scheme (dark), prefers-reduced-motion (reduce), forced-colors (active), prefers-contrast (more), color-gamut, and prefers-reduced-transparency on a LIVE page without relaunching Chrome. Worth adding as the higher-fidelity upgrade to the force-flags pass: connect once over --remote-debugging-port (a tiny WS client, no puppeteer/playwright dep needed), setEmulatedMedia({features:[{name:'prefers-color-scheme',value:'dark'}]}), re-run the probe, then setEmulatedMedia({features:[{name:'prefers-reduced-motion',value:'reduce'}]}) and re-run — all against ONE rendered page, far cheaper than three separate Chrome spawns. Also unlocks forced-colors (Windows High Contrast) and prefers-contrast verification the force-flags can't all reach. This is exactly how Playwright/Puppeteer/Cypress emulateMedia work under the hood.
backlog.license: N/A (Chrome DevTools Protocol, open spec; BSD engine)
backlog.note: Higher fidelity but needs a CDP socket (--remote-debugging-port + minimal WS client). The gate currently has NO CDP connection — it only execFile's the Chrome binary. Best framed as the v2 of the force-flags pass; covers forced-colors / prefers-contrast which CLI flags don't all expose.

backlog.name: Reduced-motion compliance probe (getAnimations() + non-zero transform/opacity transitions after reduce)
backlog.what: An in-page check that runs in the reduced-motion re-render: after the page is told prefers-reduced-motion:reduce, flag any element whose document.getAnimations() are still .playing or whose computed transition-duration on transform/opacity is non-zero (i.e. essential motion the page failed to suppress). Pairs with a settled-screenshot diff (capture at t and t+800ms under reduce; if pixels still change, motion is leaking). Worth adding because the vault ships HEAVY motion and many snippets only CLAIM reduced-motion support — this is the first check that actually PROVES a composed page honors the OS setting, turning 'ignores reduced-motion' from an invisible a11y/slop failure into a gate penalty. Vanilla, zero-dep: getAnimations() is baseline browser API, mirrors the existing probe style in inspect.js.
backlog.license: N/A (Web Animations API, W3C standard)
backlog.note: Net-new: no reduced-motion COMPLIANCE check exists in the gate (vault has many reduced-motion-aware snippets + taste/motion.css that zero durations, but nothing VERIFIES a composed page obeys reduce). Implements the 'no-essential-motion' half of the question. Reuses inspect.js probe conventions; getAnimations()+settle-diff is the reliable signal.

backlog.name: Asset-liveness + image-decode save-gate check (vanilla, in-harness via page.on('response') + naturalWidth probe)
backlog.what: A net-new save-gate diagnostic: while the page renders in the EXISTING headless Chrome (lib/inspect.js / render-smoke.mjs), capture page.on('response') for every subresource and flag any status>=400 (covers //
backlog.license: N/A (vanilla technique; Playwright/CDP is the existing harness, Apache-2.0)
backlog.note: ANTI-DUP confirmed: inspect.js:255-258 ERR_SHIM only catches resource 'error' events on img/script/link (reported at designbook-mcp/server.js:108 as 'asset 404'); render-smoke.mjs HAS page.on('response',>=400)+requestfailed but isExampleAsset() (line 224) deliberately EXEMPTS /demo/ assets so it never gates real-page liveness. NO naturalWidth>0 decode check exists; the only naturalWidth use (inspect.js:397) is an OVERSIZED check, not a decode-failed check. CSS url() liveness and decode-failure are genuinely uncovered.

backlog.name: Internal/cross-page <a href> resolution + mixed-content check (vanilla static-parse + concurrent HEAD)
backlog.what: Two net-new gate signals the headless probe alone can't see. (1) <a href> link liveness: the render harness only validates resources the browser FETCHES (img/script/link/url()) — it never navigates <a href>, so broken internal anchors (#missing-id), relative same-project page links, and dead absolute hrefs ship silently. A vanilla pass: collect every <a href>/<area href>, resolve in-page fragments against element ids, and fire a small concurrent fetch (HEAD, GET fallback for HEAD-405 hosts) with a bounded pool (e.g. 8) over the remote ones — a permissive zero-dep loop, not a SaaS crawler. (2) Mixed-content: scan the composed HTML+CSS for any http:// subresource URL (src/href/url()) — on the https Design Book preview these are blocked/insecure and are a 'renders clean' violation a same-origin headless render won't surface. Both are deterministic, fast, and fit the existing 'check -> fix' save-gate finding shape (server.js:108-130).
backlog.license: N/A (vanilla technique; uses node fetch + DOM parse)
backlog.note: ANTI-DUP confirmed: grep of inspect.js/autofix.js shows NO querySelectorAll('a')+href validation, NO mixed-content/http:// scan, NO HEAD/fetch liveness sweep. The vault's '404' hits are all error-state UI components (error-states.css, app-state-screens.css) — design, not a checker. Keep the concurrency bounded + same-origin-aware so the gate stays fast and doesn't hammer external hosts; optionally skip external hrefs by default (offline-determinism) and only HEAD them in an online mode, mirroring render-smoke's HARNESS_NET online/off split.

backlog.name: Linkinator
backlog.what: MIT, actively maintained (v7.6.1, Feb 2026, 135 releases), ESM Node broken-link checker that crawls local files OR a served dir, validates links + CSS-referenced URLs + URL fragments, with configurable concurrency (default 100) and per-status-code handling. It is the canonical PERMISSIVE reference for the crawl/concurrency/retry/back-off logic the in-harness check above needs — and a clean answer to 'what avoids the SaaS broken-link tools.' Recommend as INSPIRATION (reimplement its bounded-pool + fragment-resolution + skip-list patterns vanilla) rather than a dependency: linkinator is a full standalone crawler (heavier than the targeted single-document save-gate Design Book wants, and pulls a CDP/got stack), whereas Design Book already has the headless render to piggyback on. Note: the legacy 'broken-link-checker' npm (2k stars) is GPL-flavored-stale (last publish ~8 yrs ago) — flag as do-not-depend.
backlog.license: MIT (linkinator); broken-link-checker is stale/unmaintained — avoid
backlog.note: Use as a reference implementation, not a dep — Design Book's gate is single-document + already-has-Chrome, so a vanilla bounded-pool reimplementation (per candidate 2) is the better fit than adding a ~full crawler. Cite linkinator for the concurrency/fragment/CSS-url patterns. The mainstream Playwright recipe (document.images filtered by naturalWidth===0 + page.on('response') 4xx) is well-attested and license-free; that is what candidate 1 codifies.

backlog.name: elementFromPoint occlusion gate (WebDriver "element click intercepted" reimplemented in-page)
backlog.what: A vanilla page.evaluate() pass that, for every interactive node (button, a[href], input/select/textarea, [role=button|link|tab|menuitem|checkbox|switch], [tabindex], [onclick]/[data-*-open]), reads getBoundingClientRect, computes the center point (and 4 quarter-points for large/oddly-shaped targets), and calls document.elementFromPoint(x,y). If the returned topmost element is not the target nor an inclusive descendant of it, the control is click-blocked by a higher layer. This is exactly the WebDriver pointer-interactable-paint-tree / 'element is obscured' algorithm browsers already use to throw ElementClickInterceptedException — reimplemented client-side so it runs in the existing headless harness with zero new deps. It directly catches the #1 AI-compose failure: a decorative overlay, ::after pseudo-band, full-bleed gradient hero, or leftover modal scrim sitting over a CTA. Report the blocker's tag/class/z-index so the fix is obvious. Net-new: the current save-gate (checkCoherence) is pure static regex on HTML source and never hit-tests; render-smoke checks render+console+canvas pixels but never reachability.
backlog.license: W3C spec / public-domain algorithm (vanilla reimplementation, MIT-compatible)
backlog.note: Reimplement in-page rather than depend on Selenium/Puppeteer's native intercept (those throw only on an attempted click of one known selector; this sweeps the whole page in one evaluate). Guard against false positives from intentional pointer-events:none decorative siblings by skipping targets whose own computed pointer-events is none, and by treating a covering element whose computed pointer-events is none as transparent (walk to the next paint-tree entry via elementsFromPoint).

backlog.name: Invisible-but-present overlay (phantom scrim) detector
backlog.what: A companion DOM pass that flags elements which are effectively invisible (computed opacity 0, or visibility:hidden, or transparent background with no painted content) yet still capture pointer events (pointer-events != none) AND span a large fraction of the viewport (rect area > ~25% of viewport) sitting above interactive content in the stacking order. This is the classic 'modal closed visually but its scrim z-index:999 still swallows every click' bug — the page LOOKS fine in a screenshot (so the existing pixel/variance harness passes it) but is functionally dead. Pairs with the occlusion gate: occlusion catches 'a visible thing covers the button', this catches 'an invisible thing covers everything'. Pure vanilla: getComputedStyle + getBoundingClientRect + elementsFromPoint depth probe at a few sample points to confirm a transparent layer is intercepting before real content.
backlog.license: n/a (vanilla technique)
backlog.note: Screenshot-based gates are blind to this by definition — it's invisible — which is precisely why it deserves a dedicated DOM-level check. Allowlist legitimate full-viewport interactive layers (open dialogs/drawers whose scrim is intentional) by skipping when an [open] <dialog>, [aria-modal=true], or a known scrim class is present and active.

backlog.name: Off-screen-but-focusable / aria-hidden-focusable reachability lint (axe-core/ACT-rule reimplementation)
backlog.what: A vanilla focus-reachability sweep: enumerate the document's focusable set (the tabbable selector: a[href], button, input, select, textarea, [tabindex]:not([tabindex="-1"]), [contenteditable]), and flag two failure classes. (1) Focusable nodes positioned off-screen-but-not-hidden — rect entirely outside the layout viewport (e.g. left:-9999px, translateX(-200%), or a stuck off-canvas drawer) while NOT display:none/visibility:hidden — these are keyboard traps / phantom tab-stops. (2) The ACT-rule 6cfa84 / axe 'aria-hidden-focus' case: a focusable element inside an aria-hidden="true" subtree, which is hidden from AT but still tabbable — an invisible-focus trap. This formalizes a 'does the keyboard path actually work' dimension orthogonal to the mouse-occlusion gate, catching AI-composed pages that leave a closed off-canvas menu's links in the tab order. Reimplements the relevant axe/ACT logic in zero-dep JS so it runs in the headless harness; axe-core itself is MPL-2.0 (file-level copyleft) so we reimplement the specific rules rather than bundle it.
backlog.license: Algorithm from W3C ACT Rules (CC/public spec); reimplement vanilla. NOTE: axe-core is MPL-2.0 — do not bundle, reimplement the rule.
backlog.note: The vault's audit.js already lints STATIC CSS for removed focus outlines (focus-removed) but never checks runtime focusability/reachability of the live DOM — this is the dynamic complement. FLAG: do not depend on axe-core (MPL-2.0, file-level copyleft + ~500KB); the value here is the 3 specific rules reimplemented as a few dozen lines of vanilla, gating in the same headless pass as the occlusion checks.

backlog.name: axe-core (Deque) — WCAG audit inside the headless-Chrome save-gate
backlog.what: axe-core is the industry-standard accessibility engine. The single-file browser bundle (axe.min.js, ~560KB, exposes window.axe) is CDN-injectable into the vault's existing Playwright render-smoke.mjs save-gate via page.addScriptTag, then `await axe.run(context, {runOnly:['wcag2a','wcag2aa']})` returns a structured JSON `violations[]` array (rule id, impact serious/critical, failing nodes, target selectors, help URL). It scopes to a selector (run only the composed snippet, not chrome) and can be narrowed to high-signal rules — color-contrast, label / label-title-only / button-name / link-name (missing labels), aria-* (ARIA misuse), image-alt, html-has-lang. Worth adding because the vault's moat is its render-time verification gate (overflow / coherence / imagery floor) and it has ZERO accessibility verification today — the only a11y work is authoring-time (a11y-codemod.js focus-ring + reduced-motion) and color-SELECTION WCAG text in color.skill.md, never a runtime audit. Wire it as a new gate dimension: tally weighted violations into an 'a11y score' and block save (or warn) on serious/critical contrast/label/ARIA failures. No competing snippet library ships a verification capability like this. Implementation is ~30 lines next to the existing pixel checks; degrade gracefully (skip the gate, never block) if the CDN/script is unavailable.
backlog.license: MPL-2.0 (file-level copyleft; Exhibit-B no-copyleft-exception variant). NOTE: the brief said MIT — it is actually MPL-2.0. Still safe: CDN-injected and called read-only via axe.run(), never modified or redistributed as source, so no copyleft obligation attaches.
backlog.note: Headline net-new moat-extending gate. Scope axe.run() to the composed-page selector and runOnly high-signal tags so it scores the design, not Playwright chrome; map impact serious/critical -> block, moderate/minor -> warn. License is MPL-2.0 not MIT — flag but harmless for CDN inject.

backlog.name: Vanilla WCAG 2.1 contrast scorer (relative-luminance / 4.5:1 fail map)
backlog.what: A ~20-line zero-dep function pair (sRGB->relative-luminance + contrast-ratio) that walks rendered text nodes, reads getComputedStyle color/background, and flags pairs below 4.5:1 (3:1 for large text). This is the degraded-path / always-on companion to the axe-core gate: when axe's CDN bundle isn't loadable it still gives a real contrast verdict, and as a vanilla snippet it doubles as an in-page authoring aid. Net-new as EXECUTABLE math — color.skill.md only describes WCAG/APCA thresholds as guidance for picking colors; nothing in the vault actually computes a contrast ratio against rendered pixels. The W3C algorithm is public-domain/spec, so reimplementing it is unencumbered (unlike APCA's restricted constants).
backlog.license: Public spec (W3C WCAG 2.1 algorithm) — reimplementable, no license encumbrance.
backlog.note: Pairs with axe-core: same intent, but zero-dep and always-on so the gate never silently skips contrast when offline; also usable as a standalone authoring utility.

backlog.name: FocusTrap.init() — shared vanilla focus-trap + inert primitive
backlog.what: A ~60-line zero-dep utility (home: utils/dom.js or new utils/focus-trap.js) exposing FocusTrap.init(container,{returnFocus,initialFocus,onEscape}) -> {release()}. On init: snapshot document.activeElement, set inert (or aria-hidden fallback) on all sibling/background roots, move focus to first focusable (or initialFocus). A single keydown handler RE-QUERIES focusables on every Tab (never a stale snapshot — fixes the modals.js bug) and wraps Tab/Shift+Tab. On release(): remove inert, restore focus to the saved element. Worth adding because the vault has FIVE overlays each reimplementing partial, inconsistent focus logic — drawer.js literally comments 'focus trap basics' but ships NO trap (no Tab handling, no restore); modals.js snapshots focusables once and goes stale; command-palette.js has zero Tab trap and zero focus restore; dialog.js and celebration-screen.js each hand-roll their own copy. None use the modern inert attribute (background interaction/AT suppression). A shared primitive is reusable across every overlay genre (modal/drawer/command-palette/sheet/celebration/onboarding) and makes the engine's overlays pass WCAG 2.4.3 (Focus Order) + 2.1.2 (No Keyboard Trap) by default — table-stakes a11y the vault structurally lacks.
backlog.license: N/A (original vanilla reimplementation; modeled on focus-trap/focus-trap MIT + inert WHATWG standard)
backlog.note: Net-new. inert is baseline-supported (caniuse: all major browsers since 2023) so it can be the primary path with an aria-hidden+tabindex fallback for ancient engines. Best built into existing utils/dom.js so every overlay can need()-free import it. Should be RETROFITTED into drawer.js/modals.js/dialog.js/command-palette.js/celebration-screen.js to delete their duplicated partial logic — that consolidation is the real win.

backlog.name: Announcer.polite()/.assertive() — single persistent aria-live region primitive
backlog.what: A tiny zero-dep companion to the focus-trap: one persistent visually-hidden aria-live region mounted once at body level, with Announcer.polite(msg) / Announcer.assertive(msg) (clear-then-set so repeat messages re-announce). Verified gap: blocks/toasts.js (Toast.show/.success/.error) renders toasts visually but emits NO aria-live and NO role=status|alert, so screen-reader users never hear a toast — and there is no shared announcer anywhere. The same overlays that need a focus trap also need to announce state ('Dialog opened', 'Copied', route changes). A 25-line announcer is reusable by toasts, command-palette result counts, celebration screens, and SPA route changes, and pairs naturally with FocusTrap to make the whole overlay layer screen-reader-complete. Must use clip/sr-only (not display:none, which SRs ignore) and polite for ~90% of updates, assertive only for time-critical alerts.
backlog.license: N/A (original vanilla; pattern per MDN ARIA Live Regions + Sara Soueidan)
backlog.note: Net-new. Lower priority than FocusTrap but high leverage: also lets the existing Toast API become accessible by routing through it (Toast.show -> Announcer.polite). Reuses the existing responsive/skip-link.css .sr-only class for the hidden region.

backlog.name: Announcer.say() — vanilla ARIA live-region singleton
backlog.what: A ~1KB zero-dep JS singleton that lazily creates TWO visually-hidden live regions on first use (role=status/aria-live=polite and role=alert/aria-live=assertive), both aria-atomic=true and empty-on-load, then exposes `Announcer.say(msg, 'polite'|'assertive')`. Implements the 2025 best-practice details the vault currently lacks: clear-then-set with a microtask/rAF delay so identical consecutive messages re-announce; a small debounce/queue (~150-500ms) so a burst of rapid updates doesn't trample itself; and a `clear()`. Net-new because the vault has a `.sr-only` CSS utility (responsive/skip-link.css) but NO programmatic announcer — toasts.js, confetti.js, celebration-screen.js, achievement-popup.js, survey-flow.js and form-pack all update visually and announce nothing. One primitive instantly makes every existing dynamic component screen-reader-friendly. Reimplement the technique (don't ship the dep) as a vanilla snippet; reference impl is MIT.
backlog.license: MIT (reference impl by CalinaCristian/aria-announcer-js); reimplement as vanilla, ~60 lines
backlog.note: Reuse the existing .sr-only class from responsive/skip-link.css for the hidden regions. Footgun to encode: regions MUST be empty on load and must already exist (or wait ~2s after injection) before first text — bake lazy-create-then-defer-first-message into the snippet so it can't misfire. Expose as global `Announcer` to match the vault's IIFE-global convention (Toast, Confetti, etc.).

backlog.name: Auto-announce wiring for toasts/feedback (announce-on-show hook)
backlog.what: A tiny integration pass that calls Announcer.say() from the vault's existing feedback primitives so they speak without the author thinking about it: toasts.js (announce message+type, error->assertive else polite), achievement-popup.js / celebration-screen.js (announce the headline politely, NOT the confetti), survey-flow.js (announce 'Question X of N' + validation errors assertively), and form-pack/forms validation states (announce the inline good/warn/bad text). This is the leverage multiplier on candidate #1 — the announcer alone is inert until something calls it. Net-new: grep confirms none of these files reference aria-live or any announce call today (toasts.js only sets aria-label on its dismiss button).
backlog.license: N/A (own-code wiring against existing vault snippets)
backlog.note: Map severity->politeness once (success/info/warning=polite, error/destructive=assertive) and reuse everywhere. Keep visual confetti/animation silent; only announce the human-readable status string so AT users aren't spammed.

backlog.name: Live-region presence/wiring diagnostic (headless-Chrome a11y gate)
backlog.what: A verification capability for the save-gate: in headless Chrome, detect composed pages that mutate visible status text (toasts, validation, async 'Saved'/'Loading' swaps) but have NO live region wired — i.e. assert that if a dynamic-feedback component is present, a non-empty-on-load aria-live region exists and the Announcer was invoked. Extends the moat into accessibility, which the coherence/verify layer currently ignores entirely (grep found zero a11y-announcement awareness in mcp-server/tools). Cheap to implement: check for the announcer regions, that they start empty, and (optionally) mutation-observe that a simulated toast pushes text into them.
backlog.license: N/A (own diagnostic; builds on existing headless-Chrome harness)
backlog.note: Make it a soft/advisory check first (warn, don't hard-fail) to avoid blocking decorative pages with no dynamic status. Pairs with candidates #1 and #2 — the gate is only meaningful once the announcer + wiring exist.

backlog.name: forced-colors.css — Windows High Contrast Mode / forced-colors token layer
backlog.what: A single drop-in @media (forced-colors: active) stylesheet that restores the vault's signature visuals when the OS forces its own palette. Core moves: (1) map roles to CSS system-color keywords (Canvas/CanvasText for surfaces+text, ButtonFace/ButtonText/ButtonBorder for buttons, Field/FieldText for inputs, LinkText for links, Highlight/HighlightText for selection, GrayText for disabled, AccentColor for brand UI); (2) re-draw boundaries that vanish when gradients/glass revert by adding `border: 1px solid` (browser auto-matches to CanvasText) so gradient/mesh/aurora panels and glass cards don't merge into the flat Canvas background; (3) force decorative SVG/icon strokes to currentColor/CanvasText so they don't disappear; (4) opt out of recoloring ONLY where color is meaning (e.g. a status chip) via tightly-scoped forced-color-adjust. Net-new: the vault has dark-mode + reduced-motion + skip-link + focus-visible but ZERO forced-colors anything (grep-confirmed across all CSS/JS/MD). Directly satisfies WCAG 1.4.3 (contrast) and 1.4.11 (non-text contrast) for the ~4% of users on HCM, and specifically rescues the gradient/glass/mesh aesthetic the vault leans on — exactly the properties (background-image, box-shadow, backdrop-filter, background-clip:text) that revert in forced-colors.
backlog.license: N/A (CSS pattern from web standards / MDN; reimplement original)
backlog.note: Reimplement as original CSS — do not copy any article verbatim. Pairs with existing responsive/skip-link.css and the a11y-codemod.js tool (which currently only emits focus-visible + reduced-motion, never forced-colors). Could be auto-appended by a11y-codemod.js as a third guard.

backlog.name: Transparent-outline focus-indicator preservation trick
backlog.what: The specific technique that keeps focus rings visible in forced-colors mode: author focus as `outline: 2px solid transparent` + the real visual via box-shadow. In normal mode box-shadow shows the ring; in forced-colors the box-shadow is dropped BUT the transparent outline gets recolored to CanvasText, so a ring re-appears. This is the canonical fix for the vault's many box-shadow/glow-based focus states (buttons-fx, gradient buttons, etc.) that would otherwise become invisible-to-keyboard in HCM. The vault's existing focus rings use `outline: ... solid <hex>` or pure box-shadow glow; the hex outline survives but glow-only focus states silently break. Small, high-leverage, drops straight into the design-system focus tokens.
backlog.license: N/A (web-standards technique)
backlog.note: Best shipped as a documented rule inside forced-colors.css AND as a one-line fix the a11y-codemod.js focus block emits. Also fold a 'forced-color-adjust:none is a foot gun — scope it to the narrowest state, never the whole element' note into the design-system/structure skill (per Sarah Higley).

backlog.name: forced-colors: active verification gate (headless-Chrome HCM emulation)
backlog.what: A new diagnostic for the vault's save-gate: the verifier already drives headless Chrome, and Chromium exposes forced-colors emulation via the DevTools Protocol (Emulation.setEmulatedMedia with feature 'forced-colors':'active', the same hook Playwright's emulateMedia({forcedColors:'active'}) uses). So the gate can render any composed page twice — normal and forced-colors — and assert it survives HCM: no interactive element collapses to the same color as its background (boundary lost), focus rings still register, gradient/glass panels keep a visible border, text still meets contrast against Canvas. This is the vault's moat: a programmatic 'does it break in High Contrast Mode' check that no static snippet library has. Catches exactly the regressions the forced-colors.css layer is meant to prevent, turning an invisible a11y failure into a save-gate failure.
backlog.license: Chrome DevTools Protocol (no license issue — already a vault dependency)
backlog.note: Implement via the existing headless-Chrome harness using Emulation.setEmulatedMedia (no new dependency). Heuristic checks: compare computed bg vs border/text color of interactive nodes for 'no visible boundary'; verify focus-visible target has a non-transparent outline/border in forced-colors. Lower than the CSS layer only because it's net-new engine work, not a paste-in.

backlog.name: target-size verification rule (WCAG 2.2 2.5.8, getBoundingClientRect gate)
backlog.what: A headless-Chrome diagnostic that, in the existing save-gate, queries every interactive element (a[href], button, input, select, textarea, [role=button|link|checkbox|radio|switch|tab|menuitem], [tabindex='0'], summary, [contenteditable]) and measures getBoundingClientRect(). PASS if a 24x24 CSS-px axis-aligned square fits inside (w>=24 && h>=24). If undersized, apply the spacing exception EXACTLY as specified: center a 24px-diameter circle on each target's bounding-box center and FAIL only if that circle intersects another target's box or another undersized target's circle (center-distance < 24px between two undersized targets, or center-to-box-edge < 12px to a sized target). Honor the spec's carve-outs to avoid false positives: skip inline targets (target whose box is constrained by surrounding text line-height — detect via being inline-level inside a text run), and skip native date/color/range inputs (user-agent-controlled). Emit per-element offenders with selector + measured w/h so the agent can fix. This is mechanically verifiable, deterministic, and screenshot-independent — exactly the kind of objective render-truth check that is the vault's moat. WHY: 2.5.8 is brand-new in WCAG 2.2 (ISO/IEC 40500 adoption Oct 2025), Level AA, and the vault's gate has ZERO target-size measurement; checkCoherence is regex-only string analysis with no DOM geometry. axe-core's own target-size rule proves the getBoundingClientRect approach is the industry-standard algorithm to copy.
backlog.license: W3C Software/Document License (spec is reference; the gate code is your own, MIT-compatible)

backlog.name: axe-core target-size rule (reference algorithm for the gate)
backlog.what: Deque's open-source axe-core ships a production 'target-size' rule implementing exactly the 2.5.8 measurement + spacing-exception logic, including the 'greatest unobscured area' nuance (overlapping/obscured targets use the largest unobscured 24x24 region) and neighbor-intersection math. WORTH ADDING as INSPIRATION, not a dependency: lift the precise edge-case handling (obscured-area calc, what counts as an interactive target, how inline/UA-control exceptions are detected) into the vanilla verification rule above so it matches what real auditors flag. axe-core is MPL-2.0 (file-level copyleft) so do NOT bundle/CDN-load it into the gate — reimplement the algorithm in zero-dep JS run inside the headless page. Pulling the algorithm shape from a battle-tested implementation avoids the false-positive/false-negative pitfalls of a naive w>=24 check.
backlog.license: MPL-2.0 (file-level copyleft — reimplement, do not bundle)

backlog.name: .tap-24 / hit-area token utility (WCAG 2.2 24px floor with spacing-safe variants)
backlog.what: A small zero-dep CSS utility set scoped to the 2.5.8 24px minimum (distinct from the existing 44px HIG figures): `.tap-24 { min-block-size:24px; min-inline-size:24px }` for the size path, and `.hit-24` using a `::after { content:''; position:absolute; inset:min(-12px, calc((24px - 100%)/-2)) }` overlay so a visually-small icon control (e.g. a 16px close X) gets a >=24px hit area WITHOUT changing layout — the documented pseudo-element technique. Add a `.tap-row` helper that enforces the spacing-exception via `gap: 24px` (or padding) so adjacent undersized targets pass the circle-non-intersection test by construction. The utility and the verification rule are designed to satisfy each other: anything built with `.tap-24`/`.hit-24`/`.tap-row` provably clears the gate. NOTE on duplication: responsive/mobile-patterns.css already has `.touch-target`(44x44) and `.touch-expand::after`(inset:-8px), and the .scr-* mobile system bakes in HIG 44-50px — so a generic 'expand tap area' snippet would be duplicative; the net-new value is specifically the WCAG-2.2 24px token + the spacing-exception-by-construction `.tap-row`, which nothing in the vault expresses and which the new gate rule actively checks against.
backlog.license: MIT (original; technique is public-domain CSS)

backlog.name: RovingTabindex controller (vanilla, APG-codified) — RovingTabindex.init()
backlog.what: A single zero-dep controller that turns any container of focusable children into a single-tab-stop composite widget with arrow-key navigation, exactly per the W3C ARIA APG keyboard-interface practice. RovingTabindex.init(el, {orientation:'horizontal'|'vertical'|'both', loop, columns, selector, onActivate}) sets tabindex=0 on the active child and -1 on the rest, moves tabindex=0 + calls .focus() on Arrow/Home/End (and Up/Down/Left/Right per orientation, PageUp/Down + 2D arrows in grid mode), and returns {focus, destroy}. This is the missing engine the vault never encodes: tabs.js, menubar.js, dropdown-menu.js, combobox.js, select.js, multi-select.js, tag-input.js and range-slider.js each reimplement this loop inline, while toolbar.css and radios.css ship NO keyboard JS at all — so toolbars and custom radio groups are currently keyboard-inaccessible (WCAG 2.1.1 fail). One controller satisfies 2.1.1 across toolbars, menus, radio groups, tablists, listboxes and grids and lets the existing components delegate instead of duplicating. APG code examples are MIT (GitHub repo) / W3C Software License — safe to reimplement.
backlog.license: MIT (w3c/aria-practices repo) / W3C Software License (spec code examples) — reimplementation, not a vendored dep
backlog.note: Net-new shared engine. Ship as interactions/roving-tabindex.js with a global RovingTabindex.init(). Pairs with a short companion 'keyboard-a11y.skill.md' decision tree (which APG pattern → which roles/keys); the vault has no a11y/ARIA skill today (only incidental WCAG-contrast mentions in color.skill.md).

backlog.name: jakelazaroff/roving-tabindex (web component)
backlog.what: A single zero-dependency custom element <roving-tabindex selector="button,a" direction="horizontal|vertical|grid" columns loop> that wraps any markup and adds APG-correct roving-tabindex arrow navigation as pure progressive enhancement — no framework, no build, works on plain HTML. CDN-ready (<script type="module" src="https://unpkg.com/roving-tabindex/roving-tabindex.js">) or vendorable as one file. Role-agnostic: drop it around a toolbar, menu, tablist or grid and it just works. A great reference implementation to mirror the controller against, and shippable as-is behind a need()-guard for the declarative/markup-first path while the imperative RovingTabindex.init() covers programmatic widgets.
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: Either vendor the one file or use as the gold-reference for the vanilla controller. CDN-safe behind a need()-guard; degrades to native focusability if it fails to load.

backlog.name: makeup-roving-tabindex / eBay MIND Patterns (inspiration)
backlog.what: eBay's battle-tested vanilla roving-tabindex module and the MIND Patterns docs behind it — a mature reference for edge cases (autoReset, index wrapping, dynamic child add/remove, disabled-item skipping, RTL) that a hand-rolled controller should match. Valuable as a spec checklist for the controller's behavior, NOT as a dependency: the standalone repo was archived in 2020 and the makeup-js org is archived/deprecated; current code lives in the makeup/makeup-js monorepo but is no longer the recommended distribution.
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: FLAG: archived/unmaintained — mine for edge-case behavior (disabled-skip, dynamic items, autoReset) to harden the controller; do not ship as a dep.

backlog.name: APG-conformant 'accessible-component' compose genre (archetype kit: accordion / disclosure / combobox / tabs / dialog / menu)
backlog.what: A new compose genre that emits one of the WAI-ARIA APG interactive patterns with roles, states, and the full keyboard model baked in as a guaranteed-correct archetype (like saas/agency/deck are guaranteed-correct page skeletons). Worth adding because the vault's CURRENT widgets are NOT APG-conformant: blocks/combobox.js sets only role="listbox" and is MISSING role="combobox", aria-expanded, aria-controls, aria-activedescendant, aria-autocomplete, aria-haspopup; components/tabs.js sets aria-selected but no role="tablist/tab/tabpanel" nor aria-controls/aria-labelledby wiring; blocks/accordion-pack.css is pure-CSS with zero ARIA; components/dialog.js has a hand-rolled focus trap + Escape but no inert/background-hiding. So 'compose an accordion' today yields a slop widget. A genre that templates the APG roles + keyboard map (Down/Up/Home/End/Enter/Space/Escape, Alt+Down for combobox, arrow-roving for tabs/menu) turns accessibility into a default OUTPUT, not an afterthought — and it's net-new: the engine has page/deck/diagram genres but no interactive-widget archetype with correctness guarantees.
backlog.license: W3C Software and Document License (permissive; APG examples are reference, reimplement vanilla)

backlog.name: axe-core (Deque) — APG/WCAG conformance gate for the verify step
backlog.what: MIT accessibility engine that runs in any DOM including headless Chromium and returns a structured list of violations each tied to a WCAG/WAI-ARIA-1.2 rule. Design Book ALREADY verifies in headless Chrome (the save-gate) but its coherence check scores slop/imagery/overflow only — there is zero ARIA/a11y assertion in designbook/server.js or the frontendmaxxing mcp-server. Injecting axe.run() at the existing verify step adds an accessibility FLOOR to the moat: it asserts required-children (listbox needs option), required-attr (combobox needs aria-expanded), valid roles, name-from-content, and color-contrast — exactly the failures the current combobox/tabs/accordion widgets would trip. This is the verification half that makes the accessible-component genre trustworthy: the genre emits correct markup, axe-core PROVES it on save. It's the ACT-recognized reference implementation, 4B+ downloads, MIT, CDN/Node-loadable, new minor every 3-5 months.
backlog.license: MIT

backlog.name: inert + aria-modal background-hiding pattern for dialogs (and focus-trap hardening)
backlog.what: A vanilla-snippet upgrade for components/dialog.js. The current dialog sets aria-modal=true and traps Tab between first/last focusable controls, but the background remains in the a11y tree and reachable by screen-reader virtual cursor and by browser find — the APG dialog pattern requires the rest of the page be inert. Adding the now-baseline `inert` attribute (or aria-hidden fallback) on sibling content while a modal is open, plus restoring focus to the invoker (dialog.js already does prevFocus.focus()), closes the single most common real-world modal a11y defect. Small, zero-dep, and directly fixes an existing vault file rather than adding surface area — pairs with the dialog archetype in the genre above.
backlog.license: N/A (web platform feature)

backlog.name: Monochrome — DOM-as-source-of-truth APG component library (inspiration for the vanilla reimplementation style)
backlog.what: A no-framework library (accordion, collapsible, menu, tabs) that follows WAI-ARIA APG and targets WCAG 2.2 AA, notable for reading/writing ARIA attributes directly on the DOM with no per-component JS state — which is exactly the zero-dep, progressive-enhancement idiom the vault's vanilla snippets use. Useful as INSPIRATION (a proven architecture to mirror) for how the accessible-component genre's JS should manage aria-expanded/aria-selected/roving-tabindex by treating the rendered DOM as the single state store, so composed widgets stay correct even after the agent hand-edits markup. Verify its exact license before any code reuse; treat as a reference implementation to reimplement, not a dependency.
backlog.license: Unverified — confirm before reuse; use as reference only

backlog.name: a11y.skill.md — accessibility decision-tree skill
backlog.what: A new decision-tree skill that routes the four generation-time a11y choices the question names: (1) is an image/SVG content vs decorative -> empty alt='' + aria-hidden vs descriptive alt (W3C WAI alt decision tree); (2) does a dynamic update need a live region, and polite role=status vs assertive role=alert; (3) reduced-motion: does this effect need a flat/static fallback and what is it; (4) forced-colors safe: will this gradient/box-shadow/icon survive HCM and where to add forced-color-adjust. It sits alongside taste.skill.md and color.skill.md and makes generation pick correct a11y up front. Worth adding because the vault has 30 skills and zero for a11y; taste/art-direction skills actively produce gradient-heavy pages that fail HCM/contrast, and nothing routes these decisions today.
backlog.license: vault-authored (grounded on W3C WAI MIT-like document license + MDN CC-BY-SA prose, reimplemented as original guidance)
backlog.note: Net-new. Closest existing coverage is color.skill.md (WCAG only for color-PAIR selection, not routing) and per-snippet reduced-motion guards — neither is a decision tree. responsive/skip-link.css is the only structural a11y asset. This is the additive primary; the other three candidates are the concrete assets it should reference.

backlog.name: forced-colors / HCM hardening utility (forced-colors.css)
backlog.what: A small zero-dep CSS layer that makes the vault's gradient/glass/shadow-heavy aesthetics survive Windows High Contrast / forced-colors: a @media (forced-colors: active) block that restores visible borders on token-bordered surfaces, maps focus rings to the Highlight system color, gives icon-only buttons a CanvasText outline, and applies forced-color-adjust:none ONLY where a decorative gradient must be preserved (per Smashing/MDN guidance: use sparingly, re-check contrast). This is the single most concrete fix for the 'AI-tell gradient page fails HCM' problem the WHY-IT-MATTERS calls out. Worth adding because a grep of the ~920 snippets shows forced-colors support is essentially absent (one button effect only) — it's the biggest unguarded a11y surface in the vault.
backlog.license: MIT (vault-authored, reimplemented from MDN/Smashing/Sarah Higley public guidance)
backlog.note: Confirmed near-absent: only blocks/buttons-text-fx.css uses forced-colors today; no page-level/token-level HCM layer exists. Pairs directly with the skill's 'forced-colors safe?' branch and with the taste/ token layer.

backlog.name: Live-region announcer (live-region.js)
backlog.what: A ~40-line zero-dep utility that injects one polite and one assertive visually-hidden live region at boot and exposes Announce.polite(msg) / Announce.assert(msg), so composed flows (cart updates, form success, async/route changes, toasts) announce to screen readers without each snippet hand-rolling aria-live. Encodes the polite-status vs assertive-alert routing in code so generation just calls the right method. Worth adding because aria-live/role=status currently appears in exactly ONE template (templates/atelier); there is no reusable announcer, so any dynamic UI the engine composes is silent to AT.
backlog.license: MIT (vault-authored)
backlog.note: Not duplicative — feedback/toast and select.js components exist but none expose a shared live-region API. Realizes the skill's 'does this need a live region?' branch.

backlog.name: axe-core (gate-time a11y verification)
backlog.what: Deque's accessibility engine, run inside the existing headless-Chrome verify step (inject axe.min.js, axe.run() in page context) to add an a11y diagnostic to the save-gate: color-contrast (works in real Chrome, not JSDOM), image-alt, aria-*, and focusable-region rules — automatically catching ~57% of WCAG issues.
