Turns the a11y.skill.md from advisory into enforceable: the skill makes the right choice at generation, axe-core proves it at the gate. Worth adding because the current gate (checkCoherence) scores coherence + imagery but performs NO contrast/aria/focus check, so a11y regressions are caught at neither stage today — and verification is explicitly the vault's moat.
backlog.license: MPL-2.0 (weak copyleft — FLAG: not MIT; fine as a dev/CI verification dependency since it is injected at test time and not bundled into shipped output, but should be noted)
backlog.note: FLAG MPL-2.0 (weak copyleft) — keep as a test/CI-time injected engine, never compiled into emitted pages. color-contrast rule requires real Chromium (the gate already renders headless Chrome), not JSDOM — so it slots into the existing render-verify path, not the in-process checkCoherence.

backlog.name: WCAG 2.4.13 Focus Appearance token layer (focus-visible + outline/box-shadow + outline-offset)
backlog.what: A vanilla CSS focus-ring token system that meets WCAG 2.2 Focus Appearance (2.4.13): a 2px-solid outline (or 4px if dotted/dashed) producing focus area >= a 2px perimeter of the component, with >=3:1 contrast between focused/unfocused states. Tokens: --focus-ring-color, --focus-ring-width(2px), --focus-ring-offset(2px), --focus-ring-style(solid), driven exclusively by :focus-visible (keyboard-only, no ring on click). Net-new: the vault only has focus-visible mirroring hover on a few button packs and a skip-link reveal — there is NO systematic focus-token primitive applied across all interactive elements. Worth adding because it is a single, composable design-system primitive that upgrades every snippet's a11y for free and is mechanically verifiable.
backlog.license: N/A (W3C technique / standard CSS — public)
backlog.note: Vault's design-system.skill.md mentions 'focus ring bloom' as decoration but defines no token contract or 2.4.13 thresholds. Make it an additive token layer so existing snippets inherit it.

backlog.name: C40 two-color focus indicator (light outline + dark box-shadow, 9:1 inter-ring)
backlog.what: W3C Technique C40: layer an inner 2px solid outline in a light color (#F9F9F9) with outline-offset:0 plus an outer box-shadow 0 0 0 4px in a dark color (#193146). The two ring colors keep >=9:1 contrast WITH EACH OTHER, guaranteeing at least one band clears the 3:1 background-contrast bar on ANY solid background — so a single focus style works on light, dark, image, and gradient sections without per-section overrides. Net-new and high-value for this vault specifically because its snippets sit on aurora/mesh-gradient/shader/dark backgrounds where a single-color ring routinely fails contrast. Pair with @media (forced-colors: active){ outline: 2px solid CanvasText } for Windows High Contrast Mode.
backlog.license: N/A (W3C WAI technique — public domain guidance)
backlog.note: Directly solves the vault's busy/gradient-background contrast problem; outline (not box-shadow only) survives overflow:hidden clipping.

backlog.name: Focus Not Obscured (2.4.11) fix via scroll-padding-top
backlog.what: Set scroll-padding-top on the <html>/scroll-root equal to the sticky header height so keyboard focus auto-scroll never lands a focused element underneath a sticky/fixed header (the classic Tab-backwards-disappears-under-header failure). scroll-padding is preferred over scroll-margin because scroll-margin has Safari/caret-browsing bugs; scroll-padding is the robust cross-browser fix. As a token: --sticky-header-h drives both header height and scroll-padding-top, keeping them in sync. Net-new: vault has sticky/scroll snippets but nothing tying focus visibility to sticky offset, and no 2.4.11 coverage at all. This is the natural companion to the focus-ring token layer.
backlog.license: N/A (standard CSS)

backlog.name: Focus-appearance verification gate (ring drawn, not clipped, not obscured)
backlog.what: A headless-Chrome diagnostic that extends the existing save-gate: for each interactive element, dispatch :focus-visible (e.g. element.focus() + keyboard-origin), screenshot, and assert (a) a focus indicator actually paints (pixel delta vs unfocused state >= a threshold area approximating the 2px-perimeter rule), (b) the ring isn't clipped by an overflow:hidden ancestor, and (c) the focused element's bounding box isn't covered by any position:sticky/fixed element (2.4.11). This is the moat: it turns 2.4.13/2.4.11 from advice into a mechanical pass/fail like the existing overflow/coherence/imagery floors. Aligns with the vault's documented principle that 'screenshot is truth for did-it-draw' (memory: webgl-drawverify).
backlog.license: N/A (in-house diagnostic built on existing Puppeteer/headless-Chrome harness)
backlog.note: Highest-leverage because verification is the vault's stated moat; pairs the two token snippets above with an automated gate rather than trusting authoring discipline.

backlog.name: reduced-motion alternatives layer (motion-alt.css + motion-alt.js) — `--motion-ok` substitution token
backlog.what: A reusable CSS-variable + helper layer that SUBSTITUTES motion instead of just zeroing it, sitting alongside taste/motion.css. Core primitive: a `--motion-ok` flag set to `1` only inside `@media (prefers-reduced-motion: no-preference)` and `0` otherwise, plus blessed swap classes (e.g. `.swap-slide` resolves to a transform-slide when motion-ok, to an opacity cross-fade when not; `.swap-parallax` resolves to a static poster background). This is the canonical 2025 progressive-opt-in pattern (Comeau, web.dev, MDN): write the non-motion alternative as the BASE, layer motion on top only for no-preference users — so reduced-motion users get an equivalent fade/dissolve, not a dead jump or flash. Worth adding because the vault already has the motion-token scaffolding (motion.css/motion-profiles.js) but stops at disabling; this closes the WCAG-spirit gap with a 2-file vanilla primitive every effect snippet can opt into.
backlog.license: MIT-equivalent (technique/pattern, reimplement vanilla — Comeau hook is illustrative blog code, not a dep)
backlog.note: Net-new. Anti-dup verified: motion.css zeros durations (no substitution); `no-preference` used in only 1 vault file as a mention; no `--motion-ok`/`data-motion-alt` token exists. Ships as taste/motion-alt.css + taste/motion-alt.js, mirroring the existing motion.css/motion-profiles.js pair. Add blessed swap classes to the cohesion audit like the blessed durations.

backlog.name: cross-fade-instead-of-slide for transitions-pro.js + named-transitions (reduced-motion equivalent path)
backlog.what: An upgrade to the page/element transition controllers: today transitions-pro.js does `if (reduced()) { onSwap(); return }` — an instant cut with NO transition. The CSS-Tricks 'No Motion Isn't Always prefers-reduced-motion' guidance (and web.dev) is explicit that the right reduced-motion behavior for a slide/zoom/curtain transition is a short OPACITY CROSS-FADE, not a hard jump (a cut can be more disorienting than a fade and loses the state-change cue). Add a `reducedEffect: 'crossfade'` branch so all the named effects (slide/zoom/circle/curtain/panels…) collapse to one blessed ~120ms opacity dissolve under reduced-motion instead of snapping. Same fix applies to named-transitions.css view-transition group (already snaps to instant; a cross-fade keyframe is the accessible equivalent).
backlog.license: MIT (vanilla edit to existing zero-dep snippets)
backlog.note: alreadyInVault=false for the SUBSTITUTION behavior: the snippets exist but their reduced-motion path is 'instant jump', not 'cross-fade equivalent'. This is the concrete consumer of the motion-alt `--motion-ok` token above; bundle them.

backlog.name: static-poster fallback for scroll/parallax effects (parallax → poster, scrub → final-frame)
backlog.what: A reusable `data-rm-poster` convention + tiny init that gives the heavy scroll library a meaningful non-motion alternative. Verified gap: scroll/parallax-hero, sticky-scroll-reveal, macbook-scroll, scroll-scrub, tracing-beam, google-gemini-effect etc. have ZERO JS-level reduced-motion handling (grep: 0 matches each) — under reduced-motion they rely on the global duration-zeroing, which for scroll-scrubbed timelines means the animation is stuck at its START frame (often a blank/incomplete state), not a designed poster. Pope.tech + GSAP-matchMedia guidance: parallax/scrub should resolve to a static poster image or the timeline's FINAL/intended frame. The pattern: read `data-rm-poster` (or seek the GSAP timeline to progress=1) when `matchMedia('(prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)')`, so the reader still sees the composed end-state. Reuses the vault's existing GSAP matchMedia idiom (already in gsap.skill.md) — just standardizes it as one helper across all scroll snippets.
backlog.license: MIT (vanilla; uses already-present gsap.matchMedia pattern)
backlog.note: Net-new cross-cutting helper. The 4 existing 'static under reduced-motion' INDEX mentions are per-snippet special cases (Lottie, model-viewer, audio-viz), NOT a reusable scroll/parallax poster convention. Closes the biggest real gap: scroll effects currently freeze at frame 0, not a designed poster.

backlog.name: reduced-motion alternatives decision-tree skill (motion-a11y.skill.md)
backlog.what: A `.skill.md` that codifies WHEN to remove vs substitute vs keep motion, so the compose engine picks the right reduced-motion strategy per effect class. The web.dev / Pope.tech taxonomy maps cleanly to a decision tree: vestibular-trigger motion (parallax, large-scale slide/zoom, spin, scroll-scrub, spring overshoot) → SUBSTITUTE with fade/dissolve/poster; essential/state-conveying motion (loading spinner, error shake, carousel auto-advance) → keep but provide static equivalent (text label, manual prev/next, persistent error); tiny non-triggering motion (opacity, color, <=small-distance) → may keep. The skill tells the engine: given a snippet's tags, emit the matching `--motion-ok` swap class / poster attr, and never leave a reduced-motion user with a frozen or jumped UI. This is the 'judgment layer' that ties motion-alt.css, the cross-fade branch, and the scroll-poster helper into one policy the verifier can lint against.
backlog.license: N/A (vault-authored skill)
backlog.note: Net-new. taste.skill.md / gsap.skill.md mention reduced-motion only as 'disable'; no skill encodes the remove-vs-substitute-vs-keep decision tree. Pairs the three patterns above into a coherent, verifiable accessibility policy — and is a candidate verification/cohesion check (flag any animated snippet that has neither a `--motion-ok` swap nor a poster).

backlog.name: Accessible reorder primitive (keyboard + single-pointer move) — `interactions/accessible-reorder.js`
backlog.what: A zero-dep vanilla module that wraps any list/grid (and the vault's existing sortable.js / kanban-board.js DOM) to add the WCAG 2.5.7-compliant non-drag path: (1) per-item clickable Move up / Move down buttons = the SINGLE-POINTER alternative the SC actually requires (keyboard-only does NOT satisfy 2.5.7); (2) keyboard grab mode — focus item, Space/Enter to 'pick up', Arrow keys to move, Enter to drop, Esc to cancel — over a roving-tabindex list; (3) an aria-live='assertive' region, debounced ~100ms, announcing 'moved to position 3 of 7' (the exact pattern GitHub shipped in production, July 2024). Worth adding because the vault's three reorder primitives (sortable.js, kanban-board.js, plus the OGL drag gallery) are ALL drag-only — confirmed: grep shows only dataTransfer/dragstart handlers, zero key/aria/tabindex/live. This is a brand-new WCAG 2.2 AA criterion the whole vault currently fails, and one small primitive retrofits all of them. Must AVOID deprecated aria-grabbed/aria-dropeffect (removed in ARIA 1.1, never AT-supported).
backlog.license: MIT (own implementation; pattern per W3C G219 / public-domain technique)
backlog.note: Net-new. Closest existing files (interactions/sortable.js, components/kanban-board.js) are the drag-only code this FIXES, not duplicates. image-compare.js has keyboard but it's a 2-value slider, not list reorder. Ship as a wrapper so it upgrades existing snippets in place rather than replacing them.

backlog.name: accessibility.skill.md — WCAG 2.2 decision-tree (2.5.7 dragging, target size 2.5.8, focus-not-obscured 2.4.11)
backlog.what: A short decision-tree skill that tells the compose engine when an interaction triggers a brand-new WCAG 2.2 criterion and what the vanilla fix is. Core branch: 'Does this move content A→B by dragging?' → yes → 'is dragging essential (freehand draw/signature)?' → no → REQUIRE a single-pointer alternative (move buttons / click-to-place / two-tap), not keyboard-only. Folds in the sibling WCAG 2.2 SCs the vault also likely misses: 2.5.8 Target Size (min 24x24 CSS px, the vault has many tiny icon buttons), 2.4.11 Focus Not Obscured (sticky navbars/bottom-sheets can hide the focus ring). The vault has 29 skills and ZERO on accessibility — confirmed no a11y/wcag/inclusive skill exists; only a lone responsive/skip-link.css. High value because it converts a save-gate-able rule set into composer guidance and pairs directly with the reorder primitive above.
backlog.license: N/A (authored doc; cites W3C + GitHub Engineering Blog)
backlog.note: No accessibility skill exists in the vault's 29 *.skill.md. This is the home for the 'does a non-drag path exist?' rule and the deprecated-aria-grabbed warning.

backlog.name: Verification gate: 'drag-only without single-pointer alternative' check
backlog.what: A diagnostic for the headless-Chrome save-gate (the vault's moat): flag any composed page where an element carries draggable=true or registers dragstart/pointer-drag reordering BUT has no sibling/descendant clickable move control and no aria-live region — i.e. a 2.5.7 violation. Directly answers the WHY-IT-MATTERS verifiable question ('does a non-drag path exist?'). Cheap to implement: query draggable/dnd handlers, then assert a button[aria-label*=move]/[data-reorder] or role=button sibling exists. Also bolt on a 2.5.8 target-size sub-check (any interactive element < 24x24 CSS px). This is the only one of these that touches the verification layer, which is where the vault's defensibility lives — it turns an accessibility principle into an automatic, objective gate rather than a guideline.
backlog.license: N/A (own diagnostic)
backlog.note: Pairs with candidate #1: the gate proves the primitive was actually wired (non-drag path present), not just imported. No existing coherence/imagery/overflow gate covers a11y.

backlog.name: Contrast-audit verification gate (APCA Lc + WCAG-2 ratio) on composed pages
backlog.what: A new verification capability that walks a composed page's resolved text/background pairs and flags any below threshold, reporting BOTH the WCAG-2 ratio (4.5:1 body / 3:1 large-UI) and the APCA Lc value (Bronze: Lc 75 min body, Lc 60 non-body, Lc 45 large/heavy, polarity-aware). This is the moat move: the save-gate (checkCoherence in mcp-server/server.js) currently scores hardcoded-hex/duration/radius/slop-hover/imagery but has ZERO contrast check, so a low-contrast page renders 'clean' today. Adding a measurable contrast gate is net-new, ties into the palette token system, and is the kind of diagnostic the vault sells. WCAG-2 ratio math is already in utils/palette-generator.js; APCA's sRGBtoY+APCAcontrast core is ~30 lines and reimplementable vanilla, so no restricted dep is required.
backlog.license: Algorithm public/peer-reviewed; vanilla reimplementation avoids the restricted apca-w3 package license
backlog.note: color.skill.md DOCUMENTS APCA thresholds and palette-generator.js has WCAG-2 ratio, but neither is wired into the checkCoherence save-gate and no APCA is executable. The audit-as-gate over composed pages is the net-new piece.

backlog.name: Per-palette contrast metadata for the 61 palettes
backlog.what: Precompute and annotate each of the 61 palettes (colors/palettes.css) with a contrast matrix: for every text-role token (--fg, --muted, --accent) against every surface token (--bg, --surface), store the WCAG-2 ratio and APCA Lc, plus a pass/fail badge and a 'safe pairs' list. This lets compose_page pick provably-legible role pairings instead of trusting that '--fg hits 4.5:1' (currently only an assertion in color.skill.md, not verified per-palette). It turns the palette system from named swatches into accessibility-typed swatches and feeds the audit gate above with ground-truth.
backlog.license: N/A (generated metadata over existing vault palettes)
backlog.note: Vault has 61 palettes and a WCAG-2 contrast() helper but no stored per-palette contrast metadata. Net-new and directly leverages the existing palette token model.

backlog.name: Color.js (colorjs.io) contrast module — WCAG21 + APCA + Lightness-difference
backlog.what: MIT-licensed color library whose contrast module implements BOTH algorithms the question asks for in one place: WCAG 2.1 ratio AND APCA Lc (v0.0.98G-4g), plus Lstar lightness-difference and DeltaPhiStar. Tree-shakeable procedural API so only the contrast functions ship. This is the clean CDN-snippet dependency for the audit if a hand-rolled APCA is undesirable — notably it sidesteps the restricted Myndex apca-w3 license while still giving canonical APCA numbers. Behind a need()-guard it degrades to the existing vanilla WCAG-2 ratio.
backlog.license: MIT (permissive)
backlog.note: Strictly preferable to apca-w3 (npm), which carries a RESTRICTED 'W3 web use' license: royalty-free only for web-content contrast prediction, no commercial relicensing, carve-outs for medical/aerospace/etc. Recommend Color.js or a vanilla reimplementation over apca-w3.

backlog.name: CSS contrast-color() auto-legible text token
backlog.what: Native CSS function (Baseline Newly Available April 2026: Chrome 147, Firefox 146, Safari 26) that returns black or white for guaranteed WCAG-2 readable text against a given background: color: contrast-color(var(--brand)). This is the runtime AUTO-FIX that complements the audit gate — where the contrast audit flags a failing pair, contrast-color() (with an OKLCH round() pure-CSS fallback for older engines) self-corrects on-text color without JS. Net-new as a vault snippet/skill rule; the vault has no auto-contrast text primitive today. Caveat: WCAG-2 math only (APCA was pulled from the WCAG3 draft in 2023), so it's a fix not an audit, and pairs with the APCA gate rather than replacing it.
backlog.license: Web platform standard (no license)
backlog.note: palette-generator.js has a JS bestText() equivalent, but there is no zero-dep CSS auto-contrast token in the vault. Worth a small snippet + skill note as the auto-fix companion to the audit.

backlog.name: Semantic-outline + landmark-region gate (vanilla reimplementation of axe-core's structural a11y rules)
backlog.what: A zero-dep DOM check that runs inside the existing headless-Chrome save-gate and validates the rendered semantic structure: exactly one <h1>, no skipped heading levels (h1->h6), exactly one <main> landmark, all perceivable content inside a landmark, and repeated nav/aside/region uniquely labeled (aria-label/aria-labelledby). These are the canonical axe-core rules (page-has-heading-one, heading-order, landmark-one-main, region, landmark-unique) reimplemented from the public WCAG/ARIA-APG algorithms. Worth adding because checkCoherence() in mcp-server/server.js gates only style/authenticity (hex, durations, radius, slop-hover, imagery tells) and does ZERO DOM-semantic checks; structure.skill.md governs layout shells but never validates the outline a screen reader actually sees, and skip-link.css is a fix not a verification. Catches a common generation failure (h2->h4 jumps, two <main>, unlabeled duplicate <nav>) mechanically. axe-core itself is MPL-2.0 (copyleft) so it CANNOT be bundled — but the rules are W3C/WCAG-derived algorithms (non-copyrightable), so a vanilla reimplementation is clean. The moat: nobody else gates composed output on semantic outline.
backlog.license: Algorithm reference axe-core is MPL-2.0 (RESTRICTED/copyleft) - do NOT bundle, reimplement vanilla. The underlying WCAG 1.3.1/2.4.1/2.4.6 + ARIA-APG rules are public spec, free to reimplement.
backlog.note: Highest-value, net-new gate capability. Complements responsive/skip-link.css (a fix) by adding the missing verification. axe-core MPL-2.0 means inspiration-only for the dep; ship the rule logic as vanilla DOM walk in the existing headless-Chrome verify path.

backlog.name: html-validate unique-landmark rule (MIT algorithm reference for labeled-region check)
backlog.what: The precise, MIT-licensed reference implementation for the 'repeated landmark must be uniquely labeled' rule: when the same landmark type (nav/aside/region/form/header/footer or ARIA role equivalent) appears more than once, each must have a non-empty unique accessible name via aria-label or aria-labelledby. Unlike axe-core (MPL-2.0), html-validate is MIT, so its rule source can be read and ported freely. Worth adding as the labeled-region pillar of the verification above — it's the one rule with the most edge cases (header/footer demoted when nested in main/section, accessible-name computation), so a vetted MIT reference de-risks the reimplementation. Note: html-validate is a Node parser-based linter, not a runtime DOM check, so it's a code reference rather than a drop-in dep for the headless-Chrome gate.
backlog.license: MIT (permissive) - source readable and portable
backlog.note: Use as the MIT-clean algorithm reference for the labeled-region portion of the verification, since axe-core's version is MPL-2.0. Not a usable runtime dep (it's a build-time HTML linter), hence inspiration.

backlog.name: Heading-order / no-skipped-levels outline walk (WCAG 2.4.10 + Lighthouse 'headings-skip-levels' algorithm)
backlog.what: The specific sub-check for the heading half of the verification: walk all h1-h6 (plus role=heading + aria-level) in document order and flag any jump >1 level, missing h1, or >1 h1. This is the same algorithm Lighthouse's 'Headings don't skip levels' audit uses and is trivially deterministic from the DOM. Worth calling out as its own pillar because heading-skip is the single most common semantic failure in AI-generated pages (sections styled by eye, not by level), and it maps cleanly onto the vault's existing heading-pack.css / structure shells which emit h1/h2/h3 - the gate would catch when a composed page's visual hierarchy diverges from its heading levels.
backlog.license: Public WCAG/web.dev guidance - no license constraint on the algorithm
backlog.note: Pairs with candidate 1 as the heading-outline pillar. Mechanically verifiable, fits the headless-Chrome gate. Anchor to structure.skill.md so composed shells declare expected levels.

backlog.name: prefers-contrast token tier (contrast.css)
backlog.what: A net-new token tier that swaps the vault's existing custom-property tokens (--text, --border, --bg, --shadow-color, plus new --border-width / --title-font-weight / --decor-opacity) inside @media (prefers-contrast: more) and (forced-colors: none) for a high-contrast variant (~15:1 text, thicker/solid borders, decorative gradients flattened to flat fills) and (prefers-contrast: less) for a softened variant (~7:1, dashed outlines). The critical detail is the `and (forced-colors: none)` qualifier, which is exactly what makes this a SEPARATE tier from forced-colors (it only fires on a user-initiated contrast preference, e.g. macOS Increase Contrast, not an OS color override). Worth adding because the vault already ships dark-mode tokens in responsive/dark-mode.css and a [data-motion] motion-token tier, but has ZERO contrast tier — this slots into the exact same custom-property substrate and lets the gradient-heavy house aesthetic degrade gracefully to high-contrast on request, with no markup changes.
backlog.license: n/a (CSS technique / vault-authored, MIT-compatible)
backlog.note: Pairs with responsive/dark-mode.css token names. Gotcha (per Kilian Valkhof): on Windows prefers-contrast:more also fires under forced-colors high-contrast themes — the `and (forced-colors: none)` guard is mandatory to keep the tier distinct, which is the whole point of this addition.

backlog.name: [data-contrast] JS override (contrast-profiles.js)
backlog.what: A tiny vanilla JS controller that mirrors the prefers-contrast media-query tier onto a [data-contrast="more|less|auto"] attribute on <html>, with localStorage persistence and system-preference detection via matchMedia('(prefers-contrast: more)') — letting a user force the high/low-contrast tier independent of (or overriding) their OS setting. This is the direct analogue of the vault's existing theme-switch.js (dark-mode toggle + prefers-color-scheme + localStorage) and motion-profiles.js ([data-motion]). Worth adding because the CSS tier alone is system-driven only; an explicit in-page toggle is what production accessibility UIs ship, and the vault has the toggle pattern for theme and motion but not for contrast.
backlog.license: n/a (vault-authored, MIT-compatible)
backlog.note: Reuse the localStorage + matchMedia + dataset structure already proven in components/theme-switch.js and taste/motion-profiles.js so the three preference tiers (color-scheme, motion, contrast) share one idiom.

backlog.name: forced-colors system-color hardening (forced-colors.css)
backlog.what: A complementary snippet for @media (forced-colors: active) that makes the vault's gradient/glass/shadow-heavy components survive Windows High Contrast / OS forced-colors mode: map surfaces to system color keywords (Canvas, CanvasText, LinkText, ButtonFace, ButtonText, Field, Highlight), restore meaningful borders with `1px solid CanvasText` (since backgrounds get stripped), opt decorative-only elements out with `forced-color-adjust: none`, and ensure focus rings stay visible. This is genuinely absent from the vault and is the OTHER half of the contrast story — distinct from prefers-contrast (which the question correctly separates). Worth adding because gradient backgrounds and box-shadow borders silently vanish under forced-colors, leaving borderless/invisible UI; the modern standards-based forced-colors approach (post -ms-high-contrast deprecation, April 2024) is the correct fix.
backlog.license: n/a (CSS technique, MIT-compatible)
backlog.note: Keep separate from the prefers-contrast tier (different audience, different mechanism). System color keywords are non-customizable by design — this snippet's job is structural survival, not palette control.

backlog.name: contrast-ratio save-gate (WCAG luminance check in headless Chrome)
backlog.what: A verification capability for the existing headless-Chrome save-gate: walk visible text nodes, read computed `color` and resolved `background-color` (compositing rgba/opacity), apply the WCAG relative-luminance ratio formula (lighter+0.05)/(darker+0.05), and assert AA (4.5:1 / 3:1 large) on the default render AND that the prefers-contrast:more tier actually clears a stricter AAA-ish bar (7:1). This turns the new contrast tier from a hope into a checked guarantee and extends the vault's anti-slop moat (coherence score + imagery floor) with a measurable a11y floor. Worth adding because the vault's gradient-heavy aesthetic is exactly where low-contrast text-on-gradient slips through, and this is the one integration form competitors can't easily copy — it's a gate, not a snippet.
backlog.license: n/a (formula is public-domain W3C; implement vanilla in the existing Chrome harness)
backlog.note: Implement vanilla via getComputedStyle in page.evaluate — no axe-core/Deque dependency needed (those are heavier and some are restrictively licensed). Background resolution must composite ancestor layers + gradients, which is the hard part and where naive checkers fail.

backlog.name: export-tokens.js — DTCG 2025.10 round-trip for the vault's CSS-var token set
backlog.what: A ~150-line vanilla utility that round-trips the vault's existing CSS custom-property contract (structure.css spacing/type/radius, colors/scales.css ramps, colors/palettes.css themes) to and from a W3C DTCG 2025.10 `.tokens.json` file. EXPORT: walk `document.styleSheets[].cssRules` for `:root`/`.struct`/`pal-*` rules, read every `--*` prop via `rule.style.getPropertyValue`, then classify by name pattern into `$type` (hex→color, `8px`/`1.5rem`→dimension, `200ms`→duration, `cubic-bezier(...)`→cubicBezier, font stacks→fontFamily, `600`→fontWeight, unitless→number) and emit `{ "$type", "$value", "$description" }` grouped objects with `$type` hoisted to group level. Crucially for 2025.10, color `$value` is the new OBJECT form `{colorSpace:'srgb', components:[r,g,b], hex:'#...'}` not a bare hex string, and dimension/duration are `{value, unit}` objects — getting this right is the whole interop value. Aliases: when a var's value is `var(--blue-600)`, emit `{group.blue-600}` brace-reference instead of a literal, preserving the scales→palette→semantic indirection the vault already encodes. IMPORT: parse the JSON, dereference `{...}` aliases (with cycle detection), serialize back to a `:root{ --x: ... }` block. WHY: DTCG hit its first STABLE spec on 2025-10-28 and is now read/written by Figma, Penpot, Sketch, Framer, Style Dictionary, Tokens Studio and Terrazzo — so this single file turns every composed page into a portable artifact that opens directly in those tools, with ZERO build step. The vault already has the perfect substrate (everything is token-driven CSS vars) and currently the ONLY export is a human-readable DESIGN.md markdown — there is no machine interop path at all. This is the spine of the Export/handoff category.
backlog.license: spec is W3C Community (CC); the utility is original vanilla code, MIT-able
backlog.note: Net-new. Vault has structure.css + colors/scales.css + colors/palettes.css (the token source) and design-system.skill.md / design_system() MCP (DESIGN.md markdown export) but NO .tokens.json, no $type/$value, no export-tokens.js anywhere (verified via find). Live-import via document.styleSheets only sees same-origin sheets — for the composed-page case that's fine; note the try/catch around cross-origin cssRules. 2025.10 GOTCHA to bake in: color values are objects {colorSpace,components,hex}, NOT hex strings (that changed from earlier drafts) — emitting bare hex would fail strict validators.

backlog.name: DTCG Resolver Module 2025.10 ($sets / $modifiers / resolutionOrder) — standardized theming layer
backlog.what: The companion W3C spec that standardizes multi-context theming (light/dark, brand A/B, mobile/desktop) — the standards-track successor to Tokens Studio's de-facto `$themes`/`$metadata.tokenSetOrder` convention. A resolver doc is a small JSON sidecar: `sets` (named token collections via `sources:[{$ref}]`), `modifiers` (each a `contexts` map e.g. `{light:[...], dark:[...]}` with a `default`), and `resolutionOrder` (later entries override earlier). This maps almost 1:1 onto the vault's reality: each `pal-*` class in colors/palettes.css IS a theme context, and scales.css IS the foundation `set`. So export-tokens.js should emit a tiny `tokens.resolver.json` alongside the `.tokens.json`: scales→`sets.foundation`, each palette→a `modifiers.theme.contexts.<name>`, giving consumers a real, spec-compliant theme switch. WHY: this is what makes the export a *design SYSTEM* artifact rather than a flat dump — it carries the vault's palette/theme semantics across the boundary. Worth a short `tokens.skill.md` decision-tree: when to emit one flat file vs sets+resolver, the brace-alias rules, the color-object fidelity rule, and the validate-before-save gate.
backlog.license: W3C Community spec (draft module of 2025.10)
backlog.note: Net-new. The vault's per-page palette system (pal-* classes) is a natural fit for sets+modifiers but nothing exposes it as a resolver doc. Skill should document BOTH the standards-track resolver AND the Tokens-Studio $themes/selectedTokenSets convention (enabled/source/disabled) since real-world files in the wild still use the latter — a robust importer must read both shapes. Pair it with the export-tokens.js snippet, not as a standalone.

backlog.name: Dispersa — DTCG 2025.10 reference resolver (inspiration for the import/alias-resolve path)
backlog.what: A dependency-free TS implementation of the full DTCG 2025.10 pipeline: loads resolver docs, resolves `{...}` aliases AND JSON-Pointer `$ref`, flattens group `$extends`, does alias cycle detection, then renders to CSS custom properties / JSON / JS. It is the cleanest existing model for the hardest part of the round-trip — the IMPORT direction (dereferencing nested aliases without infinite loops, hoisting group-level `$type` down to leaves, expanding composite types like shadow/border/typography back into CSS). NOT a dependency to ship (it's a full build system, heavier than the vault wants and pulls a schema layer), but a precise blueprint to crib the ~40 lines of alias-resolution + cycle-detection logic from, reimplemented vanilla inside export-tokens.js. Validates that a zero-dep resolver is feasible and shows the exact edge cases (cycles, group inheritance, $ref vs brace-ref) the vault's importer must handle.
backlog.license: check repo (TS, appears permissive/MIT-style) — verify before any code reuse
backlog.note: Inspiration ONLY — do not add as a dep (full build-system + schema validation is exactly the Style-Dictionary-class tooling the QUESTION wants to avoid). Use it to copy the alias-resolution/cycle-detection algorithm into the vanilla utility. Confirm license in-repo before lifting any code verbatim.

backlog.name: tokens-resolve.js (DTCG Format Module 2025.10 resolver)
backlog.what: A ~150-line zero-dep resolver that ingests a standard W3C DTCG token file and flattens it to computed CSS custom properties at load time. Algorithm: (1) deep-walk the tree, treating any node with $value (or legacy `value`) as a token, prefixing names from group path -> `--group-token`; (2) resolve `{dot.path}` aliases by lookup-with-memoization, following chains until a literal, with a visited-set for circular-ref detection; (3) $type coercion to CSS strings -- color: emit `hex` or `color(srgb r g b / a)` from {colorSpace,components,alpha}; dimension/duration: `${value}${unit}` (also accept legacy bare strings like "16px"); fontFamily: join array with commas + quote multi-word names; cubicBezier: `cubic-bezier(a,b,c,d)`; (4) composite expansion -- shadow -> `offsetX offsetY blur spread color` (array -> comma list for box-shadow); border -> emit `--x-color/-width/-style` triples + a shorthand; typography -> per-axis vars (`--t-font-size`,`-line-height`,...) plus an optional `font` shorthand; gradient -> `linear-gradient(angle, color pos%, ...)`. Returns a flat {name:value} map; a 5-line apply() does el.style.setProperty per entry. This is the missing standard-file counterpart to the vault's seed-hex applyToCSS(): it lets Design Book ingest ANY DTCG export (Figma variables, Tokens Studio, Style Dictionary source) and re-theme a composed page instantly. The spec hit its first STABLE version 2025.10 on 2025-10-28, so this is current and worth pinning to.
backlog.license: Spec: W3C Community Final Spec Agreement (permissive); the resolver code is original/MIT-reimplementable
backlog.note: Net-new ingestion path. Existing applyToCSS() (utils/palette-generator.js) only writes --palette-0..N from a seed hex; design_system/apply_design_md consume a Markdown DESIGN.md, not DTCG JSON. No code in the vault parses $value/$type, resolves {aliases}, or expands composite tokens. Reimplement in vanilla (do NOT vendor a lib) to stay zero-dep; gives a deterministic, verifiable theming input the harness can screenshot-check.

backlog.name: DTCG Resolver Module (modes/themes -> one flat set)
backlog.what: Companion spec (also 2025.10) that sits above the Format Module: a small resolver config declares token SETS + MODIFIERS (e.g. theme: light|dark, density: compact|cozy), and a resolution algorithm flattens sets and applies the matching modifier contexts in order (later wins) to produce ONE flat token set for a given input like {theme:'dark'}. The vanilla add-on is tiny on top of tokens-resolve.js: pick sets/modifiers by an input object, shallow-merge in order, then run the same alias+coerce+composite pass. This is what turns 'ingest a DTCG file' into 'flip the composed page between light/dark/brand modes at runtime' -- a deterministic re-theme switch that pairs naturally with Design Book's existing theme-switch.js and View Transitions. Distinct from the format resolver: that one flattens VALUES; this one selects WHICH values per mode before flattening.
backlog.license: W3C Community Final Spec Agreement (permissive)
backlog.note: Best as a short decision-tree skill (tokens.skill.md): 'have a DTCG file? -> resolve to vars; have a resolver config? -> pick mode then resolve; want re-theme? -> swap mode + re-apply under a View Transition.' Complements, does not duplicate, the seed-hex palette generator and the DESIGN.md taste flow. Note: resolver-config support is newer/less common in exports than the base format, so guard it (degrade to single-set resolution when absent).

backlog.name: Semantic-intent token tier + brand-override convention (3-tier: primitive → intent-alias → component)
backlog.what: A net-new INTENT-alias layer that sits between the vault's flat role tokens and components. Today every .pal-* hardcodes hexes straight into ~17 roles (--bg/--accent/--ok) with ZERO var() indirection (grep: 0 var() refs in palettes.css), and components read those roles directly — so there is no place to express *intent* (--color-action, --color-critical, --surface-raised, --color-on-action) and no way to re-skin one component set across brands without rewriting palettes. The pattern: keep scales.css as Tier-1 primitives; ADD a tokens-semantic.css Tier-2 that aliases intent → role/primitive (--color-action: var(--accent); --color-critical: var(--danger); --surface-raised: var(--surface)); migrate component snippets to consume INTENT tokens. Then a brand override is just a thin file re-pointing the alias layer: [data-brand=luxe]{--color-action: var(--accent)} composes as `core + brand`. This is exactly Frost's 'multiple brands / sub-brands / white-label / seasonal' composition model. Worth adding because it unlocks true white-labeling and multi-brand re-skin on top of the existing 61 palettes without touching component CSS — the architectural gap the question names.
backlog.license: N/A (original convention; concept credit Brad Frost, CC-BY blog)

backlog.name: W3C DTCG Design Tokens Format Module 2025.10 (first stable spec) — alias + $type naming standard
backlog.what: The Oct-2025 first STABLE W3C Design Tokens spec (backed by Adobe/Google/Microsoft/Meta/Figma/Salesforce/Shopify). It standardizes curly-brace aliasing ({color.brand.primary}), $type/$value, composite types (typography/shadow/gradient), and a resolver for token sets/themes. For Design Book this is the naming + portability contract a new semantic-token skill should follow so the vault's tokens are tool-portable (Figma Variables, Style Dictionary) and not bespoke. Integration: a tokens.skill.md decision-tree that (a) defines the 3 tiers, (b) names intent tokens per DTCG conventions, (c) shows the alias→brand-override re-skin recipe, (d) maps the vault's existing --bg/--accent roles onto DTCG semantic naming. This is the 'how to name it right' half that makes the semantic tier above durable rather than ad-hoc.
backlog.license: W3C Community Group spec (open, royalty-free)

backlog.name: Multi-brand theme-override files via [data-brand] composition (core + brand + sub-brand + campaign)
backlog.what: The concrete CSS mechanism that operationalizes the semantic tier for the vault's deterministic composer: components reference ONLY semantic/intent tokens; brands are thin attribute-scoped override blocks ([data-brand=business]{--color-action: var(--gray-700)}) that re-point aliases — 'same component, same code, three results.' This extends the vault's current light/dark-only data-theme model (the sole theming primitive today) to N brands and supports STACKED layers (core + brand + sub-brand + seasonal campaign) by cascade order. For Design Book specifically it means compose_page could take a brand= param and emit a [data-brand] root, re-skinning a composed page across white-label clients without re-running snippet selection. Net-new vs. the flat one-palette-per-class model.
backlog.license: Article (concept); implementation is original vanilla CSS

backlog.name: print.css — general-purpose page-master (Chrome-honored @page + margin-box headers/footers)
backlog.what: A reusable, zero-dep print stylesheet built ONLY from what Chrome 131+ (Nov 2024) actually honors in print-to-PDF, so any composed genre can export clean PDF. Core: @page { size: A4|Letter; margin } with CSS vars (--pg-size, --pg-margin); @page :first / :left / :right for cover and mirrored binding margins; running headers/footers via margin boxes (@top-left brand, @top-right doc title, @bottom-center 'counter(page) / counter(pages)', @bottom-left date) — NOT position:running()/content:element() (confirmed broken in Chrome). Body rules: print-color-adjust:exact + -webkit- prefix, an @media print reset (hide nav/buttons, expand collapsibles, force-show link URLs after anchors). Designed to pair with the verify pipeline's existing headless Chrome (Page.printToPDF, printBackground:true). A '.pg-doc' wrapper opts a page into the master so it co-exists with screen styles. This is the missing reusable primitive — today only deck.css has print styles and they are deck-only (break-after:page, hide notes; no @page, no headers, no counters).
backlog.license: N/A (original vanilla CSS, reimplemented from public technique)
backlog.note: Net-new. deck.css @media print is deck-only (no @page/headers/counters). print-typography.skill.md covers external TOOLS (Playwright/Paged.js/WeasyPrint/Typst), not a reusable page-master snippet. CRITICAL author-time facts to bake in: (1) Chrome does NOT honor position:running()+content:element(), string-set/string(), target-counter, @footnote, or reliable named '@page chapter{}' — use margin boxes + counter(page)/counter(pages) instead. (2) HEADLESS gotcha: url() images inside @page margin boxes silently fail in headless Chrome — logos must be base64 data-URLs. (3) print-color-adjust:exact only paints backgrounds when the PDF call passes printBackground:true.

backlog.name: break-control / pagination utilities (cards, tables, figures, orphans/widows)
backlog.what: A small utility layer scoped to @media print that solves the #1 PDF-export ugliness: content slicing across page breaks. Classes/selectors: break-inside:avoid on cards/figures/blockquotes/list-items so a unit never splits across pages; break-before:page / break-after:page (.pg-break-before / .pg-break-after) for section starts; thead { display:table-header-group } + tfoot { display:table-footer-group } so long tables REPEAT their header/footer row on every page (a Chrome-honored, under-used trick); orphans:3 / widows:3 on paragraphs to kill stranded single lines; figure img { max-width:100%; break-inside:avoid } so charts/diagrams stay whole. All confirmed-honored by Chrome's print engine in 2025. Complements the page-master and directly raises the verify pipeline's PDF cleanliness for report/invoice/doc genres.
backlog.license: N/A (standard CSS, original utility set)
backlog.note: masonry.css/gallery-pack.css use break-inside for multicol layout, NOT for print pagination — different purpose, no overlap. Note orphans/widows are HINTS (Chrome may ignore when it would create a blank page). The repeating-table-header (thead display:table-header-group) is the highest-value, least-known item here.

backlog.name: PDF-handoff compose target (engine genre: docs / reports / invoices → clean PDF)
backlog.what: A new compose-genre that pairs a paginated document archetype (cover page via @page:first, body sections, data tables, a totals/summary block) with the print.css page-master so the engine can emit a 'PDF handoff' deliverable — invoices, one-pager reports, statements, proposals. The genre supplies the print-specific HTML structure (running-header source nodes, a .pg-cover, .pg-section, .pg-table, .pg-totals) that the page-master styles, and its VERIFY step renders via headless Chrome's existing Page.printToPDF (printBackground:true, format from --pg-size) then checks the PDF has the expected page count, no clipped content, and headers/footers present. This turns the vault's strong screen-composition into actual paged deliverables — a category (Export/handoff) it currently can only do for decks.
backlog.license: N/A (engine archetype built on vanilla snippet above)
backlog.note: deck.css is the only existing print/export genre and it targets 16:9 slides, not paginated A4/Letter documents. Depends on candidate #1 + #2. Pin Chromium in verify (per print-typography.skill.md guidance, pagination drifts across Chrome releases).

backlog.name: Print-readiness gate (Chrome paged-media support linter)
backlog.what: A pre-export diagnostic that scans composed CSS for paged-media features headless Chrome SILENTLY drops, and fails/warns before Design Book claims a page 'PDFs cleanly'. Detect & flag: string-set/string(), target-counter() cross-references, float:footnote, position:running() (all dropped by Chrome native -> need Paged.js), and url() images inside @page margin boxes (silently NOT fetched in headless -> only base64 data: URLs render). Also flag the headless-vs-interactive size: divergence. This is the moat: it turns 'we rendered something' into 'we verified it will PDF correctly,' which the vault's verify-with-headless-Chrome gate cannot currently assert for print. Pairs with the existing render gate.
backlog.license: N/A (original capability; sources MIT/CC-BY docs)
backlog.note: Net-new diagnostic. print-typography.skill.md lists the toolchain but has no support matrix and no gate. Detection is a static CSS scan + optional headless probe; no new dep.

backlog.name: Chrome print CSS support matrix (skill table)
backlog.what: A documented support-matrix table to fold into print-typography.skill.md: native-supported (16 margin boxes, content/counter(page)/counter(pages), named @page + page: property, :first/:left/:right, size:) vs silently-dropped (string-set/string, target-counter, float:footnote, position:running) vs the headless traps (@page url() images invisible -> base64 only; interactive Print-to-PDF ignores CSS size: while headless respects it). Each row carries a verified fallback: Paged.js for running heads/footnotes/cross-refs, base64 data URLs for margin images, position:fixed or thead/tfoot for repeating headers. Turns a half-implemented spec into a decision tree the composer can act on.
backlog.license: Docs CC-BY (Chrome) / blog posts; matrix is original synthesis
backlog.note: Augments existing skill rather than duplicating it; the matrix + fallback column is the new content.

backlog.name: Margin-box image base64-inlining helper
backlog.what: A tiny build-step pattern: any url() referenced inside an @page margin box (logos, watermark, header rules) must be inlined as a base64 data: URL, because headless Chrome silently refuses to fetch @page-referenced resources — they render invisibly with no error. The snippet reads the asset, base64-encodes it, and rewrites the @page rule before the headless pdf() call. Zero-dep, deterministic, fixes the single most common 'my PDF logo vanished only in CI' failure. Slots in as the auto-fix the print-readiness gate offers when it detects a url() in @page.
backlog.license: MIT-equivalent (original; technique is public)
backlog.note: Zero-dep Node/JS. Directly remediates a headless-only silent-drop the render gate cannot otherwise catch.

backlog.name: Cerberus email patterns (fluid + responsive + hybrid skeletons)
backlog.what: The canonical, battle-tested set of three annotated HTML email skeletons (cerberus-fluid, cerberus-responsive, cerberus-hybrid). Each is a complete, copy-pasteable shell: 600px max-width centered wrapper, MSO conditional ghost tables to constrain width in Outlook desktop (which ignores max-width), percentage cells that stack on mobile, mso-table-lspace/rspace resets, and bulletproof CTA structure. Worth adding because the vault's only sendable-email asset is email-receipt.css (a single transactional archetype, CSS-only browser preview with NO MSO/VML/ghost-table/hybrid). Cerberus is the source-of-truth skeleton the whole email-dev industry forks. Reimplement it as a compose-genre so the engine can emit newsletter/announcement/digest archetypes as real email-safe HTML with inlined styles — a net-new handoff output, not a duplicate of the receipt component.
backlog.license: MIT

backlog.name: VML bulletproof button (hybrid VML + accessible HTML CTA)
backlog.what: The standard 2025-2026 bulletproof button: a <!--[if mso]> v:roundrect VML shape (arcsize for radius, fillcolor, w:anchorlock) that Outlook desktop renders as a true clickable filled rectangle, paired with a semantic <a> styled button for every other client (Apple Mail/Gmail/web). Outlook's HTML engine still can't render padded/rounded <a> buttons in 2026, so VML remains required. The vault's email-receipt.css ships a plain <a> 'bulletproof CTA' with NO VML fallback, so the button collapses to unpadded text in Outlook desktop (7-9% B2C, 25-40% B2B opens). This is a small, self-contained vanilla snippet (HTML+MSO comment, zero deps) that plugs into the email genre and fixes the single biggest Outlook break.
backlog.license: Public technique (MIT-spirit; tutorial code, freely reusable)

backlog.name: Email dark-mode pattern (color-scheme meta + [data-ogsc]/prefers-color-scheme)
backlog.what: The cross-client dark-mode trio for email: (1) <meta name='color-scheme' content='light dark'> + <meta name='supported-color-schemes'> in <head> to opt in and stop Outlook/Gmail aggressive full-inversion; (2) @media (prefers-color-scheme: dark) overrides for Apple Mail/iOS/Outlook-app; (3) [data-ogsc]/[data-ogsb] selectors to target Outlook.com's class-rewritten dark mode, plus the rule to avoid pure #fff/#000 (which trigger partial inversion) in favor of off-whites/near-blacks. This is distinct from the vault's web-page prefers-color-scheme dark mode because the email mechanism (ogsc attribute injection, color-scheme meta, inversion avoidance) is email-client-specific and absent from email-receipt.css (its .rcpt--dark is a manual class, not client-driven). Ships as a small vanilla head+CSS snippet for the email genre.
backlog.license: Public technique (freely reusable best-practice code)

backlog.name: Maizzle (Tailwind-to-email, MSO/inline build pipeline)
backlog.what: A framework that authors emails in Tailwind utility classes and compiles to inlined, MSO-padded, table-safe HTML (handles ghost tables, CSS inlining, mso-* injection, minification). Relevant as INSPIRATION only, not a dependency: it's a Node build tool (not CDN/vanilla), so it can't run inside Design Book's deterministic compose engine. But its emitted output format and its catalog of MSO/inline transforms are the right reference for how the email compose-genre should serialize final HTML (inline styles via a juice-like step, ghost-table wrapping). Note it as the model to emulate when building the genre's export step; flag that the runtime dep is build-time Node, not browser.
backlog.license: MIT

backlog.name: Vanilla CSS-inliner (styleSheets → cssRules → specificity merge)
backlog.what: A zero-dependency browser-native inliner: iterate document.styleSheets, read each CSSStyleRule, match elements with querySelectorAll(rule.selectorText), and write declarations onto el.style ordered by computed selector specificity (so the cascade is preserved as inline style attributes). Roughly 60-100 lines of vanilla JS — no juice/premailer/WASM needed because the browser already parses the CSS and does selector matching for us. This is the load-bearing build step for the email genre: Gmail/Outlook strip or ignore <style> blocks, so composed email HTML is only send-ready once rules are flattened to style= attributes. Net-new: the vault has nothing that moves <style> rules to inline attributes (the closest hits, mesh-editor.exportCss / email-pack, are CSS-string export and inbox-UI mockups, not an inliner). Pairs with a wrapper that preserves a media-query/@font-face <style> block for clients that DO honor embedded CSS (Apple Mail).
backlog.license: N/A (original technique, MDN-documented browser APIs)
backlog.note: Caveat: cross-origin sheets throw on cssRules access — wrap in try/catch and skip. Inline-style specificity is 1-0-0-0 so !important in source rules needs explicit re-application. Reimplement, not a dependency.

backlog.name: caniemail open dataset (api/data.json)
backlog.what: The MIT-licensed machine-readable support table behind caniemail.com — api_version 1.0.4, last_update_date 2026-05-19, ~100+ HTML/CSS features × 20+ clients (Gmail, Outlook Windows/Web/macOS, Apple Mail, Yahoo, ProtonMail, Fastmail, Thunderbird…). Each entry: slug, category, keywords, and a stats matrix of support codes y/n/a/u plus notes_by_num caveats (e.g. 'web fonts unsupported in Outlook', 'border-width capped at 8px'). This is the gold asset for the email-lint pass: bundle a trimmed snapshot (the ~40 features that actually break — flex, grid, position, web fonts, background-image, css-variables, filter, transform) and the linter walks the composed DOM/CSS and emits 'will break in Outlook/Gmail' warnings sourced from real data instead of a hand-maintained blocklist. Net-new — the vault has no email-compatibility data anywhere.
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: Bundle a vendored snapshot (don't fetch at runtime — emails build offline). MIT requires attribution. Drives the 'moat' verification gate: a send-ready score per email client.

backlog.name: Gmail 102KB clipping guard
backlog.what: A tiny verification check: measure the byte length of the final inlined email HTML and warn when it crosses Gmail's clip thresholds — ~102KB desktop (the hard '[Message clipped] View entire message' cutoff, which also drops the open-tracker and strips trailing <style>), with stricter mobile floors (~75KB Android, ~20KB iOS). Best-practice gate at 80KB. Trivially vanilla: new Blob([html]).size or TextEncoder().encode(html).length, surfaced as a pass/warn/fail badge. This is a pure verification capability — the second half of the 'will this break' gate alongside the property linter, and it directly explains a real, common failure (clipped emails = broken layout + lost analytics). Net-new; nothing in the vault measures output size against a delivery limit.
backlog.license: N/A (original)
backlog.note: Inlining INCREASES byte size (style moves to every element), so the clip check must run AFTER inlining. Image bytes don't count toward the limit — measure HTML/text only.

backlog.name: VMail (le0pard/vmail)
backlog.what: A working open-source email-markup linter that parses HTML email and flags unsupported HTML/CSS by cross-referencing caniemail data, reporting which clients lack support per issue. MIT, Go compiled to WASM, runs fully client-side as a PWA. Not a drop-in dependency (Go/WASM, not vanilla/CDN-friendly), but it is the reference architecture for the lint pass: how to map a parsed declaration → a caniemail slug → an aggregated 'fails in N clients' verdict, including the web-fonts / background-image / flex / grid cases the question calls out. Worth adding as inspiration to model the linter's rule-resolution and reporting shape on a battle-tested implementation rather than inventing the mapping.
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: Go+WASM, ~heavy — NOT a vault dep. Reimplement its caniemail-slug → client-verdict mapping in vanilla JS. Confirms the approach is sound and shows the canonical rule taxonomy.

backlog.name: Token-set contrast auditor (semantic-pair contrast gate)
backlog.what: A vanilla verification module that walks a composed theme's resolved token values and checks EVERY semantic foreground/background role pair (--fg on --bg, --fg on --surface, --muted on --surface, --on-accent on --accent, --on-accent on --accent-2, semantic --ok/--warn/--danger/--info on their carriers, --border vs --surface), computing BOTH a WCAG 2.x ratio and an APCA Lc per pair, then FAILING save if any role pair drops below its floor (e.g. body Lc>=75 / 4.5:1, large/UI Lc>=60 / 3:1, non-text/border Lc>=30 / 3:1). The vault already has the EXACT token contract (design-system.skill.md lines 35-41) and per-pair WCAG math in palette-generator.js contrast(), but nothing audits a WHOLE composed theme and nothing blocks on failure. This is the missing accessibility dimension of checkCoherence() in mcp-server/server.js (which today only gates overflow + imagery). Net-new: turns scattered single-pair WCAG helpers into a theme-wide pass/fail gate keyed to the vault's own role names — directly extends the anti-slop save-gate to a11y, and a contrast-failing theme is itself an AI-slop tell.
backlog.license: N/A (original vault code; reuse existing palette-generator luminance math, add a tiny BIPM/sRGB-Y APCA-style Lc estimator)
backlog.note: WCAG path is fully reimplementable from existing palette-generator.js. For APCA: do NOT vendor apca-w3 wholesale (W3C Software License restricts use to web-accessibility scope and bars use of the 'APCA' name without correct/versioned implementation) — either pull APCA only via colorjs.io (MIT, see separate candidate) or ship the rough Lc map already documented in color.skill.md lines 130-131 (Lc60~=4.5:1, Lc75~=7:1, Lc45~=3:1) as an estimator labeled 'APCA-style', not a conformance claim.

backlog.name: Color.js (colorjs.io) — multi-algorithm contrast engine
backlog.what: MIT-licensed color library whose contrast() exposes SEVEN algorithms behind one API: WCAG21, APCA, Weber, Michelson, Lstar, and Delta-Phi-Star (polarity-insensitive perceptual). color.contrast(other, 'APCA') returns signed Lc (e.g. -110 white-on-black) and color.contrast(other, 'WCAG21') returns the ratio — so a single CDN dep powers the auditor's BOTH-models requirement without the apca-w3 license entanglement. ESM/CJS, global build at colorjs.io/dist/color.global.js, on jsDelivr/esm.sh. Best as the need()-guarded engine behind the token-contrast verification, degrading to palette-generator's built-in WCAG ratio when the CDN is absent. The vault has zero perceptual/polarity-aware contrast math today (only WCAG relative-luminance).
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: Cleanest permissive route to APCA Lc since it bundles APCA alongside WCAG21 under MIT. Wrap in a need()-guard so the auditor still runs (WCAG-only) when offline. ~30KB min — fine for a build/verify-time tool, do not ship to end pages.

backlog.name: CSS contrast-color() self-correcting --on-* tokens
backlog.what: Native CSS function (Baseline Newly Available April 2026: Chrome 147, Firefox 146, Safari 26) that returns black or white — whichever has more WCAG contrast against a given color — computed at style time before paint. Lets composed themes auto-derive --on-accent: contrast-color(var(--accent)) instead of hardcoding text-on-accent, so the theme self-corrects when an accent hue is overridden. Pairs with the auditor as the REMEDIATION half: gate flags a failing pair, contrast-color() (behind @supports) is the one-line fix the engine can emit. Limitations make the JS auditor still necessary: binary black/white only (Level 5), WCAG2.x only (no APCA), and it cannot evaluate two arbitrary colors (e.g. --muted on --surface or --border vs --surface) — exactly the pairs the auditor must check.
backlog.license: N/A (web platform feature)
backlog.note: Use behind @supports(color: contrast-color(white)) with a hardcoded --on-accent fallback. Complements, does not replace, the auditor — it only picks B/W and only for the on-accent style pairs, not muted/border/semantic pairs.

backlog.name: Build-time accessible-pair generation pattern (Style Dictionary contrast action)
backlog.what: A documented pattern (Always Twisted, design-tokens series pt.16) where a build step auto-generates a guaranteed-AA foreground token for every background token and emits warnings/failures on contrast misses — the canonical 'audit every pair at build time, not runtime' workflow that the vault's design_system() DESIGN.md generator should adopt. Worth capturing as a skill decision-tree: which role pairs MUST pass (text roles) vs SHOULD warn (decorative/border), what floor each tier uses, and how to emit a safe auto-derived --on-* fallback when a chosen pair fails — so the auditor's failures are actionable, not just blocking.
backlog.license: N/A (article/technique — inspiration to encode)
backlog.note: Encode as a short addition to color.skill.md / design-system.skill.md: the role-pair audit matrix (which pairs gate vs warn, the Lc/ratio floor per tier, auto-fallback emission). Inspiration only — Style Dictionary itself is a build dep the vanilla vault would not adopt.

backlog.name: Penpot/DTCG .tokens.json exporter (vault palette → $value/$type JSON)
backlog.what: A vanilla JS exporter that turns the vault's existing token contract (the CSS vars --bg/--surface/--border/--fg/--muted/--accent/--accent-2/--ok/--warn/--danger plus type-scale, radii, spacing, shadows from a DESIGN.md or a pal-* palette) into a Penpot-importable .tokens.json. Minimal importable shape is flat, dead-simple: each token is an object with $value (hex string for color, number/px for dimension), $type ('color'|'dimension'|'spacing'|'borderRadius'|'opacity'|'fontSize'|'fontFamily'|'fontWeight'|'shadow'|'typography'), and $description; aliases use {group.token} curly-brace refs; a filename like colors.json becomes the set name on import; a single .json works for basic import (ZIP + $themes.json/$metadata.json only needed for multi-set/themes). Worth adding because it gives Design Book its first real designer-handoff EXPORT target — the vault already HOLDS the tokens (61 palettes + the --var contract design-system.skill maps), it just can't emit them as interchange. The file is plain JSON, license-clean; only the Penpot server is AGPL (irrelevant to producing the file). Round-trips into Figma/Sketch/Framer/Style Dictionary too since they all read DTCG $value/$type.
backlog.license: MIT (our code; file format is the open W3C DTCG spec — unencumbered). Penpot SERVER is AGPL-3.0 but never touched.
backlog.note: Net-new EXPORT capability. design-system.skill.md only emits a Markdown DESIGN.md + applies CSS vars; it has no JSON token interchange. CRITICAL nuance to bake in: Penpot's own EXPORT currently emits a Tokens-Studio-flavored multi-set envelope (top-level set names, $themes.json/$metadata.json, hex color strings, 'px' dimension STRINGS, fontFamilies plural, math expressions like {spacing.small}*2) — NOT strict DTCG 2025.10 (which mandates structured color objects with colorSpace+components, typed dimension objects {value,unit}, native JSON numbers, singular fontFamily). So target the Penpot/Tokens-Studio $value-with-string-values flavor for guaranteed import; offer a strict-DTCG mode only if exporting to a pure DTCG consumer. Validate via Style Dictionary + SD-Transforms (Apache-2.0), which converts between the flavors.

backlog.name: design-tokens-handoff.skill.md (DTCG vs Tokens-Studio decision tree)
backlog.what: A decision-tree skill that documents the design-token interchange landscape so Design Book can pick the right .tokens.json flavor per destination, and explains the single biggest gotcha: 'DTCG' is two things. (1) The PROPERTY convention — DTCG uses $value/$type/$description (dollar-prefixed reserved keys), legacy Tokens Studio used bare value/type/comment; token names may not contain { } or $. (2) The FILE ENVELOPE — strict DTCG 2025.10 is a single flat file with structured color objects {colorSpace, components} and typed dimensions {value, unit} as native numbers, whereas the Tokens-Studio/Penpot envelope is multi-set (top-level set names + $themes.json + $metadata.json with tokenSetOrder/activeThemes/activeSets), hex strings, 'px' string dimensions, and math/HSL expressions. The skill maps destination -> flavor (Penpot import -> Tokens-Studio-flavored $value file/ZIP; Figma Variables / Style Dictionary build -> can take strict DTCG; CSS-var output -> Style Dictionary css/variables transform). Worth adding because the export snippet is useless without knowing which of the ~10 DTCG-adopting tools (Penpot, Figma, Sketch, Framer, Knapsack, Supernova, zeroheight...) wants which dialect — this is the 'aim correctly' moat around the exporter.
backlog.license: N/A (documentation skill); references open W3C DTCG spec + Tokens Studio docs (CC/docs) + Style Dictionary (Apache-2.0)
backlog.note: Complements (does not duplicate) design-system.skill.md: that skill is taste->parts (Markdown DESIGN.md + CSS vars); this skill is tokens->interchange (JSON handoff out to design tools). Pair the two so a DESIGN.md/palette can round-trip designer<->code. Flag for the author: Penpot SERVER is AGPL-3.0 — note it as the handoff DESTINATION, but the .tokens.json file we emit is unencumbered, so it's a deps-clean target, not a dependency.

backlog.name: DTCG .tokens.json exporter (W3C Design Tokens 2025.10) from a composed page
backlog.what: A verification/export capability: walk a composed page's resolved CSS custom properties (--color-*, --space-*, --radius-*, --font-*, type scale, durations) and emit a standards-compliant W3C DTCG `.tokens.json` file ($value/$type, with $type: color/dimension/fontFamily/fontWeight/duration/cubicBezier and alias references via {group.token}). The DTCG spec reached its FIRST STABLE version on 2025-10-28 and is the vendor-neutral interchange format that Figma (native import landing ~Nov 2026), Penpot, Sketch, Style Dictionary, and Tokens Studio all consume. Worth adding because it is the single license-safe handoff artifact: Design Book already centralizes tokens as CSS vars (design-system.skill.md), so emitting them as DTCG turns any composed page into a Figma/Penpot-importable design system with zero proprietary API. The emit is a pure JSON serialization of data the engine already owns, so it is fully vanilla-reimplementable.
backlog.license: DTCG spec is W3C Community Group (open, royalty-free); the format itself is unencumbered

backlog.name: Style Dictionary v5 (DTCG -> CSS/SCSS/JS multi-platform build)
backlog.what: Apache-2.0 Node build system (v5.5.0, 2026-06-21, very actively maintained) that accepts a DTCG `.tokens.json` as source and compiles it to CSS custom properties, SCSS, JS, iOS/Android, etc. Relevant as the canonical reference for the round-trip 'tokens -> code' direction and as a documented post-export step a user can run on Design Book's emitted .tokens.json. For Design Book itself the simple CSS-vars emit is trivially reimplementable inline (no dep needed), so the right framing is inspiration/reference rather than bundling: it validates the exact DTCG token types/aliasing Design Book should produce so the file is guaranteed to import cleanly into the broader ecosystem. Note it is Node-only (not browser/CDN), so it cannot run inside the compose runtime.
backlog.license: Apache-2.0 (permissive)

backlog.name: Tokens Studio for Figma — documented free-plugin import flow
backlog.what: MIT-licensed, free Figma community plugin (also drives native DTCG tokens in Penpot) that imports W3C DTCG / legacy tokens.json and materializes them as Figma Variables/Styles — the realistic 'get tokens INTO Figma today' path while Figma's native JSON import is still rolling out (announced for ~Nov 2026). Worth adding as a SKILL (a short decision-tree: emit DTCG -> pick import target -> Tokens Studio plugin vs native vs Penpot, with the gotchas: free Figma plan supports the plugin, alias/mode resolution, $type coverage). The plugin RUNTIME and the Figma plugin API are closed/SaaS, so this is documented-flow + inspiration, never a bundled dependency — the only reimplementable part is the .tokens.json Design Book hands off. Flag: Figma is closed SaaS; Tokens Studio also has a paid Pro/Studio SaaS tier, but the Figma import plugin core is MIT/free.
backlog.license: Plugin core MIT (free); Figma host is closed SaaS; Tokens Studio cloud/Pro is paid SaaS

backlog.name: Penpot as license-clean DTCG handoff target (MPL-2.0, self-hostable)
backlog.what: MPL-2.0 open-source design tool (v2.16.0, June 2026, very active) that natively imports W3C DTCG tokens and ships a FREE built-in Inspect/Dev-Mode (CSS/SVG/HTML handoff) with no paid Dev Mode gate, plus an MCP server. The strategically important point for this angle: it is the one handoff target that is fully license-clean and self-hostable, so Design Book's emitted .tokens.json (+ structured SVG export) can flow into a real design tool with NO closed SaaS dependency at all — unlike Figma. Worth flagging as the recommended target in the export skill (and as proof that 'export DTCG + structured SVG' is genuinely tool-agnostic, not Figma-specific). MPL-2.0 is file-level copyleft (weaker than AGPL) and only constrains Penpot's own source, not Design Book's output — safe to recommend, just don't vendor Penpot code.
backlog.license: MPL-2.0 (weak/file-level copyleft) — self-host free, no SaaS lock-in; flag MPL if ever vendoring source

backlog.name: style-guide / token-docs compose-genre (auto-introspecting theme spec page)
backlog.what: A new compose-genre that, given any vault-composed page, introspects the ACTIVE runtime theme and renders a single self-documenting style-guide page: color swatches (with hex + WCAG contrast vs --bg/--fg), the fluid type scale rendered as live specimens (display->caption with computed px from clamp), the 4/8 spacing ramp as bars, the shadow/elevation ladder as floating cards, radii samples, and motion-duration chips (animated bars at --dur-fast/base/slow). It is the rendered counterpart to the existing markdown-only design_system(palette) DESIGN.md and reuses docs-layout.css for chrome + swatches-palette.css for the color grid. Net-new because the vault has docs-layout (prose) and a static swatch grid but NOTHING that introspects a composed page's own tokens and lays them out as a visual style guide. This showcases the entire token contract (--bg/--surface/--accent/type scale/--section-y/shadows/durations) in one artifact and is the natural 'design-system docs' deliverable for the Export category.
backlog.license: N/A (original vault genre, built on existing structure.css + docs-layout.css)
backlog.note: Closest existing pieces are docs-layout.css (prose docs, no token introspection), swatches-palette.css (static color grid only), and design_system(palette) MCP (emits markdown DESIGN.md, not a rendered page). None introspect the live composed theme. This genre is the visual+exportable sibling of that markdown tool.

backlog.name: Live CSS-token introspector (getComputedStyle + styleSheets.cssRules walk)
backlog.what: A ~40-line vanilla JS utility that enumerates every --custom-property used on a page by walking document.styleSheets[].cssRules (filtering rule.style entries starting with '--'), then resolves each to its computed value via getComputedStyle(document.documentElement).getPropertyValue(name). It groups by semantic prefix (--color-*, --space-*, --shadow-*, --dur-*, --radius-*, --font-*) and emits a structured token map. This is the engine that powers the style-guide genre AND the tokens.json/theme.css export below: serialize() reads the resolved map at composition time so the export captures the EXACT theme the verifier saw render. Technique is well-documented (CSS-Tricks/Tyler Gaw) and fully vanilla; the only gotcha (cross-origin sheets throw SecurityError on cssRules) is irrelevant here since vault sheets are same-origin. Net-new: the vault has live-controls.js (Tweakpane authoring) and mesh-editor.exportCss() (one widget's CSS) but no general 'read all active tokens off the live DOM' primitive.
backlog.license: MIT-reimplementable (technique, not a dep)
backlog.note: Not duplicative of live-controls.js (that WRITES uniforms via Tweakpane) or mesh-editor.exportCss() (one component's output). This READS the whole active token state off the composed DOM and is the prerequisite for any honest 'export what actually rendered' artifact.

backlog.name: W3C DTCG tokens.json serializer (Design Tokens Format Module 2025.10)
backlog.what: Serialize the introspected token map into the now-STABLE W3C Design Tokens Community Group format (v2025.10, first stable release Oct 2025; media type application/design-tokens+json, .tokens.json extension). Each token becomes {$type, $value, $description}: colors -> {colorSpace, components, hex}, spacing -> {value, unit:'px'}, durations -> {value, unit:'ms'}, shadows -> {color, offsetX/Y, blur, spread}, typography -> composite. This makes the export round-trip into Figma, Penpot, Tokens Studio, Style Dictionary, and Terrazzo instead of being a bespoke JSON blob nobody else can read. Pairs with a flat theme.css (the resolved :root{--*} block) and the rendered theme-spec page to form the one-click 'design-system docs' triple (theme.css + tokens.json + spec.html). Net-new: the vault's design_system() emits markdown only; there is zero DTCG/standard-token-format support anywhere in the vault.
backlog.license: W3C Community spec (open, permissive) — serializer is in-house
backlog.note: Adopt the STABLE 2025.10 shape (note: color is now {colorSpace,components,hex}, dimensions/durations are {value,unit} objects, NOT bare strings — older guides show legacy string forms). Interop with Figma/Style Dictionary/Terrazzo is the payoff over a custom JSON dump.

backlog.name: Terrazzo (terrazzoapp/terrazzo) — DTCG build/codegen reference
backlog.what: MIT-licensed, actively maintained (the most DTCG-2025.10-compliant build tool, ahead of Style Dictionary on full-format support) CLI that turns DTCG tokens.json into CSS, Sass, JS/TS, JSON, and Tailwind. Worth carrying as INSPIRATION for the serializer's output shapes and as the recommended downstream tool to name in the export's README ('feed tokens.json to Terrazzo or Style Dictionary to generate iOS/Android/Sass'). NOT a runtime CDN dep (it's a Node build CLI, wrong layer for a zero-dep vault), so it should be referenced, not bundled — it validates that the DTCG export is genuinely portable and gives users a concrete next step.
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: Build-time Node CLI, not CDN/vanilla — reference it as the portability proof + downstream step, do NOT bundle. Style Dictionary (Apache-2.0) is the equally-valid alternative to name.

backlog.name: light-dark() CSS function — single-declaration bi-modal tokens
backlog.what: Browser-native CSS function: each token is declared ONCE as light-dark(lightVal, darkVal) on :root with color-scheme:light dark, and the browser picks the value from the active scheme — no [data-theme="dark"] override block, no duplicate token table. Three-state toggle (light/dark/auto) is just overriding color-scheme (e.g. html[data-scheme="dark"]{color-scheme:dark}); JS toggle = el.style.setProperty('color-scheme', mode==='auto'?'light dark':mode). This is a strictly better engine for the vault's token system: palettes.css + dark-mode.css currently maintain TWO full token declarations (light :root block + dark [data-theme] block) that drift; light-dark() collapses them to one. Worth adding as a vanilla snippet + as the modern emission mode for the design_system() token contract. Baseline 'newly available' (all evergreen browsers since May 2024, ~80%+), so ship behind a graceful fallback (see verification item).
backlog.license: n/a (web platform / CSS Color 5, royalty-free W3C spec)
backlog.note: Net-new: grep confirms zero light-dark() occurrences anywhere in the vault; responsive/dark-mode.css uses the older duplicated [data-theme]+prefers-color-scheme override pattern. Best shipped as responsive/bimodal-tokens.css PLUS upgrading the design-system.skill token contract so a palette can carry both modes in one --var. OKLCH values inside light-dark() are fine and pair with the existing colors.css OKLCH ramps.

backlog.name: @supports light-dark() progressive-enhancement fallback layer
backlog.what: The save-gate-friendly fallback wrapper that makes light-dark() safe to emit by default. Pattern: declare light-dark() tokens as the modern path, then wrap a legacy mode in @supports not (color: light-dark(#fff,#000)){ } that reproduces the vault's EXISTING dark-mode.css approach (:root light block + @media(prefers-color-scheme:dark) + [data-theme] override block). Result: progressive browsers get one-declaration bimodal tokens; old browsers degrade to the proven duplicated-token path with identical visual output. This is the missing 'graceful fallback' half the question explicitly asks for, and it is the piece that lets the headless-Chrome verifier accept light-dark() output without a coherence/regression risk. Small but load-bearing — without it the technique is unshippable as a default.
backlog.license: n/a (web platform)
backlog.note: Pairs with item 1 as the same snippet/skill. The fallback block is essentially the vault's current responsive/dark-mode.css, so reuse is high — frame dark-mode.css as the @supports-fallback tier rather than the primary tier. Headless Chrome runs a modern engine so it exercises the light-dark() path; add a verification note that the fallback tier should also be spot-rendered (color-scheme forced) if a coverage gate is wanted.

backlog.name: DTCG mode-aware token export (Resolver Module 2025.10 + Style Dictionary v4)
backlog.what: The export/handoff half: emit the vault's palette tokens as W3C DTCG JSON so a single exported artifact carries BOTH modes. Important correction to common framing — the DTCG *Format Module* 2025.10 has NO $modes key; modes are NOT inside a single token. The official mechanism is the separate **Resolver Module 2025.10**: a manifest with modifiers.theme.contexts.{light:[{$ref:'theme/light.json'}], dark:[{$ref:'theme/dark.json'}]} that resolves a base set + a per-mode set. Tool conventions in the wild: Tokens Studio 'sets', Style Dictionary 'themes' (v4 has first-class DTCG read; v5 in progress for 2025.10). For Design Book this is best as a SKILL/decision-tree that teaches design_system() to also emit (a) two DTCG color sets (light.json/dark.json with $type:'color',$value), (b) a tiny resolver manifest, and (c) a Style-Dictionary-ready folder — turning the in-vault --var contract into a portable, Figma/Token-Press-importable handoff. This is the genuine 'export quality' upgrade; the vault today has zero token-JSON export.
backlog.license: Permissive — DTCG specs are royalty-free W3C-style; Style Dictionary is Apache-2.0; Token Press (Figma) is a SaaS plugin = inspiration only, not a dep
backlog.note: Net-new and non-duplicative: design-system.skill.md defines a single-mode --var token contract and DESIGN.md markdown, but NO machine-readable DTCG/W3C JSON export and no mode resolver (grep: the only 'resolver'/'style-dictionary' hits are incidental word matches in unrelated JS + an Atlassian URL). Caveat to encode in the skill: $modes is a draft proposal, not shipped — emit Resolver-Module sets + Style-Dictionary themes (the conventions tools actually read today), and map them 1:1 to the light-dark() CSS so the exported tokens and the rendered CSS stay in lockstep.

backlog.name: token-export.js — vanilla multi-platform token transformer
