backlog.what: A dependency-free JS utility that reads the active theme's CSS custom properties (the vault already exposes --accent/--bg/--surface/--border/--fg/--muted/--radius/--section-y on .struct, plus the full pal-* and taste token set via getComputedStyle) and emits five platform-target code blocks from that one source: (1) Tailwind v4 @theme block (--accent → --color-accent, --radius → --radius-md, spacing → --spacing-*); (2) iOS Swift UIColor/CGFloat extension (hex → UIColor(red:green:blue:alpha:) normalized 0–1, dims → CGFloat); (3) Android colors.xml + dimens.xml AND a Compose Kotlin object (Color(0xFFRRGGBB), .dp); (4) React Native StyleSheet/JS object (camelCased keys, hex strings, numeric dims). This mirrors Style Dictionary's ios-swift/class.swift, compose/object, android/colors, javascript/es6 formats and Terrazzo's tailwind plugin — but as a single ~200-line browser-runnable function with NO build step, NO Node, NO npm. Worth adding because multi-platform token output is the ENTIRE value prop of Style Dictionary/Terrazzo (both heavyweight CLI/Node tools that can't run in the Design Book browser context), and it ties the vault's existing 'Drop-in for Flutter/RN translation' mobile specs (app-login.css etc.) to actual exportable code. High-leverage code-export niceness that no existing util covers (palette-generator.js makes colors; mesh-editor.exportCss() exports one gradient; neither emits platform token files).
backlog.license: original (MIT-clean reimplementation; mirrors output shapes of Apache-2.0 Style Dictionary + MIT Terrazzo, no code copied)
backlog.note: Reads source via getComputedStyle on the active .struct/pal-* node — no token JSON needed; degrades to :root if no .struct. Reference outputs verified: Swift UIColor(red:0.x,green:..,alpha:1); Compose Color(0xFFRRGGBB); <color name=..>#aarrggbb</color>; RN plain-JS object. Apache-2.0 (SD) / MIT (Terrazzo) are permissive but they are Node CLIs — cite as inspiration, ship the vanilla transformer as the dep.

backlog.name: DTCG (W3C Design Tokens Community Group) JSON format as the canonical interchange shape
backlog.what: Adopt the DTCG token JSON schema ({ "$value", "$type": "color|dimension|fontFamily|shadow", "$description" }, with {alias} references) as the optional import/export interchange format for the token transformer above. This is the open standard both Style Dictionary v4 and Terrazzo standardized on in 2024–2026, so a Design Book 'export tokens as DTCG JSON' button makes the vault's themes portable INTO the broader tooling ecosystem (Figma Variables, Tokens Studio, any real Style Dictionary pipeline) and lets a user round-trip an external token file back into a vault theme. Net-new: the vault has no token-interchange format at all today — themes only exist as CSS classes. Adding a 25-line cssVarsToDTCG()/DTCGToCssVars() pair turns the vault from a closed theming system into one that speaks the industry token standard. Lower value than the transformer itself but it is the glue that makes the export trustworthy/standards-aligned rather than bespoke.
backlog.license: W3C Community Group spec (open, royalty-free)
backlog.note: Pairs with token-export.js as its import/export interchange layer. $type maps cleanly: color→all 5 platforms, dimension→CGFloat/dp/rem/number. Implement as a vanilla helper, not a dep.

backlog.name: token-diff verification capability (vanilla DTCG .tokens.json differ + visual swatch report)
backlog.what: A browser-side, zero-dep diagnostic that loads two W3C DTCG .tokens.json files, flattens each to a canonical map keyed by dotted token path (a.b.c), and emits a structured diff: ADDED / REMOVED / CHANGED ($value old->new) / TYPE-CHANGED ($type) / DEPRECATED (new $deprecated true|false|"reason") / RENAMED (heuristic: same $value+$type, different path) / DESCRIPTION-CHANGED / EXTENSIONS-CHANGED. Renders BOTH a textual JSON/Markdown changelog AND a visual report: for color tokens, side-by-side before/after swatches with a ratio-bar; for dimension/duration tokens, old->new value chips; deprecated tokens get a strikethrough+badge row. Why net-new and why it matters: 'handoff implies versioning' — Design Book can show 'what changed in this theme' as a save-gate/verification artifact (the vault's moat is verification, and there is NO token-diff equivalent). It also forces correct DTCG authoring: the differ only works cleanly if token sets use stable ordering, $description provenance, $extensions reverse-domain namespacing, and $deprecated flags — so shipping the differ implicitly validates DTCG hygiene.
backlog.license: Public spec (W3C DTCG, CC); implementation is original vanilla JS — fully permissive
backlog.note: Diff by dotted PATH (not UUIDs) so it works on any standard DTCG file. Anti-dup: vault's changelog-popover/roadmap are display UIs, design_system() emits CSS-var DESIGN.md not .tokens.json — no token-set differ exists. Canonical-ordering matters because the spec explicitly says property ordering is NOT significant, so a raw text-diff is noisy; the tool must normalize first.

backlog.name: @adobe/token-diff-generator (Spectrum token-diff)
backlog.what: Reference implementation of exactly this idea: a CLI/library that diffs two token schema versions and reports added/deleted/renamed/deprecated/updated tokens, with CLI, JSON, and Markdown (Handlebars-templated) output and GitHub-PR-comment formatting with collapsible sections. Apache-2.0, actively maintained (v2.5.4, published 2026-03). Worth adding to Design Book as INSPIRATION for the report taxonomy and the PR-comment Markdown shape — but NOT as a runtime dep: it's a Node CLI (deps: handlebars, chalk, commander, glob, node-emoji) and its rename detection requires per-token UUIDs that only exist in Spectrum's own tokens (>= @adobe/spectrum-tokens 12.26.0), so it won't run in-browser and won't diff arbitrary DTCG files. Mine it for the changeset categories and Markdown layout, reimplement vanilla.
backlog.license: Apache-2.0 (permissive, but Node-CLI architecture + UUID dependency = not CDN-droppable)
backlog.note: Inspiration, not a dep: server/CLI-only and tied to Spectrum UUIDs. Borrow its added/deleted/renamed/deprecated/updated taxonomy and collapsible-Markdown PR-comment format for the vanilla differ above.

backlog.name: DTCG Resolver Module 2025.10 (alias + theme-mode resolution before diffing)
backlog.what: Companion spec to the Format Module that standardizes how curly-brace aliases ({color.brand.500} -> resolves to the target token's $value) and multi-mode/theme sets resolve to concrete values. Critical for a meaningful token diff: you want to diff RESOLVED values, not raw aliases, otherwise a re-pointed alias (brand.button = {brand.500} -> {brand.600}) shows as 'unchanged string mismatch' instead of the real color delta. Add as a skill/decision-tree note: 'resolve aliases (and per-mode values) on both sides, THEN diff; report both the alias-level change and the resolved-value change.' This is what makes the swatch diff truthful — the before/after swatches reflect the actual rendered color, with the alias rewrite shown as provenance.
backlog.license: W3C DTCG spec (CC / public)
backlog.note: Pairs with candidate #1: a token-diff.skill.md decision tree (resolve aliases+modes, canonicalize ordering, classify add/remove/change/deprecate/rename, render swatch+text). Resolved-value diffing is the difference between a useful changelog and a noisy one.

backlog.name: ΔE (CIEDE2000) perceptual delta for the color-token swatch diff
backlog.what: When two versions of a color token differ, a raw 'old hex != new hex' is binary; a perceptual ΔE (CIEDE2000) value classifies HOW MUCH it changed: <1 imperceptible, 1-2 just-noticeable, >2 clearly visible. In the visual swatch report, annotate each CHANGED color token with its ΔE so reviewers instantly see 'cosmetic nudge' vs 'real rebrand'. color.js (and culori) implement ΔE76/CMC/2000/ITP. For Design Book, take this as INSPIRATION/technique — the ΔE2000 formula is ~40 lines of vanilla math over OKLab/Lab (which the vault already touches via OKLCH palettes), so reimplement it inline in the differ rather than shipping a dep; it upgrades the swatch diff from 'changed/unchanged' to a graded severity badge.
backlog.license: color.js MIT; CIEDE2000 formula is public/standard (CIE)
backlog.note: Technique to reimplement, not a dep. Gives the swatch diff a severity grade (imperceptible/noticeable/major) instead of a boolean — the verification angle that makes the diff actionable for handoff review.

backlog.name: Coverage-guided mimetic variant sampling (Celestial-style fan-out)
backlog.what: The actual ALGORITHM behind 'vibe design fan-out': pick a small set of high-visual-impact axes (Structure > Content > Styling), then sample N value-combinations JOINTLY (so values stay mutually compatible = coherence) while a CoverageAnalyzer measures how much of the design space each batch covers (categorical = fraction of enum values seen; boolean = both states; numeric/string = >=3 distinct) and feeds the gaps back so the next variant fills an under-sampled region. This is precisely what turns book_variants from cosmetic reshuffles into N genuinely-different options: distinctness is MEASURED, not hoped for. Worth adding because it's the missing 'distinctness gate' counterpart to the vault's existing coherence gate.
backlog.license: arXiv preprint (technique/inspiration; reimplement — no code dep)
backlog.note: Inspiration-to-verification: implement as a book_variants distinctness gate that computes pairwise variant distance over the axes vector and rejects sets where any two variants differ only cosmetically. Complements the existing anti-slop coherence score (the moat). Not a dep.

backlog.name: variant-axes fan-out skill (ranked axes matrix + preset-snap coherence)
backlog.what: Lovable/Magic Patterns/Stitch all fan out one intent into 3-4 directions by varying a FIXED, RANKED set of axes — Lovable's explicit set: layout archetype (hero-grid/split/sidebar/masonry/bento) x type-feel (modern-tech/editorial/creative/lifestyle) x palette-mood (warm-earthy/cool-calm/bold-vibrant/soft-pastel/professional) x spacing/tone — and keep each direction coherent by 'preserving design language' per direction (never mixing two within one). The borrowable pattern for Design Book: a decision-tree skill that, given an intent, picks N points in {archetype x palette-mood x density x motion x type-feel} chosen to be maximally far apart, then SNAPS each point to one valid TastePresets bundle so coherence is guaranteed by construction. The vault already has every ingredient (taste axes, TastePresets, structure archetypes, variety_seed) but no skill that orchestrates them into a deliberate N-way fan-out — this is the net-new orchestration layer.
backlog.license: Lovable docs (closed SaaS) — pattern only, MIT-clean to reimplement
backlog.note: Lovable/Magic Patterns are closed SaaS — inspiration, not deps. Net-new = the orchestration skill (variant-axes.skill.md) that drives book_compose to emit N coherent-by-construction variants; reuses existing taste/structure/palette layers rather than adding parts.

backlog.name: Parallel-prototyping diversity principle (Dow & Klemmer, Stanford HCI)
backlog.what: The empirical WHY for fan-out and the design constraint that should shape the skill: developing multiple concepts in PARALLEL (vs serially refining one) produces measurably more divergent and higher-rated results and avoids fixation. The actionable takeaway for the variant-axes skill: generate the full set BEFORE refining any one, and require divergence across the set (which justifies the distinctness gate). Worth adding as the rationale + 'minimum pairwise divergence' rule baked into the skill's decision tree so it doesn't degenerate into one idea lightly reskinned.
backlog.license: Academic (ACM TOCHI 2010) — principle, no dep
backlog.note: Pure inspiration — supplies the divergence requirement (generate-all-then-refine, enforce minimum pairwise distance) that the variant-axes skill and distinctness gate encode.

backlog.name: axe-core (in-page WCAG/contrast self-validation gate)
backlog.what: Deque's accessibility engine — call axe.run() in-page inside the EXISTING headless-Chrome render harness, after the snippet mounts, and fold its violations into the save-gate. This is exactly the 'self-validation before presenting' loop Lovable's agent runs (browser test + inspect) and is net-new to Design Book: it computes REAL rendered color-contrast (sampling actual computed fg/bg pixels, the WCAG AA 4.5:1 / 3:1 floor), missing alt text, ARIA misuse, label-less form controls, and heading-order — none of which the vault checks. Design Book's coherence_check only flags hardcoded-hex / unblessed-duration / px-radius / slop-hover / imagery; its only contrast logic is interactive UI (color-tools-pack contrast-checker, palette-generator WCAG helper), never a gate on the composed page. Audit.js checks focus-removed and reduced-motion statically but cannot see the rendered tree. axe catches ~57% of WCAG issues automatically against the live DOM. Add as a 'a11y-gate' diagnostic: hard-fail on serious/critical contrast + alt violations, warn on the rest, with a per-snippet allow-list mirroring the existing canvas-drew allow-list pattern.
backlog.license: MPL-2.0 (file-level copyleft; used unmodified as a CDN/dev diagnostic, not redistributed-modified — safe). v4.12.1, released 2026-06-10, on jsdelivr (cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/axe-core@4.12.1/axe.min.js)
backlog.note: MPL-2.0 is weak/file-level copyleft and only triggers on modifying axe's own files; loading the unmodified CDN bundle to lint the page imposes no obligation on vault snippets. CDN-load behind need()-guard; degrade to skip if blocked. Note color-contrast rule needs a real browser (already true — the harness uses Chrome), not JSDOM.

backlog.name: Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS) gate via PerformanceObserver('layout-shift')
backlog.what: A zero-dep in-page diagnostic: register PerformanceObserver({type:'layout-shift', buffered:true}) before the snippet mounts, sum entries where !hadRecentInput, and fail the gate if CLS exceeds a threshold (e.g. >0.1 = needs-improvement). This is the 'layout-shift' check the brief flags and is the programmatic core of what Figma-agent / UI-Match style tools detect ('layout shifts, spacing errors'). It catches the classic AI-generated tells the render gate misses entirely: web-font swap reflow (FOUT jank), un-dimensioned / that push content down on load, late-injected hero banners, and reveal-on-scroll content that jumps. Design Book's harness asserts the canvas DREW and the preview is non-empty, but never measures whether the layout was STABLE while drawing — a page can pass render+coherence and still visibly jank. Pure web-platform API, no library; reuses the harness's existing rAF-settle window.
backlog.license: N/A — native browser PerformanceObserver / Layout Instability API (web standard)
backlog.note: Vanilla-reimplementable inside render-smoke.mjs; no dep. Set a generous threshold for animation-heavy snippets (intentional motion isn't layout shift, but reserve a per-snippet allow-list as with canvas-drew). Best run on compose-genre full pages where reflow matters, not on isolated component tiles.

backlog.name: Broken internal-link / dead-anchor gate (in-page href + fragment resolver)
backlog.what: The harness already hard-fails on requestfailed / HTTP>=400 for resources it actually fetches (CSS/JS/CDN), but a composed page's navigation links, in-page #fragment anchors, and that exist as markup are NOT exercised — a dead href='#pricing' pointing at a section that doesn't exist, or a relative link to a missing page, ships clean today. Add a vanilla in-page pass: collect all anchors, resolve every #fragment against document.getElementById, HEAD-probe (or route-check) same-origin hrefs, and flag / href='' placeholder-link slop (an AI-generation tell). 'Broken links' is named explicitly in the brief as a competitor auto-check (Lovable inspects console+network; link-checkers like lychee are the standalone equivalent). Implement vanilla for fragments/placeholders (zero-dep, the high-value 80%); lychee (Rust, MIT/Apache) is the inspiration/reference for the full external-URL crawl if ever needed.
backlog.license: Vanilla fragment/anchor check = no dep. Reference tool lychee is MIT/Apache-2.0 dual-licensed
backlog.note: Build the high-value half vanilla in render-smoke.mjs (fragment resolution + placeholder-href detection) — these are the AI-slop tells and need no network. Reserve external-URL probing for ONLINE mode only; lychee stays inspiration, not a bundled dep (don't ship a Rust binary in the gate).

backlog.name: Box-layout-spec compose front-door (region-records IR → deterministic snippet fill)
backlog.what: make-real and Sketch2Code both go PNG-of-canvas → VLM → single HTML file with NO structured intermediate representation (the arxiv paper explicitly says end-to-end; region classes exist only for eval). The net-new front-door for Design Book is the IR these tools deliberately skip: ingest a coarse box-layout — an ordered list of region records {role: header|nav|hero|grid-N|split|sidebar|cta|footer, box:{x,y,w,h or row/col span}, hint?} (the exact shape tldraw already serializes: x,y,w,h JSON records) — and map each region-role to the existing structure.skill section sequence + a vault snippet, then compose deterministically. This is a layout-driven path vs today's genre/intent-driven path: same ~920 snippets, but the front-door is a wireframe spec instead of a genre name. No vision model needed — the user (or an upstream sketch tool) supplies boxes; the engine fills them. Reuses dashboard-grids.css (grid-template-areas) as the placement primitive and structure.css as the backbone.
backlog.license: make-real prompt MIT (tldraw); the IR concept itself is unencumbered — reimplemented, not a dep
backlog.note: Inspiration-grade for the format; build as a real compose-genre. Vault has the placement primitives (dashboard-grids.css grid-areas, structure.css .s-grid/.s-split/.s-hero) and the role→snippet mapping (structure.skill.md) but NO front-door that takes boxes as INPUT — confirmed via grep. Deterministic + already-verifiable by the existing save-gate.

backlog.name: Region-role recognition vocabulary (make-real visual-convention decision tree as a .skill.md)
backlog.what: make-real's prompt encodes a compact, battle-tested vocabulary for reading a low-fi wireframe: 'box with an X inside = image placeholder', 'small box over a darkened/greyed area = modal dialog', 'a sidebar next to a main area = grid/flex layout', 'arrows between screens = navigation logic', 'red marks = annotations, strip them from output', 'curly/squiggly lines = body text', 'rectify hand-drawn shapes into clean components'. Captured as a decision-tree skill, this becomes the parser that turns box-roles into Design Book's section/snippet vocabulary — the missing link between a coarse spec and the structure.skill genre sequences. It also doubles as guidance for prompting a VLM front-end if one is ever bolted on. Net-new: the vault's structure.skill maps genre→sections, but nothing maps wireframe-glyph→role.
backlog.license: MIT (tldraw/make-real prompt text); reimplemented as original decision tree
backlog.note: Pairs with the compose-genre above as its parser. Complements structure.skill.md (genre→section) by adding glyph/box→role. Pure decision tree, zero deps.

backlog.name: Region-IoU layout-fidelity gate (Sketch2Code component-overlap metric as a verification capability)
backlog.what: Sketch2Code's evaluation defines 7 region classes (text-block, image, button, nav-bar, form, table, + container) and scores layout similarity by IoU (intersection-over-union) of each rendered region's bounding box against the reference — a quantitative 'did the regions land where the spec asked' score. For Design Book this is a net-new VERIFICATION gate that fits the existing headless-Chrome save-gate moat: after composing from a box-layout spec, screenshot the page, read each region's getBoundingClientRect, and IoU-compare against the requested boxes. Fails the save if header/hero/grid/footer drifted out of their requested cells — a spec-conformance check the current gate (clean render, no overflow, coherence, imagery floor) does not have. Turns the box-layout front-door into a closed loop with a measurable fidelity number.
backlog.license: Academic paper (arXiv) — metric/method is free to reimplement; no code dep required
backlog.note: Extends the existing save-gate with a spec-conformance dimension specific to the layout-spec genre. getBoundingClientRect + IoU is trivially vanilla in the headless harness — no new dependency.

backlog.name: token-snap.skill.md + checkCoherence token-snap pass (perceptual nearest-token gate)
backlog.what: A NET-NEW verification capability: extend the existing checkCoherence gate (mcp-server/server.js + tools/audit.js, kept in sync) with a 'token-snap' pass that, for every literal color/spacing/radius in composed HTML, measures DISTANCE to the nearest canonical token of the ACTIVE palette and a blessed spacing scale, then SNAPS-or-FLAGS. Today hardcoded-hex/px-radius are binary presence flags (any hex = warn) with no spacing-scale check and no snapping. The Figma/Subframe industry pattern (proven by claude2figma's 'every visual value must bind to a Variable, post-write QA verifies') is exactly this but for Figma; the vault should own the same gate for HTML. Color distance uses OKLCH ΔE (the palette ramps are already OKLCH per color.skill.md), spacing uses px-distance to a 4/8 scale. Output: per-value {literal, nearestToken, distance, action: snap|flag}. Turns the binary anti-slop hex flag into a graded 'how far off-system' score and can auto-rewrite #hex → var(--accent) when ΔE < threshold. claude2figma is the inspiration/proof (Figma-only, MCP-bound — NOT a usable dep here), reimplemented vanilla over the vault's own palette parser (parsePalettes already exists).
backlog.license: MIT (claude2figma, inspiration only) — implementation is original vanilla

backlog.name: OKLCH ΔE nearest-token matcher (vanilla utils/token-match.js)
backlog.what: The algorithmic core the snap-gate needs and the vault LACKS: a tiny zero-dep function that converts an arbitrary CSS color to OKLCH and returns the nearest token from the active palette's token set plus a perceptual distance. culori is the reference implementation (differenceCiede2000 / nearest()), but it is a dependency — reimplement just the sRGB→OKLab→OKLCH conversion + Euclidean ΔE in ~40 lines of vanilla JS (the vault already standardizes on OKLCH ramps, so OKLab Euclidean ΔE is accurate enough and far smaller than full CIEDE2000). Powers both the snap-gate above AND a new design_system/apply_design_md improvement: when matchPalette ingests an external brand's hexes it can map each to the closest repo token instead of just scoring the whole palette. The only 'nearest' code in the vault is scroll-snap position math — there is no color-distance utility at all.
backlog.license: MIT (culori, reference) — reimplemented original

backlog.name: Blessed spacing scale token set (4/8 geometric, --sp-* in structure.css + taste layer)
backlog.what: The missing canonical SET that the spacing half of the snap-gate snaps TO. The vault has blessed MOTION durations (90/120/140/160/200/260/320/480ms — enforced by unblessed-duration) and a fluid type scale, but NO discrete spacing token scale: structure.css only exposes a single fluid --gap and ad-hoc 1rem/1.5rem literals, so there is nothing to snap arbitrary margin/padding/gap px values against and no 'unblessed-spacing' check is even possible. Add a blessed geometric scale (e.g. --sp-1..--sp-9 = 4/8/12/16/24/32/48/64/96) as a token-layer addition (additive-only, like the motion tokens), then the gate can snap 13px→12px / 30px→32px and flag off-grid values. This is the direct spacing analogue of what makes Subframe/USWDS spacing 'snap to the system instead of inventing values.'
backlog.license: Public domain (USWDS reference) — original token values

backlog.name: The 16-pattern "AI Design Slop" checklist (Developers Digest) + the gstack/ui-ux-pro-max anti-pattern threshold model
backlog.what: A published, externally-validated checklist of 16 deterministic AI-UI tells with a scoring threshold (4+ patterns = 'heavy slop', 2-3 = mild, 0-1 = clean) and per-pattern detection methods. Six of its tells are NET-NEW signals the vault's coherence checker does not score today, each grep/DOM-detectable on composed HTML: (1) colored left-border on cards (`border-left:3-4px solid <hue>` — cited as 'the single most reliable AI tell'); (2) emoji used as nav/section iconography instead of SVG; (3) centered-text density (text-align:center on a high % of sections — the 'centered-card monotony'); (4) gradient-text on numbers/stats (`-webkit-background-clip:text` on metrics); (5) identical-feature-card repetition (3+ grid children with identical computed structure = hero-then-3-cards monotony); (6) banned generic-copy cadence ('Unlock the power of…', vague hero headline). Worth adding because it converts an external, citable standard into new scored signals for book_coherence — exactly the moat play — and its threshold model (count tells, gate at 4) mirrors and validates the vault's existing approach. The vault already scores hardcoded-hex, unblessed-duration, px-radius, slop-hover, and the imagery/authenticity law, so these six are non-overlapping.
backlog.license: Editorial article (heuristics are facts, not code — reimplement as deterministic checks). No license needed for the ideas.
backlog.note: Partial overlap only: taste.skill.md prose mentions 'don't center every section', 'no rainbow accents', 'no lorem' as ADVICE, but coherence_check/book_coherence does NOT machine-score colored-left-border, emoji-as-icon, centered-density, gradient-text-on-numbers, identical-card-repetition, or banned-copy. These are the addable scored signals.

backlog.name: vibecheck-slop-stopper (qinnovates) — TOML→ripgrep visual-slop rule engine
backlog.what: MIT-licensed open-source detector that compiles human-readable TOML rule stanzas into ripgrep regex at scan time: 72 rules across 11 categories, including a 10-item VISUAL slop blacklist (purple/violet/indigo gradient hex range #6366f1–#8b5cf6, >60% centered-text density, uniform 'bubbly' border-radius, generic-marketing copy, decorative glassmorphism+default-shadow combos, emoji-as-design, colored-left-border cards) with HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW confidence tiers mapped to false-positive risk, and a co-occurrence rule (only fire when 3+ banned tokens share a file) to cut false positives. Directly addable as the regex-rule design pattern for an expanded book_coherence: it proves the exact TOML→regex architecture and the confidence-tiering that the vault's audit.js (already regex-on-scrubbed-HTML) can adopt wholesale, plus its co-occurrence gating is a clean way to make the new 'AI-purple gradient' check fire only when the lavender hue AND a 135deg gradient AND no imagery co-occur (avoids dinging legit pal-royal-purple pages).
backlog.license: MIT (rules + engine). The vault should reimplement the rules in its own vanilla audit.js rather than add a Node dep; ripgrep itself is not bundleable into the zero-dep snippet model.
backlog.note: MIT and regex-based, so license-clean to reimplement. Anti-dup: vault's audit.js does the same regex-on-HTML technique but only for 4 signals; this supplies the additional rule definitions + confidence-tier + co-occurrence pattern, not a duplicate capability.

backlog.name: ThumbHash (compact image placeholder hash)
backlog.what: ~20-30 byte hash that decodes (zero-dep, ~100 lines JS) into a blurred preview encoding average color, aspect ratio AND alpha. The graceful-load answer to placeholder-gray-box syndrome: instead of a gray rect while a hero/photo loads, you show a palette-true blur-up that matches the real image's dominant tone. The vault has GradientExtract (pulls a palette FROM an already-loaded image) but nothing that produces a low-byte placeholder shown BEFORE load — that is the actual anti-gray-box mechanism agentic tools and Next/Image use. Reimplementable vanilla: decode hash -> ImageData -> canvas -> data-URL; pairs directly with book_autofill_imagery (emit a thumbhash per mflux shot so first paint is never a gray box).
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: Decode is ~100 lines, MIT, zero-dep — ship as vanilla svg/ or utils/ snippet (ThumbHash.toDataURL(hash)); encode side can run in the mflux/Sharp build step. Distinct from utils/gradient-extract.js which only samples loaded images.

backlog.name: Palette-conditioned seeded SVG scene generator (georgedoescode generative-SVG technique)
backlog.what: A reimplementable technique (random/map/spline/pointsInPath + HSL palette derivation) for generating ABSTRACT geometric/organic hero artwork deterministically from a seed, recolored from the page palette tokens. This is the true net-new gap: the vault has 12 FIXED hand-drawn duotone scenes (illustrations.js), hash->gradient avatars, and tileable CSS patterns — but no generator that emits a unique, palette-matched, full-bleed SVG hero composition (blob clusters, layered arcs, scattered geo shapes) on demand. Satisfies the art-direction imagery floor WITHOUT raster gen (instant, infinite, license-free), giving book_autofill_imagery a zero-dep fallback when mflux is unavailable. Seed it from the page slug for determinism (matches compose_page seed semantics).
backlog.license: MIT (article code; reimplement vanilla via createElementNS — no svg.js needed)
backlog.note: Reimplement as svg/generative-scene.js: GenScene.get(seed,{palette,style:'arcs|blobs|grid|confetti'}) -> themable SVG string using --gs-c1/c2/c3 from palette tokens. Distinct from illustrations.js (fixed scenes), gradient-avatar.js (square identicons), patterns-mega.css (tileable). The autofill-strategy ask in the brief.

backlog.name: GeoPattern (deterministic SVG geometric pattern from a string)
backlog.what: Generates a tileable geometric SVG background deterministically from any string (title, slug) across ~16 generators (hexagons, chevrons, plaid, overlapping rings, sine waves), with the base hue derived from the string but overridable to the page palette. Net-new vs patterns-mega.css (a FIXED set of pure-CSS patterns with no per-content seeding) and gradient-avatar.js (square avatars, not full-bleed section backgrounds). Use case: every card/section/OG-card in a composed page gets a unique-but-coherent backdrop keyed on its heading — kills the 'identical empty card grid is a wireframe' tell the art-direction law warns about.
backlog.license: BSD-2-Clause
backlog.note: BSD-2 permissive; reimplement the generator math vanilla and feed --pat palette vars so output matches tokens instead of its own hashed hue. Complements not duplicates patterns-mega.css (those are not content-seeded).

backlog.name: Lovable / v0 inline image-gen model (landscape reference)
backlog.what: Landscape data point, not a dep: Lovable ships built-in raster image-gen (since Mar 2026 with transparent-background support for icons/illustrations/UI elements) but its own docs concede it 'won't replace a dedicated tool for hero visuals or complex compositions.' This validates Design Book's positioning: pair mflux raster gen (heroes/photos, where Lovable is weak) with deterministic palette-conditioned SVG (scenes/patterns/icons — instant, infinite, on-palette, zero credits) + ThumbHash graceful loading. The combination is a moat agentic builders don't have because they lean on a single raster model and external stock (Unsplash) for everything.
backlog.license: N/A (closed SaaS — reference only)
backlog.note: SaaS-only/closed — inspiration, not a dependency. Use to frame the imagery strategy split (raster-for-heroes vs SVG-for-everything-else vs hash-for-loading) in art-direction.skill.md.

backlog.name: Per-slide layout-selection grammar (content-shape → slide archetype classifier)
backlog.what: The defining feature of 2026 deck-AI (Gamma, Alai, AI PPT 2026, Plus AI): instead of N identical bullet slides, the engine CLASSIFIES each content chunk by shape and assigns a layout archetype — 1 number → big-stat callout; 2 contrasting sets → comparison/2x2; chronology → timeline; pithy line → quote; section start → divider/agenda; 5+ flat items → bullets vs. split-media. The vault's deck genre already RENDERS 8 shells (title/bullets/twocol/stats/media/quote/cta/divider) but the shell is hand-supplied per slide in the slide spec (mcp-server/server.js DECK_SHELLS/DECK_MOD) — there is NO classifier that picks a shell from content shape, and it's missing the big-stat-callout, comparison/matrix, timeline, and agenda/TOC archetypes that deck-AI treats as standard. Net-new = a deterministic classifier skill + 3-4 new shells, turning compose-deck from 'caller picks layout' into 'engine picks layout', which is the actual deck-AI moat.
backlog.license: SaaS-only (inspiration; reimplement the rules — no dep)

backlog.name: PPTAgent — reference-clustered layout selection + content-schema editing
backlog.what: MIT, actively maintained (V2 'DeepPresenter' released 2025-12-16, 424 commits, arXiv 2501.03936). Formalizes EXACTLY the grammar the brief wants: (1) split slides into STRUCTURAL (opening/section/closing) vs CONTENT types; (2) cluster content slides and extract a per-cluster CONTENT SCHEMA of {category, description, data} (e.g. Title/Date/Image/Stat); (3) for new content, draft an outline that names the functional slide type, SELECT the matching reference layout by function, then apply edit-actions (replace text, clone/delete paragraphs, swap image) to fill it. This is the reimplementable algorithm behind a vault deck classifier — the structural-vs-content split + functional-type selection maps directly onto choosing a vault shell, and the {category,description,data} schema is a clean spec for the per-slide content model. Use as inspiration/blueprint (Python LLM agent, not a JS dep) feeding a vanilla classifier skill.
backlog.license: MIT

backlog.name: Story-beat / archetype auto-pacing (deck narrative skeleton)
backlog.what: AI PPT 2026's 'narrative engine' allocates specific STORY BEATS to each slide by deck archetype (persuasion / information / education / narrative) — the deck analog of the vault's existing per-genre web-page 'section sequence' grammar in structure.skill.md, which has NO deck equivalent. Net-new = named deck skeletons (pitch = hook→problem→solution→market→traction→ask; explainer = context→tension→reveal→so-what; report = agenda→findings→deep-dives→recommendation) that drive (a) slide ORDER, (b) which archetype each beat wants, and (c) PACING — alternating dense↔breather slides and inserting divider/section-break beats on mood changes (mirrors the existing .s-section ↔ --alt surface-alternation pacing rule). Pairs with the classifier above as a compose-genre upgrade: skeleton picks beats → classifier picks per-beat layout.
backlog.license: SaaS-only (inspiration; reimplement)

backlog.name: Vanilla Sugiyama layered-DAG auto-layout (reimplement: layering + crossing-reduction + x-assignment + orthogonal/spline routing) into the diagram compose genre
backlog.what: The vault's diagram genre (structure/diagram.css + diagrams.skill.md) currently renders ONE hand-authored SVG: .dgm-rect nodes and .dgm-edge bezier curves are placed by hand, with no automatic positioning. That means the composer can't take an arbitrary node/edge set and lay it out — it can only fill a pre-shaped template. Reimplementing the classic 4-phase Sugiyama pipeline in ~150 lines of zero-dep JS (1) break cycles via DFS, (2) longest-path/Coffman-Graham layer assignment with dummy nodes for long edges, (3) median/barycenter crossing reduction per layer, (4) x-coordinate assignment + emit orthogonal or bezier edge polylines into the existing .dgm-svg/.dgm-rect/.dgm-edge token shells. This is the missing engine: it turns the diagram genre from 'fill a fixed template' into 'compose any architecture graph deterministically', which is exactly the compose-genre contract. Plenty of MIT reference impls (dGraph, sugiyama-algorithm, sugiyama-drawings) to port from; output is clean vanilla SVG that already clears the authenticity/imagery gate.
backlog.license: Algorithm/public-domain (1981 Sugiyama paper); reference impls MIT (dGraph, auroraptor/sugiyama-algorithm)

backlog.name: Text-to-diagram grammar (Mermaid-flowchart-subset DSL: `A[Label] --> B`, `subgraph`, `LR/TD` direction) as the composer's diagram input language
backlog.what: Eraser DiagramGPT, Excalidraw AI, and Mermaid all share one move: an LLM emits a tiny diagram-as-code DSL, then a deterministic engine lays it out. The vault has no such grammar — the diagram genre takes structured template data, not prose-shaped node/edge text. Adopting a Mermaid-flowchart SUBSET as the canonical intermediate (direction header `flowchart LR`, implicit node creation on edges `A --> B`, labeled nodes `A[API Gateway]`, shaped nodes `A{Decision}` / `A((Cache))`, edge labels `A -->|reads| B`, and `subgraph ... end` containers) gives Design Book a stable, LLM-friendly text→nodes/edges parser (~80 lines of regex/tokenizer, zero deps) that feeds directly into the Sugiyama layout above. It's the 'maps prose to nodes/edges' half of the brief, and choosing the Mermaid subset means the same text also round-trips to/from real Mermaid for free. Net-new: no parser/grammar exists in the vault today.
backlog.license: MIT (Mermaid syntax reference); the parser itself is original vanilla code

backlog.name: elkjs (Eclipse Layout Kernel for JS) as a CDN need()-guarded fallback for orthogonal edge routing + port constraints
backlog.what: For the hard cases the hand-rolled Sugiyama won't nail — true ORTHOGONAL edge routing with explicit ports, nested container layout, and crossing-minimized bus routing — elkjs is the production-grade engine (the same one D2 and many editors fall back to). It's a GWT-transpiled, pure-JS, dependency-light bundle (elk.bundled.js drops in via a single <script> and exposes a global ELK) and supports algorithms 'layered' (Sugiyama with orthogonal routing + port constraints), 'force', 'mrtree', 'radial'. Fits the cdn-snippet pattern: behind a need()-guard, degrade to the vanilla Sugiyama when offline. It computes positions + edge bend-points; Design Book renders them into the existing .dgm-svg tokens, so output is still clean vanilla SVG. License caveat to FLAG: EPL-2.0 (weak/file-level copyleft, not MIT) — usable as an unmodified CDN dependency, but note it for any redistribution of modified source; the underlying GWT runtime is Apache-2.0.
backlog.license: EPL-2.0 (weak copyleft — FLAG; CDN-as-is is fine, modified-source redistribution is restricted)

backlog.name: dagre (@dagrejs/dagre) — minimal MIT directed-graph layout as the lightweight CDN layout fallback
backlog.what: If a real layout library is preferred over hand-rolling Sugiyama, dagre is the MIT-licensed, browser-ready (dagre.min.js global) directed-graph layout that most text-to-diagram tools used before ELK. You set node sizes + edges, call layout(g), and read back node {x,y} AND per-edge points[] (a polyline you stroke directly into .dgm-edge) — so it does node positioning AND basic edge routing, not just positions. It's far smaller/simpler than elkjs (good for the no-orthogonal-needed common case). Caveats to FLAG: it depends on @dagrejs/graphlib (bundle both), it does NOT do true orthogonal routing (splined/straight bend-points only), and historically had maintenance gaps — though the dagrejs org shipped v2.0.0 in Nov 2025, so it's live again. Best role: the MIT-clean default layout engine, with elkjs reserved for orthogonal/port-heavy cloud-architecture diagrams. Listed as an alternative to candidates 1/3 — pick one layout path, don't ship all three.
backlog.license: MIT (clean) — note transitive dep @dagrejs/graphlib (also MIT)

backlog.name: native-screen-transitions.css (iOS push/pop + Material 4-pattern motion grammar)
backlog.what: A vanilla CSS keyframe set implementing the NATIVE inter-screen transition grammar that the .scr-* shell only describes as 'design intent' today: (1) iOS push/pop — incoming screen slides full-width from the right while the outgoing slides ~30% left under a subtle dim+shadow overlay, ~350ms ease-out, reversed for pop; (2) Material container transform — a list row/FAB visually morphs into the detail screen; (3) Material shared-axis (x/y/z, ~30px offset, fade-cross) for forward/back steps like onboarding; (4) Material fade-through (outgoing fully fades before incoming, no spatial link) for tab switches; (5) bottom-sheet present/dismiss spring. Driven by data-attributes on .scr/.app-flow so a palette/taste re-theme carries through. Worth adding because Design Book composes mobile FLOWS but every existing transition (transitions-pro, page-transitions-2, named-transitions, View Transitions) is a WEB page-to-page effect (fade/iris/curtain/glitch/slide-stack) — none encode the native push-with-parallax-underlay, fade-through, or container-transform that separate a real app from a webpage in a frame. This is the motion half of mobile-design.skill.md that the static shell is missing.
backlog.license: Apache-2.0 (Material 3 spec as reference; CSS reimplementation is original) / iOS HIG behavior is unprotected
backlog.note: Net-new motion layer. Pair with the flow navigator below. iOS push mechanics (30% underlay slide + dim) are reimplementable from public behavior; Material timing tokens (300ms mobile / 375ms full-screen, emphasized easing cubic-bezier(0.2,0,0,1)) come from the Apache-licensed M3 spec. Must be reduced-motion guarded like the rest of the shell.

backlog.name: scr-flow.js — .scr screen-flow navigator (push/pop/replace/present stack)
backlog.what: A tiny zero-dep navigator that turns the vault's static .app-flow row of phone frames into a LIVE single-phone flow: maintains a nav stack of .scr screens and exposes Flow.push(id) / .pop() / .switchTab(id) / .present(sheetId) / .dismiss(), applying the correct native-screen-transitions.css animation per edge type (drill-down→push, tab peer→fade-through, task→sheet present) and wiring swipe-back from the left edge + drag-down-to-dismiss on sheets via the existing interactions/swipe.js. This is the 'connect screens into an interactive prototype with transitions' capability that Google Stitch shipped in Dec 2025 — but as a vanilla, ownable primitive rather than a closed SaaS. Worth adding because the shell already has the screen graph encoded in mobile-design.skill.md (tabs=roots, drill=push, task=sheet) yet nothing executes it: today .app-flow only lays phones side-by-side to SEE a flow, it can't PLAY one. A navigator lets Design Book's verifier actually exercise and screenshot a flow's transitions, not just its frozen screens.
backlog.license: MIT (original, behavior modeled on UINavigationController/Expo Router semantics which are unprotected)
backlog.note: Net-new. Direct analogue to Stitch Prototypes (closed/SaaS — inspiration only). Depends on the native-screen-transitions.css above. Slots under mobile-design.skill.md as the runtime that makes the documented graph executable + verifiable.

backlog.name: Mobile-flow compose-genre enrichment (executable flow archetype, not just static screens)
backlog.what: Extend the compose engine so a 'mobile flow' brief emits a CONNECTED, playable flow (entry screen + nav stack + tab roots + sheets, each edge assigned a native transition) rather than a static row of independent phone frames — i.e. promote mobile-design.skill.md's 7 documented archetypes (onboarding, social/feed, commerce, health, finance, productivity, media) from prose into engine-composable graphs that auto-pick the transition per edge (push for drill, fade-through for tab, sheet-present for task). This mirrors Stitch's Dec-2025 'auto-generate the logical next screen + define transitions' and Uizard Autodesigner's 'multi-screen interactive prototype from a prompt' — both closed SaaS, so this is the ownable, deterministic, verifier-gated version. Worth adding because Design Book's stated job is composing mobile flows; the taxonomy already exists in the skill, but the engine can't yet wire the screens into a transition-aware graph for the coherence gate to score as a real flow.
backlog.license: N/A (engine capability; inspiration from Stitch/Uizard which are closed SaaS)
backlog.note: Depends on native-screen-transitions.css + scr-flow.js. The 7 screen archetypes themselves are NOT net-new (already in mobile-design.skill.md) — what's net-new is making them an executable, transition-wired, verifier-scorable genre. Stitch/Uizard noted as inspiration, not deps.

backlog.name: responsive-soundness gate (multi-viewport overflow probe)
backlog.what: A headless-Chrome verification capability that renders the composed page at a sweep of widths (360/390/412/768/1280) and flags horizontal overflow via the canonical DOM probe: Array.from(document.querySelectorAll('*')).filter(el => el.scrollWidth > el.clientWidth).map(el => ({tag, class, overflow: el.scrollWidth - el.clientWidth})). It also asserts grid reflow actually happened (e.g. computed grid-template-columns collapses toward 1 track at 360px, and no card child stays wider than its column). Net-new because the current save-gate/coherence_check is a STATIC string scanner with ZERO scrollWidth/clientWidth/setViewport logic anywhere in the repo — it checks one viewport at most. Worth adding: 'survives 360px without overflow + grid reflows' is the single highest-leverage diagnostic for catching the most common AI-generated layout failure, and it is a pure moat (a gate competitors' export tools don't run).
backlog.license: MIT (technique; reimplement in own harness)
backlog.note: Reimplement as a vanilla probe inside the existing headless renderer; whitelist deliberate scrollers via [data-scroll-x]/overflow-x:auto (e.g. .table-wrap, <pre>) so intentional scroll is not flagged. The harness today (mcp-server/server.js coherence_check) does no rendering — this is additive, not a rewrite.

backlog.name: container-relative fluid type (clamp + cqi/cqw units)
backlog.what: A small CSS snippet pack for INTRINSIC component typography/spacing that scales to the CONTAINER, not the viewport: font-size: clamp(14px, 10px + 1.33cqi, 20px) and padding: clamp(.5rem, 10cqi, 1.5rem), on an inline-size container. Net-new because the vault's container-queries.css uses only fixed @container min-width thresholds (500/800px steps) and fluid-type.css uses viewport-relative clamp — a grep for cqi/cqw/cqh across the whole vault returns ZERO hits. cqi-based clamp gives a card that reads correctly in a 280px sidebar AND a 900px hero with no breakpoints, which is exactly the 'intrinsic layout' the question asks about. Pairs naturally with the existing .container-inline.
backlog.license: MIT (CSS pattern, reimplementable)
backlog.note: Add as a few utilities to fluid-type.css or container-queries.css. Gotcha to encode: don't make a grid item itself the container (wrap it); custom properties don't work inside @container conditions.

backlog.name: axe-core (Deque) injected into the existing headless probe
backlog.what: Deque's axe-core is the industry-standard accessibility rules engine: ~90+ WCAG 2.0/2.1/2.2 A/AA/AAA + best-practice rules covering exactly the categories Design Book's hand-rolled inspect.js probe does NOT have — ARIA validity (invalid/forbidden/required attributes, role-vs-allowed-attribute mismatches, aria-* referencing missing ids), required-children/parent roles, accessible-name computation, list/landmark structure, and a `tabindex>0` (broken focus-order) rule. It injects exactly the way inspect.js already works: drop axe.min.js into the page, call `axe.run()`, read the violations JSON. Net-new moat: turns the existing save-gate from a ~6-check heuristic into a real WCAG audit, and the violation count/impact becomes a gate signal alongside the coherence score. Worth adding because ARIA-correctness and role mismatches are the single biggest class of a11y bug a pixel-blind composing agent emits and the vault currently can't see them at all.
backlog.license: MPL-2.0
backlog.note: MPL-2.0 is FILE-LEVEL weak copyleft (not viral like GPL) — safe to bundle/inject as an unmodified dependency; only modifications to axe-core's own files must stay MPL. Vendor axe.min.js and inject it; do NOT fork-and-edit. Overlaps slightly with inspect.js's existing contrast/alt checks (dedupe those), but its ARIA/role/focus-order rules are entirely net-new.

backlog.name: prefers-reduced-motion HONORING verification (render-twice diff)
backlog.what: A net-new GATE signal: Design Book's autofix.js already INJECTS a prefers-reduced-motion block, but nothing ever VERIFIES the page actually quiets down under it — an injected media query that targets the wrong selectors silently does nothing. Headless Chrome can emulate the preference (`page.emulateMediaFeatures([{name:'prefers-reduced-motion',value:'reduce'}])`, or the `--force-prefers-reduced-motion=reduce` flag). The check: render once normally, render once with reduce emulated, and assert that elements with non-trivial animation-duration / transition-duration / infinite animation-iteration-count actually collapse to ~0 (or that getAnimations() returns empty) under reduce. Flag any element still animating >200ms or looping infinitely. This is a clean diagnostic the vault lacks and directly closes the gap between 'we injected the query' and 'motion is honored'.
backlog.license: N/A (built-in Chromium/Puppeteer capability + small custom probe)
backlog.note: Pairs naturally with the existing two-render screenshot path. Implement as a second emulated-media pass over the existing inspect probe; report still-animating selectors. Pure capability, no third-party dep, fully headless.

backlog.name: Focus-visible presence + positive-tabindex / focus-order probe
backlog.what: Two pure-DOM checks the existing probe doesn't do and that don't require axe: (1) Focus-visible presence — for every interactive element, detect the anti-pattern of `outline:none`/`outline:0` with NO visible :focus-visible replacement (programmatically: read computed outline/box-shadow in :focus-visible state, or scan stylesheets for `outline:none` rules lacking a paired `:focus-visible` rule), which is the most common 'keyboard users can't see where they are' regression a composing agent ships. (2) Focus-order sanity — flag any element with a positive tabindex (tabindex>0, a WCAG anti-pattern that scrambles tab order) and flag focusable elements that are visually positioned out of DOM order. Both run in the same single headless pass as inspect.js and emit gate-able counts. Net-new: inspect.js checks nameless buttons/unlabeled inputs but never checks whether focus is *visible* or *ordered*.
backlog.license: N/A (custom DOM probe, vanilla)
backlog.note: Lightweight and dependency-free — good even standalone if axe is deemed too heavy. The positive-tabindex rule overlaps with one axe rule, so if axe is adopted, keep only the focus-visible-presence half here (axe does not reliably check for a *visible* focus indicator since that needs computed-style inspection).

backlog.name: APCA / SAPC perceptual contrast scoring (Lc) as a second contrast lens
backlog.what: APCA (Accessible Perceptual Contrast Algorithm, the SAPC-derived method explored for WCAG 3) scores text contrast as a perceptual Lc value (0–100+) that accounts for text size, weight, and polarity (dark-on-light vs light-on-dark) — fixing well-known WCAG2-ratio failure cases where the simple luminance ratio passes/fails wrongly. Net-new value for Design Book: a second contrast lens on the chosen palette that catches 'technically-passes-WCAG2-but-looks-muddy' and 'fails-WCAG2-but-is-actually-fine-for-large-bold' cases the current ratio-only check in inspect.js can't distinguish, and APCA's size/weight awareness maps cleanly onto the vault's type scale.
backlog.license: FLAG: apca-w3 reference impl is W3/AGWG-licensed with 'specific limitations and exclusions' (restricted, NOT permissive); the algorithm itself is openly documented and reimplementable
backlog.note: Do NOT bundle apca-w3 (restricted license) — reimplement the published Lc formula as a vanilla helper (~40 lines: sRGB→Y, soft-clamp, power-curve). FLAG: APCA was an EXPLORATORY WCAG3 item and exploratory items were removed from WCAG3 drafts (W3C, 2023; still unsettled as of 2026) — so ship it as an ADVISORY signal, not a hard pass/fail gate. value=3 (advisory, license-encumbered reference) vs the 4-5 of the clean dependency-free checks above.

backlog.name: State-completeness gate (empty / loading / error variant audit)
backlog.what: A new diagnostic gate that, for any composed page/app containing a list/data/feed/table region, checks that the output ALSO ships the three non-happy-path states it implies: an empty state (zero-data + a next-action CTA), a loading state (skeleton matching the real layout, not a bare spinner), and error states (offline / 5xx / 403 / 404 / validation). A 2025 NNG audit found ~92% of AI-generated dashboards had no empty state, ~78% no error state, and 100% used a generic spinner instead of a skeleton — and the vibe-coder consensus is these states are the dropped 'last 30%'. The vault already RENDERS every one of these states (skeleton-pack.css, empty-states-pack-2.css, loading-pages.css, error-pages.css, components/empty-state.css, svg/illustrations empty-state scenes) — what's missing is a verifier that fails-the-gate / warns when a data view is composed WITHOUT them. This is pure moat: it turns existing assets into a guarantee. Slots beside checkCoherence() in mcp-server/server.js (which today only checks hex/durations/radius/imagery, NOT state coverage).
backlog.license: N/A (original gate; pattern sourced from public UX writing + NNG audit)
backlog.note: Net-new VERIFY capability. Vault has the state-rendering snippets but no gate that asserts a list view includes empty+loading+error. Wire as a warning class in checkCoherence (mcp-server/server.js ~line 1287) gated on presence of list/table/feed regions; reference the existing skeleton-pack / empty-states-pack-2 / error-pages snippets as the auto-fix. The 9-combination checklist (empty; skeleton; optimistic; offline; 5xx; 403; 404; validation; rate-limit) is the spec.

backlog.name: Realistic-copy generator via @faker-js/faker (seeded, domain-plausible content)
backlog.what: @faker-js/faker v10.5.0 (released Jun 17 2026, MIT, browser+ESM, runs from esm.sh/jsdelivr) behind a need()-guard to replace lorem-ipsum and generic placeholders with domain-plausible copy: real-looking names (Jordan Lee), companies, finance rows (Whole Foods Market $67.23 - today; Netflix $15.99 - yesterday), products, addresses, avatars. Crucially faker.seed(n) is DETERMINISTIC — same seed => same content — which mirrors the vault's existing compose_page seed semantics (per MEMORY: blessed/seed-driven composition), so composed pages read 'real out of the box' reproducibly. The lorem-ipsum / 'you@example.com' look is a top AI-slop tell; the composer currently hard-codes a few plausible strings (Jordan Lee, scr-chips) but has no systematic copy layer. A compose enrichment that fills section slots (testimonial names, transaction lists, product grids, dashboard stats) with seeded faker content raises perceived quality directly. Pair with a tiny vanilla detector in the coherence gate that flags literal 'Lorem ipsum'/'Placeholder' strings.
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: Confirmed absent: grep for lorem/faker/realistic-copy/content-generator across INDEX.md + skills + taste/ returned zero. Import only needed locales/modules to stay light; degrade gracefully (fall back to a small built-in name/company array, or to the existing hard-coded strings) if the CDN is unavailable so the no-network path still composes. Drive it off the same seed passed to compose_page for reproducibility.

backlog.name: state-completeness.skill.md (decision tree: which states a genre owes)
backlog.what: A small decision-tree skill that maps each compose GENRE/section type to the set of non-happy-path states it must ship and which existing vault snippet fills each slot: e.g. dashboard/table => skeleton-pack table-row + empty-states-pack-2 'no-results' + error-pages 500/503 + offline; feed/social => message-skeleton + inbox-zero + rate-limit; ecommerce list => product skeleton card + 'no products match filters' + cart empty-state; search => skeleton + no-results + offline. It also encodes the realistic-copy rule ('never lorem; seed faker per region; show real numbers/dates/merchants') and the optimistic-UI note for likes/follows/CRUD. This is the human-readable counterpart that the state-completeness GATE enforces — same role structure.skill.md plays for section sequencing. The Australian Govt Design System pattern is a clean, license-clean reference for the empty/loading/error triad spec.
backlog.license: N/A (skill is vault-authored; reference pattern is govt open content)
backlog.note: Complements (does not duplicate) structure.skill.md, which sequences SECTIONS but says nothing about state variants or copy realism. This skill is the genre->required-states->snippet mapping plus the anti-lorem copy rule; it makes the two items above usable by the composer rather than ad hoc.

backlog.name: get_variable_defs / get_theme-style token-read resource (designbook://tokens/{slug})
backlog.what: Both Figma MCP (get_variable_defs) and Subframe MCP (get_theme) expose a dedicated token-read surface that returns the EXACT resolved design tokens (colors, spacing, typography, radii, durations) in use, as structured data an agent references directly so it never re-invents hardcoded hex/px/ms. Design Book composes tokens deterministically and its coherence gate already PENALIZES hardcoded values, but there is no first-class read surface that returns a composed/saved page's resolved token table — agents must scrape the HTML. Net-new ergonomic: a get_tokens tool + a designbook://tokens/{slug} resource template (mirroring the existing frontendmaxxing://palette resource but page-scoped and post-compose-resolved) so an external agent edits a saved page in token-space and stays inside the save-gate. This is the single highest-leverage parity gap: it is the most-used tool in both competitor servers' round-trips.
backlog.license: N/A (own MCP surface)
backlog.note: frontendmaxxing has get_palette + a palette resource, but those are pre-defined palettes, NOT the resolved tokens of a specific composed/saved page. Distinct surface.

backlog.name: search_design_system-style intent-search returning component+token reuse (not just snippet matches)
backlog.what: Figma's search_design_system searches across connected libraries and returns matching COMPONENTS, VARIABLES, and STYLES so the agent reuses existing design-system assets instead of authoring net-new — the explicit goal is 'reuse over re-create.' Design Book's search_components (BM25) and recommend (intent) return snippet paths only; they do not return the reusable tokens/palette/taste-preset that should travel WITH a match, nor do they scope to 'what's already on this page.' Net-new: an intent-search tool that returns each hit bundled with its companion tokens + taste preset + palette (a reuse manifest), and optionally scoped to a saved page so the agent extends a page coherently. This closes the gap between 'find a snippet' and 'find the design-system-consistent way to do X here.'
backlog.license: N/A (own MCP surface)
backlog.note: recommend already does intent→snippets+companions; the net-new delta is returning the token/palette/preset reuse manifest AND a page-scoped variant. Treat as an enhancement, not a full duplicate.

backlog.name: get_metadata-style sparse page outline for cheap re-targeting before edit
backlog.what: Figma MCP's documented round-trip is get_metadata (a sparse XML map of node IDs/names/types/positions) FIRST, then re-fetch only the needed node(s) via get_design_context — explicitly to avoid pulling huge payloads. Design Book's book_get_page is all-or-nothing (full HTML, flagged 'token-heavy'); compact:true only swaps HTML for a byte count, giving no structural map. Net-new: a get_outline tool returning a sparse section/node tree (section id, kind, role, bbox, image-count) of a saved page so an agent can target ONE section to edit and re-fetch just that fragment — the read-before-write discovery primitive both competitor servers center their protocol on. Directly reduces the edit round-trip cost that the server's own descriptions repeatedly try to mitigate with 'save tokens' flags.
backlog.license: N/A (own MCP surface)
backlog.note: Complements the existing compose→inspect→save loop; the outline is the missing 'metadata' tier between book_list_pages and book_get_page.

backlog.name: Code-Connect-style mapping: link saved-page sections to their source vault snippets
backlog.what: Figma MCP's get_code_connect_map / get_code_connect_suggestions / send_code_connect_mappings let an agent learn which code component a design node maps to, so generated code reuses the real component. Design Book composes from ~920 vault snippets but, once saved, a page is opaque HTML with no provenance — an agent re-editing it cannot ask 'which snippet produced this section, and what are its companions/source?' Net-new: stamp compose output with snippet provenance and expose a get_provenance tool (section → source snippet path + companions + skill), turning every saved page into a reusable, traceable design system rather than dead HTML. This is the structural moat Code Connect provides and is uniquely cheap here because Design Book OWNS the composition step (Figma had to retrofit it).
backlog.license: N/A (own MCP surface)
backlog.note: No provenance/mapping surface exists today; pairs naturally with get_source/get_related already in the frontendmaxxing server.

backlog.name: Motion-intent skill: intent-verb -> coherent motion package (reveal cadence + easing + stagger budget)
backlog.what: A .skill.md decision-tree that maps a single INTENT VERB (e.g. 'energetic', 'calm-premium', 'editorial-considered', 'playful', 'technical-instant', 'cinematic') to ONE coherent motion package: a reveal cadence (which scroll-reveal direction + range), an easing role-set (entrance/exit/emphasis curves), and a stagger BUDGET (per-item delay + max items before it collapses to a group fade). Today the vault only exposes 3 generic levels via data-motion (minimal/standard/playful) on motion.css/motion-profiles.js -- those tune duration/ease/stagger NUMBERS but do not encode an intent->choreography DECISION (which reveal pattern, how much stagger is too much for this vibe, which easing for entrance vs emphasis). The 2025-26 'motion language' design-systems literature is explicit: pick a motion package per intent and choreograph rather than stack effects; stagger 50-100ms by importance; 200-400ms feedback / 600-800ms context-change. This skill turns the existing primitives (Stagger, scroll-animations.css view()/scroll(), MotionProfile, blessed durations) into an intent-addressed picker, mirroring the taste.skill.md aesthetic vocabulary but for motion choreography specifically. Net-new: the DECISION layer, not the tokens.
backlog.license: N/A (original skill authored from public design-systems guidance)
backlog.note: Builds directly on existing taste/motion.css, taste/motion-profiles.js, animations/stagger.js, animations/scroll-animations.css -- composes them per intent rather than adding a dep. Closest existing is taste.skill.md (aesthetic-level) + data-motion (3 numeric levels); neither encodes an intent->choreography mapping.

backlog.name: Motion-budget coherence signal (anti-effect-stacking gate)
backlog.what: A new diagnostic in coherence_check / book_coherence that scores MOTION COHERENCE, not just blessed durations. Today the gate (server.js ~L1290-1367) flags unblessed-duration, slop-hover scale(1.0x), and a binary NO-MOTION tell -- but it has NO signal for the opposite failure: too many independent motion mechanisms stacked on one page (e.g. AOS reveals + parallax + tilt + holo + marquee + page-transition + audio-react all at once), or mixing contradictory easings (bouncy spring AND linear AND ease-out in the same view), or a stagger budget blown past ~8-12 items. The vault's own anti-slop rule #5 ('don't glassmorphism+neon+gradient-text+3d-tilt all on one page') is exactly this principle but is currently unenforced for MOTION. This adds a measurable 'motion-budget' score: count distinct motion subsystems + distinct easing families + max simultaneous staggered items, and penalize when they exceed an intent-appropriate budget. The moat is the verifier, so a motion-coherence gate is high-leverage and net-new.
backlog.license: N/A (vault-authored diagnostic)
backlog.note: Extends the existing coherence gate in mcp-server/server.js (computeCoherence) which already counts gradients/images/durations but has only a binary NO-MOTION check and no effect-stacking/easing-mixing/stagger-budget signal. Pairs with the motion-intent skill (the skill picks the budget; the gate enforces it).

backlog.name: M3-Expressive spring token model: spatial vs effects springs + named intent schemes
backlog.what: Adopt Material 3 Expressive's two-axis spring vocabulary as blessed CSS/JS tokens: SPATIAL springs (animate position/size/shape -- allowed overshoot/bounce) vs EFFECT springs (animate color/opacity -- high damping, no bounce), each in fast/default/slow, plus named intent schemes (Expressive = low damping/bounce for hero moments; Standard = critically damped). The vault's animations/spring.js exposes raw stiffness/damping but with NO named role separation and NO intent presets -- so today a composer hand-picks stiffness numbers, the exact per-element hand-coding this angle is about. Standardizing on the spatial/effects split (the industry's emergent vocabulary, now in 21+ M3 components and Android 16) gives the motion-intent skill a principled rule: 'opacity/color fades use the effects curve (never bounce); position/scale reveals use the spatial curve.' Reimplement as a tiny taste/springs.css + spring presets (vanilla, since spring.js already does the physics) -- inspiration from M3, not a dependency (M3 itself is Apache-2.0 but we only borrow the vocabulary + the spatial/effects discipline).
backlog.license: Apache-2.0 (Material spec; vocabulary borrowed, reimplemented vanilla)
backlog.note: animations/spring.js has raw stiffness/damping but no spatial-vs-effects role split or named intent presets; this adds the blessed-token discipline (which curve for which property) the motion-intent skill needs. Borrow the model, not the lib -- reimplement as a small token set on top of existing spring.js.

backlog.name: CSS animation-trigger declarative scroll-triggered reveals (Chrome 145, 2026)
backlog.what: The new CSS animation-trigger property (Chrome 145, landing 2026) fires a normal time-based animation when the scroll crosses an offset -- a fully DECLARATIVE replacement for IntersectionObserver-driven reveal classes, with once/repeat/reset trigger behaviors built in. This is the 'declarative rather than per-element hand-coding' primitive for scroll reveals: instead of JS toggling .is-in, you declare the trigger in CSS and the browser owns it. The vault ALREADY has scroll-progress animations via animation-timeline: view()/scroll() (animations/scroll-animations.css, headers-pro.css), but those are PROGRESS-LINKED (scrub with scroll); animation-trigger is the missing TRIGGER-AND-PLAY case (cross a line -> play a normal-duration reveal once), which the vault currently only does via IntersectionObserver JS (aos-lite.js, scroll-animations.js). Add as a thin progressive-enhancement layer: @supports (animation-trigger: ...) plays the reveal declaratively, else the existing IO path. Lowest value of the four (a thin primitive extension, very new/Chromium-only) but it's the genuine 2026 declarative-reveal news and slots cleanly under the motion-intent skill's 'reveal cadence' axis.
backlog.license: N/A (web platform CSS feature)
backlog.note: Vault already has animation-timeline view()/scroll() PROGRESS reveals (animations/scroll-animations.css) and IntersectionObserver TRIGGER reveals (aos-lite.js, scroll-animations.js). animation-trigger is the net-new declarative trigger-and-play primitive that replaces the IO path where supported; ship behind @supports with the existing IO fallback. Chromium-only as of 2026.

backlog.name: editorial-spread (compose genre + vanilla CSS layout set)
backlog.what: A new compose archetype distinct from the existing web-page genre: a print-magazine 'spread' built from one vanilla CSS file. The reusable primitives that make Awwwards 2025-26 editorial winners read like print: (1) a real baseline GRID via CSS Grid rows of one --baseline unit (1.5rem) + `grid-auto-rows: var(--baseline)` so every block snaps to the rhythm — the thing vertical-rhythm.css only approximates with margins; (2) a 3-zone editorial track grid `grid-template-columns:[full-start] minmax(0,1fr) [body-start] min(65ch,100%) [body-end] minmax(0,1fr) [full-end]` so body text sits in the measure while figures opt into `full-width`/`bleed` and notes into the margin — the figure-ground asymmetric spread without absolute positioning; (3) marginalia/sidenotes via a `.note` floated into the outer track with the pure-CSS checkbox-hack toggle on narrow viewports (Tufte pattern, no JS); (4) pull-quotes that break the measure with `width:max-content` + negative inline margins; (5) multi-column body flow with `columns` + `column-rule` + `column-fill:balance` and `break-inside:avoid` on figures. This is a clean compose-genre ('editorial-spread') plus a `editorial-layout.css` snippet — net-new, the vault has the scattered decorations (drop-cap text-effect, 1-of-8 pull-quote, editorial-serif heading) but no track-grid + baseline-grid + marginalia SYSTEM and no spread genre. Pairs with existing heading-pack/quote-blocks/watercolor as fillers.
backlog.license: original (vanilla CSS, no dep)
backlog.note: Anti-dup verified: grep found NO marginalia/sidenote/track-grid/column-balance/editorial-spread coverage. Adjacent-only: typography/vertical-rhythm.css (margin rhythm, not grid-snapped), typography/quote-blocks.css (pull-quote as 1 of 8 styles), typography/heading-pack.css (editorial-serif), layout/grid-systems.css (generic 12-col), layout/masonry.css (CSS columns for cards, not text flow). The genre + the four missing primitives (baseline-grid snap, 3-track full/body/margin grid, marginalia toggle, balanced multi-column text flow) are the addable delta.

backlog.name: CSS initial-letter + text-box-trim baseline kit
backlog.what: The two modern CSS primitives that make web drop caps and baseline alignment print-grade WITHOUT float hacks or magic numbers. `initial-letter: 3 3` (with `-webkit-` prefix) sets a true 3-line-deep sunken drop cap that the engine sinks and aligns to the baseline — replacing the vault's current `::first-letter` float drop-cap (which is one of 15 effects in text-pack-2.css and doesn't baseline-align). `text-box: trim-both cap alphabetic` (Chrome 133+/Safari 18.2+, shipped late 2025) strips the invisible half-leading above/below type so headings and drop caps sit FLUSH on the baseline grid — the missing piece that makes baseline-grid alignment actually line up instead of being ~font-metrics off. Ship as a tiny vanilla snippet (`editorial-baseline.css`) with progressive-enhancement fallbacks (the float drop-cap and margin rhythm degrade gracefully where unsupported). This is the technical core that elevates the editorial-spread from 'looks editorial' to 'measures like print'.
backlog.license: web standard (CSS)
backlog.note: Anti-dup: grep for initial-letter / text-box-trim / text-box-edge / half-leading returned ZERO hits. Vault drop-cap is the old ::first-letter approach in typography/text-pack-2.css. License/support caveat: text-box-trim is NOT yet full Baseline (Firefox behind a flag), initial-letter unsupported in Firefox — must ship with the existing float-drop-cap + margin-rhythm as the fallback so verification's overflow/render gate stays clean cross-browser (vault verifies in headless Chrome, where both ARE supported).

backlog.name: Tufte CSS (sidenote/marginalia pattern)
backlog.what: The canonical pure-CSS marginalia system: numbered sidenotes (`.sidenote`/`.marginnote` via CSS counters + an `<input type=checkbox>`/`<label>` toggle), full-width figures that bleed past the measure, epigraphs, and `.newthought` small-caps lead-ins — exactly the print apparatus the editorial-spread needs and the cleanest reference implementation of the auto-numbered-sidenote counter trick. MIT, zero-JS, feature-complete/stable. Use as INSPIRATION to reimplement (not a dep): lift the counter-based auto-numbering and the checkbox-toggle responsive collapse into the vault's own `editorial-layout.css` so it inherits vault tokens/palettes instead of Tufte's ET-Book typography. Settles the one genuinely fiddly part of marginalia (auto-numbering + mobile collapse) with a proven pattern.
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: MIT, pure CSS, safe to reimplement. Not shipped as-is (opinionated ET-Book type + global element selectors would clash with vault tokens) — extract the sidenote-counter + checkbox-toggle pattern only. Closest vault item is ai/citation-popover.css (inline AI source footnotes on hover), which is a different concern (sources, not margin commentary).

backlog.name: Pretext (text layout/measurement engine)
backlog.what: Cheng Lou's (Midjourney engineer) zero-dep ~15KB TS/JS engine, MIT, ~48.6k stars, released early 2026 — measures and lays out text with pure arithmetic via Canvas2D + Intl.Segmenter: column balancing (equal-height columns across a spread), variable-width line flow to route text AROUND floated images/obstacles, and shrink-wrap (tightest fitting width). These are the precise print-magazine effects CSS still can't do (true balanced columns, text reflow around irregular shapes). Fits as an OPTIONAL cdn-snippet behind a need()-guard for the 'pro' editorial-spread tier: when present, upgrade columns to balanced + enable wrap-around-figure; when absent, fall back to the vanilla `columns:2; column-fill:balance` + rectangular float. Degrades gracefully, never required.
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: MIT, zero-dep, ESM/CDN-loadable, framework-agnostic — CDN-safe. Caveat: it is a MEASUREMENT engine, not a renderer — the wrap-around-shape / balanced-column rendering must be applied by the snippet on top (non-trivial), so the vanilla CSS path stays the default and Pretext is a progressive upgrade. No vault overlap (vault has no text-layout/measurement engine; OGL/GSAP/three are motion/3D, not typesetting). Verify the wrapped result still passes the headless-Chrome no-overflow gate.

backlog.name: Neo-brutalist taste preset + token layer (`data-aesthetic="brutalist"`)
backlog.what: A 7th aesthetic + new taste preset(s) (e.g. 'raw-brutalist', 'acid-brutalist') that bind the brutalist system into ONE coherent, composable token bundle: a `--nb-*` token set (--nb-border:3px solid #000; --nb-radius:0; --nb-shadow:6px 6px 0 #000; --nb-translate:translate(3px,3px) hover) plus an `[data-aesthetic="brutalist"]` block in aesthetic.css that forces radius:0, opaque hard borders, the press-into-shadow hover, and routes house.* to the atoms the vault ALREADY has (effects/shadows.css .shadow-brutal/.shadow-sharp, blocks/buttons-extra.css .xbtn-brutalist, structure/section-frames.css grid-guides/brackets, backgrounds/grid-bg-spotlight.css .grid-bg-spot-light, taste/fonts.css mono-technical). The vault has all the raw atoms scattered but NO coordinating preset/aesthetic — exactly the 'taste folder coordinates type+motion+spacing' gap. This is the canonical, codifiable answer: brutalism IS a token layer, not glitch one-offs. Also directly dodges the MEMORY 'gradients+no-images = AI tell' authenticity law by being a flat, raw, anti-perfection system.
backlog.license: N/A (vault-native; reference neobrutalism.dev/NN/g spec is MIT/editorial)
backlog.note: NOT a new shadow snippet — effects/shadows.css already has .shadow-brutal/.shadow-sharp and section-frames has grid-guides. The net-new is the COORDINATION: a brutalist aesthetic block in taste/aesthetic.css + a preset row in taste/presets.js + a small --nb-* token group. Ships as a brutalist.skill.md decision tree (when to clash vs restrain, contrast/a11y floor per NN/g, mono-for-labels-not-body) that routes to existing packs. Add an `avoid` list (soft shadows, blur, gradient soup, rounded corners, low-contrast pastel borders) so verify/coherence can gate it.

backlog.name: Brutalist 'intentional clash' palette (e.g. `.pal-acid-brutal` / `.pal-raw-paper`)
backlog.what: A net-new named palette filling the one structural hole in the 61: bright saturated primaries (electric blue #2563ff / signal red #ff3b30 / acid yellow #ffe000 / lime #b6ff3a) on off-white #fefefe ground with PURE-BLACK #000 ink and OPAQUE hard borders. Verified gap: the closest existing palettes (crayon, playful-bright) use soft translucent borders rgba(20,20,40,.10) and pastel pinks — the literal opposite of brutalist hard-edge clash; vercel-mono/mono-noir are monochrome. Brutalism's defining color rule is '2-3 saturated hues clashing on off-white with black ink, opaque borders', which no palette encodes. Needed for the preset above to point at a real --pal-* name.
backlog.license: N/A (vault-native color set)
backlog.note: Must keep WCAG/APCA contrast on the off-white ground (NN/g warns yellow/cyan-on-white fail readability) — pair bright fills with black ink only, never colored text on colored bg. Ship as a light clash variant + optional dark (#0d0d0d ground, same primaries) so the brutalist preset has both. Borders must be opaque #000, NOT the soft rgba borders crayon/playful-bright use.

backlog.name: Departure Mono (brutalist pixel-mono typeface)
backlog.what: A lo-fi pixel monospace by Helena Zhang that has become a signature 2024-26 brutalist/anti-design display face (Daring Fireball, HN front page). Adds the missing TYPE dimension to the brutalist system: the vault's mono-technical pair is ui-monospace/SF Mono (clean dev-tool mono), which reads 'technical SaaS', not 'raw brutalist'. Departure Mono gives oversized brutalist headers/labels the intentional-unpolished, sci-fi-terminal character the aesthetic needs while staying a real, legible monospace. Self-hostable woff2 or via CDN; pairs with a clean grotesk/Inter body per NN/g 'chunky header + neutral body' rule.
backlog.license: SIL Open Font License 1.1 (permissive, embed/self-host OK)
backlog.note: Add as a new font pair (e.g. 'brutalist-mono': Departure Mono headings + Inter/Space Grotesk body) in taste/fonts.css, behind a need()-style @font-face that degrades to ui-monospace if the font fails to load. OFL allows webfont embedding. Use for headers/labels/UI chrome ONLY — keep body in a legible grotesk (NN/g: condensed/decorative faces hurt paragraph legibility). Lettra Mono (Pangram Pangram) is a paid alternative — note as inspiration only, not a dep.

backlog.name: Progressive Blur (graduated backdrop-filter depth-of-field ramp)
backlog.what: Apple-style 'progressive blur' = a stack of ~7 absolutely-positioned divs, each with an increasing backdrop-filter blur (1/2/4/8/16/32/64px) and an offset linear-gradient mask, so blur ramps smoothly from sharp (foreground) to heavy (depth).
