A single backdrop-filter:blur() gives a hard blur edge; this gives a true gradient/feathered focus falloff. It is the missing depth-of-field cue: it reads as camera focus / atmospheric recession on a flat screen, keeps foreground text razor-sharp, and is the canonical 2026 'spatial design' surface treatment (visionOS hero fades, fixed nav bars over scrolling content, modal scrims, image bottom-fades). The vault has glassmorphism.css (single-layer) and glass-refraction.css but NOTHING that ramps blur by distance.
backlog.license: No explicit license on the canonical writeups (kennethnym, devslovecoffee) — technique is public-domain CSS, reimplement from primitives (backdrop-filter + mask + linear-gradient); do not copy verbatim.
backlog.note: Net-new, zero-dep. Reimplement (do not copy any single author's exact stops). Pairs with the imagery floor (image bottom-fade) and glassmorphism. Add a prefers-reduced-transparency / low-end fallback to a flat scrim.

backlog.name: Native scroll-timeline Z-dolly depth scene (animation-timeline: view()/scroll() + translateZ)
backlog.what: A pure-CSS 'fly into the scene' depth traversal: a perspective + preserve-3d stage whose layers are pushed apart on the Z axis and then dollied toward the viewer via animation-timeline: scroll()/view() (no JS, runs off main thread, GPU-composited). Distinct from the vault's parallax (which only translates X/Y at different speeds) and from css-3d.js coverflow (a horizontal carousel translateZ). This is scroll = camera dolly along +Z, the literal 'spatial depth on flat screens' headline effect. Vault's scroll-animations.css uses native scroll-timeline but has ZERO z-axis/perspective; no snippet links scroll to translateZ dolly. Zero-dep, degrades to a static layered hero where unsupported (Firefox), no WebGL.
backlog.license: Web-platform API (CSS Scroll-Driven Animations) — no license; vanilla CSS.
backlog.note: Net-new and zero-dep — the closest the vault gets is GSAP ScrollTrigger parallax (heavier) and macbook-scroll (single-element open). Ship with @supports + reduced-motion fallbacks baked in.

backlog.name: Atmospheric-perspective depth kit (per-layer desaturation + blur + scale falloff)
backlog.what: A coherent 'depth scene' / diorama compose pattern: 3-5 stacked parallax layers where FAR layers are programmatically desaturated, lightened toward a cool haze color, slightly blurred, and scaled down — implementing the real monocular depth cue (aerial/atmospheric perspective) instead of just speed-offset parallax. Layers driven by CSS custom property --depth (0..1) that simultaneously ramps filter: blur()+saturate()+brightness(), transform scale, and parallax speed. The vault has many parallax primitives (hero-parallax, parallax-stars, scroll-fx-pack, parallax.js) but they all offset position only; none fade/desaturate/blur by distance, and there is no single 'depth-kit' that bundles the cues. Best as a small skill/decision-tree that wires existing parallax + the new progressive-blur into a depth recipe, plus a thin --depth helper snippet.
backlog.license: Technique (public domain); Codrops articles CC-BY-style for learning — reimplement, cite as inspiration.
backlog.note: Net-new as a *combined* depth recipe; individual parallax pieces exist but the atmospheric-perspective cue (desaturate+blur+lighten by distance) does not. Lower value than the two snippets because it leans on reimplementing-from-primitives and overlaps partially with existing parallax.

backlog.name: DTCG export/import skill (W3C Design Tokens Format Module 2025.10)
backlog.what: A bidirectional mapping skill that serializes the vault's 61 palettes + taste presets to DTCG-compliant `.tokens.json` and parses external DTCG files back into vault role-tokens. The vault already speaks 'tokens' as CSS vars by role (bg/bg-alt/surface/border/fg/muted/faint/accent/accent-2/on-accent + semantic ok/warn/danger/info), and taste presets bundle motion/density/fontPair — these map almost 1:1 onto DTCG `$type`s: each color role -> a `color` token (2025.10 wants the structured `{colorSpace:'srgb', components:[r,g,b], hex}` object, NOT a raw hex string, which is the one real conversion the exporter must do), motion durations -> `duration` (ms), density spacing/radius -> `dimension` (px/rem), fontPair -> `fontFamily`, plus `{base.accent}` curly-brace aliases so semantic tokens reference base ones. Worth adding because it is the vault's FIRST interchange format: a `.tokens.json` export drops straight into Figma (Tokens Studio, Token Press), Style Dictionary 4, Penpot, Sketch, Framer, Supernova, zeroheight — making the vault's taste interoperable with the broader ecosystem instead of being a closed CSS-var island. The spec is a permissive W3C Community Group report (no patent/copyright encumbrance on implementing it).
backlog.license: W3C Community Group Report (permissive, implementable freely)
backlog.note: Net-new. Closest existing pieces: design-system.skill.md generates a Markdown DESIGN.md and get_palette returns role-token JSON, but neither is W3C-DTCG-shaped ($value/$type/aliases) nor importable. The non-trivial bit is color: 2025.10 deprecates flat hex strings for the structured color object + supports Oklch/Display-P3, so the exporter should emit components + keep hex as a fallback. Theming/modes are NOT in the stable spec body (handled via group $extends / separate files), so map each palette to its own token file or group rather than inventing a modes block.

backlog.name: Style Dictionary 4 (DTCG build/transform target)
backlog.what: The canonical open-source transformer that turns DTCG `.tokens.json` into CSS vars, SCSS, JS/TS, iOS, Android, Flutter. v4 has first-class DTCG support ($value/$type/$description; you pick DTCG or legacy, not both in one instance). For Design Book it is the downstream proof that an export is real: round-trip the vault's exported tokens through Style Dictionary back to CSS custom properties and diff against the original palette to prove fidelity. Not a runtime vault dependency (it is a Node build tool, no CDN runtime), so it belongs INSIDE the export skill as the recommended consumer + a round-trip verification step, not as a snippet. Adds credibility: 'export → Style Dictionary → CSS' is the path every design-system team already runs.
backlog.license: Apache-2.0
backlog.note: Node-only build tool (no browser/CDN runtime) — frame as the export skill's reference consumer + round-trip verifier, NOT a vanilla/cdn snippet. Pairs with the DTCG export skill above.

backlog.name: DTCG token-file validator (@terrazzo/parser or nclsndr/design-tokens-format-module)
backlog.what: TypeScript implementations that validate a design-token tree against the 2025.10 spec: catch invalid `$type`/`$value` shape, unresolved or circular `{alias}` references, bad color objects, etc. This is the 'verification' form and the vault's moat: a save-gate that asserts every exported `.tokens.json` is spec-valid before it leaves the workbench (so Figma/Style Dictionary never reject it), and that any IMPORTED external token file is well-formed before it is mapped to vault roles. @terrazzo/parser (MIT, v2.x, published within the last ~2 weeks as of mid-2026) is the heavier full parser; nclsndr/design-tokens-format-module (MIT) is a lean type-level validator/helper. Either gives the vault a deterministic 'is this a valid design-token file?' check that fits the existing headless-verify philosophy.
backlog.license: MIT (both @terrazzo/parser and design-tokens-format-module)
backlog.note: Node-side validator (runs in the verify harness like tools/render-smoke.mjs / lottie-check.mjs), not browser runtime. Use it as the gate for both export-out and import-in. Prefer @terrazzo/parser for full resolution/alias-cycle detection; nclsndr lib if you want a tiny type-only check.

backlog.name: Widow/orphan + short-last-line detector (Range.getClientRects line-splitting)
backlog.what: A post-render check that, for each body paragraph, wraps its text in a Range and reads Range.getClientRects() to reconstruct actual line boxes (one rect per visual line), then flags: (a) WIDOW = final line is a single short word or <25-35% of the measure width, and (b) very-short last lines (<2 words). This is the canonical AI/template tell that text-wrap:pretty does NOT fix (per Rutter/clagnut: pretty only helps the greedy break, leaves widows). Browser-truth, language-agnostic, runs in the same headless pass as overflow. Surfaces as a tasteScore deduction + per-selector report, and the fix is a one-line nudge (text-wrap:pretty/balance, or a non-breaking space before the last word). Vault has zero widow/orphan logic today (inspect.js only collects longLines>95ch and typeScale.ratio).
backlog.license: n/a (vanilla algorithm)
backlog.note: Net-new gate. Extends the existing inspect.js typography block. The Range-rects trick is the only reliable post-render way to count visual lines + measure the last line; no library needed. Author explicitly notes CSS-only (pretty/balance) does NOT eliminate widows — making a JS verifier the right tool.

backlog.name: Measure / CPL floor + ceiling enforcement (45-75ch, WCAG 80 ceiling)
backlog.what: Compute true characters-per-line for each text block by measuring the rendered content-box width in `ch` units (width / size-of-'0'-glyph, sampled via a canvas TextMetrics or a probe span) rather than the current crude char-count heuristic. Gate against the researched band: warn <45ch (broken reading rhythm / cramped column), warn >75ch, hard-flag >80ch (WCAG 2.1 max for non-CJK; 40ch for CJK). The vault currently only flags approxCh>95 — that is a loose ceiling with NO floor and NO measure-vs-font-size relationship. Adding the floor catches the equally-common 'too-narrow sidebar prose' failure and ties measure to font-size, which is the actual readability lever. Reports the offending selector + measured ch so the fix (max-width/min-width in ch, or font-size) is obvious.
backlog.license: n/a (vanilla algorithm)
backlog.note: Upgrades an existing crude check into a proper two-sided, font-size-aware gate with the WCAG-backed 80ch hard ceiling. Numbers: 45-75 ideal, 66 optimal, 80 WCAG max, 40 for CJK.

backlog.name: Baseline-grid / vertical-rhythm drift detector (line-height vs grid step)
backlog.what: Pick the body line-height as the rhythm step (or read a --rhythm/--baseline custom prop if present), then measure each major block's effective line-height and top-margin and check whether they are integer multiples of the step within ~1px tolerance, using getBoundingClientRect tops to detect cumulative baseline drift down the page. Flags headings/figures/lists whose line-box or spacing knocks subsequent text off the grid. This is the print-design fundamental the vault preaches (vertical-rhythm.css exists as a SNIPPET) but never VERIFIES. Caveat: CSS can't read true font baselines, so this measures line-box rhythm (the practical, web-honest proxy) — report it as 'rhythm drift', not pixel-perfect baseline alignment, to avoid false confidence.
backlog.license: n/a (vanilla algorithm)
backlog.note: Net-new gate, but lower-signal/noisier than widows+measure: true baselines aren't readable from CSS, so it can only verify line-box rhythm and is prone to false positives on intentionally off-grid display type. Ship it as a soft warning, gated behind a 'prose'/article context, not a hard save-gate fail. Hanging-punctuation is NOT worth a separate check — caniuse shows it's Safari-only and unmeasurable post-render via layout APIs; treat as an inspiration nudge, not a gate.

backlog.name: Risograph misregistration / overprint effect (CSS layer-offset + mix-blend-mode: multiply)
backlog.what: Authentic riso look built from 2-3 spot-color ink layers, each as a stacked copy of the content tinted to a riso ink (fluorescent pink, blue, yellow, green), nudged a few px apart (the 'misregistration') and composited with mix-blend-mode: multiply so overlaps overprint into a third color, plus a feTurbulence grain layer for ink-absorption speckle. This is the centerpiece of the 2026 'tactile brutalism / anti-AI' trend and is the single biggest hole: the vault's step-risograph is only a banded gradient palette and halftone.glsl.js is a shader — neither produces the channel-split/overprint-on-real-content riso aesthetic. 100% vanilla (no JS, no shader). Pairs with existing noise-grain.css for the speckle. Ship as riso palettes + .riso-* utility (ink-1/ink-2 vars, --riso-offset, animated 'drift' misregistration). Directly counters AI-flatness.
backlog.license: Technique (public CSS pattern; reimplement from scratch — the studio-ity URL is a closed SaaS reference only)

backlog.name: Pure-CSS halftone (3-declaration radial-gradient + multiply + contrast)
backlog.what: Ana Tudor's technique: a tiled radial-gradient 'dot' layer + a gradient/image 'map' layer, background-blend-mode: multiply, then filter: contrast(16) snaps greys to black/white so dots grow/shrink by the map's luminance — a true content/luminance-driven halftone in ~3 declarations, zero deps. The vault's ONLY halftone today is shaders/halftone.glsl.js (WebGL, needs three.js/GL); there is no CSS halftone, and dot-pattern.css is just a uniform fixed-dot background, not luminance-driven. Adds a no-GPU, degrade-friendly print-halftone usable on text blocks, hero panels, and over images (luminosity/screen blend variant). Classic CMYK/riso-print tactile surface. Ship as .halftone-* with size/angle/color vars + a duotone-halftone variant that reuses existing duotone.css palettes.
backlog.license: Technique / blog tutorial (CSS pattern, freely reimplementable; credit Ana Tudor)

backlog.name: Deckle / torn-edge filter utility (feTurbulence + feDisplacementMap on element edges)
backlog.what: A reusable filter: url(#deckle) class where feTurbulence (fractalNoise) feeds feDisplacementMap to distort the ACTUAL edges of whatever element it's applied to — torn-paper cards, ragged section dividers, hand-cut sticker/photo edges, rough underlines. Vanilla SVG+CSS, dimension-agnostic, applies to any element. Distinct from what's in the vault: buttons-extra.css torn edges are gradient-mask cutouts (fixed, button-only) and image-distortion-hover.js uses feDisplacementMap only as a JS hover-warp on images. No general reusable deckle/rough-edge displacement utility exists. Provide tuned presets (subtle paper-edge → heavy torn) via baseFrequency/scale vars so authors can rough-edge cards, dividers, quote blocks. Core building block for the tactile-brutalism genre.
backlog.license: Technique / blog tutorial (vanilla SVG filter, freely reimplementable)

backlog.name: 'Tactile brutalism' compose genre / taste preset (riso + halftone + deckle + paper grain + letterpress)
backlog.what: A composition archetype + taste preset that wires the physical-material vocabulary into a coherent, save-gate-passing surface: cream/newsprint paper-grain ground (existing watercolor wc-paper / gradient-noise), riso 2-ink overprint headers (candidate 1), luminance halftone panels (candidate 2), deckle-edged cards/dividers (candidate 3), letterpress/deboss type (existing text-pack-2 debossed + buttons-extra emboss), spot-color riso palettes, and slow no-parallax motion. This is the Fireart/StudioMeyer 2026 'tactile, human, anti-flat' reaction packaged as something the engine can compose deterministically — turning four loose snippets into a named, on-trend genre. Includes a .skill.md decision tree (when paper vs riso vs halftone vs deckle; how much grain before it reads as noise; print-color-accuracy notes).
backlog.license: Permissive (composition of vanilla snippets + reference techniques)

backlog.name: a11y.js — shared vanilla a11y primitives utility (Radix-behavior layer)
backlog.what: A single zero-dep utils/a11y.js exporting the four headless behaviors Radix packages (react-focus-scope, react-roving-focus, react-dismissable-layer) provide, but framework-free: (1) getFocusable(root) + trapFocus(root) that RE-QUERY on every Tab (the vault's dialog.js caches first/last once at open, so it breaks the moment a focusable is added/removed/hidden — a real correctness bug), (2) rovingTabindex(container,{orientation}) that wires the tabindex 0/-1 + Arrow/Home/End pattern tabs.js already hardcodes, so menubar/select/dropdown-menu/tree-menu/context-menu stop each reinventing it, (3) inert + scroll-lock helper (hideBackground) using the native `inert` attribute + body overflow lock — the vault has NO inert/scroll-lock anywhere, so background content stays in the AT tree behind every modal/drawer. Net-new, raises quality of ~10 existing components at once, and is the kind of moat-y primitive the vault lacks. Radix is React (inspiration only — reimplement, don't depend).
backlog.license: MIT (Radix Primitives — reference/behavior only, not a dependency)
backlog.note: Radix itself is React-coupled — this is inspiration to reimplement as vanilla utils/a11y.js. Behaviors are spec'd by WAI-ARIA APG (vendor-neutral). Confirmed gaps: utils/dom.js has no a11y helpers; dialog.js focus trap caches first/last once; no inert/scroll-lock in any JS file.

backlog.name: Dismiss-stack manager (DismissableLayer pattern, vanilla)
backlog.what: A tiny global LIFO layer stack so Escape and pointer-down-outside dismiss only the TOPMOST overlay, not all of them. Right now dialog.js, drawer.js, menubar.js, context-menu.js, dropdown-menu.js, popover.js, bottom-sheet.js each attach their OWN document-level Escape/outside-click listener independently — confirmed by grep. So a menu opened inside a drawer inside a dialog: one Escape fires all three handlers and collapses the whole stack at once (wrong per Radix DismissableLayer semantics, where nested layers close one level at a time). A ~40-line registerLayer()/popLayer() utility with a shared keydown router fixes this for every overlay component simultaneously. This is exactly the @radix-ui/react-dismissable-layer behavior, fully reimplementable in vanilla JS.
backlog.license: MIT (Radix DismissableLayer — behavior reference)
backlog.note: Verified each overlay attaches its own Escape listener (no shared stack). Pairs with a11y.js. Pure vanilla — no dep.

backlog.name: Type-ahead (printable-character listbox/menu search) helper
backlog.what: A typeAhead(items,getLabel) helper implementing the WAI-ARIA APG behavior where typing printable characters jumps focus to the next item whose label starts with the buffered string (with the ~500ms reset + same-char-cycles-matches rule). Grep confirms ZERO type-ahead anywhere in the vault — select.js, menubar.js, dropdown-menu.js, tree-menu.js, combobox.js all do Arrow/Enter/Esc only. This is the single most-missed listbox/menu keyboard affordance (sighted keyboard users expect to type 'g' to jump to 'Germany' in a country select). Small, vanilla, and instantly upgrades select/multi-select/dropdown-menu/menubar/tree-menu.
backlog.license: Public spec (W3C WAI-ARIA APG) — implementation original/MIT
backlog.note: Confirmed absent: grep for type-ahead/String.fromCharCode/printable across components+blocks returns nothing relevant. Headless UI famously got this rule wrong (issue #1046) — getting it right is a differentiator.

backlog.name: Live-region announcer (aria-live polite/assertive utility)
backlog.what: A singleton announce(message,{assertive}) that lazily creates one persistent visually-hidden aria-live='polite' (and one 'assertive') region at load and pushes text into it — the standard pattern AT needs to hear async/JS-driven changes. Grep confirms the vault has NO programmatic aria-live announcer: toasts.js, toast-stack-2.css, inline-toast, notifications-pro all render visible toasts but never announce them to screen readers, so a 'Saved!' / 'Error' toast is silent to AT users. One ~25-line utility lets toasts, combobox result counts ('5 results'), form validation, drag-reorder, and command-palette navigation all become audible. Highest-leverage a11y gap relative to effort.
backlog.license: Public spec (W3C WAI-ARIA) — implementation original/MIT
backlog.note: Confirmed absent: no liveRegion/announce/programmatic aria-live in any JS. Existing toast/notification components are visual-only. Could even become a verification hook (flag interactive components with no announce path).

backlog.name: Broken-grid layout primitive (dense-grid overlap + z-stack)
backlog.what: A vanilla CSS layout system that encodes the 2026 'broken grid / deconstructed' counter-trend deterministically: one fixed dense grid (e.g. grid-template-columns: repeat(12,1fr); grid-template-rows: repeat(N,1fr)) where items are placed by explicit line numbers (grid-column: 2/8, grid-row: 3/6) and are ALLOWED to share cells so they overlap, with z-index controlling stack order. Add a small token set (--bg-rot for a capped tilt like -4deg..4deg, --bg-z, --bg-bleed for negative-margin overhang) so an engine can place blocks asymmetrically yet 100% reproducibly. This is the exact net-new gap: the vault has tidy bento/masonry/12-col grids but NO overlapping/misaligned/z-stacked page-layout. Deterministic line-number + capped-rotation rules make it engine-safe (verifier can still check no-overflow because the outer grid is bounded).
backlog.license: Unlicensed repo (inspiration); the technique itself is plain CSS Grid spec, fully reimplementable, no dep
backlog.note: Repo has no license file — treat as inspiration and reimplement clean. Encode as layout/broken-grid.css with .bgrid container + .bgrid-item data-col/data-row/data-z/data-rot. Counter to bento saturation = high differentiation value.

backlog.name: Cutout-collage layout (CSS Grid + clip-path polygons)
backlog.what: Magazine/scrapbook collage where images sit on a 12x12 grid, overlap across shared cells, and each gets an irregular clip-path: polygon(...) cutout so torn-edge shapes interlock; z-index orders the stack, position:relative on the container enables layering. Fully vanilla (Codrops uses only Grid + clip-path + flexbox; the Rellax parallax is optional, not required). Net-new vs vault: gallery-pack.css has 'polaroid-scatter' (rotated rectangles) and 'mosaic-featured', but NOTHING does clip-path torn/cutout edges or overlapping interlocked shapes — this is the editorial-collage / moodboard aesthetic the watercolor/print-typography skills gesture at but can't lay out. Encode as a fixed set of ~6 named clip-path 'cutout' classes + grid-area presets so composer drops a deterministic collage of N images.
backlog.license: Codrops/Tympanus tutorial code — MIT-style permissive, reimplementable
backlog.note: Pre-bake clip-path coordinates into named classes (.cut-torn-a..f) so output is deterministic and verifier-safe; pair with images.skill imagery floor. Complements polaroid-scatter rather than duplicating it.

backlog.name: Deconstructed-hero archetype (compose-genre / structure archetype)
backlog.what: An explicit 2026 trend section archetype: hero that breaks the centered/split mold via layered elements, overlapping headline-over-image, off-grid eyebrow, and a diagonal reading flow. structure.skill.md currently maps every genre to tidy archetypes and hero-pack's 'asymmetric' is just a 2-col big-left/small-right split (verified in hero-pack.css line 231) — there is no OVERLAPPING/z-stacked deconstructed hero. Add it as a new hero variant + a 'broken-editorial' page archetype row in structure.skill.md that sequences a deconstructed hero into the broken-grid body primitive above. Gives composer a way to deliberately AVOID the bento cliche when the brief wants editorial/agency/portfolio personality — a counter-default the engine can pick on purpose.
backlog.license: n/a (archetype/skill content, original)
backlog.note: Lives as a structure.skill.md archetype + one hero-pack variant reusing the broken-grid primitive; deterministic because it composes from fixed line-placed slots, not random scatter. Differentiation play vs over-produced bento.

backlog.name: Overflow/coherence gate for intentional-misalignment layouts
backlog.what: A verification refinement, not a dep: broken/overlap layouts deliberately break the usual 'no element overflows its parent' assumption, which would make the existing headless-Chrome save-gate either false-flag intentional bleed or, worse, pass genuinely-broken slop. Add a layout-aware check: when a block opts into data-broken/.bgrid, allow bounded overhang (within the page's safe gutter, no horizontal document scroll, no clipped text) but still reject true overflow and z-index text-occlusion. This protects the moat — it lets the engine ship the anti-bento aesthetic WITHOUT lowering the anti-slop bar (distinguishing 'designed misalignment' from 'CSS fell apart').
backlog.license: n/a (own diagnostic logic)
backlog.note: Smaller/optional but high-leverage: without it, broken-grid snippets will fight the verifier. Scope = teach book_inspect/coherence to treat bounded bleed inside the page gutter as intentional while still catching real overflow + occluded text.

backlog.name: scroll-state() container queries (@container scroll-state stuck/snapped/scrollable/scrolled)
backlog.what: Style elements based on a scroll container's live state with ZERO JS: container-type: scroll-state, then @container scroll-state(stuck: top) for sticky-header shadows, scroll-state(snapped: x) for highlighting/dimming the active snap slide, and scroll-state(scrollable: top/bottom) for scroll shadows / fade edges. Worth adding because the vault's sticky-header logic (.hdr is-stuck/is-scrolled in headers-pro.js), scroll-shadow edges, and snap highlighting are all currently done with rAF + scroll listeners; this replaces them with off-main-thread CSS — a net-new zero-dep capability that improves verification pass-rates (no layout thrash, no JS scroll handler). Ships Chrome/Edge 133+ (Feb 2025), 'scrolled' in Chrome 144 (Jan 2026); guard behind @supports(container-type: scroll-state) and degrade to a static style (no FF/Safari yet).
backlog.license: N/A (web platform feature, CSS)
backlog.note: Not in vault — grep for scroll-state/container-type:scroll-state returns ZERO files. Chrome/Edge-only (133+/144+); MUST sit behind @supports and degrade gracefully (static shadow/opacity). Pairs naturally with existing components/headers-pro.css and scroll-snap-scenes.

backlog.name: Native CSS pin & scrub via named view-timeline-name / scroll-timeline-name + timeline-scope
backlog.what: The vault's scroll-animations.css only uses ANONYMOUS view()/scroll(root) on single elements (simple reveals/parallax). Net-new is the NAMED-timeline tier: declare scroll-timeline-name/view-timeline-name on a scroller, reference it from any element with animation-timeline: --name, and use timeline-scope on a common ancestor to drive elements that are NOT descendants of the scroller. Combined with position: sticky this yields CSS-only PIN + SCRUB sequences (image-sequence/MacBook-lid/horizontal-pin) and a reading 'tracing-beam' — the zero-dep replacement for gsap/pin-section.js, gsap/horizontal-scroll.js and scroll/scroll-pin.js, scroll/macbook-scroll.js, scroll/horizontal-pin.js. Off-main-thread, jank-free. Worth adding as a vanilla snippet set because it eliminates a whole class of JS scroll handlers the vault currently ships.
backlog.license: N/A (web platform feature, CSS)
backlog.note: Anonymous view()/scroll() reveals + parallax ARE in animations/scroll-animations.css — do NOT re-add those. NET-NEW is named timelines + timeline-scope (ZERO vault files use view-timeline-name/scroll-timeline-name/timeline-scope) and the sticky-pin scrub sequences. Key pitfall to document: the `animation` shorthand RESETS animation-timeline to auto, so declare animation-timeline on its own line AFTER the shorthand. Include @supports + reduced-motion guards; Safari pre-26 still needs the existing JS fallbacks.

backlog.name: CSS-only carousel pseudo-elements (::scroll-marker / ::scroll-marker-group / ::scroll-button / :target-current)
backlog.what: CSS Overflow-5 generates accessible carousel UI with no JS: ::scroll-button(left/right) for prev/next, ::scroll-marker-group + ::scroll-marker for dot navigation, and :target-current to style the active dot — with built-in keyboard focusgroup behavior and ARIA tablist/scrollbar semantics for free. Relevant because the vault's carousels (components/carousel-pack.js, testimonials.js, css-3d coverflow) are all JS. Logged primarily as INSPIRATION / progressive-enhancement: Chrome/Edge 135+ ONLY (no FF/Safari), so it can layer on top of an existing scroll-snap track behind @supports but cannot replace the JS carousels yet. Lower priority than the two above because working JS carousels already exist and this is single-engine.
backlog.license: N/A (web platform feature, CSS)
backlog.note: Net-new technically (no ::scroll-marker/::scroll-button anywhere in vault) but Chrome-only and duplicative of working JS carousels → treat as inspiration / @supports enhancement, not a dependency. Accessibility win (native tablist/keyboard) is the real draw if added as an enhancement layer.

backlog.name: COLRv1 color fonts + CSS font-palette (@font-palette-values / override-colors)
backlog.what: Vector color fonts (gradients/layers baked into the glyphs) recolored entirely from CSS via @font-palette-values --p { font-family; base-palette; override-colors: 0 #hex, 1 #hex } + font-palette: --p. Free CDN-loadable COLRv1 families exist on Google Fonts (Nabla, Foldit, Bungee Spice, Rocher Color). Net-new: the vault has NO color-font / font-palette / COLRv1 coverage anywhere — its 'colorful headline' tools are CSS gradient-text clips and SVG, not real palette-driven chromatic glyphs. Degrades cleanly: Safari renders the base outline in a single solid `color`, so it is a graceful-degradation snippet, not a hard dep. Raises display-type craft and gives brand/dark-mode recoloring of one font file for free, feeding the typographic/coherence gate.
backlog.license: OFL-1.1 (the fonts; Nabla/Foldit/Bungee/Rocher all SIL Open Font License) — CSS technique is unencumbered

backlog.name: Scroll-driven variable-font weight (animation-timeline: scroll()/view() on font-variation-settings)
backlog.what: Zero-JS kinetic type: a @keyframes that interpolates `font-variation-settings: 'wght'/'wdth'/'opsz'` from..to, driven by animation-timeline: view(--section) or scroll(root) with animation-range. Headline morphs thin->bold (or condensed->expanded) as it enters/scrolls. Net-new: the vault uses animation-timeline EXACTLY ONCE (headers-pro.css, for header solidify) and NEVER on font axes; its variable-font kinetics are either hover/keyframe-time (variable-fonts.css) or cursor-DISTANCE lerp (variable-font-cursor.js) — scroll-POSITION-driven axis morphing is absent. Pure CSS, @supports-guarded, trivially reduced-motion-safe (animation: none). Gotcha to encode: wrappers need `overflow: clip` not `hidden` or the timeline breaks. High leverage for editorial/landing hero composition.
backlog.license: Unencumbered CSS standard (article CC-style, technique not licensable)

backlog.name: Velocity-driven variable-font axis (weight/width on scroll or pointer speed)
backlog.what: JS rAF loop that measures scroll (or pointer) VELOCITY and maps speed -> 'wght'/'wdth' on a heading via a CSS var, lerping back to rest — letters fatten/stretch when you scroll/move fast and relax when still. Net-new and distinct from everything present: variable-font-cursor.js interpolates by cursor DISTANCE (not speed), marquee.js uses scroll-velocity only for strip POSITION, and webgl-drag-gallery warps IMAGES by velocity — none drive a font axis by velocity. ~30 lines vanilla, sits beside variable-font-cursor.js as the 'velocity' sibling, reduced-motion clamps to a fixed weight. This is the specific 'motion-as-identity / weight-on-velocity' 2026 signal called out in the brief and is the bespoke-feeling tell.
backlog.license: Reimplemented vanilla (inspiration from Codrops/Fancy-components patterns); MIT-equivalent original code

backlog.name: Contextual optical sizing (font-optical-sizing: auto + size-matched opsz default)
backlog.what: A craft DEFAULT, not an effect: set `font-optical-sizing: auto` so opsz-equipped fonts (Inter, Roboto Flex, Newsreader, Source Serif 4, Fraunces) auto-tune stroke contrast/spacing to render size — display sizes get high-contrast display cuts, body gets sturdier text cuts. Net-new as PRACTICE: vault's variable-fonts.css exposes an opsz utility class and heading-pack/fluid-type ship clamp() type scales, but NOTHING wires optical sizing to the size ramp; it is mentioned in print-typography.skill.md only in passing. Best added as a one-line rule baked into the fluid type scale + a skill note ('match opsz to font-size; don't hardcode opsz against the size'). Cheap, invisible-but-felt polish that distinguishes bespoke from default and reinforces the typographic gate.
backlog.license: Unencumbered CSS standard; recommended fonts OFL-1.1

backlog.name: Adaptive theming engine: color-scheme + light-dark() + contrast-color()
backlog.what: A net-new theming skill + a vanilla CSS layer built on the 2024-26 native stack: `color-scheme: light dark` to opt in, `light-dark(<lightVal>, <darkVal>)` to collapse the duplicated dark/light token blocks into single declarations, and `contrast-color(var(--bg))` to auto-pick legible foreground/border text — a 'self-correcting' palette where one --brand property drives bg, text, border, placeholder via color-mix(in oklch, contrast-color(...) X%, ...). This is the single biggest theming upgrade available and directly modernizes the vault's two oldest theme files. Worth adding because the vault's dark-mode.css duplicates every token across light/dark/@media blocks (3x maintenance) and has no auto-contrast — light-dark() halves the CSS and contrast-color() makes the 65 palettes self-correct instead of hand-tuning each. Ship as a skill (decision tree: when to use light-dark vs custom props vs @media; color-scheme three-state light/dark/auto; per-component subtree overrides) plus a vanilla snippet that pairs the modern path with the existing var()-fallback so it degrades on old browsers.
backlog.license: n/a (native CSS, MDN/CSS Color L5 — royalty-free spec)
backlog.note: Partial overlap: vault has responsive/dark-mode.css (data-theme + prefers-color-scheme) and theme-switch.js, and color.skill mentions WCAG/APCA — but NONE of light-dark(), contrast-color(), color-scheme three-state, or self-correcting OKLCH derivation. Net-new technique layered on existing tokens. Must ship with @supports/var() fallback (contrast-color is binary black/white and weak on mid-tones — pre-pick a neutral fallback).

backlog.name: forced-colors / Windows High Contrast support layer
backlog.what: A vanilla CSS snippet (and a skill rule) that makes composed pages survive Windows High Contrast / forced-colors mode: rely on system color keywords (Canvas, CanvasText, ButtonText, LinkText, Highlight, ButtonBorder) inside `@media (forced-colors: active)`, restore visibility of borders/focus rings that get flattened, and AVOID the `forced-color-adjust: none` footgun. The vault composes lots of gradient/glassy surfaces with hairline alpha borders and box-shadow-only separation — exactly the things that vanish in forced-colors, so this is a real net-new accessibility gap. Pairs naturally with the light-dark skill. Worth adding because forced-colors is a measurable accessibility baseline (used by low-vision users) the vault currently ignores entirely (zero grep hits), and it's cheap, zero-dep, and degrades to nothing on browsers/users not in that mode.
backlog.license: n/a (native CSS media feature, MDN — royalty-free)
backlog.note: Zero coverage in vault (no forced-colors / forced-color-adjust / Canvas / CanvasText anywhere). Key guidance: prefer semantic HTML (auto-maps to system colors), only patch what disappears, never blanket forced-color-adjust:none.

backlog.name: Contrast + forced-colors render VERIFICATION gate
backlog.what: The moat play: extend the headless-Chrome verify step with two new diagnostics. (1) A contrast-ratio gate — sample computed fg/bg pairs for body text and assert WCAG 2 AA (4.5:1 / 3:1 large) OR APCA Lc thresholds the color.skill already references (Lc60/45), failing the save like overflow/coherence does. (2) A forced-colors render check — relaunch the page with Chrome's `--force-prefers-contrast` / emulated forced-colors (DevTools `Emulation.setEmulatedMedia` forced-colors=active) and screenshot to confirm nothing renders invisible (text==bg, vanished borders/focus). This turns accessibility from advice into a measurable save-gate, consistent with the existing anti-slop/imagery-floor philosophy. Worth adding because contrast and forced-colors are 2026 baseline expectations AND objectively checkable in the existing Chrome harness — it's the kind of verification capability that is the vault's differentiator, not a snippet anyone can copy.
backlog.license: n/a (built on existing headless-Chrome harness + WCAG/APCA math, both open)
backlog.note: No contrast/forced-colors gate exists (coherence check is anti-slop/overflow/imagery, not a11y). color.skill claims palettes are 'contrast-checked' by hand — this makes it enforced and automated. Highest-moat item: a diagnostic, not a copyable snippet.

backlog.name: prefers-contrast adaptive high-contrast theme tier
backlog.what: A small CSS pattern (fold into the theming skill) that adds a third theme dimension beyond light/dark: respond to `@media (prefers-contrast: more)` by boosting border/text contrast, making muted text hit full 4.5:1, and strengthening focus outlines; optionally `prefers-contrast: less` for low-contrast preference. Because the vault's token model is role-based (--fg/--muted/--faint/--border), a high-contrast tier is just a handful of token overrides per palette — high leverage. Worth adding as the lightweight, broadly-supported complement to forced-colors (which is heavier and Windows-specific): prefers-contrast is honored on macOS/iOS 'Increase Contrast' and Android too, so it covers users the forced-colors layer misses.
backlog.license: n/a (native CSS media feature, MDN — royalty-free)
backlog.note: No prefers-contrast usage in vault. Lower standalone value — best bundled into the same theming skill as item 1 rather than a separate skill. Complements forced-colors (cross-platform vs Windows-centric).

backlog.name: Scroll-stacking cards section (sticky pin + scale-down stack)
backlog.what: The single most-shipped 2025-26 award-page 'How it works / feature reveal' archetype: a column of full-bleed cards where each pins with position:sticky;top:0 and the previous card scales down + dims as the next slides over it, building a physical stack as you scroll (Apple, Stripe, navigator.com, every recent Awwwards SOTD agency/3D portfolio). The vault has the INGREDIENTS but not THIS section: card-stack.css is a rotating auto-deck (top slides off on a timer), and sticky-scroll-reveal.css is a two-column text-scrolls/image-swaps pattern — neither is the pinned-and-scaling vertical stack. Worth adding as a compose-genre section block (a labeled slot in structure.skill.md's SaaS/Agency archetypes) implemented as a small vanilla scroll-stack.css/.js: N children, each .stack-card { position:sticky; top:Npx } with a scale/translate driven by index (native animation-timeline:view() with an IntersectionObserver fallback for Safari/Firefox). Pairs with existing aos-lite/ScrollAnim infra; degrades to a plain stacked list with reduced-motion.
backlog.license: Public-domain technique (CSS sticky + scroll-driven animations); reimplement, not a dep

backlog.name: Tabbed feature-showcase section (tab-nav ↔ large product preview)
backlog.what: A distinct marketing SECTION archetype now standard on Figma/Linear/Vercel/Notion 2026 pages: a horizontal (or vertical) tab rail of feature/use-case/persona labels wired to one big sticky preview panel (screenshot, browser-frame mock, short loop, or interactive demo) that swaps on tab change, often auto-advancing with a progress bar. The vault has generic UI tabs (tabs.css, animated-tabs.css, tabs-pack-2.css) and a device-frame/demo-video mockup, but NOT the COMPOSED section that marries a tab rail to a swapping product-preview pane as a page block — it's a structure.skill.md gap (no 'feature-tabs' / 'product-tour' slot in any archetype). Add as a compose-genre section: .ftour-rail (tabs, reuses animated-tabs) + .ftour-stage (sticky preview that crossfades, reuses device-frame mockups), optional auto-advance with the existing progress-bar primitives. Net-new because it's the wiring/archetype, not the tabs themselves; high realism payoff for SaaS pages.
backlog.license: N/A (composition of existing MIT-spirit vault primitives)

backlog.name: Structured-data / JSON-LD injector + verify gate (FAQPage, Product, SoftwareApplication, Organization, BreadcrumbList)
backlog.what: Across all 970 components the vault has ZERO schema.org / JSON-LD / structured-data capability, yet every real award/marketing page ships it. This is the highest-leverage gap because it maps to the verification MOAT, not just a snippet: a composer step that derives JSON-LD from already-composed sections (FAQ accordion → FAQPage, pricing → Product/Offer, hero+nav → Organization/SoftwareApplication, breadcrumbs → BreadcrumbList) and a verify check that (a) the page emits valid JSON-LD and (b) it matches the visible content (Google requires Q&A to be visible on-page). IMPORTANT 2026 nuance to encode in the skill: Google REMOVED FAQ rich results from Search (gone May 7 2026), so do NOT sell it as 'rich snippets' — its current value is GEO/AEO: FAQPage/structured pages are cited far more by AI Overviews, ChatGPT and Perplexity. Add as a vanilla-snippet (a tiny buildJsonLd() helper + <script type=application/ld+json> emitter) PLUS a verification gate; degrades to nothing if absent.
backlog.license: schema.org vocabulary is open (CC/community); JSON-LD is a W3C standard — fully permissive

backlog.name: Animated/interactive bento feature section (hover + scroll-reveal + in-context ticker stats)
backlog.what: Bento is now the default product-page feature layout (Apple, Pixel, Microsoft, Spotify, Linear), but the 2026 AWARD version is not the static grid the vault ships (bento-grid.css = size classes only) — it's an ANIMATED bento: tiles stagger-reveal on scroll, lift/spotlight on hover, and embed live content (count-up ticker stats, mini-charts, a looping clip, a marquee) INSIDE specific cells. The vault has every ingredient separately (bento-grid.css, card-fx cfx-bento stagger-lift, aos-lite/ScrollAnim, stats.js count-up, sparklines) but no composed 'feature-bento' section archetype that wires them, and structure.skill.md only references bento as a raw grid slot. Add as a compose-genre section block: a bento where each cell maps to a content-type recipe (stat-tile→count-up, chart-tile→sparkline, media-tile→clip/marquee, text-tile) with scroll-stagger entrance and hover-lift baked in. Mostly assembly of existing snippets; net-new as a named, composable section.
backlog.license: N/A (composition of existing vault primitives)

backlog.name: reduced-motion compliance gate (audit.js upgrade: semantic, not string-presence)
backlog.what: The vault's existing reduced-motion check (tools/audit.js L179-180) is a crude substring test: animated CSS passes if the literal 'prefers-reduced-motion' appears ANYWHERE (even in a comment), it only scans .css (the JS else-branch never checks motion at all), and it never validates the fallback is correct. Upgrade it into a real gate that (a) parses the @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) block and confirms it actually neutralizes the animated selectors (duration->0/0.01ms or animation:none on the SAME selectors that animate) rather than just name-dropping the query; (b) flags the 'animate-in-from-hidden stays invisible' footgun (opacity:0/transform start-state with no snap-to-end under reduce); (c) extends to JS snippets — require a matchMedia('(prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)') guard in any file that calls requestAnimationFrame / WAAPI .animate() / setInterval-driven motion. This is the net-new moat: a verification capability that FAILS snippets lacking a real fallback, matching the briefs MotionScore/Pope-Tech 2025 methodology. Reuses the audit's existing --strict soft-warning plumbing and a11y-codemod auto-fix.
backlog.license: n/a (in-repo tooling; methodology per WCAG C39/SCR40, public)

backlog.name: MotionScore / Web Animation Performance Tier List (GPU-cost + will-change budget grading)
backlog.what: Motion.dev's static animation-performance audit grades animated PROPERTIES by compositor cost (transform/opacity = cheap; layout-triggering width/top/margin = expensive; repaint-heavy box-shadow/filter = costly) and flags anti-patterns like excessive standing will-change and flashing. The product itself is closed (Motion+ subscription / score.motion.dev SaaS) so it is INSPIRATION, not a dependency — but the underlying 'tier list' methodology is fully reimplementable as a zero-dep static scan. Net-new for the vault: a will-change/contain budget check (vault USES will-change in ~8+ snippets and gsap.skill.md merely warns 'don't hand-set it on many elements' but nothing ENFORCES it) plus a 'animating a layout property' flag. Pairs naturally with the reduced-motion gate above as one 'motion health' verification pass — performant AND accessible motion in one gate.
backlog.license: closed / Motion+ paywall (SaaS) — reimplement methodology only, do NOT depend on it

backlog.name: In-page motion toggle (WCAG 2.2.2 Pause/Stop/Hide) — user-override beyond the OS setting
backlog.what: The vault honors the OS prefers-reduced-motion everywhere but has ZERO in-page control to pause/stop motion (grep for 'motion toggle / 2.2.2 / data-reduce' returns nothing). The 2025 consensus (Pope Tech, GSAP a11y, MDN) is that OS-reliance 'isn't bulletproof' — many motion-sensitive users never find the OS toggle — so a UI control is the accessibility baseline for WCAG 2.2.2. Ship a tiny vanilla snippet: a toggle that sets a documentElement[data-reduce-motion] / [data-motion-off] attribute persisted to localStorage, plus a one-line CSS contract ([data-reduce-motion] *{animation-duration:.01ms!important; transition-duration:.01ms!important}) so every existing snippet inherits it for free. It should compose the OS query with the user override (reduce = OS-reduce OR user-toggle), exactly the gsap.matchMedia conditions pattern. Net-new, low-surface, and it makes the whole 920-snippet motion library WCAG 2.2.2-compliant with one control.
backlog.license: MIT-equivalent (original vanilla impl; pattern per WCAG 2.2.2 / SCR40)

backlog.name: Vestibular-safe 'reduce != remove' fallback recipe (crossfade-instead-of-translate)
backlog.what: The vault's current a11y-codemod SNAPS animations to their end-state under reduce (good for entrance fade-ins, avoids the invisible footgun) — but the 2025 best practice (GSAP a11y, Apple HIG, Chrome VT guidance) is a graded 'reduce vs remove' decision: large x/y translations and parallax should be REPLACED with a gentle opacity crossfade that preserves spatial/relational meaning, not just zeroed. Add a short decision-tree skill (.skill.md): is the motion triggering (large translate/scale/parallax/loop = yes) vs decorative-micro (opacity/color = safe); is it functional (progress, state-change — keep but simplify to crossfade) vs purely decorative (remove). Codifies the Apple 'swipe -> crossfade' substitution and the dual-branch (no-preference vs reduce) authoring pattern so composed pages choose vestibular-safe fallbacks by default. This is the authoring-judgment companion to the verification gate.
backlog.license: n/a (guidance synthesized from GSAP a11y docs + WCAG, public)

backlog.name: Inspiration-gallery landscape + ToS/license map (Godly, Mobbin, Land-book, Savee, Httpster, Refero, Awwwards)
backlog.what: A single reference doc that taxonomizes the live web-inspiration-sourcing landscape and, critically, encodes each source's ToS/license posture so the planner knows WHERE to mine net-new archetypes and which are SaaS-closed (inspiration-only, never a dep, never scrape). The vault has awwwards-3d-inspiration.md (Awwwards, 3D-only) and asset-libraries.md (CC0 asset sources) but NOTHING mapping the categorized-UI-pattern galleries or their reuse constraints. Concrete, verified-June-2026 facts to encode: Refero = 125K+ real screens taxonomized by page-type/flow/UX-pattern/UI-element/site-category/font + brand/mood/color/typography axes (richest taxonomy to mine archetype names from); Mobbin = mobile+web app flows, ToS (updated 2026-05-16) EXPLICITLY prohibits scraping/mirroring/caching/re-hosting and commercial reuse, free tier limited, Pro $40/seat/mo -> inspiration-only, never automate; Land-book = categories by color/style/industry/device, free tier + Pro ~$9/mo -> good cheap human-readable taxonomy; Savee = human-curated moodboards, NO free plan, ToS grants Savee broad license over user content -> inspiration-only; Godly = bold brand-led sites (Awwwards-adjacent), free browse; Httpster = grid/style-grouped real sites, still active 2026. Encoding the do/don't (study the taxonomy + reverse-engineer the technique vs. never scrape/cache/redistribute) keeps anti-duplication grounded in real-world taxonomies and protects the 'inspiration, never a dep' rule.
backlog.license: Mixed/SaaS — Mobbin ToS prohibits scraping+commercial reuse (closed); Savee ToS broad-license, no free plan (closed); Land-book free+Pro; Godly/Httpster free browse; all copyright-protected third-party UI (study, do not redistribute)
backlog.url: https://refero.design/ https://mobbin.com/terms https://land-book.com/ https://savee.com/terms/ https://godly.website/ https://httpster.net/
backlog.note: Partial overlap: awwwards-3d-inspiration.md covers ONLY Awwwards 3D as a study-reference, and asset-libraries.md covers CC0 ASSET sources (not inspiration galleries). No file maps the categorized-UI-pattern galleries or their ToS. Best home: a sibling 'inspiration-galleries.md' reference next to awwwards-3d-inspiration.md, cross-linked from art-direction.skill.md + taste.skill.md. CRITICAL CAVEAT to bake in: Mobbin/Savee ToS forbid scraping/caching/redistribution and commercial exploitation — usable ONLY as human-eyes inspiration, never as an automated source or dep.

backlog.name: Refero Styles MCP (lorecraft-io/refero-design-mcp)
backlog.what: An MIT-licensed, free-to-search MCP server that does natural-language search across the curated styles.refero.design DESIGN.md library and synthesizes a DESIGN.md (role-tagged colors, typography, spacing, components, per-style do/don't) locally from Refero's public designSystem blocks. The vault ALREADY documents the DESIGN.md *format* and credits styles.refero.design in design-system.skill.md, and even ships an MCP design_system(palette) tool that GENERATES a DESIGN.md from a repo palette — but that is generate-from-our-own-palette. What is net-new is the open, free, no-account-required MCP that pulls TASTE FROM THE LIVE ~2000-style external library on demand, which is exactly the 'fetch external taste, realise with vault parts' bridge design-system.skill.md describes but currently can only hand-author. Wiring this in (or porting its catalog-search idea) turns the documented bridge into a live pipeline: search Refero -> get DESIGN.md -> map onto palettes + snippets via the existing skill. No paid key needed (OpenAI key only optional for semantic vibe-search; keyword scoring works without it), unlike the official referodesign/refero_skill which needs a paid bearer token.
backlog.license: MIT (server) — Refero Styles catalog free to search; DESIGN.md synthesized from Refero's public designSystem blocks (third-party UI is copyright-protected, treat output as taste-reference)
backlog.note: Anti-dup: the DESIGN.md concept and Refero credit are ALREADY in design-system.skill.md, so propose this as the OPERATIONALIZATION of that existing skill, not a new concept. Two open MIT mirrors exist (lorecraft-io and fidgetcoding/refero-design-mcp); prefer the MIT one with no required API key. Flag the official referodesign/refero_skill (also MIT) as needing a PAID refero.design/mcp bearer token — note it but the lorecraft MCP is the dependency-free path. Best add: a short 'live source' section in design-system.skill.md pointing at this MCP as the way to pull external taste on demand.

backlog.name: ThumbHash (vanilla decoder snippet)
backlog.what: MIT, ~280-line zero-dep JS that decodes a ~25-char base64 hash to a 32x32 RGBA preview and renders it via thumbHashToDataURL() (uncompressed PNG data URL) or thumbHashToRGBA() onto a . Encodes a higher-res luminance + low-res chroma + optional alpha + aspect ratio in the same space BlurHash uses, with better colors. Worth adding because the vault has skeleton shimmers and a reveal-mask animation but NO runtime placeholder that shows a blurred preview of the actual image. Ship as a vanilla snippet (decoder + a wireup that reads data-thumbhash on every , paints the data URL as background, then fades the real image in on .decode()/onload) — raises the perceived-quality floor and prevents CLS since the hash carries aspect ratio. Encode side is the existing build skill (Sharp -> rgbaToThumbHash).
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: Decoder is fully reimplementable as a vault .js (no deps, uses Uint8Array + btoa). ~25-char string lives in HTML as data-thumbhash. Pair with build-time encode in images.skill.md. Net-new 'placeholder/skeleton' family member — complements, does not duplicate, skeleton-pack/image-reveal-mask.

backlog.name: CSS-only LQIP (Lean Rada, single --lqip integer)
backlog.what: A blurry image placeholder decoded ENTIRELY in CSS — zero JavaScript. One integer (-999,999..999,999, ~20 bits: base OKLAB color + a 3x2 grid of 6 brightness components) lives in style="--lqip:567213" and is unpacked via calc()/mod()/round(down) into 6 overlapping radial gradients + a base linear gradient. Renders instantly with the first paint (no decode step, no canvas, no JS), so it's the lightest-touch option and the natural default for the engine's composed pages. Strong fit because Design Book composes deterministically and prizes vanilla CSS; this is a pure CSS snippet with a tiny build-side encoder. Lower fidelity than ThumbHash but unbeatable cost.
backlog.license: Unspecified on the post (technique/algorithm) — reimplement the encoder + CSS from scratch as inspiration; do not copy author's code wholesale.
backlog.note: Depends on OKLAB + mod()/round() in calc() (Baseline 2024-ish) — degrade to a solid average-color background on older engines. Zero-JS makes it ideal for the default imagery floor; pair ThumbHash for higher-fidelity heroes.

backlog.name: Image-placeholder verification gate (does every carry an LQIP?)
backlog.what: A new diagnostic in the headless-Chrome save-gate: flag any composed that lacks BOTH (a) an intrinsic-size hint (width/height or aspect-ratio) AND (b) a runtime placeholder (background data URL from a ThumbHash decode, or a --lqip custom property). This turns the new placeholder snippets into an enforceable standard and directly targets CLS — the gate already has an 'imagery floor' philosophy, so this extends the moat from 'has imagery' to 'imagery loads without layout shift / blank flashes'. Cheap to implement (DOM query in the existing render-smoke harness) and high leverage because it makes the floor measurable.
backlog.license: N/A (own diagnostic)
backlog.note: Builds on the existing save-gate (render-smoke.mjs, coherence score, imagery floor). Only valuable once at least one placeholder snippet (ThumbHash and/or CSS-LQIP) is in the vault to satisfy the check.

backlog.name: SplatHash
backlog.what: Newer (v1.3.0, Feb 2026) MIT alternative: exactly 16 bytes / 22-char base64url, decodes to a 32x32 preview in ~0.067ms (vs ThumbHash ~0.5ms, BlurHash ~6.5ms) using a background color + 6 Gaussian blobs placed by matching pursuit, all in OKLAB. Bit-identical hashes across Go/TS/Python. Smallest fixed-size hash and fastest decode; representable as a single integer. Worth tracking as the cutting-edge contender, but younger ecosystem and only ~200 stars — so propose as inspiration / second-choice rather than the primary, since ThumbHash's alpha+aspect support and battle-tested canonical status make it the safer default.
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: Note as the 'newest/fastest/smallest' option to watch; lacks ThumbHash's alpha channel and broad porting. Don't ship as primary until adoption matures — flag as inspiration, ThumbHash is the recommended dep.

backlog.name: canvas-scroll-sequence (vanilla snippet — the AirPods scroll-to-play-a-video effect)
backlog.what: The single most-requested premium product-reveal pattern of 2025, and the vault has NO image-sequence canvas player. A ~120-line zero-dep ES module: ScrollSequence.init(canvas, {frames:'/seq/airpods_####.jpg', count:148, start, end}) preloads N Image() objects (padStart-numbered URLs), tracks scroll progress over a pinned/sticky section via one scroll listener throttled into rAF, computes nearest frame = round(progress*(count-1)), and drawImage()s it to a DPR-scaled, object-fit:cover canvas. Degrades to a single poster (frame 0) when JS is off, reduced-motion is set, or the connection is slow (navigator.connection.saveData). Pairs directly with the existing scroll/ folder (sits beside scroll-scrub.js / macbook-scroll.js) and the imagery floor in the verifier. This is net-new: scroll-scrub.js only tweens DOM opacity/x/y, and macbook/container-scroll are CSS-transform device frames — none draw frames to canvas.
backlog.license: N/A (original implementation; technique is public-domain, CSS-Tricks/Codepen reference)

backlog.name: @mediamonks/fast-image-sequence
backlog.what: MIT, genuinely zero-dependency, actively maintained (v2.2.0, 415 commits) production reference for high-FPS canvas image sequences. Its standout 2025 capability: decode frames on demand from a single MP4 via WebCodecs VideoDecoder (H.264; Chrome 94+/Safari 16.4+/Firefox 130+) and/or load frames packed in one .tar, instead of firing N separate HTTP requests for N JPEGs — plus a hierarchical/LRU cache (maxCachedImages, hierarchicalCacheFraction) and progressive low-to-high quality fallback. Exposes progress/frameCount/loadProgress/getFrameImage()/onLoadProgress(). Best added as INSPIRATION, not a dep: it is frame-DISPLAY only (no built-in scroll binding) and is npm/ESM-first (React subpath), so the vault should reimplement its tar/WebCodecs preload+cache strategy inside the vanilla snippet above rather than CDN-loading it.
backlog.license: MIT

backlog.name: Scroll-to-scrub single MP4 via requestVideoFrameCallback + WebCodecs
backlog.what: The 2025 evolution of the AirPods pattern that ships ONE optimized MP4 (dense keyframes — keyframe every ~5 frames for Chrome/Safari, every 2 for Firefox) instead of 148 JPEGs, slashing payload and decode cost. Vanilla technique: map scroll progress to video.currentTime = progress*duration, and use HTMLVideoElement.requestVideoFrameCallback() to know exactly when the seeked frame is painted (frame-accurate, jank-free) — optionally drawing to canvas. No vault snippet does video-currentTime scroll seeking or rVFC (video-player-pro is a play/pause UI). Ideal as a SECOND MODE of the canvas-scroll-sequence snippet ({source:'mp4'} vs {source:'frames'}), degrading to a paused <video> poster where rVFC is unsupported. Net-new and complementary to the frame-folder mode.
backlog.license: N/A (Web Platform API — HTMLVideoElement.requestVideoFrameCallback, baseline-ish)

backlog.name: CSS scroll-driven sprite-sheet sequence (animation-timeline: scroll() + steps())
backlog.what: A JS-free, main-thread-free degrade/variant: pack frames into a sprite sheet (or use a steps() keyframe over object-position / background-position) and drive it with animation-timeline: scroll() so frames advance off the compositor with no scroll listener and no canvas. ~85% caniuse (Chrome/Edge/Safari ship it; Firefox behind a flag) with an official polyfill, so it is the perfect reduced-JS fallback layer for the canvas snippet and an accessible lightweight option for short sequences. Vault confirmed to have ZERO scroll-driven-CSS / animation-timeline / sprite-sheet coverage. Best as a small vanilla CSS+JS snippet (or the no-JS branch of the main snippet) with the polyfill behind a need()-guard.
backlog.license: N/A (CSS Web Platform feature; scroll-timeline polyfill is Apache-2.0)

backlog.name: CSS scroll-driven sprite/frame-sequence scrub (animation-timeline + steps())
backlog.what: A zero-JS scroll-scrubbed image-sequence: a sprite sheet (or single tall/wide strip) is stepped frame-by-frame as the user scrolls, using `animation-timeline: scroll()` or `view()` to bind progress to scroll, and `steps(N, jump-none)` to snap `background-position` (or `object-position`) to discrete frames. steps(N) where N = frame count produces crisp frame swaps with NO interpolation. This is the JS-free version of the classic Apple AirPods pinned scroll sequence, which the vault only does today via raf/canvas-style JS (scroll-scrub.js). Worth adding because it is a genuinely new 2024-2026 native capability the vault encodes nowhere — its scroll-animations.css uses ONLY continuous easing (ease/linear) and never steps(), and there is zero sprite-sheet/frame-swap coverage. Lets the engine emit JS-free scroll scrubbing where supported. 2026 support: Chrome/Edge 115+, Safari 26 (full, landed early 2026), Firefox partial/flagged; gate behind `@supports (animation-timeline: scroll())` and fall back to a single static hero frame (and honor prefers-reduced-motion).
backlog.license: Native CSS (no license / public web platform)
backlog.note: Net-new: animations/scroll-animations.css covers view()/scroll() for opacity/transform/parallax with continuous easing only — never steps() and never a sprite/frame swap; scroll/scroll-scrub.js is the JS (raf) equivalent. Ship as a .css snippet + a decision note pointing to scroll-scrub.js as the cross-browser fallback. Pair with a WebP/AVIF sprite-sheet recommendation (Apple's 65-frame PNG seq = 15MB drops to ~1.7MB as WebP).

backlog.name: CSS Carousel API (::scroll-marker / ::scroll-marker-group / ::scroll-button())
backlog.what: Native, zero-JS carousel chrome: `::scroll-marker` generates the dot indicators (grouped via `scroll-marker-group`), `::scroll-button(left/right)` generates prev/next buttons that scroll the list, all on top of a scroll-snap container — no JS for active-dot tracking or button wiring (the browser does it, including focus/keyboard a11y). This is the other headline JS-free 2026 scroll primitive and the vault has zero coverage (no scroll-marker/scroll-button hits in INDEX; scroll-snap-scenes.css is snap-only with no markers/buttons). Directly on-brand for the zero-dep ethos: replaces a whole class of carousel JS. Support is newer/narrower than scroll-driven animations: Chrome/Edge 135+ (Mar 2025), Safari 18.2+ (Dec 2025), Firefox not yet shipped mid-2026 — so gate behind `@supports (scroll-marker-group: after)` and fall back to the plain scroll-snap carousel (which the vault already has), where markers/buttons simply don't render.
backlog.license: Native CSS (no license / public web platform)
backlog.note: Net-new: grep of INDEX.md finds no scroll-marker/::scroll-button/css-carousel-without-js. Builds directly on existing scroll/scroll-snap-scenes.css as the fallback layer. Note narrower support than the sprite-scrub technique — Firefox absent mid-2026 — so it must degrade to a bare scroll-snap strip.

backlog.name: Scroll-driven 'when to go JS-free' decision note (skill addendum)
backlog.what: A short decision-tree addendum (for images.skill.md or a new scroll skill) that codifies WHEN the engine should emit pure-CSS scroll scrubbing vs. the existing JS path. Rule: if the effect is a frame-sequence/parallax/reveal AND target is evergreen Chromium/Safari 26+, emit the @supports-guarded CSS (animation-timeline + steps()/easing) and ship a static-frame fallback; if it needs Firefox parity, precise pause/scrub control, or canvas-quality crossfade between frames, route to scroll-scrub.js / canvas. Encodes the 2026 support matrix (Chrome/Edge 115+, Safari 26 full, Firefox flagged for scroll-driven; Chrome 135+/Safari 18.2+ for carousel pseudo-elements, Firefox absent), the two @supports guards, the Firefox 'non-zero animation-duration' gotcha, and mandatory prefers-reduced-motion handling. Worth adding so the two snippets above are emitted SAFELY and deterministically rather than ad hoc, and so the verifier's imagery/coherence gates know a static fallback frame must exist.
backlog.license: N/A (internal decision note)
backlog.note: Complements existing scroll-animations.css + scroll-scrub.js by adding the missing 'discrete frames / steps() / JS-free vs JS' decision layer and the carousel-pseudo-element support caveats. Keep additive per the vault's additive-only policy.

backlog.name: Iconify on-demand API loader (vanilla need()-guarded resolver)
backlog.what: A ~40-line vanilla JS loader that resolves named icons like 'lucide:arrow-right', 'ph:bell', 'tabler:user' at runtime by hitting the public Iconify API. Two endpoints, both verified live: the SVG endpoint GET https://api.iconify.design/{prefix}/{name}.svg (defaults to width/height=1em + stroke/fill=currentColor, so it themes for free) and — leaner for batches — the JSON endpoint GET https://api.iconify.design/{prefix}.json?icons=a,b,c which returns {width,height,icons:{name:{body}}} where body already uses currentColor; the loader wraps each body in <svg viewBox='0 0 W H'> and injects inline. Covers Lucide (ISC), Phosphor 'ph' (MIT), Tabler (MIT) and ~200k more from one resolver. This is exactly the gap: composed pages can scan for <i data-icon='lucide:arrow-right'> and hydrate. CORS is '*', cache is 7-day immutable, and it degrades gracefully behind a need()-style guard (if fetch fails, fall back to the 36 pure-CSS icons). 'Tree-shaking' is inherent — only requested names are fetched, nothing is bundled. Net-new: vault knows these only as asset libraries in asset-libraries.md, never as a runtime resolver.
backlog.license: API code MIT; icon sets per-set (Lucide ISC, Phosphor MIT, Tabler MIT — all attribution-free). API service free for production, no key.
backlog.note: Best leanest path. Verified live: default SVG = width/height=1em + currentColor. Guard against network with a need()-style check (pattern already in 3d/scene-runner.js) falling back to ic-* pure-CSS icons. Pin which sets you use in a license note (asset-libraries.md already tracks this).

backlog.name: Self-hosted Iconify-JSON sprite (offline @iconify-json/{set} -> <symbol> + <use href>)
backlog.what: The license-clean, zero-network fallback to the API loader: ship a build-once-at-runtime SVG symbol sprite. A vanilla builder fetches the offline IconifyJSON package (verified live on jsdelivr: @iconify-json/lucide/icons.json = 566KB full Lucide set, already monotone-normalized to currentColor and script-free), picks only the names a page references, and emits a single hidden <svg><symbol id='i-arrow-right' viewBox='0 0 24 24'>{body}</symbol>...</svg> block; pages then reference <svg class='ic'><use href='#i-arrow-right'/></svg>, which inherits currentColor and any font-size. This is the classic sprite pattern but assembled at runtime from a real, MIT-packaged data source — no build dep, no per-icon HTTP, fully offline/self-hostable (good for headless-Chrome verification where external fetches are flaky). Same @iconify-json/{prefix} packages exist for ph and tabler, so one builder spans all three sets. Net-new: vault has gradient-defs SVG <defs> injection but no symbol-sprite icon assembler.
backlog.license: @iconify-json/* packages MIT-packaged; underlying sets Lucide ISC / Phosphor MIT / Tabler MIT.
backlog.note: Pair with finding #1 as the offline/degraded path so the verifier never depends on a live API. <use href> + currentColor is the cleanest theming. lucide-static/sprite.svg also exists on jsdelivr (verified 200) as a prebuilt full-set sprite if you prefer a single static file.

backlog.name: Iconify search/autocomplete endpoint -> upgrade icon-picker from emoji to real line-icons
backlog.what: The existing blocks/icon-picker.js is emoji-based (70+ chars) with a documented hook for custom SVG sets, but has no discovery. The verified Iconify search endpoint GET /search?query=arrow&limit=N returns a ranked list of fully-qualified names (e.g. ['mdi:arrow','uil:arrow','clarity:arrow-line',...]) across all sets, which the picker can render as live previews via the SVG endpoint from finding #1. This turns the emoji picker into a real Notion/Linear-grade icon search over 200k named icons with zero local index — a small, high-leverage wire-up of the picker the vault already ships. Scope it to lucide/ph/tabler prefixes for license cleanliness.
backlog.license: API MIT; results filterable to ISC/MIT sets.
backlog.note: Smallest add of the three; an enhancement layer on an existing block. Behind a need()-guard, degrades to the current emoji set when offline. Filter prefixes to license-clean sets per asset-libraries.md guidance.

backlog.name: Icon-family taste decision skill (Lucide / Phosphor / Tabler / Heroicons / Material Symbols / Remix)
backlog.what: A .skill.md decision tree that maps page aesthetic to the right icon family, with each family's 2026 stroke/grid/license characteristics baked in so the engine picks one coherent set per page instead of mixing. Verified specs: Lucide (ISC, 1,800+ icons, 24px grid, 2px default stroke, CSS-tunable stroke-width, rounded caps — Feather heir, shadcn/Tailwind default → clean minimal SaaS/dev/startup); Tabler (MIT, 5,800+, 24px grid, 2px stroke, line+filled, stroke-width tunable → dense dashboards/admin, widest coverage on one grid); Phosphor (MIT, 7,700+, 256px design grid, SIX weights thin/light/regular/bold/fill/duotone → playful/editorial/marketing, duotone for warmth); Heroicons (MIT, ~316 curated, 24px outline @1.5px + 20px/16px solid/mini → polished Tailwind product UI with built-in outline/solid state pairs); Material Symbols (Apache-2.0, ~3,600, variable font with 4 axes weight 100-700/fill 0-100/grade -50..200/optical-size 20-48, outlined/rounded/sharp → Google/Android-feel, animatable axes); Remix Icon (Apache-2.0, ~3,200, 24px grid, line+fill, neutral 1.5px → mixes cleanly with Lucide/Feather, general system UI). Net-new because the vault only has a flat 'default to Lucide/Phosphor/Tabler' line in asset-libraries.md with NO vibe mapping, no Heroicons/Material/Remix, and no stroke/grid/weight characteristics — exactly the taste-aware selection the brief calls for.
backlog.license: Skill content; references ISC (Lucide), MIT (Phosphor/Tabler/Heroicons), Apache-2.0 (Material Symbols/Remix) — all permissive, no attribution
backlog.note: Partial overlap only: asset-libraries.md line 28-31 lists Lucide/Phosphor/Tabler + licenses but is a flat 'default to these' with zero decision logic, no stroke/grid/weight specs, and omits Heroicons/Material Symbols/Remix. This skill is the missing taste layer, not a duplicate. Pair it with the loader snippet below.

backlog.name: iconify-icon web component (on-demand icon loader, any of 200k+ icons, zero build)
backlog.what: A single ~3KB web component (`<iconify-icon icon="lucide:home">`) that fetches just the icons used on a page from the Iconify API at runtime and renders inline SVG in shadow DOM — no bundling, no build step, degrades to nothing if blocked. This is the zero-dep vanilla loader that lets the decision-skill's family choice (lucide:, ph:, tabler:, heroicons:, material-symbols:, ri:) actually render with one syntax across ALL six families. The vault has NO icon-rendering mechanism at all — icon-picker.js is emoji chars, social-icons expects you to paste your own SVG. Wrapping this behind a need()-guard gives the engine a real icon pipeline while keeping the verifier's imagery floor honest (icons render as crisp vectors, not missing glyphs).
backlog.license: MIT (component); per-set licenses for the icon data, but the six families in the skill are all ISC/MIT/Apache
backlog.note: Iconify aggregates per-set licenses — the loader snippet should pin to the skill's six permissive families by prefix and record licenses, per asset-libraries.md line 30's existing guidance. Shadow-DOM render means page CSS can't recolor it unless you set color/font-size on the host — note this in the snippet.

backlog.name: Variable-axes icon animation pattern (Material Symbols fill/weight/grade transitions)
backlog.what: A reimplementable technique: because Material Symbols is a single variable font, you can CSS-transition its axes (`font-variation-settings: 'FILL' 0..1, 'wght' 400..700, 'GRAD', 'opsz'`) to animate an icon from outline→filled or thin→bold on hover/active/state-change with zero JS and zero extra assets — the same glyph morphs. This is a genuinely different motion primitive from the vault's existing svg-animations.js (path-length stroke-draw / morph between two paths): no two SVGs needed, it's pure font-variation interpolation, GPU-cheap, and gives the 'Google/Material' tactile feel (e.g. nav active-state fill, favorite-toggle, play/pause weight pop). Reimplement as a tiny CSS recipe in the icon skill rather than a dependency.
backlog.license: Apache-2.0 (Material Symbols font itself)
backlog.note: Distinct from svg/svg-animations.js (path-draw + two-path morph). This is font-variation-settings interpolation on one glyph. Note: needs the variable font loaded (not the per-icon SVGs), so it's a Material-Symbols-only trick — call that out so the engine only reaches for it on Material/Google-feel pages.

backlog.name: feSpecularLighting / feDiffuseLighting lit-emboss paper-fiber texture (nnnoise technique)
backlog.what: A noise overlay that pipes feTurbulence into feSpecularLighting (or feDiffuseLighting) with a feDistantLight, producing a LIT, embossed paper/fiber surface with real highlight-and-shadow depth rather than the vault's flat grey speckle. surfaceScale + elevation + specularConstant give a tactile 'this was printed on stock' read that directly attacks the gradients-look-AI tell. Fully data-URI-able, zero raster. Net-new: the vault's only feSpecularLighting use is image duotone (effects/duotone.js), and every existing grain snippet (noise-grain, gradient-noise, post-processing) uses only flat fractalNoise — none produce a lit/embossed surface.
backlog.license: Technique is MIT-reimplementable from MDN/SVG spec primitives; fffuel itself is inspiration-only (output free for commercial use but NOT redistributable/sublicensable, no attribution required)
backlog.note: Add as a new .paper / .paper-lit class family in a paper-texture.css snippet (light surfaceScale=2-4, elevation ~55deg). Reimplement from primitives, do NOT ship fffuel SVG output. Pairs with print-typography.skill.md.

backlog.name: Self-contained grain-over-gradient SVG via feComponentTransfer (gggrain single-layer technique)
backlog.what: Composites the color gradient AND the grain inside ONE data-URI SVG (feTurbulence -> feComponentTransfer to shape grain alpha/contrast -> feColorMatrix desaturate -> blended over an SVG linear/radial gradient), so the whole tactile background is a single background-image with no ::after overlay, no isolation/z-index plumbing, and no extra DOM node. feComponentTransfer (feFuncA tableValues) is the missing primitive: it lets you sculpt exactly which noise values show and at what darkness — finer control than the vault's blunt opacity+mix-blend-mode approach. Net-new: every vault grain class layers a separate pseudo-element overlay; none composite grain into the gradient SVG itself, and feComponentTransfer appears nowhere in the vault.
backlog.license: MIT-reimplementable from CSS-Tricks 'Grainy Gradients' + MDN feComponentTransfer; gggrain inspiration-only (same fffuel terms)
backlog.note: Ship as .grain-solo background-image utility for cases where adding a pseudo-element is impossible (e.g. -like single elements, mask layers, canvas fallbacks). Smaller blast radius than overlay approach.

backlog.name: Theme-aware + performance-gated grain decision-tree
backlog.what: A skill covering the two things the vault's grain snippets omit: (1) THEME-AWARENESS — invert grain blend/opacity under prefers-color-scheme (light grain reads on dark bg and vice-versa; soft-light vs overlay flip), so grain survives a theme switch instead of vanishing or smearing; (2) PERFORMANCE CEILING — numOctaves<=3 (gains above don't justify cost), bound filter paint with clip-path:inset(0) / contain:paint, cap animated grain at steps()-quantized frames + prefers-reduced-motion off, and a documented raster-escape-hatch (convert to tiny WebP/AVIF) when SVG-filter paint chokes on large areas/mobile. Net-new: no vault grain file references prefers-color-scheme or a paint-cost budget; only gradient-noise has reduced-motion.
backlog.license: N/A (original decision-tree synthesized from MDN/CSS-Tricks/FrontendMasters)
backlog.note: Could fold into print-typography.skill.md or color-grade.skill.md as a 'tactile finish' section rather than a standalone skill. This is the moat-y guidance that turns three grain snippets into a coherent, theme-safe, verify-passable choice.

backlog.name: responsive-picture.js — vanilla <picture>/srcset emitter (art-directed crops + AVIF/WebP/JPEG + fetchpriority)
backlog.what: A zero-dep helper that takes ONE source descriptor — base name, a crop map ({mobile: '...-portrait', desktop: '...-wide'} with media breakpoints), a widths array, and per-format flags — and emits the full correct : ordered elements (media for art-directed crops × type for AVIF→WebP→JPEG fallback), srcset width descriptors, a sizes attribute, plus an fallback carrying width+height (CLS reservation), loading, decoding, and fetchpriority. This is the COMPOSED-MARKUP counterpart to images.skill.md, which does Sharp encoding and literally says 'serve via /srcset + sizes' but ships no helper that writes that markup. The 2026 canonical rule is locked: source order is media-then-type, browser takes the first matching source, media = different crops (art direction) while srcset/sizes = same-image resolution switching — both combined per crop. It also encodes the LCP discipline (fetchpriority='high' on exactly the hero, never lazy-load the LCP image, always set width/height). Net-new: the vault has image-crop.js (interactive cropper) and aspect-ratios.css (CSS utility) but NOTHING that emits responsive picture markup.
backlog.license: Pattern (MDN/web.dev canonical, CC0-ish docs); reimplemented vanilla — no dep
backlog.note: Pairs with images.skill.md (Sharp emits the .avif/.webp variants, this helper emits the markup that references them). Keep API single-source so the engine can auto-emit from a hero image path.

backlog.name: Hero LCP preload pattern — <link rel=preload as=image imagesrcset/imagesizes fetchpriority=high>
backlog.what: The companion snippet for above-the-fold heroes: a rel='preload' as='image' link whose imagesrcset + imagesizes MIRROR the hero 's srcset/sizes so the browser preloads the exact resolution it will paint (not a wasted size), with fetchpriority='high'. Google's own test cut LCP 2.6s→1.9s from the fetchpriority attribute alone. Worth bundling with the picture emitter as an optional 'hero mode' that also emits the matching preload link — closing the loop the encoding skill can't (it has no LCP/preload guidance). Small but high-leverage for any composed landing page hero.
