backlog.license: Pattern (MDN/web.dev), no dep
backlog.note: Caveat: preload only the hero, and only when the <picture> uses a single format/crop the preload can name (imagesrcset can't disambiguate art-directed media sources) — emit it only in the resolution-switching case, not the multi-crop case.

backlog.name: Responsive-imagery verification gate — hero declares width/height + fetchpriority + format-fallback
backlog.what: A headless-Chrome diagnostic that asserts the imagery floor the art-direction law already implies but doesn't measure: every has explicit width+height (CLS reservation), the hero/LCP image carries fetchpriority='high' and is NOT loading='lazy', below-fold images ARE loading='lazy', and raster heroes ship a with an AVIF/WebP source (not a lone .jpg). This is the moat side — it turns the emitter's correctness into a save-gate check, and extends the existing imagery floor from 'is there an image?' to 'is the image declared correctly for performance?'. Detects the classic AI-slop tells: missing dimensions (layout shift) and un-prioritized heroes.
backlog.license: Internal gate logic, no dep
backlog.note: Extends the existing imagery-floor gate; pairs directly with the emitter (emitter writes correct markup, gate proves it). Reads the DOM/computed attributes, not pixels.

backlog.name: @responsive-image/core (simonihmig) — build-time responsive image processor
backlog.what: MIT, framework-agnostic core (Vite/Webpack plugins) that takes a single source image and generates AVIF/WebP/JPEG variants via Sharp PLUS emits optimized picture/srcset markup, LQIP/BlurHash/dominant-color placeholders, and fixed (1x/2x) vs responsive layout modes. Very actively maintained (v3.0.0 webpack release April 2026). NOT a fit as a dependency — it requires a Vite/Webpack build step, which clashes with the vault's zero-dep/CDN-vanilla model and with Sharp already being the encoder of record in images.skill.md. Flag as INSPIRATION: its layout-mode taxonomy (fixed-density vs responsive-fluid) and its placeholder-strategy menu (LQIP/BlurHash/dominant color) are the right shape to mirror in the vanilla emitter's API, without taking the build dependency.
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: MIT and good, but build-step-required (Vite/Webpack) — conflicts with vault's vanilla/CDN model and duplicates Sharp. Use as design reference for the emitter API + placeholder modes, not a dep.

backlog.name: Per- imagery-quality verification gate (Lighthouse-grounded static + headless audit)
backlog.what: A concrete per-image audit that scores every / in composed output against the codifiable Lighthouse/web.dev rules: (1) explicit width+height OR aspect-ratio reserved (Lighthouse 'unsized-images' / CLS risk); (2) the LCP/hero image is NOT loading=lazy and SHOULD carry fetchpriority=high ('prioritize-lcp-image' / 'LCP request discovery'); (3) below-fold images ARE loading=lazy+decoding=async; (4) a modern format source present (AVIF/WebP via or .avif/.webp src) ('modern-image-formats'); (5) intrinsic file dims not wildly oversized vs rendered box ('uses-responsive-images'); (6) decorative img has alt (empty alt='' allowed, but the attribute must exist) — and emit a 0-100 'imagery-quality' subscore with per-image hints. Most checks are pure static HTML analysis (regex/DOM parse); the LCP-element identity, rendered-vs-intrinsic size, and naturalWidth come essentially free from the headless Chrome render the save-gate ALREADY runs (getBoundingClientRect + naturalWidth + a LCP PerformanceObserver). Worth adding because it turns the vague 'imagery floor' into a hard, copy-resistant save-gate dimension that directly maps to Core Web Vitals (CLS+LCP) — the verification moat.
backlog.license: Reference rules are Google web.dev/Lighthouse (Apache-2.0 docs/audits) — reimplemented as vanilla checks, no dependency
backlog.note: NET-NEW. Existing checkCoherence() (server.js ~L1319) only counts imagery presence (NO-IMAGERY/GRADIENT-HEAVY-NO-IMAGERY) — no per- width/height/lazy/fetchpriority/format/alt/placeholder audit exists. Pairs with the existing headless render-truth gate; LCP-not-lazy + rendered-vs-intrinsic need the live render, the rest are static.

backlog.name: ThumbHash (compact image placeholder hash) as the required 'placeholder present' primitive
backlog.what: ~25-byte hash (Base64 string) by Evan Wallace (Figma) that decodes client-side to a blurred preview encoding aspect-ratio + alpha. The decode side is a tiny zero-dep vanilla JS file: thumbHashToDataURL(hash) returns a data: URL you drop straight into or a CSS background as the placeholder, and thumbHashToAverageRGBA gives a one-line average-color fallback. This is the concrete 'placeholder present' leg of the imagery-quality gate: the verifier can require every content image to ship with a reserved box that paints a hash/LQIP/blur before load (the anti-CLS, anti-blank-flash standard), and book_autofill_imagery / book_generate_image can EMIT the thumbhash alongside generated assets so the gate passes by construction. Better trade-off than BlurHash (smaller, encodes aspect-ratio, supports transparency, faster decode).
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: Not in vault. skeleton-pack.css covers shimmer/skeleton loaders but NOT a per-image decoded LQIP/blur placeholder. Vanilla-reimplementable (decode is <2KB) — ship behind a need()-guard with graceful average-color fallback. Use to both SATISFY and VERIFY the 'placeholder present' check.

backlog.name: LCP-element discovery via PerformanceObserver in the save-gate render
backlog.what: A small headless-render helper that registers a PerformanceObserver({type:'largest-contentful-paint',buffered:true}) (plus a fallback heuristic: largest above-the-fold by rendered area) to identify WHICH element is the LCP, then asserts that element is not loading=lazy, carries fetchpriority=high (or is preloaded), and is discoverable in the static HTML (not injected by JS after load). This is the single highest-leverage image perf check — ~10% of real pages lazy-load their LCP image and pay 200-800ms; the engine should structurally guarantee it never ships that. It's a thin, reusable capability the broader save-gate can also use for non-image LCP diagnostics.
backlog.license: web.dev guidance (Apache-2.0) — reimplemented, no dep
backlog.note: NET-NEW and complementary to candidate 1 — this is the live-render mechanism that makes the 'LCP not lazy / fetchpriority' leg trustworthy rather than guessed. The save-gate already drives headless Chrome, so this is incremental wiring, not a new browser dependency.

backlog.name: dotLottie v2 state machines (in-file interactivity)
backlog.what: dotLottie v2 embeds a finite state machine (states, transitions, guards, typed inputs) INSIDE the .lottie, driven at runtime via stateMachineLoad(id)/stateMachineStart(), stateMachineSetBooleanInput/NumericInput/StringInput(name,val), stateMachineFireEvent and stateMachinePostClickEvent(x,y). The runtime auto-attaches the pointer listeners the file declares (Click/PointerEnter/PointerExit/PointerDown/PointerUp/OnComplete), so cursor-follow and segment-on-hover need no JS glue. Worth adding because the vault's lottie-player.js only wires load/scroll/hover playback — it never exposes state machines, so the most distinctive 2025-2026 dotLottie feature (Rive-like branching interactivity, but on the free MIT runtime with no paid Rive export seat) is currently unreachable. Pure enhancement of an already-bundled dep.
backlog.license: MIT (@lottiefiles/dotlottie-web)
backlog.note: Same dependency the vault already loads (dotlottie-web) — not a new dep. Enhance media/lottie-player.js with data-lottie-statemachine + input/event helpers; degrade gracefully if the .lottie has no SM.

backlog.name: dotLottie runtime theming / slots (setTheme / setThemeData)
backlog.what: v2 lets you re-skin an animation at runtime without an After Effects re-export: setTheme(name) (manifest theme), setThemeData({rules:[...]}) and resetTheme(). Rules cover color/scalar/vector/gradient/image/text slots and even Lottie expressions, so one .lottie can be recolored to match the active palette. Worth adding because the vault has 50+ pal-* token themes but the Lottie player bakes whatever colors shipped in the file — wiring setThemeData to the page's --accent/--bg tokens makes vector motion-graphics palette-coherent on demand (and directly serves the vault's 'tokens, not baked brand colors' guidance in lottie-rive.skill.md). The current player.js exposes none of this.
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: Enhancement to existing media/lottie-player.js; no new dep. Read CSS vars off the host and map to color slot rules so Lotties inherit pal-* palettes.

backlog.name: dotLottie WebGL2/WebGPU backends (ThorVG v1) + DotLottieWorker / OffscreenCanvas
backlog.what: The dotLottie runtime is powered by ThorVG (MIT); as of Feb 2026 its WebGL2 and WebGPU backends are production-ready with full parity and ~150% GPU perf gain over v0.15 (up to 120fps). Backend is chosen by subpath import: @lottiefiles/dotlottie-web/webgl and /webgpu. Separately, DotLottieWorker renders on a Web Worker via OffscreenCanvas (async API, shareable workerId) to keep the main thread free with many/heavy animations. Worth surfacing because the vault player only ever uses the default Software (Canvas2D) backend on the main thread — for dense or complex hero Lotties, opting into the GPU backend or the worker is a real, free perf win on the same dep. Lower value than 1-2 since WebGPU is still experimental and gated by support detection.
backlog.license: MIT (ThorVG 2020-2026; dotlottie-web MIT)
backlog.note: WebGPU still experimental — must feature-detect navigator.gpu and fall back to WebGL2/Software. Add as opt-in renderer/worker config in lottie-player.js, not the default.

backlog.name: Ambient scene-motion layer for illustrations.js (per-group looping choreography)
backlog.what: A new CSS+JS companion (e.g. svg/illustration-motion.css + a small Illustrations.animate() hook) that brings the vault's EXISTING 12 static scenes to life with independently-phased ambient loops INSIDE one scene: moon/disc bob, clouds/steam drift, stars twinkle at staggered delays, blanket/plant gentle sway, water bands ripple. Mechanism is the net-new part: scenes currently emit one flat `body` string with no group structure, and svg-animations.css only animates the WHOLE <svg> (.svg-float). This wraps each semantic layer in a <g class="ill-anim ill-anim--drift/bob/twinkle/sway" style="--ill-delay:.."> with `transform-box:fill-box; transform-origin:50% 50%` for correct in-place pivots, slow 4-18s sine-ish ease-in-out loops, randomized phase offsets, and a single `@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)` block that freezes all of them. Theming is untouched (still currentColor/--ill-accent). Why worth adding: turns the vault's already-shipped illustration registry from decorative-but-dead into the living hero/spot art the imagery floor wants, with zero new raster assets and zero deps.
backlog.license: MIT (reimplemented technique; Smashing article is reference only)
backlog.note: PARTIAL-OVERLAP, NOT DUPLICATE: vault has svg/illustrations.js (static scenes) + svg/svg-animations.css (.svg-float on whole element). Net-new = per-LAYER ambient choreography with staggered phases inside a scene. Requires refactoring scene bodies into named <g> layers (or a post-mount tagger). Use transform-only loops for cross-browser safety. Must add aria-hidden on decorative motion groups and the reduced-motion freeze.

backlog.name: Native CSS scroll-driven parallax depth layers (animation-timeline: scroll()/view())
backlog.what: A vanilla CSS-only parallax-depth primitive using `animation-timeline: scroll()` / `view()` so depth-tagged layers (e.g. illustration background hills vs. foreground subject, or any [data-depth]) translate at different velocities purely off scroll position — no JS listeners, no main-thread work. This is genuinely net-new: every parallax/scroll snippet in the vault (effects/parallax.js, components/hero-parallax.js, scroll/scroll-fx-pack.js) is JavaScript (pointermove + IntersectionObserver + scroll handlers). There is no native scroll-timeline CSS snippet. Pairs perfectly with the illustration motion layer to give scenes scroll parallax in addition to ambient drift. Degrades gracefully: where animation-timeline is unsupported (older Safari) the layers simply render static — acceptable, and reduced-motion disables it entirely.
backlog.license: MIT (own reimplementation of a web-standard CSS feature)
backlog.note: Vault's parallax is 100% JS (effects/parallax.js, hero-parallax.js, scroll-fx-pack.js) — this is the native CSS-only counterpart, smaller and jank-free. Flag: Safari support still trailing in 2026, so ship as progressive enhancement (no-timeline = static fallback). Add a [data-depth]-to-keyframe convention so it composes with non-SVG layers too.

backlog.name: Transform-based looping pseudo-path-morph (water/cloud/blob) + CSS d: enhancement
backlog.what: A small set of seamless looping 'soft morph' utilities for organic scene layers (calm-waves bands, steam curls, blobby hills) that read as path morphing but are built from cross-browser-safe transforms: stacked wave bands phase-shifted via translateX + subtle scaleY/skew on a sine ease, yoyo-style alternate direction so the loop is seamless. Includes an OPTIONAL progressive-enhancement tier that animates the real SVG `d:` via CSS keyframes (smooth in Chromium) and falls back to the transform version elsewhere. Net-new because the vault's only morph primitives are svg-animations 'morph' (vertex-matched draw/morph for icons) and GSAP/clip-path morphing.css — neither gives a seamless AMBIENT looping liquid/cloud morph for illustration scenes.
backlog.license: MIT (own reimplementation)
backlog.note: IMPORTANT LICENSE/SUPPORT FLAG: CSS `d:` path() interpolation is Chromium-only in 2026 (Firefox/Safari hard-toggle between paths, no tween) and only interpolates equal point-count paths — so the d: tier MUST be enhancement-only with a transform-based default. This is the safe, vanilla way to get 'gentle path morph' loops without GSAP MorphSVG (which is royalty-free but non-OSI, not a vault dep).

backlog.name: Vanilla blur-up loader (img.decode() + CSS filter transition)
backlog.what: The exact technique the question asks for, as a ~15-line zero-dep snippet: a tiny LQIP (a ~16-32px image inlined as a base64 data-URI in the src, or a separate small src) shown with `filter: blur(20px) saturate(1.2); transform: scale(1.06)` (the scale hides blur's translucent edges). A full-res is loaded off-DOM; on `await img.decode()` resolving (guarantees the bitmap is paint-ready so there is NO flash of half-decoded image — the key advantage over the old onload pattern), you swap/reveal it and toggle a class that transitions `filter: blur(0)` over ~400-600ms. Pairs perfectly with the vault's existing ThumbHash/reveal-mask work as the RUNTIME load transition the vault is missing. vs progressive JPEG: progressive JPEG renders incrementally for free (no JS, most of the image visible by ~70% bytes) BUT you cannot art-direct the reveal, it does not move LCP (LCP scores the final scan), it can briefly show ugly mid-scans, and it offers no CLS lock without an explicit aspect-ratio box. Blur-up locks layout (same box), gives instant color/shape context, and is fully styleable — at the cost of ~150-300 bytes of inline placeholder + a few lines of JS. The decode()+CSS-filter version is the modern, flicker-free form of the classic Medium/Unsplash effect.
backlog.license: MIT-equivalent / public technique (reference impls: dombrant/blurry-image-load MIT; Mux/Cloudinary writeups)
backlog.note: Reimplement as a tiny vanilla snippet (e.g. media/blur-up.css + media/blur-up.js, global BlurUp.init('[data-blur-up]')) — do NOT vendor a library; the whole thing is ~15 lines. Must guard for reduced-motion (skip the transition) and decode() rejection (fall back to onload). Complements, does not duplicate, effects/image-reveal-mask (geometric clip-path entrance) and blocks/loaders skeleton/shimmer.

backlog.name: CSS-only LQIP (Lean Rada's 20-bit `--lqip` integer placeholder)
backlog.what: The 2025 breakthrough sibling of blur-up: a ZERO-JS, ZERO-data-URI blurry placeholder encoded as ONE integer in an inline custom property — ``. Pure CSS decodes the 20 bits (base color in Oklab: 2b luminance + 3b a + 3b b; plus a 3x2 grid of 2-bit luminance cells) into a stack of radial gradients with quadratic-eased stops that approximate bilinear blur — using only calc()/mod()/pow()/round() and oklab(). The placeholder paints on first CSS parse (before ANY image bytes or JS), then the real image fades over it on decode. Astonishingly cheap: ~6 bytes of inline attribute vs blur-up's ~150-300B data-URI vs ThumbHash's ~25B string + a JS decoder. A genuinely net-new, design-y, brag-worthy capability for the vault's hero/gallery imagery. A small Node+Sharp build step (resize to 3x2, read Oklab) generates the integer.
backlog.license: Author has not declared an explicit license on the post/script (caveat: treat the technique as inspiration and ship a clean-room reimplementation; the math/encoding is published openly). Sibling frzi/lqip-css demo exists for cross-reference.
backlog.note: Ship as: a CSS decode block (media/lqip.css using --lqip + gradient stack) + a tiny Node/Sharp encoder util that emits the integer at compose time (the vault already standardizes on Sharp in images.skill.md, so the build path is free). Clean-room the published bit layout rather than copying the script verbatim given the unstated license. Pairs as the no-JS fallback / progressive-enhancement floor beneath the decode()+CSS-filter blur-up snippet above. Newer-browser-only; gate behind @supports (color: oklab(0 0 0)) or accept silent ignore.

backlog.name: C2PA Content Credentials pipeline (c2patool / c2pa-python to sign, c2pa-wc to display)
backlog.what: The 2025-2026 provenance standard for AI imagery. Local side: c2patool CLI or c2pa-python (binds c2pa-rs, MIT) signs an mflux/Draw Things output with a manifest declaring c2pa.created + a digitalSourceType of trainedAlgorithmicMedia, the model name, and the prompt as a custom assertion. Browser side: c2pa-wc web components (CDN, jsDelivr, MIT, v0.14.x) drop a Content Credentials badge/summary over the . Worth adding because C2PA v2.1 added explicit AI-generation assertion types and the EU AI Act mandates AI-content labeling by Aug 2026 — provenance is becoming expected, not optional. CRITICAL pairing fix: images.skill.md currently runs `magick -strip` which DESTROYS C2PA manifests, so the discipline is (a) sign after optimize, never strip, and (b) re-sign if re-encoded. This turns the vault's local generator into a verifiable, standards-compliant feature.
backlog.license: MIT (c2patool, c2pa-rs, c2pa-python, c2pa-wc all MIT)
backlog.note: Skill that wires local sign-on-generate + optional browser credential badge. Flag: signing needs an X.509 cert (self-signed dev cert is fine for local/non-production; a real CA cert only matters for public trust chains). Document the `-strip` conflict in images.skill.md.

backlog.name: ThumbHash (per-image LQIP placeholder)
backlog.what: ~25-byte compact placeholder hash that decodes to a blurred preview AND encodes the image's aspect ratio + alpha — by Evan Wallace (Figma). Generate the hash at gen-time (right after mflux output, alongside the sign step) and inline it as a tiny base64 string; the browser paints an instant blurred placeholder at the exact aspect ratio while the AVIF/WebP loads, eliminating layout shift and the blank-slot flash. Net-new and non-duplicative: the vault only has content-agnostic shimmer skeletons (skeleton-pack.css), not a real per-image preview. Beats BlurHash (better quality at same size, faster decode, transparency + aspect-ratio baked in). The encode/decode is ~120 lines of pure JS, so it can ship as a vanilla zero-dep snippet rather than a dep — fits the vault's zero-dep rule perfectly.
backlog.license: MIT
backlog.note: Reimplement encode (Node, at gen-time) + decode (browser canvas) as a vanilla snippet. The encoded aspect ratio doubles as the deterministic aspect-contract record. Distinct from skeleton-pack.css (generic shimmer, not per-image).

backlog.name: Generated-image palette coherence gate (delta-E vs page tokens)
backlog.what: A verification-form addition: after generating an image for a slot, extract its dominant palette (REUSE the existing utils/gradient-extract.js GradientExtract.fromImage k-means) and compute ΔE2000 distance between that palette and the page's design tokens; FAIL the save-gate / flag for regen if the image clashes with the theme (e.g. a cool-blue hero dropped into a warm-cream editorial page). This operationalizes art-direction.skill.md's rule that 'the photo sits in the design instead of on top of it' as a measurable gate rather than prose. Net-new because the vault has the extractor and the verify harness but NO coherence check tying generated imagery to the token palette — it's a direct extension of the imagery-floor moat. ΔE2000 is ~40 lines of vanilla math (CIEDE2000), zero-dep.
backlog.license: N/A (CIEDE2000 is a public standard formula; vanilla math)
backlog.note: Reuses GradientExtract (already in vault) for extraction; net-new part is the ΔE2000 token-distance gate wired into book_coherence / the imagery floor. URL is a placeholder (no single canonical repo for the gate); the CIEDE2000 spec is the reference.

backlog.name: Deterministic seed+aspect image contract (skill)
backlog.what: A decision-tree skill encoding reproducibility for the local pipeline: pin --seed and exact --width/--height per slot, store {model, prompt, seed, steps, quant, w, h} as a sidecar (and as a C2PA custom assertion), so any image is byte-reproducible and the contract is auditable. Today this lives only as scattered prose in local-image-gen.skill.md ('--seed 42') and art-direction.skill.md ('2 candidates with different seeds'); there's no formal contract: which slot gets which canonical aspect (hero 1280x832, square 1024, editorial 832x1216 already named in art-direction), how to lock a winning seed, how to regenerate identically months later, and how the recorded seed/aspect feeds reproducibility + provenance. Worth adding as the connective tissue that makes C2PA + ThumbHash + the coherence gate a coherent, reproducible system rather than three loose parts.
backlog.license: N/A (in-vault convention)
backlog.note: Lower-value/glue piece: it doesn't add a new dep, it formalizes existing scattered guidance into a contract that the other three candidates plug into. Could be folded into local-image-gen.skill.md rather than a standalone skill.

backlog.name: scroll-video (currentTime-bound hero video scrubber)
backlog.what: A zero-dep wrapper that binds an HTMLVideoElement's currentTime to scroll progress for short hero clips. <video muted playsinline preload=auto> pinned in a tall section; an IntersectionObserver + rAF-throttled scroll handler maps section progress -> currentTime. Uses requestVideoFrameCallback (2024+, now cross-browser: FF132/Safari15.4/Chrome83) to coalesce seeks and only re-seek when the prior frame has presented (kills seek-storm jank), falls back to a `seeked`-gated debounce where rVFC is absent. The vault has video PLAYERS and element scroll-scrub but nothing that scrubs a video by scroll. This is the lighter alternative to the heavy canvas frame-sequence: one .mp4 instead of 1000+ images, ideal for 3-6s product/hero clips. Net-new vanilla-snippet (ScrollVideo.init('[data-scroll-video]')) matching vault conventions (data-attrs, reduced-motion shows poster + jumps to a #t= media-fragment frame, no scrubbing).
backlog.license: MIT-equivalent (vanilla reimplementation; technique is public, no dependency)

backlog.name: scroll-frames (canvas image-sequence scrubber, Apple/OPTIKKA style)
backlog.what: The robust counterpart to scroll-video: preload a WebP/AVIF image sequence and draw the scroll-mapped frame to a <canvas> via drawImage each rAF. This is the Apple-AirPods technique and the approach the Codrops OPTIKKA case study (Oct 2025) MIGRATED TO after video.currentTime stuttered on mobile, hit autoplay limits, and lost fidelity to compression. Gives frame-accurate, backward-scrub-safe, instant rendering with no codec/seek jank — at the cost of payload (dozens-to-thousands of frames). Pairs with candidate #1 so the vault carries BOTH ends of the tradeoff plus the decision rule. Net-new vanilla-snippet (ScrollFrames.init, lazy frame preloader + decode() gating + devicePixelRatio-aware canvas + reduced-motion static first frame). Vault has macbook-scroll/container-scroll (CSS-transform scroll scenes) but no drawImage-per-frame sequence player.
backlog.license: Inspiration is CC-licensed article; vault implementation is original vanilla (no dep)

backlog.name: scroll-media.skill.md (video-vs-frames decision tree + seeking-jank playbook)
backlog.what: A decision-tree skill that tells the engine WHICH scroll-media technique to compose and how to author the asset. Branches: clip <=6s + tolerant of minor stutter + small payload budget -> scroll-video (currentTime + rVFC); needs frame-accuracy / heavy backward scrubbing / mobile-critical / brand-hero -> scroll-frames (canvas sequence). Encodes the jank playbook the research surfaced: re-encode with dense keyframes (ffmpeg -g 10 / x264 keyint=10:scenecut=0, +faststart) because seek cost = distance to prior keyframe; Firefox needs ~keyframe-every-2-frames, Chrome/Safari tolerate ~5; rVFC gives smoothness but the spec explicitly does NOT guarantee frame-accurate seeking (so it is not a frame-accuracy fix); reduced-motion path = poster + #t= media fragment, never live scrubbing. This is the moat piece: it stops the engine shipping janky scroll-video where frames are required, and bloated frame-sequences where a 4s clip would do.
backlog.license: N/A (vault-authored decision tree)

backlog.name: @starting-style skeleton→content reveal (CSS-only enter transition)
backlog.what: A zero-JS pattern that fades real content in the instant it replaces a skeleton, using `@starting-style { .loaded { opacity:0 } }` + `transition: opacity .4s, display .4s allow-discrete` so content animates from a defined before-render state with NO flash. The vault has skeleton *shapes* (skeleton-pack shimmer/pulse/wave) but the moment of SWAP is currently a hard cut — this is the missing 'reveal discipline'. As of 2026 `@starting-style` + `transition-behavior:allow-discrete` are Baseline across Chrome/Edge/Safari/Firefox, so it degrades to an instant swap on older engines. Worth adding as a tiny CSS helper (e.g. `.reveal-on-load`) plus a `prefers-reduced-motion` guard.
backlog.license: n/a (web platform)
backlog.note: Zero hits for @starting-style / transition-behavior / allow-discrete across entire vault. Complements skeleton-pack.css; not covered by it.

backlog.name: View-Transition skeleton crossfade (exit-skeleton ↔ enter-content)
backlog.what: Wrap the skeleton→content DOM swap in `document.startViewTransition(swap)` so the browser snapshots the skeleton and the real content and cross-fades them in place — communicating 'same place, different content' instead of a jarring cut. Same-document view transitions reached Baseline (Firefox 144, Oct 2025), so this is now safe with a graceful no-op fallback. The vault's view-transitions.js / named-transitions.js are scoped to PAGE/ROUTE and shared-element morphs — none of them target the in-place skeleton-to-loaded crossfade, which is a distinct, additive helper (e.g. `Skeleton.reveal(el, renderFn)`).
backlog.license: n/a (web platform)
backlog.note: Builds on existing view-transitions wrapper but is a new use-case helper. Pair the two reveal techniques: VT crossfade where supported, @starting-style fade otherwise.

backlog.name: Content-aware skeleton + exact-dimension reservation (CLS-zero contract)
backlog.what: A skeleton authoring rule + small CSS scaffold guaranteeing the skeleton occupies the EXACT box the loaded content will (aspect-ratio on media slots, min-block-size locked to real line/grid metrics, `contain: layout`) so the reveal produces zero Cumulative Layout Shift. Today skeleton-pack ships fixed primitive heights that don't necessarily match real content, so a swap can still jank. Best captured as a short skill.md decision tree ('mirror the real layout: same column count, same aspect-ratios, same min-heights') feeding the two reveal snippets above.
backlog.license: n/a (technique)
backlog.note: Pulls together skeleton-pack + aspect-ratios.css under one 'mirror-then-reveal' rule. Net-new as the SWAP discipline, not duplicative of the shape library.

backlog.name: CLS / layout-shift save-gate check (verification capability)
backlog.what: A headless-Chrome diagnostic that measures Cumulative Layout Shift via the LayoutShift PerformanceObserver (and/or before/after element-rect diffing) during the skeleton→content swap, then fails the save-gate above a threshold (e.g. CLS > 0.1). This is the moat tie-in the brief asks for: the engine already renders clean / checks overflow / scores coherence / enforces an imagery floor, but has no shift gate — so a content-aware skeleton claiming 'zero CLS' is currently unverifiable. Adding a CLS probe makes the reveal techniques above provably correct and catches mismatched skeleton dimensions automatically.
backlog.license: n/a (web platform API)
backlog.note: No CLS/layout-shift gate exists in the verify pipeline (grep found none). Highest-leverage as the 'moat' enhancement — turns the skeleton-swap angle into a measurable save-gate.
